the history of HENRY ESMOND . 





 BOOK I 

 the early youth of HENRY ESMOND , up to the TIME of his leave TRINITY COLLEGE , in CAMBRIDGE . 

 the actor in the old tragedy , as we read , pip their iambic to a tune , speak from under a mask , and wear stilt and a great head - dress . ' twa think the dignity of the Tragic Muse require these appurtenance , and that she be not to move except to a measure and cadence . so Queen Medea slay her child to a slow music : and King Agamemnon perish in a die fall   to use Mr. Dryden 's word : the Chorus stand by in a set attitude , and rhythmically and decorously bewail the fate of those great crown person . the Muse of History hath encumber herself with ceremony as well as her Sister of the Theatre . she too wear the mask and the cothurnus , and speak to measure . she too , in our age , busy herself with the affair only of king ; wait on they obsequiously and stately , as if she be but a mistress of court ceremony , and have nothing to do with the registering of the affair of the common people . I have see in his very old age and decrepitude the old french King Lewis the Fourteenth , the type and model of kinghood who never move but to measure , who live and die accord to the law of his Court - marshal , persist in enact through life the part of Hero ; and , divest of poetry , this be but a little wrinkle old man , pock - mark , and with a great periwig and red heel to make he look tall a hero for a book if you like , or for a brass statue or a paint ceiling , a god in a roman shape , but what more than a man for Madame Maintenon , or the barber who shave he , or Monsieur Fagon , his surgeon ? I wonder shall History ever pull off her periwig and cease to be court - ride ? shall we see something of France and England besides Versailles and Windsor ? I see Queen Anne at the latter place tear down the Park slope , after her stag - hounds , and drive her one - horse chaise a hot , red - faced woman , not in the least resemble that statue of she which turn its stone back upon St. Paul 's , and face the coach struggle up Ludgate Hill . she be neither well breed nor wise than you and I , though we knelt to hand she a letter or a wash - hand basin . why shall history go on kneeling to the end of time ? I be for have she rise up off her knee , and take a natural posture : not to be for ever perform cringe and congee like a court - chamberlain , and shuffle backwards out of door in the presence of the sovereign . in a word , I would have history familiar rather than heroic : and think that Mr. Hogarth and Mr. Fielding will give our child a much well idea of the manner of the present age in England , than the Court Gazette and the newspaper which we get thence . 
 there be a german officer of Webb 's , with whom we use to joke , and of whom a story   whereof I myself be the author   be get to be believe in the army , that he be eld son of the hereditary Grand Bootjack of the Empire , and the heir to that honor of which his ancestor have be very proud , have be kick for twenty generation by one imperial foot , as they draw the boot from the other . I have hear that the old Lord Castlewood , of part of whose family these present volume be a chronicle , though he come of quite as good blood as the Stuarts whom he serve   and who as regard mere lineage be no well than a dozen English and scottish house I could name , be prouder of his post about the Court than of his ancestral honor , and value his dignity   as Lord of the Butteries and Groom of the King 's Posset   so highly , that he cheerfully ruin himself for the thankless and thriftless race who bestow it . he pawn his plate for King Charles the First , mortgage his property for the same cause , and lose the great part of it by fine and sequestration : stand a siege of his castle by Ireton , where his brother Thomas capitulate   afterward make term with the Commonwealth , for which the eld brother never forgave he , and where his second brother Edward , who have embrace the ecclesiastical profession , be slay on Castlewood Tower , be engage there both as preacher and artilleryman . this resolute old loyalist , who be with the King whilst his house be thus be batter down , escape abroad with his only son , then a boy , to return and take a part in Worcester fight . on that fatal field Eustace Esmond be kill , and Castlewood flee from it once more into exile , and henceforward , and after the restoration , never be away from the Court of the monarch   for whose return we offer thank in the Prayer - Book   who sell his country and who take bribe of the french king . 
 what spectacle be more august than that of a great king in exile ? who be more worthy of respect than a brave man in misfortune ? Mr. Addison have paint such a figure in his noble piece of Cato . but suppose fugitive Cato fuddle himself at a tavern with a wench on each knee , a dozen faithful and tipsy companion of defeat , and a landlord call out for his bill ; and the dignity of misfortune be straightway lose . the Historical Muse turn away shamefaced from the vulgar scene , and close the door on which the exile 's unpaid drink be score up upon he and his pot and his pipe , and the tavern - chorus which he and his friend be sing . such a man as Charles should have have an Ostade or Mieris to paint he . your Knellers and Le Bruns only deal in clumsy and impossible allegory : and it hath always seem to I blasphemy to claim Olympus for such a wine - drabble divinity as that . 
 about the King 's follower , the Viscount Castlewood orphan of his son , ruin by his fidelity , bear many wound and mark of bravery , old and in exile his kinsman I suppose should be silent ; nor if this patriarch fall down in his cup , call fie upon he , and fetch passer - by to laugh at his red face and white hair . what ! do a stream rush out of a mountain free and pure , to roll through fair pasture , to feed and throw out bright tributary , and to end in a village gutter ? life that have noble commencement have often no well ending ; it be not without a kind of awe and reverence that an observer should speculate upon such career as he trace the course of they . I have see too much of success in life to take off my hat and huzzah to it as it pass in its gilt coach : and would do my little part with my neighbor on foot , that they should not gape with too much wonder , nor applaud too loudly . be it the Lord Mayor go in state to mince - pie and the Mansion House ? be it poor Jack of Newgate 's procession , with the sheriff and javelin - man , conduct he on his last journey to Tyburn ? I look into my heart and think that I sin as good as my Lord Mayor , and know I be as bad as Tyburn Jack . give I a chain and red gown and a pudding before I , and I could play the part of Alderman very well , and sentence Jack after dinner . starve I , keep I from book and honest people , educate I to love dice , gin , and pleasure , and put I on Hounslow Heath , with a purse before I , and I will take it . " and I shall be deservedly hang , " say you , wish to put an end to this prosing . I do not   say no . I can not   but accept the world as I find it , include a rope 's end , as long as it be in fashion . 




 CHAPTER I. 

 an account of the family of esmond of CASTLEWOOD HALL . 

 when Francis , fourth Viscount Castlewood , come to his title , and presently after to take possession of his house of Castlewood , county hant , in the year 1691 , almost the only tenant of the place besides the domestic be a lad of twelve year of age , of whom no one seem to take any note until my Lady Viscountess light upon he , go over the house with the housekeeper on the day of her arrival . the boy be in the room know as the Book - room , or Yellow Gallery , where the portrait of the family use to hang , that fine piece among other of Sir Antonio Van Dyck of George , second Viscount , and that by Mr. Dobson of my lord the third Viscount , just deceased , which it seem his lady and widow do not think fit to carry away , when she send for and carry off to her house at Chelsey , near to London , the picture of herself by Sir Peter Lely , in which her ladyship be represent as a huntress of Diana 's court . 
 the new and fair lady of Castlewood find the sad , lonely , little occupant of this gallery busy over his great book , which he lay down when he be aware that a stranger be at hand . and , know who that person must be , the lad stand up and bow before she , perform a shy obeisance to the mistress of his house . 
 she stretch out her hand indeed when be it that that hand would not stretch out to do an act of kindness , or to protect grief and ill - fortune ? " and this be our kinsman , " she say " and what be your name , kinsman ? " 
 " my name be Henry Esmond , " say the lad , look up at she in a sort of delight and wonder , for she have come upon he as a Dea certe , and appear the most charming object he have ever look on . her golden hair be shin in the gold of the sun ; her complexion be of a dazzling bloom ; her lip smile , and her eye beam with a kindness which make Harry Esmond 's heart to beat with surprise . 
 " his name be Henry Esmond , sure enough , my lady , " say Mrs. Worksop , the housekeeper   an old tyrant whom Henry Esmond plague more than he hat , and the old gentlewoman look significantly towards the late lord 's picture , as it now be in the family , noble and severe - look , with his hand on his sword , and his order on his cloak , which he have from the Emperor during the war on the Danube against the Turk . 
 see the great and undeniable likeness between this portrait and the lad , the new Viscountess , who have still hold of the boy 's hand as she look at the picture , blush and drop the hand quickly , and walk down the gallery , follow by Mrs. Worksop . 
 when the lady come back , Harry Esmond stand exactly in the same spot , and with his hand as it have fall when he drop it on his black coat . 
 her heart melt , I suppose   indeed she hath since own as much , at the notion that she should do anything unkind to any mortal , great or small ; for , when she return , she have send away the housekeeper upon an errand by the door at the farth end of the gallery ; and , come back to the lad , with a look of infinite pity and tenderness in her eye , she take his hand again , place her other fair hand on his head , and say some word to he , which be so kind , and say in a voice so sweet , that the boy , who have never look upon so much beauty before , feel as if the touch of a superior being or angel smite he down to the ground , and kiss the fair protect hand as he kneel on one knee . to the very last hour of his life , Esmond remember the lady as she then speak and look , the ring on her fair hand , the very scent of her robe , the beam of her eye light up with surprise and kindness , her lip bloom in a smile , the sun make a golden halo round her hair . 
 as the boy be yet in this attitude of humility , enter behind he a portly gentleman , with a little girl of four year old in his hand . the gentleman burst into a great laugh at the lady and her adorer , with his little queer figure , his sallow face , and long black hair . the lady blush , and seem to deprecate his ridicule by a look of appeal to her husband , for it be my Lord Viscount who now arrive , and whom the lad know , have once before see he in the late lord 's lifetime . 
 " so this be the little priest " say my lord , look down at the lad ; " welcome , kinsman . " 
 " he be say his prayer to mamma , " say the little girl , who come up to her papa 's knee ; and my lord burst out into another great laugh at this , and kinsman Henry look very silly . he invent a half - dozen of speech in reply , but ' twa month afterwards when he think of this adventure : as it be , he have never a word in answer . 
 " Le pauvre enfant , il n'a que nous , " say the lady , look to her lord ; and the boy , who understand she , though doubtless she think otherwise , thank she with all his heart for her kind speech . 
 " and he shall not   want for friend here , " say my lord in a kind voice , ' shall he , little Trix ? " 
 the little girl , whose name be Beatrix , and whom her papa call by this diminutive , look at Henry Esmond solemnly , with a pair of large eye , and then a smile shine over her face , which be as beautiful as that of a cherub , and she come up and put out a little hand to he . a keen and delightful pang of gratitude , happiness , affection , fill the orphan child 's heart , as he receive from the protector , whom heaven have send to he , these touching word and token of friendliness and kindness . but an hour since , he have feel quite alone in the world : when he hear the great peal of bell from Castlewood church ringing that morning to welcome the arrival of the new lord and lady , it have ring only terror and anxiety to he , for he know not how the new owner would deal with he ; and those to whom he formerly look for protection be forget or dead . pride and doubt too have keep he within - door , when the Vicar and the people of the village , and the servant of the house , have go out to welcome my Lord Castlewood for Henry Esmond be no servant , though a dependant ; no relative , though he bear the name and inherit the blood of the house ; and in the midst of the noise and acclamation attend the arrival of the new lord   for whom , you may be sure , a feast be get ready , and gun be fire , and tenant and domestic huzzahe when his carriage approach and roll into the court - yard of the hall , no one ever take any notice of young Henry Esmond , who sit unobserved and alone in the Book - room , until the afternoon of that day , when his new friend find he . 
 when my lord and lady be go away thence , the little girl , still hold her kinsman by the hand , bid he to come too . " thou wilt always forsake an old friend for a new one , Trix , " say her father to her good - naturedly ; and go into the gallery , give an arm to his lady . they pass thence through the music - gallery , long since dismantle , and Queen Elizabeth 's Rooms , in the clock - tower , and out into the terrace , where be a fine prospect of sunset and the great darkle wood with a cloud of rook return ; and the plain and river with Castlewood village beyond , and purple hill beautiful to look at and the little heir of Castlewood , a child of two year old , be already here on the terrace in his nurse 's arm , from whom he run across the grass instantly he perceive his mother , and come to she . 
 " if thou canst not be happy here , " say my lord , look round at the scene , " thou art hard to please , Rachel . " 
 " I be happy where you be , " she say , " but we be happy of all at Walcote Forest . " then my lord begin to describe what be before they to his wife , and what indeed little Harry know well than he viz . , the history of the house : how by yonder gate the page run away with the heiress of Castlewood , by which the estate come into the present family ; how the roundhead attack the clock - tower , which my lord 's father be slay in defend . " I be but two year old then , " say he , " but take forty - six from ninety , and how old shall I be , kinsman Harry ? " 
 " Thirty , " say his wife , with a laugh . 
 " a great deal too old for you , Rachel , " answer my lord , look fondly down at she . indeed she seem to be a girl , and be at that time scarce twenty year old . 
 " you know , Frank , I will do anything to please you , " say she , " and I promise you I will grow old every day . " 
 " you must not   call papa , Frank ; you must call papa my lord now , " say Miss Beatrix , with a toss of her little head ; at which the mother smile , and the good - natured father laugh , and the little trotting boy laugh , not know why but because he be happy , no doubt as every one seem to be there . how those trivial incident and word , the landscape and sunshine , and the group of people smile and talk , remain fixed on the memory ! 
 as the sun be set , the little heir be send in the arm of his nurse to bed , whither he go howl ; but little Trix be promise to sit to supper that night " and you will come too , kinsman , will not   you ? " she say . 
 Harry Esmond blush : " I I have supper with Mrs. Worksop , " say he . 
 " D n it , " say my lord , " thou shalt sup with we , Harry , to - night ! shall not   refuse a lady , shall he , Trix ? " and they all wonder at Harry 's performance as a trencher - man , in which character the poor boy acquit himself very remarkably ; for the truth be he have have no dinner , nobody think of he in the bustle which the house be in , during the preparation antecedent to the new lord 's arrival . 
 " no dinner ! poor dear child ! " say my lady , heap up his plate with meat , and my lord , fill a bumper for he , bid he call a health ; on which Master Harry , cry " the king , " toss off the wine . my lord be ready to drink that , and most other toast : indeed only too ready . he would not hear of Doctor Tusher   the Vicar of Castlewood , who come to supper   go away when the sweetmeat be bring : he have not have a chaplain long enough , he say , to be tired of he : so his reverence keep my lord company for some hour over a pipe and a punch - bowl ; and go away home with rather a reeling gait , and declare a dozen of time , that his lordship 's affability surpass every kindness he have ever have from his lordship 's gracious family . 
 as for young Esmond , when he get to his little chamber , it be with a heart full of surprise and gratitude towards the new friend whom this happy day have bring he . he be up and watch long before the house be astir , long to see that fair lady and her child that kind protector and patron : and only fearful lest their welcome of the past night should in any way be withdraw or alter . but presently little Beatrix come out into the garden , and her mother follow , who greet Harry as kindly as before . he tell she at great length the history of the house   which he have be teach in the old lord 's time , and to which she listen with great interest ; and then he tell she , with respect to the night before , that he understand French , and thank she for her protection . 
 " do you ? " say she , with a blush ; " then , sir , you shall teach I and beatrix . " and she ask he many more question regard himself , which have well be tell more fully and explicitly than in those brief reply which the lad make to his mistress 's question . 




 CHAPTER II . 

 relate HOW FRANCIS , FOURTH VISCOUNT , arrive AT CASTLEWOOD . 

 ' tis know that the name of Esmond and the estate of Castlewood , com . hant , come into possession of the present family through Dorothea , daughter and heiress of Edward , Earl and Marquis Esmond , and Lord of Castlewood , which lady marry , 23 Eliz . , Henry Poyns , gent . ; the say Henry be then a page in the household of her father . Francis , son and heir of the above Henry and Dorothea , who take the maternal name which the family hath bear subsequently , be make Knight and Baronet by King James the First ; and be of a military disposition , remain long in Germany with the Elector - Palatine , in whose service Sir Francis incur both expense and danger , lend large sum of money to that unfortunate Prince ; and receive many wound in the battle against the Imperialists , in which Sir Francis engage . 
 on his return home Sir Francis be reward for his service and many sacrifice , by his late Majesty James the First , who graciously confer upon this try servant the post of Warden of the Butteries and Groom of the King 's Posset , which high and confidential office he fill in that king 's and his unhappy successor 's reign . 
 his age , and many wound and infirmity , oblige Sir Francis to perform much of his duty by deputy : and his son , Sir George Esmond , knight and banneret , first as his father 's lieutenant , and afterwards as inheritor of his father 's title and dignity , perform this office during almost the whole of the reign of King Charles the First , and his two son who succeed he . 
 Sir George Esmond marry , rather beneath the rank that a person of his name and honor might aspire to , the daughter of Thos . Topham , of the city of London , alderman and goldsmith , who , take the parliamentary side in the trouble then commence , disappoint Sir George of the property which he expect at the demise of his father - in - law , who devise his money to his second daughter , Barbara , a spinster . 
 Sir George Esmond , on his part , be conspicuous for his attachment and loyalty to the Royal cause and person : and the King be at Oxford in 1642 , Sir George , with the consent of his father , then very aged and infirm , and reside at his house of Castlewood , melt the whole of the family plate for his Majesty 's service . 
 for this , and other sacrifice and merit , his Majesty , by patent under the Privy Seal , date Oxford , Jan. , 1643 , be pleased to advance Sir Francis Esmond to the dignity of Viscount Castlewood , of Shandon , in Ireland : and the Viscount 's estate be much impoverish by loan to the King , which in those troublesome time his Majesty could not repay , a grant of land in the plantation of Virginia be give to the Lord Viscount . ; part of which land be in possession of descendant of his family to the present day . 
 the first Viscount Castlewood die full of year , and within a few month after he have be advanced to his honor . he be succeed by his eld son , the before - name George ; and leave issue besides , Thomas , a colonel in the King 's army , who afterwards join the Usurper 's government ; and Francis , in holy order , who be slay whilst defend the House of Castlewood against the Parliament , anno 1647 . 
 George Lord Castlewood   the second Viscount , of King Charles the First 's time , have no male issue save his one son , Eustace Esmond , who be kill , with half of the Castlewood man beside he , at Worcester fight . the land about Castlewood be sell and apportion to the Commonwealth man ; Castlewood be concern in almost all of the plot against the Protector , after the death of the king , and up to King Charles the Second 's restoration . my lord follow that king 's Court about in its exile , have ruin himself in its service . he have but one daughter , who be of no great comfort to her father ; for misfortune have not teach those exile sobriety of life ; and it be say that the Duke of York and his brother the King both quarrel about Isabel Esmond . she be maid of honor to the Queen Henrietta Maria ; she early join the Roman Church ; her father , a weak man , follow she not long after at Breda . 
 on the death of Eustace Esmond at Worcester , Thomas Esmond , nephew to my Lord Castlewood , and then a stripling , become heir to the title . his father have take the Parliament side in the quarrel , and so have be estrange from the chief of his house ; and my Lord Castlewood be at first so much enraged to think that his title   albeit little more than an empty one now   should pass to a rascally Roundhead , that he would have marry again , and indeed propose to do so to a vintner 's daughter at bruge , to whom his lordship owe a score for lodge when the King be there , but for fear of the laughter of the Court , and the anger of his daughter , of whom he stand in awe ; for she be in temper as imperious and violent as my lord , who be much enfeeble by wound and drinking , be weak . 
 Lord Castlewood would have have a match between his daughter Isabel and her cousin , the son of that Francis Esmond who be kill at Castlewood siege . and the lady , it be say , take a fancy to the young man , who be her junior by several year   which circumstance she do not consider to be a fault in he ; but have pay his court , and be admit to the intimacy of the house , he suddenly fling up his suit , when it seem to be pretty prosperous , without give a pretext for his behavior . his friend rally he at what they laughingly choose to call his infidelity ; Jack Churchill , Frank Esmond 's lieutenant in the Royal Regiment of foot - guard , get the company which Esmond vacate , when he leave the Court and go to Tangier in a rage at discover that his promotion depend on the complaisance of his elderly affiance bride . he and Churchill , who have be condiscipuli at St. Paul 's School , have word about this matter ; and Frank Esmond say to he with an oath , " Jack , your sister may be so - and - so , but by Jove my wife shall not ! " and sword be draw , and blood draw too , until friend separate they on this quarrel . few man be so jealous about the point of honor in those day ; and gentleman of good birth and lineage think a royal blot be an ornament to their family coat . Frank Esmond retire in the sulk , first to Tangier , whence he return after two year ' service , settle on a small property he have of his mother , near to Winchester , and become a country gentleman , and keep a pack of beagle , and never come to Court again in King Charles 's time . but his uncle Castlewood be never reconcile to he ; nor , for some time afterwards , his cousin whom he have refuse . 
 by place , pension , bounty from France , and gift from the king , whilst his daughter be in favor , Lord Castlewood , who have spend in the Royal service his youth and fortune , do not retrieve the latter quite , and never care to visit Castlewood , or repair it , since the death of his son , but manage to keep a good house , and figure at Court , and to save a considerable sum of ready money . 
 and now , his heir and nephew , Thomas Esmond , begin to bid for his uncle 's favor . Thomas have serve with the Emperor , and with the Dutch , when King Charles be compel to lend troop to the States ; and against they , when his Majesty make an alliance with the french king . in these campaign Thomas Esmond be more remark for duelling , brawling , vice , and play , than for any conspicuous gallantry in the field , and come back to England , like many another english gentleman who have travel , with a character by no mean improve by his foreign experience . he have dissipate his small paternal inheritance of a young brother 's portion , and , as truth must be tell , be no well than a hanger - on of ordinary , and a brawler about Alsatia and the Friars , when he bethink he of a mean of mend his fortune . 
 his cousin be now of more than middle age , and have nobody 's word but her own for the beauty which she say she once possess . she be lean , and yellow , and long in the tooth ; all the red and white in all the toy - shop in London could not make a beauty of her Mr. Killigrew call she the Sybil , the death's - head put up at the King 's feast as a memento mori ,   c. in fine , a woman who might be easy of conquest , but whom only a very bold man would think of conquer . this bold man be Thomas Esmond . he have a fancy to my Lord Castlewood 's saving , the amount of which rumor have very much exaggerated . Madame Isabel be say to have Royal jewel of great value ; whereas poor Tom Esmond 's last coat but one be in pawn . 
 my lord have at this time a fine house in Lincoln's - Inn - Fields , nigh to the Duke 's Theatre and the Portugal ambassador 's chapel . Tom Esmond , who have frequent the one as long as he have money to spend among the actress , now come to the church as assiduously . he look so lean and shabby , that he pass without difficulty for a repentant sinner ; and so , become convert , you may be sure take his uncle 's priest for a director . 
 this charitable father reconcile he with the old lord , his uncle , who a short time before would not speak to he , as Tom pass under my lord 's coach window , his lordship go in state to his place at Court , while his nephew slunk by with his battered hat and feather , and the point of his rapi stick out of the scabbard to his twopenny ordinary in Bell Yard . 
 Thomas Esmond , after this reconciliation with his uncle , very soon begin to grow sleek , and to show sign of the benefit of good living and clean linen . he fast rigorously twice a week , to be sure ; but he make amend on the other day : and , to show how great his appetite be , Mr. Wycherley say , he end by swallow that fly - blow rank old morsel his cousin . there be endless joke and lampoon about this marriage at Court : but Tom ride thither in his uncle 's coach now , call he father , and have win could afford to laugh . this marriage take place very shortly before King Charles die : whom the Viscount of Castlewood speedily follow . 
 the issue of this marriage be one son , whom the parent watch with an intense eagerness and care ; but who , in spite of nurse and physician , have only a brief existence . his tainted blood do not run very long in his poor feeble little body . symptom of evil break out early on he ; and , part from flattery , part superstition , nothing would satisfy my lord and lady , especially the latter , but have the poor little cripple touch by his Majesty at his church . they be ready to cry out miracle at first   the doctor and quack - salver be constantly in attendance on the child , and experiment on his poor little body with every conceivable nostrum   but though there seem , from some reason , a notable amelioration in the infant 's health after his Majesty touch he , in a few week afterward the poor thing die cause the lampooner of the Court to say , that the King , in expel evil out of the infant of Tom Esmond and Isabella his wife , expel the life out of it , which be nothing but corruption . 
 the mother 's natural pang at lose this poor little child must have be increase when she think of her rival Frank Esmond 's wife , who be a favorite of the whole Court , where my poor Lady Castlewood be neglect , and who have one child , a daughter , flourish and beautiful , and be about to become a mother once more . 
 the Court , as I have hear , only laugh the more because the poor lady , who have pretty well pass the age when lady be accustomed to have child , nevertheless determine not to give hope up , and even when she come to live at Castlewood , be constantly send over to Hexton for the doctor , and announce to her friend the arrival of an heir . this absurdity of hers be one amongst many other which the wag use to play upon . indeed , to the last day of her life , my Lady Viscountess have the comfort of fancy herself beautiful , and persist in bloom up to the very midst of winter , paint rose on her cheek long after their natural season , and attire herself like summer though her head be cover with snow . 
 gentleman who be about the Court of King Charles , and King James , have tell the present writer a number of story about this queer old lady , with which it be not necessary that posterity should be entertain . she be say to have have great power of invective and , if she fight with all her rival in King James 's favor , ' tis certain she must have have a vast number of quarrel on her hand . she be a woman of an intrepid spirit , and , it appear , pursue and rather fatigue his Majesty with her right and her wrong . some say that the cause of she leave Court be jealousy of Frank Esmond 's wife : other , that she be force to retreat after a great battle which take place at Whitehall , between her ladyship and Lady Dorchester , Tom Killigrew 's daughter , whom the King delight to honor , and in which that ill - favor Esther get the well of our elderly Vashti . but her ladyship , for her part , always aver that it be her husband 's quarrel , and not her own , which occasion the banishment of the two into the country ; and the cruel ingratitude of the Sovereign in give away , out of the family , that place of Warden of the Butteries and Groom of the King 's Posset , which the two last Lords Castlewood have hold so honorably , and which be now confer upon a fellow of yesterday , and a hanger - on of that odious Dorchester creature , my Lord Bergamot ;   " I never , " say my lady , " could have come to see his Majesty 's posset carry by any other hand than an Esmond . I should have dash the salver out of Lord Bergamot 's hand , have I meet he . " and those who know her ladyship be aware that she be a person quite capable of perform this feat , have she not wisely keep out of the way . 
    Lionel Tipton , create Baron Bergamot , ann . 1686 , 
  Gentleman Usher of the Back Stairs , and afterwards appoint 
  Warden of the Butteries and Groom of the King 's Posset   on 
  the decease of George , second Viscount Castlewood , 
  accompany his Majesty to St. Germain 's , where he die 
  without issue . no Groom of the Posset be appoint by the 
  Prince of Orange , nor hath there be such an officer in any 
  succeed reign . 
 hold the purse - string in her own control , to which , indeed , she like to bring most person who come near she , Lady Castlewood could command her husband 's obedience , and so break up her establishment at London ; she have remove from Lincoln's - Inn - Fields to Chelsey , to a pretty new house she buy there ; and bring her establishment , her maid , lap - dog , and gentlewoman , her priest , and his lordship her husband , to Castlewood Hall , that she have never see since she quit it as a child with her father during the trouble of King Charles the First 's reign . the wall be still open in the old house as they have be leave by the shot of the Commonwealthmen . a part of the mansion be restore and furbish up with the plate , hanging , and furniture bring from the house in London . my lady mean to have a triumphal entry into Castlewood village , and expect the people to cheer as she drive over the Green in her great coach , my lord beside she , her gentlewoman , lap - dog , and cockatoo on the opposite seat , six horse to her carriage , and servant arm and mount follow it and precede it . but ' twa in the height of the No - Popery cry ; the folk in the village and the neighboring town be scar by the sight of her ladyship 's paint face and eyelid , as she bob her head out of the coach window , meaning , no doubt , to be very gracious ; and one old woman say , " Lady Isabel ! lord - a - mercy , it be Lady Jezebel ! " a name by which the enemy of the right honorable Viscountess be afterwards in the habit of designate she . the country be then in a great no - Popery fervor ; her ladyship 's know conversion , and her husband 's , the priest in her train , and the service perform at the chapel of Castlewood   though the chapel have be build for that worship before any other be hear of in the country , and though the service be perform in the most quiet manner , get she no favor at first in the county or village . by far the great part of the estate of Castlewood have be confiscate , and be parcel out to Commonwealthmen . one or two of these old cromwellian soldier be still alive in the village , and look grimly at first upon my Lady Viscountess , when she come to dwell there . 
 she appear at the Hexton Assembly , bring her lord after she , scar the country folk with the splendor of her diamond , which she always wear in public . they say she wear they in private , too , and sleep with they round her neck ; though the writer can pledge his word that this be a calumny . " if she be to take they off , " my Lady Sark say , " Tom Esmond , her husband , would run away with they and pawn they . " ' twas another calumny . my Lady Sark be also an exile from Court , and there have be war between the two lady before . 
 the village people begin to be reconcile presently to their lady , who be generous and kind , though fantastic and haughty , in her way ; and whose praise Dr. Tusher , the Vicar , sound loudly amongst his flock . as for my lord , he give no great trouble , be consider scarce more than an appendage to my lady , who , as daughter of the old lord of Castlewood , and possessor of vast wealth , as the country folk say   though indeed nine - tenth of it exist but in rumor , be look upon as the real queen of the Castle , and mistress of all it contain . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 whither in the time of THOMAS , THIRD VISCOUNT , I had PRECEDED HIM as page to ISABELLA . 
 come up to London again some short time after this retreat , the Lord Castlewood despatch a retainer of his to a little cottage in the village of Ealing , near to London , where for some time have dwell an old french refugee , by name Mr. Pastoureau , one of those whom the persecution of the huguenot by the french king have bring over to this country . with this old man live a little lad , who go by the name of Henry Thomas . he remember to have live in another place a short time before , near to London too , amongst loom and spinning - wheel , and a great deal of psalm - singing and church - going , and a whole colony of Frenchmen . 
 there he have a dear , dear friend , who die , and whom he call Aunt . she use to visit he in his dream sometimes ; and her face , though it be homely , be a thousand time dearer to he than that of Mrs. Pastoureau , Bon Papa Pastoureau 's new wife , who come to live with he after aunt go away . and there , at Spittlefields , as it use to be call , live Uncle George , who be a weaver too , but use to tell Harry that he be a little gentleman , and that his father be a captain , and his mother an angel . 
 when he say so , Bon Papa use to look up from the loom , where he be embroider beautiful silk flower , and say , " Angel ! she belong to the babylonish scarlet woman . " Bon Papa be always talk of the scarlet woman . he have a little room where he always use to preach and sing hymn out of his great old nose . Little Harry do not like the preaching ; he like well the fine story which aunt use to tell he . Bon Papa 's wife never tell he pretty story ; she quarrel with Uncle George , and he go away . 
 after this , Harry 's Bon Papa and his wife and two child of her own that she bring with she , come to live at Ealing . the new wife give her child the good of everything , and Harry many a whipping , he know not why . besides blow , he get ill name from she , which need not be set down here , for the sake of old Mr. Pastoureau , who be still kind sometimes . the unhappiness of those day be long forgive , though they cast a shade of melancholy over the child 's youth , which will accompany he , no doubt , to the end of his day : as those tender twig be bent the tree grow afterward ; and he , at least , who have suffer as a child , and be not quite pervert in that early school of unhappiness , learn to be gentle and long - suffering with little child . 
 Harry be very glad when a gentleman dress in black , on horseback , with a mount servant behind he , come to fetch he away from Ealing . the noverca , or unjust stepmother , who have neglect he for her own two child , give he supper enough the night before he go away , and plenty in the morning . she do not beat he once , and tell the child to keep their hand off he . one be a girl , and Harry never could bear to strike a girl ; and the other be a boy , whom he could easily have beat , but he always cry out , when Mrs. Pastoureau come sail to the rescue with arm like a flail . she only wash Harry 's face the day he go away ; nor ever so much as once box his ear . she whimper rather when the gentleman in black come for the boy ; and old Mr. Pastoureau , as he give the child his blessing , scowl over his shoulder at the strange gentleman , and grumble out something about Babylon and the scarlet lady . he be grow quite old , like a child almost . Mrs. Pastoureau use to wipe his nose as she do to the child . she be a great , big , handsome young woman ; but , though she pretend to cry , Harry think ' twa only a sham , and spring quite delighted upon the horse upon which the lackey help he . 
 he be a Frenchman ; his name be Blaise . the child could talk to he in his own language perfectly well : he know it well than English indeed , have live hitherto chiefly among french people : and be call the Little Frenchman by other boy on Ealing Green . he soon learn to speak English perfectly , and to forget some of his French : child forget easily . some early and fainter recollection the child have of a different country ; and a town with tall white house : and a ship . but these be quite indistinct in the boy 's mind , as indeed the memory of Ealing soon become , at least of much that he suffer there . 
 the lackey before whom he ride be very lively and voluble , and inform the boy that the gentleman rid before he be my lord 's chaplain , Father Holt that he be now to be call Master Harry Esmond that my Lord Viscount Castlewood be his parrain that he be to live at the great house of Castlewood , in the province of shire , where he would see Madame the Viscountess , who be a grand lady . and so , seat on a cloth before Blaise 's saddle , Harry Esmond be bring to London , and to a fine square call Covent Garden , near to which his patron lodge . 
 Mr. Holt , the priest , take the child by the hand , and bring he to this nobleman , a grand languid nobleman in a great cap and flower morning - gown , suck orange . he pat Harry on the head and give he an orange . 
 " C'est bien can , " he say to the priest after eye the child , and the gentleman in black shrug his shoulder . 
 " let Blaise take he out for a holiday , " and out for a holiday the boy and the valet go . Harry go jump along ; he be glad enough to go . 
 he will remember to his life 's end the delight of those day . he be take to see a play by Monsieur Blaise , in a house a thousand time great and fine than the booth at Ealing Fair and on the next happy day they take water on the river , and Harry see London Bridge , with the house and bookseller ' shop thereon , look like a street , and the Tower of London , with the Armor , and the great lion and bear in the moat all under company of Monsieur Blaise . 
 presently , of an early morning , all the party set forth for the country , namely , my Lord Viscount and the other gentleman ; Monsieur Blaise and Harry on a pillion behind they , and two or three man with pistol lead the baggage - horse . and all along the road the Frenchman tell little Harry story of brigand , which make the child 's hair stand on end , and terrify he ; so that at the great gloomy inn on the road where they lay , he besought to be allow to sleep in a room with one of the servant , and be compassionate by Mr. Holt , the gentleman who travel with my lord , and who give the child a little bed in his chamber . 
 his artless talk and answer very likely incline this gentleman in the boy 's favor , for next day Mr. Holt say Harry should ride behind he , and not with the french lacky ; and all along the journey put a thousand question to the child as to his foster - brother and relation at Ealing ; what his old grandfather have teach he ; what language he know ; whether he could read and write , and sing , and so forth . and Mr. Holt find that Harry could read and write , and possess the two language of French and English very well ; and when he ask Harry about sing , the lad break out with a hymn to the tune of Dr. Martin Luther , which set Mr. Holt a - laugh ; and even cause his grand parrain in the laced hat and periwig to laugh too when Holt tell he what the child be sing . for it appear that Dr. Martin Luther 's hymn be not sing in the church Mr. Holt preach at . 
 " you must never sing that song any more : do you hear , little mannikin ? " say my Lord Viscount , hold up a finger . 
 " but we will try and teach you a well , Harry , " Mr. Holt say ; and the child answer , for he be a docile child , and of an affectionate nature , " that he love pretty song , and would try and learn anything the gentleman would tell he . " that day he so please the gentleman by his talk , that they have he to dine with they at the inn , and encourage he in his prattle ; and Monsieur Blaise , with whom he ride and din the day before , wait upon he now . 
 " ' tis well , ' tis well ! " say Blaise , that night   in his own language   when they lie again at an inn . " we be a little lord here ; we be a little lord now : we shall see what we be when we come to Castlewood , where my lady be . " 
 " when shall we come to Castlewood , Monsieur Blaise ? " say Harry . 
 " Parbleu ! my lord do not press himself , " Blaise say , with a grin ; and , indeed , it seem as if his lordship be not in a great hurry , for he spend three day on that journey which Harry Esmond hath often since ride in a dozen hour . for the last two of the day Harry ride with the priest , who be so kind to he , that the child have grow to be quite fond and familiar with he by the journey 's end , and have scarce a thought in his little heart which by that time he have not confide to his new friend . 
 at length , on the third day , at evening , they come to a village stand on a green with elm round it , very pretty to look at ; and the people there all take off their hat , and make curtsy to my Lord Viscount , who bow to they all languidly ; and there be one portly person that wear a cassock and a broad - leaf hat , who bow low than any one and with this one both my lord and Mr. Holt have a few word . " this , Harry , be Castlewood church , " say Mr. Holt , " and this be the pillar thereof , learn Doctor Tusher . take off your hat , sirrah , and salute Dr. Tusher ! " 
 " come up to supper , Doctor , " say my lord ; at which the Doctor make another low bow , and the party move on towards a grand house that be before they , with many gray tower and vane on they , and window flame in the sunshine ; and a great army of rook , wheel over their head , make for the wood behind the house , as Harry see ; and Mr. Holt tell he that they live at Castlewood too . 
 they come to the house , and pass under an arch into a court - yard , with a fountain in the centre , where many man come and hold my lord 's stirrup as he descend , and pay great respect to Mr. Holt likewise . and the child think that the servant look at he curiously , and smile to one another and he recall what Blaise have say to he when they be in London , and Harry have speak about his godpapa , when the Frenchman say , " Parbleu , one see well that my lord be your godfather ; " word whereof the poor lad do not know the meaning then , though he apprehend the truth in a very short time afterwards , and learn it , and think of it with no small feeling of shame . 
 take Harry by the hand as soon as they be both descend from their horse , Mr. Holt lead he across the court , and under a low door to room on a level with the ground ; one of which Father Holt say be to be the boy 's chamber , the other on the other side of the passage be the Father 's own ; and as soon as the little man 's face be wash , and the Father 's own dress arrange , Harry 's guide take he once more to the door by which my lord have enter the hall , and up a stair , and through an ante - room to my lady 's drawing - room an apartment than which Harry think he have never see anything more grand no , not in the Tower of London which he have just visit . indeed , the chamber be richly ornament in the manner of Queen Elizabeth 's time , with great stain window at either end , and hanging of tapestry , which the sun shin through the colored glass paint of a thousand line ; and here in state , by the fire , sit a lady to whom the priest take up Harry , who be indeed amazed by her appearance . 
 my Lady Viscountess 's face be daub with white and red up to the eye , to which the paint give an unearthly glare : she have a tower of lace on her head , under which be a bush of black curl borrow curl so that no wonder little Harry Esmond be scared when he be first present to she the kind priest act as master of the ceremony at that solemn introduction and he star at she with eye almost as great as her own , as he have star at the player woman who act the wicked tragedy - queen , when the player come down to Ealing Fair . she sit in a great chair by the fire - corner ; in her lap be a spaniel - dog that bark furiously ; on a little table by she be her ladyship 's snuff - box and her sugar - plum box . she wear a dress of black velvet , and a petticoat of flame - color brocade . she have as many ring on her finger as the old woman of Banbury Cross ; and pretty small foot which she be fond of show , with great gold clock to her stocking , and white pantofle with red heel ; and an odor of musk be shake out of her garment whenever she move or quit the room , lean on her tortoise - shell stick , little fury barking at her heel . 
 Mrs. Tusher , the parson 's wife , be with my lady . she have be wait - woman to her ladyship in the late lord 's time , and , have her soul in that business , take naturally to it when the Viscountess of Castlewood return to inhabit her father 's house . 
 " I present to your ladyship your kinsman and little page of honor , Master Henry Esmond , " Mr. Holt say , bow lowly , with a sort of comical humility . " make a pretty bow to my lady , Monsieur ; and then another little bow , not so low , to Madame Tusher the fair priestess of Castlewood . " 
 " where I have live and hope to die , sir , " say Madame Tusher , give a hard glance at the brat , and then at my lady . 
 upon she the boy 's whole attention be for a time direct . he could not keep his great eye off from she . since the Empress of Ealing , he have see nothing so awful . 
 " do my appearance please you , little page ? " ask the lady . 
 " he would be very hard to please if it do not , " cry Madame Tusher . 
 " have do , you silly Maria , " say Lady Castlewood . 
 " where I be attach , I be attach , Madame and I would die rather than not say so . " 
 " Je meur ou je m'attache , " Mr. Holt say with a polite grin . " the ivy say so in the picture , and cling to the oak like a fond parasite as it be . " 
 " Parricide , sir ! " cry Mrs. Tusher . 
 " Hush , Tusher you be always bicker with Father Holt , " cry my lady . " come and kiss my hand , child ; " and the oak hold out a BRANCH to little Harry Esmond , who take and dutifully kiss the lean old hand , upon the gnarl knuckle of which there glitter a hundred ring . 
 " to kiss that hand would make many a pretty fellow happy ! " cry Mrs. Tusher : on which my lady cry out , " go , you foolish Tusher ! " and tap she with her great fan , Tusher run forward to seize her hand and kiss it . Fury arise and bark furiously at Tusher ; and Father Holt look on at this queer scene , with arch , grave glance . 
 the awe exhibit by the little boy perhaps please the lady to whom this artless flattery be bestow : for have go down on his knee   as Father Holt have direct he , and the mode then be   and perform his obeisance , she say , " Page Esmond , my groom of the chamber will inform you what your duty be , when you wait upon my lord and I ; and good Father Holt will instruct you as become a gentleman of our name . you will pay he obedience in everything , and I pray you may grow to be as learn and as good as your tutor . " 
 the lady seem to have the great reverence for Mr. Holt , and to be more afraid of he than of anything else in the world . if she be ever so angry , a word or look from Father Holt make her calm : indeed he have a vast power of subject those who come near he ; and , among the rest , his new pupil give himself up with an entire confidence and attachment to the good Father , and become his willing slave almost from the first moment he see he . 
 he put his small hand into the Father 's as he walk away from his first presentation to his mistress , and ask many question in his artless childish way . " who be that other woman ? " he ask . " she be fat and round ; she be more pretty than my Lady Castlewood . " 
 " she be Madame Tusher , the parson 's wife of Castlewood . she have a son of your age , but big than you . " 
 " why do she like so to kiss my lady 's hand . it be not good to kiss . " 
 " taste be different , little man . Madame Tusher be attach to my lady , have be her waiting - woman before she be marry , in the old lord 's time . she marry Doctor Tusher the chaplain . the english household divine often marry the waiting - woman . " 
 " you will not marry the french woman , will you ? I see she laugh with Blaise in the buttery . " 
 " I belong to a church that be old and well than the english church , " Mr. Holt say   make a sign whereof Esmond do not then understand the meaning , across his breast and forehead ; " in our church the clergy do not marry . you will understand these thing well soon . " 
 " be not Saint Peter the head of your church ? Dr. Rabbits of Ealing tell we so . " 
 the Father say , " yes , he be . " 
 " but Saint Peter be marry , for we hear only last Sunday that his wife 's mother lie sick of a fever . " on which the Father again laugh , and say he would understand this too well soon , and talk of other thing , and take away Harry Esmond , and show he the great old house which he have come to inhabit . 
 it stand on a rise green hill , with wood behind it , in which be rook ' nest , where the bird at morning and return home at evening make a great cawing . at the foot of the hill be a river , with a steep ancient bridge cross it ; and beyond that a large pleasant green flat , where the village of Castlewood stand , and stand , with the church in the midst , the parsonage hard by it , the inn with the blacksmith 's forge beside it , and the sign of the " Three Castles " on the elm . the London road stretch away towards the rise sun , and to the west be swell hill and peak , behind which many a time Harry Esmond see the same sun setting , that he now look on thousand of mile away across the great ocean in a new Castlewood , by another stream , that bear , like the new country of wander AEneas , the fond name of the land of his youth . 
 the Hall of Castlewood be build with two court , whereof one only , the fountain - court , be now inhabit , the other have be batter down in the cromwellian war . in the fountain - court , still in good repair , be the great hall , near to the kitchen and buttery . a dozen of living - room look to the north , and communicate with the little chapel that face eastward and the building stretch from that to the main gate , and with the hall   which look to the west   into the court now dismantle . this court have be the most magnificent of the two , until the Protector 's cannon tear down one side of it before the place be take and storm . the besieger enter at the terrace under the clock - tower , slay every man of the garrison , and at their head my lord 's brother , Francis Esmond . 
 the Restoration do not bring enough money to the Lord Castlewood to restore this ruin part of his house ; where be the morning parlor , above they the long music - gallery , and before which stretch the garden - terrace , where , however , the flower grow again which the boot of the roundhead have tread in their assault , and which be restore without much cost , and only a little care , by both lady who succeed the second viscount in the government of this mansion . round the terrace - garden be a low wall with a wicket lead to the woode height beyond , that be call Cromwell 's Battery to this day . 
 Young Harry Esmond learn the domestic part of his duty , which be easy enough , from the groom of her ladyship 's chamber : serve the Countess , as the custom commonly be in his boyhood , as page , wait at her chair , bring her scented water and the silver basin after dinner sit on her carriage - step on state occasion , or on public day introduce her company to she . this be chiefly of the catholic gentry , of whom there be a pretty many in the country and neighboring city ; and who ride not seldom to Castlewood to partake of the hospitality there . in the second year of their residence , the company seem especially to increase . my lord and my lady be seldom without visitor , in whose society it be curious to contrast the difference of behavior between Father Holt , the director of the family , and Doctor Tusher , the rector of the parish Mr. Holt move amongst the very high as quite their equal , and as command they all ; while poor Doctor Tusher , whose position be indeed a difficult one , have be chaplain once to the Hall , and still to the protestant servant there , seem more like an usher than an equal , and always rise to go away after the first course . 
 also there come in these time to Father Holt many private visitor , whom , after a little , Henry Esmond have little difficulty in recognize as ecclesiastic of the Father 's persuasion , whatever their dress   and they adopt all   might be . these be closet with the Father constantly , and often come and ride away without pay their devoir to my lord and lady to the lady and lord rather his lordship be little more than a cipher in the house , and entirely under his domineering partner . a little fowling , a little hunting , a great deal of sleep , and a long dine at card and table , carry through one day after another with his lordship . when meeting take place in this second year , which often would happen with closed door , the page find my lord 's sheet of paper scribble over with dog and horse , and ' twa say he have much ado to keep himself awake at these council : the Countess rule over they , and he act as little more than her secretary . 
 Father Holt begin speedily to be so much occupy with these meeting as rather to neglect the education of the little lad who so gladly put himself under the kind priest 's order . at first they read much and regularly , both in Latin and French ; the Father not neglect in anything to impress his faith upon his pupil , but not force he violently , and treat he with a delicacy and kindness which surprise and attach the child , always more easily win by these method than by any severe exercise of authority . and his delight in their walk be to tell Harry of the glory of his order , of its martyr and hero , of its Brethren convert the heathen by myriad , traverse the desert , face the stake , rule the court and council , or brave the torture of king ; so that Harry Esmond think that to belong to the Jesuits be the great prize of life and brave end of ambition ; the great career here , and in heaven the sure reward ; and begin to long for the day , not only when he should enter into the one church and receive his first communion , but when he might join that wonderful brotherhood , which be present throughout all the world , and which number the wise , the brave , the highest bear , the most eloquent of man among its member . Father Holt bid he keep his view secret , and to hide they as a great treasure which would escape he if it be reveal ; and , proud of this confidence and secret vest in he , the lad become fondly attach to the master who initiate he into a mystery so wonderful and awful . and when little Tom Tusher , his neighbor , come from school for his holiday , and say how he , too , be to be breed up for an english priest , and would get what he call an exhibition from his school , and then a college scholarship and fellowship , and then a good living it task young Harry Esmond 's power of reticence not to say to his young companion , " Church ! priesthood ! fat living ! my dear Tommy , do you call yours a church and a priesthood ? what be a fat living compare to convert a hundred thousand heathen by a single sermon ? what be a scholarship at Trinity by the side of a crown of martyrdom , with angel await you as your head be take off ? could your master at school sail over the Thames on his gown ? have you statue in your church that can bleed , speak , walk , and cry ? my good Tommy , in dear Father Holt 's church these thing take place every day . you know Saint Philip of the Willows appear to Lord Castlewood , and cause he to turn to the one true church . no saint ever come to you . " and Harry Esmond , because of his promise to Father Holt , hide away these treasure of faith from T. Tusher , deliver himself of they nevertheless simply to Father Holt ; who stroke his head , smile at he with his inscrutable look , and tell he that he do well to meditate on these great thing , and not to talk of they except under direction . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 I be place under a popish priest and breed to that RELIGION . VISCOUNTESS castlewood . 
 have time enough be give , and his childish inclination be properly nurture , Harry Esmond have be a Jesuit priest ere he be a dozen year old , and might have finish his day a martyr in China or a victim on Tower Hill : for , in the few month they spend together at Castlewood , Mr. Holt obtain an entire mastery over the boy 's intellect and affection ; and have bring he to think , as indeed Father Holt think with all his heart too , that no life be so noble , no death so desirable , as that which many brother of his famous order be ready to undergo . by love , by a brightness of wit and good - humor that charm all , by an authority which he know how to assume , by a mystery and silence about he which increase the child 's reverence for he , he win Harry 's absolute fealty , and would have keep it , doubtless , if scheme great and more important than a poor little boy 's admission into order have not call he away . 
 after be at home for a few month in tranquillity   if theirs might be call tranquillity , which be , in truth , a constant bickering , my lord and lady leave the country for London , take their director with they : and his little pupil scarce ever shed more bitter tear in his life than he do for night after the first parting with his dear friend , as he lie in the lonely chamber next to that which the Father use to occupy . he and a few domestic be leave as the only tenant of the great house : and , though Harry sedulously do all the task which the Father set he , he have many hour unoccupied , and read in the library , and bewilder his little brain with the great book he find there . 
 after a while , the little lad grow accustomed to the loneliness of the place ; and in after day remember this part of his life as a period not unhappy . when the family be at London the whole of the establishment travel thither with the exception of the porter who be , moreover , brewer , gardener , and woodman and his wife and child . these have their lodging in the gate - house hard by , with a door into the court ; and a window look out on the green be the Chaplain 's room ; and next to this a small chamber where Father Holt have his book , and Harry Esmond his sleep closet . the side of the house face the east have escape the gun of the Cromwellians , whose battery be on the height face the western court ; so that this eastern end bear few mark of demolition , save in the chapel , where the paint window survive Edward the Sixth have be break by the Commonwealthmen . in Father Holt 's time little Harry Esmond act as his familiar and faithful little servitor ; beat his clothe , fold his vestment , fetch his water from the well long before daylight , ready to run anywhere for the service of his beloved priest . when the Father be away , he lock his private chamber ; but the room where the book be be leave to little Harry , who , but for the society of this gentleman , be little less solitary when Lord Castlewood be at home . 
 the french wit saith that a hero be none to his valet - de - chambre , and it require less quick eye than my lady 's little page be naturally endow with , to see that she have many quality by no means heroic , however much Mrs. Tusher might flatter and coax she . when Father Holt be not by , who exercise an entire authority over the pair , my lord and my lady quarrel and abuse each other so as to make the servant laugh , and to frighten the little page on duty . the poor boy tremble before his mistress , who call he by a hundred ugly name , who make nothing of box his ear , and tilt the silver basin in his face which it be his business to present to she after dinner . she hath repair , by subsequent kindness to he , these severity , which it must be own make his childhood very unhappy . she be but unhappy herself at this time , poor soul ! and I suppose make her dependant lead her own sad life . I think my lord be as much afraid of she as her page be , and the only person of the household who master she be Mr. Holt . Harry be only too glad when the Father din at table , and to slink away and prattle with he afterwards , or read with he , or walk with he . luckily my Lady Viscountess do not rise till noon . Heaven help the poor waiting - woman who have charge of her toilet ! I have often see the poor wretch come out with red eye from the closet where those long and mysterious rite of her ladyship 's dress be perform , and the backgammon - box lock up with a rap on Mrs. Tusher 's finger when she play ill , or the game be go the wrong way . 
 bless be the king who introduce card , and the kind inventor of piquet and cribbage , for they employ six hour at least of her ladyship 's day , during which her family be pretty easy . without this occupation my lady frequently declare she should die . her dependant one after another relieved guard ' twa rather a dangerous post to play with her ladyship and take the card turn about . Mr. Holt would sit with she at piquet during hour together , at which time she behave herself properly ; and as for Dr. Tusher , I believe he would have leave a parishioner 's die bed , if summon to play a rubber with his patroness at Castlewood . sometimes , when they be pretty comfortable together , my lord take a hand . besides these my lady have her faithful poor Tusher , and one , two , three gentlewoman whom Harry Esmond could recollect in his time . they could not bear that genteel service very long ; one after another try and fail at it . these and the housekeeper , and little Harry Esmond , have a table of their own . poor lady their life be far hard than the page 's . he be sound asleep , tuck up in his little bed , whilst they be sit by her ladyship read she to sleep , with the " News Letter " or the " Grand Cyrus . " my lady use to have box of new play from London , and Harry be forbid , under the pain of a whipping , to look into they . I be afraid he deserve the penalty pretty often , and get it sometimes . Father Holt apply it twice or thrice , when he catch the young scapegrace with a delightful wicked comedy of Mr. Shadwell 's or Mr. Wycherley 's under his pillow . 
 these , when he take any , be my lord 's favorite reading . but he be averse to much study , and , as his little page fancy , to much occupation of any sort . 
 it always seem to young Harry Esmond that my lord treat he with more kindness when his lady be not present , and Lord Castlewood would take the lad sometimes on his little journey a - hunting or a - birding ; he love to play at card and tric - trac with he , which game the boy learn to pleasure his lord : and be grow to like he well daily , show a special pleasure if Father Holt give a good report of he , pat he on the head , and promise that he would provide for the boy . however , in my lady 's presence , my lord show no such mark of kindness , and affect to treat the lad roughly , and rebuke he sharply for little fault , for which he in a manner ask pardon of young Esmond when they be private , say if he do not speak roughly , she would , and his tongue be not such a bad one as his lady 's a point whereof the boy , young as he be , be very well assure . 
 great public event be happen all this while , of which the simple young page take little count . but one day , rid into the neighboring town on the step of my lady 's coach , his lordship and she and Father Holt be inside , a great mob of people come hoot and jeer round the coach , bawl out " the Bishops for ever ! " " down with the Pope ! " " No Popery ! no Popery ! Jezebel , Jezebel ! " so that my lord begin to laugh , my lady 's eye to roll with anger , for she be as bold as a lioness , and fear nobody ; whilst Mr. Holt , as Esmond see from his place on the step , sink back with rather an alarmed face , cry out to her ladyship , " for God 's sake , madam , do not speak or look out of window ; sit still . " but she do not obey this prudent injunction of the Father ; she thrust her head out of the coach window , and scream out to the coachman , " flog your way through they , the brute , James , and use your whip ! " 
 the mob answer with a roar jeer of laughter , and fresh cry of " Jezebel ! Jezebel ! " my lord only laugh the more : he be a languid gentleman : nothing seem to excite he commonly , though I have see he cheer and halloo the hound very briskly , and his face   which be generally very yellow and calm   grow quite red and cheerful during a burst over the Downs after a hare , and laugh , and swear , and huzzah at a cockfight , of which sport he be very fond . and now , when the mob begin to hoot his lady , he laugh with something of a mischievous look , as though he expect sport , and think that she and they be a match . 
 James the coachman be more afraid of his mistress than the mob , probably , for he whip on his horse as he be bid , and the post - boy that ride with the first pair   my lady always ride with her coach - and - six ,   give a cut of his thong over the shoulder of one fellow who put his hand out towards the lead horse 's rein . 
 it be a market - day , and the country - people be all assemble with their basket of poultry , egg , and such thing ; the postilion have no soon lash the man who would have take hold of his horse , but a great cabbage come whirl like a bombshell into the carriage , at which my lord laugh more , for it knock my lady 's fan out of her hand , and plump into Father Holt 's stomach . then come a shower of carrot and potato . 
 " for Heaven 's sake be still ! " say Mr. Holt ; " we be not ten pace from the ' Bell ' archway , where they can shut the gate on we , and keep out this canaille . " 
 the little page be outside the coach on the step , and a fellow in the crowd aim a potato at he , and hit he in the eye , at which the poor little wretch set up a shout ; the man laugh , a great big saddler 's apprentice of the town . " ah ! you d - little yell popish bastard , " he say , and stoop to pick up another ; the crowd have gather quite between the horse and the inn door by this time , and the coach be bring to a dead stand - still . my lord jump as briskly as a boy out of the door on his side of the coach , squeeze little Harry behind it ; have hold of the potato - thrower 's collar in an instant , and the next moment the brute 's heel be in the air , and he fall on the stone with a thump . 
 " you hulk coward ! " say he ; " you pack of scream blackguard ! how dare you attack child , and insult woman ? fle another shot at that carriage , you sneak pigskin cobbler , and by the Lord I will send my rapi through you ! " 
 some of the mob cry , " Huzzah , my lord ! " for they know he , and the saddler 's man be a know bruiser , near twice as big as my lord Viscount . 
 " make way there , " say he   he speak in a high shrill voice , but with a great air of authority . " make way , and let her ladyship 's carriage pass . " the man that be between the coach and the gate of the " Bell " actually do make way , and the horse go in , my lord walk after they with his hat on his head . 
 as he be go in at the gate , through which the coach have just roll , another cry begin , of " No Popery no papist ! " my lord turn round and face they once more . 
 " God save the king ! " say he at the high pitch of his voice . " who dare abuse the King 's religion ? you , you d d psalm - sing cobbler , as sure as I be a magistrate of this county I will commit you ! " the fellow shrink back , and my lord retreat with all the honor of the day . but when the little flurry cause by the scene be over , and the flush pass off his face , he relapse into his usual languor , trifle with his little dog , and yawn when my lady speak to he . 
 this mob be one of many thousand that be go about the country at that time , huzzahe for the acquittal of the seven bishop who have be try just then , and about whom little Harry Esmond at that time know scarce anything . it be assize at Hexton , and there be a great meeting of the gentry at the " Bell ; " and my lord 's people have their new livery on , and Harry a little suit of blue and silver , which he wear upon occasion of state ; and the gentlefolk come round and talk to my lord : and a judge in a red gown , who seem a very great personage , especially compliment he and my lady , who be mighty grand . Harry remember her train bear up by her gentlewoman . there be an assembly and ball at the great room at the " Bell , " and other young gentleman of the county family look on as he do . one of they jeer he for his black eye , which be swell by the potato , and another call he a bastard , on which he and Harry fall to fisticuff . my lord 's cousin , Colonel Esmond of Walcote , be there , and separate the two lad a great tall gentleman , with a handsome good - natured face . the boy do not know how nearly in after - life he should be ally to Colonel Esmond , and how much kindness he should have to owe he . 
 there be little love between the two family . my lady use not to spare Colonel Esmond in talk of he , for reason which have be hint already ; but about which , at his tender age , Henry Esmond could be expect to know nothing . 
 very soon afterwards , my lord and lady go to London with Mr. Holt , leave , however , the page behind they . the little man have the great house of Castlewood to himself ; or between he and the housekeeper , Mrs. Worksop , an old lady who be a kinswoman of the family in some distant way , and a protestant , but a staunch Tory and king' - man , as all the Esmonds be . he use to go to school to Dr. Tusher when he be at home , though the doctor be much occupy too . there be a great stir and commotion everywhere , even in the little quiet village of Castlewood , whither a party of people come from the town , who would have break Castlewood Chapel window , but the village people turn out , and even old Sieveright , the republican blacksmith , along with they : for my lady , though she be a Papist , and have many odd way , be kind to the tenantry , and there be always a plenty of beef , and blanket , and medicine for the poor at Castlewood Hall . 
 a kingdom be change hand whilst my lord and lady be away . King James be fly , the Dutchmen be come ; awful story about they and the Prince of Orange use old Mrs. Worksop to tell to the idle little page . 
 he like the solitude of the great house very well ; he have all the play - book to read , and no Father Holt to whip he , and a hundred childish pursuit and pastime , without door and within , which make this time very pleasant . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 my superiors be engage in plots for the restoration of KING JAMES II . 

 not have be able to sleep , for thinking of some line for eel which he have place the night before , the lad be lie in his little bed , wait for the hour when the gate would be open , and he and his comrade , John Lockwood , the porter 's son , might go to the pond and see what fortune have bring they . at daybreak John be to awaken he , but his own eagerness for the sport have serve as a reveillez long since so long , that it seem to he as if the day never would come . 
 it might have be four o'clock when he hear the door of the opposite chamber , the Chaplain 's room , open , and the voice of a man cough in the passage . Harry jump up , think for certain it be a robber , or hop perhaps for a ghost , and , fling open his own door , see before he the Chaplain 's door open , and a light inside , and a figure stand in the doorway , in the midst of a great smoke which issue from the room . 
 " who be there ? " cry out the boy , who be of a good spirit . 
 " silentium ! " whisper the other ; " ' tis I , my boy ! " and , hold his hand out , Harry have no difficulty in recognize his master and friend , Father Holt . a curtain be over the window of the Chaplain 's room that look to the court , and Harry see that the smoke come from a great flame of paper which be burn in a brazier when he enter the Chaplain 's room . after give a hasty greeting and blessing to the lad , who be charm to see his tutor , the Father continue the burning of his paper , draw they from a cupboard over the mantel - piece wall , which Harry have never see before . 
 Father Holt laugh , see the lad 's attention fix at once on this hole . " that be right , Harry , " he say ; " faithful little famulus , see all and say nothing . you be faithful , I know . " 
 " I know I would go to the stake for you , " say Harry . 
 " I do not   want your head , " say the Father , pat it kindly ; " all you have to do be to hold your tongue . let we burn these paper , and say nothing to anybody . should you like to read they ? " 
 Harry Esmond blush , and hold down his head ; he have look as the fact be , and without think , at the paper before he ; and though he have see it , could not understand a word of it , the letter be quite clear enough , but quite without meaning . they burn the paper , beat down the ash in a brazier , so that scarce any trace of they remain . 
 Harry have be accustom to see Father Holt in more dress than one ; it not be safe , or worth the danger , for popish ecclesiastic to wear their proper dress ; and he be , in consequence , in no wise astonish that the priest should now appear before he in a riding - dress , with large buff leather boot , and a feather to his hat , plain , but such as gentleman wear . 
 " you know the secret of the cupboard , " say he , laugh , " and must be prepare for other mystery ; " and he open but not a secret cupboard this time only a wardrobe , which he usually keep lock , and from which he now take out two or three dress and perruque of different color , and a couple of sword of a pretty make   Father Holt be an expert practitioner with the small - sword , and every day , whilst he be at home , he and his pupil practise this exercise , in which the lad become a very great proficient , a military coat and cloak , and a farmer 's smock , and place they in the large hole over the mantel - piece from which the paper have be take . 
 " if they miss the cupboard , " he say , " they will not find these ; if they find they , they will tell no tale , except that Father Holt wear more suit of clothe than one . all Jesuits do . you know what deceiver we be , Harry . " 
 Harry be alarm at the notion that his friend be about to leave he ; but " no , " the priest say , " I may very likely come back with my lord in a few day . we be to be tolerate ; we be not to be persecute . but they may take a fancy to pay a visit at Castlewood ere our return ; and , as gentleman of my cloth be suspect , they might choose to examine my paper , which concern nobody at least not they . " and to this day , whether the paper in cipher relate to politic , or to the affair of that mysterious society whereof Father Holt be a member , his pupil , Harry Esmond , remain in entire ignorance . 
 the rest of his good , his small wardrobe ,   c. Holt leave untouched on his shelf and in his cupboard , take down with a laugh , however and flinging into the brazier , where he only half burn they , some theological treatise which he have be write against the English divine . " and now , " say he , " Henry , my son , you may testify , with a safe conscience , that you see I burn Latin sermon the last time I be here before I go away to London ; and it will be daybreak directly , and I must be away before Lockwood be stir . "
