 " will not Lockwood let you out , sir ? " Esmond ask . Holt laugh ; he be never more gay or good - humor than when in the midst of action or danger . 
 " Lockwood know nothing of my be here , mind you , " he say ; " nor would you , you little wretch ! have you sleep well . you must forget that I have be here ; and now farewell . close the door , and go to your own room , and do not   come out till stay , why should you not know one secret more ? I know you will never betray I . " 
 in the Chaplain 's room be two window ; the one look into the court face westward to the fountain ; the other , a small casement strongly bar , and look on to the green in front of the Hall . this window be too high to reach from the ground ; but , mount on a buffet which stand beneath it , Father Holt show I how , by press on the base of the window , the whole framework of lead , glass , and iron stanchion descend into a cavity work below , from which it could be draw and restore to its usual place from without ; a broken pane be purposely open to admit the hand which be to work upon the spring of the machine . 
 " when I be go , " Father Holt say , " you may push away the buffet , so that no one may fancy that an exit have be make that way ; lock the door ; place the key where shall we put the key ? under ' Chrysostom ' on the book - shelf ; and if any ask for it , say I keep it there , and tell you where to find it , if you have need to go to my room . the descent be easy down the wall into the ditch ; and so , once more farewell , until I see thee again , my dear son . " and with this the intrepid Father mount the buffet with great agility and briskness , step across the window , lift up the bar and framework again from the other side , and only leave room for Harry Esmond to stand on tiptoe and kiss his hand before the casement close , the bar fix as firmly as ever , seemingly , in the stone arch overhead . when Father Holt next arrive at Castlewood , it be by the public gate on horseback ; and he never so much as allude to the existence of the private issue to Harry , except when he have need of a private messenger from within , for which end , no doubt , he have instruct his young pupil in the mean of quit the Hall . 
 Esmond , young as he be , would have die soon than betray his friend and master , as Mr. Holt well know ; for he have try the boy more than once , put temptation in his way , to see whether he would yield to they and confess afterwards , or whether he would resist they , as he do sometimes , or whether he would lie , which he never do . Holt instruct the boy on this point , however , that if to keep silence be not to lie , as it certainly be not , yet silence be , after all , equivalent to a negation and therefore a downright no , in the interest of justice or your friend , and in reply to a question that may be prejudicial to either , be not criminal , but , on the contrary , praiseworthy ; and as lawful a way as the other of elude a wrongful demand . for instance   say he , suppose a good citizen , who have see his Majesty take refuge there , have be ask , " be King Charles up that oak - tree ? " his duty would have be not to say , yes so that the Cromwellians should seize the king and murder he like his father but no ; his Majesty be private in the tree , and therefore not to be see there by loyal eye : all which instruction , in religion and moral , as well as in the rudiment of the tongue and science , the boy take eagerly and with gratitude from his tutor . when , then , Holt be go , and tell Harry not to see he , it be as if he have never be . and he have this answer pat when he come to be question a few day after . 
 the Prince of Orange be then at Salisbury , as young Esmond learn from see Doctor Tusher in his good cassock   though the road be muddy , and he never be know to wear his silk , only his stuff one , a - horseback , with a great orange cockade in his broad - leaf hat , and Nahum , his clerk , ornament with a like decoration . the doctor be walk up and down in front of his parsonage , when little Esmond see he , and hear he say he be go to pay his duty to his Highness the Prince , as he mount his pad and ride away with Nahum behind . the village people have orange cockade too , and his friend the blacksmith 's laugh daughter pin one into Harry 's old hat , which he tear out indignantly when they bid he to cry " God save the Prince of Orange and the protestant religion ! " but the people only laugh , for they like the boy in the village , where his solitary condition move the general pity , and where he find friendly welcome and face in many house . Father Holt have many friend there too , for he not only would fight the blacksmith at theology , never lose his temper , but laugh the whole time in his pleasant way ; but he cure he of an ague with quinquina , and be always ready with a kind word for any man that ask it , so that they say in the village ' twas a pity the two be Papists . 
 the Director and the Vicar of Castlewood agree very well ; indeed , the former be a perfectly - breed gentleman , and it be the latter 's business to agree with everybody . Doctor Tusher and the lady's - maid , his spouse , have a boy who be about the age of little Esmond ; and there be such a friendship between the lad , as propinquity and tolerable kindness and good - humor on either side would be pretty sure to occasion . Tom Tusher be send off early , however , to a school in London , whither his father take he and a volume of sermon , in the first year of the reign of King James ; and Tom return but once , a year afterwards , to Castlewood for many year of his scholastic and collegiate life . thus there be less danger to Tom of a perversion of his faith by the Director , who scarce ever see he , than there be to Harry , who constantly be in the Vicar 's company ; but as long as Harry 's religion be his Majesty 's , and my lord 's , and my lady 's , the Doctor say gravely , it should not be for he to disturb or disquiet he : it be far from he to say that his Majesty 's Church be not a branch of the Catholic Church ; upon which Father Holt use , accord to his custom , to laugh , and say that the Holy Church throughout all the world , and the noble Army of Martyrs , be very much oblige to the Doctor . 
 it be while Dr. Tusher be away at Salisbury that there come a troop of dragoon with orange scarfs , and quarter in Castlewood , and some of they come up to the Hall , where they take possession , rob nothing however beyond the hen - house and the beer - cellar : and only insist upon go through the house and look for paper . the first room they ask to look at be Father Holt 's room , of which Harry Esmond bring the key , and they open the drawer and the cupboard , and toss over the paper and clothe but find nothing except his book and clothe , and the vestment in a box by themselves , with which the dragoon make merry , to Harry Esmond 's horror . and to the question which the gentleman put to Harry , he reply that Father Holt be a very kind man to he , and a very learn man , and Harry suppose would tell he none of his secret if he have any . he be about eleven year old at this time , and look as innocent as boy of his age . 
 the family be away more than six month , and when they return they be in the deep state of dejection , for King James have be banish , the Prince of Orange be on the throne , and the dire persecution of those of the catholic faith be apprehend by my lady , who say she do not believe that there be a word of truth in the promise of toleration that dutch monster make , or in a single word the perjure wretch say . my lord and lady be in a manner prisoner in their own house ; so her ladyship give the little page to know , who be by this time grow of an age to understand what be pass about he , and something of the character of the people he live with . 
 " we be prisoner , " say she ; " in everything but chain , we be prisoner . let they come , let they consign I to dungeon , or strike off my head from this poor little throat "   and she clasp it in her long finger . " the blood of the Esmonds will always flow freely for their king . we be not like the Churchills the Judases , who kiss their master and betray he . we know how to suffer , how even to forgive in the royal cause "   no doubt it be to that fatal business of lose the place of Groom of the Posset to which her ladyship allude , as she do half a dozen time in the day . " let the tyrant of Orange bring his rack and his odious dutch torture the beast ! the wretch ! I spit upon he and defy he . cheerfully will I lay this head upon the block ; cheerfully will I accompany my lord to the scaffold : we will cry ' God save King James ! ' with our die breath , and smile in the face of the executioner . " and she tell her page , a hundred time at least , of the particular of the last interview which she have with his Majesty . 
 " I fling myself before my liege 's foot , " she say , " at Salisbury . I devote myself my husband my house , to his cause . perhaps he remember old time , when Isabella Esmond be young and fair ; perhaps he recall the day when ' twa not I that knelt at least he speak to I with a voice that remind I of day go by . ' egad ! ' say his Majesty , ' you should go to the Prince of Orange ; if you want anything . ' ' no , sire , ' I reply , ' I would not kneel to a Usurper ; the Esmond that would have serve your Majesty will never be groom to a traitor 's posset . ' the royal exile smile , even in the midst of his misfortune ; he deign to raise I with word of consolation . the Viscount , my husband , himself , could not be angry at the august salute with which he honor I ! " 
 the public misfortune have the effect of make my lord and his lady well friend than they ever have be since their courtship . my lord Viscount have show both loyalty and spirit , when these be rare quality in the dispirit party about the king ; and the praise he get elevate he not a little in his wife 's good opinion , and perhaps in his own . he waken up from the listless and supine life which he have be lead ; be always rid to and fro in consultation with this friend or that of the king 's ; the page of course know little of his doing , but remark only his great cheerfulness and alter demeanor . 
 Father Holt come to the Hall constantly , but officiate no long openly as chaplain ; he be always fetch and carry : stranger , military and ecclesiastic   Harry know the latter , though they come in all sort of disguise , be continually arrive and depart . my lord make long absence and sudden reappearance , use sometimes the mean of exit which Father Holt have employ , though how often the little window in the Chaplain 's room let in or let out my lord and his friend , Harry could not tell . he stoutly keep his promise to the Father of not pry , and if at midnight from his little room he hear noise of person stir in the next chamber , he turn round to the wall , and hide his curiosity under his pillow until it fall asleep . of course he could not help remark that the priest 's journey be constant , and understanding by a hundred sign that some active though secret business employ he : what this be may pretty well be guess by what soon happen to my lord . 
 no garrison or watch be put into Castlewood when my lord come back , but a Guard be in the village ; and one or other of they be always on the Green keep a look - out on our great gate , and those who go out and in . Lockwood say that at night especially every person who come in or go out be watch by the outlying sentry . ' twa lucky that we have a gate which their Worships know nothing about . my lord and Father Holt must have make constant journey at night : once or twice little Harry act as their messenger and discreet little aide - de - camp . he remember he be bid to go into the village with his fishing - rod , enter certain house , ask for a drink of water , and tell the good man , " there would be a horse - market at Newbury next Thursday , " and so carry the same message on to the next house on his list . 
 he do not know what the message mean at the time , nor what be happen : which may as well , however , for clearness ' sake , be explain here . the Prince of Orange be go to Ireland , where the King be ready to meet he with a great army , it be determined that a great rise of his Majesty 's party should take place in this country ; and my lord be to head the force in our county . of late he have take a great lead in affair than before , have the indefatigable Mr. Holt at his elbow , and my Lady Viscountess strongly urge he on ; and my Lord Sark be in the Tower a prisoner , and Sir Wilmot Crawley , of Queen 's Crawley , have go over to the Prince of Orange 's side my lord become the most considerable person in our part of the county for the affair of the King . 
 it be arrange that the regiment of Scots Grays and Dragoons , then quarter at Newbury , should declare for the King on a certain day , when likewise the gentry affect to his Majesty 's cause be to come in with their tenant and adherent to Newbury , march upon the dutch troop at read under Ginckel ; and , these overthrow , and their indomitable little master away in Ireland , ' twa think that our side might move on London itself , and a confident victory be predict for the King . 
 as these great matter be in agitation , my lord lose his listless manner and seem to gain health ; my lady do not scold he , Mr. Holt come to and fro , busy always ; and little Harry long to have be a few inch tall , that he might draw a sword in this good cause . 
 one day , it must have be about the month of July , 1690 , my lord , in a great horseman 's coat , under which Harry could see the shining of a steel breastplate he have on , call little Harry to he , put the hair off the child 's forehead , and kiss he , and bade God bless he in such an affectionate way as he never have use before . Father Holt bless he too , and then they take leave of my Lady Viscountess , who come from her apartment with a pocket - handkerchief to her eye , and her gentlewoman and Mrs. Tusher support she . " you be go to to ride , " say she . " oh , that I might come too but in my situation I be forbid horse exercise . " 
 " we kiss my Lady Marchioness 's hand , " say Mr. Holt . 
 " my lord , God speed you ! " she say , step up and embrace my lord in a grand manner . " Mr. Holt , I ask your blessing : " and she kneel down for that , whilst Mrs. Tusher toss her head up . 
 Mr. Holt give the same benediction to the little page , who go down and hold my lord 's stirrup for he to mount ; there be two servant wait there too and they ride out of Castlewood gate . 
 as they cross the bridge , Harry could see an officer in scarlet ride up touch his hat , and address my lord . 
 the party stop , and come to some parley or discussion , which presently end , my lord put his horse into a canter after take off his hat and make a bow to the officer , who ride alongside he step for step : the trooper accompany he fall back , and rid with my lord 's two man . they canter over the Green , and behind the elm   my lord wave his hand , Harry think , and so they disappear . that evening we have a great panic , the cow - boy come at milking - time rid one of our horse , which he have find graze at the outer park - wall . 
 all night my Lady Viscountess be in a very quiet and subdued mood . she scarce find fault with anybody ; she play at card for six hour ; little page Esmond go to sleep . he pray for my lord and the good cause before close his eye . 
 it be quite in the gray of the morning when the porter 's bell ring , and old Lockwood , wake up , let in one of my lord 's servant , who have go with he in the morning , and who return with a melancholy story . the officer who ride up to my lord have , it appear , say to he , that it be his duty to inform his lordship that he be not under arrest , but under surveillance , and to request he not to ride abroad that day . 
 my lord reply that riding be good for his health , that if the Captain choose to accompany he he be welcome ; and it be then that he make a bow , and they canter away together . 
 when he come on to Wansey Down , my lord all of a sudden pull up , and the party come to a halt at the cross - way . 
 " Sir , " say he to the officer , " we be four to two ; will you be so kind as to take that road , and leave I go mine ? " 
 " your road be mine , my lord , " say the officer . 
 " then " say my lord ; but he have no time to say more , for the officer , draw a pistol , snap it at his lordship ; as at the same moment Father Holt , draw a pistol , shoot the officer through the head . it be do , and the man dead in an instant of time . the orderly , gaze at the officer , look seared for a moment , and gallop away for his life . 
 " Fire ! fire ! " cry out Father Holt , send another shot after the trooper , but the two servant be too much surprised to use their piece , and my lord call to they to hold their hand , the fellow get away . 
 " Mr. Holt , qui pensait a tout , " say Blaise , " get off his horse , examine the pocket of the dead officer for paper , give his money to we two , and say , ' the wine be draw , M. le Marquis , ' why do he say Marquis to M. le Vicomte ? ' we must drink it . ' 
 " the poor gentleman 's horse be a well one than that I ride , " Blaise continue ; " Mr. Holt bid I get on he , and so I give a cut to Whitefoot , and she trot home . we ride on towards Newbury ; we hear fire towards midday : at two o'clock a horseman come up to we as we be give our cattle water at an inn and say , ' all be do ! the Ecossais declare an hour too soon General Ginckel be down upon they . ' the whole thing be at an end . 
 " ' and we have   shoot an officer on duty , and let his orderly escape , ' say my lord . 
 " ' Blaise , ' say Mr. Holt , write two line on his table - book , one for my lady and one for you , Master Harry ; ' you must go back to Castlewood , and deliver these , ' and behold I . " 
 and he give Harry the two paper . he read that to himself , which only say , " burn the paper in the cupboard , burn this . you know nothing about anything . " Harry read this , run up stair to his mistress 's apartment , where her gentlewoman sleep near to the door , make she bring a light and wake my lady , into whose hand he give the paper . she be a wonderful object to look at in her night attire , nor have Harry ever see the like . 
 as soon as she have the paper in her hand , Harry step back to the Chaplain 's room , open the secret cupboard over the fireplace , burn all the paper in it , and , as he have see the priest do before , take down one of his reverence 's manuscript sermon , and half burn that in the brazier . by the time the paper be quite destroy it be daylight . Harry run back to his mistress again . her gentlewoman usher he again into her ladyship 's chamber ; she tell he   from behind her nuptial curtain   to bid the coach be get ready , and that she would ride away anon . 
 but the mystery of her ladyship 's toilet be as awfully long on this day as on any other , and , long after the coach be ready , my lady be still attire herself . and just as the Viscountess step forth from her room , ready for departure , young John Lockwood come run up from the village with news that a lawyer , three officer , and twenty or four - and - twenty soldier , be march thence upon the house . John have but two minute the start of they , and , ere he have well tell his story , the troop ride into our court - yard . 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 the issue of the plots . the death of THOMAS , THIRD viscount of castlewood ; and the imprisonment of his VISCOUNTESS . 
 at first my lady be for die like Mary , queen of Scots   to whom she fancy she bear a resemblance in beauty , and , stroke her scraggy neck , say , " they will find Isabel of Castlewood be equal to her fate . " her gentlewoman , Victoire , persuade she that her prudent course be , as she could not fly , to receive the troop as though she suspect nothing , and that her chamber be the good place wherein to await they . so her black Japan casket , which Harry be to carry to the coach , be take back to her ladyship 's chamber , whither the maid and mistress retire . Victoire come out presently , bid the page to say her ladyship be ill , confine to her bed with the rheumatism . 
 by this time the soldier have reach Castlewood . Harry Esmond see they from the window of the tapestry parlor ; a couple of sentinel be post at the gate a half - dozen more walk towards the stable ; and some other , precede by their commander , and a man in black , a lawyer probably , be conduct by one of the servant to the stair lead up to the part of the house which my lord and lady inhabit . 
 so the Captain , a handsome kind man , and the lawyer , come through the ante - room to the tapestry parlor , and where now be nobody but young Harry Esmond , the page . 
 " tell your mistress , little man , " say the Captain , kindly , " that we must speak to she . " 
 " my mistress be ill a - bed , " say the page . 
 " what complaint have she ? " ask the Captain . 
 the boy say , " the rheumatism ! " 
 " Rheumatism ! that be a sad complaint , " continue the good - natured Captain ; " and the coach be in the yard to fetch the doctor , I suppose ? " 
 " I do not   know , " say the boy . 
 " and how long have her ladyship be ill ? " 
 " I do not   know , " say the boy . 
 " when do my lord go away ? " 
 " yesterday night . " 
 " with Father Holt ? " 
 " with Mr. Holt . " 
 " and which way do they travel ? " ask the lawyer . 
 " they travel without I , " say the page . 
 " we must see Lady Castlewood . " 
 " I have order that nobody go in to her ladyship she be sick , " say the page ; but at this moment Victoire come out . " hush ! " say she ; and , as if not know that any one be near , " what be this noise ? " say she . " be this gentleman the Doctor ? " 
 " Stuff ! we must see Lady Castlewood , " say the lawyer , push by . 
 the curtain of her ladyship 's room be down , and the chamber dark , and she be in bed with a nightcap on her head , and prop up by her pillow , look none the less ghastly because of the red which be still on her cheek , and which she could not afford to forego . 
 " be that the doctor ? " she say . 
 " there be no use with this deception , madam , " Captain Westbury say   for so he be name . " my duty be to arrest the person of Thomas , Viscount Castlewood , a nonjuring peer of Robert Tusher , Vicar of Castlewood and Henry Holt , know under various other name and designation , a Jesuit priest , who officiate as chaplain here in the late king 's time , and be now at the head of the conspiracy which be about to break out in this country against the authority of their Majesties King William and Queen Mary and my order be to search the house for such paper or trace of the conspiracy as may be find here . your ladyship will please give I your key , and it will be as well for yourself that you should help we , in every way , in our search . " 
 " you see , sir , that I have the rheumatism , and can not move , " say the lady , look uncommonly ghastly as she sit up in her bed , where , however , she have have her cheek paint , and a new cap put on , so that she might at least look she good when the officer come . 
 " I shall take leave to place a sentinel in the chamber , so that your ladyship , in case you should wish to rise , may have an arm to lean on , " Captain Westbury say . " your woman will show I where I be to look ; " and Madame Victoire , chatter in her half french and half english jargon , open while the Captain examine one drawer after another ; but , as Harry Esmond think , rather carelessly , with a smile on his face , as if he be only conduct the examination for form 's sake . 
 before one of the cupboard Victoire fling herself down , stretch out her arm , and , with a pierce shriek , cry , " Non , jamais , monsieur l'officier ! Jamais ! I will rather die than let you see this wardrobe . " 
 but Captain Westbury would open it , still with a smile on his face , which , when the box be open , turn into a fair burst of laughter . it contain not paper regard the conspiracy but my lady 's wig , wash , and rouge - pot , and Victoire say man be monster , as the Captain go on with his perquisition . he tap the back to see whether or no it be hollow , and as he thrust his hand into the cupboard , my lady from her bed call out , with a voice that do not sound like that of a very sick woman , " be it your commission to insult lady as well as to arrest gentleman , Captain ? " 
 " these article be only dangerous when wear by your ladyship , " the Captain say , with a low bow , and a mock grin of politeness . " I have find nothing which concern the Government as yet only the weapon with which beauty be authorize to kill , " say he , point to a wig with his sword - tip . " we must now proceed to search the rest of the house . " 
 " you be not go to leave that wretch in the room with I , " cry my lady , point to the soldier . 
 " what can I do , madam ? somebody you must have to smooth your pillow and bring your medicine permit I " 
 " Sir ! " scream out my lady . 
 " Madam , if you be too ill to leave the bed , " the Captain then say , rather sternly , " I must have in four of my man to lift you off in the sheet . I must examine this bed , in a word ; paper may be hide in a bed as elsewhere ; we know that very well and       . " 
 here it be her ladyship 's turn to shriek , for the Captain , with his fist shake the pillow and bolster , at last come to " burn " as they say in the play of forfeit , and wrench away one of the pillow , say , " look ! do not I tell you so ? here be a pillow stuff with paper . " 
 " some villain have betray we , " cry out my lady , sit up in the bed , show herself full dress under her night - rail . 
 " and now your ladyship can move , I be sure ; permit I to give you my hand to rise . you will have to travel for some distance , as far as Hexton Castle to - night . will you have your coach ? your woman shall attend you if you like and the japan - box ? " 
 " Sir ! you do not   strike a man when he be down , " say my lady , with some dignity : " can you not spare a woman ? " 
 " your ladyship must please to rise , and let I search the bed , " say the Captain ; " there be no more time to lose in bandy talk . " 
 and , without more ado , the gaunt old woman get up . Harry Esmond recollect to the end of his life that figure , with the brocade dress and the white night - rail , and the gold - clock red stocking , and white red - heeled shoe , sit up in the bed , and step down from it . the trunk be ready pack for departure in her ante - room , and the horse ready harness in the stable : about all which the Captain seem to know , by information get from some quarter or other ; and whence Esmond could make a pretty shrewd guess in after - time , when Dr. Tusher complain that King William 's government have basely treat he for service do in that cause . 
 and here he may relate , though he be then too young to know all that be happen , what the paper contain , of which Captain Westbury have make a seizure , and which paper have be transfer from the japan - box to the bed when the officer arrive . 
 there be a list of gentleman of the county in Father Holt 's hand write Mr. Freeman 's   King James 's   friend a similar paper be find among those of Sir John Fenwick and Mr. Coplestone , who suffer death for this conspiracy . 
 there be a patent confer the title of Marquis of Esmond on my Lord Castlewood and the heir - male of his body ; his appointment as Lord - Lieutenant of the County , and Major - General . 
    to have this rank of Marquis restore in the family have 
  always be my Lady Viscountess 's ambition ; and her old 
  maiden aunt , Barbara Topham , the goldsmith 's daughter , die 
  about this time , and leave all her property to Lady 
  Castlewood , I have hear that her ladyship send almost the 
  whole of the money to King James , a proceeding which so 
  irritate my Lord Castlewood that he actually go to the 
  parish church , and be only appease by the Marquis 's title 
  which his exile Majesty send to he in return for the 
  15,000L. his faithful subject lend he . 
 there be various letter from the nobility and gentry , some ardent and some doubtful , in the King 's service ; and   very luckily for he   two letter concern Colonel Francis Esmond : one from Father Holt , which say , " I have be to see this Colonel at his house at Walcote , near to Wells , where he reside since the king 's departure , and press he very eagerly in Mr. Freeman 's cause , show he the great advantage he would have by trade with that merchant , offer he large premium there as agree between we . but he say no : he consider Mr. Freeman the head of the firm , will never trade against he or embark with any other trading company , but consider his duty be do when Mr. Freeman leave England . this Colonel seem to care more for his wife and his beagle than for affair . he ask I much about young H. E. , ' that bastard , ' as he call he ; doubt my lord 's intention respect he . I reassure he on this head , state what I know of the lad , and our intention respect he , but with regard to Freeman he be inflexible . " 
 and another letter be from Colonel Esmond to his kinsman , to say that one Captain Holton have be with he offer he large bribe to join , you know WHO , and say that the head of the house of Castlewood be deeply engage in that quarter . but for his part he have break his sword when the K. leave the country , and would never again fight in that quarrel . the P. of O. be a man , at least , of a noble courage , and his duty , and , as he think , every Englishman 's , be to keep the country quiet , and the French out of it : and , in fine , that he would have nothing to do with the scheme . 
 of the existence of these two letter and the content of the pillow , Colonel Frank Esmond , who become Viscount Castlewood , tell Henry Esmond afterwards , when the letter be show to his lordship , who congratulate himself , as he have good reason , that he have not join in the scheme which prove so fatal to many concerned in it . but , naturally , the lad know little about these circumstance when they happen under his eye : only be aware that his patron and his mistress be in some trouble , which have cause the flight of the one and the apprehension of the other by the officer of King William . 
 the seizure of the paper effect , the gentleman do not pursue their further search through Castlewood House very rigorously . they examine Mr. Holt 's room , be lead thither by his pupil , who show , as the Father have bid he , the place where the key of his chamber lay , open the door for the gentleman , and conduct they into the room . 
 when the gentleman come to the half - burn paper in the brazier , they examine they eagerly enough , and their young guide be a little amused at their perplexity . 
 " what be these ? " say one . 
 " they be write in a foreign language , " say the lawyer . " what be you laugh at , little whelp ? " add he , turn round as he see the boy smile . 
 " Mr. Holt say they be sermon , " Harry say , " and bid I to burn they ; " which indeed be true of those paper . 
 " sermon indeed it be treason , I would lay a wager , " cry the lawyer . 
 " egad ! it be greek to I , " say Captain Westbury . " can you read it , little boy ? " 
 " yes , sir , a little , " Harry say . 
 " then read , and read in English , sir , on your peril , " say the lawyer . and Harry begin to translate : 
 " hath not one of your own writer say , ' the child of Adam be now labor as much as he himself ever do , about the tree of the knowledge of good and evil , shake the bough thereof , and seek the fruit , be for the most part unmindful of the tree of life . ' oh blind generation ! ' tis this tree of knowledge to which the serpent have lead you " and here the boy be oblige to stop , the rest of the page be char by the fire : and ask of the lawyer " shall I go on , sir ? " 
 the lawyer say " this boy be deep than he seem : who know that he be not laugh at we ? " 
 " let us have in Dick the Scholar , " cry Captain Westbury , laugh : and he call to a trooper out of the window " Ho , Dick , come in here and construe . " 
 a thick - set soldier , with a square good - humor face , come in at the summon , salute his officer . 
 " tell we what be this , Dick , " say the lawyer . 
 " my name be Steele , sir , " say the soldier . " I may be Dick for my friend , but I do not   name gentleman of your cloth amongst they . " 
 " well then , Steele . " 
 " Mr. Steele , sir , if you please . when you address a gentleman of his Majesty 's Horse Guards , be pleased not to be so familiar . " 
 " I do not   know , sir , " say the lawyer . 
 " how should you ? I take it you be not accustomed to meet with gentleman , " say the trooper . 
 " hold thy prate , and read that bit of paper , " say Westbury . 
 " ' Tis Latin , " say Dick , glance at it , and again salute his officer , " and from a sermon of Mr. Cudworth 's , " and he translate the word pretty much as Henry Esmond have render they . 
 " what a young scholar you be , " say the Captain to the boy . 
 " depend on't , he know more than he tell , " say the lawyer . " I think we will pack he off in the coach with old Jezebel . " 
 " for construe a bit of Latin ? " say the Captain , very good - naturedly . 
 " I would as lief go there as anywhere , " Harry Esmond say , simply , " for there be nobody to care for I . " 
 there must have be something touch in the child 's voice , or in this description of his solitude for the Captain look at he very good - naturedly , and the trooper , call Steele , put his hand kindly on the lad 's head , and say some word in the Latin tongue . 
 " what do he say ? " say the lawyer . 
 " Faith , ask Dick himself , " cry Captain Westbury . 
 " I say I be not ignorant of misfortune myself , and have learn to succor the miserable , and that be not your trade , Mr. Sheepskin , " say the trooper . 
 " you have well leave Dick the Scholar alone , Mr. Corbet , " the Captain say . and Harry Esmond , always touch by a kind face and kind word , feel very grateful to this good - natured champion . 
 the horse be by this time harness to the coach ; and the Countess and Victoire come down and be put into the vehicle . this woman , who quarrel with Harry Esmond all day , be melt at part with he , and call he " dear angel , " and " poor infant , " and a hundred other name . 
 the Viscountess , give he her lean hand to kiss , bid he always be faithful to the house of Esmond . " if evil should happen to my lord , " say she , " his SUCCESSOR , I trust , will be find , and give you protection . situate as I be , they will not dare wreak their vengeance on I now . " and she kiss a medal she wear with great fervor , and Henry Esmond know not in the least what her meaning be ; but hath since learn that , old as she be , she be for ever expect , by the good office of saint and relic , to have an heir to the title of Esmond . 
 Harry Esmond be too young to have be introduce into the secret of politic in which his patron be implicate ; for they put but few question to the boy   who be little of stature , and look much young than his age , and such question as they put he answer cautiously enough , and profess even more ignorance than he have , for which his examiner willingly enough give he credit . he do not say a word about the window or the cupboard over the fireplace ; and these secret quite escape the eye of the searcher . 
 so then my lady be consign to her coach , and send off to Hexton , with her woman and the man of law to bear her company , a couple of trooper rid on either side of the coach . and Harry be leave behind at the Hall , belong as it be to nobody , and quite alone in the world . the captain and a guard of man remain in possession there ; and the soldier , who be very good - natured and kind , eat my lord 's mutton and drink his wine , and make themselves comfortable , as they well might do in such pleasant quarter . 
 the captain have their dinner serve in my lord 's tapestry parlor , and poor little Harry think his duty be to wait upon Captain Westbury 's chair , as his custom have be to serve his lord when he sit there . 
 after the departure of the Countess , Dick the Scholar take Harry Esmond under his special protection , and would examine he in his humanity and talk to he both of French and Latin , in which tongue the lad find , and his new friend be willing enough to acknowledge , that he be even more proficient than Scholar Dick . hear that he have learn they from a Jesuit , in the praise of whom and whose goodness Harry be never tired of speak , Dick , rather to the boy 's surprise , who begin to have an early shrewdness , like many child breed up alone , show a great deal of theological science , and knowledge of the point at issue between the two church ; so that he and Harry would have hour of controversy together , in which the boy be certainly worst by the argument of this singular trooper . " I be no common soldier , " Dick would say , and indeed it be easy to see by his learning , breeding , and many accomplishment , that he be not . " I be of one of the most ancient family in the empire ; I have have my education at a famous school , and a famous university ; I learn my first rudiment of Latin near to Smithfield , in London , where the martyr be roast . " 
 " you hang as many of our , " interpose Harry ; " and , for the matter of persecution , Father Holt tell I that a young gentleman of Edinburgh , eighteen year of age , student at the college there , be hang for heresy only last year , though he recant , and solemnly ask pardon for his error . " 
 " Faith ! there have be too much persecution on both side : but ' twa you teach we . " 
 " Nay , ' twas the Pagans begin it , " cry the lad , and begin to instance a number of saint of the Church , from the proto - martyr downward " this one 's fire go out under he : that one 's oil cool in the caldron : at a third holy head the executioner chop three time and it would not come off . show we martyr in your church for whom such miracle have be do . " 
 " Nay , " say the trooper gravely , " the miracle of the first three century belong to my church as well as your , Master Papist , " and then add , with something of a smile upon his countenance , and a queer look at Harry " and yet , my little catechiser , I have sometimes think about those miracle , that there be not much good in they , since the victim 's head always finish by come off at the third or fourth chop , and the caldron , if it do not boil one day , boil the next . Howbeit , in our time , the Church have lose that questionable advantage of respite . there never be a shower to put out Ridley 's fire , nor an angel to turn the edge of Campion 's axe . the rack tear the limb of Southwell the Jesuit and Sympson the Protestant alike . for faith , everywhere multitude die willingly enough . I have read in Monsieur Rycaut 's ' history of the Turks , ' of thousand of Mahomet 's follower rush upon death in battle as upon certain Paradise ; and in the great Mogul 's dominion people fle themselves by hundred under the car of the idol annually , and the widow burn themselves on their husband ' body , as ' tis well know . ' tis not the dying for a faith that be so hard , Master Harry every man of every nation have do that ' tis the living up to it that be difficult , as I know to my cost , " he add with a sigh . " and ah ! " he add , " my poor lad , I be not strong enough to convince thee by my life though to die for my religion would give I the great of joy but I have a dear friend in Magdalen College in Oxford ; I wish Joe Addison be here to convince thee , as he quickly could for I think he be a match for the whole College of Jesuits ; and what be more , in his life too . in that very sermon of Dr. Cudworth 's which your priest be quote from , and which suffer martydom in the brazier , " Dick add with a smile , " I have a thought of wear the black coat   but be ashamed of my life , you see , and take to this sorry red one ; I have often think of Joe Addison Dr. Cudworth say , ' a good conscience be the good look - glass of heaven ' and there be serenity in my friend 's face which always reflect it I wish you could see he , Harry . " 
 " do he do you a great deal of good ? " ask the lad , simply . 
 " he might have do , " say the other " at least he teach I to see and approve well thing . ' tis my own fault , deteriora sequi . " 
 " you seem very good , " the boy say . 
 " I be not what I seem , alas ! " answer the trooper and indeed , as it turn out , poor Dick tell the truth for that very night , at supper in the hall , where the gentleman of the troop take their repast , and pass most part of their day dice and smoking of tobacco , and singing and cursing , over the Castlewood ale Harry Esmond find Dick the Scholar in a woful state of drunkenness . he hiccup out a sermon and his laugh companion bid he sing a hymn , on which Dick , swear he would run the scoundrel through the body who insult his religion , make for his sword , which be hang on the wall , and fall down flat on the floor under it , say to Harry , who run forward to help he , " ah , little Papist , I wish Joseph Addison be here ! " 
 though the trooper of the King 's Life - Guards be all gentleman , yet the rest of the gentleman seem ignorant and vulgar boor to Harry Esmond , with the exception of this good - natured Corporal Steele the Scholar , and Captain Westbury and Lieutenant Trant , who be always kind to the lad . they remain for some week or month encamp in Castlewood , and Harry learn from they , from time to time , how the lady at Hexton Castle be treat , and the particular of her confinement there . ' tis know that King William be disposed to deal very leniently with the gentry who remain faithful to the old King 's cause ; and no prince usurp a crown , as his enemy say he do ,   righteously take it , as I think now ,   ever cause less blood to be shed . as for woman - conspirator , he keep spy on the least dangerous , and lock up the other . Lady Castlewood have the good room in Hexton Castle , and the gaoler 's garden to walk in ; and though she repeatedly desire to be lead out to execution , like Mary Queen of Scots , there never be any thought of take she paint old head off , or any desire to do aught but keep her person in security . 
 and it appear she find that some be friend in her misfortune , whom she have , in her prosperity , consider as her bad enemy . Colonel Francis Esmond , my lord 's cousin and her ladyship 's , who have marry the Dean of Winchester 's daughter , and , since King James 's departure out of England , have live not very far away from Hexton town , hear of his kinswoman 's strait , and be friend with Colonel Brice , command for King William in Hexton , and with the Church dignitary there , come to visit her ladyship in prison , offer to his uncle 's daughter any friendly service which lie in his power . and he bring his lady and little daughter to see the prisoner , to the latter of whom , a child of great beauty and many win way , the old Viscountess take not a little liking , although between her ladyship and the child 's mother there be little more love than formerly . there be some injury which woman never forgive one another ; and Madam Francis Esmond , in marry her cousin , have do one of those irretrievable wrong to Lady Castlewood . but as she be now humiliate , and in misfortune , Madam Francis could allow a truce to her enmity , and could be kind for a while , at least , to her husband 's discard mistress . so the little beatrix , her daughter , be permit often to go and visit the imprisoned Viscountess , who , in so far as the child and its father be concern , get to abate in her anger towards that branch of the Castlewood family . and the letter of Colonel Esmond come to light , as have be say , and his conduct be know to the King 's council , the Colonel be put in a well position with the exist government than he have ever before be ; any suspicion regard his loyalty be entirely do away ; and so he be enable to be of more service to his kinswoman than he could otherwise have be . 
 and now there befall an event by which this lady recover her liberty , and the house of Castlewood get a new owner , and fatherless little Harry Esmond a new and most kind protector and friend . whatever that secret be which Harry be to hear from my lord , the boy never hear it ; for that night when Father Holt arrive , and carry my lord away with he , be the last on which Harry ever see his patron . what happen to my lord may be briefly tell here . have find the horse at the place where they be lie , my lord and Father Holt ride together to Chatteris , where they have temporary refuge with one of the Father 's penitent in that city ; but the pursuit be hot for they , and the reward for the apprehension of one or the other considerable , it be deem advisable that they should separate ; and the priest betake himself to other place of retreat know to he , whilst my lord pass over from Bristol into Ireland , in which kingdom King James have a court and an army . my lord be but a small addition to this ; bring , indeed , only his sword and the few piece in his pocket ; but the King receive he with some kindness and distinction in spite of his poor plight , confirm he in his new title of Marquis , give he a regiment , and promise he further promotion . but title or promotion be not to benefit he now . my lord be wound at the fatal battle of the Boyne , fly from which field   long after his master have set he an example   he lie for a while conceal in the marshy country near to the town of Trim , and more from catarrh and fever catch in the bog than from the steel of the enemy in the battle , sank and die . may the earth lie light upon Thomas of Castlewood ! he who write this must speak in charity , though this lord do he and his two grievous wrong : for one of these he would have make amend , perhaps , have life be spar he ; but the other lie beyond his power to repair , though ' tis to be hop that a great power than a priest have absolve he of it . he get the comfort of this absolution , too , such as it be : a priest of Trim write a letter to my lady to inform she of this calamity . 
 but in those day letter be slow of travel , and our priest 's take two month or more on its journey from Ireland to England : where , when it do arrive , it do not find my lady at her own house ; she be at the King 's house of Hexton Castle when the letter come to Castlewood , but it be open for all that by the officer in command there . 
 Harry Esmond well remember the receipt of this letter , which Lockwood bring in as Captain Westbury and Lieutenant Trant be on the green playing at bowl , young Esmond look on at the sport , or read his book in the arbor . 
 " here 's news for Frank Esmond , " say Captain Westbury ; " Harry , do you ever see Colonel Esmond ? " and Captain Westbury look very hard at the boy as he speak . 
 Harry say he have see he but once when he be at Hexton , at the ball there . 
 " and do he say anything ? " 
 " he say what I do not   care to repeat , " Harry answer . for he be now twelve year of age : he know what his birth be , and the disgrace of it ; and he feel no love towards the man who have most likely stain his mother 's honor and his own . 
 " do you love my Lord Castlewood ? " 
 " I wait until I know my mother , sir , to say , " the boy answer , his eye fill with tear . 
 " something have happen to Lord Castlewood , " Captain Westbury say in a very grave tone ' something which must happen to we all . he be dead of a wound receive at the Boyne , fight for King James . " 
 " I be glad my lord fight for the right cause , " the boy say . 
 " it be well to meet death on the field like a man , than face it on Tower - hill , as some of they may , " continue Mr. Westbury . " I hope he have make some testament , or provide for thee somehow . this letter say he recommend unicum filium suum dilectissimum to his lady . I hope he have leave you more than that . " 
 Harry do not know , he say . he be in the hand of Heaven and Fate ; but more lonely now , as it seem to he , than he have be all the rest of his life ; and that night , as he lie in his little room which he still occupy , the boy think with many a pang of shame and grief of his strange and solitary condition : how he have a father and no father ; a nameless mother that have be bring to ruin , perhaps , by that very father whom Harry could only acknowledge in secret and with a blush , and whom he could neither love nor revere . and he sicken to think how Father Holt , a stranger , and two or three soldier , his acquaintance of the last six week , be the only friend he have in the great wide world , where he be now quite alone . the soul of the boy be full of love , and he long as he lie in the darkness there for some one upon whom he could bestow it . he remember , and must to his die day , the thought and tear of that long night , the hour tolling through it . who be he , and what ? why here rather than elsewhere ? I have a mind , he think , to go to that priest at Trim , and find out what my father say to he on his death - bed confession . be there any child in the whole world so unprotected as I be ? shall I get up and quit this place , and run to Ireland ? with these thought and tear the lad pass that night away until he weep himself to sleep . 
 the next day , the gentleman of the guard , who have hear what have befall he , be more than usually kind to the child , especially his friend Scholar Dick , who tell he about his own father 's death , which have happen when Dick be a child at Dublin , not quite five year of age . " that be the first sensation of grief , " Dick say , " I ever know . I remember I go into the room where his body lay , and my mother sit weep beside it . I have my battledore in my hand , and fall a - beat the coffin , and call papa ; on which my mother catch I in her arm , and tell I in a flood of tear Papa could not hear I , and would play with I no more , for they be go to put he under ground , whence he could never come to we again . and this , " say Dick kindly , " have make I pity all child ever since ; and cause I to love thee , my poor fatherless , motherless lad . and , if ever thou want a friend , thou shalt have one in Richard Steele . " 
 Harry Esmond thank he , and be grateful . but what could Corporal Steele do for he ? take he to ride a spare horse , and be servant to the troop ? though there might be a bar in Harry Esmond 's shield , it be a noble one . the counsel of the two friend be , that little Harry should stay where he be , and abide his fortune : so Esmond stay on at Castlewood , await with no small anxiety the fate , whatever it be , which be over he . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 I be LEFT AT castlewood an orphan , and FIND MOST kind PROTECTORS there . 

 during the stay of the soldier in Castlewood , honest Dick the Scholar be the constant companion of the lonely little orphan lad Harry Esmond : and they read together , and they play bowl together , and when the other trooper or their officer , who be free - speak over their cup ,   as be the way of that day , when neither man nor woman be over - nice ,   talk unbecomingly of their amour and gallantry before the child , Dick , who very likely be set the whole company laugh , would stop their joke with a maxima debetur pueris reverentia , and once offer to lug out against another trooper call Hulking Tom , who want to ask Harry Esmond a ribald question . 
 also , Dick see that the child have , as he say , a sensibility above his year , and a great and praiseworthy discretion , confide to Harry his love for a vintner 's daughter , near to the Tollyard , Westminster , whom Dick address as Saccharissa in many verse of his composition , and without whom he say it would be impossible that he could continue to live . he vow this a thousand time in a day , though Harry smile to see the love - lorn swain have his health and appetite as well as the most heart - whole trooper in the regiment : and he swear Harry to secrecy too , which vow the lad religiously keep , until he find that officer and private be all take into Dick 's confidence , and have the benefit of his verse . and it must be own likewise that , while Dick be sigh after Saccharissa in London , he have consolation in the country ; for there come a wench out of Castlewood village who have wash his linen , and who cry sadly when she hear he be go : and without pay her bill too , which Harry Esmond take upon himself to discharge by give the girl a silver pocket - piece , which Scholar Dick have present to he , when , with many embrace and prayer for his prosperity , Dick part from he , the garrison of Castlewood be order away . Dick the Scholar say he would never forget his young friend , nor indeed do he : and Harry be sorry when the kind soldier vacate Castlewood , look forward with no small anxiety   for care and solitude have make he thoughtful beyond his year   to his fate when the new lord and lady of the house come to live there . he have live to be past twelve year old now ; and have never have a friend , save this wild trooper , perhaps , and Father Holt ; and have a fond and affectionate heart , tender to weakness , that would fain attach itself to somebody , and do not seem at rest until it have find a friend who would take charge of it . 
 the instinct which lead Henry Esmond to admire and love the gracious person , the fair apparition of whose beauty and kindness have so move he when he first behold she , become soon a devoted affection and passion of gratitude , which entirely fill his young heart , that as yet , except in the case of dear Father Holt , have have very little kindness for which to be thankful . o Dea certe , think he , remember the line out of the AEneas which Mr. Holt have teach he . there seem , as the boy think , in every look or gesture of this fair creature , an angelical softness and bright pity in motion or repose she seem gracious alike ; the tone of her voice , though she utter word ever so trivial , give he a pleasure that amount almost to anguish . it can not be call love , that a lad of twelve year of age , little more than a menial , feel for an exalt lady , his mistress : but it be worship . to catch her glance , to divine her errand and run on it before she have speak it ; to watch , follow , adore she ; become the business of his life . meanwhile , as be the way often , his idol have idol of her own , and never think of or suspect the admiration of her little pigmy adorer . 
 my lady have on her side her three idol : first and foremost , Jove and supreme ruler , be her lord , Harry 's patron , the good Viscount of Castlewood . all wish of his be law with she . if he have a headache , she be ill . if he frown , she tremble . if he joke , she smile and be charm . if he go a - hunting , she be always at the window to see he ride away , her little son crow on her arm , or on the watch till his return . she make dish for his dinner : spice wine for he : make the toast for his tankard at breakfast : hush the house when he sleep in his chair , and watch for a look when he wake . if my lord be not a little proud of his beauty , my lady adore it . she cling to his arm as he pace the terrace , her two fair little hand clasp round his great one ; her eye be never tired of look in his face and wonder at its perfection . her little son be his son , and have his father 's look and curly brown hair . her daughter Beatrix be his daughter , and have his eye be there ever such beautiful eye in the world ? all the house be arrange so as to bring he ease and give he pleasure . she like the small gentry round about to come and pay he court , never care for admiration for herself ; those who want to be well with the lady must admire he . not regard her dress , she would wear a gown to rag , because he have once like it : and , if he bring she a brooch or a ribbon , would prefer it to all the most costly article of her wardrobe . 
 my lord go to London every year for six week , and the family be too poor to appear at Court with any figure , he go alone . it be not until he be out of sight that her face show any sorrow : and what a joy when he come back ! what preparation before his return ! the fond creature have his arm - chair at the chimney - side delight to put the child in it , and look at they there . nobody take his place at the table ; but his silver tankard stand there as when my lord be present . 
 a pretty sight it be to see , during my lord 's absence , or on those many morning when sleep or headache keep he a - bed , this fair young lady of Castlewood , her little daughter at her knee , and her domestic gather round she , read the Morning Prayer of the English Church . esmond long remember how she look and speak , kneel reverently before the sacred book , the sun shin upon her golden hair until it make a halo round about she . a dozen of the servant of the house kneel in a line opposite their mistress ; for a while Harry Esmond keep apart from these mystery , but Doctor Tusher show he that the prayer read be those of the Church of all age , and the boy 's own inclination prompt he to be always as near as he might to his mistress , and to think all thing she do right , from listen to the prayer in the ante - chamber , he come presently to kneel down with the rest of the household in the parlor ; and before a couple of year my lady have make a thorough convert . indeed , the boy love his catechiser so much that he would have subscribe to anything she bid he , and be never tired of listen to her fond discourse and simple comment upon the book , which she read to he in a voice of which it be difficult to resist the sweet persuasion and tender appeal kindness . this friendly controversy , and the intimacy which it occasion , bind the lad more fondly than ever to his mistress . the happy period of all his life be this ; and the young mother , with her daughter and son , and the orphan lad whom she protect , read and work and play , and be child together . if the lady look forward as what fond woman do not ? towards the future , she have no plan from which Harry Esmond be leave out ; and a thousand and a thousand time , in his passionate and impetuous way , he vow that no power should separate he from his mistress ; and only ask for some chance to happen by which he might show his fidelity to she . now , at the close of his life , as he sit and recall in tranquillity the happy and busy scene of it , he can think , not ungratefully , that he have be faithful to that early vow . such a life be so simple that year may be chronicle in a few line . but few man 's life - voyage be destine to be all prosperous ; and this calm of which we be speak be soon to come to an end . 
 as Esmond grow , and observe for himself , he find of necessity much to read and think of outside that fond circle of kinsfolk who have admit he to join hand with they . he read more book than they care to study with he ; be alone in the midst of they many a time , and pass night over labor , futile perhaps , but in which they could not join he . his dear mistress divine his thought with her usual jealous watchfulness of affection : begin to forebode a time when he would escape from his home - nest ; and , at his eager protestation to the contrary , would only sigh and shake her head . before those fatal decree in life be execute , there be always secret prevision and warning omen . when everything yet seem calm , we be aware that the storm be come . Ere the happy day be over , two at least of that home - party feel that they be draw to a close ; and be uneasy , and on the look - out for the cloud which be to obscure their calm . 
 ' twas easy for Harry to see , however much his lady persist in obedience and admiration for her husband , that my lord tired of his quiet life , and grow weary , and then testy , at those gentle bond with which his wife would have hold he . as they say the Grand Lama of Thibet be very much fatigue by his character of divinity , and yawn on his altar as his bonze kneel and worship he , many a home - god grow heartily sick of the reverence with which his family - devotee pursue he , and sigh for freedom and for his old life , and to be off the pedestal on which his dependant would have he sit for ever , whilst they adore he , and ply he with flower , and hymn , and incense , and flattery ; so , after a few year of his marriage my honest Lord Castlewood begin to tire ; all the high - fly rapture and devotional ceremony with which his wife , his chief priestess , treat he , first send he to sleep , and then drive he out of door ; for the truth must be tell , that my lord be a jolly gentleman , with very little of the august or divine in his nature , though his fond wife persist in revere it and , besides , he have to pay a penalty for this love , which person of his disposition seldom like to defray : and , in a word , if he have a love wife , have a very jealous and exact one . then he weary of this jealousy ; then he break away from it ; then come , no doubt , complaint and recrimination ; then , perhaps , promise of amendment not fulfil ; then upbraiding not the more pleasant because they be silent , and only sad look and tearful eye convey they . then , perhaps , the pair reach that other stage which be not uncommon in married life , when the woman perceive that the god of the honeymoon be a god no more ; only a mortal like the rest of we and so she look into her heart , and lo ! vacuae sede et inania arcana . and now , suppose our lady to have a fine genius and a brilliant wit of her own , and the magic spell and infatuation remove from she which have lead she to worship as a god a very ordinary mortal and what follow ? they live together , and they dine together , and they say " my dear " and " my love " as heretofore ; but the man be himself , and the woman herself : that dream of love be over as everything else be over in life ; as flower and fury , and grief and pleasure , be over . 
 very likely the Lady Castlewood have cease to adore her husband herself long before she get off her knee , or would allow her household to discontinue worship he . to do he justice , my lord never exact this subservience : he laugh and joke and drank his bottle , and swear when he be angry , much too familiarly for any one pretend to sublimity ; and do his good to destroy the ceremonial with which his wife choose to surround he . and it require no great conceit on young Esmond 's part to see that his own brain be well than his patron 's , who , indeed , never assume any air of superiority over the lad , or over any dependant of his , save when he be displeased , in which case he would express his mind in oath very freely ; and who , on the contrary , perhaps , spoil " Parson Harry , " as he call young Esmond , by constantly praise his part and admire his boyish stock of learn . 
 it may seem ungracious in one who have receive a hundred favor from his patron to speak in any but a reverential manner of his elder ; but the present writer have have descendant of his own , whom he have bring up with as little as possible of the servility at present exact by parent from child   under which mask of duty there often lurk indifference , contempt , or rebellion : and as he would have his grandson believe or represent he to be not an inch tall than Nature have make he : so , with regard to his past acquaintance , he would speak without anger , but with truth , as far as he know it , neither extenuate nor set down aught in malice . 
 so long , then , as the world move accord to Lord Castlewood 's wish , he be good - humor enough ; of a temper naturally sprightly and easy , like to joke , especially with his inferior , and charm to receive the tribute of their laughter . all exercise of the body he could perform to perfection shoot at a mark and fly , break horse , rid at the ring , pitch the quoit , play at all game with great skill . and not only do he do these thing well , but he think he do they to perfection ; hence he be often trick about horse , which he pretend to know well than any jockey ; be make to play at ball and billiard by sharper who take his money , and come back from London wofully poorer each time than he go , as the state of his affair testify when the sudden accident come by which his career be bring to an end . 
 he be fond of the parade of dress , and pass as many hour daily at his toilette as an elderly coquette . a tenth part of his day be spend in the brushing of his tooth and the oiling of his hair , which be curl and brown , and which he do not like to conceal under a periwig , such as almost everybody of that time wear .   we have the liberty of our hair back now , but powder and pomatum along with it . when , I wonder , will these monstrous poll - taxis of our age be withdraw , and man allow to carry their color , black , red , or gray , as Nature make they ?   and as he like she to be well dress , his lady spar no pain in that matter to please he ; indeed , she would dress her head or cut it off if he have bid she . 
 it be a wonder to young Esmond , serve as page to my lord and lady , to hear , day after day , to such company as come , the same boisterous story tell by my lord , at which his lady never fail to smile or hold down her head , and Doctor Tusher to burst out laugh at the proper point , or cry , " Fie , my lord , remember my cloth ! " but with such a faint show of resistance , that it only provoke my lord far . Lord Castlewood 's story rise by degree , and become strong after the ale at dinner and the bottle afterwards ; my lady always take flight after the very first glass to Church and King , and leave the gentleman to drink the rest of the toast by themselves . 
 and , as Harry Esmond be her page , he also be call from duty at this time . " my lord have live in the army and with soldier , " she would say to the lad , " amongst whom great license be allow . you have have a different nurture , and I trust these thing will change as you grow old ; not that any fault attache to my lord , who be one of the good and most religious man in this kingdom . " and very likely she believe so . ' tis strange what a man may do , and a woman yet think he an angel . 
 and as Esmond have take truth for his motto , it must be own , even with regard to that other angel , his mistress , that she have a fault of character which flaw her perfection . with the other sex perfectly tolerant and kindly , of her own she be invariably jealous ; and a proof that she have this vice be , that though she would acknowledge a thousand fault that she have not , to this which she have she could never be get to own . but if there come a woman with even a semblance of beauty to Castlewood , she be so sure to find out some wrong in she , that my lord , laugh in his jolly way , would often joke with she concern her foible . comely servant - maid might come for hire , but none be take at Castlewood . the housekeeper be old ; my lady 's own waiting - woman squint , and be mark with the small - pox ; the housemaid and scullion be ordinary country wench , to whom Lady Castlewood be kind , as her nature make she to everybody almost ; but as soon as ever she have to do with a pretty woman , she be cold , retire , and haughty . the country lady find this fault in she ; and though the man all admire she , their wife and daughter complain of her coldness and aim , and say that Castlewood be pleasanter in Lady Jezebel 's time   as the dowager be call   than at present . some few be of my mistress 's side . Old Lady Blenkinsop Jointure , who have be at court in King James the First 's time , always take her side ; and so do old Mistress Crookshank , Bishop Crookshank 's daughter , of Hexton , who , with some more of their like , pronounce my lady an angel : but the pretty woman be not of this mind ; and the opinion of the country be that my lord be tie to his wife 's apron - string , and that she rule over he . 
 the second fight which Harry Esmond have , be at fourteen year of age , with Bryan Hawkshaw , Sir John Hawkshaw 's son , of Bramblebrook , who , advance this opinion , that my lady be jealous and henpecke my lord , put Harry in such a fury , that Harry fall on he and with such rage , that the other boy , who be two year old and by far big than he , have by far the bad of the assault , until it be interrupt by Doctor Tusher walk out of the dinner - room . 
 Bryan Hawkshaw get up bleed at the nose , have , indeed , be surprised , as many a strong man might have be , by the fury of the assault upon he . 
 " you little bastard beggar ! " he say , " I will murder you for this ! " 
 and indeed he be big enough . 
 " Bastard or not , " say the other , grind his tooth , " I have a couple of sword , and if you like to meet I , as a man , on the terrace to - night " 
 and here the Doctor come up , the colloquy of the young champion end . very likely , big as he be , Hawkshaw do not care to continue a fight with such a ferocious opponent as this have be . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 after good fortune COMES EVIL . 

 since my Lady Mary Wortley Montagu bring home the custom of inoculation from Turkey   a perilous practice many deem it , and only a useless rushing into the jaw of danger , I think the severity of the small - pox , that dreadful scourge of the world , have somewhat be abate in our part of it ; and remember in my time hundred of the young and beautiful who have be carry to the grave , or have only rise from their pillow frightfully scarred and disfigure by this malady . many a sweet face hath leave its rose on the bed on which this dreadful and wither blight have lay they . in my early day , this pestilence would enter a village and destroy half its inhabitant : at its approach , it may well be imagine , not only the beautiful but the strong be alarmed , and those flee who could . one day in the year 1694   I have good reason to remember it , Doctor Tusher run into Castlewood House , with a face of consternation , say that the malady have make its appearance at the blacksmith 's house in the village , and that one of the maid there be down in the small - pox . 
 the blacksmith , besides his forge and iron for horse , have an ale - house for man , which his wife keep , and his company sit on bench before the inn - door , look at the smithy while they drink their beer . now , there be a pretty girl at this inn , the landlord 's man call Nancy Sievewright , a bounce , fresh - look lass , whose face be as red as the hollyhock over the pale of the garden behind the inn . at this time Harry Esmond be a lad of sixteen , and somehow in his walk and ramble it often happen that he fall in with Nancy Sievewright 's bonny face ; if he do not want something do at the blacksmith 's he would go and drink ale at the " Three Castles , " or find some pretext for see this poor Nancy . poor thing , Harry mean or imagine no harm ; and she , no doubt , as little , but the truth be they be always meet in the lane , or by the brook , or at the garden - paling , or about Castlewood : it be , " Lord , Mr. Henry ! " and " how do you do , Nancy ? " many and many a time in the week . ' tis surprising the magnetic attraction which draw people together from ever so far . I blush as I think of poor Nancy now , in a red bodice and buxom purple cheek and a canvas petticoat ; and that I devise scheme , and set trap , and make speech in my heart , which I seldom have courage to say when in presence of that humble enchantress , who know nothing beyond milk a cow , and open her black eye with wonder when I make one of my fine speech out of Waller or Ovid . Poor Nancy ! from the midst of far - off year thine honest country face beam out ; and I remember thy kind voice as if I have hear it yesterday . 
 when Doctor Tusher bring the news that the small - pox be at the " Three Castles , " whither a tramper , it be say , have bring the malady , Henry Esmond 's first thought be of alarm for poor Nancy , and then of shame and disquiet for the Castlewood family , lest he might have bring this infection ; for the truth be that Mr. Harry have be sit in a back room for an hour that day , where Nancy Sievewright be with a little brother who complain of headache , and be lie stupefied and cry , either in a chair by the corner of the fire , or in Nancy 's lap , or on mine . 
 Little Lady Beatrix scream out at Dr. Tusher 's news ; and my lord cry out , " God bless I ! " he be a brave man , and not afraid of death in any shape but this . he be very proud of his pink complexion and fair hair but the idea of death by small - pox scar he beyond all other end . " we will take the child and ride away to - morrow to Walcote : " this be my lord 's small house , inherit from his mother , near to Winchester . 
 " that be the good refuge in case the disease spread , " say Dr. Tusher . " ' tis awful to think of it begin at the ale - house ; half the people of the village have visit that to - day , or the blacksmith 's , which be the same thing . my clerk Nahum lodge with they I can never go into my reading - desk and have that fellow so near I . I won't have that man near I . " 
 " if a parishioner die in the small - pox send to you , would you not go ? " ask my lady , look up from her frame of work , with her calm blue eye . 
 " by the Lord , I would not , " say my lord . 
 " we be not in a popish country ; and a sick man doth not absolutely need absolution and confession , " say the Doctor . " ' tis true they be a comfort and a help to he when attainable , and to be administer with hope of good . but in a case where the life of a parish priest in the midst of his flock be highly valuable to they , he be not call upon to risk it   and therewith the life , future prospect , and temporal , even spiritual welfare of his own family   for the sake of a single person , who be not very likely in a condition even to understand the religious message whereof the priest be the bringer be uneducated , and likewise stupefied or delirious by disease . if your ladyship or his lordship , my excellent good friend and patron , be to take it . . . " 
 " God forbid ! " cry my lord . 
 " amen , " continue Dr. Tusher . " amen to that prayer , my very good lord ! for your sake I would lay my life down " and , to judge from the alarmed look of the Doctor 's purple face , you would have think that that sacrifice be about to be call for instantly . 
 to love child , and be gentle with they , be an instinct , rather than a merit , in Henry Esmond ; so much so , that he think almost with a sort of shame of his liking for they , and of the softness into which it betray he ; and on this day the poor fellow have not only have his young friend , the milkmaid 's brother , on his knee , but have be draw picture and tell story to the little Frank Castlewood , who have occupy the same place for an hour after dinner , and be never tired of Henry 's tale , and his picture of soldier and horse . as luck would have it , Beatrix have not on that evening take her usual place , which generally she be glad enough to have , upon her tutor 's lap . for beatrix , from the early time , be jealous of every caress which be give to her little brother Frank . she would fling away even from the maternal arm , if she see Frank have be there before she ; insomuch that Lady Esmond be oblige not to show her love for her son in the presence of the little girl , and embrace one or the other alone . she would turn pale and red with rage if she catch sign of intelligence or affection between Frank and his mother : would sit apart , and not speak for a whole night , if she think the boy have a well fruit or a large cake than her ; would fling away a ribbon if he have one ; and from the early age , sit up in her little chair by the great fireplace opposite to the corner where Lady Castlewood commonly sit at her embroidery , would utter infantine sarcasm about the favor show to her brother . these , if speak in the presence of Lord Castlewood , tickle and amuse his humor ; he would pretend to love Frank well , and dandle and kiss he , and roar with laughter at Beatrix 's jealousy . but the truth be , my lord do not often witness these scene , nor very much trouble the quiet fireside at which his lady pass many long evening . my lord be hunt all day when the season admit ; he frequent all the cock - fight and fair in the country , and would ride twenty mile to see a main fought , or two clown break their head at a cudgelling - match ; and he like well to sit in his parlor drinking ale and punch with Jack and Tom , than in his wife 's drawing - room : whither , if he come , he bring only too often bloodshot eye , a hiccupping voice , and a reeling gait . the management of the house , and the property , the care of the few tenant and the village poor , and the account of the estate , be in the hand of his lady and her young secretary , Harry Esmond . my lord take charge of the stable , the kennel , and the cellar and he fill this and empty it too . 
 so it chance that upon this very day , when poor Harry Esmond have have the blacksmith 's son , and the peer 's son , alike upon his knee , little beatrix , who would come to her tutor willingly enough with her book and her writing , have refuse he , see the place occupy by her brother , and , luckily for she , have sit at the further end of the room , away from he , play with a spaniel dog which she have ,   and for which , by fit and start , she would take a great affection ,   and talk at Harry Esmond over her shoulder , as she pretend to caress the dog , say that Fido would love she , and she would love Fido , and nothing but Fido all her life . 
 when , then , the news be bring that the little boy at the " Three Castles " be ill with the small - pox , poor Harry Esmond feel a shock of alarm , not so much for himself as for his mistress 's son , whom he might have bring into peril . beatrix , who have pout sufficiently ,   and who , whenever a stranger appear , begin , from infancy almost , to play off little grace to catch his attention ,   her brother be now go to bed , be for take her place upon Esmond 's knee : for , though the doctor be very obsequious to she , she do not like he , because he have thick boot and dirty hand   the pert young miss say , and because she hat learn the catechism . 
 but as she advance towards Esmond from the corner where she have be sulk , he start back and place the great chair on which he be sit between he and her say in the french language to Lady Castlewood , with whom the young lad have read much , and whom he have perfect in this tongue " Madam , the child must not approach I ; I must tell you that I be at the blacksmith 's to - day , and have his little boy upon my lap . " 
 " where you take my son afterwards , " Lady Castlewood say , very angry , and turn red . " I thank you , sir , for give he such company . beatrix , " she say in English , " I forbid you to touch Mr. Esmond . come away , child come to your room . come to your room I wish your Reverence good - night and you , sir , have you not well go back to your friend at the ale - house ?
