" her eye , ordinarily so kind , dart flash of anger as she speak ; and she toss up her head   which hang down commonly   with the mien of a princess . 
 " hey - day ! " say my lord , who be stand by the fireplace indeed he be in the position to which he generally come by that hour of the evening " Hey - day ! Rachel , what be you in a passion about ? lady ought never to be in a passion . ought they , Doctor Tusher ? though it do good to see Rachel in a passion Damme , Lady Castlewood , you look dev'lish handsome in a passion . " 
 " it be , my lord , because Mr. Henry Esmond , have nothing to do with his time here , and not have a taste for our company , have be to the ale - house , where he have some friend . " 
 my lord burst out , with a laugh and an oath " you young slyboot , you have   be at Nancy Sievewright . D - the young hypocrite , who would have think it in he ? I say , Tusher , he be be after " 
 " Enough , my lord , " say my lady , " do not   insult I with this talk . " 
 " upon my word , " say poor Harry , ready to cry with shame and mortification , " the honor of that young person be perfectly unstaine for I . " 
 " oh , of course , of course , " say my lord , more and more laugh and tipsy . " upon his HONOR , Doctor Nancy Sieve . . . " 
 " take mistress Beatrix to bed , " my lady cry at this moment to Mrs. Tucker her woman , who come in with her ladyship 's tea . " put she into my room no , into your , " she add quickly . " go , my child : go , I say : not a word ! " and beatrix , quite surprised at so sudden a tone of authority from one who be seldom accustomed to raise her voice , go out of the room with a scared countenance , and wait even to burst out a - crying until she get to the door with Mrs. Tucker . 
 for once her mother take little heed of her sobbing , and continue to speak eagerly " my lord , " she say , " this young man your dependant tell I just now in French he be ashamed to speak in his own language that he have be at the ale - house all day , where he have have that little wretch who be now ill of the small - pox on his knee . and he come home reeking from that place yes , reek from it and take my boy into his lap without shame , and sit down by I , yes , by I . he may have kill Frank for what I know kill our child . why be he bring in to disgrace our house ? why be he here ? let he go let he go , I say , to - night , and pollute the place no more . " 
 she have never once utter a syllable of unkindness to Harry Esmond ; and her cruel word smite the poor boy , so that he stand for some moment bewilder with grief and rage at the injustice of such a stab from such a hand . he turn quite white from red , which he have be . 
 " I can not help my birth , madam , " he say , " nor my other misfortune . and as for your boy , if if my come nigh to he pollute he now , it be not so always . good - night , my lord . Heaven bless you and yours for your goodness to I . I have tire her ladyship 's kindness out , and I will go ; " and , sink down on his knee , Harry Esmond take the rough hand of his benefactor and kiss it . 
 " he want to go to the ale - house let he go , " cry my lady . 
 " I be d d if he shall , " say my lord . " I do not   think you could be so d d ungrateful , Rachel . " 
 her reply be to burst into a flood of tear , and to quit the room with a rapid glance at Harry Esmond , as my lord , not heed they , and still in great good - humor , raise up his young client from his kneeling posture   for a thousand kindness have cause the lad to revere my lord as a father , and put his broad hand on Harry Esmond 's shoulder . 
 " she be always so , " my lord say ; " the very notion of a woman drive her mad . I take to liquor on that very account , by Jove , for no other reason than that ; for she can not   be jealous of a beer - barrel or a bottle of rum , can she , Doctor ? D - it , look at the maid just look at the maid in the house "   my lord pronounce all the word together just - look - at - the - maze - in - the - house : jever - see - such - maze ?   " you would not   take a wife out of Castlewood now , would you , Doctor ? " and my lord burst out laugh . 
 the Doctor , who have be look at my Lord Castlewood from under his eyelid , say , " but joke apart , and , my lord , as a divine , I can not treat the subject in a jocular light , nor , as a pastor of this congregation , look with anything but sorrow at the idea of so very young a sheep go astray . " 
 " Sir , " say young Esmond , burst out indignantly , ' she tell I that you yourself be a horrid old man , and have offer to kiss she in the dairy . " 
 " for shame , Henry , " cry Doctor Tusher , turn as red as a turkey - cock , while my lord continue to roar with laughter . " if you listen to the falsehood of an abandon girl " 
 " she be as honest as any woman in England , and as pure for I , " cry out Henry , " and , as kind , and as good . for shame on you to malign she ! " 
 " far be it from I to do so , " cry the Doctor . " heaven grant I may be mistake in the girl , and in you , sir , who have a truly precocious genius ; but that be not the point at issue at present . it appear that the small - pox break out in the little boy at the ' three Castles ; ' that it be on he when you visit the ale - house , for your own reason ; and that you sit with the child for some time , and immediately afterwards with my young lord . " the Doctor raise his voice as he speak , and look towards my lady , who have now come back , look very pale , with a handkerchief in her hand . 
 " this be all very true , sir , " say Lady Esmond , look at the young man . 
 " ' tis to be fear that he may have bring the infection with he . " 
 " from the ale - house yes , " say my lady . 
 " D - it , I forget when I collar you , boy , " cry my lord , step back . " keep off , Harry my boy ; there be no good in run into the wolf 's jaw , you know . " 
 my lady look at he with some surprise , and instantly advance to Henry Esmond , take his hand . " I beg your pardon , Henry , " she say ; " I speak very unkindly . I have no right to interfere with you with your " 
 my lord break out into an oath . " can not   you leave the boy alone , my lady ? " she look a little red , and faintly press the lad 's hand as she drop it . 
 " there be no use , my lord , " she say ; " Frank be on his knee as he be make picture , and be run constantly from Henry to I . the evil be do , if any . " 
 " not with I , damme , " cry my lord . " I have   be smoke , " and he light his pipe again with a coal " and it keep off infection ; and as the disease be in the village plague take it I would have you leave it . we will go to - morrow to Walcote , my lady . " 
 " I have no fear , " say my lady ; " I may have have it as an infant : it break out in our house then ; and when four of my sister have it at home , two year before our marriage , I escape it , and two of my dear sister die . " 
 " I will not   run the risk , " say my lord ; " I be as bold as any man , but I will not bear that . " 
 " take beatrix with you and go , " say my lady . " for we the mischief be do ; and Tucker can wait upon we , who have have the disease . " 
 " you take care to choose them ugly enough , " say my lord , at which her ladyship hang down her head and look foolish : and my lord , call away Tusher , bid he come to the oak parlor and have a pipe . the Doctor make a low bow to her ladyship   of which salaam he be profuse , and walk off on his creak square - toe after his patron . 
 when the lady and the young man be alone , there be a silence of some moment , during which he stand at the fire , look rather vacantly at the die ember , whilst her ladyship busy herself with the tambour - frame and needle . 
 " I be sorry , " she say , after a pause , in a hard , dry voice , " I repeat I be sorry that I show myself so ungrateful for the safety of my son . it be not at all my wish that you should leave we , I be sure , unless you find pleasure elsewhere . but you must perceive , Mr. Esmond , that at your age , and with your taste , it be impossible that you can continue to stay upon the intimate footing in which you have be in this family . you have wish to go to the University , and I think ' tis quite as well that you should be send thither . I do not press this matter , think you a child , as you be , indeed , in year quite a child ; and I should never have think of treat you otherwise until until these circumstance come to light . and I shall beg my lord to despatch you as quick as possible : and will go on with Frank 's learning as well as I can ,   I owe my father thank for a little grounding , and you , I be sure , for much that you have teach I ,   and and I wish you a good - night , Mr. Esmond . " 
 and with this she drop a stately curtsy , and , take her candle , go away through the tapestry door , which lead to her apartment . esmond stand by the fireplace , blankly star after she . indeed , he scarce seem to see until she be go ; and then her image be impress upon he , and remain for ever fix upon his memory . he see her retreating , the taper light up her marble face , her scarlet lip quivering , and her shin golden hair . he go to his own room , and to bed , where he try to read , as his custom be ; but he never know what he be read until afterwards he remember the appearance of the letter of the book   it be in Montaigne 's essay , and the event of the day pass before he that be , of the last hour of the day ; for as for the morning , and the poor milkmaid yonder , he never so much as once think . and he could not get to sleep until daylight , and wake with a violent headache , and quite unrefreshed . 
 he have bring the contagion with he from the " Three Castles " sure enough , and be presently lay up with the smallpox , which spar the hall no more than it do the cottage . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 I have the SMALL - POX , and prepare to leave castlewood . 

 when Harry Esmond pass through the crisis of that malady , and return to health again , he find that little Frank Esmond have also suffer and rally after the disease , and the lady his mother be down with it , with a couple more of the household . " it be a Providence , for which we all ought to be thankful , " Doctor Tusher say , " that my lady and her son be spar , while Death carry off the poor domestic of the house ; " and rebuke Harry for ask , in his simple way , for which we ought to be thankful that the servant be kill , or the gentlefolk be save ? nor could young Esmond agree in the Doctor 's vehement protestation to my lady , when he visit she during her convalescence , that the malady have not in the least impair her charm , and have not be churl enough to injure the fair feature of the Viscountess of Castlewood ; whereas , in spite of these fine speech , Harry think that her ladyship 's beauty be very much injure by the small - pox . when the mark of the disease clear away , they do not , it be true , leave furrow or scar on her face   except one , perhaps , on her forehead over her left eyebrow ; but the delicacy of her rosy color and complexion be go : her eye have lose their brilliancy , her hair fall , and her face look old . it be as if a coarse hand have rub off the delicate tint of that sweet picture , and bring it , as one have see unskilful painting - cleaner do , to the dead color . also , it must be own , that for a year or two after the malady , her ladyship 's nose be swollen and redder . 
 there would be no need to mention these triviality , but that they actually influence many life , as trifle will in the world , where a gnat often play a great part than an elephant , and a mole - hill , as we know in King William 's case , can upset an empire . when Tusher in his courtly way   at which Harry Esmond always chafe and speak scornfully   vow and protest that my lady 's face be none the bad the lad break out and say , " it be bad and my mistress be not near so handsome as she be ; " on which poor Lady Castlewood give a rueful smile , and a look into a little Venice glass she have , which show she , I suppose , that what the stupid boy say be only too true , for she turn away from the glass , and her eye fill with tear . 
 the sight of these in Esmond 's heart always create a sort of rage of pity , and see they on the face of the lady whom he love well , the young blunderer sink down on his knee , and besought she to pardon he , say that he be a fool and an idiot , that he be a brute to make such a speech , he who have cause she malady ; and Doctor Tusher tell he that a bear he be indeed , and a bear he would remain , at which speech poor young Esmond be so dumbstricken that he do not even growl . 
 " he be MY bear , and I will not have he bait , Doctor , " my lady say , pat her hand kindly on the boy 's head , as he be still kneel at her foot . " how your hair have come off ! and mine , too , " she add with another sigh . 
 " it be not for myself that I care , " my lady say to Harry , when the parson have take his leave ; " but be I very much change ? alas ! I fear ' tis too true . " 
 " Madam , you have the dear , and kind , and sweetest face in the world , I think , " the lad say ; and indeed he think and think so . 
 " will my lord think so when he come back ? " the lady ask with a sigh , and another look at her Venice glass . " suppose he should think as you do , sir , that I be hideous yes , you say hideous he will cease to care for I . ' tis all man care for in woman , our little beauty . why do he select I from among my sister ? ' twas only for that . we reign but for a day or two : and be sure that Vashti know Esther be come . " 
 " Madam , " say Mr. Esmond , " Ahasuerus be the Grand Turk , and to change be the manner of his country , and accord to his law . " 
 " you be all Grand Turks for that matter , " say my lady , " or would be if you could . come , Frank , come , my child . you be well , praise be Heaven . your lock be not thin by this dreadful small - pox : nor your poor face scar be it , my angel ? " 
 Frank begin to shout and whimper at the idea of such a misfortune . from the very early time the young lord have be teach to admire his beauty by his mother : and esteem it as highly as any reign toast value her . 
 one day , as he himself be recover from his fever and illness , a pang of something like shame shoot across young Esmond 's breast , as he remember that he have never once during his illness give a thought to the poor girl at the smithy , whose red cheek but a month ago he have be so eager to see . poor Nancy ! her cheek have share the fate of rose , and be wither now . she have take the illness on the same day with Esmond she and her brother be both dead of the small - pox , and bury under the Castlewood yew - tree . there be no bright face look now from the garden , or to cheer the old smith at his lonely fireside . esmond would have like to have kiss she in her shroud   like the lass in Mr. Prior 's pretty poem ; but she rest many a foot below the ground , when Esmond after his malady first trod on it . 
 Doctor Tusher bring the news of this calamity , about which Harry Esmond long to ask , but do not like . he say almost the whole village have be stricken with the pestilence ; seventeen person be dead of it , among they mention the name of poor Nancy and her little brother . he do not fail to say how thankful we survivor ought to be . it be this man 's business to flatter and make sermon , it must be own he be most industrious in it , and be do the one or the other all day . 
 and so Nancy be go ; and Harry Esmond blush that he have not a single tear for she , and fall to compose an elegy in Latin verse over the rustic little beauty . he bid the dryad mourn and the river - nymph deplore she . as her father follow the calling of Vulcan , he say that surely she be like a daughter of Venus , though Sievewright 's wife be an ugly shrew , as he remember to have hear afterwards . he make a long face , but , in truth , feel scarcely more sorrowful than a mute at a funeral . these first passion of man and woman be mostly abortive ; and be dead almost before they be bear . esmond could repeat , to his last day , some of the doggerel line in which his muse bewail his pretty lass ; not without shame to remember how bad the verse be , and how good he think they ; how false the grief , and yet how he be rather proud of it . ' tis an error , surely , to talk of the simplicity of youth . I think no person be more hypocritical , and have a more affected behavior to one another , than the young . they deceive themselves and each other with artifice that do not impose upon man of the world ; and so we get to understand truth well , and grow simple as we grow old . 
 when my lady hear of the fate which have befall poor Nancy , she say nothing so long as Tusher be by , but when he be go , she take Harry Esmond 's hand and say 
 " Harry , I beg your pardon for those cruel word I use on the night you be take ill . I be shock at the fate of the poor creature , and be sure that nothing have happen of that with which , in my anger , I charge you . and the very first day we go out , you must take I to the blacksmith , and we must see if there be anything I can do to console the poor old man . poor man ! to lose both his child ! what should I do without mine ? " 
 and this be , indeed , the very first walk which my lady take , lean on Esmond 's arm , after her illness . but her visit bring no consolation to the old father ; and he show no softness , or desire to speak . " the Lord give and take away , " he say ; and he know what his servant 's duty be . he want for nothing less now than ever before , as there be few mouth to feed . he wish her ladyship and Master Esmond good morning he have grow tall in his illness , and be but very little marked ; and with this , and a surly bow , he go in from the smithy to the house , leave my lady , somewhat silence and shameface , at the door . he have a handsome stone put up for his two child , which may be see in Castlewood churchyard to this very day ; and before a year be out his own name be upon the stone . in the presence of death , that sovereign ruler , a woman 's coquetry be sear ; and her jealousy will hardly pass the boundary of that grim kingdom . ' tis entirely of the earth , that passion , and expire in the cold blue air , beyond our sphere . 
 at length , when the danger be quite over , it be announce that my lord and his daughter would return . esmond well remember the day . the lady his mistress be in a flurry of fear : before my lord come , she go into her room , and return from it with reddened cheek . her fate be about to be decide . her beauty be go be her reign , too , over ? a minute would say . my lord come rid over the bridge he could be see from the great window , clothe in scarlet , and mount on his gray hackney his little daughter amble by he in a bright riding - dress of blue , on a shin chestnut horse . my lady lean against the great mantel - piece , look on , with one hand on her heart she seem only the more pale for those red mark on either cheek . she put her handkerchief to her eye , and withdraw it , laugh hysterically the cloth be quite red with the rouge when she take it away . she run to her room again , and come back with pale cheek and red eye her son in her hand just as my lord enter , accompany by young Esmond , who have go out to meet his protector , and to hold his stirrup as he descend from horseback . 
 " what , Harry , boy ! " my lord say , good - naturedly , " you look as gaunt as a greyhound . the small - pox have not   improve your beauty , and your side of the house have not   never too much of it ho , ho ! " 
 and he laugh , and spring to the ground with no small agility , look handsome and red , within a jolly face and brown hair , like a Beef - eater ; esmond kneeling again , as soon as his patron have descend , perform his homage , and then go to greet the little beatrix , and help she from her horse . 
 " Fie ! how yellow you look , " she say ; " and there be one , two , red hole in your face ; " which , indeed , be very true ; Harry Esmond 's harsh countenance bearing , as long as it continue to be a human face , the mark of the disease . 
 my lord laugh again , in high good - humor . 
 " D - it ! " say he , with one of his usual oath , " the little slut see everything . she see the Dowager 's paint t'other day , and ask she why she wear that red stuff do not   you , Trix ? and the Tower ; and St. James 's ; and the play ; and the Prince George , and the Princess Anne do not   you , Trix ? " 
 " they be both very fat , and smell of brandy , " the child say . 
 Papa roar with laughing . 
 " Brandy ! " he say . " and how do you know , Miss Pert ? " 
 " because your lordship smell of it after supper , when I embrace you before you go to bed , " say the young lady , who , indeed , be as pert as her father say , and look as beautiful a little gipsy as eye ever gaze on . 
 " and now for my lady , " say my lord , go up the stair , and pass under the tapestry curtain that hang before the drawing - room door . esmond remember that noble figure , handsomely array in scarlet . within the last few month he himself have grow from a boy to be a man , and with his figure his thought have shoot up , and grow manly . 
 my lady 's countenance , of which Harry Esmond be accustom to watch the change , and with a solicitous affection to note and interpret the sign of gladness or care , wear a sad and depressed look for many week after her lord 's return : during which it seem as if , by caress and entreaty , she strove to win he back from some ill humor he have , and which he do not choose to throw off . in her eagerness to please he she practise a hundred of those art which have formerly charm he , but which seem now to have lose their potency . her song do not amuse he ; and she hush they and the child when in his presence . my lord sit silent at his dinner , drink greatly , his lady opposite to he , look furtively at his face , though also speechless . her silence annoy he as much as her speech ; and he would peevishly , and with an oath , ask she why she hold her tongue and look so glum ; or he would roughly check she when speak , and bid she not talk nonsense . it seem as if , since his return , nothing she could do or say could please he . 
 when a master and mistress be at strife in a house , the subordinate in the family take the one side or the other . Harry Esmond stand in so great fear of my lord , that he would run a league barefoot to do a message for he ; but his attachment for Lady Esmond be such a passion of grateful regard , that to spare she a grief , or to do she a service , he would have give his life daily : and it be by the very depth and intensity of this regard that he begin to divine how unhappy his adore lady 's life be , and that a secret care   for she never speak of her anxiety   be weigh upon she . 
 can any one , who have pass through the world and watch the nature of man and woman there , doubt what have befall she ? I have see , to be sure , some people carry down with they into old age the actual bloom of their youthful love , and I know that Mr. Thomas Parr live to be a hundred and sixty year old . but , for all that , threescore and ten be the age of man , and few get beyond it ; and ' tis certain that a man who marry for mere beaux yeux , as my lord do , consider this part of the contract at an end when the woman cease to fulfil her , and his love do not survive her beauty . I know ' tis often otherwise , I say ; and can think   as most man in their own experience may   of many a house , where , light in early year , the sainted lamp of love hath never be extinguish ; but so there be Mr. Parr , and so there be the great giant at the fair that be eight foot high exception to man and that poor lamp whereof I speak , that light at first the nuptial chamber , be extinguish by a hundred wind and draught down the chimney , or sputter out for want of feed . and then and then it be Chloe , in the dark , stark awake , and Strephon snore unheeding ; or vice versa , ' tis poor Strephon that have marry a heartless jilt , and awake out of that absurd vision of conjugal felicity , which be to last for ever , and be over like any other dream . one and other have make his bed , and so must lie in it , until that final day when life end , and they sleep separate . 
 about this time young Esmond , who have a knack of string verse , turn some of Ovid 's Epistles into rhyme , and bring they to his lady for her delectation . those which treat of forsake woman touch she immensely , Harry remark ; and when Oenone call after Paris , and Medea bade Jason come back again , the lady of Castlewood sigh , and say she think that part of the verse be the most pleasing . indeed , she would have chop up the Dean , her old father , in order to bring her husband back again . but her beautiful Jason be go , as beautiful Jasons will go , and the poor enchantress have never a spell to keep he . 
 my lord be only sulky as long as his wife 's anxious face or behavior seem to upbraid he . when she have get to master these , and to show an outwardly cheerful countenance and behavior , her husband 's good - humor return partially , and he swear and storm no long at dinner , but laugh sometimes , and yawn unrestrainedly ; absent himself often from home , invite more company thither , pass the great part of his day in the hunting - field , or over the bottle as before ; but with this difference , that the poor wife could no long see now , as she have do formerly , the light of love kindle in his eye . he be with she , but that flame be out : and that once welcome beacon no more shone there . 
 what be this lady 's feeling when force to admit the truth whereof her foreboding glass have give she only too true warning , that within her beauty her reign have end , and the day of her love be over ? what do a seaman do in a storm if mast and rudder be carry away ? he ship a jurymast , and steer as he well can with an oar . what happen if your roof fall in a tempest ? after the first stun of the calamity the sufferer start up , grope around to see that the child be safe , and put they under a shed out of the rain . if the palace burn down , you take shelter in the barn . what man 's life be not overtake by one or more of these tornado that send we out of the course , and fle we on rock to shelter as good we may ? 
 when Lady Castlewood find that her great ship have go down , she begin as good she might after she have rally from the effect of the loss , to put out small venture of happiness ; and hope for little gain and return , as a merchant on ' Change , indocilis pauperiem pati , have lose his thousand , embark a few guineas upon the next ship . she lay out she all upon her child , indulge they beyond all measure , as be inevitable with one of her kindness of disposition ; give all her thought to their welfare learning , that she might teach they ; and improve her own many natural gift and feminine accomplishment , that she might impart they to her young one . to be do good for some one else , be the life of most good woman . they be exuberant of kindness , as it be , and must impart it to some one . she make herself a good scholar of french , italian , and Latin , have be ground in these by her father in her youth ; hide these gift from her husband out of fear , perhaps , that they should offend he , for my lord be no bookman pish'd and psha'd at the notion of learn lady , and would have be angry that his wife could construe out of a latin book of which he could scarce understand two word . Young Esmond be usher , or house tutor , under she or over she , as it might happen . during my lord 's many absence , these school - day would go on uninterruptedly : the mother and daughter learn with surprising quickness ; the latter by fit and start only , and as suit her wayward humor . as for the little lord , it must be own that he take after his father in the matter of learn like marble and play , and the great horse and the little one which his father bring he , and on which he take he out a - hunting , a great deal well than Corderius and Lily ; marshal the village boy , and have a little court of they , already flog they , and domineer over they with a fine imperious spirit , that make his father laugh when he behold it , and his mother fondly warn he . the cook have a son , the woodman have two , the big lad at the porter 's lodge take his cuff and his order . Doctor Tusher say he be a young nobleman of gallant spirit ; and Harry Esmond , who be his tutor , and eight year his little lordship 's senior , have hard work sometimes to keep his own temper , and hold his authority over his rebellious little chief and kinsman . 
 in a couple of year after that calamity have befall which have rob Lady Castlewood of a little a very little of her beauty , and her careless husband 's heart   if the truth must be tell , my lady have find not only that her reign be over , but that her successor be appoint , a Princess of a noble house in Drury Lane somewhere , who be instal and visit by my lord at the town eight mile off pudet haec opprobria dicere nobis   a great change have take place in her mind , which , by struggle only know to herself , at least never mention to any one , and unsuspecte by the person who cause the pain she endure have be school into such a condition as she could not very likely have imagine possible a score of month since , before her misfortune have begin . 
 she have oldene in that time as people do who suffer silently great mental pain ; and learn much that she have never suspect before . she be teach by that bitter teacher Misfortune . a child the mother of other child , but two year back her lord be a god to she ; his word her law ; his smile her sunshine ; his lazy commonplace listen to eagerly , as if they be word of wisdom all his wish and freak obey with a servile devotion . she have be my lord 's chief slave and blind worshipper . some woman bear far than this , and submit not only to neglect but to unfaithfulness too but here this lady 's allegiance have fail she . her spirit rebel , and disown any more obedience . first she have to bear in secret the passion of lose the adore object ; then to get further initiation , and to find this worship be be but a clumsy idol : then to admit the silent truth , that it be she be superior , and not the monarch her master : that she have thought which his brain could never master , and be the well of the two ; quite separate from my lord although tie to he , and bind , as almost all people   save a very happy few , to work all her life alone . my lord sit in his chair , laugh his laugh , crack his joke , his face flush with wine my lady in her place over against he he never suspect that his superior be there , in the calm resign lady , cold of manner , with downcast eye . when he be merry in his cup , he would make joke about her coldness , and , " D - it , now my lady be go , we will have t'other bottle , " he would say . he be frank enough in tell his thought , such as they be . there be little mystery about my lord 's word or action . his Fair Rosamond do not live in a Labyrinth , like the lady of Mr. Addison 's opera , but parade with paint cheek and a tipsy retinue in the country town . have she a mind to be revenge , Lady Castlewood could have find the way to her rival 's house easily enough ; and , if she have come with bowl and dagger , would have be rout off the ground by the enemy with a volley of Billingsgate , which the fair person always keep by she . 
 meanwhile , it have be say , that for Harry Esmond his benefactress 's sweet face have lose none of its charm . it have always the kind of look and smile for he smile , not so gay and artless perhaps as those which Lady Castlewood have formerly wear , when , a child herself , play with her child , her husband 's pleasure and authority be all she think of ; but out of her grief and care , as will happen I think when these trial fall upon a kindly heart , and be not too unbearable , grow up a number of thought and excellence which have never come into existence , have not her sorrow and misfortune engender they . sure , occasion be the father of most that be good in we . as you have see the awkward finger and clumsy tool of a prisoner cut and fashion the most delicate little piece of carve work ; or achieve the most prodigious underground labor , and cut through wall of masonry , and see iron bar and fetter ; ' tis misfortune that awaken ingenuity , or fortitude , or endurance , in heart where these quality have never come to life but for the circumstance which give they a being . 
 " ' Twas after Jason leave she , no doubt , " Lady Castlewood once say with one of her smile to young Esmond   who be read to she a version of certain line out of euripide , " that Medea become a learn woman and a great enchantress . " 
 " and she could conjure the star out of heaven , " the young tutor add , " but she could not bring Jason back again . " 
 " what do you mean ? " ask my lady , very angry . 
 " indeed I mean nothing , " say the other , ' save what I have   read in book . what should I know about such matter ? I have see no woman save you and little beatrix , and the parson 's wife and my late mistress , and your ladyship 's woman here . " 
 " the man who write your book , " say my lady , " your Horaces , and ovid , and Virgils , as far as I know of they , all think ill of we , as all the hero they write about use we basely . we be breed to be slave always ; and even of our own time , as you be still the only lawgiver , I think our sermon seem to say that the good woman be she who bear her master 's chain most gracefully . ' tis a pity there be no nunnery permit by our church : beatrix and I would fly to one , and end our day in peace there away from you . " 
 " and be there no slavery in a convent ? " say Esmond . 
 " at least if woman be slave there , no one see they , " answer the lady . " they do not   work in street gang with the public to jeer they : and if they suffer , suffer in private . here come my lord home from hunting . take away the book . my lord do not love to see they . lesson be over for to - day , Mr. Tutor . " and with a curtsy and a smile she would end this sort of colloquy . 
 indeed " Mr. Tutor , " as my lady call Esmond , have now business enough on his hand in Castlewood house . he have three pupil , his lady and her two child , at whose lesson she would always be present ; besides write my lord 's letter , and arrange his accompt for he when these could be get from Esmond 's indolent patron . 
 of the pupil the two young people be but lazy scholar , and as my lady would admit no discipline such as be then in use , my lord 's son only learn what he like , which be but little , and never to his life 's end could be get to construe more than six line of Virgil . Mistress Beatrix chatter french prettily , from a very early age ; and sing sweetly , but this be from her mother 's teach not Harry Esmond 's , who could scarce distinguish between " Green Sleeves " and " Lillibullero ; " although he have no great delight in life than to hear the lady sing . he see they now   will he ever forget they ?   as they use to sit together of the summer evening the two golden head over the page the child 's little hand , and the mother 's beat the time , with their voice rise and fall in unison . 
 but if the child be careless , ' twas a wonder how eagerly the mother learn from her young tutor and teach he too . the happy instinctive faculty be this lady 's a faculty for discern latent beauty and hidden grace of book , especially book of poetry , as in a walk she would spy out field - flower and make posy of they , such as no other hand could . she be a critic , not by reason but by feel ; the sweet commentator of those book they read together ; and the happy hour of young Esmond 's life , perhaps , be those pass in the company of this kind mistress and her child . 
 these happy day be to end soon , however ; and it be by the Lady Castlewood 's own decree that they be bring to a conclusion . it happen about Christmas - time , Harry Esmond be now past sixteen year of age , that his old comrade , adversary , and friend , Tom Tusher , return from his school in London , a fair , well - grow , and sturdy lad , who be about to enter college , with an exhibition from his school , and a prospect of after promotion in the church . Tom Tusher 's talk be of nothing but Cambridge now ; and the boy , who be good friend , examine each other eagerly about their progress in book . Tom have learn some Greek and Hebrew , besides Latin , in which he be pretty well skilled , and also have give himself to mathematical study under his father 's guidance , who be a proficient in those science , of which esmond know nothing ; nor could he write Latin so well as Tom , though he could talk it well , have be teach by his dear friend the Jesuit Father , for whose memory the lad ever retain the warm affection , read his book , keep his sword clean in the little crypt where the Father have show they to Esmond on the night of his visit ; and often of a night sit in the chaplain 's room , which he inhabit , over his book , his verse , and rubbish , with which the lad occupy himself , he would look up at the window , think he wish it might open and let in the good Father . he have come and pass away like a dream ; but for the sword and book Harry might almost think the Father be an imagination of his mind and for two letter which have come to he , one from abroad , full of advice and affection , another soon after he have be confirm by the Bishop of Hexton , in which Father Holt deplore his fall away . but Harry Esmond feel so confident now of his be in the right , and of his own power as a casuist , that he think he be able to face the Father himself in argument , and possibly convert he . 
 to work upon the faith of her young pupil , Esmond 's kind mistress send to the library of her father the Dean , who have be distinguish in the dispute of the late king 's reign ; and , an old soldier now , have hang up his weapon of controversy . these he take down from his shelf willingly for young Esmond , whom he benefit by his own personal advice and instruction . it do not require much persuasion to induce the boy to worship with his beloved mistress . and the good old nonjure Dean flatter himself with a conversion which , in truth , be owe to a much gentle and fair persuader . 
 under her ladyship 's kind eye   my lord 's be seal in sleep pretty generally , Esmond read many volume of the work of the famous British Divines of the last age , and be familiar with Wake and Sherlock , with Stillingfleet and Patrick . his mistress never tired to listen or to read , to pursue the text with fond comment , to urge those point which her fancy dwelt on most , or her reason deem most important . since the death of her father the Dean , this lady hath admit a certain latitude of theological reading which her orthodox father would never have allow ; his favorite writer appeal more to reason and antiquity than to the passion or imagination of their reader , so that the work of Bishop Taylor , nay , those of Mr. Baxter and Mr. Law , have in reality find more favor with my Lady Castlewood than the severer volume of our great english schoolman . 
 in later life , at the University , Esmond reopen the controversy , and pursue it in a very different manner , when his patron have determine for he that he be to embrace the ecclesiastical life . but though his mistress 's heart be in this calling , his own never be much . after that first fervor of simple devotion , which his beloved Jesuit priest have inspire in he , speculative theology take but little hold upon the young man 's mind . when his early credulity be disturb , and his saint and virgin take out of his worship , to rank little high than the divinity of Olympus , his belief become acquiescence rather than ardor ; and he make his mind up to assume the cassock and band , as another man do to wear a breastplate and jack - boot , or to mount a merchant 's desk , for a livelihood , and from obedience and necessity , rather than from choice . there be score of such man in Mr. Esmond 's time at the university , who be go to the church with no well call than his . 
 when Thomas Tusher be go , a feeling of no small depression and disquiet fall upon young Esmond , of which , though he do not complain , his kind mistress must have divine the cause : for soon after she show not only that she understand the reason of Harry 's melancholy , but could provide a remedy for it . her habit be thus to watch , unobservedly , those to whom duty or affection bind she , and to prevent their design , or to fulfil they , when she have the power . it be this lady 's disposition to think kindness , and devise silent bounty and to scheme benevolence , for those about she . we take such goodness , for the most part , as if it be our due ; the Marys who bring ointment for our foot get but little thank . some of we never feel this devotion at all , or be move by it to gratitude or acknowledgment ; other only recall it year after , when the day be past in which those sweet kindness be spend on we , and we offer back our return for the debt by a poor tardy payment of tear . then forget tone of love recur to we , and kind glance shine out of the past oh so bright and clear ! oh so long after ! because they be out of reach ; as holiday music from withinside a prison wall or sunshine see through the bar ; more prized because unattainable more bright because of the contrast of present darkness and solitude , whence there be no escape . 
 all the notice , then , which Lady Castlewood seem to take of Harry Esmond 's melancholy , upon Tom Tusher 's departure , be , by a gayety unusual to she , to attempt to dispel his gloom . she make his three scholar   herself be the chief one   more cheerful than ever they have be before , and more docile , too , all of they learn and read much more than they have be accustom to do . " for who know , " say the lady , " what may happen , and whether we may be able to keep such a learn tutor long ? " 
 Frank Esmond say he for his part do not want to learn any more , and cousin Harry might shut up his book whenever he like , if he would come out a - fishing ; and little Beatrix declare she would send for Tom Tusher , and he would be glad enough to come to Castlewood , if Harry choose to go away . 
 at last come a messenger from Winchester one day , bearer of a letter , with a great black seal , from the Dean there , to say that his sister be dead , and have leave her fortune of 2,000l. among her six niece , the Dean 's daughter ; and many a time since have Harry Esmond recall the flushed face and eager look wherewith , after this intelligence , his kind lady regard he . she do not pretend to any grief about the deceased relative , from whom she and her family have be many year part . 
 when my lord hear of the news , he also do not make any very long face . " the money will come very handy to furnish the music - room and the cellar , which be get low , and buy your ladyship a coach and a couple of horse that will do indifferent to ride or for the coach . and , beatrix , you shall have a spinnet : and , Frank , you shall have a little horse from Hexton Fair ; and , Harry , you shall have five pound to buy some book , " say my lord , who be generous with his own , and indeed with other folk 's money . " I wish your aunt would die once a year , Rachel ; we could spend your money , and all your sister ' , too . " 
 " I have but one aunt and and I have another use for the money , my lord , " say my lady , turn very red . 
 " another use , my dear ; and what do you know about money ? " cry my lord . " and what the devil be there that I do not   give you which you want ! " 
 " I intend to give this money can not   you fancy how , my lord ? " 
 my lord swear one of his large oath that he do not know in the least what she mean . 
 " I intend it for Harry Esmond to go to college . Cousin Harry , " say my lady , " you must not   stay long in this dull place , but make a name to yourself , and for we too , Harry . " 
 " d n it , Harry be well enough here , " say my lord , for a moment look rather sulky . 
 " be Harry go away ? you do not   mean to say you will go away ? " cry out Frank and Beatrix at one breath . 
 " but he will come back : and this will always be his home , " cry my lady , with blue eye look a celestial kindness : " and his scholar will always love he ; will not   they ? " 
 " by G - d , Rachel , you be a good woman ! " say my lord , seize my lady 's hand , at which she blush very much , and shrink back , put her child before she . " I wish you joy , my kinsman , " he continue , give Harry Esmond a hearty slap on the shoulder . " I will not   balk your luck . go to Cambridge , boy , and when Tusher die you shall have the living here , if you be not well provide by that time . we will furnish the dining - room and buy the horse another year . I will give thee a nag out of the stable : take any one except my hack and the bay geld and the coach - horse ; and God speed thee , my boy ! " 
 " have the sorrel , Harry ; ' tis a good one . Father say ' tis the good in the stable , " say little Frank , clap his hand , and jump up . " let us come and see he in the stable . " and the other , in his delight and eagerness , be for leave the room that instant to arrange about his journey . 
 the Lady Castlewood look after he with sad penetrating glance . " he wish to be go already , my lord , " say she to her husband . 
 the young man hang back abash . " indeed , I would stay for ever , if your ladyship bid I , " he say . 
 " and thou wouldst be a fool for thy pain , kinsman , " say my lord . " Tut , tut , man . go and see the world . sow thy wild oat ; and take the good luck that Fate send thee . I wish I be a boy again , that I might go to college , and taste the Trumpington ale . " 
 " Ours , indeed , be but a dull home , " cry my lady , with a little of sadness and , maybe , of satire , in her voice : " an old glum house , half ruin , and the rest only half furnish ; a woman and two child be but poor company for man that be accustom to well . we be only fit to be your worship 's handmaid , and your pleasure must of necessity lie elsewhere than at home . " 
 " curse I , Rachel , if I know now whether thou art in earnest or not , " say my lord . 
 " in earnest , my lord ! " say she , still cling by one of her child . " be there much subject here for joke ? " and she make he a grand curtsy , and , give a stately look to Harry Esmond , which seem to say , " remember ; you understand I , though he do not , " she leave the room with her child . 
 " since she find out that confound Hexton business , " my lord say " and be hang to they that tell she ! she have not be the same woman . she , who use to be as humble as a milkmaid , be as proud as a princess , " say my lord . " take my counsel , Harry Esmond , and keep clear of woman . since I have have anything to do with the jade , they have give I nothing but disgust . I have a wife at Tangier , with whom , as she could not   speak a word of my language , you would have think I might lead a quiet life . but she try to poison I , because she be jealous of a Jew girl . there be your aunt , for aunt she be aunt Jezebel , a pretty life your father lead with she ! and here be my lady . when I see she on a pillion , rid behind the Dean her father , she look and be such a baby , that a sixpenny doll might have please she . and now you see what she be hand off , highty - tighty , high and mighty , an empress could not   be grander . pass we the tankard , Harry my boy . a mug of beer and a toast at morn , say my host . a toast and a mug of beer at noon , say my dear . D n it , Polly love a mug of ale , too , and lace with brandy , by Jove ! " indeed , I suppose they drink it together ; for my lord be often thick in his speech at mid - day dinner ; and at night at supper , speechless altogether . 
 Harry Esmond 's departure resolve upon , it seem as if the Lady Castlewood , too , rejoice to lose he ; for more than once , when the lad , ashamed perhaps at his own secret eagerness to go away   at any rate stricken with sadness at the idea of leave those from whom he have receive so many proof of love and kindness inestimable , try to express to his mistress his sense of gratitude to she , and his sorrow at quit those who have so sheltered and tend a nameless and houseless orphan , Lady Castlewood cut short his protest of love and his lamentation , and would hear of no grief , but only look forward to Harry 's fame and prospect in life . " our little legacy will keep you for four year like a gentleman . Heaven 's Providence , your own genius , industry , honor , must do the rest for you . Castlewood will always be a home for you ; and these child , whom you have teach and love , will not forget to love you . and , Harry , " say she   and this be the only time when she speak with a tear in her eye , or a tremor in her voice , " it may happen in the course of nature that I shall be call away from they : and their father and and they will need true friend and protector . promise I that you will be true to they as as I think I have be to you and a mother 's fond prayer and blessing go with you . " 
 " so help I God , madam , I will , " say Harry Esmond , fall on his knee , and kiss the hand of his dear mistress . " if you will have I stay now , I will . what matter whether or no I make my way in life , or whether a poor bastard die as unknown as he be now ? ' tis enough that I have your love and kindness surely ; and to make you happy be duty enough for I . " 
 " happy ! " say she ; " but indeed I ought to be , with my child , and " 
 " not happy ! " cry Esmond   for he know what her life be , though he and his mistress never speak a word concern it . " if not happiness , it may be ease . let I stay and work for you let I stay and be your servant . " 
 " indeed , you be well away , " say my lady , laugh , as she put her hand on the boy 's head for a moment . " you shall stay in no such dull place . you shall go to college and distinguish yourself as become your name . that be how you shall please I well ; and and if my child want you , or I want you , you shall come to we ; and I know we may count on you . " 
 " may heaven forsake I if you may not ! " Harry say , get up from his knee . 
 " and my knight long for a dragon this instant that he may fight , " say my lady , laughing ; which speech make Harry Esmond start , and turn red ; for indeed the very thought be in his mind , that he would like that some chance should immediately happen whereby he might show his devotion . and it please he to think that his lady have call he " her knight , " and often and often he recall this to his mind , and pray that he might be her true knight , too . 
 my lady 's bed - chamber window look out over the country , and you could see from it the purple hill beyond Castlewood village , the green common betwixt that and the Hall , and the old bridge which cross over the river . when Harry Esmond go away for Cambridge , little Frank run alongside his horse as far as the bridge , and there Harry stop for a moment , and look back at the house where the good part of his life have be pass . it lie before he with its gray familiar tower , a pinnacle or two shin in the sun , the buttress and terrace wall cast great blue shade on the grass . and Harry remember , all his life after , how he see his mistress at the window look out on he in a white robe , the little Beatrix 's chestnut curl rest at her mother 's side . both wave a farewell to he , and little Frank sob to leave he . yes , he would be his lady 's true knight , he vow in his heart ; he wave she an adieu with his hat . the village people have good - by to say to he too . all know that Master Harry be go to college , and most of they have a kind word and a look of farewell . I do not stop to say what adventure he begin to imagine , or what career to devise for himself before he have ride three mile from home . he have not read Monsieur Galland 's ingenious arabian tale as yet ; but be sure that there be other folk who build castle in the air , and have fine hope , and kick they down too , besides honest Alnaschar . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 I GO to CAMBRIDGE , and DO but little good there . 

 Mr lord , who say he should like to revisit the old haunt of his youth , kindly accompany Harry Esmond in his first journey to Cambridge . their road lie through London , where my Lord Viscount would also have Harry stay a few day to show he the pleasure of the town before he enter upon his university study , and whilst here Harry 's patron conduct the young man to my Lady Dowager 's house at Chelsey near London : the kind lady at Castlewood have specially order that the young gentleman and the old should pay a respectful visit in that quarter . 
 her ladyship the Viscountess Dowager occupy a handsome new house in Chelsey , with a garden behind it , and face the river , always a bright and animate sight with its swarm of sailor , barge , and wherry . Harry laugh at recognize in the parlor the well - remember old piece of Sir Peter Lely , wherein his father 's widow be represent as a virgin huntress , arm with a gilt bow - and - arrow , and encumber only with that small quantity of drapery which it would seem the virgin in King Charles 's day be accustom to wear . 
 my Lady Dowager have leave off this peculiar habit of huntress when she marry . but though she be now considerably past sixty year of age , I believe she think that airy nymph of the picture could still be easily recognize in the venerable personage who give an audience to Harry and his patron . 
 she receive the young man with even more favor than she show to the elder , for she choose to carry on the conversation in French , in which my Lord Castlewood be no great proficient , and express her satisfaction at find that Mr. Esmond could speak fluently in that language . " ' twas the only one fit for polite conversation , " she condescend to say , " and suitable to person of high breeding . " 
 my lord laugh afterwards , as the gentleman go away , at his kinswoman 's behavior . he say he remember the time when she could speak English fast enough , and joke in his jolly way at the loss he have have of such a lovely wife as that . 
 my Lady Viscountess deign to ask his lordship news of his wife and child ; she have hear that Lady Castlewood have have the small - pox ; she hop she be not so very much disfigure as people say . 
 at this remark about his wife 's malady , my Lord Viscount wince and turn red ; but the Dowager , in speak of the disfigurement of the young lady , turn to her look - glass and examine her old wrinkle countenance in it with such a grin of satisfaction , that it be all her guest could do to refrain from laugh in her ancient face . 
 she ask Harry what his profession be to be ; and my lord , say that the lad be to take order , and have the living of Castlewood when old Dr. Tusher vacate it , she do not seem to show any particular anger at the notion of Harry 's become a Church of England clergyman , nay , be rather glad than otherwise , that the youth should be so provide for . she bid Mr. Esmond not to forget to pay she a visit whenever he pass through London , and carry her graciousness so far as to send a purse with twenty guineas for he , to the tavern at which my lord put up   the " Greyhound , " in Charing Cross ; and , along with this welcome gift for her kinsman , she send a little doll for a present to my lord 's little daughter Beatrix , who be grow beyond the age of doll by this time , and be as tall almost as her venerable relative . 
 after see the town , and go to the play , my Lord Castlewood and Esmond ride together to Cambridge , spend two pleasant day upon the journey . those rapid new coach be not establish , as yet , that perform the whole journey between London and the University in a single day ; however , the road be pleasant and short enough to Harry Esmond , and he always gratefully remember that happy holiday which his kind patron give he . 
 Mr. Esmond be enter a pensioner of Trinity College in Cambridge , to which famous college my lord have also in his youth belong . Dr. Montague be master at this time , and receive my Lord Viscount with great politeness : so do Mr. Bridge , who be appoint to be Harry 's tutor . Tom Tusher , who be of Emanuel College , and be by this time a junior soph , come to wait upon my lord , and to take Harry under his protection ; and comfortable room be provide for he in the great court close by the gate , and near to the famous Mr. Newton 's lodging , Harry 's patron take leave of he with many kind word and blessing , and an admonition to he to behave well at the University than my lord himself have ever do . 
 ' tis needless in these memoir to go at any length into the particular of Harry Esmond 's college career . it be like that of a hundred young gentleman of that day . but he have the ill fortune to be old by a couple of year than most of his fellow - student ; and by his previous solitary mode of bring up , the circumstance of his life , and the peculiar thoughtfulness and melancholy that have naturally engender , he be , in a great measure , cut off from the society of comrade who be much young and high - spirited than he . his tutor , who have bow down to the ground , as he walk my lord over the college grass - plat , change his behavior as soon as the nobleman 's back be turn , and be at least Harry think so harsh and overbearing . when the lad use to assemble in their grege in hall , Harry find himself alone in the midst of that little flock of boy ; they raise a great laugh at he when he be set on to read Latin , which he do with the foreign pronunciation teach to he by his old master , the Jesuit , than which he know no other . Mr. Bridge , the tutor , make he the object of clumsy joke , in which he be fond of indulge . the young man 's spirit be chafe , and his vanity mortify ; and he find himself , for some time , as lonely in this place as ever he have be at Castlewood , whither he long to return . his birth be a source of shame to he , and he fancy a hundred slight and sneer from young and old , who , no doubt , have treat he well have he meet they himself more frankly . and as he look back , in calm day , upon this period of his life , which he think so unhappy , he can see that his own pride and vanity cause no small part of the mortification which he attribute to other 's ill will . the world deal good - naturedly with good - natured people , and I never know a sulky misanthropist who quarrel with it , but it be he , and not it , that be in the wrong . Tom Tusher give Harry plenty of good advice on this subject , for Tom have both good sense and good humor ; but Mr. Harry choose to treat his senior with a great deal of superfluous disdain and absurd scorn , and would by no mean part from his darling injury , in which , very likely , no man believe but himself . as for honest Doctor Bridge , the tutor find , after a few trial of wit with the pupil , that the young man be an ugly subject for wit , and that the laugh be often turn against he . this do not make tutor and pupil any well friend ; but have , so far , an advantage for Esmond , that Mr. Bridge be induce to leave he alone ; and so long as he keep his chapel , and do the college exercise require of he , Bridge be content not to see Harry 's glum face in his class , and to leave he to read and sulk for himself in his own chamber . 
 a poem or two in Latin and English , which be pronounce to have some merit , and a latin oration ,   for Mr. Esmond could write that language well than pronounce it ,   get he a little reputation both with the authority of the University and amongst the young man , with whom he begin to pass for more than he be worth . a few victory over their common enemy , Mr. Bridge , make they incline towards he , and look upon he as the champion of their order against the senior . such of the lad as he take into his confidence find he not so gloomy and haughty as his appearance lead they to believe ; and Don Dismallo , as he be call , become presently a person of some little importance in his college , and be , as he believe , set down by the senior there as rather a dangerous character . 
 Don Dismallo be a staunch young Jacobite , like the rest of his family ; give himself many absurd air of loyalty ; use to invite young friend to Burgundy , and give the King 's health on King James 's birthday ; wear black on the day of his abdication ; fast on the anniversary of King William 's coronation ; and perform a thousand absurd antic , of which he smile now to think . 
 these folly cause many remonstrance on Tom Tusher 's part , who be always a friend to the power that be , as Esmond be always in opposition to they . Tom be a Whig , while Esmond be a tory . Tom never miss a lecture , and cap the proctor with the profound of bow . no wonder he sigh over Harry 's insubordinate course , and be angry when the other laugh at he . but that Harry be know to have my Lord Viscount 's protection , Tom no doubt would have break with he altogether . but honest Tom never give up a comrade as long as he be the friend of a great man . this be not out of scheme on Tom 's part , but a natural inclination towards the great . ' twa no hypocrisy in he to flat , but the bent of his mind , which be always perfectly good - humor , obliging , and servile . 
 Harry have very liberal allowance , for his dear mistress of Castlewood not only regularly supply he , but the Dowager of Chelsey make her donation annual , and receive Esmond at her house near London every Christmas ; but , in spite of these benefaction , Esmond be constantly poor ; whilst ' twa a wonder with how small a stipend from his father Tom Tusher contrive to make a good figure . ' tis true that Harry both spend , give , and lend his money very freely , which Thomas never do . I think he be like the famous Duke of Marlborough in this instance , who , get a present of fifty piece , when a young man , from some foolish woman who fall in love with his good look , show the money to Cadogan in a drawer score of year after , where it have lain ever since he have sell his beardless honor to procure it . I do not mean to say that Tom ever let out his good look so profitably , for nature have not endow he with any particular charm of person , and he ever be a pattern of moral behavior , lose no opportunity of give the very good advice to his young comrade ; with which article , to do he justice , he part very freely . not but that he be a merry fellow , too , in his way ; he love a joke , if by good fortune he understand it , and take his share generously of a bottle if another pay for it , and especially if there be a young lord in company to drink it . in these case there be not a hard drinker in the University than Mr. Tusher could be ; and it be edify to behold he , fresh shave and with smug face , sing out " amen ! " at early chapel in the morning . in his reading , poor Harry permit himself to go a - gad after all the Nine Muses , and so very likely have but little favor from any one of they ; whereas Tom Tusher , who have no more turn for poetry than a ploughboy , nevertheless , by a dogged perseverance and obsequiousness in court the divine Calliope , get himself a prize , and some credit in the University , and a fellowship at his college , as a reward for his scholarship . in this time of Mr. Esmond 's life , he get the little reading which he ever could boast of , and pass a good part of his day greedily devour all the book on which he could lay hand . in this desultory way the work of most of the English , French , and italian poet come under his eye , and he have a smattering of the spanish tongue likewise , besides the ancient language , of which , at least of Latin , he be a tolerable master . 
 then , about midway in his University career , he fall to read for the profession to which worldly prudence rather than inclination call he , and be perfectly bewildered in theological controversy . in the course of his reading   which be neither pursue with that seriousness or that devout mind which such a study require   the youth find himself at the end of one month a Papist , and be about to proclaim his faith ; the next month a protestant , with Chillingworth ; and the third a sceptic , with Hobbes and Bayle . whereas honest Tom Tusher never permit his mind to stray out of the prescribed University path , accept the thirty - nine Articles with all his heart , and would have sign and swear to other nine - and - thirty with entire obedience . Harry 's wilfulness in this matter , and disorderly thought and conversation , so shocked and afflict his senior , that there grow up a coldness and estrangement between they , so that they become scarce more than mere acquaintance , from have be intimate friend when they come to college first . politic run high , too , at the University ; and here , also , the young man be at variance . Tom profess himself , albeit a high - churchman , a strong King William's - man ; whereas Harry bring his family Tory politic to college with he , to which he must add a dangerous admiration for Oliver Cromwell , whose side , or King James 's by turn , he often choose to take in the dispute which the young gentleman use to hold in each other 's room , where they debate on the state of the nation , crown and depose king , and toast past and present hero and beauty in flagon of college ale . 
 thus , either from the circumstance of his birth , or the natural melancholy of his disposition , Esmond come to live very much by himself during his stay at the University , have neither ambition enough to distinguish himself in the college career , nor care to mingle with the mere pleasure and boyish frolic of the student , who be , for the most part , two or three year young than he . he fancy that the gentleman of the common - room of his college slight he on account of his birth , and hence keep aloof from their society . it may be that he make the ill will , which he imagine come from they , by his own behavior , which , as he look back on it in after life , he now see be morose and haughty . at any rate , he be as tenderly grateful for kindness as he be susceptible of slight and wrong ; and , lonely as he be generally , yet have one or two very warm friendship for his companion of those day . 
 one of these be a queer gentleman that reside in the University , though he be no member of it , and be the professor of a science scarce recognize in the common course of college education . this be a french refugee - officer , who have be drive out of his native country at the time of the protestant persecution there , and who come to Cambridge , where he teach the science of the small - sword , and set up a saloon - of - arm . though he declare himself a protestant , ' twa say Mr. Moreau be a Jesuit in disguise ; indeed , he bring very strong recommendation to the Tory party , which be pretty strong in that University , and very likely be one of the many agent whom King James have in this country . esmond find this gentleman 's conversation very much more agreeable and to his taste than the talk of the college divine in the common - room ; he never weary of Moreau 's story of the war of Turenne and Conde , in which he have bear a part ; and be familiar with the french tongue from his youth , and in a place where but few speak it , his company become very agreeable to the brave old professor of arm , whose favorite pupil he be , and who make Mr. Esmond a very tolerable proficient in the noble science of escrime . 
 at the next term Esmond be to take his degree of Bachelor of Arts , and afterwards , in proper season , to assume the cassock and band which his fond mistress would have he wear . Tom Tusher himself be a parson and a fellow of his college by this time ; and Harry feel that he would very gladly cede his right to the living of Castlewood to Tom , and that his own calling be in no way to the pulpit . but as he be bind , before all thing in the world , to his dear mistress at home , and know that a refusal on his part would grieve she , he determine to give she no hint of his unwillingness to the clerical office : and it be in this unsatisfactory mood of mind that he go to spend the last vacation he should have at Castlewood before he take order . 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 I come home for a HOLIDAY to castlewood , and FIND a skeleton in the HOUSE . 
 at his third long vacation , Esmond come as usual to Castlewood , always feel an eager thrill of pleasure when he find himself once more in the house where he have pass so many year , and behold the kind familiar eye of his mistress look upon he . she and her child   out of whose company she scarce ever see he   come to greet he . Miss Beatrix be grow so tall that Harry do not quite know whether he might kiss she or no ; and she blush and hold back when he offer that salutation , though she take it , and even court it , when they be alone . the young lord be shoot up to be like his gallant father in look , though with his mother 's kind eye : the lady of Castlewood herself seem grown , too , since Harry see she in her look more stately , in her person full , in her face still as ever most tender and friendly , a great air of command and decision than have appear in that guileless sweet countenance which Harry remember so gratefully . the tone of her voice be so much deep and sad when she speak and welcome he , that it quite startled Esmond , who look up at her surprised as she speak , when she withdraw her eye from he ; nor do she ever look at he afterwards when his own eye be gaze upon she . a something hint at grief and secret , and fill his mind with alarm undefinable , seem to speak with that low thrilling voice of her , and look out of those clear sad eye . her greeting to Esmond be so cold that it almost pain the lad ,   who would have like to fall on his knee , and kiss the skirt of her robe , so fond and ardent be his respect and regard for she ,   and he falter in answer the question which she , hesitate on her side , begin to put to he . be he happy at Cambridge ? do he study too hard ? she hop not . he have grow very tall , and look very well . 
 " he have get a moustache ! " cry out Master Esmond . 
 " why do he not wear a peruke like my Lord Mohun ? " ask Miss Beatrix . " my lord say that nobody wear their own hair . " 
 " I believe you will have to occupy your old chamber , " say my lady . " I hope the housekeeper have get it ready . " 
 " why , mamma , you have be there ten time these three day yourself ! " exclaim Frank . 
 " and she cut some flower which you plant in my garden do you remember , ever so many year ago ? when I be quite a little girl , " cry out Miss Beatrix , on tiptoe . " and mamma put they in your window . " 
 " I remember when you grow well after you be ill that you use to like rose , " say the lady , blush like one of they . they all conduct Harry Esmond to his chamber ; the child run before , Harry walk by his mistress hand - in - hand . 
 the old room have be ornament and beautify not a little to receive he . the flower be in the window in a china vase ; and there be a fine new counterpane on the bed , which chatterbox Beatrix say mamma have make too . a fire be crackle on the hearth , although it be June . my lady think the room want warming ; everything be do to make he happy and welcome : " and you be not to be a page any long , but a gentleman and kinsman , and to walk with papa and mamma , " say the child . and as soon as his dear mistress and child have leave he to himself , it be with a heart overflow with love and gratefulness that he fling himself down on his knee by the side of the little bed , and ask a blessing upon those who be so kind to he . 
 the child , who be always house tell - tale , soon make he acquaint with the little history of the house and family . Papa have be to London twice . Papa often go away now . Papa have take beatrix to Westlands , where she be tall than Sir George Harper 's second daughter , though she be two year old . Papa have take Beatrix and Frank both to Bellminster , where Frank have get the well of Lord Bellminster 's son in a boxing - match my lord , laughing , tell Harry afterwards . many gentleman come to stop with papa , and papa have get a new game from London , a french game , call a billiard that the french king play it very well : and the Dowager Lady Castlewood have send Miss Beatrix a present ; and papa have get a new chaise , with two little horse , which he drive himself , beside the coach , which mamma go in ; and Dr. Tusher be a cross old plague , and they do not like to learn from he at all ; and papa do not care about they learn , and laugh when they be at their book , but mamma like they to learn , and teach they ; and " I do not   think papa be fond of mamma , " say Miss Beatrix , with her great eye . she have come quite close up to Harry Esmond by the time this prattle take place , and be on his knee , and have examine all the point of his dress , and all the good or bad feature of his homely face . 
 " you should not   say that papa be not fond of mamma , " say the boy , at this confession . " Mamma never say so ; and mamma forbid you to say it , Miss Beatrix . " 
 ' twas this , no doubt , that account for the sadness in Lady Castlewood 's eye , and the plaintive vibration of her voice . who do not know of eye , light by love once , where the flame shin no more ? of lamp extinguish , once properly trim and tend ? every man have such in his house . such memento make our splendidest chamber look blank and sad ; such face see in a day cast a gloom upon our sunshine . so oath mutually swear , and invocation of heaven , and priestly ceremony , and fond belief , and love , so fond and faithful that it never doubt but that it should live for ever , be all of no avail towards make love eternal : it die , in spite of the bann and the priest ; and I have often think there should be a visitation of the sick for it , and a funeral service , and an extreme unction , and an abi in pace . it have its course , like all mortal thing its beginning , progress , and decay . it bud and it bloom out into sunshine , and it wither and end . Strephon and Chloe languish apart ; join in a rapture : and presently you hear that Chloe be cry , and Strephon have break his crook across her back . can you mend it so as to show no mark of rupture ? not all the priest of Hymen , not all the incantation to the god , can make it whole ! 
 wake up from dream , book , and vision of college honor , in which for two year , Harry Esmond have be immerse , he find himself , instantly , on his return home , in the midst of this actual tragedy of life , which absorb and interest he more than all his tutor have teach he . the person whom he love well in the world , and to whom he owe most , be live unhappily together . the gentle and kind of woman be suffer ill usage and shed tear in secret : the man who make she wretche by neglect , if not by violence , be Harry 's benefactor and patron . in house where , in place of that sacred , inmost flame of love , there be discord at the centre , the whole household become hypocritical , and each lie to his neighbor . the husband   or it may be the wife   lie when the visitor come in , and wear a grin of reconciliation or politeness before he . the wife lie   indeed , her business be to do that , and to smile , however much she be beat , swallow her tear , and lie to her lord and master ; lie in bid little Jackey respect dear papa ; lie in assure grandpapa that she be perfectly happy . the servant lie , wear grave face behind their master 's chair , and pretend to be unconscious of the fighting ; and so , from morning till bedtime , life be pass in falsehood . and wiseacre call this a proper regard of moral , and point out Baucis and Philemon as example of a good life . 
 if my lady do not speak of her grief to Harry Esmond , my lord be by no mean reserve when in his cup , and speak his mind very freely , bid Harry in his coarse way , and with his blunt language , beware of all woman as cheat , jade , jilt , and use other unmistakable monosyllable in speak of they . indeed , ' twa the fashion of the day , as I must own ; and there be not a writer of my time of any note , with the exception of poor Dick Steele , that do not speak of a woman as of a slave , and scorn and use she as such . Mr. Pope , Mr. Congreve , Mr. Addison , Mr. Gay , every one of them , sing in this key , each accord to his nature and politeness , and loud and fouler than all in abuse be Dr. Swift , who speak of they as he treat they , bad of all . 
 much of the quarrel and hatred which arise between married people come in my mind from the husband 's rage and revolt at discover that his slave and bedfellow , who be to minister to all his wish , and be church - swear to honor and obey he be his superior ; and that he , and not she , ought to be the subordinate of the twain ; and in these controversy , I think , lie the cause of my lord 's anger against his lady . when he leave she , she begin to think for herself , and her thought be not in his favor . after the illumination , when the love - lamp be put out that anon we speak of , and by the common daylight we look at the picture , what a daub it look ! what a clumsy effigy ! how many man and wife come to this knowledge , think you ? and if it be painful to a woman to find herself mat for life to a boor , and order to love and honor a dullard ; it be bad still for the man himself perhaps , whenever in his dim comprehension the idea dawn that his slave and drudge yonder be , in truth , his superior ; that the woman who do his bidding , and submit to his humor , should be his lord ; that she can think a thousand thing beyond the power of his muddled brain ; and that in yonder head , on the pillow opposite to he , lie a thousand feeling , mystery of thought , latent scorn and rebellion , whereof he only dimly perceive the existence as they look out furtively from her eye : treasure of love doom to perish without a hand to gather they ; sweet fancy and image of beauty that would grow and unfold themselves into flower ; bright wit that would shine like diamond could it be bring into the sun : and the tyrant in possession crush the outbreak of all these , drive they back like slave into the dungeon and darkness , and chafe without that his prisoner be rebellious , and his sworn subject undutiful and refractory . so the lamp be out in Castlewood Hall , and the lord and lady there see each other as they be . with her illness and alter beauty my lord 's fire for his wife disappear ; with his selfishness and faithlessness her foolish fiction of love and reverence be rent away . love ! who be to love what be base and unlovely ?
