respect ! who be to respect what be gross and sensual ? not all the marriage oath swear before all the parson , cardinal , minister , muftis , and rabbin in the world , can bind to that monstrous allegiance . this couple be live apart then ; the woman happy to be allow to love and tend her child   who be never of her own good - will away from she , and thankful to have save such treasure as these out of the wreck in which the well part of her heart go down . 
 these young one have have no instructor save their mother , and Doctor Tusher for their theology occasionally , and have make more progress than might have be expect under a tutor so indulgent and fond as Lady Castlewood . beatrix could sing and dance like a nymph . her voice be her father 's delight after dinner . she rule over the house with little imperial way , which her parent coax and laugh at . she have long learn the value of her bright eye , and try experiment in coquetry , in corpore vili , upon rustic and country squire , until she should prepare to conquer the world and the fashion . she put on a new ribbon to welcome Harry Esmond , make eye at he , and direct her young smile at he , not a little to the amusement of the young man , and the joy of her father , who laugh his great laugh , and encourage she in her thousand antic . Lady Castlewood watch the child gravely and sadly : the little one be pert in her reply to her mother , yet eager in her protestation of love and promise of amendment ; and as ready to cry   after a little quarrel bring on by her own giddiness   until she have win back her mamma 's favor , as she be to risk the kind lady 's displeasure by fresh outbreak of restless vanity . from her mother 's sad look she flee to her father 's chair and boozy laughter . she already set the one against the other : and the little rogue delight in the mischief which she know how to make so early . 
 the young heir of Castlewood be spoil by father and mother both . he take their caress as man do , and as if they be his right . he have his hawk and his spaniel dog , his little horse and his beagle . he have learn to ride , and to drink , and to shoot fly : and he have a small court , the son of the huntsman and woodman , as become the heir - apparent , take after the example of my lord his father . if he have a headache , his mother be as much frightened as if the plague be in the house : my lord laugh and jeered in his abrupt way   indeed , ' twa on the day after New Year 's day , and an excess of mince - pie   and say with some of his usual oath " D n it , Harry Esmond you see how my lady take on about Frank 's megrim . she use to be sorry about I , my boy   pass the tankard , Harry , and to be frightened if I have a headache once . she do not   care about my head now . they be like that woman be all the same , Harry , all jilt in their heart . Stick to college stick to punch and buttery ale : and never see a woman that be handsomer than an old cinder - face bed - maker . that be my counsel . " 
 it be my lord 's custom to fle out many joke of this nature , in presence of his wife and child , at meal clumsy sarcasm which my lady turn many a time , or which , sometimes , she affect not to hear , or which now and again would hit their mark and make the poor victim wince   as you could see by her flush face and eye fill with tear , or which again work she up to anger and retort , when , in answer to one of these heavy bolt , she would flash back with a quivering reply . the pair be not happy ; nor indeed be it happy to be with they . alas that youthful love and truth should end in bitterness and bankruptcy ! to see a young couple love each other be no wonder ; but to see an old couple love each other be the good sight of all . Harry Esmond become the confidant of one and the other that be , my lord tell the lad all his grief and wrong   which be indeed of Lord Castlewood 's own making , and Harry divine my lady 's ; his affection lead he easily to penetrate the hypocrisy under which Lady Castlewood generally choose to go disguised , and see her heart ache whilst her face wear a smile . ' tis a hard task for woman in life , that mask which the world bid they wear . but there be no great crime than for a woman who be ill used and unhappy to show that she be so . the world be quite relentless about bid she to keep a cheerful face ; and our woman , like the Malabar wife , be force to go smile and paint to sacrifice themselves with their husband ; their relation be the most eager to push they on to their duty , and , under their shout and applause , to smother and hush their cry of pain . 
 so , into the sad secret of his patron 's household , Harry Esmond become initiate , he scarce know how . it have pass under his eye two year before , when he could not understand it ; but read , and think , and experience of man , have oldene he ; and one of the deep sorrow of a life which have never , in truth , be very happy , come upon he now , when he be compel to understand and pity a grief which he stand quite powerless to relieve . 
 it hath be say my lord would never take the oath of allegiance , nor his seat as a peer of the kingdom of Ireland , where , indeed , he have but a nominal estate ; and refuse an english peerage which King William 's government offer he as a bribe to secure his loyalty . 
 he might have accept this , and would doubtless , but for the earnest remonstrance of his wife , who rule her husband 's opinion well than she could govern his conduct , and who be a simple - hearted woman , with but one rule of faith and right , never think of swerve from her fidelity to the exiled family , or of recognize any other sovereign but King James ; and though she acquiesce in the doctrine of obedience to the reign power , no temptation , she think , could induce she to acknowledge the Prince of Orange as rightful monarch , nor to let she lord so acknowledge he . so my Lord Castlewood remain a nonjuror all his life nearly , though his self - denial cause he many a pang , and leave he sulky and out of humor . 
 the year after the Revolution , and all through King William 's life , ' tis know there be constant intrigue for the restoration of the exile family ; but if my Lord Castlewood take any share of these , as be probable , ' twa only for a short time , and when Harry Esmond be too young to be introduce into such important secret . 
 but in the year 1695 , when that conspiracy of Sir John Fenwick , Colonel Lowick , and other , be set on foot , for waylay King William as he come from Hampton Court to London , and a secret plot be form , in which a vast number of the nobility and people of honor be engage , Father Holt appear at Castlewood , and bring a young friend with he , a gentleman whom ' twa easy to see that both my lord and the Father treat with uncommon deference . Harry Esmond see this gentleman , and know and recognize he in after life , as shall be show in its place ; and he have little doubt now that my Lord Viscount be implicate somewhat in the transaction which always keep Father Holt employ and travel hither and thither under a dozen of different name and disguise . the Father 's companion go by the name of Captain James ; and it be under a very different name and appearance that Harry Esmond afterwards see he . 
 it be the next year that the Fenwick conspiracy blow up , which be a matter of public history now , and which end in the execution of Sir John and many more , who suffer manfully for their treason , and who be attend to Tyburn by my lady 's father Dean Armstrong , Mr. Collier , and other stout nonjure clergyman , who absolve they at the gallow - foot . 
 ' tis know that when Sir John be apprehend , discovery be make of a great number of name of gentleman engage in the conspiracy ; when , with a noble wisdom and clemency , the Prince burn the list of conspirator furnish to he , and say he would know no more . now it be after this that Lord Castlewood swear his great oath , that he would never , so help he heaven , be engage in any transaction against that brave and merciful man ; and so he tell Holt when the indefatigable priest visit he , and would have have he engage in a farth conspiracy . after this my lord ever speak of King William as he be as one of the wise , the brave , and the great of man . my Lady Esmond   for her part   say she could never pardon the king , first , for oust his father - in - law from his throne , and secondly , for not be constant to his wife , the Princess Mary . indeed , I think if Nero be to rise again , and be king of England , and a good family man , the lady would pardon he . my lord laugh at his wife 's objection the standard of virtue do not fit he much . 
 the last conference which Mr. Holt have with his lordship take place when Harry be come home for his first vacation from college   Harry see his old tutor but for a half - hour , and exchange no private word with he , and their talk , whatever it might be , leave my Lord Viscount very much disturb in mind so much so , that his wife , and his young kinsman , Henry Esmond , could not but observe his disquiet . after Holt be go , my lord rebuff Esmond , and again treat he with the great deference ; he shun his wife 's question and company , and look at his child with such a face of gloom and anxiety , muttering , " poor child poor child ! " in a way that could not but fill those whose life it be to watch he and obey he with great alarm . for which gloom , each person interested in the Lord Castlewood , frame in his or her own mind an interpretation . 
 my lady , with a laugh of cruel bitterness say , " I suppose the person at Hexton have be ill , or have scold he "   for my lord 's infatuation about Mrs. Marwood be know only too well . Young Esmond fear for his money affair , into the condition of which he have be initiate ; and that the expense , always great than his revenue , have cause Lord Castlewood disquiet . 
 one of the cause why my Lord Viscount have take young Esmond into his special favor be a trivial one , that hath not before be mention , though it be a very lucky accident in Henry Esmond 's life . a very few month after my lord 's come to Castlewood , in the winter time the little boy , be a child in a petticoat , trot about it happen that little Frank be with his father after dinner , who fall asleep over his wine , heedless of the child , who crawl to the fire ; and , as good fortune would have it , Esmond be send by his mistress for the boy just as the poor little scream urchin 's coat be set on fire by a log ; when Esmond , rush forward , tear the dress off the infant , so that his own hand be burn more than the child 's , who be frighten rather than hurt by this accident . but certainly ' twa providential that a resolute person should have come in at that instant , or the child have be burn to death probably , my lord sleep very heavily after drinking , and not wake so cool as a man should who have a danger to face . 
 ever after this the father , loud in his expression of remorse and humility for be a tipsy good - for - nothing , and of admiration for Harry Esmond , whom his lordship would style a hero for do a very trifling service , have the tender regard for his son 's preserver , and Harry become quite as one of the family . his burn be tend with the great care by his kind mistress , who say that heaven have send he to be the guardian of her child , and that she would love he all her life . 
 and it be after this , and from the very great love and tenderness which have grow up in this little household , rather than from the exhortation of Dean Armstrong   though these have no small weight with he , that Harry come to be quite of the religion of his house and his dear mistress , of which he have ever since be a professing member . as for Dr. Tusher 's boast that he be the cause of this conversion even in these young day Mr. Esmond have such a contempt for the Doctor , that have Tusher bade he believe anything   which he do not never meddle at all , Harry would that instant have question the truth on't . 
 my lady seldom drink wine ; but on certain day of the year , such as birthday   poor Harry have never a one   and anniversary , she take a little ; and this day , the 29th December , be one . at the end , then , of this year , ' 96 , it might have be a fortnight after Mr. Holt 's last visit , Lord Castlewood be still very gloomy in mind , and sit at table my lady bid a servant bring she a glass of wine , and look at her husband with one of her sweet smile , say 
 " my lord , will you not fill a bumper too , and let I call a toast ? " 
 " what be it , Rachel ? " say he , hold out his empty glass to be fill . 
 " ' tis the 29th of December , " say my lady , with her fond look of gratitude : " and my toast be , ' Harry and God bless he , who save my boy 's life ! ' " 
 my lord look at Harry hard , and drink the glass , but clap it down on the table in a moment , and , with a sort of groan , rise up , and go out of the room . what be the matter ? we all know that some great grief be over he . 
 whether my lord 's prudence have make he rich , or legacy have fall to he , which enable he to support a great establishment than that frugal one which have be too much for his small mean , Harry Esmond know not ; but the house of Castlewood be now on a scale much more costly than it have be during the first year of his lordship 's come to the title . there be more horse in the stable and more servant in the hall , and many more guest come and go now than formerly , when it be find difficult enough by the strict economy to keep the house as befit one of his lordship 's rank , and the estate out of debt . and it do not require very much penetration to find that many of the new acquaintance at Castlewood be not agreeable to the lady there : not that she ever treat they or any mortal with anything but courtesy ; but they be person who could not be welcome to she ; and whose society a lady so refined and reserved could scarce desire for her child . there come fuddle squire from the country round , who bawl their song under her window and drink themselves tipsy with my lord 's punch and ale : there come officer from Hexton , in whose company our little lord be make to hear talk and to drink , and swear too , in a way that make the delicate lady tremble for her son . esmond try to console she by say what he know of his College experience ; that with this sort of company and conversation a man must fall in soon or later in his course through the world : and it matter very little whether he hear it at twelve year old or twenty the youth who quit mother 's apron - string the late be not uncommonly the wild rake . but it be about her daughter that Lady Castlewood be the most anxious , and the danger which she think menace the little beatrix from the indulgence which her father give she ,   it must be own that my lord , since these unhappy domestic difference especially , be at once violent in his language to the child when angry , as he be too familiar , not to say coarse , when he be in a good humor ,   and from the company into which the careless lord bring the child . 
 not very far off from Castlewood be Sark Castle , where the Marchioness of Sark live , who be know to have be a mistress of the late King Charles and to this house , whither indeed a great part of the country gentry go , my lord insist upon go , not only himself , but on take his little daughter and son , to play with the child there . the child be nothing loth , for the house be splendid , and the welcome kind enough . but my lady , justly no doubt , think that the child of such a mother as that note Lady Sark have be , could be no good company for her two ; and speak her mind to her lord . his own language when he be thwart be not indeed of the gentle : to be brief , there be a family dispute on this , as there have be on many other point and the lady be not only force to give in , for the other 's will be law nor could she , on account of their tender age , tell her child what be the nature of her objection to their visit of pleasure , or indeed mention to they any objection at all but she have the additional secret mortification to find they return delighted with their new friend , load with present from they , and eager to be allow to go back to a place of such delight as Sark Castle . every year she think the company there would be more dangerous to her daughter , as from a child beatrix grow to a woman , and her daily increase beauty , and many fault of character too , expand . 
 it be Harry Esmond 's lot to see one of the visit which the old Lady of Sark pay to the Lady of Castlewood Hall : whither she come in state with six chestnut horse and blue ribbon , a page on each carriage step , a gentleman of the horse , and armed servant rid before and behind she . and , but that it be unpleasant to see Lady Castlewood 's face , it be amusing to watch the behavior of the two enemy : the frigid patience of the young lady , and the unconquerable good - humor of the elder who would see no offence whatever her rival intend , and who never cease to smile and to laugh , and to coax the child , and to pay compliment to every man , woman , child , nay dog , or chair and table , in Castlewood , so bent be she upon admire everything there . she laud the child , and wish as indeed she well might that her own family have be bring up as well as those cherub . she have never see such a complexion as dear Beatrix 's though to be sure she have a right to it from father and mother Lady Castlewood 's be indeed a wonder of freshness , and Lady Sark sigh to think she have not be bear a fair woman ; and remark Harry Esmond , with a fascinating superannuated smile , she compliment he on his wit , which she say she could see from his eye and forehead ; and vow that she would never have HIM at Sark until her daughter be out of the way . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 MY LORD MOHUN come among US for no good . 

 there have ride along with this old Princess 's cavalcade , two gentleman : her son , my Lord Firebrace , and his friend , my Lord Mohun , who both be greet with a great deal of cordiality by the hospitable Lord of Castlewood . my Lord Firebrace be but a feeble - minded and weak - limbed young nobleman , small in stature and limit in understanding to judge from the talk young Esmond have with he ; but the other be a person of a handsome presence , with the bel air , and a bright daring warlike aspect , which , accord to the chronicle of those day , have already achieve for he the conquest of several beauty and toast . he have fight and conquer in France , as well as in Flanders ; he have serve a couple of campaign with the Prince of Baden on the Danube , and witness the rescue of Vienna from the Turk . and he speak of his military exploit pleasantly , and with the manly freedom of a soldier , so as to delight all his hearer at Castlewood , who be little accustomed to meet a companion so agreeable . 
 on the first day this noble company come , my lord would not hear of their departure before dinner , and carry away the gentleman to amuse they , whilst his wife be leave to do the honor of her house to the old Marchioness and her daughter within . they look at the stable where my Lord Mohun praise the horse , though there be but a poor show there : they walk over the old house and garden , and fight the siege of Oliver 's time over again : they play a game of racket in the old court , where my Lord Castlewood beat my Lord Mohun , who say he love ball of all thing , and would quickly come back to Castlewood for his revenge . after dinner they play bowl and drink punch in the green alley ; and when they part they be swear friend , my Lord Castlewood kiss the other lord before he mount on horseback , and pronounce he the good companion he have meet for many a long day . all night long , over his tobacco - pipe , Castlewood do not cease to talk to Harry Esmond in praise of his new friend , and in fact do not leave off speak of he until his lordship be so tipsy that he could not speak plainly any more . 
 at breakfast next day it be the same talk renew ; and when my lady say there be something free in the Lord Mohun 's look and manner of speech which cause she to mistrust he , her lord burst out with one of his laugh and oath ; say that he never like man , woman , or beast , but what she be sure to be jealous of it ; that Mohun be the prettiest fellow in England ; that he hop to see more of he whilst in the country ; and that he would let Mohun know what my Lady Prude say of he . 
 " indeed , " Lady Castlewood say , " I like his conversation well enough . ' tis more amusing than that of most people I know . I think it , I own , too free ; not from what he say , as rather from what he imply . " 
 " Psha ! your ladyship do not know the world , " say her husband ; " and you have always be as squeamish as when you be a miss of fifteen . " 
 " you find no fault when I be a miss at fifteen . " 
 " Begad , madam , you be grow too old for a pinafore now ; and I hold that ' tis for I to judge what company my wife shall see , " say my lord , slap the table . 
 " indeed , Francis , I never think otherwise , " answer my lady , rise and drop he a curtsy , in which stately action , if there be obedience , there be defiance too ; and in which a bystander , deeply interested in the happiness of that pair as Harry Esmond be , might see how hopelessly separate they be ; what a great gulf of difference and discord have run between they . 
 " by G - d ! Mohun be the good fellow in England ; and I will invite he here , just to plague that woman . do you ever see such a frigid insolence as it be , Harry ? that be the way she treat I , " he break out , storm , and his face grow red as he clench his fist and go on . " I be nobody in my own house . I be to be the humble servant of that parson 's daughter . by Jove ! I would rather she should fling the dish at my head than sneer at I as she do . she put I to shame before the child with her d d air ; and , I will swear , tell Frank and Beaty that papa be a reprobate , and that they ought to despise I . " 
 " indeed and indeed , sir , I never hear she say a word but of respect regard you , " Harry Esmond interpose . 
 " no , curse it ! I wish she would speak . but she never do . she scorn I , and hold her tongue . she keep off from I , as if I be a pestilence . by George ! she be fond enough of her pestilence once . and when I come a - courting , you would see miss blush blush red , by George ! for joy . why , what do you think she say to I , Harry ? she say herself , when I joke with she about her d d smile red cheek : " tis as they do at St. James 's ; I put up my red flag when my king come . ' I be the king , you see , she mean . but now , sir , look at she ! I believe she would be glad if I be dead ; and dead I have   be to she these five year ever since you all of you have the small - pox : and she never forgave I for go away . " 
 " indeed , my lord , though ' twa hard to forgive , I think my mistress forgave it , " Harry Esmond say ; " and remember how eagerly she watch your lordship 's return , and how sadly she turn away when she see your cold look . " 
 " Damme ! " cry out my lord ; " would you have have I wait and catch the small - pox ? where the deuce have be the good of that ? I will bear danger with any man but not useless danger no , no . thank you for nothing . and you nod your head , and I know very well , Parson Harry , what you mean . there be the the other affair to make she angry . but be a woman never to forgive a husband who go a - tripping ? do you take I for a saint ? " 
 " indeed , sir , I do not , " say Harry , with a smile . 
 " since that time my wife 's as cold as the statue at Charing Cross . I tell thee she have no forgiveness in she , Henry . her coldness blight my whole life , and send I to the punch - bowl , or drive about the country . my child be not mine , but her , when we be together . ' tis only when she be out of sight with her abominable cold glance , that run through I , that they will come to I , and that I dare to give they so much as a kiss ; and that be why I take them and love them in other people 's house , Harry . I be kill by the very virtue of that proud woman . Virtue ! give I the virtue that can forgive ; give I the virtue that think not of preserve itself , but of make other folk happy . Damme , what matter a scar or two if ' tis get in help a friend in ill fortune ? " 
 and my lord again slap the table , and take a great draught from the tankard . Harry Esmond admire as he listen to he , and think how the poor preacher of this self - sacrifice have flee from the small - pox , which the lady have bear so cheerfully , and which have be the cause of so much disunion in the life of all in this house . " how well man preach , " think the young man , " and each be the example in his own sermon . how each have a story in a dispute , and a true one , too , and both be right or wrong as you will ! " Harry 's heart be pain within he , to watch the struggle and pang that tear the breast of this kind , manly friend and protector . 
 " indeed , sir , " say he , " I wish to God that my mistress could hear you speak as I have hear you ; she would know much that would make her life the happy , could she hear it . " but my lord fling away with one of his oath , and a jeer ; he say that Parson Harry be a good fellow ; but that as for woman , all woman be alike all jade and heartless . so a man dash a fine vase down , and despise it for be break . it may be worthless true : but who have the keeping of it , and who shatter it ? 
 Harry , who would have give his life to make his benefactress and her husband happy , bethink he , now that he see what my lord 's state of mind be , and that he really have a great deal of that love leave in his heart , and ready for his wife 's acceptance if she would take it , whether he could not be a mean of reconciliation between these two person , whom he revere the most in the world . and he cast about how he should break a part of his mind to his mistress , and warn she that in his , Harry 's opinion , at least , her husband be still her admirer , and even her lover . 
 but he find the subject a very difficult one to handle , when he venture to remonstrate , which he do in the very grave tone ,   for long confidence and reiterate proof of devotion and loyalty have give he a sort of authority in the house , which he resume as soon as ever he return to it ,   and with a speech that should have some effect , as , indeed , it be utter with the speaker 's own heart , he venture most gently to hint to his adore mistress that she be do her husband harm by her ill opinion of he , and that the happiness of all the family depend upon set she right . 
 she , who be ordinarily calm and most gentle , and full of smile and soft attention , flush up when young Esmond so speak to she , and rise from her chair , look at he with a haughtiness and indignation that he have never before know she to display . she be quite an altered be for that moment ; and look an angry princess insult by a vassal . 
 " have you ever hear I utter a word in my lord 's disparagement ? " she ask hastily , hiss out her word , and stamp her foot . 
 " indeed , no , " Esmond say , look down . 
 " be you come to I as his ambassador you ? " she continue . 
 " I would soon see peace between you than anything else in the world , " Harry answer , " and would go of any embassy that have that end . " 
 " so you be my lord 's go - between ? " she go on , not regard this speech . " you be send to bid I back into slavery again , and inform I that my lord 's favor be graciously restore to his handmaid ? he be weary of Covent Garden , be he , that he come home and would have the fat calf kill ? " 
 " there be good authority for it , surely , " say Esmond . 
 " for a son , yes ; but my lord be not my son . it be he who cast I away from he . it be he who break our happiness down , and he bid I to repair it . it be he who show himself to I at last , as he be , not as I have think he . it be he who come before my child stupid and senseless with wine who leave our company for that of frequenter of tavern and bagnio who go from his home to the City yonder and his friend there , and when he be tired of they return hither , and expect that I shall kneel and welcome he . and he send you as his chamberlain ! what a proud embassy ! Monsieur , I make you my compliment of the new place . " 
 " it would be a proud embassy , and a happy embassy too , could I bring you and my lord together , " Esmond reply . 
 " I presume you have fulfil your mission now , sir . ' twas a pretty one for you to undertake . I do not   know whether ' tis your Cambridge philosophy , or time , that have alter your way of thinking , " Lady Castlewood continue , still in a sarcastic tone . " perhaps you too have learn to love drink , and to hiccup over your wine or punch ; which be your worship 's favorite liquor ? perhaps you too put up at the ' rose ' on your way to London , and have your acquaintance in Covent Garden . my service to you , sir , to principal and ambassador , to master and and lackey . " 
 " Great heavens ! madam , " cry Harry . " what have I do that thus , for a second time , you insult I ? do you wish I to blush for what I use to be proud of , that I live on your bounty ? next to do you a service   which my life would pay for , you know that to receive one from you be my high pleasure . what wrong have I do you that you should wound I so , cruel woman ? " 
 " what wrong ? " she say , look at Esmond with wild eye . " well , none none that you know of , Harry , or could help . why do you bring back the small - pox , " she add , after a pause , " from Castlewood village ? you could not help it , could you ? which of we know whither fate lead we ? but we be all happy , Henry , till then . " and Harry go away from this colloquy , think still that the estrangement between his patron and his beloved mistress be remediable , and that each have at heart a strong attachment to the other . 
 the intimacy between the Lords Mohun and Castlewood appear to increase as long as the former remain in the country ; and my Lord of Castlewood especially seem never to be happy out of his new comrade 's sight . they sport together , they drank , they play bowl and tennis : my Lord Castlewood would go for three day to Sark , and bring back my Lord Mohun to Castlewood where indeed his lordship make himself very welcome to all person , have a joke or a new game at romp for the child , all the talk of the town for my lord , and music and gallantry and plenty of the beau langage for my lady , and for Harry Esmond , who be never tired of hear his story of his campaign and his life at Vienna , Venice , Paris , and the famous city of Europe which he have visit both in peace and war . and he sing at my lady 's harpsichord , and play card or backgammon , or his new game of billiard with my lord   of whom he invariably get the well   always have a consummate good - humor , and bear himself with a certain manly grace , that might exhibit somewhat of the camp and Alsatia perhaps , but that have its charm , and stamp he a gentleman : and his manner to Lady Castlewood be so devoted and respectful , that she soon recover from the first feeling of dislike which she have conceive against he nay , before long , begin to be interested in his spiritual welfare , and hopeful of his conversion , lend he book of piety , which he promise dutifully to study . with she my lord talk of reform , of settle into quiet life , quit the court and town , and buy some land in the neighborhood though it must be own that , when the two lord be together over their Burgundy after dinner , their talk be very different , and there be very little question of conversion on my Lord Mohun 's part . when they get to their second bottle , Harry Esmond use commonly to leave these two noble toper , who , though they talk freely enough , heaven know , in his presence   Good Lord , what a set of story , of Alsatia and Spring Garden , of the tavern and gaming - house , of the lady of the court , and madame of the theatre , he can recall out of their godly conversation !   although , I say , they talk before Esmond freely , yet they seem pleased when he go away , and then they have another bottle , and then they fall to card , and then my Lord Mohun come to her ladyship 's drawing - room ; leave his boon companion to sleep off his wine . 
 ' twas a point of honor with the fine gentleman of those day to lose or win magnificently at their horse - match , or game of card and dice and you could never tell , from the demeanor of these two lord afterwards , which have be successful and which the loser at their game . and when my lady hint to my lord that he play more than she like , he dismiss she with a " pish , " and swear that nothing be more equal than play betwixt gentleman , if they do but keep it up long enough . and these keep it up long enough , you may be sure . a man of fashion of that time often pass a quarter of his day at card , and another quarter at drink : I have know many a pretty fellow , who be a wit too , ready of repartee , and possess of a thousand grace , who would be puzzle if he have to write more than his name . 
 there be scarce any thoughtful man or woman , I suppose , but can look back upon his course of past life , and remember some point , trifle as it may have seem at the time of occurrence , which have nevertheless turn and alter his whole career . ' tis with almost all of we , as in M. Massillon 's magnificent image regard King William , a grain de sable that pervert or perhaps overthrow we ; and so it be but a light word fling in the air , a mere freak of perverse child 's temper , that bring down a whole heap of crush woe upon that family whereof Harry Esmond form a part . 
 come home to his dear Castlewood in the third year of his academical course ,   wherein he have now obtain some distinction , his Latin Poem on the death of the Duke of Gloucester , Princess Anne of Denmark 's son , have gain he a medal , and introduce he to the society of the University wit ,   Esmond find his little friend and pupil Beatrix grow to be tall than her mother , a slim and lovely young girl , with cheek mantle with health and rose : with eye like star shin out of azure , with wave bronze hair cluster about the fair young forehead ever see : and a mien and shape haughty and beautiful , such as that of the famous antique statue of the huntress Diana at one time haughty , rapid , imperious , with eye and arrow that dart and kill . Harry watch and wonder at this young creature , and liken she in his mind to Artemis with the ringing bow and shaft flash death upon the child of Niobe ; at another time she be coy and melt as Luna shin tenderly upon endymion . this fair creature , this lustrous Phoebe , be only young as yet , nor have nearly reach her full splendor : but crescent and brilliant , our young gentleman of the University , his head full of poetical fancy , his heart perhaps throb with desire undefined , admire this rise young divinity ; and gaze at she   though only as at some " bright particular star , " far above his earth   with endless delight and wonder . she have be a coquette from the early time almost , try her freak and jealousy , her wayward frolic and win caress , upon all that come within her reach ; she set her woman quarrel in the nursery , and practise her eye on the groom as she ride behind he on the pillion . 
 she be the darling and torment of father and mother . she intrigue with each secretly ; and bestow her fondness and withdraw it , ply they with tear , smile , kiss , cajolement ; when the mother be angry , as happen often , fly to the father , and shelter behind he , pursue her victim ; when both be displease , transfer her caress to the domestic , or watch until she could win back her parent ' good grace , either by surprise they into laughter and good - humor , or appease they by submission and artful humility . she be saevo laeta negotio , like that fickle goddess Horace describe , and of whose " malicious joy " a great poet of our own have write so nobly who , famous and heroic as he be , be not strong enough to resist the torture of woman . 
 it be but three year before that the child , then but ten year old , have nearly manage to make a quarrel between Harry Esmond and his comrade , good - nature , phlegmatic Thomas Tusher , who never of his own seeking quarrel with anybody : by quote to the latter some silly joke which Harry have make regard he   it be the mere idle jest , though it near drive two old friend to blow , and I think such a battle would have please she   and from that day Tom keep at a distance from she ; and she respect he , and coax he sedulously whenever they meet . but Harry be much more easily appease , because he be fonder of the child : and when she make mischief , use cut speech , or cause her friend pain , she excuse herself for her fault , not by admit and deplore it , but by plead not guilty , and assert innocence so constantly , and with such seeming artlessness , that it be impossible to question her plea . in her childhood , they be but mischief then which she do ; but her power become more fatal as she grow old as a kitten first play with a ball , and then pounce on a bird and kill it . ' tis not to be imagine that Harry Esmond have all this experience at this early stage of his life , whereof he be now write the history many thing here note be but know to he in later day . almost everything Beatrix do or undo seem good , or at least pardonable , to he then , and year afterwards . 
 it happen , then , that Harry Esmond come home to Castlewood for his last vacation , with good hope of a fellowship at his college , and a contented resolve to advance his fortune that way . ' twa in the first year of the present century , Mr. Esmond   as far as he know the period of his birth   be then twenty - two year old . he find his quondam pupil shoot up into this beauty of which we have speak , and promise yet more : her brother , my lord 's son , a handsome high - spirited brave lad , generous and frank , and kind to everybody , save perhaps his sister , with whom Frank be at war   and not from his but her fault   adore his mother , whose joy he be : and take her side in the unhappy matrimonial difference which be now permanent , while of course Mistress Beatrix range with her father . when head of family fall out , it must naturally be that their dependant wear the one or the other party 's color ; and even in the parliament in the servant ' hall or the stable , Harry , who have an early observant turn , could see which be my lord 's adherent and which my lady 's , and conjecture pretty shrewdly how their unlucky quarrel be debate . our lackey sit in judgment on we . my lord 's intrigue may be ever so stealthily conduct , but his valet know they ; and my lady 's woman carry her mistress 's private history to the servant ' scandal market , and exchange it against the secret of other abigail . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

 MY LORD LEAVES US and his evil behind HIM . 

 my Lord Mohun   of whose exploit and fame some of the gentleman of the University have bring down but ugly report   be once more a guest at Castlewood , and seemingly more intimately ally with my lord even than before . once in the spring those two nobleman have ride to Cambridge from Newmarket , whither they have go for the horse - racing , and have honor Harry Esmond with a visit at his room ; after which Doctor Montague , the master of the College , who have treat Harry somewhat haughtily , see his familiarity with these great folk , and that my Lord Castlewood laugh and walk with his hand on Harry 's shoulder , relent to Mr. Esmond , and condescend to be very civil to he ; and some day after his arrival , Harry , laughing , tell this story to Lady Esmond , remark how strange it be that man famous for learn and renowned over Europe , should , nevertheless , so bow down to a title , and cringe to a nobleman ever so poor . at this Mistress Beatrix fling up her head , and say it become those of low origin to respect their better ; that the parson make themselves a great deal too proud , she think ; and that she like the way at Lady Sark 's good , where the chaplain , though he love pudding , as all parson do , always go away before the custard . 
 " and when I be a parson , " say Mr. Esmond , " will you give I no custard , beatrix ? " 
 " you you be different , " beatrix answer . " you be of our blood . " 
 " my father be a parson , as you call he , " say my lady . 
 " but mine be a peer of Ireland , " say Mistress Beatrix , toss her head . " let people know their place . I suppose you will have I go down on my knee and ask a blessing of Mr. Thomas Tusher , that have just be make a curate and whose mother be a waiting - maid . " 
 and she toss out of the room , be in one of her flighty humor then . 
 when she be go , my lady look so sad and grave , that Harry ask the cause of her disquietude . she say it be not merely what he say of Newmarket , but what she have remark , with great anxiety and terror , that my lord , ever since his acquaintance with the Lord Mohun especially , have recur to his fondness for play , which he have renounce since his marriage . 
 " but man promise more than they be able to perform in marriage , " say my lady , with a sigh . " I fear he have lose large sum ; and our property , always small , be dwindle away under this reckless dissipation . I hear of he in London with very wild company . since his return , letter and lawyer be constantly come and go : he seem to I to have a constant anxiety , though he hide it under boisterousness and laughter . I look through through the door last night , and and before , " say my lady , " and see they at card after midnight ; no estate will bear that extravagance , much less our , which will be so diminish that my son will have nothing at all , and my poor beatrix no portion ! " 
 " I wish I could help you , madam , " say Harry Esmond , sighing , and wish that unavailingly , and for the thousandth time in his life . 
 " who can ? only God , " say Lady Esmond " only God , in whose hand we be . " and so it be , and for his rule over his family , and for his conduct to wife and child subject over whom his power be monarchical any one who watch the world must think with trembling sometimes of the account which many a man will have to render . for in our society there be no law to control the King of the Fireside . he be master of property , happiness life almost . he be free to punish , to make happy or unhappy to ruin or to torture . he may kill a wife gradually , and be no more question than the Grand seignior who drown a slave at midnight . he may make slave and hypocrite of his child ; or friend and freeman ; or drive they into revolt and enmity against the natural law of love . I have hear politician and coffee - house wiseacre talk over the newspaper , and rail at the tyranny of the french king , and the Emperor , and wonder how these   who be monarchs , too , in their way   govern their own dominion at home , where each man rule absolute . when the annal of each little reign be show to the Supreme Master , under whom we hold sovereignty , history will be lay bare of household tyrant as cruel as Amurath , and as savage as Nero , and as reckless and dissolute as Charles . 
 if Harry Esmond 's patron err , ' twa in the latter way , from a disposition rather self - indulgent than cruel ; and he might have be bring back to much well feeling , have time be give to he to bring his repentance to a last reform . 
 as my lord and his friend Lord Mohun be such close companion , Mistress Beatrix choose to be jealous of the latter ; and the two gentleman often entertain each other by laugh , in their rude boisterous way , at the child 's freak of anger and show of dislike . " when thou art old enough , thou shalt marry Lord Mohun , " Beatrix 's father would say : on which the girl would pout and say , " I would rather marry Tom Tusher . " and because the Lord Mohun always show an extreme gallantry to my Lady Castlewood , whom he profess to admire devotedly , one day , in answer to this old joke of her father 's , Beatrix say , " I think my lord would rather marry mamma than marry I ; and be wait till you die to ask she . " 
 the word be say lightly and pertly by the girl one night before supper , as the family party be assemble near the great fire . the two lord , who be at card , both give a start ; my lady turn as red as scarlet , and bade Mistress Beatrix go to her own chamber ; whereupon the girl , put on , as she will not be , the most innocent air , say , " I be sure I mean no wrong ; I be sure mamma talk a great deal more to Harry Esmond than she do to papa and she cry when Harry go away , and she never do when papa go away ! and last night she talk to Lord Mohun for ever so long , and send we out of the room , and cry when we come back , and " 
 " D n ! " cry out my Lord Castlewood , out of all patience . " go out of the room , you little viper ! " and he start up and fling down his card . 
 " ask Lord Mohun what I say to he , Francis , " her ladyship say , rise up with a scared face , but yet with a great and touching dignity and candor in her look and voice . " come away with I , beatrix . " beatrix spring up too ; she be in tear now . 
 " Dearest mamma , what have I do ? " she ask . " sure I mean no harm . " and she cling to her mother , and the pair go out sob together . 
 " I will tell you what your wife say to I , Frank , " my Lord Mohun cry . " Parson Harry may hear it ; and , as I hope for heaven , every word I say be true . last night , with tear in her eye , your wife implore I to play no more with you at dice or at card , and you know good whether what she ask be not for your good . " 
 " of course , it be , Mohun , " say my lord in a dry hard voice . " of course you be a model of a man : and the world know what a saint you be . " 
 my Lord Mohun be separate from his wife , and have have many affair of honor : of which woman as usual have be the cause . 
 " I be no saint , though your wife be and I can answer for my action as other people must for their word , " say my Lord Mohun . 
 " by G , my lord , you shall , " cry the other , start up . 
 " we have another little account to settle first , my lord , " say Lord Mohun . Whereupon Harry Esmond , fill with alarm for the consequence to which this disastrous dispute might lead , break out into the most vehement expostulation with his patron and his adversary . " gracious heaven ! " he say , " my lord , be you go to draw a sword upon your friend in your own house ? can you doubt the honor of a lady who be as pure as heaven , and would die a thousand time rather than do you a wrong ? be the idle word of a jealous child to set friend at variance ? have not my mistress , as much as she dare do , besought your lordship , as the truth must be tell , to break your intimacy with my Lord Mohun ; and to give up the habit which may bring ruin on your family ? but for my Lord Mohun 's illness , have he not leave you ? " 
 " ' Faith , Frank , a man with a gouty toe can not   run after other man 's wife , " break out my Lord Mohun , who indeed be in that way , and with a laugh and a look at his swathe limb so frank and comical , that the other dash his fist across his forehead be catch by that infectious good - humor , and say with his oath , " it , Harry , I believe thee , " and so this quarrel be over , and the two gentleman , at sword draw but just now , drop their point , and shake hand . 
 Beati pacifici . " go , bring my lady back , " say Harry 's patron . esmond go away only too glad to be the bearer of such good news . he find she at the door ; she have be listen there , but go back as he come . she take both his hand , her be marble cold . she seem as if she would fall on his shoulder . " thank you , and God bless you , my dear brother Harry , " she say . she kiss his hand , Esmond feel her tear upon it : and lead she into the room , and up to my lord , the Lord Castlewood , with an outbreak of feeling and affection such as he have not exhibit for many a long day , take his wife to his heart , and bend over and kiss she and ask her pardon . 
 " ' tis time for I to go to roost . I will have my gruel a - bed , " say my Lord Mohun : and limp off comically on Harry Esmond 's arm . " by George , that woman be a pearl ! " he say ; " and ' tis only a pig that would not   value she . have you see the vulgar traipse orange - girl whom esmond " but here Mr. Esmond interrupt he , say , that these be not affair for he to know . 
 my lord 's gentleman come in to wait upon his master , who be no soon in his nightcap and dressing - gown than he have another visitor whom his host insist on send to he : and this be no other than the Lady Castlewood herself with the toast and gruel , which her husband bid her make and carry with her own hand in to her guest . 
 Lord Castlewood stand look after his wife as she go on this errand , and as he look , Harry Esmond could not but gaze on he , and remark in his patron 's face an expression of love , and grief , and care , which very much move and touch the young man . Lord Castlewood 's hand fall down at his side , and his head on his breast , and presently he say , 
 " you hear what Mohun say , parson ? " 
 " that my lady be a saint ? " 
 " that there be two account to settle . I have be go wrong these five year , Harry Esmond . ever since you bring that damn small - pox into the house , there have be a fate pursue I , and I have well have die of it , and not run away from it like a coward . I leave beatrix with her relation , and go to London ; and I fall among thief , Harry , and I get back to confound card and dice , which I have not   touch since my marriage no , not since I be in the Duke 's guard , with those wild mohock . and I have be play bad and bad , and go deep and deep into it ; and I owe Mohun two thousand pound now ; and when it be pay I be little well than a beggar . I do not   like to look my boy in the face ; he hat I , I know he do . and I have spend Beaty 's little portion : and the Lord know what will come if I live ; the good thing I can do be to die , and release what portion of the estate be redeemable for the boy . " 
 Mohun be as much master at Castlewood as the owner of the Hall itself ; and his equipage fill the stable , where , indeed , there be room and plenty for many more horse than Harry Esmond 's impoverished patron could afford to keep . he have arrive on horseback with his people ; but when his gout break out my Lord Mohun send to London for a light chaise he have , draw by a pair of small horse , and run as swift , wherever road be good , as a Laplander 's sledge . when this carriage come , his lordship be eager to drive the Lady Castlewood abroad in it , and do so many time , and at a rapid pace , greatly to his companion 's enjoyment , who love the swift motion and the healthy breeze over the down which lie hard upon Castlewood , and stretch thence towards the sea . as this amusement be very pleasant to she , and her lord , far from show any mistrust of her intimacy with Lord Mohun , encourage she to be his companion as if willing by his present extreme confidence to make up for any past mistrust which his jealousy have show the Lady Castlewood enjoy herself freely in this harmless diversion , which , it must be own , her guest be very eager to give she ; and it seem that she grow the more free with Lord Mohun , and please with his company , because of some sacrifice which his gallantry be pleased to make in her favor . 
 see the two gentleman constantly at card still of evening , Harry Esmond one day deplore to his mistress that this fatal infatuation of her lord should continue ; and now they seem reconcile together , beg his lady to hint to her husband that he should play no more . 
 but Lady Castlewood , smile archly and gayly , say she would speak to he presently , and that , for a few night more at least , he might be let to have his amusement . 
 " indeed , madam , " say Harry , " you know not what it cost you ; and ' tis easy for any observer who know the game , to see that Lord Mohun be by far the strong of the two . " 
 " I know he be , " say my lady , still with exceed good - humor ; " he be not only the good player , but the kind player in the world . " 
 " Madam , madam ! " Esmond cry , transport and provoke . " debt of honor must be pay some time or other ; and my master will be ruin if he go on . " 
 " Harry , shall I tell you a secret ? " my lady reply , with kindness and pleasure still in her eye . " Francis will not be ruin if he go on ; he will be rescue if he go on . I repent of have speak and think unkindly of the Lord Mohun when he be here in the past year . he be full of much kindness and good ; and ' tis my belief that we shall bring he to well thing . I have lend he ' Tillotson ' and your favorite ' Bishop Taylor , ' and he be much touch , he say ; and as a proof of his repentance   and herein lie my secret   what do you think he be do with Francis ? he be let poor Frank win his money back again . he hath win already at the last four night ; and my Lord Mohun say that he will not be the mean of injure poor Frank and my dear child . " 
 " and in God 's name , what do you return he for the sacrifice ? " ask Esmond , aghast ; who know enough of man , and of this one in particular , to be aware that such a finished rake give nothing for nothing . " how , in heaven 's name , be you to pay he ? " 
 " pay he ! with a mother 's blessing and a wife 's prayer ! " cry my lady , clasp her hand together . Harry Esmond do not know whether to laugh , to be angry , or to love his dear mistress more than ever for the obstinate innocency with which she choose to regard the conduct of a man of the world , whose design he know well how to interpret . he tell the lady , guardedly , but so as to make his meaning quite clear to she , what he know in respect of the former life and conduct of this nobleman ; of other woman against whom he have plot , and whom he have overcome ; of the conversation which he , Harry himself , have have with Lord Mohun , wherein the lord make a boast of his libertinism , and frequently avow that he hold all woman to be fair game   as his lordship style this pretty sport , and that they be all , without exception , to be win . and the return Harry have for his entreaty and remonstrance be a fit of anger on Lady Castlewood 's part , who would not listen to his accusation ; she say and retort that he himself must be very wicked and pervert to suppose evil design where she be sure none be mean . " and this be the good meddler get of interfere , " Harry think to himself with much bitterness ; and his perplexity and annoyance be only the great , because he could not speak to my Lord Castlewood himself upon a subject of this nature , or venture to advise or warn he regard a matter so very sacred as his own honor , of which my lord be naturally the good guardian . 
 but though Lady Castlewood would listen to no advice from her young dependant , and appear indignantly to refuse it when offer , Harry have the satisfaction to find that she adopt the counsel which she profess to reject ; for the next day she plead a headache , when my Lord Mohun would have have her drive out , and the next day the headache continue ; and next day , in a laugh gay way , she propose that the child should take her place in his lordship 's car , for they would be charm with a ride of all thing ; and she must not have all the pleasure for herself . my lord give they a drive with a very good grace , though , I dare say , with rage and disappointment inwardly not that his heart be very seriously engage in his design upon this simple lady : but the life of such man be often one of intrigue , and they can no more go through the day without a woman to pursue , than a fox - hunter without his sport after breakfast . 
 under an affected carelessness of demeanor , and though there be no outward demonstration of doubt upon his patron 's part since the quarrel between the two lord , Harry yet see that Lord Castlewood be watch his guest very narrowly ; and catch sight of distrust and smother rage   as Harry think   which forebode no good . on the point of honor Esmond know how touchy his patron be ; and watch he almost as a physician watch a patient , and it seem to he that this one be slow to take the disease , though he could not throw off the poison when once it have mingle with his blood . we read in Shakspeare   whom the writer for his part consider to be far beyond Mr. Congreve , Mr. Dryden , or any of the wit of the present period ,   that when jealousy be once declare , nor poppy , nor mandragora , nor all the drowsy syrup of the East , will ever soothe it or medicine it away . 
 in fine , the symptom seem to be so alarming to this young physician   who , indeed , young as he be , have feel the kind pulse of all those dear kinsman , that Harry think it would be his duty to warn my Lord Mohun , and let he know that his design be suspect and watch . so one day , when in rather a pettish humor his lordship have send to Lady Castlewood , who have promise to drive with he , and now refuse to come , Harry say " my lord , if you will kindly give I a place by your side I will thank you ; I have much to say to you , and would like to speak to you alone . " 
 " you honor I by give I your confidence , Mr. Henry Esmond , " say the other , with a very grand bow . my lord be always a fine gentleman , and young as he be there be that in Esmond 's manner which show that he be a gentleman too , and that none might take a liberty with he so the pair go out , and mount the little carriage , which be in wait for they in the court , with its two little cream - color hanoverian horse cover with splendid furniture and champ at the bit . 
 " my lord , " say Harry Esmond , after they be get into the country , and point to my Lord Mohun 's foot , which be swathe in flannel , and put up rather ostentatiously on a cushion " my lord , I study medicine at Cambridge . " 
 " indeed , Parson Harry , " say he ; " and be you go to take out a diploma : and cure your fellow - student of the " 
 " of the gout , " say Harry , interrupt he , and look he hard in the face ; " I know a good deal about the gout . " 
 " I hope you may never have it . ' tis an infernal disease , " say my lord , " and its twinge be diabolical . ah ! " and he make a dreadful wry face , as if he just feel a twinge . 
 " your lordship would be much well if you take off all that flannel it only serve to inflame the toe , " Harry continue , look his man full in the face . 
 " oh ! it only serve to inflame the toe , do it ? " say the other , with an innocent air . 
 " if you take off that flannel , and fling that absurd slipper away , and wear a boot , " continue Harry . 
 " you recommend I boot , Mr. Esmond ? " ask my lord . 
 " yes , boot and spur . I see your lordship three day ago run down the gallery fast enough , " Harry go on . " I be sure that take gruel at night be not so pleasant as claret to your lordship ; and besides it keep your lordship 's head cool for play , whilst my patron 's be hot and fluster with drink . " 
 " ' Sdeath , sir , you dare not say that I do not   play fair ? " cry my lord , whip his horse , which go away at a gallop . 
 " you be cool when my lord be drunk , " Harry continue ; " your lordship get the well of my patron . I have watch you as I look up from my book . " 
 " you young Argus ! " say Lord Mohun , who like Harry Esmond and for whose company and wit , and a certain daring manner , Harry have a great liking too " you young Argus ! you may look with all your hundred eye and see we play fair . I have   play away an estate of a night , and I have   play my shirt off my back ; and I have   play away my periwig and go home in a nightcap . but no man can say I ever take an advantage of he beyond the advantage of the game . I play a dice - cog scoundrel in Alsatia for his ear and win them , and have one of them in my lodging in Bow Street in a bottle of spirit . Harry Mohun will play any man for anything always would . " 
 " you be play awful stake , my lord , in my patron 's house , " Harry say , " and more game than be on the card . " 
 " what do you mean , sir ? " cry my lord , turn round , with a flush on his face . 
 " I mean , " answer Harry , in a sarcastic tone , " that your gout be well if ever you have it . " 
 " Sir ! " cry my lord , get hot . 
 " and to tell the truth I believe your lordship have no more gout than I have . at any rate , change of air will do you good , my Lord Mohun . and I mean fairly that you have well go from Castlewood . " 
 " and be you appoint to give I this message ? " cry the Lord Mohun . " do Frank Esmond commission you ? " 
 " no one do . ' twas the honor of my family that commission I . " 
 " and you be prepared to answer this ? " cry the other , furiously lash his horse . 
 " quite , my lord : your lordship will upset the carriage if you whip so hotly . " 
 " by George , you have a brave spirit ! " my lord cry out , burst into a laugh . " I suppose ' tis that infernal botte de Jesuite that make you so bold , " he add . 
 " ' tis the peace of the family I love well in the world , " Harry Esmond say warmly " ' tis the honor of a noble benefactor the happiness of my dear mistress and her child . I owe they everything in life , my lord ; and would lay it down for any one of they . what bring you here to disturb this quiet household ? what keep you linger month after month in the country ? what make you feign illness , and invent pretext for delay ? be it to win my poor patron 's money ? be generous , my lord , and spare his weakness for the sake of his wife and child . be it to practise upon the simple heart of a virtuous lady ? you might as well storm the Tower single - handed . but you may blemish her name by light comment on it , or by lawless pursuit and I do not   deny that ' tis in your power to make her unhappy . spare these innocent people , and leave they . " 
 " by the Lord , I believe thou hast an eye to the pretty Puritan thyself , Master Harry , " say my lord , with his reckless , good - humor laugh , and as if he have be listen with interest to the passionate appeal of the young man . " Whisper , Harry . art thou in love with she thyself ? Hath tipsy Frank Esmond come by the way of all flesh ? " 
 " my lord , my lord , " cry Harry , his face flush and his eye fill as he speak , " I never have a mother , but I love this lady as one . I worship she as a devotee worship a saint . to hear her name speak lightly seem blasphemy to I . would you dare think of your own mother so , or suffer any one so to speak of she ? it be a horror to I to fancy that any man should think of she impurely . I implore you , I beseech you , to leave she . danger will come out of it . " 
 " Danger , psha ! " say my lord , give a cut to the horse , which at this minute for we be get on to the Downs fairly run off into a gallop that no pulling could stop . the rein break in Lord Mohun 's hand , and the furious beast scamper madly forwards , the carriage sway to and fro , and the person within it hold on to the side as well they might , until see a great ravine before they , where an upset be inevitable , the two gentleman leapt for their life , each out of his side of the chaise . Harry Esmond be quit for a fall on the grass , which be so severe that it stun he for a minute ; but he get up presently very sick , and bleed at the nose , but with no other hurt . the Lord Mohun be not so fortunate ; he fall on his head against a stone , and lie on the ground , dead to all appearance . 
 this misadventure happen as the gentleman be on their return homeward ; and my Lord Castlewood , with his son and daughter , who be go out for a ride , meet the pony as they be gallop with the car behind , the broken trace entangle their heel , and my lord 's people turn and stop they . it be young Frank who spy out Lord Mohun 's scarlet coat as he lie on the ground , and the party make up to that unfortunate gentleman and Esmond , who be now stand over he . his large periwig and feather hat have fall off , and he be bleed profusely from a wound on the forehead , and look , and be , indeed , a corpse . 
 " Great God ! he be dead ! " say my lord . " Ride , some one : fetch a doctor stay . I will go home and bring back Tusher ; he know surgery , " and my lord , with his son after he , gallop away . 
 they be scarce go when Harry Esmond , who be indeed but just come to himself , bethink he of a similar accident which he have see on a ride from Newmarket to Cambridge , and take off a sleeve of my lord 's coat , Harry , with a penknife , open a vein of his arm , and be greatly relieve , after a moment , to see the blood flow . he be near half an hour before he come to himself , by which time Doctor Tusher and little Frank arrive , and find my lord not a corpse indeed , but as pale as one . 
 after a time , when he be able to bear motion , they put my lord upon a groom 's horse , and give the other to Esmond , the man walk on each side of my lord , to support he , if need be , and worthy Doctor Tusher with they . Little Frank and Harry ride together at a foot pace . 
 when we ride together home , the boy say : " we meet mamma , who be walk on the terrace with the doctor , and papa frighten she , and tell she you be dead . . . " 
 " that I be dead ! " ask Harry . 
 " yes . Papa say : ' here 's poor Harry kill , my dear ; ' on which mamma give a great scream ; and oh , Harry ! she drop down ; and I think she be dead too . and you never see such a way as papa be in : he swear one of his great oath : and he turn quite pale ; and then he begin to laugh somehow , and he tell the Doctor to take his horse , and I to follow he ; and we leave he . and I look back , and see he dash water out of the fountain on to mamma . oh , she be so frightened ! " 
 Musing upon this curious history for my Lord Mohun 's name be Henry too , and they call each other Frank and Harry often and not a little disturbed and anxious , Esmond ride home . his dear lady be on the terrace still , one of her woman with she , and my lord no long there . there be step and a little door thence down into the road . my lord pass , look very ghastly , with a handkerchief over his head , and without his hat and periwig , which a groom carry , but his politeness do not desert he , and he make a bow to the lady above . 
 " thank heaven , you be safe , " she say . 
 " and so be Harry too , mamma , " say little Frank , " huzzay ! " 
 Harry Esmond get off the horse to run to his mistress , as do little Frank , and one of the groom take charge of the two beast , while the other , hat and periwig in hand , walk by my lord 's bridle to the front gate , which lie half a mile away . 
 " oh , my boy ! what a fright you have give I ! " Lady Castlewood say , when Harry Esmond come up , greet he with one of her shin look , and a voice of tender welcome ; and she be so kind as to kiss the young man   ' twas the second time she have so honor he , and she walk into the house between he and her son , hold a hand of each . 




 CHAPTER XIV . 

 we RIDE after HIM to LONDON . 

 after a repose of a couple of day , the Lord Mohun be so far recover of his hurt as to be able to announce his departure for the next morning ; when , accordingly , he take leave of Castlewood , propose to ride to London by easy stage , and lie two night upon the road . his host treat he with a studied and ceremonious courtesy , certainly different from my lord 's usual frank and careless demeanor ; but there be no reason to suppose that the two lord part otherwise than good friend , though Harry Esmond remark that my Lord Viscount only see his guest in company with other person , and seem to avoid be alone with he . nor do he ride any distance with Lord Mohun , as his custom be with most of his friend , whom he be always eager to welcome and unwilling to lose ; but content himself , when his lordship 's horse be announce , and their owner appear , boot for his journey , to take a courteous leave of the lady of Castlewood , by follow the Lord Mohun down stair to his horse , and by bow and wish he a good - day , in the court - yard . " I shall see you in London before very long , Mohun , " my lord say , with a smile , " when we will settle our account together . " 
 " do not let they trouble you , Frank , " say the other good - naturedly , and hold out his hand , look rather surprised at the grim and stately manner in which his host receive his parting salutation ; and so , follow by his people , he ride away . 
 Harry Esmond be witness of the departure . it be very different to my lord 's come , for which great preparation have be make   the old house put on its good appearance to welcome its guest , and there be a sadness and constraint about all person that day , which fill Mr. Esmond with gloomy foreboding , and sad indefinite apprehension . Lord Castlewood stand at the door watch his guest and his people as they go out under the arch of the outer gate . when he be there , Lord Mohun turn once more , my Lord Viscount slowly raise his beaver and bow . his face wear a peculiar livid look , Harry think . he curse and kick away his dog , which come jump about he then he walk up to the fountain in the centre of the court , and lean against a pillar and look into the basin . as Esmond cross over to his own room , late the chaplain 's , on the other side of the court , and turn to enter in at the low door , he see Lady Castlewood look through the curtain of the great window of the drawing - room overhead , at my lord as he stand regard the fountain . there be in the court a peculiar silence somehow ; and the scene remain long in Esmond 's memory : the sky bright overhead ; the buttress of the building and the sun - dial cast shadow over the gilt memento mori inscribe underneath ; the two dog , a black greyhound and a spaniel nearly white , the one with his face up to the sun , and the other snuffing amongst the grass and stone , and my lord lean over the fountain , which be bubble audibly . ' tis strange how that scene , and the sound of that fountain , remain fixed on the memory of a man who have behold a hundred sight of splendor , and danger too , of which he have keep no account . 
 it be Lady Castlewood she have be laugh all the morning , and especially gay and lively before her husband and his guest who as soon as the two gentleman go together from her room , run to Harry , the expression of her countenance quite change now , and with a face and eye full of care , and say , " follow they , Harry , I be sure something have go wrong . " and so it be that Esmond be make an eavesdropper at this lady 's order and retire to his own chamber , to give himself time in truth to try and compose a story which would soothe his mistress , for he could not but have his own apprehension that some serious quarrel be pende between the two gentleman . 
 and now for several day the little company at Castlewood sit at table as of evening : this care , though unnamed and invisible , be nevertheless present alway , in the mind of at least three person there . my lord be exceed gentle and kind . whenever he quit the room , his wife 's eye follow he . he behave to she with a kind of mournful courtesy and kindness remarkable in one of his blunt way and ordinary rough manner . he call she by her christian name often and fondly , be very soft and gentle with the child , especially with the boy , whom he do not love , and be lax about church generally , he go thither and perform all the office   down even to listen to Dr. Tusher 's sermon   with great devotion . 
 " he pace his room all night ; what be it ? Henry , find out what it be , " Lady Castlewood say constantly to her young dependant . " he have send three letter to London , " she say , another day . 
 " indeed , madam , they be to a lawyer , " Harry answer , who know of these letter , and have see a part of the correspondence , which relate to a new loan my lord be raise ; and when the young man remonstrate with his patron , my lord say , " he be only raise money to pay off an old debt on the property , which must be discharge . " 
 regard the money , Lady Castlewood be not in the least anxious . few fond woman feel money - distressed ; indeed you can hardly give a woman a great pleasure than to bid her pawn her diamond for the man she love ; and I remember hear Mr. Congreve say of my Lord Marlborough , that the reason why my lord be so successful with woman as a young man , be because he take money of they . " there be few man who will make such a sacrifice for they , " say Mr. Congreve , who know a part of the sex pretty well . 
 Harry Esmond 's vacation be just over , and , as hath be say , he be prepare to return to the University for his last term before take his degree and enter into the Church . he have make up his mind for this office , not indeed with that reverence which become a man about to enter upon a duty so holy , but with a worldly spirit of acquiescence in the prudence of adopt that profession for his calling . but his reasoning be that he owe all to the family of Castlewood , and love well to be near they than anywhere else in the world ; that he might be useful to his benefactor , who have the utmost confidence in he and affection for he in return ; that he might aid in bring up the young heir of the house and act as his governor ; that he might continue to be his dear patron 's and mistress 's friend and adviser , who both be pleased to say that they should ever look upon he as such ; and so , by make himself useful to those he love well , he propose to console himself for give up of any scheme of ambition which he might have have in his own bosom . indeed , his mistress have tell he that she would not have he leave she ; and whatever she command be will to he . 
 the Lady Castlewood 's mind be greatly relieve in the last few day of this well - remember holiday time , by my lord 's announce one morning , after the post have bring he letter from London , in a careless tone , that the Lord Mohun be go to Paris , and be about to make a great journey in Europe ; and though Lord Castlewood 's own gloom do not wear off , or his behavior alter , yet this cause of anxiety be remove from his lady 's mind , she begin to be more hopeful and easy in her spirit , strive too , with all her heart , and by all the mean of soothe in her power , to call back my lord 's cheerfulness and dissipate his moody humor . 
 he account for it himself , by say that he be out of health ; that he want to see his physician ; that he would go to London , and consult Doctor Cheyne . it be agree that his lordship and Harry Esmond should make the journey as far as London together ; and of a Monday morning , the 11th of October , in the year 1700 , they set forwards towards London on horseback . the day before be Sunday , and the rain pour down , the family do not visit church ; and at night my lord read the service to his family very finely , and with a peculiar sweetness and gravity speak the parting benediction , Harry think , as solemn as ever he hear it . and he kiss and embrace his wife and child before they go to their own chamber with more fondness than he be ordinarily will not to show , and with a solemnity and feeling of which they think in after day with no small comfort . 
 they take horse the next morning   after adieux from the family as tender as on the night previous , lie that night on the road , and enter London at nightfall ; my lord go to the " Trumpet , " in the Cockpit , Whitehall , a house use by the military in his time as a young man , and accustom by his lordship ever since . 
 an hour after my lord 's arrival   which show that his visit have be arrange beforehand , my lord 's man of business arrive from Gray 's Inn ; and think that his patron might wish to be private with the lawyer , Esmond be for leave they : but my lord say his business be short ; introduce Mr. Esmond particularly to the lawyer , who have be engage for the family in the old lord 's time ; who say that he have pay the money , as desire that day , to my Lord Mohun himself , at his lodging in Bow Street ; that his lordship have express some surprise , as it be not customary to employ lawyer , he say , in such transaction between man of honor ; but nevertheless , he have return my Lord Viscount 's note of hand , which he hold at his client 's disposition . 
 " I think the Lord Mohun have be in Paris ! " cry Mr. Esmond , in great alarm and astonishment . 
 " he be come back at my invitation , " say my Lord Viscount . " we have account to settle together . " 
 " I pray heaven they be over , sir , " say Esmond . 
 " oh , quite , " reply the other , look hard at the young man . " he be rather troublesome about that money which I tell you I have lose to he at play . and now ' tis pay , and we be quit on that score , and we shall meet good friend again . " 
 " my lord , " cry out Esmond , " I be sure you be deceive I , and that there be a quarrel between the Lord Mohun and you . " 
 " quarrel pish ! we shall sup together this very night , and drink a bottle . every man be ill - humor who lose such a sum as I have lose . but now ' tis pay , and my anger be go with it . " 
 " where shall we sup , sir ? " say Harry . 
 " we ! let some gentleman wait till they be ask , " say my Lord Viscount with a laugh . " you go to Duke Street , and see Mr. Betterton . you love the play , I know . leave I to follow my own device : and in the morning we will breakfast together , with what appetite we may , as the play say . " 
 " by G ! my lord , I will not leave you this night , " say Harry Esmond . " I think I know the cause of your dispute . I swear to you ' tis nothing . on the very day the accident befell Lord Mohun , I be speak to he about it . I know that nothing have pass but idle gallantry on his part . " 
 " you know that nothing have pass but idle gallantry between Lord Mohun and my wife , " say my lord , in a thunder voice " you know of this and do not tell I ? " 
 " I know more of it than my dear mistress do herself , sir a thousand time more . how be she , who be as innocent as a child , to know what be the meaning of the covert address of a villain ? " 
 " a villain he be , you allow , and would have take my wife away from I . " 
 " Sir , she be as pure as an angel , " cry young Esmond . 
 " have I say a word against she ? " shriek out my lord . " do I ever doubt that she be pure ? it would have be the last day of her life when I do . do you fancy I think that she would go astray ? no , she have not   passion enough for that . she neither sin nor forgive .
