I know her temper and now I have   lose she , by heaven I love her ten thousand time more than ever I do yes , when she be as young and as beautiful as an angel when she smile at I in her old father 's house , and use to lie in wait for I there as I come from hunting when I use to fle my head down on her little knee and cry like a child on her lap and swear I would reform , and drink no more and play no more , and follow woman no more ; when all the man of the Court use to be follow she when she use to look with her child more beautiful , by George , than the Madonna in the Queen 's Chapel . I be not good like she , I know it . who be by heaven , who be ? I tired and weary she , I know that very well . I could not talk to she . you man of wit and book could do that , and I could not   I feel I could not . why , when you be but a boy of fifteen I could hear you two together talk your poetry and your book till I be in such a rage that I be fit to strangle you . but you be always a good lad , Harry , and I love you , you know I do . and I feel she do not   belong to I : and the child do not . and I besotte myself , and gamble and drank , and take to all sort of deviltry out of despair and fury . and now come this Mohun , and she like he , I know she like he . " 
 " indeed , and on my soul , you be wrong , sir , " Esmond cry . 
 " she take letter from he , " cry my lord " look here , Harry , " and he pull out a paper with a brown stain of blood upon it . " it fall from he that day he be not   kill . one of the groom pick it up from the ground and give it I . here it be in their d d comedy jargon . ' divine Gloriana why look so coldly on your slave who adore you ? have you no compassion on the torture you have see I suffer ? do you vouchsafe no reply to billet that be write with the blood of my heart . ' she have more letter from he . " 
 " but she answer none , " cry Esmond . 
 " that be not Mohun 's fault , " say my lord , " and I will be revenge on he , as God 's in heaven , I will . " 
 " for a light word or two , will you risk your lady 's honor and your family 's happiness , my lord ? " Esmond interpose beseechingly . 
 " Psha there shall be no question of my wife 's honor , " say my lord ; " we can quarrel on plenty of ground beside . if I live , that villain will be punish ; if I fall , my family will be only the well : there will only be a spendthrift the less to keep in the world : and Frank have well teaching than his father . my mind be make up , Harry Esmond , and whatever the event be , I be easy about it . I leave my wife and you as guardian to the child . " 
 see that my lord be bent upon pursue this quarrel , and that no entreaty would draw he from it , Harry Esmond   then of a hotter and more impetuous nature than now , when care , and reflection , and gray hair have calm he   think it be his duty to stand by his kind , generous patron , and say , " my lord , if you be determine upon war , you must not go into it alone . ' tis the duty of our house to stand by its chief ; and I should neither forgive myself nor you if you do not call I , or I should be absent from you at a moment of danger . " 
 " why , Harry , my poor boy , you be breed for a parson , " say my lord , take Esmond by the hand very kindly ; " and it be a great pity that you should meddle in the matter . " 
 " your lordship think of be a churchman once , " Harry answer , " and your father 's order do not prevent he fight at Castlewood against the roundhead . your enemy be mine , sir ; I can use the foil , as you have see , indifferently well , and do not   think I shall be afraid when the button be take off them . " and then Harry explain , with some blush and hesitation   for the matter be delicate , and he fear lest , by have put himself forward in the quarrel , he might have offend his patron , how he have himself expostulate with the Lord Mohun , and propose to measure sword with he if need be , and he could not be get to withdraw peaceably in this dispute . " and I should have beat he , sir , " say Harry , laugh . " he never could parry that botte I bring from Cambridge . let we have half an hour of it , and rehearse I can teach it your lordship : ' tis the most delicate point in the world , and if you miss it , your adversary 's sword be through you . " 
 " by George , Harry , you ought to be the head of the house , " say my lord , gloomily . " you have be a well Lord Castlewood than a lazy sot like I , " he add , draw his hand across his eye , and survey his kinsman with very kind and affectionate glance . 
 " let we take our coat off and have half an hour 's practice before nightfall , " say Harry , after thankfully grasp his patron 's manly hand . 
 " you be but a little bit of a lad , " say my lord , good - humoredly ; " but , in faith , I believe you could do for that fellow . no , my boy , " he continue , " I will have none of your feint and trick of stab : I can use my sword pretty well too , and will fight my own quarrel my own way . " 
 " but I shall be by to see fair play ? " cry Harry . 
 " yes , God bless you you shall be by . " 
 " when be it , sir ? " say Harry , for he see that the matter have be arrange privately and beforehand by my lord . 
 " ' tis arrange thus : I send off a courier to Jack Westbury to say that I want he specially . he know for what , and will be here presently , and drink part of that bottle of sack . then we shall go to the theatre in Duke Street , where we shall meet Mohun ; and then we shall all go sup at the ' Rose ' or the ' Greyhound . ' then we shall call for card , and there will be probably a difference over the card and then , God help we ! either a wicked villain and traitor shall go out of the world , or a poor worthless devil , that do not   care to remain in it . I be well away , hal my wife will be all the happy when I be go , " say my lord , with a groan , that tear the heart of Harry Esmond , so that he fairly break into a sob over his patron 's kind hand . 
 " the business be talk over with Mohun before he leave home Castlewood I mean " my lord go on . " I take the letter in to he , which I have read , and I charge he with his villainy , and he could make no denial of it , only he say that my wife be innocent . " 
 " and so she be ; before heaven , my lord , she be ! " cry Harry . 
 " no doubt , no doubt . they always be , " say my lord . " no doubt , when she hear he be kill , she faint from accident . " 
 " but , my lord , MY name be Harry , " cry out Esmond , burn red . " you tell my lady , ' Harry be kill ! ' " 
 " damnation ! shall I fight you too ? " shout my lord in a fury . " be you , you little serpent , warm by my fire , go to ste you ? no , my boy , you be an honest boy ; you be a good boy . "   and here he break from rage into tear even more cruel to see .   " you be an honest boy , and I love you ; and , by heaven , I be so wretched that I do not   care what sword it be that end I . stop , here 's Jack Westbury . well , Jack ! welcome , old boy ! this be my kinsman , Harry Esmond . " 
 " who bring your bowl for you at Castlewood , sir ? " say Harry , bow ; and the three gentleman sit down and drink of that bottle of sack which be prepare for they . 
 " Harry be number three , " say my lord . " you need not   be afraid of he , Jack . " and the Colonel give a look , as much as to say , " indeed , he do not   look as if I need . " and then my lord explain what he have only tell by hint before . when he quarrel with Lord Mohun he be indebted to his lordship in a sum of sixteen hundred pound , for which Lord Mohun say he propose to wait until my Lord Viscount should pay he . my lord have raise the sixteen hundred pound and send they to Lord Mohun that morning , and before quit home have put his affair into order , and be now quite ready to abide the issue of the quarrel . 
 when we have drink a couple of bottle of sack , a coach be call , and the three gentleman go to the Duke 's Playhouse , as agree . the play be one of Mr. Wycherley 's " love in a wood . " 
 Harry Esmond have think of that play ever since with a kind of terror , and of Mrs. Bracegirdle , the actress who perform the girl 's part in the comedy . she be disguise as a page , and come and stand before the gentleman as they sit on the stage , and look over her shoulder with a pair of arch black eye , and laugh at my lord , and ask what ail the gentleman from the country , and have he have bad news from Bullock fair ? 
 between the act of the play the gentleman cross over and converse freely . there be two of Lord Mohun 's party , Captain Macartney , in a military habit , and a gentleman in a suit of blue velvet and silver in a fair periwig , with a rich fall of point of Venice lace my Lord the Earl of Warwick and Holland . my lord have a paper of orange , which he eat and offer to the actress , joke with they . and Mrs. Bracegirdle , when my Lord Mohun say something rude , turn on he , and ask he what he do there , and whether he and his friend have come to stab anybody else , as they do poor Will Mountford ? my lord 's dark face grow dark at this taunt , and wear a mischievous , fatal look . they that see it remember it , and say so afterward . 
 when the play be end the two party join company ; and my Lord Castlewood then propose that they should go to a tavern and sup . Lockit 's , the " Greyhound , " in Charing Cross , be the house select . all six march together that way ; the three lord go a - head , Lord Mohun 's captain , and Colonel Westbury , and Harry Esmond , walk behind they . as they walk , Westbury tell Harry Esmond about his old friend Dick the Scholar , who have get promotion , and be Cornet of the Guards , and have write a book call the " Christian Hero , " and have all the Guards to laugh at he for his pain , for the Christian Hero be break the commandment constantly , Westbury say , and have fight one or two duel already . and , in a low tone , Westbury beseech young Mr. Esmond to take no part in the quarrel . " there be no need for more second than one , " say the Colonel , " and the Captain or Lord Warwick might easily withdraw . " but Harry say no ; he be bent on go through with the business . indeed , he have a plan in his head , which , he think , might prevent my Lord Viscount from engage . 
 they go in at the bar of the tavern , and desire a private room and wine and card , and when the drawer have bring these , they begin to drink and call health , and as long as the servant be in the room appear very friendly . 
 Harry Esmond 's plan be no other than to engage in talk with Lord Mohun , to insult he , and so get the first of the quarrel . so when card be propose he offer to play . " Psha ! " say my Lord Mohun   whether wish to save Harry , or not choose , to try the botte de Jesuite , it be not to be know   " young gentleman from college should not play these stake . you be too young . " 
 " who dare say I be too young ? " break out Harry . " be your lordship afraid ? " 
 " Afraid ! " cry out Mohun . 
 but my good Lord Viscount see the move " I will play you for ten moidore , Mohun , " say he . " you silly boy , we do not   play for groat here as you do at Cambridge . " and Harry , who have no such sum in his pocket   for his half - year 's salary be always pretty well spend before it be due , fall back with rage and vexation in his heart that he have not money enough to stake . 
 " I will stake the young gentleman a crown , " say the Lord Mohun 's captain . 
 " I think crown be rather scarce with the gentleman of the army , " say Harry . 
 " do they birch at College ? " say the Captain . 
 " they birch fool , " say Harry , " and they cane bully , and they fling puppy into the water . " 
 " Faith , then , there be some escape drown , " say the Captain , who be an Irishman ; and all the gentleman begin to laugh , and make poor Harry only more angry . 
 my Lord Mohun presently snuff a candle . it be when the drawer bring in fresh bottle and glass and be in the room on which my Lord Viscount say " the Deuce take you , Mohun , how damned awkward you be . light the candle , you drawer . " 
 " damned awkward be a damned awkward expression , my lord , " say the other . " town gentleman do not   use such word or ask pardon if they do . " 
 " I be a country gentleman , " say my Lord Viscount . 
 " I see it by your manner , " say my Lord Mohun . " no man shall say damned awkward to I . " 
 " I fle the word in your face , my lord , " say the other ; ' shall I send the card too ? " 
 " gentleman , gentleman ! before the servant ? " cry out Colonel Westbury and my Lord Warwick in a breath . the drawer go out of the room hastily . they tell the people below of the quarrel up stair . 
 " enough have be say , " say Colonel Westbury . " will your lordship meet to - morrow morning ? " 
 " will my Lord Castlewood withdraw his word ? " ask the Earl of Warwick . 
 " my Lord Castlewood will be first , " say Colonel Westbury . 
 " then we have nothing for it . take notice , gentleman , there have be outrageous word reparation ask and refuse . " 
 " and refuse , " say my Lord Castlewood , put on his hat . " where shall the meeting be ? and when ? " 
 " since my Lord refuse I satisfaction , which I deeply regret , there be no time so good as now , " say my Lord Mohun . " let we have chair and go to Leicester Field . " 
 " be your lordship and I to have the honor of exchange a pass or two ? " say Colonel Westbury , with a low bow to my Lord of Warwick and Holland . 
 " it be an honor for I , " say my lord , with a profound congee , " to be match with a gentleman who have be at Mons and Namur . " 
 " will your Reverence permit I to give you a lesson ? " say the Captain . 
 " Nay , nay , gentleman , two on a side be plenty , " say Harry 's patron . " spare the boy , Captain Macartney , " and he shake Harry 's hand for the last time , save one , in his life . 
 at the bar of the tavern all the gentleman stop , and my Lord Viscount say , laugh , to the barwoman , that those card set people sadly a - quarrelling ; but that the dispute be over now , and the party be all go away to my Lord Mohun 's house , in Bow Street , to drink a bottle more before go to bed . 
 a half - dozen of chair be now call , and the six gentleman step into they , the word be privately give to the chairman to go to Leicester Field , where the gentleman be set down opposite the " Standard Tavern . " it be midnight , and the town be abe by this time , and only a few light in the window of the house ; but the night be bright enough for the unhappy purpose which the disputant come about ; and so all six enter into that fatal square , the chairman stand without the railing and keep the gate , lest any person should disturb the meeting . 
 all that happen there hath be matter of public notoriety , and be record , for warn to lawless man , in the annal of our country . after be engage for not more than a couple of minute , as Harry Esmond think   though be occupy at the time with his own adversary 's point , which be active , he may not have take a good note of time , a cry from the chairman without , who be smoke their pipe , and lean over the railing of the field as they watch the dim combat within , announce that some catastrophe have happen , which cause Esmond to drop his sword and look round , at which moment his enemy wound he in the right hand . but the young man do not heed this hurt much , and run up to the place where he see his dear master be down . 
 my Lord Mohun be stand over he . 
 " be you much hurt , Frank ? " he ask in a hollow voice . 
 " I believe I be a dead man , " my lord say from the ground . 
 " no , no , not so , " say the other ; " and I call God to witness , Frank Esmond , that I would have ask your pardon , have you but give I a chance . in in the first cause of our fall out , I swear that no one be to blame but I , and and that my lady " 
 " hush ! " say my poor Lord Viscount , lift himself on his elbow and speak faintly . " ' twas a dispute about the card the curse card . Harry my boy , be you wound , too ? God help thee ! I love thee , Harry , and thou must watch over my little Frank and and carry this little heart to my wife . " 
 and here my dear lord feel in his breast for a locket he wear there , and , in the act , fall back faint . 
 we be all at this terrified , think he dead ; but Esmond and Colonel Westbury bade the chairman come into the field ; and so my lord be carry to one Mr. Aimes , a surgeon , in Long Acre , who keep a bath , and there the house be waken up , and the victim of this quarrel carry in . 
 my Lord Viscount be put to bed , and his wound look to by the surgeon , who seem both kind and skilful . when he have look to my lord , he bandage up Harry Esmond 's hand   who , from loss of blood , have faint too , in the house , and may have be some time unconscious ; and when the young man come to himself , you may be sure he eagerly ask what news there be of his dear patron ; on which the surgeon carry he to the room where the Lord Castlewood lay ; who have already send for a priest ; and desire earnestly , they say , to speak with his kinsman . he be lie on a bed , very pale and ghastly , with that fix , fatal look in his eye , which betoken death ; and faintly beckon all the other person away from he with his hand , and cry out " only Harry Esmond , " the hand fall powerless down on the coverlet , as Harry come forward , and kneel down and kiss it . 
 " thou art all but a priest , Harry , " my Lord Viscount gasp out , with a faint smile , and pressure of his cold hand . " be they all go ? let I make thee a death - bed confession . " 
 and with sacred Death wait , as it be , at the bed - foot , as an awful witness of his word , the poor die soul gasp out his last wish in respect of his family ; his humble profession of contrition for his fault ; and his charity towards the world he be leave . some thing he say concern Harry Esmond as much as they astonish he . and my Lord Viscount , sink visibly , be in the midst of these strange confession , when the ecclesiastic for whom my lord have send , Mr. Atterbury , arrive . 
 this gentleman have reach to no great church dignity as yet , but be only preacher at St. Bride 's , draw all the town thither by his eloquent sermon . he be godson to my lord , who have be pupil to his father ; have pay a visit to Castlewood from Oxford more than once ; and it be by his advice , I think , that Harry Esmond be send to Cambridge , rather than to Oxford , of which place Mr. Atterbury , though a distinguished member , speak but ill . 
 our messenger find the good priest already at his book at five o'clock in the morning , and he follow the man eagerly to the house where my poor Lord Viscount lie Esmond watch he , and take his die word from his mouth . 
 my lord , hear of Mr. Atterbury 's arrival , and squeeze Esmond 's hand , ask to be alone with the priest ; and Esmond leave they there for this solemn interview . you may be sure that his own prayer and grief accompany that die benefactor . my lord have say to he that which confound the young man inform he of a secret which greatly concern he . indeed , after hear it , he have have good cause for doubt and dismay ; for mental anguish as well as resolution . while the colloquy between Mr. Atterbury and his die penitent take place within , an immense contest of perplexity be agitate Lord Castlewood 's young companion . 
 at the end of an hour it may be more Mr. Atterbury come out of the room , look very hard at Esmond , and hold a paper . 
 " he be on the brink of God 's awful judgment , " the priest whisper . " he have make his breast clean to I . he forgive and believe , and make restitution . shall it be in public ? shall we call a witness to sign it ? " 
 " God know , " sob out the young man , " my dear lord have only do I kindness all his life . " 
 the priest put the paper into Esmond 's hand . he look at it . it swam before his eye . 
 " ' tis a confession , " he say . 
 " ' tis as you please , " say Mr. Atterbury . 
 there be a fire in the room where the cloth be dry for the bath , and there lie a heap in a corner saturate with the blood of my dear lord 's body . esmond go to the fire , and throw the paper into it . ' twas a great chimney with glaze dutch tile . how we remember such trifle at such awful moment ! the scrap of the book that we have read in a great grief the taste of that last dish that we have eat before a duel , or some such supreme meeting or parting . on the dutch tile at the Bagnio be a rude picture represent Jacob in hairy glove , cheat Isaac of Esau 's birthright . the burn paper light it up . 
 " ' tis only a confession , Mr. Atterbury , " say the young man . he lean his head against the mantel - piece : a burst of tear come to his eye . they be the first he have shed as he sit by his lord , scar by this calamity , and more yet by what the poor die gentleman have tell he , and shock to think that he should be the agent of bring this double misfortune on those he love well . 
 " let we go to he , " say Mr. Esmond . and accordingly they go into the next chamber , where by this time , the dawn have break , which show my lord 's poor pale face and wild appeal eye , that wear that awful fatal look of come dissolution . the surgeon be with he . he go into the chamber as Atterbury come out thence . my Lord Viscount turn round his sick eye towards Esmond . it choke the other to hear that rattle in his throat . 
 " my Lord Viscount , " say Mr. Atterbury , " Mr. Esmond want no witness , and hath burn the paper . " 
 " my dear master ! " Esmond say , kneel down , and take his hand and kiss it . 
 my Lord Viscount spring up in his bed , and fling his arm round Esmond . " God bl bless " be all he say . the blood rush from his mouth , deluge the young man . my dear lord be no more . he be go with a blessing on his lip , and love and repentance and kindness in his manly heart . 
 " Benedicti benedicente , " say Mr. Atterbury , and the young man , kneel at the bedside , groan out an " amen . " 
 " who shall take the news to she ? " be Mr. Esmond 's next thought . and on this he beseech Mr. Atterbury to bear the tiding to Castlewood . he could not face his mistress himself with those dreadful news . Mr. Atterbury comply kindly , Esmond writ a hasty note on his table - book to my lord 's man , bid he get the horse for Mr. Atterbury , and ride with he , and send Esmond 's own valise to the Gatehouse prison , whither he resolve to go and give himself up . 




 BOOK II . 

 contain MR . ESMOND 'S MILITARY life , and other matter appertain to the ESMOND FAMILY . 




 CHAPTER I. 

 I be in prison , and visit , but not console there . 

 those may imagine , who have see death untimely strike down person revere and beloved , and know how unavaile consolation be , what be Harry Esmond 's anguish after be an actor in that ghastly midnight scene of blood and homicide . he could not , he feel , have face his dear mistress , and tell she that story . he be thankful that kind Atterbury consent to break the sad news to she ; but , besides his grief , which he take into prison with he , he have that in his heart which secretly cheer and console he . 
 a great secret have be tell to Esmond by his unhappy stricken kinsman , lie on his death - bed . be he to disclose it , as in equity and honor he might do , the discovery would but bring great grief upon those whom he love well in the world , and who be sad enough already . should he bring down shame and perplexity upon all those being to whom he be attach by so many tender tie of affection and gratitude ? degrade his father 's widow ? impeach and sully his father 's and kinsman 's honor ? and for what ? for a barren title , to be wear at the expense of an innocent boy , the son of his dear benefactress . he have debate this matter in his conscience , whilst his poor lord be make his die confession . on one side be ambition , temptation , justice even ; but love , gratitude , and fidelity , plead on the other . and when the struggle be over in Harry 's mind , a glow of righteous happiness fill it ; and it be with grateful tear in his eye that he return thank to God for that decision which he have be enable to make . 
 " when I be deny by my own blood , " think he , " these dear friend receive and cherish I . when I be a nameless orphan myself , and need a protector , I find one in yonder kind soul , who have go to his account repenting of the innocent wrong he have do . " 
 and with this consoling think he go away to give himself up at the prison , after kiss the cold lip of his benefactor . 
 it be on the third day after he have come to the Gatehouse prison ,   where he lie in no small pain from his wound , which inflame and ache severely ,   and with those thought and resolution that have be just speak of , to depress , and yet to console he , that H. Esmond 's keeper come and tell he that a visitor be ask for he , and though he could not see her face , which be envelop in a black hood , her whole figure , too , be veil and cover with the deep mourning , Esmond know at once that his visitor be his dear mistress . 
 he get up from his bed , where he be lie , be very weak ; and advance towards she as the retire keeper shut the door upon he and his guest in that sad place , he put forward his left hand   for the right be wound and bandage , and he would have take that kind one of his mistress , which have do so many office of friendship for he for so many year . 
 but the Lady Castlewood go back from he , put back her hood , and lean against the great stanchioned door which the gaoler have just close upon they . her face be ghastly white , as Esmond see it , look from the hood ; and her eye , ordinarily so sweet and tender , be fix on he with such a tragic glance of woe and anger , as cause the young man , unaccustomed to unkindness from that person , to avert his own glance from her face . 
 " and this , Mr. Esmond , " she say , " be where I see you ; and ' tis to this you have bring I ! " 
 " you have come to console I in my calamity , madam , " say he   though , in truth , he scarce know how to address she , his emotion at behold she so overpower he . 
 she advance a little , but stand silent and tremble , look out at he from her black drapery , with her small white hand clasp together , and quiver lip and hollow eye . 
 " not to reproach I , " he continue after a pause . " my grief be sufficient as it be . " 
 " take back your hand do not touch I with it ! " she cry . " look ! there be blood on it ! " 
 " I wish they have take it all , " say Esmond ; " if you be unkind to I . " 
 " where be my husband ? " she break out . " give I back my husband , Henry . why do you stand by at midnight and see he murder ? why do the traitor escape who do it ? you , the champion of your house , who offer to die for we ! you that he love and trust , and to whom I confide he you that vow devotion and gratitude , and I believe you yes , I believe you why be you here , and my noble Francis go ? why do you come among we ? you have only bring we grief and sorrow ; and repentance , bitter , bitter repentance , as a return for our love and kindness . do I ever do you a wrong , Henry ? you be but an orphan child when I first see you when he first see you , who be so good , and noble , and trust . he would have have you send away , but , like a foolish woman , I besought he to let you stay . and you pretend to love we , and we believe you and you make our house wretche , and my husband 's heart go from I : and I lose he through you I lose he the husband of my youth , I say . I worship he : you know I worship he and he be change to I . he be no more my Francis of old my dear , dear soldier . he love I before he see you ; and I love he . oh , God be my witness how I love he ! why do he not send you from among we ? ' twas only his kindness , that could refuse I nothing then . and , young as you be yes , and weak and alone there be evil , I know there be evil in keep you . I read it in your face and eye . I see that they bode harm to we and it come , I know it would . why do you not die when you have the small - pox and I come myself and watch you , and you do not   know I in your delirium and you call out for I , though I be there at your side ? all that have happen since , be a just judgment on my wicked heart my wicked jealous heart . oh , I be punish awfully punish ! my husband lie in his blood murder for defend I , my kind , kind , generous lord and you be by , and you let he die , Henry ! " 
 these word , utter in the wildness of her grief , by one who be ordinarily quiet , and speak seldom except with a gentle smile and a soothe tone , ring in Esmond 's ear ; and ' tis say that he repeat many of they in the fever into which he now fall from his wound , and perhaps from the emotion which such passionate , undeserved upbraiding cause he . it seem as if his very sacrifice and love for this lady and her family be to turn to evil and reproach : as if his presence amongst they be indeed a cause of grief , and the continuance of his life but woe and bitterness to theirs . as the Lady Castlewood speak bitterly , rapidly , without a tear , he never offer a word of appeal or remonstrance : but sit at the foot of his prison - bed , stricken only with the more pain at think it be that soft and beloved hand which should stab he so cruelly , and powerless against her fatal sorrow . her word as she speak strike the chord of all his memory , and the whole of his boyhood and youth pass within he ; whilst this lady , so fond and gentle but yesterday this good angel whom he have love and worship stand before he , pursue he with keen word and aspect malign . 
 " I wish I be in my lord 's place , " he groan out . " it be not my fault that I be not there , madam . but Fate be strong than all of we , and will what have come to pass . it have be well for I to have die when I have the illness . " 
 " yes , Henry , " say she and as she speak she look at he with a glance that be at once so fond and so sad , that the young man , toss up his arm , wildly fall back , hide his head in the coverlet of the bed . as he turn he strike against the wall with his wounded hand , displace the ligature ; and he feel the blood rush again from the wound . he remember feel a secret pleasure at the accident and thinking , " suppose I be to end now , who would grieve for I ? " 
 this hemorrhage , or the grief and despair in which the luckless young man be at the time of the accident , must have bring on a deliquium presently ; for he have scarce any recollection afterwards , save of some one , his mistress probably , seize his hand and then of the buzzing noise in his ear as he awake , with two or three person of the prison around his bed , whereon he lie in a pool of blood from his arm . 
 it be now bandage up again by the prison surgeon , who happen to be in the place ; and the governor 's wife and servant , kind people both , be with the patient . esmond see his mistress still in the room when he awake from his trance ; but she go away without a word ; though the governor 's wife tell he that she sit in her room for some time afterward , and do not leave the prison until she hear that Esmond be likely to do well . 
 day afterwards , when Esmond be bring out of a fever which he have , and which attack he that night pretty sharply , the honest keeper 's wife bring her patient a handkerchief fresh washed and iron , and at the corner of which he recognize his mistress 's well - know cipher and viscountess 's crown . " the lady have bind it round his arm when he faint , and before she call for help , " the keeper 's wife say . " poor lady ! she take on sadly about her husband . he have be bury to - day , and a many of the coach of the nobility go with he my Lord Marlborough 's and my Lord Sunderland 's , and many of the officer of the Guards , in which he serve in the old King 's time ; and my lady have be with her two child to the King at Kensington , and ask for justice against my Lord Mohun , who be in hiding , and my Lord the Earl of Warwick and Holland , who be ready to give himself up and take his trial . " 
 such be the news , couple with assertion about her own honesty and that of molly her maid , who would never have steal a certain trumpery gold sleeve - button of Mr. Esmond 's that be miss after his fainting fit , that the keeper 's wife bring to her lodger . his thought follow to that untimely grave , the brave heart , the kind friend , the gallant gentleman , honest of word and generous of thought ,   if feeble of purpose , but be his better much strong than he ?   who have give he bread and shelter when he have none ; home and love when he need they ; and who , if he have keep one vital secret from he , have do that of which he repent ere die a wrong indeed , but one follow by remorse , and occasion by almost irresistible temptation . 
 Esmond take his handkerchief when his nurse leave he , and very likely kiss it , and look at the bauble embroider in the corner . " it have cost thee grief enough , " he think , " dear lady , so loving and so tender . shall I take it from thee and thy child ? no , never ! keep it , and wear it , my little Frank , my pretty boy . if I can not make a name for myself , I can die without one . some day , when my dear mistress see my heart , I shall be right ; or if not here or now , why , elsewhere ; where Honor doth not follow we , but where Love reign perpetual . " 
 ' tis needless to relate here , as the report of the lawyer already have chronicle they , the particular or issue of that trial which ensue upon my Lord Castlewood 's melancholy homicide . of the two lord engage in that sad matter , the second , my Lord the Earl of Warwick and Holland , who have be engage with Colonel Westbury , and wound by he , be find not guilty by his peer , before whom he be try   under the presidence of the Lord Steward , Lord Somers ; and the principal , the Lord Mohun , be find guilty of the manslaughter ,   which , indeed , be force upon he , and of which he repent most sincerely ,   plead his clergy , and so be discharge without any penalty . the widow of the slain nobleman , as it be tell we in prison , show an extraordinary spirit ; and , though she have to wait for ten year before her son be old enough to compass it , declare she would have revenge of her husband 's murderer . so much and suddenly have grief , anger , and misfortune appear to change she . but fortune , good or ill , as I take it , do not change man and woman . it but develop their character . as there be a thousand thought lie within a man that he do not know till he take up the pen to write , so the heart be a secret even to he   or she   who have it in his own breast . who hath not find himself surprised into revenge , or action , or passion , for good or evil , whereof the seed lie within he , latent and unsuspected , until the occasion call they forth ? with the death of her lord , a change seem to come over the whole conduct and mind of Lady Castlewood ; but of this we shall speak in the right season and anon . 
 the lord be try then before their peer at Westminster , accord to their privilege , be bring from the Tower with state procession and barge , and accompany by lieutenant and axe - man , the commoner engage in that melancholy fray take their trial at Newgate , as become they ; and , be all find guilty , plead likewise their benefit of clergy . the sentence , as we all know in these case , be , that the culprit lie a year in prison , or during the King 's pleasure , and be burn in the hand , or only stamp with a cold iron ; or this part of the punishment be altogether remit at the grace of the Sovereign . so Harry Esmond find himself a criminal and a prisoner at two - and - twenty year old ; as for the two colonel , his comrade , they take the matter very lightly . duelling be a part of their business ; and they could not in honor refuse any invitation of that sort . 
 but the case be different with Mr. Esmond . his life be change by that stroke of the sword which destroy his kind patron 's . as he lie in prison , old Dr. Tusher fall ill and die ; and Lady Castlewood appoint Thomas Tusher to the vacant living ; about the filling of which she have a thousand time fondly talk to Harry Esmond : how they never should part ; how he should educate her boy ; how to be a country clergyman , like saintly George Herbert or pious Dr. Ken , be the happy and great lot in life ; how   if he be obstinately bent on it , though , for her part , she own rather to hold Queen Bess 's opinion , that a bishop should have no wife , and if not a bishop why a clergyman ?   she would find a good wife for Harry Esmond : and so on , with a hundred pretty prospect tell by fireside evening , in fond prattle , as the child play about the hall . all these plan be overthrow now . Thomas Tusher write to Esmond , as he lie in prison , announce that his patroness have confer upon he the live his reverend father have hold for many year ; that she never , after the tragical event which have occur   whereof Tom speak with a very edify horror , could see in the revere Tusher 's pulpit , or at her son 's table , the man who be answerable for the father 's life ; that her ladyship bid he to say that she pray for her kinsman 's repentance and his worldly happiness ; that he be free to command her aid for any scheme of life which he might propose to himself ; but that on this side of the grave she would see he no more . and Tusher , for his own part , add that Harry should have his prayer as a friend of his youth , and commend he whilst he be in prison to read certain work of theology , which his reverence pronounce to be very wholesome for sinner in his lamentable condition . 
 and this be the return for a life of devotion this the end of year of affectionate intercourse and passionate fidelity ! Harry would have die for his patron , and be hold as little well than his murderer : he have sacrifice , she do not know how much , for his mistress , and she throw he aside ; he have endow her family with all they have , and she talk about give he alm as to a menial ! the grief for his patron 's loss ; the pain of his own present position , and doubt as to the future : all these be forget under the sense of the consummate outrage which he have to endure , and overpower by the superior pang of that torture . 
 he writ back a letter to Mr. Tusher from his prison , congratulate his reverence upon his appointment to the living of Castlewood : sarcastically bid he to follow in the footstep of his admirable father , whose gown have descend upon he ; thank her ladyship for her offer of alm , which he say he should trust not to need ; and beseech she to remember that , if ever her determination should change towards he , he would be ready to give her proof of a fidelity which have never waver , and which ought never to have be question by that house . " and if we meet no more , or only as stranger in this world , " Mr. Esmond conclude , " a sentence against the cruelty and injustice of which I disdain to appeal ; hereafter she will know who be faithful to she , and whether she have any cause to suspect the love and devotion of her kinsman and servant . " 
 after the sending of this letter , the poor young fellow 's mind be more at ease than it have be previously . the blow have be strike , and he have bear it . his cruel goddess have shake her wing and flee : and leave he alone and friendless , but virtute sua . and he have to bear he up , at once the sense of his right and the feeling of his wrong , his honor and his misfortune . as I have see man wake and run to arm at a sudden trumpet , before emergency a manly heart leap up resolute ; meet the threatening danger with undaunted countenance ; and , whether conquered or conquering , face it always . ah ! no man know his strength or his weakness , till occasion prove they . if there be some thought and action of his life from the memory of which a man shrink with shame , sure there be some which he may be proud to own and remember ; forgive injury , conquer temptation   now and then   and difficulty vanquish by endurance . 
 it be these thought regard the living , far more than any great poignancy of grief respect the dead , which affect Harry Esmond whilst in prison after his trial : but it may be imagine that he could take no comrade of misfortune into the confidence of his feeling , and they think it be remorse and sorrow for his patron 's loss which affect the young man , in error of which opinion he choose to leave they . as a companion he be so moody and silent that the two officer , his fellow - sufferer , leave he to himself mostly , like little very likely what they know of he , console themselves with dice , card , and the bottle , and while away their own captivity in their own way . it seem to Esmond as if he live year in that prison : and be change and age when he come out of it . at certain period of life we live year of emotion in a few week and look back on those time , as on great gap between the old life and the new . you do not know how much you suffer in those critical malady of the heart , until the disease be over and you look back on it afterwards . during the time , the suffering be at least sufferable . the day pass in more or less of pain , and the night wear away somehow . ' tis only in after day that we see what the danger have be as a man out a - hunting or rid for his life look at a leap , and wonder how he should have survive the taking of it . o dark month of grief and rage ! of wrong and cruel endurance ! he be old now who recall you . long ago he have forgive and bl the soft hand that wound he : but the mark be there , and the wound be cicatrize only no time , tear , caress , or repentance , can obliterate the scar . we be indocile to put up with grief , however . Reficimus rate quassas : we tempt the ocean again and again , and try upon new venture . esmond think of his early time as a novitiate , and of this past trial as an initiation before enter into life as our young Indians undergo torture silently before they pass to the rank of warrior in the tribe . 
 the officer , meanwhile , who be not let into the secret of the grief which be gnaw at the side of their silent young friend , and be accustom to such transaction , in which one comrade or another be daily pay the forfeit of the sword , do not , of course , bemoan themselves very inconsolably about the fate of their late companion in arm . this one tell story of former adventure of love , or war , or pleasure , in which poor Frank Esmond have be engage ; t'other recollect how a constable have be bilk , or a tavern - bully beat : whilst my lord 's poor widow be sit at his tomb worship he as an actual saint and spotless hero so the visitor say who have news of Lady Castlewood ; and Westbury and Macartney have pretty nearly have all the town to come and see they . 
 the duel , its fatal termination , the trial of the two peer and the three commoner concern , have cause the great excitement in the town . the print and News Letters be full of they . the three gentleman in Newgate be almost as much crowded as the bishop in the Tower , or a highwayman before execution . we be allow to live in the Governor 's house , as hath be say , both before trial and after condemnation , wait the King 's pleasure ; nor be the real cause of the fatal quarrel know , so closely have my lord and the two other person who know it keep the secret , but every one imagine that the origin of the meeting be a gambling dispute . except fresh air , the prisoner have , upon payment , most thing they could desire . interest be make that they should not mix with the vulgar convict , whose ribald chorus and loud laughter and curse could be hear from their own part of the prison , where they and the miserable debtor be confine pell - mell . 




 CHAPTER II . 

 I come to the END of MY CAPTIVITY , but not of MY TROUBLE . 

 among the company which come to visit the two officer be an old acquaintance of Harry Esmond ; that gentleman of the Guards , namely , who have be so kind to Harry when Captain Westbury 's troop have be quarter at Castlewood more than seven year before . Dick the Scholar be no long Dick the Trooper now , but Captain Steele of Lucas 's Fusiliers , and secretary to my Lord Cutts , that famous officer of King William 's , the brave and most beloved man of the english army . the two jolly prisoner have be drink with a party of friend   for our cellar and that of the keeper of Newgate , too , be supply with endless hamper of Burgundy and Champagne that the friend of the Colonels send in ; and Harry , have no wish for their drink or their conversation , be too feeble in health for the one and too sad in spirit for the other , be sit apart in his little room , read such book as he have , one evening , when honest Colonel Westbury , flush with liquor , and always good - humor in and out of his cup , come laugh into Harry 's closet and say , " Ho , young Killjoy ! here be a friend come to see thee ; he will pray with thee , or he will drink with thee ; or he will drink and pray turn about . Dick , my christian hero , here be the little scholar of Castlewood . " 
 Dick come up and kiss Esmond on both cheek , impart a strong perfume of burn sack along with his caress to the young man . 
 " what ! be this the little man that use to talk Latin and fetch our bowl ? how tall thou art grow ! I protest I should have know thee anywhere . and so you have turn ruffian and fighter ; and want to measure sword with Mohun , do you ? I protest that Mohun say at the Guard dinner yesterday , where there be a pretty company of we , that the young fellow want to fight he , and be the well man of the two . " 
 " I wish we could have try and prove it , Mr. Steele , " say Esmond , think of his dead benefactor , and his eye fill with tear . 
 with the exception of that one cruel letter which he have from his mistress , Mr. Esmond hear nothing from she , and she seem determined to execute her resolve of part from he and disown he . but he have news of she , such as it be , which Mr. Steele assiduously bring he from the Prince 's and Princess 's Court , where our honest Captain have be advance to the post of gentleman waiter . when off duty there , Captain Dick often come to console his friend in captivity ; a good nature and a friendly disposition towards all who be in ill - fortune no doubt prompt he to make his visit , and good - fellowship and good wine to prolong they . 
 " faith , " say Westbury , " the little scholar be the first to begin the quarrel I mind I of it now at Lockit 's . I always hat that fellow Mohun . what be the real cause , of the quarrel betwixt he and poor Frank ? I would wager ' twas a woman . " 
 " ' twas a quarrel about play on my word , about play , " Harry say . " my poor lord lose great sum to his guest at Castlewood . angry word pass between they ; and , though Lord Castlewood be the kind and most pliable soul alive , his spirit be very high ; and hence that meeting which have bring we all here , " say Mr. Esmond , resolve never to acknowledge that there have ever be any other cause but card for the duel . 
 " I do not like to use bad word of a nobleman , " say Westbury ; " but if my Lord Mohun be a commoner , I would say , ' twas a pity he be not hang . he be familiar with dice and woman at a time other boy be at school be birch ; he be as wicked as the old rake , year ere he have do grow ; and handle a sword and a foil , and a bloody one , too , before he ever use a razor . he hold poor Will Mountford in talk that night , when bloody Dick Hill run he through . he will come to a bad end , will that young lord ; and no end be bad enough for he , " say honest Mr. Westbury : whose prophecy be fulfil twelve year after , upon that fatal day when Mohun fall , drag down one of the brave and great gentleman in England in his fall . 
 from Mr. Steele , then , who bring the public rumor , as well as his own private intelligence , Esmond learn the movement of his unfortunate mistress . Steele 's heart be of very inflammable composition ; and the gentleman usher speak in term of boundless admiration both of the widow   that most beautiful woman , as he say   and of her daughter , who , in the Captain 's eye , be a still great paragon . if the pale widow , whom Captain Richard , in his poetic rapture compare to a Niobe in tear to a Sigismunda to a weep Belvidera , be an object the most lovely and pathetic which his eye have ever behold , or for which his heart have melt , even her ripened perfection and beauty be as nothing compare to the promise of that extreme loveliness which the good Captain see in her daughter . it be matre pulcra filia pulcrior . Steele compose sonnet whilst he be on duty in his Prince 's ante - chamber , to the maternal and filial charm . he would speak for hour about they to Harry Esmond ; and , indeed , he could have choose few subject more likely to interest the unhappy young man , whose heart be now as always devoted to these lady ; and who be thankful to all who love they , or praise they , or wish they well . 
 not that his fidelity be recompense by any answer kindness , or show of relent even , on the part of a mistress obdurate now after ten year of love and benefaction . the poor young man get no answer , save Tusher 's , to that letter which he have write , and be too proud to write more , open a part of his heart to Steele , than whom no man , when unhappy , could find a kind hearer , or more friendly emissary ; describe   in word which be no doubt pathetic , for they come imo pectore , and cause honest Dick to weep plentifully   his youth , his constancy , his fond devotion to that household which have rear he ; his affection , how earn , and how tenderly requite until but yesterday , and   as far as he might   the circumstance and cause for which that sad quarrel have make of Esmond a prisoner under sentence , a widow and orphan of those whom in life he hold dearest . in term that might well move a hard - hearted man than young Esmond 's confidant for , indeed , the speaker 's own heart be half broke as he utter they he describe a part of what have take place in that only sad interview which his mistress have grant he ; how she have leave he with anger and almost imprecation , whose word and thought until then have be only blessing and kindness ; how she have accuse he of the guilt of that blood , in exchange for which he would cheerfully have sacrifice his own   indeed , in this the Lord Mohun , the Lord Warwick , and all the gentleman engage , as well as the common rumor out of door Steele tell he bear out the luckless young man ; and with all his heart , and tear , he beseech Mr. Steele to inform his mistress of her kinsman 's unhappiness , and to deprecate that cruel anger she show he . half frantic with grief at the injustice do he , and contrast it with a thousand soft recollection of love and confidence go by , that make his present misery inexpressibly more bitter , the poor wretch pass many a lonely day and wakeful night in a kind of powerless despair and rage against his iniquitous fortune . it be the soft hand that strike he , the gentle and most compassionate nature that persecute he . " I would as lief , " he say , " have plead guilty to the murder , and have suffer for it like any other felon , as have to endure the torture to which my mistress subject I . " 
 although the recital of Esmond 's story , and his passionate appeal and remonstrance , draw so many tear from Dick who hear they , they have no effect upon the person whom they be design to move . Esmond 's ambassador come back from the mission with which the poor young gentleman have charge he , with a sad blank face and a shake of the head , which tell that there be no hope for the prisoner ; and scarce a wretched culprit in that prison of Newgate order for execution , and tremble for a reprieve , feel more cast down than Mr. Esmond , innocent and condemn . 
 as have be arrange between the prisoner and his counsel in their consultation , Mr. Steele have go to the dowager 's house in Chelsey , where it have be say the widow and her orphan be , have see my Lady Viscountess , and plead the cause of her unfortunate kinsman . " and I think I speak well , my poor boy , " say Mr. Steele ; " for who would not speak well in such a cause , and before so beautiful a judge ? I do not see the lovely beatrix   sure her famous namesake of Florence be never half so beautiful , only the young Viscount be in the room with the Lord Churchill , my Lord of Marlborough 's eld son . but these young gentleman go off to the garden ; I could see they from the window tilting at each other with pole in a mimic tournament   grief touch the young but lightly , and I remember that I beat a drum at the coffin of my own father . my Lady Viscountess look out at the two boy at their game and say ' you see , sir , child be teach to use weapon of death as toy , and to make a sport of murder ; ' and as she speak she look so lovely , and stand there in herself so sad and beautiful , an instance of that doctrine whereof I be a humble preacher , that have I not dedicate my little volume of the ' Christian Hero '   I perceive , Harry , thou hast not cut the leaf of it . the sermon be good , believe I , though the preacher 's life may not answer it   I say , have not   I dedicate the volume to Lord Cutts , I would have ask permission to place her ladyship 's name on the first page . I think I never see such a beautiful violet as that of her eye , Harry . her complexion be of the pink of the blush - rose , she hath an exquisite turn wrist and dimpled hand , and I make no doubt " 
 " do you come to tell I about the dimple on my lady 's hand ? " break out Mr. Esmond , sadly . 
 " a lovely creature in affliction seem always doubly beautiful to I , " say the poor Captain , who indeed be but too often in a state to see double , and so check he resume the interrupted thread of his story . " as I speak my business , " Mr. Steele say , " and narrate to your mistress what all the world know , and the other side hath be eager to acknowledge that you have try to put yourself between the two lord , and to take your patron 's quarrel on your own point ; I recount the general praise of your gallantry , besides my Lord Mohun 's particular testimony to it ; I think the widow listen with some interest , and her eye I have never see such a violet , Harry look up at mine once or twice . but after I have speak on this theme for a while she suddenly break away with a cry of grief . ' I would to God , sir , ' she say , ' I have never hear that word gallantry which you use , or know the meaning of it . my lord might have be here but for that ; my home might be happy ; my poor boy have a father . it be what you gentleman call gallantry come into my home , and drive my husband on to the cruel sword that kill he . you should not speak the word to a christian woman , sir , a poor widow mother of orphan , whose home be happy until the world come into it the wicked godless world , that take the blood of the innocent , and let the guilty go free . ' 
 " as the afflict lady speak in this strain , sir , " Mr. Steele continue , " it seem as if indignation move she , even more than grief . ' Compensation ! ' she go on passionately , her cheek and eye kindling ; ' what compensation do your world give the widow for her husband , and the child for the murderer of their father ? the wretch who do the deed have not even a punishment . Conscience ! what conscience have he , who can enter the house of a friend , whisper falsehood and insult to a woman that never harm he , and stab the kind heart that trust he ? my Lord my Lord Wretch 's , my Lord Villain 's , my Lord Murderer 's peer meet to try he , and they dismiss he with a word or two of reproof and send he into the world again , to pursue woman with lust and falsehood , and to murder unsuspecting guest that harbor he . that day , my Lord my Lord Murderer   I will never name he   be let loose , a woman be execute at Tyburn for steal in a shop . but a man may rob another of his life , or a lady of her honor , and shall pay no penalty ! I take my child , run to the throne , and on my knee ask for justice , and the King refuse I . the King ! he be no king of mine he never shall be . he , too , rob the throne from the king his father the true king and he have go unpunished , as the great do . ' 
 " I then think to speak for you , " Mr. Steele continue , " and I interpose by say , ' there be one , madam , who , at least , would have put his own breast between your husband 's and my Lord Mohun 's sword . your poor young kinsman , Harry Esmond , hath tell I that he try to draw the quarrel on himself . ' 
 " ' be you come from HIM ? ' ask the lady   so Mr. Steele go on   rise up with a great severity and stateliness . ' I think you have come from the Princess . I see Mr. Esmond in his prison , and bid he farewell . he bring misery into my house . he never should have enter it . ' 
 " ' Madam , madam , he be not to blame , ' I interpose , " continue Mr. Steele . 
 " ' do I blame he to you , sir ? ' ask the widow . ' if ' tis he who send you , say that I have take counsel , where ' she speak with a very pallid cheek now , and a break in her voice ' where all who ask may have it ; and that it bid I to part from he , and to see he no more . we meet in the prison for the last time at least for year to come . it may be , in year hence , when when our knee and our tear and our contrition have change our sinful heart , sir , and work our pardon , we may meet again but not now . after what have pass , I could not bear to see he . I wish he well , sir ; but I wish he farewell , too ; and if he have that that regard towards we which he speak of , I beseech he to prove it by obey I in this . ' 
 " ' I shall break the young man 's heart , madam , by this hard sentence , ' " Mr. Steele say . 
 " the lady shake her head , " continue my kind scholar . " ' the heart of young man , Mr. Steele , be not so make , ' she say . ' Mr. Esmond will find other other friend . the mistress of this house have relent very much towards the late lord 's son , ' she add , with a blush , ' and have promise I , that is , have promise that she will care for his fortune . whilst I live in it , after the horrid horrid deed which have pass , Castlewood must never be a home to he never . nor would I have he write to I except no I would have he never write to I , nor see he more . give he , if you will , my part Hush ! not a word of this before my daughter . ' 
 " here the fair beatrix enter from the river , with her cheek flush with health , and look only the more lovely and fresh for the mourning habiliment which she wear . and my Lady Viscountess say 
 " ' beatrix , this be Mr. Steele , gentleman - usher to the Prince 's Highness . when do your new comedy appear , Mr. Steele ? ' I hope thou wilt be out of prison for the first night , Harry . " 
 the sentimental Captain conclude his sad tale , say , " Faith , the beauty of Filia pulcrior drive pulcram matrem out of my head ; and yet as I come down the river , and think about the pair , the pallid dignity and exquisite grace of the matron have the uppermost , and I think she even more noble than the virgin ! " 
 the party of prisoner live very well in Newgate , and with comfort very different to those which be award to the poor wretch there   his insensibility to their misery , their gayety still more frightful , their curse and blasphemy , hath strike with a kind of shame since as prove how selfish , during his imprisonment , his own particular grief be , and how entirely the thought of it absorb he : if the three gentleman live well under the care of the Warden of Newgate , it be because they pay well : and indeed the cost at the dear ordinary or the grand tavern in London could not have furnish a long reckoning , than our host of the " Handcuff Inn " as Colonel Westbury call it . our room be the three in the gate over Newgate on the second story look up Newgate Street towards Cheapside and Paul 's Church . and we have leave to walk on the roof , and could see thence Smithfield and the Bluecoat Boys ' School , Gardens , and the Chartreux , where , as Harry Esmond remember , Dick the Scholar , and his friend Tom Tusher , have have their schooling . 
 Harry could never have pay his share of that prodigious heavy reckoning which my landlord bring to his guest once a week : for he have but three piece in his pocket that fatal night before the duel , when the gentleman be at card , and offer to play five . but whilst he be yet ill at the Gatehouse , after Lady Castlewood have visit he there , and before his trial , there come one in an orange - tawny coat and blue lace , the livery which the esmond always wear , and bring a sealed packet for Mr. Esmond , which contain twenty guineas , and a note say that a counsel have be appoint for he , and that more money would be forthcoming whenever he need it . 
 ' twas a queer letter from the scholar as she be , or as she call herself : the Dowager Viscountess Castlewood , write in the strange barbarous french which she and many other fine lady of that time witness her Grace of Portsmouth employ . indeed , spelling be not an article of general commodity in the world then , and my Lord Marlborough 's letter can show that he , for one , have but a little share of this part of grammar : 
 " MONG COUSSIN , " my Lady Viscountess Dowager write , " je scay que vous vous ete bravement batew et grievement blessay du coste de feu M. le Vicomte . M. le Compte de Varique ne se playt qua parlay de vous : M. de Moon aucy . Il di que vous avay voulew vous bastre avecque luy que vous este plus fort que luy fur l'ayscrimme quil'y a surtout certaine Botte que vous scavay quil n'a jammay sceu pariay : et que c'en eut ete fay de luy si vouseluy vous vous fussiay battew ansamb . Aincy ce pauv Vicompte est mort . Mort et pontayt Mon coussin , mon coussin ! jay dans la tayste que vous n'este quung pety Monst angcy que les esmond ong tousjour este . La veuve est chay moy . J'ay recuilly cet ' pauve famme . Elle est furieuse cont vous , allan tous les jours chercher ley Roy   d'icy   demandant a gran cri revanche pour son Mary . Elle ne veux voyre ni entende parlay de vous : pourtant elle ne fay qu'en parlay milfoy par jour . quand vous seray hor prison venay I voyre . j'auray soe de vous . si cette petite Prude veut se defaire de song pety Monste   Helas je crae quil ne soy trotar !   je m'on chargeray . J'ay encor quelqu interay et quelques escus de costay . 
 " La Veuve se raccommode avec Miladi Marlboro qui est tout puicante avecque la Reine Anne . Cet dam senteraysent pour la petite prude ; qui pourctant a un fi du mesme asge que vous savay . 
 " En sortant de prisong venez icy . Je ne puy vous recevoir chaymoy a cause des mechansete du monde , may pre du moy vous aurez logement . 
 " ISABELLE VICOMTESSE D'ESMOND " 
 Marchioness of Esmond this lady sometimes call herself , in virtue of that patent which have be give by the late King James to Harry Esmond 's father ; and in this state she have her train carry by a knight 's wife , a cup and cover of assay to drink from , and fringed cloth . 
 he who be of the same age as little Francis , whom we shall henceforth call Viscount Castlewood here , be H. R. H. the Prince of Wales , bear in the same year and month with Frank , and just proclaim at Saint Germains , King of Great Britain , France , and Ireland . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 I take the queen 's pay in QUIN 's REGIMENT . 

 the fellow in the orange - tawny livery with blue lace and facing be in wait when Esmond come out of prison , and , take the young gentleman 's slender baggage , lead the way out of that odious newgate , and by Fleet Conduit , down to the Thames , where a pair of oar be call , and they go up the river to Chelsey . esmond think the sun have never shine so bright ; nor the air feel so fresh and exhilarating . Temple Garden , as they row by , look like the garden of Eden to he , and the aspect of the quay , wharf , and building by the river , Somerset House , and Westminster   where the splendid new bridge be just begin , Lambeth tower and palace , and that busy shin scene of the Thames swarm with boat and barge , fill his heart with pleasure and cheerfulness as well such a beautiful scene might to one who have be a prisoner so long , and with so many dark thought deepen the gloom of his captivity . they row up at length to the pretty village of Chelsey , where the nobility have many handsome country - house ; and so come to my Lady Viscountess 's house , a cheerful new house in the row face the river , with a handsome garden behind it , and a pleasant look - out both towards Surrey and Kensington , where stand the noble ancient palace of the Lord Warwick , Harry 's reconciled adversary . 
 here in her ladyship 's saloon , the young man see again some of those picture which have be at Castlewood , and which she have remove thence on the death of her lord , Harry 's father . specially , and in the place of honor , be Sir Peter Lely 's picture of the honorable Mistress Isabella Esmond as Diana , in yellow satin , with a bow in her hand and a crescent in her forehead ; and dog frisk about she . ' twa paint about the time when royal Endymions be say to find favor with this virgin huntress ; and , as goddess have youth perpetual , this one believe to the day of her death that she never grow old : and always persist in suppose the picture be still like she . 
 after he have be show to her room by the groom of the chamber , who fill many office besides in her ladyship 's modest household , and after a proper interval , his elderly goddess Diana vouchsafe to appear to the young man . a blackamoor in a turkish habit , with red boot and a silver collar , on which the Viscountess 's arm be engraven , precede she and bear her cushion ; then come her gentlewoman ; a little pack of spaniel bark and frisk about precede the austere huntress then , behold , the Viscountess herself " dropping odor . " esmond recollect from his childhood that rich aroma of musk which his mother - in - law   for she may be call so   exhale . as the sky grow red and redder towards sunset , so , in the decline of her year , the cheek of my Lady Dowager blush more deeply . her face be illuminate with vermilion , which appear the brighter from the white paint employ to set it off . she wear the ringlet which have be in fashion in King Charles 's time ; whereas the lady of King William 's have head - dress like the tower of Cybele . her eye gleam out from the midst of this queer structure of paint , dye , and pomatum . such be my Lady Viscountess , Mr. Esmond 's father 's widow . 
 he make she such a profound bow as her dignity and relationship merit , and advance with the great gravity , and once more kiss that hand , upon the trembling knuckle of which glitter a score of ring remember old time when that trembling hand make he tremble . " Marchioness , " say he , bow , and on one knee , " be it only the hand I may have the honor of salute ? " for , accompany that inward laughter , which the sight of such an astonishing old figure might well produce in the young man , there be good will too , and the kindness of consanguinity . she have be his father 's wife , and be his grandfather 's daughter . she have suffer he in old day , and be kind to he now after her fashion . and now that bar - sinister be remove from Esmond 's thought , and that secret opprobrium no long cast upon his mind , he be pleased to feel family tie and own they perhaps secretly vain of the sacrifice he have make , and to think that he , Esmond , be really the chief of his house , and only prevent by his own magnanimity from advance his claim . 
 at least , ever since he have learn that secret from his poor patron on his die bed , actually as he be stand beside it , he have feel an independency which he have never know before , and which since do not desert he . so he call his old aunt Marchioness , but with an air as if he be the Marquis of Esmond who so address she . 
 do she read in the young gentleman 's eye , which have now no fear of hers or their superannuated authority , that he know or suspect the truth about his birth ? she give a start of surprise at his altered manner : indeed , it be quite a different bearing to that of the Cambridge student who have pay she a visit two year since , and whom she have dismiss with five piece send by the groom of the chamber . she eye he , then tremble a little more than be she will not , perhaps , and say , " welcome , cousin , " in a frightened voice . 
 his resolution , as have be say before , have be quite different , namely , so to bear himself through life as if the secret of his birth be not know to he ; but he suddenly and rightly determine on a different course . he ask that her ladyship 's attendant should be dismiss , and when they be private " welcome , nephew , at least , madam , it should be , " he say . " a great wrong have be do to I and to you , and to my poor mother , who be no more . " 
 " I declare before heaven that I be guiltless of it , " she cry out , give up her cause at once . " it be your wicked father who " 
 " who bring this dishonor on our family , " say Mr. Esmond . " I know it full well . I want to disturb no one . those who be in present possession have be my dearest benefactor , and be quite innocent of intentional wrong to I . the late lord , my dear patron , know not the truth until a few month before his death , when Father Holt bring the news to he . " 
 " the wretch ! he have it in confession ! he have it in confession ! " cry out the Dowager Lady . 
 " not so . he learn it elsewhere as well as in confession , " Mr. Esmond answer . " my father , when wound at the Boyne , tell the truth to a french priest , who be in hiding after the battle , as well as to the priest there , at whose house he die . this gentleman do not think fit to divulge the story till he meet with Mr. Holt at Saint Omer 's . and the latter keep it back for his own purpose , and until he have learn whether my mother be alive or no . she be dead year since , my poor patron tell I with his die breath , and I doubt he not . I do not know even whether I could prove a marriage . I would not if I could . I do not care to bring shame on our name , or grief upon those whom I love , however hardly they may use I . my father 's son , madam , will not   aggravate the wrong my father do you . continue to be his widow , and give I your kindness . ' tis all I ask from you ; and I shall never speak of this matter again . " 
 " Mais vous ete un noble jeune homme ! " break out my lady , speak , as usual with she when she be agitate , in the french language . 
 " Noblesse oblige , " say Mr. Esmond , make she a low bow . " there be those alive to whom , in return for their love to I , I often fondly say I would give my life away . shall I be their enemy now , and quarrel about a title ? what matter who have it ? ' tis with the family still . " 
 " what can there be in that little prude of a woman that make man so raffoler about she ? " cry out my Lady Dowager . " she be here for a month petition the king . she be pretty , and well conserve ; but she have not the bel air . in his late Majesty 's Court all the man pretend to admire she , and she be no well than a little wax doll . she be well now , and look the sister of her daughter ; but what mean you all by bepraise she ? Mr. Steele , who be in wait on Prince George , see she with her two child go to Kensington , writ a poem about she , and say he shall wear her color , and dress in black for the future . Mr. Congreve say he will write a ' Mourning Widow , ' that shall be well than his ' Mourning Bride . ' though their husband quarrel and fight when that wretch Churchill desert the king   for which he deserve to be hang , Lady Marlborough have again go wild about the little widow ; insult I in my own drawing - room , by say ' twa not the old widow , but the young Viscountess , she have come to see . little Castlewood and little Lord Churchill be to be swear friend , and have box each other twice or thrice like brother already . ' twas that wicked young Mohun who , come back from the province last year , where he have disinter she , rave about she all the winter ; say she be a pearl set before swine ; and kill poor stupid Frank . the quarrel be all about his wife . I know ' twa all about she . be there anything between she and Mohun , nephew ? tell I now be there anything ? about yourself , I do not ask you to answer question . " 
 Mr. Esmond blush up . " my lady 's virtue be like that of a saint in heaven , madam , " he cry out . 
 " Eh ! mon neveu . many saint get to heaven after have a deal to repent of . I believe you be like all the rest of the fool , and madly in love with she . " 
 " indeed , I love and honor she before all the world , " Esmond answer . " I take no shame in that . " 
 " and she have shut her door on you give the living to that horrid young cub , son of that horrid old bear , Tusher , and say she will never see you more . Monsieur mon neveu we be all like that . when I be a young woman , I be positive that a thousand duel be fight about I . and when poor Monsieur de Souchy drown himself in the canal at bruge because I dance with Count Springbock , I could not   squeeze out a single tear , but dance till five o'clock the next morning . ' twas the Count no , ' twa my Lord Ormond that play the fiddle , and his Majesty do I the honor of dance all night with I . how you be grow ! you have get the bel air . you be a black man . our esmond be all black . the little prude 's son be fair ; so be his father fair and stupid . you be an ugly little wretch when you come to Castlewood you be all eye , like a young crow . we intend you should be a priest . that awful Father Holt how he use to frighten I when I be ill ! I have a comfortable director now the Abbe Douillette a dear man . we make meagre on Fridays always . my cook be a devout pious man . you , of course , be of the right way of thinking . they say the Prince of Orange be very ill indeed . " 
 in this way the old Dowager rattle on remorselessly to Mr. Esmond , who be quite astounded with her present volubility , contrast it with her former haughty behavior to he . but she have take he into favor for the moment , and choose not only to like he , as far as her nature permit , but to be afraid of he ; and he find himself to be as familiar with she now as a young man , as , when a boy , he have be timorous and silent . she be as good as her word respect he . she introduce he to her company , of which she entertain a good deal of the adherent of King James of course and a great deal of loud intriguing take place over her card - table . she present Mr. Esmond as her kinsman to many person of honor ; she supply he not illiberally with money , which he have no scruple in accept from she , consider the relationship which he bear to she , and the sacrifice which he himself be make in behalf of the family . but he have make up his mind to continue at no woman 's apron - string long ; and perhaps have cast about how he should distinguish himself , and make himself a name , which his singular fortune have deny he . a discontent with his former bookish life and quietude , a bitter feeling of revolt at that slavery in which he have choose to confine himself for the sake of those whose hardness towards he make his heart bleed , a restless wish to see man and the world , lead he to think of the military profession : at any rate , to desire to see a few campaign , and accordingly he press his new patroness to get he a pair of color ; and one day have the honor of find himself appoint an ensign in Colonel Quin 's regiment of Fusileers on the irish establishment . 
 Mr. Esmond 's commission be scarce three week old when that accident befell King William which end the life of the great , the wise , the brave , and most clement sovereign whom England ever know . ' twas the fashion of the hostile party to assail this great prince 's reputation during his life ; but the joy which they and all his enemy in Europe show at his death , be a proof of the terror in which they hold he . young as Esmond be , he be wise enough   and generous enough too , let it be say   to scorn that indecency of gratulation which break out amongst the follower of King James in London , upon the death of this illustrious prince , this invincible warrior , this wise and moderate statesman . loyalty to the exile king 's family be traditional , as have be say , in that house to which Mr. Esmond belong . his father 's widow have all her hope , sympathy , recollection , prejudice , engage on King James 's side ; and be certainly as noisy a conspirator as ever assert the King 's right , or abuse his opponent 's , over a quadrille table or a dish of bohea . her ladyship 's house swarm with ecclesiastic , in disguise and out ; with tale - bearer from St. Germains ; and quidnunc that know the last news from Versailles ; nay , the exact force and number of the next expedition which the french king be to send from Dunkirk , and which be to swallow up the Prince of Orange , his army and his court . she have receive the Duke of Berwick when he land here in ' 96 . she keep the glass he drink from , vow she never would use it till she drink King James the Third 's health in it on his Majesty 's return ; she have token from the Queen , and relic of the saint who , if the story be true , have not always be a saint as far as she and many other be concern . she believe in the miracle work at his tomb , and have a hundred authentic story of wondrous cure effect by the blessed king 's rosary , the medal which he wear , the lock of his hair , or what not . esmond remember a score of marvellous tale which the credulous old woman tell he . there be the Bishop of Autun , that be heal of a malady he have for forty year , and which leave he after he say mass for the repose of the king 's soul . there be M. Marais , a surgeon in Auvergne , who have a palsy in both his leg , which be cure through the king 's intercession . there be Philip Pitet , of the Benedictines , who have a suffocating cough , which wellnigh kill he , but he besought relief of heaven through the merit and intercession of the blessed king , and he straightway feel a profuse sweat break out all over he , and be recover perfectly . and there be the wife of Mons . Lepervier , dancing - master to the Duke of Saxe - Gotha , who be entirely ease of a rheumatism by the king 's intercession , of which miracle there could be no doubt , for her surgeon and his apprentice have give their testimony , under oath , that they do not in any way contribute to the cure . of these tale , and a thousand like they , Mr. Esmond believe as much as he choose . his kinswoman 's great faith have swallow for they all .
