 the English High Church party do not adopt these legend . but truth and honor , as they think , bind they to the exile king 's side ; nor have the banish family any warm supporter than that kind lady of Castlewood , in whose house Esmond be bring up . she influence her husband , very much more perhaps than my lord know , who admire his wife prodigiously though he might be inconstant to she , and who , adverse to the trouble of think himself , gladly enough adopt the opinion which she choose for he . to one of her simple and faithful heart , allegiance to any sovereign but the one be impossible . to serve King William for interest 's sake would have be a monstrous hypocrisy and treason . her pure conscience could no more have consent to it than to a theft , a forgery , or any other base action . Lord Castlewood might have be win over , no doubt , but his wife never could : and he submit his conscience to hers in this case as he do in most other , when he be not tempt too sorely . and it be from his affection and gratitude most likely , and from that eager devotion for his mistress , which characterize all Esmond 's youth , that the young man subscribe to this , and other article of faith , which his fond benefactress set he . have she be a Whig , he have be one ; have she follow Mr. Fox , and turn Quaker , no doubt he would have abjure ruffle and a periwig , and have forswear sword , lace - coat , and clocked stocking . in the scholar ' boyish dispute at the University , where party run very high , Esmond be note as a Jacobite , and very likely from vanity as much as affection take the side of his family . 
 almost the whole of the clergy of the country and more than a half of the nation be on this side . ours be the most loyal people in the world surely ; we admire our king , and be faithful to they long after they have cease to be true to we . ' tis a wonder to any one who look back at the history of the Stuart family to think how they kick their crown away from they ; how they fling away chance after chance ; what treasure of loyalty they dissipate , and how fatally they be bent on consummate their own ruin . if ever man have fidelity , ' twa they ; if ever man squander opportunity , ' twa they ; and , of all the enemy they have , they themselves be the most fatal . 
 when the Princess Anne succeed , the weary nation be glad enough to cry a truce from all these war , controversy , and conspiracy , and to accept in the person of a Princess of the blood royal a compromise between the party into which the country be divide . the Tories could serve under she with easy conscience ; though a Tory herself , she represent the triumph of the Whig opinion . the people of England , always like that their Princes should be attach to their own family , be pleased to think the Princess be faithful to her ; and up to the very last day and hour of her reign , and but for that fatality which he inherit from his father along with their claim to the english crown , King James the Third might have wear it . but he neither know how to wait an opportunity , nor to use it when he have it ; he be venturesome when he ought to have be cautious , and cautious when he ought to have dare everything . ' tis with a sort of rage at his inaptitude that one think of his melancholy story . do the Fates deal more specially with king than with common man ? one be apt to imagine so , in consider the history of that royal race , in whose behalf so much fidelity , so much valor , so much blood be desperately and bootlessly expended . 
 the King dead then , the Princess Anne   ugly Anne Hyde 's daughter , our Dowager at Chelsey call she   be proclaim by trumpet herald all over the town from Westminster to Ludgate Hill , amidst immense jubilation of the people . 
 next week my Lord Marlborough be promote to the Garter , and to be Captain - General of her Majesty 's force at home and abroad . this appointment only inflame the Dowager 's rage , or , as she think it , her fidelity to her rightful sovereign . " the Princess be but a puppet in the hand of that fury of a woman , who come into my drawing - room and insult I to my face . what can come to a country that be give over to such a woman ? " say the Dowager : " as for that double - faced traitor , my Lord Marlborough , he have betray every man and every woman with whom he have have to deal , except his horrid wife , who make he tremble . ' tis all over with the country when it have get into the clutch of such wretch as these . " 
 Esmond 's old kinswoman salute the new power in this way ; but some good fortune at last occur to a family which stand in great need of it , by the advancement of these famous personage who benefit humbler people that have the luck of be in their favor . before Mr. Esmond leave England in the month of August , and be then at Portsmouth , where he have join his regiment , and be busy at drill , learn the practice and mystery of the musket and pike , he hear that a pension on the Stamp Office have be get for his late beloved mistress , and that the young Mistress Beatrix be also to be take into court . so much good , at least , have come of the poor widow 's visit to London , not revenge upon her husband 's enemy , but reconcilement to old friend , who pity , and seem inclined to serve she . as for the comrade in prison and the late misfortune , Colonel Westbury be with the Captain - General go to Holland ; Captain Macartney be now at Portsmouth , with his regiment of Fusileers and the force under command of his grace the Duke of Ormond , bind for Spain it be say ; my Lord Warwick be return home ; and Lord Mohun , so far from be punish for the homicide which have bring so much grief and change into the Esmond family , be go in company of my Lord Macclesfield 's splendid embassy to the Elector of Hanover , carry the Garter to his Highness , and a complimentary letter from the Queen . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 recapitulation . 

 from such fitful light as could be cast upon his dark history by the broken narrative of his poor patron , tear by remorse and struggle in the last pang of dissolution , Mr. Esmond have be make to understand so far , that his mother be long since dead ; and so there could be no question as regard she or her honor , tarnish by her husband 's desertion and injury , to influence her son in any step which he might take either for prosecute or relinquish his own just claim . it appear from my poor lord 's hurried confession , that he have be make acquainted with the real fact of the case only two year since , when Mr. Holt visit he , and would have implicate he in one of those many conspiracy by which the secret leader of King James 's party in this country be ever endeavor to destroy the Prince of Orange 's life or power : conspiracy so like murder , so cowardly in the mean use , so wicked in the end , that our nation have sure do well in throw off all allegiance and fidelity to the unhappy family that could not vindicate its right except by such treachery by such dark intrigue and base agent . there be design against King William that be no more honorable than the ambush of cut - throat and footpad . ' tis humiliating to think that a great Prince , possessor of a great and sacred right , and upholder of a great cause , should have stoop to such baseness of assassination and treason as be prove by the unfortunate King James 's own warrant and sign manual give to his supporter in this country . what he and they call levy war be , in truth , no well than instigate murder . the noble Prince of Orange burst magnanimously through those feeble mesh of conspiracy in which his enemy try to envelop he : it seem as if their cowardly dagger break upon the breast of his undaunted resolution . after King James 's death , the Queen and her people at St. Germains priest and woman for the most part continue their intrigue in behalf of the young Prince , James the Third , as he be call in France and by his party here   this Prince , or Chevalier de St. George , be bear in the same year with Esmond 's young pupil Frank , my Lord Viscount 's son ; and the Prince 's affair , be in the hand of priest and woman , be conduct as priest and woman will conduct they , artfully , cruelly , feebly , and to a certain bad issue . the moral of the jesuit ' story I think as wholesome a one as ever be writ : the artfullest , the wise , the most toilsome , and dexterous plot - builder in the world there always come a day when the rouse public indignation kick their flimsy edifice down , and send its cowardly enemy a - flying . Mr. Swift hath finely describe that passion for intrigue , that love of secrecy , slander , and lying , which belong to weak people , hanger - on of weak court . ' tis the nature of such to hate and envy the strong , and conspire their ruin ; and the conspiracy succeed very well , and everything presage the satisfactory overthrow of the great victim ; until one day Gulliver rouse himself , shake off the little vermin of an enemy , and walk away unmoleste . ah ! the irish soldier might well say after the Boyne , " change king with we and we will fight it over again . " indeed , the fight be not fair between the two . ' twas a weak , priest - ride , woman - ride man , with such puny ally and weapon as his own poor nature lead he to choose , contend against the scheme , the generalship , the wisdom , and the heart of a hero . 
 on one of these many coward 's errand then ,   for , as I view they now , I can call they no less ,   Mr. Holt have come to my lord at Castlewood , propose some infallible plan for the Prince of Orange 's destruction , in which my Lord Viscount , loyalist as he be , have indignantly refuse to join . as far as Mr. Esmond could gather from his die word , Holt come to my lord with a plan of insurrection , and offer of the renewal , in his person , of that marquis 's title which King James have confer on the precede viscount ; and on refusal of this bribe , a threat be make , on Holt 's part , to upset my Lord Viscount 's claim to his estate and title of Castlewood altogether . to back this astounding piece of intelligence , of which Henry Esmond 's patron now have the first light , Holt come armed with the late lord 's die declaration , after the affair of the Boyne , at Trim , in Ireland , make both to the irish priest and a french ecclesiastic of Holt 's order , that be with King James 's army . Holt show , or pretend to show , the marriage certificate of the late Viscount Esmond with my mother , in the city of Brussels , in the year 1677 , when the viscount , then Thomas Esmond , be serve with the english army in Flanders ; he could show , he say , that this Gertrude , desert by her husband long since , be alive , and a profess nun in the year 1685 , at Brussels , in which year Thomas Esmond marry his uncle 's daughter , Isabella , now call Viscountess Dowager of Castlewood ; and leave he , for twelve hour , to consider this astounding news   so the poor die lord say , disappear with his paper in the mysterious way in which he come . Esmond know how , well enough : by that window from which he have see the Father issue : but there be no need to explain to my poor lord , only to gather from his parting lip the word which he would soon be able to utter no more . 
 Ere the twelve hour be over , Holt himself be a prisoner , implicate in Sir John Fenwick 's conspiracy , and lock up at Hexton first , whence he be transfer to the Tower ; leave the poor Lord Viscount , who be not aware of the other be take , in daily apprehension of his return , when   as my Lord Castlewood declare , call God to witness , and with tear in his die eye   it have be his intention at once to give up his estate and his title to their proper owner , and to retire to his own house at Walcote with his family . " and would to God I have do it , " the poor lord say . " I would not be here now , wound to death , a miserable , stricken man ! " 
 my lord wait day after day , and , as may be suppose , no messenger come ; but at a month 's end Holt get mean to convey to he a message out of the Tower , which be to this effect : that he should consider all unsaid that have be say , and that thing be as they be . 
 " I have a sore temptation , " say my poor lord . " since I have come into this cursed title of Castlewood , which hath never prosper with I , I have spend far more than the income of that estate , and my paternal one , too . I calculate all my mean down to the last shilling , and find I never could pay you back , my poor Harry , whose fortune I have have for twelve year . my wife and child must have go out of the house dishonor , and beggar . God know , it hath be a miserable one for I and mine . like a coward , I cling to that respite which Holt give I . I keep the truth from Rachel and you . I try to win money of Mohun , and only plunge deeply into debt ; I scarce dare look thee in the face when I see thee . this sword hath be hang over my head these two year . I swear I feel happy when Mohun 's blade enter my side . " 
 after lie ten month in the Tower , Holt , against whom nothing could be find except that he be a Jesuit priest , know to be in King James 's interest , be put on shipboard by the incorrigible forgiveness of King William , who promise he , however , a hanging if ever he should again set foot on English shore . More than once , whilst he be in prison himself , Esmond have think where those paper could be , which the Jesuit have show to his patron , and which have such an interest for himself . they be not find on Mr. Holt 's person when that Father be apprehend , for have such be the case my Lords of the Council have see they , and this family history have long since be make public . however , Esmond care not to seek the paper . his resolution be take ; his poor mother dead ; what matter to he that document exist prove his right to a title which he be determined not to claim , and of which he vow never to deprive that family which he love well in the world ? perhaps he take a great pride out of his sacrifice than he would have have in those honor which he be resolve to forego . again , as long as these title be not forthcoming , Esmond 's kinsman , dear young Francis , be the honorable and undisputed owner of the Castlewood estate and title . the mere word of a Jesuit could not overset Frank 's right of occupancy , and so Esmond 's mind feel actually at ease to think the paper be miss , and in their absence his dear mistress and her son the lawful Lady and Lord of Castlewood . 
 very soon after his liberation , Mr. Esmond make it his business to ride to that village of Ealing where he have pass his early year in this country , and to see if his old guardian be still alive and inhabitant of that place . but the only relique which he find of old M. Pastoureau be a stone in the churchyard , which tell that Athanasius Pastoureau , a native of Flanders , lie there bury , age 87 year . the old man 's cottage , which esmond perfectly recollect , and the garden   where in his childhood he have pass many hour of play and reverie , and have many a beating from his termagant of a foster - mother , be now in the occupation of quite a different family ; and it be with difficulty that he could learn in the village what have come of Pastoureau 's widow and child . the clerk of the parish recollect she the old man be scarce alter in the fourteen year that have pass since last Esmond set eye on he . it appear she have pretty soon console herself after the death of her old husband , whom she rule over , by take a new one young than herself , who spend her money and ill - treat she and her child . the girl die ; one of the boy ' list ; the other have go apprentice . old Mr. Rogers , the clerk , say he have hear that Mrs. Pastoureau be dead too . she and her husband have leave eale this seven year ; and so Mr. Esmond 's hope of gain any information regard his parentage from this family be bring to an end . he give the old clerk a crown - piece for his news , smile to think of the time when he and his little playfellow have slink out of the churchyard or hide behind the gravestone , at the approach of this awful authority . 
 who be his mother ? what have her name be ? when do she die ? esmond long to find some one who could answer these question to he , and think even of put they to his aunt the Viscountess , who have innocently take the name which belong of right to Henry 's mother . but she know nothing , or choose to know nothing , on this subject , nor , indeed , could Mr. Esmond press she much to speak on it . Father Holt be the only man who could enlighten he , and Esmond feel he must wait until some fresh chance or new intrigue might put he face to face with his old friend , or bring that restless indefatigable spirit back to England again . 
 the appointment to his ensigncy , and the preparation necessary for the campaign , presently give the young gentleman other matter to think of . his new patroness treat he very kindly and liberally ; she promise to make interest and pay money , too , to get he a company speedily ; she bid he procure a handsome outfit , both of clothe and of arm , and be pleased to admire he when he make his first appearance in his laced scarlet coat , and to permit he to salute she on the occasion of this interesting investiture . " Red , " say she , toss up her old head , " hath always be the color wear by the Esmonds . " and so her ladyship wear it on her own cheek very faithfully to the last . she would have he be dress , she say , as become his father 's son , and pay cheerfully for his five - pound beaver , his black buckle periwig , and his fine holland shirt , and his sword , and his pistol , mount with silver . since the day he be bear , poor Harry have never look such a fine gentleman : his liberal step - mother fill his purse with guineas , too , some of which Captain Steele and a few choice spirit help Harry to spend in an entertainment which Dick order   and , indeed , would have pay for , but that he have no money when the reckoning be call for ; nor would the landlord give he any more credit   at the " Garter , " over against the gate of the Palace , in Pall Mall . 
 the old Viscountess , indeed , if she have do Esmond any wrong formerly , seem inclined to repair it by the present kindness of her behavior : she embrace he copiously at parting , weep plentifully , bid he write by every packet , and give he an inestimable relic , which she besought he to wear round his neck a medal , bless by I know not what pope , and wear by his late sacred Majesty King James . so Esmond arrive at his regiment with a well equipage than most young officer could afford . he be old than most of his senior , and have a further advantage which belong but to very few of the army gentleman in his day many of whom could do little more than write their name that he have read much , both at home and at the University , be master of two or three language , and have that further education which neither book nor year will give , but which some man get from the silent teaching of adversity . she be a great schoolmistress , as many a poor fellow know , that hath hold his hand out to her ferule , and whimper over his lesson before her awful chair . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 I go on the VIGO BAY EXPEDITION , TASTE SALT - WATER and SMELL POWDER . 

 the first expedition in which Mr. Esmond have the honor to be engage , rather resemble one of the invasion project by the redoubte Captain Avory or Captain Kidd , than a war between crown head , carry on by general of rank and honor . on the 1st day of July , 1702 , a great fleet , of a hundred and fifty sail , set sail from Spithead , under the command of Admiral Shovell , have on board 12,000 troop , with his grace the Duke of Ormond as the Capt.-General of the expedition . one of these 12,000 hero have never be to sea before , or , at least , only once in his infancy , when he make the voyage to England from that unknown country where he be bear one of those 12,000 the junior ensign of Colonel Quin 's regiment of Fusileers be in a quite unheroic state of corporal prostration a few hour after sailing ; and an enemy , have he board the ship , would have have easy work of he . from Portsmouth we put into Plymouth , and take in fresh reinforcement . we be off Finisterre on the 31st of July , so Esmond 's table - book inform he : and on the 8th of August make the rock of Lisbon . by this time the ensign be grow as bold as an admiral , and a week afterwards have the fortune to be under fire for the first time and under water , too , his boat be swamp in the surf in Toros Bay , where the troop land . the ducking of his new coat be all the harm the young soldier get in this expedition , for , indeed , the Spaniards make no stand before our troop , and be not in strength to do so . 
 but the campaign , if not very glorious , be very pleasant . new sight of nature , by sea and land a life of action , begin now for the first time occupy and excite the young man . the many accident , and the routine of shipboard the military duty the new acquaintance , both of his comrade in arm , and of the officer of the fleet serve to cheer and occupy his mind , and waken it out of that selfish depression into which his late unhappy fortune have plunge he . he feel as if the ocean separate he from his past care , and welcome the new era of life which be dawn for he . wound heal rapidly in a heart of two - and - twenty ; hope revive daily ; and courage rally in spite of a man . perhaps , as Esmond think of his late despondency and melancholy , and how irremediable it have seem to he , as he lie in his prison a few month back , he be almost mortified in his secret mind at find himself so cheerful . 
 to see with one 's own eye man and country , be well than read all the book of travel in the world : and it be with extreme delight and exultation that the young man find himself actually on his grand tour , and in the view of people and city which he have read about as a boy . he behold war for the first time the pride , pomp , and circumstance of it , at least , if not much of the danger . he see actually , and with his own eye , those spanish cavalier and lady whom he have behold in imagination in that immortal story of Cervantes , which have be the delight of his youthful leisure . ' tis forty year since Mr. Esmond witness those scene , but they remain as fresh in his memory as on the day when first he see they as a young man . a cloud , as of grief , that have lower over he , and have wrap the last year of his life in gloom , seem to clear away from Esmond during this fortunate voyage and campaign . his energy seem to awaken and to expand under a cheerful sense of freedom . be his heart secretly glad to have escape from that fond but ignoble bondage at home ? be it that the inferiority to which the idea of his base birth have compel he , vanish with the knowledge of that secret , which though , perforce , keep to himself , be yet enough to cheer and console he ? at any rate , young Esmond of the army be quite a different being to the sad little dependant of the kind Castlewood household , and the melancholy student of Trinity Walks ; discontent with his fate , and with the vocation into which that drive he , and think , with a secret indignation , that the cassock and band , and the very sacred office with which he have once propose to invest himself , be , in fact , but mark of a servitude which be to continue all his life long . for , disguise it as he might to himself , he have all along feel that to be Castlewood 's chaplain be to be Castlewood 's inferior still , and that his life be but to be a long , hopeless servitude . so , indeed , he be far from grudge his old friend Tom Tusher 's good fortune   as Tom , no doubt , think it . have it be a mitre and Lambeth which his friend offer he , and not a small living and a country parsonage , he would have feel as much a slave in one case as in the other , and be quite happy and thankful to be free . 
 the brave man I ever know in the army , and who have be present in most of King William 's action , as well as in the campaign of the great Duke of Marlborough , could never be get to tell we of any achievement of his , except that once Prince Eugene order he up a tree to reconnoitre the enemy , which feat he could not achieve on account of the horseman 's boot he wear ; and on another day that he be very nearly take prisoner because of these jack - boot , which prevent he from run away . the present narrator shall imitate this laudable reserve , and doth not intend to dwell upon his military exploit , which be in truth not very different from those of a thousand other gentleman . this first campaign of Mr. Esmond 's lasted but a few day ; and as a score of book have be write concern it , it may be dismiss very briefly here . 
 when our fleet come within view of Cadiz , our commander send a boat with a white flag and a couple of officer to the Governor of Cadiz , Don Scipio de Brancaccio , with a letter from his grace , in which he hop that as Don Scipio have formerly serve with the Austrians against the French , ' twa to be hop that his Excellency would now declare himself against the french king , and for the Austrian in the war between King Philip and King Charles . but his Excellency , Don Scipio , prepare a reply , in which he announce that , have serve his former king with honor and fidelity , he hop to exhibit the same loyalty and devotion towards his present sovereign , King Philip V. ; and by the time this letter be ready , the two officer have be take to see the town , and the alameda , and the theatre , where bull - fight be fight , and the convent , where the admirable work of Don Bartholomew Murillo inspire one of they with a great wonder and delight such as he have never feel before concern this divine art of painting ; and these sight over , and a handsome refection and chocolate be serve to the english gentleman , they be accompany back to their shallop with every courtesy , and be the only two officer of the english army that see at that time that famous city . 
 the general try the power of another proclamation on the Spaniards , in which he announce that we only come in the interest of Spain and King Charles , and for ourselves want to make no conquest nor settlement in Spain at all . but all this eloquence be lose upon the Spaniards , it would seem : the Captain - General of Andalusia would no more listen to we than the Governor of Cadiz ; and in reply to his grace 's proclamation , the Marquis of Villadarias fire off another , which those who know the spanish thought rather the good of the two ; and of this number be Harry Esmond , whose kind Jesuit in old day have instruct he , and now have the honor of translate for his grace these harmless document of war . there be a hard touch for his grace , and , indeed , for other general in her Majesty 's service , in the conclude sentence of the Don : " that he and his council have the generous example of their ancestor to follow , who have never yet seek their elevation in the blood or in the flight of their king . ' mori pro patria ' be his device , which the Duke might communicate to the Princess who govern England . " 
 whether the troop be angry at this repartee or no , ' tis certain something put they in a fury ; for , not be able to get possession of Cadiz , our people seize upon Port Saint Mary 's and sack it , burn down the merchant ' storehouse , get drunk with the famous wine there , pillaging and rob quiet house and convent , murder and do bad . and the only blood which Mr. Esmond draw in this shameful campaign , be the knock down an english sentinel with a half - pike , who be offer insult to a poor trembling nun . be she go to turn out a beauty ? or a princess ? or perhaps Esmond 's mother that he have lose and never see ? alas no , it be but a poor wheezy old dropsical woman , with a wart upon her nose . but have be early teach a part of the roman religion , he never have the horror of it that some protestant have show , and seem to think to be a part of our . 
 after the pillage and plunder of St. Mary 's and an assault upon a fort or two , the troop all take shipping , and finish their expedition , at any rate , more brilliantly than it have begin . hear that the french fleet with a great treasure be in Vigo Bay , our Admirals , Rooke and Hopson , pursue the enemy thither ; the troop land and carry the fort that protect the bay , Hopson pass the boom first on board his ship the " Torbay , " and the rest of the ship , English and Dutch , follow he . twenty ship be burn or take in the Port of Redondilla , and a vast deal more plunder than be ever account for ; but poor man before that expedition be rich afterwards , and so often be it find and remark that the Vigo officer come home with pocket full of money , that the notorious Jack Shafto , who make such a figure at the coffeehouse and gaming - table in London , and give out that he have be a soldier at Vigo , own , when he be about to be hang , that Bagshot Heath have be his Vigo , and that he only speak of La Redondilla to turn away people 's eye from the real place where the booty lie . indeed , Hounslow or Vigo which matter much ? the latter be a bad business , though Mr. Addison do sing its praise in Latin . that honest gentleman 's muse have an eye to the main chance ; and I doubt whether she see much inspiration in the lose side . 
 but though Esmond , for his part , get no share of this fabulous booty , one great prize which he have out of the campaign be , that excitement of action and change of scene , which shake off a great deal of his previous melancholy . he learn at any rate to bear his fate cheerfully . he bring back a brown face , a heart resolute enough , and a little pleasant store of knowledge and observation , from that expedition , which be over with the autumn , when the troop be back in England again ; and esmond give up his post of secretary to General Lumley , whose command be over , and part with that officer with many kind expression of good will on the General 's side , have leave to go to London , to see if he could push his fortune any way far , and find himself once more in his dowager aunt 's comfortable quarter at Chelsey , and in great favor than ever with the old lady . he propitiate she with a present of a comb , a fan , and a black mantle , such as the lady of Cadiz wear , and which my Lady Viscountess pronounce become her style of beauty mightily . and she be greatily edify at hearing of that story of his rescue of the nun , and feel very little doubt but that her King James 's relic , which he have always dutifully wear in his desk , have keep he out of danger , and avert the shot of the enemy . my lady make feast for he , introduce he to more company , and push his fortune with such enthusiasm and success , that she get a promise of a company for he through the Lady Marlborough 's interest , who be graciously pleased to accept of a diamond worth a couple of hundred guinea , which Mr. Esmond be enable to present to her ladyship through his aunt 's bounty , and who promise that she would take charge of Esmond 's fortune . he have the honor to make his appearance at the Queen 's drawing - room occasionally , and to frequent my Lord Marlborough 's levee . that great man receive the young one with very especial favor , so Esmond 's comrade say , and deign to say that he have receive the good report of Mr. Esmond , both for courage and ability , whereon you may be sure the young gentleman make a profound bow , and express himself eager to serve under the most distinguished captain in the world . 
 whilst his business be go on thus prosperously , Esmond have his share of pleasure too , and make his appearance along with other young gentleman at the coffee - house , the theatre , and the Mall . he long to hear of his dear mistress and her family : many a time , in the midst of the gayetie and pleasure of the town , his heart fondly revert to they ; and often as the young fellow of his society be make merry at the tavern , and call toast   as the fashion of that day be   over their wine , Esmond think of person of two fair woman , whom he have be use to adore almost , and empty his glass with a sigh . 
 by this time the eld Viscountess have grow tired again of the young , and whenever she speak of my lord 's widow , ' twa in term by no mean complimentary towards that poor lady : the young woman not need her protection any long , the elder abuse she . Most of the family quarrel that I have see in life   save always those arise from money dispute , when a division of twopence halfpenny will often drive the dear relative into war and estrangement ,   spring out of jealousy and envy . Jack and Tom , bear of the same family and to the same fortune , live very cordially together , not until Jack be ruin when Tom desert he , but until Tom make a sudden rise in prosperity , which Jack can not   forgive . ten time to one ' tis the unprosperous man that be angry , not the other who be in fault . ' Tis Mrs. Jack , who can only afford a chair , that sicken at Mrs. Tom 's new coach - and - sick , cry out against her sister 's air , and set her husband against his brother . ' tis Jack who see his brother shake hand with a lord   with whom Jack would like to exchange snuff - box himself , that go home and tell his wife how poor Tom be spoil , he fear , and no well than a sneak , parasite , and beggar on horse back . I remember how furious the coffee - house wit be with Dick Steele when he set up his coach and fine house in Bloomsbury : they begin to forgive he when the bailiff be after he , and abuse Mr. Addison for sell Dick 's country - house . and yet Dick in the sponge - house , or Dick in the Park , with his four mare and plat harness , be exactly the same gentle , kindly , improvident , jovial Dick Steele : and yet Mr. Addison be perfectly right in get the money which be his , and not give up the amount of his just claim , to be spend by Dick upon champagne and fiddler , laced clothe , fine furniture , and parasite , Jew and Christian , male and female , who cling to he . as , accord to the famous maxim of Monsieur de Rochefoucault , " in our friend ' misfortune there be something secretly pleasant to we ; " so , on the other hand , their good fortune be disagreeable . if ' tis hard for a man to bear his own good luck , ' tis hard still for his friend to bear it for he and but few of they ordinarily can stand that trial : whereas one of the " precious us " of adversity be , that it be a great reconciler ; that it bring back avert kindness , disarm animosity , and cause yesterday 's enemy to fle his hatred aside , and hold out a hand to the fall friend of old day . there 's pity and love , as well as envy , in the same heart and towards the same person . the rivalry stop when the competitor tumble ; and , as I view it , we should look at these agreeable and disagreeable quality of our humanity humbly alike . they be consequent and natural , and our kindness and meanness both manly . 
 so you may either read the sentence , that the elder of Esmond 's two kinswoman pardon the young her beauty , when that have lose somewhat of its freshness , perhaps ; and forget most her grievance against the other , when the subject of they be no long prosperous and enviable ; or we may say more benevolently   but the sum come to the same figure , work either way ,   that Isabella repent of her unkindness towards Rachel , when Rachel be unhappy ; and , bestir herself in behalf of the poor widow and her child , give they shelter and friendship . the lady be quite good friend as long as the weak one need a protector . before Esmond go away on his first campaign , his mistress be still on term of friendship   though a poor little chit , a woman that have evidently no spirit in she ,   c.   with the eld Lady Castlewood ; and Mistress Beatrix be allow to be a beauty . 
 but between the first year of Queen Anne 's reign , and the second , sad change for the bad have take place in the two young lady , at least in the elder 's description of they . Rachel , Viscountess Castlewood , have no more face than a dumpling , and Mrs. Beatrix be grow quite coarse , and be lose all her beauty . little Lord Blandford   she never would call he Lord Blandford ; his father be Lord Churchill the King , whom he betray , have make he Lord Churchill , and he be Lord Churchill still   might be make eye at she ; but his mother , that vixen of a Sarah Jennings , would never hear of such a folly . Lady Marlborough have get she to be a maid of honor at Court to the Princess , but she would repent of it . the widow Francis   she be but Mrs. Francis Esmond   be a scheming , artful , heartless hussy . she be spoil her brat of a boy , and she would end by marry her chaplain . 
 " what , Tusher ! " cry Mr. Esmond , feel a strange pang of rage and astonishment . 
 " yes Tusher , my maid 's son ; and who have get all the quality of his father the lackey in black , and his accomplished mamma the waiting - woman , " cry my lady . " what do you suppose that a sentimental widow , who will live down in that dingy dungeon of a Castlewood , where she spoil her boy , kill the poor with her drug , have prayer twice a day and see nobody but the chaplain what do you suppose she can do , mon Cousin , but let the horrid parson , with his great square toe and hideous little green eye , make love to she ? Cela c'est vu , mon Cousin . when I be a girl at Castlewood , all the chaplain fall in love with I they have   nothing else to do . " 
 my lady go on with more talk of this kind , though , in truth , Esmond have no idea of what she say far , so entirely do her first word occupy his thought . be they true ? not all , nor half , nor a tenth part of what the garrulous old woman say , be true . could this be so ? no ear have Esmond for anything else , though his patroness chat on for an hour . 
 some young gentleman of the town , with whom Esmond have make acquaintance , have promise to present he to that most charming of actress , and lively and agreeable of woman , Mrs. Bracegirdle , about whom Harry 's old adversary Mohun have draw sword , a few year before my poor lord and he fall out . the famous Mr. Congreve have stamp with his high approval , to the which there be no gainsaying , this delightful person : and she be act in Dick Steele 's comedy , and finally , and for twenty - four hour after behold she , Mr. Esmond feel himself , or think himself , to be as violently enamored of this lovely brunette , as be a thousand other young fellow about the city . to have once see she be to long to behold she again ; and to be offer the delightful privilege of her acquaintance , be a pleasure the very idea of which set the young lieutenant 's heart on fire . a man can not live with comrade under the tent without find out that he too be five - and - twenty . a young fellow can not be cast down by grief and misfortune ever so severe but some night he begin to sleep sound , and some day when dinner - time come to feel hungry for a beefsteak . time , youth and good health , new scene and the excitement of action and a campaign , have pretty well bring Esmond 's mourning to an end ; and his comrade say that Don Dismal , as they call he , be Don Dismal no more . so when a party be make to dine at the " Rose , " and go to the playhouse afterward , Esmond be as pleased as another to take his share of the bottle and the play . 
 how be it that the old aunt 's news , or it might be scandal , about Tom Tusher , cause such a strange and sudden excitement in Tom 's old playfellow ? had not   he swear a thousand time in his own mind that the Lady of Castlewood , who have treat he with such kindness once , and then have leave he so cruelly , be , and be to remain henceforth , indifferent to he for ever ? have his pride and his sense of justice not long since help he to cure the pain of that desertion be it even a pain to he now ? why , but last night as he walk across the field and meadow to Chelsey from Pall Mall , have he not compose two or three stanzas of a song , celebrate Bracegirdle 's brown eye , and declare they a thousand time more beautiful than the bright blue one that ever languish under the lash of an insipid fair beauty ! but Tom Tusher ! Tom Tusher , the waiting - woman 's son , raise up his little eye to his mistress ! Tom Tusher presume to think of Castlewood 's widow ! rage and contempt fill Mr. Harry 's heart at the very notion ; the honor of the family , of which he be the chief , make it his duty to prevent so monstrous an alliance , and to chastise the upstart who could dare to think of such an insult to their house . ' tis true Mr. Esmond often boast of republican principle , and could remember many fine speech he have make at college and elsewhere , with WORTH and not birth for a text : but Tom Tusher to take the place of the noble Castlewood faugh ! ' twa as monstrous as King Hamlet 's widow take off her weed for Claudius . esmond laugh at all widow , all wife , all woman ; and be the bann about to be publish , as no doubt they be , that very next Sunday at Walcote Church , Esmond swear that he would be present to shout no ! in the face of the congregation , and to take a private revenge upon the ear of the bridegroom . 
 instead of go to dinner then at the " Rose " that night , Mr. Esmond bid his servant pack a portmanteau and get horse , and be at Farnham , half - way on the road to Walcote , thirty mile off , before his comrade have get to their supper after the play . he bid his man give no hint to my Lady Dowager 's household of the expedition on which he be go ; and as Chelsey be distant from London , the road bad , and infest by footpad , and esmond often in the habit , when engage in a party of pleasure , of lie at a friend 's lodging in town , there be no need that his old aunt should be disturb at his absence indeed , nothing more delight the old lady than to fancy that mon cousin , the incorrigible young sinner , be abroad box the watch , or scour St. Giles 's . when she be not at her book of devotion , she think Etheridge and Sedley very good reading . she have a hundred pretty story about Rochester , Harry Jermyn , and Hamilton ; and if Esmond would but have run away with the wife even of a citizen , ' tis my belief she would have pawn her diamond   the good of they go to our Lady of Chaillot   to pay his damage . 
 my lord 's little house of Walcote which he inhabit before he take his title and occupy the house of Castlewood lie about a mile from Winchester , and his widow have return to Walcote after my lord 's death as a place always dear to she , and where her early and happy day have be spend , cheerfuller than Castlewood , which be too large for her straiten mean , and give she , too , the protection of the ex - dean , her father . the young Viscount have a year 's schooling at the famous college there , with Mr. Tusher as his governor . so much news of they Mr. Esmond have have during the past year from the old Viscountess , his own father 's widow ; from the young one there have never be a word . 
 twice or thrice in his benefactor 's lifetime , Esmond have be to Walcote ; and now , take but a couple of hour ' rest only at the inn on the road , he be up again long before daybreak , and make such good speed that he be at Walcote by two o'clock of the day . he rid to the end of the village , where he alight and send a man thence to Mr. Tusher , with a message that a gentleman from London would speak with he on urgent business . the messenger come back to say the Doctor be in town , most likely at prayer in the Cathedral . my Lady Viscountess be there , too ; she always go to cathedral prayer every day . 
 the horse belong to the post - house at Winchester . esmond mount again and ride on to the " George ; " whence he walk , leave his grumbling domestic at last happy with a dinner , straight to the Cathedral . the organ be play : the winter 's day be already grow gray : as he pass under the street - arch into the Cathedral yard , and make his way into the ancient solemn edifice . 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 the 29TH DECEMBER . 

 there be scarce a score of person in the Cathedral beside the Dean and some of his clergy , and the chorister , young and old , that perform the beautiful evening prayer . but Mr. Tusher be one of the officiant , and read from the eagle in an authoritative voice , and a great black periwig ; and in the stall , still in her black widow 's hood , sit Esmond 's dear mistress , her son by her side , very much grown , and indeed a noble - look youth , with his mother 's eye , and his father 's curl brown hair , that fall over his point de Venise a pretty picture such as Van Dyck might have paint . mon . Rigaud 's portrait of my Lord Viscount , do at Paris afterwards , give but a french version of his manly , frank , english face . when he look up there be two sapphire beam out of his eye such as no painter 's palette have the color to match , I think . on this day there be not much chance of see that particular beauty of my young lord 's countenance ; for the truth be , he keep his eye shut for the most part , and , the anthem be rather long , be asleep . 
 but the music cease , my lord wake up , look about he , and his eye light on Mr. Esmond , who be sit opposite he , gaze with no small tenderness and melancholy upon two person who have so much of his heart for so many year , Lord Castlewood , with a start , pull at his mother 's sleeve   her face have scarce be lift from her book , and say , " look , mother ! " so loud , that Esmond could hear on the other side of the church , and the old Dean on his throned stall . Lady Castlewood look for an instant as her son bid she , and hold up a warning finger to Frank ; Esmond feel his whole face flush , and his heart throbbing , as that dear lady behold he once more . the rest of the prayer be speedily over ; Mr. Esmond do not hear they ; nor do his mistress , very likely , whose hood go more closely over her face , and who never lift her head again until the service be over , the blessing give , and Mr. Dean , and his procession of ecclesiastic , out of the inner chapel . 
 Young Castlewood come clamber over the stall before the clergy be fairly go , and run up to Esmond , eagerly embrace he . " my dear , dear old Harry ! " he say , " be you come back ? have you be to the war ? you will take I with you when you go again ? why do not   you write to we ? come to mother . " 
 Mr. Esmond could hardly say more than a " God bless you , my boy , " for his heart be very full and grateful at all this tenderness on the lad 's part ; and he be as much move at see Frank as he be fearful about that other interview which be now to take place : for he know not if the widow would reject he as she have do so cruelly a year ago . 
 " it be kind of you to come back to we , Henry , " Lady Esmond say . " I think you might come . " 
 " we read of the fleet come to Portsmouth . why do you not come from Portsmouth ? " Frank ask , or my Lord Viscount , as he now must be call . 
 Esmond have think of that too . he would have give one of his eye so that he might see his dear friend again once more ; but believe that his mistress have forbid he her house , he have obey she , and remain at a distance . 
 " you have but to ask , and you know I would be here , " he say . 
 she give he her hand , her little fair hand ; there be only her marriage ring on it . the quarrel be all over . the year of grief and estrangement be pass . they never have be separate . his mistress have never be out of his mind all that time . no , not once . no , not in the prison ; nor in the camp ; nor on shore before the enemy ; nor at sea under the star of solemn midnight ; nor as he watch the glorious rise of the dawn : not even at the table , where he sit carouse with friend , or at the theatre yonder , where he try to fancy that other eye be bright than her . bright eye there might be , and face more beautiful , but none so dear no voice so sweet as that of his beloved mistress , who have be sister , mother , goddess to he during his youth goddess now no more , for he know of her weakness ; and by thought , by suffer , and that experience it bring , be old now than she ; but more fondly cherish as woman perhaps than ever she have be adore as divinity . what be it ? where lie it ? the secret which make one little hand the dearest of all ? whoever can unriddle that mystery ? here she be , her son by his side , his dear boy . here she be , weep and happy . she take his hand in both her ; he feel her tear . it be a rapture of reconciliation . 
 " here come Squaretoes , " say Frank . " here be Tusher . " 
 Tusher , indeed , now appear , creak on his great heel . Mr. Tom have divest himself of his alb or surplice , and come forward habit in his cassock and great black periwig . how have Esmond ever be for a moment jealous of this fellow ? 
 " give we thy hand , Tom Tusher , " he say . the chaplain make he a very low and stately bow . " I be charm to see Captain Esmond , " say he . " my lord and I have read the Reddas incolumem precor , and apply it , I be sure , to you . you come back with gaditanian laurel ; when I hear you be bind thither , I wish , I be sure , I be another Septimius . my Lord Viscount , your lordship remember Septimi , Gades aditure mecum ? " 
 " there be an angle of earth that I love well than Gades , Tusher , " say Mr. Esmond . " ' tis that one where your reverence hath a parsonage , and where our youth be bring up . " 
 " a house that have so many sacred recollection to I , " say Mr. Tusher   and Harry remember how Tom 's father use to flog he there   " a house near to that of my respected patron , my most honor patroness , must ever be a dear abode to I . but , madam , the verger wait to close the gate on your ladyship . " 
 " and Harry 's come home to supper . Huzzay ! huzzay ! " cry my lord . " Mother , I shall run home and bid beatrix put her ribbon on . beatrix be a maid of honor , Harry . such a fine set - up minx ! " 
 " your heart be never in the Church , Harry , " the widow say , in her sweet low tone , as they walk away together .   now , it seem they never have be part , and again , as if they have be age asunder .   " I always think you have no vocation that way ; and that ' twas a pity to shut you out from the world . you would but have pin and chafe at Castlewood : and ' tis well you should make a name for yourself . I often say so to my dear lord . how he love you ! ' twas my lord that make you stay with we . " 
 " I ask no well than to stay near you always , " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " but to go be good , Harry . when the world can not give peace , you will know where to find it ; but one of your strong imagination and eager desire must try the world first before he tire of it . ' twa not to be think of , or if it once be , it be only by my selfishness , that you should remain as chaplain to a country gentleman and tutor to a little boy . you be of the blood of the Esmonds , kinsman ; and that be always wild in youth . look at Francis . he be but fifteen , and I scarce can keep he in my nest . his talk be all of war and pleasure , and he long to serve in the next campaign . perhaps he and the young Lord Churchill shall go the next . Lord Marlborough have be good to we . you know how kind they be in my misfortune . and so be your your father 's widow . no one know how good the world be , till grief come to try we . ' tis through my Lady Marlborough 's goodness that beatrix hath her place at Court ; and Frank be under my Lord Chamberlain . and the dowager lady , your father 's widow , have promise to provide for you have she not ? " 
 Esmond say , " yes . as far as present favor go , Lady Castlewood be very good to he . and should her mind change , " he add gayly , " as lady ' mind will , I be strong enough to bear my own burden , and make my way somehow . not by the sword very likely . thousand have a well genius for that than I , but there be many way in which a young man of good part and education can get on in the world ; and I be pretty sure , one way or other , of promotion ! " indeed , he have find patron already in the army , and amongst person very able to serve he , too ; and tell his mistress of the flattering aspect of fortune . they walk as though they have never be part , slowly , with the gray twilight closing round they . 
 " and now we be draw near to home , " she continue , " I know you would come , Harry , if if it be but to forgive I for have speak unjustly to you after that horrid horrid misfortune . I be half frantic with grief then when I see you . and I know now they have tell I . that wretch , whose name I can never mention , even have say it : how you try to avert the quarrel , and would have take it on yourself , my poor child : but it be God 's will that I should be punish , and that my dear lord should fall . " 
 " he give I his blessing on his death - bed , " Esmond say . " thank God for that legacy ! " 
 " amen , amen ! dear Henry , " say the lady , press his arm . " I know it . Mr. Atterbury , of St. Bride 's , who be call to he , tell I so . and I thank God , too , and in my prayer ever since remember it . " 
 " you have spar I many a bitter night , have you tell I soon , " Mr. Esmond say . 
 " I know it , I know it , " she answer , in a tone of such sweet humility , as make Esmond repent that he should ever have dare to reproach she . " I know how wicked my heart have be ; and I have suffer too , my dear . I confess to Mr. Atterbury I must not tell any more . he I say I would not write to you or go to you and it be well even that have part , we should part . but I know you would come back I own that . that be no one 's fault . and to - day , Henry , in the anthem , when they sing it , ' when the Lord turn the captivity of Zion , we be like they that dream , ' I think , yes , like they that dream they that dream . and then it go , ' they that sow in tear shall reap in joy ; and he that goeth forth and weepeth , shall doubtless come again with rejoice , bring his sheaf with he ; ' I look up from the book , and see you . I be not surprised when I see you . I know you would come , my dear , and see the gold sunshine round your head . " 
 she smile an almost wild smile as she look up at he . the moon be up by this time , glitter keen in the frosty sky . he could see , for the first time now clearly , her sweet careworn face . 
 " do you know what day it be ? " she continue . " it be the 29th of December it be your birthday ! but last year we do not drink it no , no . my lord be cold , and my Harry be likely to die : and my brain be in a fever ; and we have no wine . but now now you be come again , bring your sheaf with you , my dear . " she burst into a wild flood of weeping as she speak ; she laugh and sob on the young man 's heart , cry out wildly , " bring your sheaf with you your sheaf with you ! " 
 as he have sometimes feel , gaze up from the deck at midnight into the boundless starlit depth overhead , in a rapture of devout wonder at that endless brightness and beauty in some such a way now , the depth of this pure devotion   which be , for the first time , reveal to he   quite smite upon he , and fill his heart with thanksgiving . gracious God , who be he , weak and friendless creature , that such a love should be pour out upon he ? not in vain not in vain have he live hard and thankless should he be to think so that have such a treasure give he . what be ambition compare to that , but selfish vanity ? to be rich , to be famous ? what do these profit a year hence , when other name sound loud than your , when you lie hide away under the ground , along with idle title engraven on your coffin ? but only true love live after you follow your memory with secret blessing or precede you , and intercede for you . non omni moriar if die , I yet live in a tender heart or two ; nor be lose and hopeless living , if a sainted depart soul still love and pray for I . 
 " if if ' tis so , dear lady , " Mr. Esmond say , " why should I ever leave you ? if God hath give I this great boon and near or far from I , as I know now , the heart of my dear mistress follow I , let I have that blessing near I , nor ever part with it till death separate we . come away leave this Europe , this place which have so many sad recollection for you . begin a new life in a new world . my good lord often talk of visit that land in Virginia which King Charles give we give his ancestor . Frank will give we that . no man there will ask if there be a blot on my name , or inquire in the wood what my title be . " 
 " and my child and my duty and my good father , Henry ? " she break out . " he have none but I now ! for soon my sister will leave he , and the old man will be alone . he have conform since the new Queen 's reign ; and here in Winchester , where they love he , they have find a church for he . when the child leave I , I will stay with he . I can not follow they into the great world , where their way lie it scar I . they will come and visit I ; and you will , sometimes , Henry yes , sometimes , as now , in the Holy Advent season , when I have see and bless you once more . " 
 " I would leave all to follow you , " say Mr. Esmond ; " and can you not be as generous for I , dear lady ? " 
 " hush , boy ! " she say , and it be with a mother 's sweet plaintive tone and look that she speak . " the world be begin for you . for I , I have be so weak and sinful that I must leave it , and pray out an expiation , dear Henry . have we house of religion as there be once , and many divine of our Church would have they again , I often think I would retire to one and pass my life in penance . but I would love you still yes , there be no sin in such a love as mine now ; and my dear lord in heaven may see my heart ; and know the tear that have wash my sin away and now now my duty be here , by my child whilst they need I , and by my poor old father , and " 
 " and not by I ? " Henry say . 
 " hush ! " she say again , and raise her hand up to his lip . " I have be your nurse . you could not see I , Harry , when you be in the small - pox , and I come and sit by you . ah ! I pray that I might die , but it would have be in sin , Henry . oh , it be horrid to look back to that time . it be over now and past , and it have be forgive I . when you need I again , I will come ever so far . when your heart be wound , then come to I , my dear . be silent ! let I say all . you never love I , dear Henry no , you do not now , and I thank heaven for it . I use to watch you , and know by a thousand sign that it be so . do you remember how glad you be to go away to college ? ' twa I send you . I tell my papa that , and Mr. Atterbury too , when I speak to he in London . and they both give I absolution both and they be godly man , have authority to bind and to loose . and they forgave I , as my dear lord forgave I before he go to heaven . " 
 " I think the angel be not all in heaven , " Mr. Esmond say . and as a brother fold a sister to his heart ; and as a mother cleave to her son 's breast so for a few moment Esmond 's beloved mistress come to he and bless he . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 I be make welcome at WALCOTE . 

 as they come up to the house at Walcote , the window from within be light up with friendly welcome ; the supper - table be spread in the oak - parlor ; it seem as if forgiveness and love be await the return prodigal . two or three familiar face of domestic be on the look - out at the porch the old housekeeper be there , and young Lockwood from Castlewood in my lord 's livery of tawny and blue . his dear mistress press his arm as they pass into the hall . her eye beam out on he with affection indescribable . " welcome , " be all she say , as she look up , put back her fair curl and black hood . a sweet rosy smile blush on her face ; Harry think he have never see she look so charming . her face be light with a joy that be bright than beauty she take a hand of her son who be in the hall wait his mother she do not quit Esmond 's arm . 
 " welcome , Harry ! " my young lord echo after she . " here , we be all come to say so . here be old Pincot , have not   she grow handsome ? " and Pincot , who be old , and no handsomer than usual , make a curtsy to the Captain , as she call Esmond , and tell my lord to " have do , now . " 
 " and here be Jack Lockwood . he will make a famous grenadier , Jack ; and so shall I ; we will both ' list under you , Cousin . as soon as I be seventeen , I go to the army every gentleman go to the army . look ! who come here ho , ho ! " he burst into a laugh . " ' Tis Mistress Trix , with a new ribbon ; I know she would put one on as soon as she hear a captain be come to supper . " 
 this laugh colloquy take place in the hall of Walcote House : in the midst of which be a staircase that lead from an open gallery , where be the door of the sleep chamber : and from one of these , a wax candle in her hand , and illuminate she , come Mistress Beatrix the light fall indeed upon the scarlet ribbon which she wear , and upon the most brilliant white neck in the world . 
 Esmond have leave a child and find a woman , grow beyond the common height ; and arrive at such a dazzling completeness of beauty , that his eye might well show surprise and delight at behold she . in hers there be a brightness so lustrous and melt , that I have see a whole assembly follow she as if by an attraction irresistible : and that night the great Duke be at the playhouse after ramillie , every soul turn and look   she chance to enter at the opposite side of the theatre at the same moment   at she , and not at he . she be a brown beauty : that is , her eye , hair , and eyebrow and eyelash be dark : her hair curl with rich undulation , and wave over her shoulder ; but her complexion be as dazzle white as snow in sunshine ; except her cheek , which be a bright red , and her lip , which be of a still deep crimson . her mouth and chin , they say , be too large and full , and so they might be for a goddess in marble , but not for a woman whose eye be fire , whose look be love , whose voice be the sweetest low song , whose shape be perfect symmetry , health , decision , activity , whose foot as it plant itself on the ground be firm but flexible , and whose motion , whether rapid or slow , be always perfect grace agile as a nymph , lofty as a queen , now melt , now imperious , now sarcastic there be no single movement of hers but be beautiful . as he think of she , he who write feel young again , and remember a paragon . 
 so she come hold her dress with one fair rounded arm , and her taper before she , trip down the stair to greet Esmond . 
 " she hath put on her scarlet stocking and white shoe , " say my lord , still laugh . " oh , my fine mistress ! be this the way you set your cap at the Captain ? " she approach , shin smile upon Esmond , who could look at nothing but her eye . she advance hold forward her head , as if she would have he kiss she as he use to do when she be a child . 
 " stop , " she say , " I be grow too big ! welcome , cousin Harry , " and she make he an arch curtsy , sweep down to the ground almost , with the most gracious bend , look up the while with the bright eye and sweetest smile . Love seem to radiate from she . Harry eye she with such a rapture as the first lover be describe as have by Milton . 
 " N'est - ce pas ? " say my lady , in a low , sweet voice , still hang on his arm . 
 Esmond turn round with a start and a blush , as he meet his mistress 's clear eye . he have forget she , rapt in admiration of the filia pulcrior . 
 " right foot forward , toe turn out , so : now drop the curtsy , and show the red stocking , Trix . they have   silver clock , Harry . the Dowager send them . she go to put them on , " cry my lord . 
 " hush , you stupid child ! " say Miss , smother her brother with kiss ; and then she must come and kiss her mamma , look all the while at Harry , over his mistress 's shoulder . and if she do not kiss he , she give he both her hand , and then take one of his in both hand , and say , " oh , Harry , we be so , so glad you be come ! " 
 " there be woodcock for supper , " say my lord . " Huzzay ! it be such a hungry sermon . " 
 " and it be the 29th of December ; and our Harry have come home . " 
 " Huzzay , old Pincot ! " again say my lord ; and my dear lady 's lip look as if they be tremble with a prayer . she would have Harry lead in Beatrix to the supper - room , go herself with my young Lord Viscount ; and to this party come Tom Tusher directly , whom four at least out of the company of five wish away . away he go , however , as soon as the sweetmeat be put down , and then , by the great crackling fire , his mistress or beatrix , with her blushing grace , fill his glass for he , Harry tell the story of his campaign , and pass the most delightful night his life have ever know . the sun be up long ere he be , so deep , sweet , and refreshing be his slumber . he wake as if angel have be watch at his bed all night . I dare say one that be as pure and loving as an angel have bless his sleep with her prayer . 
 next morning the chaplain read prayer to the little household at Walcote , as the custom be ; esmond think Mistress Beatrix do not listen to Tusher 's exhortation much : her eye be wander everywhere during the service , at least whenever he look up he meet they . perhaps he also be not very attentive to his Reverence the Chaplain . " this might have be my life , " he be think ; " this might have be my duty from now till old age . well , be it not a pleasant one to be with these dear friend and part from them no more ? until until the destine lover come and take away pretty beatrix " and the good part of Tom Tusher 's exposition , which may have be very learn and eloquent , be quite lose to poor Harry by this vision of the destine lover , who put the preacher out . 
 all the while of the prayer , beatrix knelt a little way before Harry Esmond . the red stocking be change for a pair of gray , and black shoe , in which her foot look to the full as pretty . all the rose of spring could not vie with the brightness of her complexion ; Esmond think he have never see anything like the sunny lustre of her eye . my Lady Viscountess look fatigued , as if with watching , and her face be pale . 
 Miss Beatrix remark these sign of indisposition in her mother and deplore they . " I be an old woman , " say my lady , with a kind smile ; " I can not hope to look as young as you do , my dear . " 
 " she will never look as good as you do if she live till she be a hundred , " say my lord , take his mother by the waist , and kiss her hand . 
 " do I look very wicked , cousin ? " say beatrix , turn full round on Esmond , with her pretty face so close under his chin , that the soft perfume hair touch it . she lay her finger - tip on his sleeve as she speak ; and he put his other hand over her . 
 " I be like your look - glass , " say he , " and that can not   flatter you . " 
 " he mean that you be always look at he , my dear , " say her mother , archly . beatrix run away from Esmond at this , and fly to her mamma , whom she kiss , stop my lady 's mouth with her pretty hand . 
 " and Harry be very good to look at , " say my lady , with her fond eye regard the young man . 
 " if ' tis good to see a happy face , " say he , " you see that . " my lady say , " amen , " with a sigh ; and Harry think the memory of her dear lord rise up and rebuke she back again into sadness ; for her face lose the smile , and resume its look of melancholy . 
 " why , Harry , how fine we look in our scarlet and silver , and our black periwig , " cry my lord . " Mother , I be tired of my own hair . when shall I have a peruke ? where do you get your steenkirk , Harry ? " 
 " it be some of my Lady Dowager 's lace , " say Harry ; ' she give I this and a number of other fine thing . " 
 " my Lady Dowager be not   such a bad woman , " my lord continue . 
 " she be not so so red as she be paint , " say Miss Beatrix . 
 her brother break into a laugh . " I will tell she you say so ; by the Lord , Trix , I will , " he cry out . 
 " she will know that you have not   the wit to say it , my lord , " say Miss Beatrix . 
 " we will not   quarrel the first day Harry be here , will we , mother ? " say the young lord . " we will see if we can get on to the new year without a fight . have some of this Christmas pie . and here come the tankard ; no , it be Pincot with the tea . " 
 " will the Captain choose a dish ? " ask Mistress Beatrix . 
 " I say , Harry , " my lord go on , " I will show thee my horse after breakfast ; and we will go a bird - net to - night , and on Monday there be a cock - match at Winchester do you love cock - fighting , Harry ? between the gentleman of Sussex and the gentleman of Hampshire , at ten pound the battle , and fifty pound the odd battle to show one - and - twenty cock . " 
 " and what will you do , beatrix , to amuse our kinsman ? " ask my lady . 
 " I will listen to he , " say beatrix . " I be sure he have a hundred thing to tell we . and I be jealous already of the spanish lady . be that a beautiful nun at Cadiz that you rescue from the soldier ? your man talk of it last night in the kitchen , and Mrs. Betty tell I this morning as she comb my hair . and he say you must be in love , for you sit on deck all night , and scribble verses all day in your tablebook . " Harry think if he have want a subject for verse yesterday , to - day he have find one : and not all the Lindamiras and Ardelias of the poet be half so beautiful as this young creature ; but he do not say so , though some one do for he . 
 this be his dear lady , who , after the meal be over , and the young people be go , begin talk of her child with Mr. Esmond , and of the character of one and the other , and of her hope and fear for both of they . " ' tis not while they be at home , " she say , " and in their mother 's nest , I fear for they ' tis when they be go into the world , whither I shall not be able to follow they . beatrix will begin her service next year . you may have hear a rumor about about my Lord Blandford . they be both child ; and it be but idle talk . I know my kinswoman would never let he make such a poor marriage as our beatrix would be . there be scarce a princess in Europe that she think be good enough for he or for her ambition . " 
 " there be not a princess in Europe to compare with she , " say Esmond . 
 " in beauty ? no , perhaps not , " answer my lady . " she be most beautiful , be not   she ? ' tis not a mother 's partiality that deceive I . I mark you yesterday when she come down the stair : and read it in your face . we look when you do not   fancy we look , and see well than you think , dear Harry : and just now when they speak about your poem you writ pretty line when you be but a boy you think beatrix be a pretty subject for verse , do not you , Harry ? "   the gentleman could only blush for a reply .   " and so she be nor be you the first her pretty face have captivate . ' tis quickly do . such a pair of bright eye as hers learn their power very soon , and use it very early . " and , look at he keenly with her , the fair widow leave he . 
 and so it be a pair of bright eye with a dozen glance suffice to subdue a man ; to enslave he , and inflame he ; to make he even forget ; they dazzle he so that the past become straightway dim to he ; and he so prize they that he would give all his life to possess them . what be the fond love of dear friend compare to this treasure ? be memory as strong as expectancy ? fruition , as hunger ? gratitude , as desire ? I have look at royal diamond in the jewel - room in Europe , and think how war have be make about them ; Mogul sovereign depose and strangle for they , or ransom with they ; million expend to buy they ; and dare life lose in dig out the little shin toy that I value no more than the button in my hat . and so there be other glitter bauble   of rare water too   for which man have be set to kill and quarrel ever since mankind begin ; and which last but for a score of year , when their sparkle be over . where be those jewel now that beam under Cleopatra 's forehead , or shine in the socket of Helen ? 
 the second day after Esmond 's come to Walcote , Tom Tusher have leave to take a holiday , and go off in his very good gown and band to court the young woman whom his Reverence desire to marry , and who be not a viscount 's widow , as it turn out , but a brewer 's relict at Southampton , with a couple of thousand pound to her fortune : for honest Tom 's heart be under such excellent control , that Venus herself without a portion would never have cause it to flutter . so he ride away on his heavy - pace gelding to pursue his jog - trot love , leave Esmond to the society of his dear mistress and her daughter , and with his young lord for a companion , who be charm , not only to see an old friend , but to have the tutor and his latin book put out of the way . 
 the boy talk of thing and people , and not a little about himself , in his frank artless way . ' twa easy to see that he and his sister have the well of their fond mother , for the first place in whose affection , though they fight constantly , and though the kind lady persist that she love both equally , ' twa not difficult to understand that Frank be his mother 's darling and favorite . he rule the whole household   always except rebellious beatrix   not less now than when he be a child marshal the village boy in play at soldier , and can they lustily too , like the sturdy corporal . as for Tom Tusher , his Reverence treat the young lord with that politeness and deference which he always show for a great man , whatever his age or his stature be . indeed , with respect to this young one , it be impossible not to love he , so frank and win be his manner , his beauty , his gayety , the ring of his laughter , and the delightful tone of his voice . wherever he go , he charm and domineer . I think his old grandfather the Dean , and the grim old housekeeper , Mrs. Pincot , be as much his slave as his mother be : and as for Esmond , he find himself presently submit to a certain fascination the boy have , and slave it like the rest of the family . the pleasure which he have in Frank 's mere company and converse exceed that which he ever enjoy in the society of any other man , however delightful in talk , or famous for wit . his presence bring sunshine into a room , his laugh , his prattle , his noble beauty and brightness of look cheer and charm indescribably . at the least tale of sorrow , his hand be in his purse , and he be eager with sympathy and bounty . the way in which woman love and pet he , when , a year or two afterwards , he come upon the world , yet a mere boy , and the folly which they do for he   as indeed he for they , recall the career of Rochester , and outdo the success of Grammont . his very creditor love he ; and the hard usurer , and some of the rigid prude of the other sex too , could deny he nothing . he be no more witty than another man , but what he say , he say and look as no man else could say or look it . I have see the woman at the comedy at Bruxelles crowd round he in the lobby : and as he sit on the stage more people look at he than at the actor , and watch he ; and I remember at ramillie , when he be hit and fall , a great big red - haired Scotch sergeant fling his halbert down , burst out a - cry like a woman , seize he up as if he have be an infant , and carry he out of the fire . this brother and sister be the most beautiful couple ever see ; though after he wing away from the maternal nest this pair be seldom together . 
 sit at dinner two day after Esmond 's arrival   it be the last day of the year , and so happy a one to Harry Esmond , that to enjoy it be quite worth all the previous pain which he have endure and forget , my young lord , fill a bumper , and bid Harry take another , drink to his sister , salute she under the title of " Marchioness . " 
 " Marchioness ! " say Harry , not without a pang of wonder , for he be curious and jealous already . 
 " Nonsense , my lord , " say beatrix , with a toss of her head . my Lady Viscountess look up for a moment at Esmond , and cast her eye down . 
 " the Marchioness of Blandford , " say Frank . " do not   you know hath not Rouge Dragon tell you ? "   my lord use to call the Dowager of Chelsey by this and other name .   " Blandford have a lock of her hair : the Duchess find he on his knee to Mistress Trix , and box his ear , and say Dr. Hare should whip he . " 
 " I wish Mr. Tusher would whip you too , " say Beatrix . 
 my lady only say : " I hope you will tell none of these silly story elsewhere than at home , Francis . " 
 " ' tis true , on my word , " continue Frank : " look at Harry scowling , mother , and see how beatrix blush as red as the silver - clock stocking . " 
 " I think we have well leave the gentleman to their wine and their talk , " say Mistress Beatrix , rise up with the air of a young queen , toss her rustle flow drapery about she , and quit the room , follow by her mother . 
 Lady Castlewood again look at Esmond , as she stoop down and kiss Frank . " do not tell those silly story , child , " she say : " do not drink much wine , sir ; Harry never love to drink wine . " and she go away , too , in her black robe , look back on the young man with her fair , fond face . 
 " egad ! it be true , " say Frank , sip his wine with the air of a lord . " what think you of this Lisbon real collare ? ' tis well than your heady port : we get it out of one of the spanish ship that come from Vigo last year : my mother buy it at Southampton , as the ship be lie there the ' rose , ' Captain Hawkins . " 
 " why , I come home in that ship , " say Harry . 
 " and it bring home a good fellow and good wine , " say my lord . " I say , Harry , I wish thou hadst not that curse bar sinister . " 
 " and why not the bar sinister ? " ask the other . 
 " suppose I go to the army and be kill every gentleman go to the army who be to take care of the woman ? trix will never stop at home ; mother 's in love with you , yes , I think mother 's in love with you . she be always praise you , and always talk about you ; and when she go to Southampton , to see the ship , I find she out . but you see it be impossible : we be of the old blood in England ; we come in with the conqueror ; we be only baronet , but what then ? we be force into that . James the First force our great grandfather . we be above title ; we old english gentry do not   want them ; the Queen can make a duke any day . look at Blandford 's father , Duke Churchill , and Duchess Jennings , what be they , Harry ? damn it , sir , what be they , to turn up their nose at we ? where be they when our ancestor ride with King Henry at Agincourt , and fill up the french King 's cup after poictier ? ' Fore George , sir , why should not   Blandford marry beatrix ? by G ! he shall marry beatrix , or tell I the reason why . we will marry with the good blood of England , and none but the good blood of England . you be an Esmond , and you can not   help your birth , my boy . let us have another bottle . what ! no more ? I have   drunk three part of this myself . I have many a night with my father ; you stand to he like a man , Harry . you back your blood ; you can not   help your misfortune , you know , no man can help that . "
