 the elder say he would go in to his mistress 's tea - table . the young lad , with a heighten color and voice , begin sing a snatch of a song , and march out of the room . esmond hear he presently call his dog about he , and cheer and talk to they ; and by a hundred of his look and gesture , trick of voice and gait , be remind of the dead lord , Frank 's father . 
 and so , the sylvest night pass away ; the family part long before midnight , Lady Castlewood remembering , no doubt , former New Years ' eve , when health be drunk , and laughter go round in the company of he , to whom year , past , and present , and future , be to be as one ; and so care not to sit with her child and hear the cathedral bell ring the birth of the year 1703 . esmond hear the chime as he sit in his own chamber , ruminate by the blaze fire there , and listen to the last note of they , look out from his window towards the city , and the great gray tower of the Cathedral lie under the frosty sky , with the keen star shin above . 
 the sight of these brilliant orb no doubt make he think of other luminary . " and so her eye have already do execution , " think Esmond " on whom ? who can tell I ? " luckily his kinsman be by , and Esmond know he would have no difficulty in find out Mistress beatrix 's history from the simple talk of the boy . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 FAMILY TALK . 

 what Harry admire and submit to in the pretty lad his kinsman be   for why should he resist it ?   the calmness of patronage which my young lord assume , as if to command be his undoubted right , and all the world   below his degree   ought to bow down to Viscount Castlewood . 
 " I know my place , Harry , " he say . " I be not proud the boy at Winchester College say I be proud : but I be not proud . I be simply Francis James Viscount Castlewood in the peerage of Ireland . I might have be   do you know that ?   Francis James Marquis and Earl of Esmond in that of England . the late lord refuse the title which be offer to he by my godfather , his late Majesty . you should know that you be of our family , you know you can not help your bar sinister , Harry , my dear fellow ; and you belong to one of the good family in England , in spite of that ; and you stand by my father , and by G ! I will stand by you . you shall never want a friend , Harry , while Francis James Viscount Castlewood have a shilling . it be now 1703 I shall come of age in 1709 . I shall go back to Castlewood ; I shall live at Castlewood ; I shall build up the house . my property will be pretty well restore by then . the late viscount mismanage my property , and leave it in a very bad state . my mother be live close , as you see , and keep I in a way hardly befit a peer of these realm ; for I have but a pair of horse , a governor , and a man that be valet and groom . but when I be of age , these thing will be set right , Harry . our house will be as it should be . you will always come to Castlewood , will not   you ? you shall always have your two room in the court keep for you ; and if anybody slight you , d - they ! let they have a care of ME . I shall marry early Trix will be a duchess by that time , most likely ; for a cannon ball may knock over his grace any day , you know . " 
 " how ? " say Harry . 
 " Hush , my dear ! " say my Lord Viscount . " you be of the family you be faithful to we , by George , and I tell you everything . Blandford will marry she or " and here he put his little hand on his sword " you understand the rest . Blandford know which of we two be the good weapon . at small - sword , or back - sword , or sword and dagger if he like ; I can beat he . I have try he , Harry ; and begad he know I be a man not to be trifle with . " 
 " but you do not mean , " say Harry , conceal his laughter , but not his wonder , " that you can force my Lord Blandford , the son of the first man of this kingdom , to marry your sister at sword 's point ? " 
 " I mean to say that we be cousin by the mother 's side , though that be nothing to boast of . I mean to say that an Esmond be as good as a Churchill ; and when the King come back , the Marquis of Esmond 's sister may be a match for any nobleman 's daughter in the kingdom . there be but two marquis in all England , William Herbert Marquis of Powis , and Francis James Marquis of Esmond ; and hark you , Harry , now swear you will never mention this . give I your honor as a gentleman , for you be a gentleman , though you be a " 
 " well , well ? " say Harry , a little impatient . 
 " well , then , when after my late viscount 's misfortune , my mother go up with we to London , to ask for justice against you all   as for Mohun , I will have his blood , as sure as my name be Francis Viscount Esmond   we go to stay with our cousin my Lady Marlborough , with whom we have quarrel for ever so long . but when misfortune come , she stand by her blood : so do the Dowager Viscountess stand by her blood , so do you . well , sir , whilst my mother be petition the late Prince of Orange for I will never call he king and while you be in prison , we live at my Lord Marlborough 's house , who be only a little there , be away with the army in Holland . and then . . . I say , Harry , you will not   tell , now ? " 
 Harry again make a vow of secrecy . 
 " well , there use to be all sort of fun , you know : my Lady Marlborough be very fond of we , and she say I be to be her page ; and she get Trix to be a maid of honor , and while she be up in her room cry , we use to be always have fun , you know ; and the Duchess use to kiss I , and so do her daughter , and Blandford fall tremendous in love with Trix , and she like he ; and one day he he kiss she behind a door he do though , and the Duchess catch he , and she bang such a box of the ear both at Trix and Blandford you should have see it ! and then she say that we must leave directly , and abuse my mamma who be cognizant of the business ; but she be not   never think about anything but father . and so we come down to Walcote . Blandford be lock up , and not allow to see Trix . but I get at he . I climb along the gutter , and in through the window , where he be cry . 
 " ' Marquis , ' say I , when he have open it and help I in , ' you know I wear a sword , ' for I have bring it . 
 " ' oh , viscount , ' say he ' oh , my dearest Frank ! ' and he throw himself into my arm and burst out a - crying . ' I do love Mistress Beatrix so , that I shall die if I do not   have she . ' 
 " ' my dear Blandford , ' say I , ' you be young to think of marrying ; ' for he be but fifteen , and a young fellow of that age can scarce do so , you know . 
 " ' but I will wait twenty year , if she will have I , ' say he . ' I will never marry no , never , never , never , marry anybody but she . no , not a princess , though they would have I do it ever so . if beatrix will wait for I , her Blandford swear he will be faithful . ' and he write a paper   it be not   spelt right , for he write ' I be ready to sine with MY BLODE , ' which , you know , Harry , be not   the way of spell it , and vow that he would marry none other but the Honorable Mistress Gertrude Beatrix Esmond , only sister of his dear friend Francis James , fourth Viscount Esmond . and so I give he a locket of her hair . " 
 " a locket of her hair ? " cry Esmond . 
 " yes . Trix give I one after the fight with the Duchess that very day . I be sure I do not   want it ; and so I give it he , and we kiss at parting , and say ' good - by , brother . ' and I get back through the gutter ; and we set off home that very evening . and he go to King 's College , in Cambridge , and I'M go to Cambridge soon ; and if he do not   stand to his promise   for he be only write once , he know I wear a sword , Harry . come along , and let us go see the cocking - match at Winchester . 
 " . . . . but I say , " he add , laugh , after a pause , " I do not   think Trix will break her heart about he . La bless you ! whenever she see a man , she make eye at he ; and young Sir Wilmot Crawley of Queen 's Crawley , and Anthony Henley of Airesford , be at sword draw about she , at the Winchester Assembly , a month ago . " 
 that night Mr. Harry 's sleep be by no mean so pleasant or sweet as it have be on the first two evening after his arrival at Walcote . " so the bright eye have be already shin on another , " think he , " and the pretty lip , or the cheek at any rate , have begin the work which they be make for . here be a girl not sixteen , and one young gentleman be already whimper over a lock of her hair , and two country squire be ready to cut each other 's throat that they may have the honor of a dance with she . what a fool be I to be dally about this passion , and singe my wing in this foolish flame . wing ! why not say crutch ? ' there be but eight year ' difference between we , to be sure ; but in life I be thirty year old . how could I ever hope to please such a sweet creature as that , with my rough way and glum face ? say that I have merit ever so much , and win myself a name , could she ever listen to I ? she must be my Lady Marchioness , and I remain a nameless bastard . oh ! my master , my master ! "   here he fall to think with a passionate grief of the vow which he have make to his poor die lord .   " oh ! my mistress , dear and kind , will you be content with the sacrifice which the poor orphan make for you , whom you love , and who so love you ? " 
 and then come a fiercer pang of temptation . " a word from I , " Harry think , " a syllable of explanation , and all this might be change ; but no , I swear it over the die bed of my benefactor . for the sake of he and his ; for the sacred love and kindness of old day ; I give my promise to he , and may kind heaven enable I to keep my vow ! " 
 the next day , although Esmond give no sign of what be go on in his mind , but strove to be more than ordinarily gay and cheerful when he meet his friend at the morning meal , his dear mistress , whose clear eye it seem no emotion of his could escape , perceive that something trouble he , for she look anxiously towards he more than once during the breakfast , and when he go up to his chamber afterwards she presently follow he , and knock at his door . 
 as she enter , no doubt the whole story be clear to she at once , for she find our young gentleman pack his valise , pursuant to the resolution which he have come to over - night of make a brisk retreat out of this temptation . 
 she close the door very carefully behind she , and then leant against it , very pale , her hand fold before she , look at the young man , who be kneel over his work of packing . " be you go so soon ? " she say . 
 he rise up from his knee , blushing , perhaps , to be so discover , in the very act , as it be , and take one of her fair little hand it be that which have her marriage ring on and kiss it . 
 " it be good that it should be so , dear lady , " he say . 
 " I know you be go , at breakfast . I I think you might stay . what have happen ? why can not   you remain long with we ? what have Frank tell you you be talk together late last night ? " 
 " I have but three day ' leave from Chelsey , " Esmond say , as gayly as he could . " my aunt she let I call her aunt be my mistress now ! I owe she my lieutenancy and my laced coat . she have take I into high favor ; and my new General be to dine at Chelsey to - morrow General Lumley , madam who have appoint I his aide - de - camp , and on whom I must have the honor of wait . see , here be a letter from the Dowager ; the post bring it last night ; and I would not speak of it , for fear of disturb our last merry meeting . " 
 my lady glance at the letter , and put it down with a smile that be somewhat contemptuous . " I have no need to read the letter , " say she   indeed , ' twas as well she do not ; for the Chelsey missive , in the poor Dowager 's usual french jargon , permit he a long holiday than he say . " Je vous donne , " quoth her ladyship , " oui jour , pour vous fatigay parfaictement de vos parens fatigans "   " I have no need to read the letter , " say she . " what be it Frank tell you last night ? " 
 " he tell I little I do not know , " Mr. Esmond answer . " but I have think of that little , and here be the result : I have no right to the name I bear , dear lady ; and it be only by your sufferance that I be allow to keep it . if I think for an hour of what have perhaps cross your mind too " 
 " yes , I do , Harry , " say she ; " I think of it ; and think of it . I would soon call you my son than the great prince in Europe yes , than the great prince . for who be there so good and so brave , and who would love she as you would ? but there be reason a mother can not   tell . " 
 " I know they , " say Mr. Esmond , interrupt she with a smile . " I know there be Sir Wilmot Crawley of Queen 's Crawley , and Mr. Anthony Henley of the Grange , and my Lord Marquis of Blandford , that seem to be the favor suitor . you shall ask I to wear my Lady Marchioness 's favor and to dance at her ladyship 's wedding . " 
 " oh ! Harry , Harry , it be none of these folly that frighten I , " cry out Lady Castlewood . " Lord Churchill be but a child , his outbreak about Beatrix be a mere boyish folly . his parent would rather see he bury than marry to one below he in rank . and do you think that I would stoop to sue for a husband for Francis Esmond 's daughter ; or submit to have my girl smuggle into that proud family to cause a quarrel between son and parent , and to be treat only as an inferior ? I would disdain such a meanness . beatrix would scorn it . ah ! Henry , ' tis not with you the fault lie , ' tis with she . I know you both , and love you : need I be ashamed of that love now ? no , never , never , and ' tis not you , dear Harry , that be unworthy . ' tis for my poor beatrix I tremble whose headstrong will frighten I ; whose jealous temper   they say I be jealous too , but , pray God , I be cure of that sin   and whose vanity no word or prayer of mine can cure only suffer , only experience , and remorse afterwards . oh ! Henry , she will make no man happy who love she . go away , my son : leave she : love we always , and think kindly of we : and for I , my dear , you know that these wall contain all that I love in the world . " 
 in after life , do Esmond find the word true which his fond mistress speak from her sad heart ? warn he have : but I doubt other have warn before his time , and since : and he benefit by it as most man do . 
 my young Lord Viscount be exceed sorry when he hear that Harry could not come to the cock - match with he , and must go to London , but no doubt my lord console himself when the Hampshire cock win the match ; and he see every one of the battle , and crow properly over the conquered Sussex gentleman . 
 as Esmond ride towards town his servant , come up to he , inform he with a grin , that Mistress Beatrix have bring out a new gown and blue stocking for that day 's dinner , in which she intend to appear , and have fly into a rage and give she maid a slap on the face soon after she hear he be go away . Mistress beatrix 's woman , the fellow say , come down to the servant ' hall crying , and with the mark of a blow still on her cheek : but esmond peremptorily order he to fall back and be silent , and ride on with thought enough of his own to occupy he some sad one , some inexpressibly dear and pleasant . 
 his mistress , from whom he have be a year separate , be his dear mistress again . the family from which he have be part , and which he love with the fond devotion , be his family once more . if Beatrix 's beauty shine upon he , it be with a friendly lustre , and he could regard it with much such a delight as he bring away after see the beautiful picture of the smile Madonnas in the convent at Cadiz , when he be despatch thither with a flag ; and as for his mistress , ' twa difficult to say with what a feeling he regard she . ' twas happiness to have see she ; ' twa no great pang to part ; a filial tenderness , a love that be at once respect and protection , fill his mind as he think of she ; and near she or far from she , and from that day until now , and from now till death be past and beyond it , he pray that sacred flame may ever burn . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 I make the campaign of 1704 . 

 Mr. Esmond ride up to London then , where , if the Dowager have be angry at the abrupt leave of absence he take , she be mightily pleased at his speedy return . 
 he go immediately and pay his court to his new general , General Lumley , who receive he graciously , have know his father , and also , he be pleased to say , have have the very good account of Mr. Esmond from the officer whose aide - de - camp he have be at Vigo . during this winter Mr. Esmond be gazette to a lieutenancy in Brigadier Webb 's regiment of Fusileers , then with their colonel in Flanders ; but be now attach to the suite of Mr. Lumley , Esmond do not join his own regiment until more than a year afterwards , and after his return from the campaign of Blenheim , which be fight the next year . the campaign begin very early , our troop march out of their quarter before the winter be almost over , and invest the city of Bonn , on the Rhine , under the Duke 's command . his grace join the army in deep grief of mind , with crape on his sleeve , and his household in mourning ; and the very same packet which bring the Commander - in - chief over , bring letter to the force which precede he , and one from his dear mistress to Esmond , which interest he not a little . 
 the young Marquis of Blandford , his grace 's son , who have be enter in King 's College in Cambridge ,   whither my Lord Viscount have also go , to Trinity , with Mr. Tusher as his governor ,   have be seize with small - pox , and be dead at sixteen year of age , and so poor Frank 's scheme for his sister 's advancement be over , and that innocent childish passion nip in the birth . 
 Esmond 's mistress would have have he return , at least her letter hint as much ; but in the presence of the enemy this be impossible , and our young man take his humble share in the siege , which need not be describe here , and have the good luck to escape without a wound of any sort , and to drink his general 's health after the surrender . he be in constant military duty this year , and do not think of ask for a leave of absence , as one or two of his less fortunate friend do , who be cast away in that tremendous storm which happen towards the close of November , that " which of late o'er pale Britannia past "   as Mr. Addison sing of it , and in which score of our great ship and 15,000 of our seaman go down . 
 they say that our Duke be quite heart - break by the calamity which have befall his family ; but his enemy find that he could subdue they , as well as master his grief . successful as have be this great general 's operation in the past year , they be far enhance by the splendor of his victory in the ensue campaign . his Grace the Captain - General go to England after Bonn , and our army fall back into Holland , where , in April 1704 , his grace again find the troop , embark from Harwich and landing at Maesland Sluys : thence his grace come immediately to the Hague , where he receive the foreign minister , general officer , and other people of quality . the great honor be pay to his grace everywhere at the Hague , Utrecht , Ruremonde , and Maestricht ; the civil authority come to meet his coach : salvos of cannon salute he , canopy of state be erect for he where he stop , and feast prepare for the numerous gentleman follow in his suite . his grace review the troop of the States - General between Liege and Maestricht , and afterwards the english force , under the command of General Churchill , near Bois - le - Duc . every preparation be make for a long march ; and the army hear , with no small elation , that it be the Commander - in - Chief 's intention to carry the war out of the Low Countries , and to march on the Mozelle . before leave our camp at Maestricht , we hear that the French , under the Marshal Villeroy , be also bind towards the Mozelle . 
 towards the end of May , the army reach Coblentz ; and next day , his grace , and the general accompany he , go to visit the Elector of Treves at his Castle of Ehrenbreitstein , the horse and dragoon pass the Rhine whilst the Duke be entertain at a grand feast by the Elector . all as yet be novelty , festivity , and splendor a brilliant march of a great and glorious army through a friendly country , and sure through some of the most beautiful scene of nature which I ever witness . 
 the foot and artillery , follow after the horse as quick as possible , cross the Rhine under Ehrenbreitstein , and so to Castel , over against Mayntz , in which city his grace , his general , and his retinue be receive at the landing - place by the elector 's coach , carry to his Highness 's palace amidst the thunder of cannon , and then once more magnificently entertain . Gidlingen , in Bavaria , be appoint as the general rendezvous of the army , and thither , by different route , the whole force of English , Dutch , Danes , and german auxiliary take their way . the foot and artillery under General Churchill pass the Neckar , at Heidelberg ; and Esmond have an opportunity of see that city and palace , once so famous and beautiful   though shatter and batter by the French , under Turenne , in the late war , where his grandsire have serve the beautiful and unfortunate Electress - Palatine , the first King Charles 's sister . 
 at Mindelsheim , the famous Prince of Savoy come to visit our commander , all of we crowd eagerly to get a sight of that brilliant and intrepid warrior ; and our troop be draw up in battalia before the Prince , who be pleased to express his admiration of this noble english army . at length we come in sight of the enemy between Dillingen and Lawingen , the Brentz lie between the two army . the Elector , judge that Donauwort would be the point of his grace 's attack , send a strong detachment of his good troop to Count Darcos , who be post at Schellenberg , near that place , where great intrenchment be throw up , and thousand of pioneer employ to strengthen the position . 
 on the 2nd of July his grace storm the post , with what success on our part need scarce be tell . his grace advance with six thousand foot , English and Dutch , thirty squadron , and three regiment of Imperial Cuirassiers , the Duke cross the river at the head of the cavalry . although our troop make the attack with unparalleled courage and fury rush up to the very gun of the enemy , and be slaughter before their work we be drive back many time , and should not have carry they , but that the Imperialists come up under the Prince of Baden , when the enemy could make no head against we : we pursue they into the trench , make a terrible slaughter there , and into the very Danube , where a great part of his troop , follow the example of their general , Count Darcos and the elector himself , try to save themselves by swim . our army enter Donauwort , which the Bavarians evacuate ; and where ' twa say the Elector purpose to have give we a warm reception , by burn we in our bed ; the cellar of the house , when we take possession of they , be find stuff with straw . but though the link be there , the link - boy have run away . the townsman save their house , and our General take possession of the enemy 's ammunition in the arsenal , his store , and magazine . five day afterwards a great " Te Deum " be sing in Prince Lewis 's army , and a solemn day of thanksgiving hold in our own ; the Prince of Savoy 's compliment come to his grace the Captain - General during the day 's religious ceremony , and conclude , as it be , with an amen . 
 and now , have see a great military march through a friendly country ; the pomp and festivity of more than one german court ; the severe struggle of a hotly contest battle , and the triumph of victory , Mr. Esmond behold another part of military duty : our troop enter the enemy 's territory , and put all around they to fire and sword ; burn farm , waste field , shriek woman , slaughter son and father , and drunken soldiery , curse and carouse in the midst of tear , terror , and murder . why do the stately Muse of History , that delight in describe the valor of hero and the grandeur of conquest , leave out these scene , so brutal , mean , and degrading , that yet form by far the great part of the drama of war ? you , gentleman of England , who live at home at ease , and compliment yourself in the song of triumph with which our chieftain be bepraise you pretty maidens , that come tumble down the stair when the fife and drum call you , and huzzah for the british Grenadiers do you take account that these item go to make up the amount of the triumph you admire , and form part of the duty of the hero you fondle ? our chief , whom England and all Europe , save only the Frenchmen , worship almost , have this of the godlike in he , that he be impassible before victory , before danger , before defeat . before the great obstacle or the most trivial ceremony ; before a hundred thousand man draw in battalia , or a peasant slaughter at the door of his burn hovel ; before a carouse of drunken german lord , or a monarch 's court or a cottage table , where his plan be lay , or an enemy 's battery , vomit flame and death , and strew corps round about he ; he be always cold , calm , resolute , like fate . he perform a treason or a court - bow , he tell a falsehood as black as Styx , as easily as he pay a compliment or speak about the weather . he take a mistress , and leave she ; he betray his benefactor , and support he , or would have murder he , with the same calmness always , and have no more remorse than Clotho when she weave the thread , or lachesis when she cut it . in the hour of battle I have hear the Prince of Savoy 's officer say , the Prince become possess with a sort of warlike fury ; his eye light up ; he rush hither and thither , rag ; he shriek curse and encouragement , yell and hark his bloody war - dog on , and himself always at the first of the hunt . our duke be as calm at the mouth of the cannon as at the door of a drawing - room . perhaps he could not have be the great man he be , have he have a heart either for love or hatred , or pity or fear , or regret or remorse . he achieve the high deed of daring , or deep calculation of thought , as he perform the very mean action of which a man be capable ; tell a lie , or cheat a fond woman , or rob a poor beggar of a halfpenny , with a like awful serenity and equal capacity of the high and low act of our nature . 
 his quality be pretty well know in the army , where there be party of all politic , and of plenty of shrewdness and wit ; but there exist such a perfect confidence in he , as the first captain of the world , and such a faith and admiration in his prodigious genius and fortune , that the very man whom he notoriously cheat of their pay , the chief whom he use and injure   for he use all man , great and small , that come near he , as his instrument alike , and take something of theirs , either some quality or some property   the blood of a soldier , it might be , or a jewel hat , or a hundred thousand crown from a king , or a portion out of a starving sentinel 's three - farthing ; or   when he be young   a kiss from a woman , and the gold chain off her neck , take all he could from woman or man , and have , as I have say , this of the godlike in he , that he could see a hero perish or a sparrow fall , with the same amount of sympathy for either . not that he have no tear ; he could always order up this reserve at the proper moment to battle ; he could draw upon tear or smile alike , and whenever need be for use this cheap coin . he would cringe to a shoeblack , as he would flatter a minister or a monarch ; be haughty , be humble , threaten , repent , weep , grasp your hand ,   or stab you whenever he see occasion   but yet those of the army , who know he well and have suffer most from he , admire he most of all : and as he ride along the line to battle or gallop up in the nick of time to a battalion reel from before the enemy 's charge or shot , the faint man and officer get new courage as they see the splendid calm of his face , and feel that his will make they irresistible . 
 after the great victory of Blenheim the enthusiasm of the army for the Duke , even of his bitter personal enemy in it , amount to a sort of rage nay , the very officer who curse he in their heart be among the most frantic to cheer he . who could refuse his meed of admiration to such a victory and such a victor ? not he who write : a man may profess to be ever so much a philosopher ; but he who fight on that day must feel a thrill of pride as he recall it . 
 the french right be post near to the village of Blenheim , on the Danube , where the Marshal Tallard 's quarter be ; their line extend through , it may be a league and a half , before Lutzingen and up to a woody hill , round the base of which , and act against the Prince of Savoy , be forty of his squadron . 
 here be a village that the frenchman have burn , the wood being , in fact , a well shelter and easy of guard than any village . 
 before these two village and the french line run a little stream , not more than two foot broad , through a marsh   that be mostly dry up from the heat of the weather , and this stream be the only separation between the two army our come up and range themselves in line of battle before the French , at six o'clock in the morning ; so that our line be quite visible to theirs ; and the whole of this great plain be black and swarm with troop for hour before the cannonading begin . 
 on one side and the other this cannonading last many hour . the french gun be in position in front of their line , and do severe damage among our horse especially , and on our right wing of Imperialists under the Prince of Savoy , who could neither advance his artillery nor his line , the ground before he be cut up by ditch , morass , and very difficult of passage for the gun . 
 it be past mid - day when the attack begin on our left , where Lord Cutts command , the brave and most beloved officer in the english army . and now , as if to make his experience in war complete , our young aide - de - camp have see two great army face each other in line of battle , and have the honor of rid with order from one end to other of the line , come in for a not uncommon accompaniment of military glory , and be knock on the head , along with many hundred of brave fellow , almost at the very commencement of this famous day of Blenheim . a little after noon , the disposition for attack be complete with much delay and difficulty , and under a severe fire from the enemy 's gun , that be well post and more numerous than our , a body of English and Hessians , with Major - General Wilkes command at the extreme left of our line , march upon Blenheim , advance with great gallantry , the Major - General on foot , with his officer , at the head of the column , and marching , with his hat off , intrepidly in the face of the enemy , who be pour in a tremendous fire from his gun and musketry , to which our people be instruct not to reply , except with pike and bayonet when they reach the french palisade . to these Wilkes walk intrepidly , and strike the woodwork with his sword before our people charge it . he be shoot down at the instant , with his colonel , major , and several officer ; and our troop cheer and huzzaing , and come on , as they do , with immense resolution and gallantry , be nevertheless stop by the murderous fire from behind the enemy 's defence , and then attack in flank by a furious charge of french horse which sweep out of Blenheim , and cut down our man in great number . three fierce and desperate assault of our foot be make and repulse by the enemy ; so that our column of foot be quite shatter , and fall back , scramble over the little rivulet , which we have cross so resolutely an hour before , and pursue by the french cavalry , slaughter we and cut we down . 
 and now the conqueror be meet by a furious charge of english horse under Esmond 's general , General Lumley , behind whose squadron the fly foot find refuge , and form again , whilst Lumley drive back the french horse , charge up to the village of Blenheim and the palisade where Wilkes , and many hundred more gallant Englishmen , lie in slaughter heap . beyond this moment , and of this famous victory , Mr. Esmond know nothing ; for a shot bring down his horse and our young gentleman on it , who fall crush and stun under the animal , and come to his sens he know not how long after , only to lose they again from pain and loss of blood . a dim sense , as of people groan round about he , a wild incoherent thought or two for she who occupy so much of his heart now , and that here his career , and his hope , and misfortune be end , he remember in the course of these hour . when he wake up , it be with a pang of extreme pain , his breastplate be take off , his servant be hold his head up , the good and faithful lad of Hampshire   be blubber over his master , whom he find and have think dead , and a surgeon be probe a wound in the shoulder , which he must have get at the same moment when his horse be shoot and fall over he . the battle be over at this end of the field , by this time : the village be in possession of the English , its brave defender prisoner , or flee , or drown , many of they , in the neighboring water of Donau . but for honest Lockwood 's faithful search after his master , there have no doubt be an end of Esmond here , and of this his story . the marauder be out riffle the body as they lie on the field , and Jack have brain one of these gentry with the club - end of his musket , who have ease Esmond of his hat and periwig , his purse , and fine silver - mount pistol which the Dowager give he , and be fumble in his pocket for further treasure , when Jack Lockwood come up and put an end to the scoundrel 's triumph . 
    my mistress , before I go this campaign , send I John 
  Lockwood out of Walcote , who hath ever since remain with 
  I . H. E. 
 Hospitals for our wound be establish at Blenheim , and here for several week esmond lie in very great danger of his life ; the wound be not very great from which he suffer , and the ball extract by the surgeon on the spot where our young gentleman receive it ; but a fever set in next day , as he be lie in hospital , and that almost carry he away . Jack Lockwood say he talk in the wild manner during his delirium ; that he call himself the Marquis of Esmond , and seize one of the surgeon 's assistant who come to dress his wound , swear that he be Madam Beatrix , and that he would make she a duchess if she would but say yes . he be pass the day in these crazy fancy , and vana somnia , whilst the army be sing " Te Deum " for the victory , and those famous festivity be take place at which our Duke , now make a Prince of the Empire , be entertain by the King of the Romans and his nobility . his grace go home by Berlin and Hanover , and Esmond lose the festivity which take place at those city , and which his general share in company of the other general officer who travel with our great captain . when he could move , it be by the Duke of Wurtemberg 's city of Stuttgard that he make his way homeward , revisit Heidelberg again , whence he go to Manheim , and hence have a tedious but easy water journey down the river of Rhine , which he have think a delightful and beautiful voyage indeed , but that his heart be long for home , and something far more beautiful and delightful . 
 as bright and welcome as the eye almost of his mistress shine the light of harwich , as the packet come in from Holland . it be not many hour ere he , Esmond , be in London , of that you may be sure , and receive with open arm by the old Dowager of Chelsey , who vow , in her jargon of French and English , that he have the air noble , that his pallor embellish he , that he be an Amadis and deserve a Gloriana ; and oh ! flame and dart ! what be his joy at hear that his mistress be come into waiting , and be now with her Majesty at Kensington ! although Mr. Esmond have tell Jack Lockwood to get horse and they would ride for Winchester that night , when he hear this news he countermand the horse at once ; his business lie no long in hant ; all his hope and desire lie within a couple of mile of he in Kensington Park wall . Poor Harry have never look in the glass before so eagerly to see whether he have the bel air , and his paleness really do become he ; he never take such pain about the curl of his periwig , and the taste of his embroidery and point - lace , as now , before Mr. Amadis present himself to Madam Gloriana . be the fire of the french line half so murderous as the killing glance from her ladyship 's eye ? oh ! dart and rapture , how beautiful be they ! 
 and as , before the blaze sun of morning , the moon fade away in the sky almost invisible , Esmond think , with a blush perhaps , of another sweet pale face , sad and faint , and fade out of sight , with its sweet fond gaze of affection ; such a last look it seem to cast as Eurydice might have give , yearn after her lover , when Fate and Pluto summon she , and she pass away into the shade . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 an old story about a FOOL and a woman . 

 any taste for pleasure which Esmond have   and he like to desipere in loco , neither more nor less than most young man of his age   he could now gratify to the utmost extent , and in the good company which the town afford . when the army go into winter quarter abroad , those of the officer who have interest or money easily get leave of absence , and find it much pleasanter to spend their time in Pall Mall and Hyde Park , than to pass the winter away behind the fortification of the dreary old Flanders town , where the english troop be gather . yacht and packet pass daily between the dutch and flemish port and Harwich ; the road thence to London and the great inn be crowd with army gentleman ; the tavern and ordinary of the town swarm with red - coat ; and our great Duke 's levee at St. James 's be as throng as they have be at Ghent and Brussels , where we treat he , and he we , with the grandeur and ceremony of a sovereign . though Esmond have be appoint to a lieutenancy in the Fusileer regiment , of which that celebrated officer , Brigadier John Richmond Webb , be colonel , he have never join the regiment , nor be introduce to its excellent commander , though they have make the same campaign together , and be engage in the same battle . but be aide - de - camp to General Lumley , who command the division of horse , and the army marching to its point of destination on the Danube by different route , Esmond have not fall in , as yet , with his commander and future comrade of the fort ; and it be in London , in Golden Square , where Major - General Webb lodge , that Captain Esmond have the honor of first pay his respect to his friend , patron , and commander of after day . 
 those who remember this brilliant and accomplished gentleman may recollect his character , upon which he pride himself , I think , not a little , of be the handsome man in the army ; a poet who writ a dull copy of verse upon the battle of Oudenarde three year after , describe Webb , say : 
  " to noble danger Webb conduct the way , 
  his great example all his troop obey ; 
  before the front the general sternly rid , 
  with such an air as Mars to battle stride : 
  Propitious heaven must sure a hero save , 
  like Paris handsome , and like Hector brave . " 
 Mr. Webb think these verse quite as fine as Mr. Addison 's on the Blenheim Campaign , and , indeed , to be Hector a la mode de Paris , be part of this gallant gentleman 's ambition . it would have be difficult to find an officer in the whole army , or amongst the splendid courtier and cavalier of the Maison du Roy , that fight under Vendosme and Villeroy in the army oppose to ours , who be a more accomplished soldier and perfect gentleman , and either braver or well - looking . and if Mr. Webb believe of himself what the world say of he , and be deeply convinced of his own indisputable genius , beauty , and valor , who have a right to quarrel with he very much ? this self - content of his keep he in general good - humor , of which his friend and dependant get the benefit . 
 he come of a very ancient Wiltshire family , which he respect above all family in the world : he could prove a lineal descent from King Edward the First , and his first ancestor , Roaldus de Richmond , ride by William the Conqueror 's side on Hastings field . " we be gentleman , Esmond , " he use to say , " when the Churchills be horse - boy . " he be a very tall man , stand in his pump six foot three inch   in his great jack - boot , with his tall fair periwig , and hat and feather , he could not have be less than eight foot high . " I be tall than Churchill , " he would say , survey himself in the glass , " and I be a well make man ; and if the woman will not   like a man that have not   a wart on his nose , faith , I can not   help myself , and Churchill have the well of I there . " indeed , he be always measure himself with the Duke , and always ask his friend to measure they . and talk in this frank way , as he would do , over his cup , wag would laugh and encourage he ; friend would be sorry for he ; schemer and flatterer would egg he on , and tale - bearer carry the story to headquarters , and widen the difference which already exist there , between the great captain and one of the able and brave lieutenant he ever have . 
 his rancor against the Duke be so apparent , that one see it in the first half - hour 's conversation with General Webb ; and his lady , who adore her General , and think he a hundred time tall , handsomer , and braver than a prodigal nature have make he , hat the great Duke with such an intensity as it become faithful wife to feel against their husband ' enemy . not that my Lord Duke be so yet ; Mr. Webb have say a thousand thing against he , which his superior have pardon ; and his Grace , whose spy be everywhere , have hear a thousand thing more that Webb have never say . but it cost this great man no pain to pardon ; and he pass over an injury or a benefit alike easily . 
 should any child of mine take the pain to read these his ancestor 's memoir , I would not have he judge of the great Duke   by what a contemporary have write of he . no man hath be so immensely laud and decry as this great statesman and warrior ; as , indeed , no man ever deserve well the very great praise and the strong censure . if the present writer join with the latter faction , very likely a private pique of his own may be the cause of his ill - feeling .

  insert into the MS . book , and date 1744 , probably after 
  he have hear of the Duchess 's death . 
 on present himself at the Commander - in - Chief 's levee , his grace have not the least remembrance of General Lumley 's aide - de - camp , and though he know Esmond 's family perfectly well , have serve with both lord   my Lord Francis and the Viscount Esmond 's father   in Flanders , and in the Duke of York 's Guard , the Duke of Marlborough , who be friendly and serviceable to the   so - style   legitimate representative of the Viscount Castlewood , take no sort of notice of the poor lieutenant who bear their name . a word of kindness or acknowledgment , or a single glance of approbation , might have change Esmond 's opinion of the great man ; and instead of a satire , which his pen can not help write , who know but that the humble historian might have take the other side of panegyric ? we have but to change the point of view , and the great action look mean ; as we turn the perspective - glass , and a giant appear a pigmy . you may describe , but who can tell whether your sight be clear or not , or your mean of information accurate ? have the great man say but a word of kindness to the small one   as he would have step out of his gilt chariot to shake hand with Lazarus in rag and sore , if he think Lazarus could have be of any service to he , no doubt Esmond would have fight for he with pen and sword to the utmost of his might ; but my lord the lion do not want master mouse at this moment , and so Muscipulus go off and nibble in opposition . 
 so it be , however , that a young gentleman , who , in the eye of his family , and in his own , doubtless , be look upon as a consummate hero , find that the great hero of the day take no more notice of he than of the small drummer in his grace 's army . the Dowager at Chelsey be furious against this neglect of her family , and have a great battle with Lady Marlborough   as Lady Castlewood insist on call the Duchess . her grace be now mistress of the Robes to her Majesty , and one of the great personage in this kingdom , as her husband be in all Europe , and the battle between the two lady take place in the Queen 's drawing - room . 
 the Duchess , in reply to my aunt 's eager clamor , say haughtily , that she have do she good for the legitimate branch of the Esmonds , and could not be expect to provide for the bastard brat of the family . 
 " bastard ! " say the Viscountess , in a fury . " there be bastard among the Churchills , as your grace know , and the Duke of Berwick be provide for well enough . " 
 " Madam , " say the Duchess , " you know whose fault it be that there be no such duke in the Esmond family too , and how that little scheme of a certain lady miscarry . " 
 Esmond 's friend , Dick Steele , who be in wait on the Prince , hear the controversy between the lady at court . " and faith , " say Dick , " I think , Harry , thy kinswoman have the bad of it . " 
 he could not keep the story quiet ; ' twa all over the coffee - house ere night ; it be print in a News Letter before a month be over , and " the reply of her grace the Duchess of M - rlb - r - gh to a Popish Lady of the Court , once a favorite of the late K - J - m - s , " be print in half a dozen place , with a note state that " this duchess , when the head of this lady 's family come by his death lately in a fatal duel , never rest until she get a pension for the orphan heir , and widow , from her Majesty 's bounty . " the squabble do not advance poor Esmond 's promotion much , and indeed make he so ashamed of himself that he dare not show his face at the Commander - in - Chief 's levee again . 
 during those eighteen month which have pass since Esmond see his dear mistress , her good father , the old Dean , quit this life , firm in his principle to the very last , and enjoin his family always to remember that the Queen 's brother , King James the Third , be their rightful sovereign . he make a very edify end , as his daughter tell Esmond , and not a little to her surprise , after his death   for he have live always very poorly   my lady find that her father have leave no less a sum than 3,000L. behind he , which he bequeath to she . 
 with this little fortune Lady Castlewood be enable , when her daughter 's turn at Court come , to come to London , where she take a small genteel house at Kensington , in the neighborhood of the Court , bring her child with she , and here it be that Esmond find his friend . 
 as for the young lord , his university career have end rather abruptly . Honest Tusher , his governor , have find my young gentleman quite ungovernable . my lord worry his life away with trick ; and break out , as home - breed lad will , into a hundred youthful extravagance , so that Dr. Bentley , the new master of Trinity , think fit to write to the Viscountess Castlewood , my lord 's mother , and beg she to remove the young nobleman from a college where he decline to learn , and where he only do harm by his riotous example . indeed , I believe he nearly set fire to Nevil 's Court , that beautiful new quadrangle of our college , which Sir Christopher Wren have lately build . he knock down a proctor 's man that want to arrest he in a midnight prank ; he give a dinner - party on the Prince of Wales 's birthday , which be within a fortnight of his own , and the twenty young gentleman then present sally out after their wine , have toast King James 's health with open window , and sing cavalier song , and shout " God save the king ! " in the great court , so that the master come out of his lodge at midnight , and dissipate the riotous assembly . 
 this be my lord 's crowning freak , and the Rev. Thomas Tusher , domestic chaplain to the Right Honorable the Lord Viscount Castlewood , find his prayer and sermon of no earthly avail to his lordship , give up his duty of governor ; go and marry his brewer 's widow at Southampton , and take she and her money to his parsonage house at Castlewood . 
 my lady could not be angry with her son for drink King James 's health , be herself a loyal tory , as all the Castlewood family be , and acquiesce with a sigh , know , perhaps , that her refusal would be of no avail to the young lord 's desire for a military life . she would have like he to be in Mr. Esmond 's regiment , hop that Harry might act as a guardian and adviser to his wayward young kinsman ; but my young lord would hear of nothing but the Guards , and a commission be get for he in the Duke of Ormond 's regiment ; so Esmond find my lord , ensign and lieutenant , when he return from Germany after the Blenheim campaign . 
 the effect produce by both Lady Castlewood 's child when they appear in public be extraordinary , and the whole town speedily ring with their fame : such a beautiful couple , it be declare , never have be see ; the young maid of honor be toast at every table and tavern , and as for my young lord , his good look be even more admired than his sister 's . a hundred song be write about the pair , and as the fashion of that day be , my young lord be praise in these Anacreontics as warmly as Bathyllus . you may be sure that he accept very complacently the town 's opinion of he , and acquiesce with that frankness and charming good - humor he always show in the idea that he be the prettiest fellow in all London . 
 the old Dowager at Chelsey , though she could never be get to acknowledge that Mistress Beatrix be any beauty at all ,   in which opinion , as it may be imagine , a vast number of the lady agree with she , yet , on the very first sight of young Castlewood , she own she fall in love with he : and Henry Esmond , on his return to Chelsey , find himself quite supersede in her favor by her young kinsman . the feat of drink the King 's health at Cambridge would have win her heart , she say , if nothing else do . " how have the dear young fellow get such beauty ? " she ask . " not from his father certainly not from his mother . how have he come by such noble manner , and the perfect bel air ? that countrifie walcote widow could never have teach he . " Esmond have his own opinion about the countrified Walcote widow , who have a quiet grace and serene kindness , that have always seem to he the perfection of good breeding , though he do not try to argue this point with his aunt . but he could agree in most of the praise which the enraptured old dowager bestow on my Lord Viscount , than whom he never behold a more fascinating and charming gentleman . Castlewood have not wit so much as enjoyment . " the lad look good thing , " Mr. Steele use to say ; " and his laugh light up a conversation as much as ten repartee from Mr. Congreve . I would as soon sit over a bottle with he as with Mr. Addison ; and rather listen to his talk than hear Nicolini . be ever man so gracefully drunk as my Lord Castlewood ? I would give anything to carry my wine "   though , indeed , Dick bear his very kindly , and plenty of it , too , " like this incomparable young man . when he be sober he be delightful ; and when tipsy , perfectly irresistible . " and refer to his favorite , Shakspeare   who be quite out of fashion until Steele bring he back into the mode , Dick compare Lord Castlewood to Prince Hal , and be pleased to dub Esmond as ancient Pistol . 
 the Mistress of the Robes , the great lady in England after the Queen , or even before her Majesty , as the world say , though she never could be get to say a civil word to beatrix , whom she have promote to her place as maid of honor , take her brother into instant favor . when young Castlewood , in his new uniform , and look like a prince out of a fairy tale , go to pay his duty to her grace , she look at he for a minute in silence , the young man blush and in confusion before she , then fairly burst out a - crying , and kiss he before her daughter and company . " he be my boy 's friend , " she say , through her sob . " my Blandford might have be like he . " and everybody see , after this mark of the Duchess 's favor , that my young lord 's promotion be secure , and people crowd round the favorite 's favorite , who become vainer and gayer , and more good - humor than ever . 
 meanwhile Madam Beatrix be make her conquest on her own side , and amongst they be one poor gentleman , who have be shoot by her young eye two year before , and have never be quite cure of that wound ; he know , to be sure , how hopeless any passion might be , direct in that quarter , and have take that well , though ignoble , remedium amoris , a speedy retreat from before the charmer , and a long absence from she ; and not be dangerously smite in the first instance , Esmond pretty soon get the well of his complaint , and if he have it still , do not know he have it , and bear it easily . but when he return after Blenheim , the young lady of sixteen , who have appear the most beautiful object his eye have ever look on two year back , be now advanced to a perfect ripeness and perfection of beauty , such as instantly enthral the poor devil , who have already be a fugitive from her charm . then he have see she but for two day , and flee ; now he behold her day after day , and when she be at Court watch after she ; when she be at home , make one of the family party ; when she go abroad , ride after her mother 's chariot ; when she appear in public place , be in the box near she , or in the pit look at she ; when she go to church be sure to be there , though he might not listen to the sermon , and be ready to hand she to her chair if she deign to accept of his service , and select he from a score of young man who be always hang round about she . when she go away , accompany her Majesty to Hampton Court , a darkness fall over London . god , what night have Esmond pass , think of she , rhyme about she , talk about she ! his friend Dick Steele be at this time court the young lady , Mrs. Scurlock , whom he marry ; she have a lodging in Kensington Square , hard by my Lady Castlewood 's house there . Dick and Harry , be on the same errand , use to meet constantly at Kensington . they be always prowl about that place , or dismally walk thence , or eagerly run thither . they empty score of bottle at the " King 's arm , " each man prating of his love , and allow the other to talk on condition that he might have his own turn as a listener . hence arise an intimacy between they , though to all the rest of their friend they must have be insufferable . Esmond 's verse to " Gloriana at the Harpsichord , " to " Gloriana 's Nosegay , " to " Gloriana at Court , " appear this year in the Observator . have you never read they ? they be think pretty poem , and attribute by some to Mr. Prior . 
 this passion do not escape how should it ? the clear eye of Esmond 's mistress : he tell she all ; what will a man not do when frantic with love ? to what baseness will he not demean himself ? what pang will he not make other suffer , so that he may ease his selfish heart of a part of its own pain ? day after day he would seek his dear mistress , pour insane hope , supplication , rhapsody , rapture , into her ear . she listen , smile , console , with untire pity and sweetness . esmond be the eld of her child , so she be pleased to say ; and as for her kindness , who ever have or would look for aught else from one who be an angel of goodness and pity ? after what have be say , ' tis needless almost to add that poor Esmond 's suit be unsuccessful . what be a nameless , penniless lieutenant to do , when some of the great in the land be in the field ? esmond never so much as thought of ask permission to hope so far above his reach as he know this prize be and pass his foolish , useless life in mere abject sigh and impotent longing . what night of rage , what day of torment , of passionate unfulfilled desire , of sicken jealousy can he recall ! beatrix think no more of he than of the lackey that follow her chair . his complaint do not touch she in the least ; his rapture rather fatigue she ; she care for his verse no more than for Dan Chaucer 's , who be dead these ever so many hundred year ; she do not hate he ; she rather despise he , and just suffer he . 
 one day , after talk to Beatrix 's mother , his dear , fond , constant mistress for hour for all day long pour out his flame and his passion , his despair and rage , return again and again to the theme , pace the room , tear up the flower on the table , twisting and break into bit the wax out of the stand - dish , and perform a hundred mad freak of passionate folly ; see his mistress at last quite pale and tired out with sheer weariness of compassion , and watch over his fever for the hundredth time , Esmond seize up his hat , and take his leave . as he get into Kensington Square , a sense of remorse come over he for the wearisome pain he have be inflict upon the dearest and kind friend ever man have . he go back to the house , where the servant still stand at the open door , run up the stair , and find his mistress where he have leave she in the embrasure of the window , look over the field towards Chelsey . she laugh , wipe away at the same time the tear which be in her kind eye ; he fling himself down on his knee , and bury his head in her lap . she have in her hand the stalk of one of the flower , a pink , that he have tear to piece . " oh , pardon I , pardon I , my dearest and kind , " he say ; " I be in hell , and you be the angel that bring I a drop of water . " 
 " I be your mother , you be my son , and I love you always , " she say , hold her hand over he : and he go away comfort and humble in mind , as he think of that amazing and constant love and tenderness with which this sweet lady ever bless and pursue he . 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 the FAMOUS MR . JOSEPH ADDISON . 

 the gentleman usher have a table at Kensington , and the Guard a very splendid dinner daily at St. James 's , at either of which ordinarie Esmond be free to dine . Dick Steele like the guard - table well than his own at the gentleman usher ' , where there be less wine and more ceremony ; and Esmond have many a jolly afternoon in company of his friend , and a hundred time at least see Dick into his chair . if there be verity in wine , accord to the old adage , what an amiable - natured character Dick 's must have be ! in proportion as he take in wine he overflow with kindness . his talk be not witty so much as charming . he never say a word that could anger anybody , and only become the more benevolent the more tipsy he grow . many of the wag deride the poor fellow in his cup , and choose he as a butt for their satire : but there be a kindness about he , and a sweet playful fancy , that seem to Esmond far more charming than the point talk of the bright wit , with their elaborate repartee and affected severity . I think Steele shine rather than sparkle . those famous beaux - esprit of the coffee - house   Mr. William Congreve , for instance , when his gout and his grandeur permit he to come among we   would make many brilliant hit half a dozen in a night sometimes but , like sharp - shooter , when they have fire their shot , they be oblige to retire under cover till their piece be load again , and wait till they get another chance at their enemy ; whereas Dick never think that his bottle companion be a butt to aim at only a friend to shake by the hand . the poor fellow have half the town in his confidence ; everybody know everything about his love and his debt , his creditor or his mistress 's obduracy . when Esmond first come on to the town , honest Dick be all flame and rapture for a young lady , a West India fortune , whom he marry . in a couple of year the lady be dead , the fortune be all but spend , and the honest widower be as eager in pursuit of a new paragon of beauty , as if he have never court and marry and bury the last one . 
 quit the guard - table one Sunday afternoon , when by chance Dick have a sober fit upon he , he and his friend be make their way down Germain Street , and Dick all of a sudden leave his companion 's arm , and run after a gentleman who be pore over a folio volume at the book - shop near to St. James 's Church . he be a fair , tall man , in a snuff - color suit , with a plain sword , very sober , and almost shabby in appearance at least when compare to Captain Steele , who love to adorn his jolly round person with the fine of clothe , and shine in scarlet and gold lace . the Captain rush up , then , to the student of the book - stall , take he in his arm , hug he , and would have kiss he for Dick be always hug and buss his friend but the other step back with a flush on his pale face , seem to decline this public manifestation of Steele 's regard . 
 " my dear Joe , where hast thou hide thyself this age ? " cry the Captain , still hold both his friend 's hand ; " I have be languish for thee this fortnight . " 
 " a fortnight be not an age , Dick , " say the other , very good - humoredly .   he have light blue eye , extraordinary bright , and a face perfectly regular and handsome , like a tinted statue .   " and I have be hide myself where do you think ? " 
 " what ! not across the water , my dear Joe ? " say Steele , with a look of great alarm : " thou know I have always " 
 " no , " say his friend , interrupt he with a smile : " we be not come to such strait as that , Dick . I have be hide , sir , at a place where people never think of find you at my own lodging , whither I be go to smoke a pipe now and drink a glass of sack : will your honor come ? " 
 " Harry Esmond , come hither , " cry out Dick . " Thou hast hear I talk over and over again of my dear Joe , my guardian angel ? " 
 " indeed , " say Mr. Esmond , with a bow , " it be not from you only that I have learn to admire Mr. Addison . we love good poetry at Cambridge as well as at Oxford ; and I have some of your by heart , though I have put on a red coat . . . . ' o qui canoro blandius Orpheo vocale ducis carmen ; ' shall I go on , sir ? " say Mr. Esmond , who , indeed , have read and love the charming Latin poem of Mr. Addison , as every scholar of that time know and admire they . 
 " this be Captain Esmond who be at Blenheim , " say Steele . 
 " Lieutenant Esmond , " say the other , with a low bow , " at Mr. Addison 's service . 
 " I have hear of you , " say Mr. Addison , with a smile ; as , indeed , everybody about town have hear that unlucky story about Esmond 's dowager aunt and the Duchess . 
 " we be go to the ' George ' to take a bottle before the play , " say Steele : " wilt thou be one , Joe ? " 
 Mr. Addison say his own lodging be hard by , where he be still rich enough to give a good bottle of wine to his friend ; and invite the two gentleman to his apartment in the Haymarket , whither we accordingly go . 
 " I shall get credit with my landlady , " say he , with a smile , " when she see two such fine gentleman as you come up my stair . " and he politely make his visitor welcome to his apartment , which be indeed but a shabby one , though no grandee of the land could receive his guest with a more perfect and courtly grace than this gentleman . a frugal dinner , consist of a slice of meat and a penny loaf , be await the owner of the lodging . " my wine be well than my meat , " say Mr. Addison ; " my Lord Halifax send I the Burgundy . " and he set a bottle and glass before his friend , and eat his simple dinner in a very few minute , after which the three fall to , and begin to drink . " you see , " say Mr. Addison , point to his writing - table , whereon be a map of the action at Hochstedt , and several other gazette and pamphlet relate to the battle , " that I , too , be busy about your affair , Captain . I be engage as a poetical gazetteer , to say truth , and be write a poem on the campaign . " 
 so Esmond , at the request of his host , tell he what he know about the famous battle , draw the river on the table aliquo mero , and with the aid of some bit of tobacco - pipe show the advance of the left wing , where he have be engage . 
 a sheet or two of the verse lay already on the table beside our bottle and glass , and Dick have plentifully refresh himself from the latter , take up the page of manuscript , writ out with scarce a blot or correction , in the author 's slim , neat handwriting , and begin to read therefrom with great emphasis and volubility . at pause of the verse , the enthusiastic reader stop and fire off a great salvo of applause . 
 esmond smile at the enthusiasm of Addison 's friend . " you be like the german Burghers , " say he , " and the Princes on the Mozelle : when our army come to a halt , they always send a deputation to compliment the chief , and fire a salute with all their artillery from their wall . " 
 " and drunk the great chief health afterward , do not they ? " say Captain Steele , gayly fill up a bumper ; he never be tardy at that sort of acknowledgment of a friend 's merit . 
 " and the Duke , since you will have I act his grace 's part , " say Mr. Addison , with a smile , and something of a blush , " pledge his friend in return . Most Serene Elector of Covent Garden , I drink to your Highness 's health , " and he fill himself a glass . Joseph require scarce more pressing than Dick to that sort of amusement ; but the wine never seem at all to fluster Mr. Addison 's brain ; it only unloose his tongue : whereas Captain Steele 's head and speech be quite overcome by a single bottle . 
 no matter what the verse be , and , to say truth , Mr. Esmond find some of they more than indifferent , Dick 's enthusiasm for his chief never falter , and in every line from Addison 's pen , Steele find a master - stroke . by the time Dick have come to that part of the poem , wherein the bard describe as blandly as though he be record a dance at the opera , or a harmless bout of bucolic cudgelling at a village fair , that bloody and ruthless part of our campaign , with the remembrance whereof every soldier who bear a part in it must sicken with shame when we be order to ravage and lie waste the Elector 's country ; and with fire and murder , slaughter and crime , a great part of his dominion be overrun ; when Dick come to the line 
  " in vengeance rouse the soldier fill his hand 
  with sword and fire , and ravage the land , 
  in crackling flame a thousand harvest burn , 
  a thousand village to ash turn . 
  to the thick wood the woolly flock retreat , 
  and mix with bellow herd confusedly bleat . 
  their trembling lord the common shade partake , 
  and cry of infant find in every brake . 
  the listen soldier fix in sorrow stand , 
  Loth to obey his leader 's just command . 
  the leader grieve , by generous pity sway , 
  to see his just command so well obey ; " 
 by this time wine and friendship have bring poor Dick to a perfectly maudlin state , and he hiccup out the last line with a tenderness that set one of his auditor a - laughing . 
 " I admire the license of your poet , " say Esmond to Mr. Addison .   Dick , after reading of the verse , be fain to go off , insist on kiss his two dear friend before his departure , and reel away with his periwig over his eye .   " I admire your art : the murder of the campaign be do to military music , like a battle at the opera , and the virgin shriek in harmony , as our victorious grenadier march into their village . do you know what a scene it be ? "   by this time , perhaps , the wine have warm Mr. Esmond 's head too ,   " what a triumph you be celebrate ? what scene of shame and horror be enact , over which the commander 's genius preside , as calm as though he do not   belong to our sphere ? you talk of the ' listen soldier fix in sorrow , ' the ' leader 's grief sway by generous pity ; ' to my belief the leader care no more for bleat flock than he do for infant ' cry , and many of our ruffian butcher one or the other with equal alacrity . I be ashamed of my trade when I see those horror perpetrate , which come under every man 's eye . you hew out of your polished verse a stately image of smile victory ; I tell you ' tis an uncouth , distort , savage idol ; hideous , bloody , and barbarous . the rite perform before it be shocking to think of . you great poet should show it as it be ugly and horrible , not beautiful and serene . oh , sir , have you make the campaign , believe I , you never would have sing it so . " 
 during this little outbreak , Mr. Addison be listen , smoke out of his long pipe , and smile very placidly . " what would you have ? " say he . " in our polished day , and accord to the rule of art , ' tis impossible that the Muse should depict torture or begrime her hand with the horror of war . these be indicate rather than describe ; as in the greek tragedy , that , I dare say , you have read   and sure there can be no more elegant specimen of composition , Agamemnon be slain , or Medea 's child destroy , away from the scene ; the chorus occupy the stage and singing of the action to pathetic music . something of this I attempt , my dear sir , in my humble way : ' tis a panegyric I mean to write , and not a satire . be I to sing as you would have I , the town would tear the poet in piece , and burn his book by the hand of the common hangman . do you not use tobacco ? of all the weed grow on earth , sure the nicotian be the most soothing and salutary . we must paint our great Duke , " Mr. Addison go on , " not as a man , which no doubt he be , with weakness like the rest of we , but as a hero . ' tis in a triumph , not a battle , that your humble servant be rid his sleek Pegasus . we college poet trot , you know , on very easy nag ; it hath be , time out of mind , part of the poet 's profession to celebrate the action of hero in verse , and to sing the deed which you man of war perform . I must follow the rule of my art , and the composition of such a strain as this must be harmonious and majestic , not familiar , or too near the vulgar truth . Si parva licet : if Virgil could invoke the divine Augustus , a humbler poet from the bank of the Isis may celebrate a victory and a conqueror of our own nation , in whose triumph every Briton have a share , and whose glory and genius contribute to every citizen 's individual honor . when hath there be , since our Henrys ' and Edwards ' day , such a great feat of arm as that from which you yourself have bring away mark of distinction ? if ' tis in my power to sing that song worthily , I will do so , and be thankful to my Muse . if I fail as a poet , as a Briton at least I will show my loyalty , and fle up my cap and huzzah for the conqueror : 
  " ' rheni pacator et Istri 
  Omnis in hoc uno variis discordia cessit 
  Ordinibus ; laetatur eque , plauditque senator , 
  votaque patricio certant plebeia favori . ' " 
 " there be as brave man on that field , " say Mr. Esmond   who never could be make to love the Duke of Marlborough , nor to forget those story which he use to hear in his youth regard that great chiefs selfishness and treachery   " there be man at Blenheim as good as the leader , whom neither knight nor senator applaud , nor voice plebeian or patrician favor , and who lie there forget , under the clod . what poet be there to sing they ? " 
 " to sing the gallant soul of hero send to Hades ! " say Mr. Addison , with a smile . " would you celebrate they all ? if I may venture to question anything in such an admirable work , the catalogue of the ship in Homer hath always appear to I as somewhat wearisome ; what have the poem be , suppose the writer have chronicle the name of captain , lieutenant , rank and file ? one of the great of a great man 's quality be success ; ' tis the result of all the other ; ' tis a latent power in he which compel the favor of the god , and subjugate fortune . of all his gift I admire that one in the great Marlborough . to be brave ? every man be brave . but in be victorious , as he be , I fancy there be something divine . in presence of the occasion , the great soul of the leader shin out , and the god be confess . death itself respect he , and pass by he to lay other low . war and carnage flee before he to ravage other part of the field , as Hector from before the divine Achilles . you say he hath no pity ; no more have the god , who be above it , and superhuman . the fainting battle gather strength at his aspect ; and , wherever he rid , victory charge with he . " 
 a couple of day after , when Mr. Esmond revisit his poetic friend , he find this thought , strike out in the fervor of conversation , improve and shape into those famous line , which be in truth the noble in the poem of the " Campaign . " as the two gentleman sit engage in talk , Mr. Addison solace himself with his customary pipe , the little maid - servant that wait on his lodging come up , precede a gentleman in fine laced clothe , that have evidently be figure at Court or a great man 's levee . the courtier cough a little at the smoke of the pipe , and look round the room curiously , which be shabby enough , as be the owner in his worn , snuff - color suit and plain tie - wig . 
 " how go on the magnum opus , Mr. Addison ? " say the Court gentleman on look down at the paper that be on the table . 
 " we be but now over it , " say Addison   the great courtier in the land could not have a more splendid politeness , or great dignity of manner . " here be the plan , " say he , " on the table : hac ibat Simois , here run the little river Nebel : hic est Sigeia tellus , here be Tallard 's quarter , at the bowl of this pipe , at the attack of which Captain Esmond be present . I have the honor to introduce he to Mr. Boyle ; and Mr. Esmond be but now depict aliquo proelia mixta mero , when you come in . " in truth , the two gentleman have be so engaged when the visitor arrive , and Addison , in his smile way , speak of Mr. Webb , colonel of Esmond 's regiment   who command a brigade in the action , and greatly distinguish himself there , be lament that he could find never a suitable rhyme for Webb , otherwise the brigade should have have a place in the poet 's verse . " and for you , you be but a lieutenant , " say Addison , " and the Muse can not   occupy herself with any gentleman under the rank of a field officer . " 
 Mr. Boyle be all impatient to hear , say that my Lord Treasurer and my Lord Halifax be equally anxious ; and Addison , blushing , begin read of his verse , and , I suspect , know their weak part as well as the most critical hearer . when he come to the line describe the angel , that 
  " inspire repulse battalion to engage , 
  and teach the doubtful battle where to rage , " 
 he read with great animation , look at Esmond , as much as to say , " you know where that simile come from from our talk , and our bottle of Burgundy , the other day . " 
 the poet 's two hearer be catch with enthusiasm , and applaud the verse with all their might . the gentleman of the Court spring up in great delight . " not a word more , my dear sir , " say he . " trust I with the paper I will defend they with my life . let I read they over to my Lord Treasurer , whom I be appoint to see in half an hour . I venture to promise , the verse shall lose nothing by my reading , and then , sir , we shall see whether Lord Halifax have a right to complain that his friend 's pension be no long pay . " and without more ado , the courtier in lace seize the manuscript page , place they in his breast with his ruffled hand over his heart , execute a most gracious wave of the hat with the disengage hand , and smile and bow out of the room , leave an odor of pomander behind he . 
 " do not the chamber look quite dark ? " say Addison , survey it , " after the glorious appearance and disappearance of that gracious messenger ? why , he illuminate the whole room . your scarlet , Mr. Esmond , will bear any light ; but this threadbare old coat of mine , how very worn it look under the glare of that splendor ! I wonder whether they will do anything for I , " he continue . " when I come out of Oxford into the world , my patron promise I great thing ; and you see where their promise have land I , in a lodge up two pair of stair , with a sixpenny dinner from the cook 's shop . well , I suppose this promise will go after the other , and fortune will jilt I , as the jade have be do any time these seven year . ' I puff the prostitute away , ' " say he , smile , and blow a cloud out of his pipe . " there be no hardship in poverty , Esmond , that be not bearable ; no hardship even in honest dependence that an honest man may not put up with . I come out of the lap of Alma Mater , puff up with her praise of I , and think to make a figure in the world with the part and learning which have get I no small name in our college . the world be the ocean , and Isis and Charwell be but little drop , of which the sea take no account . my reputation end a mile beyond Maudlin Tower ; no one take note of I ; and I learn this at least , to bear up against evil fortune with a cheerful heart . Friend Dick hath make a figure in the world , and have pass I in the race long ago . what matter a little name or a little fortune ? there be no fortune that a philosopher can not endure . I have be not unknown as a scholar , and yet force to live by turn bear - leader , and teach a boy to spell . what then ? the life be not pleasant , but possible the bear be bearable . should this venture fail , I will go back to Oxford ; and some day , when you be a general , you shall find I a curate in a cassock and band , and I shall welcome your honor to my cottage in the country , and to a mug of penny ale . ' tis not poverty that be the hard to bear , or the least happy lot in life , " say Mr. Addison , shake the ash out of his pipe . " see , my pipe be smoke out . shall we have another bottle ? I have still a couple in the cupboard , and of the right sort . no more ? let we go abroad and take a turn on the Mall , or look in at the theatre and see Dick 's comedy . ' tis not a masterpiece of wit ; but Dick be a good fellow , though he doth not set the Thames on fire . "
