 within a month after this day , Mr. Addison 's ticket have come up a prodigious prize in the lottery of life . all the town be in an uproar of admiration of his poem , the " Campaign , " which Dick Steele be spout at every coffee - house in Whitehall and Covent Garden . the wit on the other side of Temple Bar salute he at once as the great poet the world have see for age ; the people huzza'ed for Marlborough and for Addison , and , more than this , the party in power provide for the meritorious poet , and Addison get the appointment of Commissioner of Excise , which the famous Mr. Locke vacate , and rise from this place to other dignity and honor ; his prosperity from henceforth to the end of his life be scarce ever interrupt . but I doubt whether he be not happy in his garret in the haymarket , than ever he be in his splendid palace at Kensington ; and I believe the fortune that come to he in the shape of the countess his wife be no well than a shrew and a vixen . 
 Gay as the town be , ' twas but a dreary place for Mr. Esmond , whether his charmer be in or out of it , and he be glad when his general give he notice that he be go back to his division of the army which lie in winter - quarter at Bois - le - Duc . his dear mistress bid he farewell with a cheerful face ; her blessing he know he have always , and wheresoever fate carry he . Mistress Beatrix be away in attendance on her Majesty at Hampton Court , and kiss her fair fingertip to he , by way of adieu , when he ride thither to take his leave . she receive her kinsman in a waiting - room , where there be half a dozen more lady of the Court , so that his high - fly speech , have he intend to make any   and very likely he do , be impossible ; and she announce to her friend that her cousin be go to the army , in as easy a manner as she would have say he be go to a chocolate - house . he ask with a rather rueful face , if she have any order for the army ? and she be pleased to say that she would like a mantle of Mechlin lace . she make he a saucy curtsy in reply to his own dismal bow . she deign to kiss her fingertip from the window , where she stand laugh with the other lady , and chance to see he as he make his way to the " Toy . " the Dowager at Chelsey be not sorry to part with he this time . " Mon cher , vous ete triste comme un sermon , " she do he the honor to say to he ; indeed , gentleman in his condition be by no mean amusing companion , and besides , the fickle old woman have now find a much more amiable favorite , and raffole for her darling lieutenant of the Guard . Frank remain behind for a while , and do not join the army till later , in the suite of his grace the Commander - in - chief . his dear mother , on the last day before Esmond go away , and when the three din together , make esmond promise to befriend her boy , and besought Frank to take the example of his kinsman as of a loyal gentleman and brave soldier , so she be pleased to say ; and at parting , betray not the least sign of faltering or weakness , though , God know , that fond heart be fearful enough when other be concern , though so resolute in bear its own pain . 
 Esmond 's general embark at Harwich . ' twas a grand sight to see Mr. Webb dress in scarlet on the deck , wave his hat as our yacht put off , and the gun salute from the shore . Harry do not see his viscount again , until three month after , at Bois - le - Duc , when his grace the Duke come to take the command , and Frank bring a budget of news from home : how he have sup with this actress , and get tired of that ; how he have get the well of Mr. St. John , both over the bottle , and with Mrs. Mountford , of the Haymarket Theatre   a veteran charmer of fifty , with whom the young scapegrace choose to fancy himself in love ; how his sister be always at her trick , and have jilt a young baron for an old earl . " I can not   make out beatrix , " he say ; ' she care for none of we she only think about herself ; she be never happy unless she be quarrel ; but as for my mother my mother , Harry , be an angel . " Harry try to impress on the young fellow the necessity of do everything in his power to please that angel ; not to drink too much ; not to go into debt ; not to run after the pretty flemish girl , and so forth , as become a senior speaking to a lad . " but Lord bless thee ! " the boy say ; " I may do what I like , and I know she will love I all the same ; " and so , indeed , he do what he like . everybody spoil he , and his grave kinsman as much as the rest . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 I get a company in the campaign of 1706 . 

 on Whit - Sunday , the famous 23rd of May , 1706 , my young lord first come under the fire of the enemy , whom we find post in order of battle , their line extend three mile or more , over the high ground behind the little Gheet river , and have on his left the little village of Anderkirk or autre - eglise , and on his right ramillie , which have give its name to one of the most brilliant and disastrous day of battle that history ever hath record . 
 our Duke here once more meet his old enemy of Blenheim , the Bavarian Elector and the Marechal Villeroy , over whom the Prince of Savoy have gain the famous victory of Chiari . what Englishman or Frenchman doth not know the issue of that day ? have choose his own ground , have a force superior to the English , and besides the excellent spanish and bavarian troop , the whole Maison - du - Roy with he , the most splendid body of horse in the world , in an hour   and in spite of the prodigious gallantry of the French Royal Household , who charge through the centre of our line and break it ,   this magnificent army of Villeroy be utterly rout by troop that have be march for twelve hour , and by the intrepid skill of a commander , who do , indeed , seem in the presence of the enemy to be the very Genius of Victory . 
 I think it be more from conviction than policy , though that policy be surely the most prudent in the world , that the great Duke always speak of his victory with an extraordinary modesty , and as if it be not so much his own admirable genius and courage which achieve these amazing success , but as if he be a special and fatal instrument in the hand of Providence , that will irresistibly the enemy 's overthrow . before his action he always have the church service read solemnly , and profess an undoubting belief that our Queen 's arm be bless and our victory sure . all the letter which he writ after his battle show awe rather than exultation ; and he attribute the glory of these achievement , about which I have hear mere petty officer and man brag with a pardonable vainglory , in nowise to his own bravery or skill , but to the superintend protection of heaven , which he ever seem to think be our especial ally . and our army get to believe so , and the enemy learn to think so too ; for we never enter into a battle without a perfect confidence that it be to end in a victory ; nor do the French , after the issue of Blenheim , and that astonishing triumph of ramillie , ever meet we without feel that the game be lose before it be begin to be play , and that our general 's fortune be irresistible . here , as at Blenheim , the Duke 's charger be shoot , and ' twa think for a moment he be dead . as he mount another , Binfield , his master of the horse , kneel to hold his grace 's stirrup , have his head shoot away by a cannon - ball . a french gentleman of the Royal Household , that be a prisoner with we , tell the writer that at the time of the charge of the Household , when their horse and our be mingle , an irish officer recognize the Prince - Duke , and call out " Marlborough , Marlborough ! " fire his pistol at he a bout - portant , and that a score more carbine and pistol be discharge at he . not one touch he : he ride through the French Curiassiers sword - in - hand , and entirely unhurt , and calm and smile , rally the German Horse , that be reel before the enemy , bring these and twenty squadron of Orkney 's back upon they , and drive the French across the river , again lead the charge himself , and defeat the only dangerous move the French make that day . 
 Major - General Webb command on the left of our line , and have his own regiment under the order of their beloved colonel . neither he nor they belie their character for gallantry on this occasion ; but it be about his dear young lord that Esmond be anxious , never have sight of he save once , in the whole course of the day , when he bring an order from the Commander - in - chief to Mr. Webb . when our horse , have charge round the right flank of the enemy by Overkirk , have throw he into entire confusion , a general advance be make , and our whole line of foot , cross the little river and the morass , ascend the high ground where the French be post , cheer as they go , the enemy retreat before they . ' twas a service of more glory than danger , the french battalion never wait to exchange push of pike or bayonet with our ; and the gunner fly from their piece , which our line leave behind we as they advance , and the French fall back . 
 at first it be a retreat orderly enough ; but presently the retreat become a rout , and a frightful slaughter of the French ensue on this panic : so that an army of sixty thousand man be utterly crush and destroy in the course of a couple of hour . it be as if a hurricane have seize a compact numerous fleet , fling it all to the wind , shatter , sunk , and annihilate it : afflavit Deus , et dissipati sunt . the french army of Flanders be go , their artillery , their standard , their treasure , provision , and ammunition be all leave behind they : the poor devil have even flee without their soup - kettle , which be as much the palladia of the french infantry as of the Grand Seignior 's Janissaries , and round which they rally even more than round their lily . 
 the pursuit , and a dreadful carnage which ensue   for the dreg of a battle , however brilliant , be ever a base residue of rapine , cruelty , and drunken plunder ,   be carry far beyond the field of ramillie . 
 Honest Lockwood , Esmond 's servant , no doubt want to be among the marauder himself and take his share of the booty ; for when , the action over , and the troop get to their ground for the night , the Captain bade Lockwood get a horse , he ask , with a very rueful countenance , whether his honor would have he come too ; but his honor only bid he go about his own business , and Jack hop away quite delighted as soon as he see his master mount . Esmond make his way , and not without danger and difficulty , to his grace 's headquarter , and find for himself very quickly where the aide - de - camp ' quarter be , in an out - building of a farm , where several of these gentleman be seat , drinking and singing , and at supper . if he have any anxiety about his boy , ' twa relieve at once . one of the gentleman be sing a song to a tune that Mr. Farquhar and Mr. Gay both have use in their admirable comedy , and very popular in the army of that day ; and after the song come a chorus , " over the hill and far away ; " and Esmond hear Frank 's fresh voice , soar , as it be , over the song of the rest of the young man a voice that have always a certain artless , indescribable pathos with it , and indeed which cause Mr. Esmond 's eye to fill with tear now , out of thankfulness to God the child be safe and still alive to laugh and sing . 
 when the song be over Esmond enter the room , where he know several of the gentleman present , and there sit my young lord , have take off his cuirass , his waistcoat open , his face flush , his long yellow hair hang over his shoulder , drink with the rest ; the young , gayest , handsome there . as soon as he see Esmond , he clap down his glass , and run towards his friend , put both his arm round he and embrace he . the other 's voice tremble with joy as he greet the lad ; he have think but now as he stand in the court - yard under the clear - shin moonlight : " Great God ! what a scene of murder be here within a mile of we ; what hundred and thousand have face danger to - day ; and here be these lad sing over their cup , and the same moon that be shin over yonder horrid field be look down on Walcote very likely , while my lady sit and think about her boy that be at the war . " as Esmond embrace his young pupil now , ' twa with the feeling of quite religious thankfulness and an almost paternal pleasure that he behold he . 
 round his neck be a star with a striped ribbon , that be make of small brilliant and might be worth a hundred crown . " look , " say he , " will not   that be a pretty present for mother ? " 
 " who give you the order ? " say Harry , salute the gentleman : " do you win it in battle ? " 
 " I win it , " cry the other , " with my sword and my spear . there be a mousquetaire that have it round his neck such a big mousquetaire , as big as General Webb . I call out to he to surrender , and that I would give he quarter : he call I a petit polisson and fire his pistol at I , and then send it at my head with a curse . I ride at he , sir , drive my sword right under his arm - hole , and break it in the rascal 's body . I find a purse in his holster with sixty - five Louis in it , and a bundle of love - letter , and a flask of Hungary - water . Vive la guerre ! there be the ten piece you lend I . I should like to have a fight every day ; " and he pull at his little moustache and bade a servant bring a supper to Captain Esmond . 
 Harry fall to with a very good appetite ; he have taste nothing since twenty hour ago , at early dawn . Master Grandson , who read this , do you look for the history of battle and siege ? go , find they in the proper book ; this be only the story of your grandfather and his family . far more pleasant to he than the victory , though for that too he may say meminisse juvat , it be to find that the day be over , and his dear young Castlewood be unhurt . 
 and would you , sirrah , wish to know how it be that a sedate Captain of Foot , a studious and rather solitary bachelor of eight or nine and twenty year of age , who do not care very much for the jollity which his comrade engage in , and be never know to lose his heart in any garrison - town should you wish to know why such a man have so prodigious a tenderness , and tend so fondly a boy of eighteen , wait , my good friend , until thou art in love with thy schoolfellow 's sister , and then see how mighty tender thou wilt be towards he . Esmond 's general and his grace the Prince - Duke be notoriously at variance , and the former 's friendship be in nowise likely to advance any man 's promotion of whose service Webb speak well ; but rather likely to injure he , so the army say , in the favor of the great man . however , Mr. Esmond have the good fortune to be mention very advantageously by Major - General Webb in his report after the action ; and the major of his regiment and two of the captain have be kill upon the day of Ramillies , Esmond , who be second of the lieutenant , get his company , and have the honor of serve as Captain Esmond in the next campaign . 
 my lord go home in the winter , but Esmond be afraid to follow he . his dear mistress write he letter more than once , thank he , as mother know how to thank , for his care and protection of her boy , extoll Esmond 's own merit with a great deal more praise than they deserve ; for he do his duty no well than any other officer ; and speak sometimes , though gently and cautiously , of beatrix . news come from home of at least half a dozen grand match that the beautiful maid of honor be about to make . she be engage to an earl , our gentleman of St. James 's say , and then jilt he for a duke , who , in his turn , have draw off . Earl or duke it might be who should win this Helen , Esmond know she would never bestow herself on a poor captain . her conduct , it be clear , be little satisfactory to her mother , who scarcely mention she , or else the kind lady think it be good to say nothing , and leave time to work out its cure . at any rate , Harry be well away from the fatal object which always work he so much mischief ; and so he never ask for leave to go home , but remain with his regiment that be garrison in Brussels , which city fall into our hand when the victory of ramillie drive the French out of Flanders . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

 I meet an old acquaintance in flanders , and FIND MY mother 's GRAVE and my own cradle there . 
 be one day in the Church of St. Gudule , at Brussels , admire the antique splendor of the architecture   and always entertain a great tenderness and reverence for the Mother Church , that hath be as wickedly persecute in England as ever she herself persecute in the day of her prosperity , Esmond see kneeling at a side altar an officer in a green uniform coat , very deeply engage in devotion . something familiar in the figure and posture of the kneeling man strike Captain Esmond , even before he see the officer 's face . as he rise up , put away into his pocket a little black breviary , such as priest use , Esmond behold a countenance so like that of his friend and tutor of early day , Father Holt , that he break out into an exclamation of astonishment and advance a step towards the gentleman , who be make his way out of church . the german officer too look surprised when he see Esmond , and his face from be pale grow suddenly red . by this mark of recognition , the Englishman know that he could not be mistake ; and though the other do not stop , but on the contrary rather hastily walk away towards the door , Esmond pursue he and face he once more , as the officer , help himself to holy water , turn mechanically towards the altar , to bow to it ere he quit the sacred edifice . 
 " my Father ! " say Esmond in English . 
 " Silence ! I do not understand . I do not speak English , " say the other in Latin . 
 esmond smile at this sign of confusion , and reply in the same language " I should know my Father in any garment , black or white , shaven or beard ; " for the austrian officer be habit quite in the military manner , and have as warlike a mustachio as any Pandour . 
 he laugh we be on the church step by this time , pass through the crowd of beggar that usually be there hold up little trinket for sale and whine for alm . " you speak Latin , " say he , " in the english way , Harry Esmond ; you have forsake the old true roman tongue you once know . " his tone be very frank , and friendly quite ; the kind voice of fifteen year back ; he give Esmond his hand as he speak . 
 " other have change their coat too , my Father , " say Esmond , glance at his friend 's military decoration . 
 " Hush ! I be Mr. or Captain von Holtz , in the Bavarian Elector 's service , and on a mission to his Highness the Prince of Savoy . you can keep a secret I know from old time . " 
 " Captain von Holtz , " say Esmond , " I be your very humble servant . " 
 " and you , too , have change your coat , " continue the other in his laugh way ; " I have hear of you at Cambridge and afterwards : we have friend everywhere ; and I be tell that Mr. Esmond at Cambridge be as good a fencer as he be a bad theologian . "   so , think Esmond , my old maitre d'arme be a Jesuit , as they say . 
 " perhaps you be right , " say the other , read his thought quite as he use to do in old day ; " you be all but kill at Hochstedt of a wound in the left side . you be before that at Vigo , aide - de - camp to the Duke of Ormonde . you get your company the other day after ramillie ; your general and the Prince - Duke be not friend ; he be of the Webbs of Lydiard Tregoze , in the county of York , a relation of my Lord St. John . your cousin , M. de Castlewood , serve his first campaign this year in the Guard ; yes , I do know a few thing , as you see . " 
 Captain Esmond laugh in his turn . " you have indeed a curious knowledge , " he say . a foible of Mr. Holt 's , who do know more about book and man than , perhaps , almost any person Esmond have ever meet , be omniscience ; thus in every point he here profess to know , he be nearly right , but not quite . Esmond 's wound be in the right side , not the left ; his first general be General Lumley ; Mr. Webb come out of Wiltshire , not out of Yorkshire ; and so forth . esmond do not think fit to correct his old master in these trifle blunder , but they serve to give he a knowledge of the other 's character , and he smile to think that this be his oracle of early day ; only now no long infallible or divine . 
 " yes , " continue Father Holt , or Captain von Holtz , " for a man who have not be in England these eight year , I know what go on in London very well . the old Dean be dead , my Lady Castlewood 's father . do you know that your recusant bishop want to consecrate he Bishop of Southampton , and that Collier be Bishop of Thetford by the same imposition ? the Princess Anne have the gout and eat too much ; when the King return , Collier will be an archbishop . " 
 " amen ! " say Esmond , laugh ; " and I hope to see your eminence no long in jack - boot , but red stocking , at Whitehall . " 
 " you be always with we I know that I hear of that when you be at Cambridge ; so be the late lord ; so be the young viscount . " 
 " and so be my father before I , " say Mr. Esmond , look calmly at the other , who do not , however , show the least sign of intelligence in his impenetrable gray eye how well Harry remember they and their look ! only crow ' foot be wrinkle round they mark of black old Time have settle there . 
 Esmond 's face choose to show no more sign of meaning than the Father 's . there may have be on the one side and the other just the faint glitter of recognition , as you see a bayonet shin out of an ambush ; but each party fall back , when everything be again dark . 
 " and you , mon capitaine , where have you be ? " say Esmond , turn away the conversation from this dangerous ground , where neither choose to engage . 
 " I may have be in Pekin , " say he , " or I may have be in Paraguay who know where ? I be now Captain von Holtz , in the service of his Electoral Highness , come to negotiate exchange of prisoner with his Highness of Savoy . " 
 ' twa well know that very many officer in our army be well - affect towards the young king at St. Germains , whose right to the throne be undeniable , and whose accession to it , at the death of his sister , by far the great part of the english people would have prefer , to the have a petty german prince for a sovereign , about whose cruelty , rapacity , boorish manner , and odious foreign way , a thousand story be current . it wound our english pride to think that a shabby high - dutch duke , whose revenue be not a tithe as great as those of many of the prince of our ancient english nobility , who could not speak a word of our language , and whom we choose to represent as a sort of german boor , feed on train - oil and sour - crout , with a bevy of mistress in a barn , should come to reign over the proud and most polished people in the world . be we , the conqueror of the Grand Monarch , to submit to that ignoble domination ? what do the Hanoverian 's Protestantism matter to we ? be it not notorious   we be tell and lead to believe so   that one of the daughter of this protestant hero be be breed up with no religion at all , as yet , and ready to be make Lutheran or Roman , accord as the husband might be whom her parent should find for she ? this talk , very idle and abusive much of it be , go on at a hundred mess - table in the army ; there be scarce an ensign that do not hear it , or join in it , and everybody know , or affect to know , that the Commander - in - Chief himself have relation with his nephew , the Duke of Berwick   ' twa by an Englishman , thank God , that we be beat at Almanza , and that his grace be most anxious to restore the royal race of his benefactor , and to repair his former treason . 
 this be certain , that for a considerable period no officer in the Duke 's army lose favor with the Commander - in - chief for entertain or proclaim his loyalty towards the exile family . when the Chevalier de St. George , as the King of England call himself , come with the duke of the french blood royal , to join the french army under Vendosme , hundred of our see he and cheer he , and we all say he be like his father in this , who , see the action of La Hogue fight between the french ship and our , be on the side of his native country during the battle . but this , at least the Chevalier know , and every one know , that , however well our troop and their general might be incline towards the prince personally , in the face of the enemy there be no question at all . wherever my Lord Duke find a french army , he would fight and beat it , as he do at Oudenarde , two year after Ramillies , where his grace achieve another of his transcendent victory ; and the noble young prince , who charge gallantly along with the magnificent Maison - du - Roy , send to compliment his conqueror after the action . 
 in this battle , where the young Electoral Prince of Hanover behave himself very gallantly , fight on our side , Esmond 's dear General Webb distinguish himself prodigiously , exhibit consummate skill and coolness as a general , and fight with the personal bravery of a common soldier . Esmond 's good - luck again attend he ; he escape without a hurt , although more than a third of his regiment be kill , have again the honor to be favorably mention in his commander 's report , and be advanced to the rank of major . but of this action there be little need to speak , as it hath be relate in every Gazette , and talk of in every hamlet in this country . to return from it to the writer 's private affair , which here , in his old age , and at a distance , he narrate for his child who come after he . before Oudenarde , after that chance rencontre with Captain von Holtz at Brussels , a space of more than a year elapse , during which the captain of Jesuits and the captain of Webb 's Fusileers be throw very much together . esmond have no difficulty in find out   indeed , the other make no secret of it to he , be assure from old time of his pupil 's fidelity , that the negotiator of prisoner be an agent from St. Germains , and that he carry intelligence between great personage in our camp and that of the French . " my business , " say he " and I tell you , both because I can trust you and your keen eye have already discover it be between the King of England and his subject here engage in fight the french king . as between you and they , all the Jesuits in the world will not prevent your quarrelling : fight it out , gentleman . St. George for England , I say and you know who say so , wherever he may be . " 
 I think Holt love to make a parade of mystery , as it be , and would appear and disappear at our quarter as suddenly as he use to return and vanish in the old day at Castlewood . he have pass between both army , and seem to know   but with that inaccuracy which belong to the good Father 's omniscience   equally well what pass in the french camp and in our . one day he would give Esmond news of a great feste that take place in the french quarter , of a supper of Monsieur de Rohan 's , where there be play and violin , and then dance and masque ; the King drive thither in Marshal Villars ' own guinguette . another day he have the news of his Majesty 's ague : the King have not have a fit these ten day , and might be say to be well . Captain Holtz make a visit to England during this time , so eager be he about negotiate prisoner ; and ' twa on return from this voyage that he begin to open himself more to Esmond , and to make he , as occasion serve , at their various meeting , several of those confidence which be here set down all together . 
 the reason of his increase confidence be this : upon go to London , the old director of Esmond 's aunt , the dowager , pay her ladyship a visit at Chelsey , and there learn from she that Captain Esmond be acquaint with the secret of his family , and be determine never to divulge it . the knowledge of this fact raise Esmond in his old tutor 's eye , so Holt be pleased to say , and he admire Harry very much for his abnegation . 
 " the family at Castlewood have do far more for I than my own ever do , " Esmond say . " I would give my life for they . why should I grudge the only benefit that ' tis in my power to confer on they ? " the good Father 's eye fill with tear at this speech , which to the other seem very simple : he embrace Esmond , and break out into many admire expression ; he say he be a noble coeur , that he be proud of he , and fond of he as his pupil and friend regret more than ever that he have lose he , and be force to leave he in those early time , when he might have have an influence over he , have bring he into that only true church to which the Father belong , and enlist he in the noble army in which a man ever engage mean his own society of Jesus , which number   say he   in its troop the great hero the world ever know ; warrior brave enough to dare or endure anything , to encounter any odd , to die any death soldier that have win triumph a thousand time more brilliant than those of the great general ; that have bring nation on their knee to their sacred banner , the Cross ; that have achieve glory and palm incomparably bright than those award to the most splendid earthly conqueror crown of immortal light , and seat in the high place of heaven . 
 Esmond be thankful for his old friend 's good opinion , however little he might share the Jesuit - father 's enthusiasm . " I have think of that question , too , " say he , " dear Father , " and he take the other 's hand " think it out for myself , as all man must , and contrive to do the right , and trust to heaven as devoutly in my way as you in your . another six month of you as a child , and I have desire no well . I use to weep upon my pillow at Castlewood as I think of you , and I might have be a brother of your order ; and who know , " Esmond add , with a smile , " a priest in full order , and with a pair of mustachio , and a bavarian uniform ? " 
 " my son , " say Father Holt , turn red , " in the cause of religion and loyalty all disguise be fair . " 
 " yes , " break in Esmond , " all disguise be fair , you say ; and all uniform , say I , black or red , a black cockade or a white one or a laced hat , or a sombrero , with a tonsure under it . I can not believe that St. Francis Xavier sail over the sea in a cloak , or raise the dead I try , and very nearly do once , but can not . suffer I to do the right , and to hope for the good in my own way . " 
 Esmond wish to cut short the good Father 's theology , and succeed ; and the other , sigh over his pupil 's invincible ignorance , do not withdraw his affection from he , but give he his utmost confidence as much , that be to say , as a priest can give : more than most do ; for he be naturally garrulous , and too eager to speak . 
 Holt 's friendship encourage Captain Esmond to ask , what he long wish to know , and none could tell he , some history of the poor mother whom he have often imagine in his dream , and whom he never know . he describe to Holt those circumstance which be already put down in the first part of this story the promise he have make to his dear lord , and that die friend 's confession ; and he beseech Mr. Holt to tell he what he know regard the poor woman from whom he have be take . 
 " she be of this very town , " Holt say , and take Esmond to see the street where her father live , and where , as he believe , she be bear . " in 1676 , when your father come hither in the retinue of the late king , then Duke of York , and banish hither in disgrace , Captain Thomas Esmond become acquainted with your mother , pursue she , and make a victim of she ; he hath tell I in many subsequent conversation , which I feel bound to keep private then , that she be a woman of great virtue and tenderness , and in all respect a most fond , faithful creature . he call himself Captain Thomas , have good reason to be ashamed of his conduct towards she , and hath speak to I many time with sincere remorse for that , as with fond love for her many amiable quality , he own to have treat she very ill : and that at this time his life be one of profligacy , gambling , and poverty . she become with child of you ; be curse by her own parent at that discovery ; though she never upbraid , except by her involuntary tear , and the misery depict on her countenance , the author of her wretchedness and ruin . 
 " Thomas Esmond Captain Thomas , as he be call become engage in a gaming - house brawl , of which the consequence be a duel , and a wound so severe that he never his surgeon say could outlive it . think his death certain , and touch with remorse , he send for a priest of the very Church of St. Gudule where I meet you ; and on the same day , after his make submission to our Church , be married to your mother a few week before you be bear . my Lord Viscount Castlewood , Marquis of Esmond , by King James 's patent , which I myself take to your father , your lordship be christen at St. Gudule by the same cure who marry your parent , and by the name of Henry Thomas , son of E. Thomas , officier Anglois , and Gertrude Maes . you see you belong to we from your birth , and why I do not christen you when you become my dear little pupil at Castlewood . 
 " your father 's wound take a favorable turn perhaps his conscience be ease by the right he have do and to the surprise of the doctor he recover . but as his health come back , his wicked nature , too , return . he be tired of the poor girl , whom he have ruin ; and receive some remittance from his uncle , my lord the old viscount , then in England , he pretend business , promise return , and never see your poor mother more . 
 " he own to I , in confession first , but afterwards in talk before your aunt , his wife , else I never could have disclose what I now tell you , that on come to London he writ a pretended confession to poor Gertrude Maes Gertrude Esmond of his have be marry in England previously , before unite himself with she ; say that his name be not Thomas ; that he be about to quit Europe for the virginian plantation , where , indeed , your family have a grant of land from King Charles the First ; send she a supply of money , the half of the last hundred guineas he have , entreat her pardon , and bade her farewell . 
 " poor Gertrude never think that the news in this letter might be untrue as the rest of your father 's conduct to she . but though a young man of her own degree , who know her history , and whom she like before she see the english gentleman who be the cause of all her misery , offer to marry she , and to adopt you as his own child , and give you his name , she refuse he . this refusal only anger her father , who have take her home ; she never hold up her head there , be the subject of constant unkindness after her fall ; and some devout lady of her acquaintance offer to pay a little pension for she , she go into a convent , and you be put out to nurse . 
 " a sister of the young fellow who would have adopt you as his son be the person who take charge of you . your mother and this person be cousin . she have just lose a child of her own , which you replace , your own mother be too sick and feeble to feed you ; and presently your nurse grow so fond of you , that she even grudge let you visit the convent where your mother be , and where the nun pet the little infant , as they pity and love its unhappy parent . her vocation become strong every day , and at the end of two year she be receive as a sister of the house . 
 " your nurse 's family be silk - weaver out of France , whither they return to Arras in french Flanders , shortly before your mother take her vow , carry you with they , then a child of three year old . ' twas a town , before the late vigorous measure of the french king , full of Protestants , and here your nurse 's father , old Pastoureau , he with whom you afterwards live at Ealing , adopt the reform doctrine , pervert all his house with he . they be expel thence by the edict of his most christian Majesty , and come to London , and set up their loom in Spittlefields . the old man bring a little money with he , and carry on his trade , but in a poor way . he be a widower ; by this time his daughter , a widow too , keep house for he , and his son and he labor together at their vocation . meanwhile your father have publicly own his conversion just before King Charles 's death   in whom our Church have much such another convert , be reconcile to my Lord Viscount Castlewood , and marry , as you know , to his daughter . 
 " it chance that the young Pastoureau , go with a piece of brocade to the mercer who employ he , on Ludgate Hill , meet his old rival come out of an ordinary there . Pastoureau know your father at once , seize he by the collar , and upbraid he as a villain , who have seduce his mistress , and afterwards desert she and her son . Mr. Thomas Esmond also recognize Pastoureau at once , besought he to calm his indignation , and not to bring a crowd round about they ; and bid he to enter into the tavern , out of which he have just step , when he would give he any explanation . Pastoureau enter , and hear the landlord order the drawer to show Captain Thomas to a room ; it be by his christian name that your father be familiarly call at his tavern haunt , which , to say the truth , be none of the most reputable . 
 " I must tell you that Captain Thomas , or my Lord Viscount afterwards , be never at a loss for a story , and could cajole a woman or a dun with a volubility , and an air of simplicity at the same time , of which many a creditor of his have be the dupe . his tale use to gather verisimilitude as he go on with they . he string together fact after fact with a wonderful rapidity and coherence . it require , save your presence , a very long habit of acquaintance with your father to know when his lordship be l , tell the truth or no . 
 " he tell I with rueful remorse when he be ill for the fear of death set he instantly repent , and with shriek of laughter when he be well , his lordship have a very great sense of humor how in a half an hour 's time , and before a bottle be drunk , he have completely succeed in bite poor Pastoureau . the seduction he own to : that he could not help : he be quite ready with tear at a moment 's warning , and shed they profusely to melt his credulous listener . he weep for your mother even more than Pastoureau do , who cry very heartily , poor fellow , as my lord inform I ; he swear upon his honor that he have twice send money to Brussels , and mention the name of the merchant with whom it be lie for poor Gertrude 's use . he do not even know whether she have a child or no , or whether she be alive or dead ; but get these fact easily out of honest Pastoureau 's answer to he . when he hear that she be in a convent , he say he hop to end his day in one himself , should he survive his wife , whom he hat , and have be force by a cruel father to marry ; and when he be tell that Gertrude 's son be alive , and actually in London , ' I start , ' say he ; ' for then , damme , my wife be expect to lie in , and I think should this old Put , my father - in - law , run rusty , here would be a good chance to frighten he . ' 
 " he express the deep gratitude to the Pastoureau family for the care of the infant : you be now near six year old ; and on Pastoureau bluntly tell he , when he propose to go that instant and see the darling child , that they never wish to see his ill - omen face again within their door ; that he might have the boy , though they should all be very sorry to lose he ; and that they would take his money , they be poor , if he give it ; or bring he up , by God 's help , as they have hitherto do , without : he acquiesce in this at once , with a sigh , say , ' well , ' twa well that the dear child should remain with friend who have be so admirably kind to he ; ' and in his talk to I afterwards , honestly praise and admire the weaver 's conduct and spirit ; own that the Frenchman be a right fellow , and he , the Lord have mercy upon he , a sad villain . 
 " your father , " Mr. Holt go on to say , " be good - natured with his money when he have it ; and have that day receive a supply from his uncle , give the weaver ten piece with perfect freedom , and promise he further remittance . he take down eagerly Pastoureau 's name and place of abode in his table - book , and when the other ask he for his own , give , with the utmost readiness , his name as Captain Thomas , New Lodge , Penzance , Cornwall ; he say he be in London for a few day only on business connect with his wife 's property ; describe she as a shrew , though a woman of kind disposition ; and depict his father as a cornish squire , in an infirm state of health , at whose death he hop for something handsome , when he promise richly to reward the admirable protector of his child , and to provide for the boy . ' and by Gad , sir , ' he say to I in his strange laugh way , ' I order a piece of brocade of the very same pattern as that which the fellow be carry , and present it to my wife for a morning wrapper , to receive company after she lie in of our little boy . ' 
 " your little pension be pay regularly enough ; and when your father become Viscount Castlewood on his uncle 's demise , I be employ to keep a watch over you , and ' twa at my instance that you be bring home . your foster - mother be dead ; her father make acquaintance with a woman whom he marry , who quarrel with his son . the faithful creature come back to Brussels to be near the woman he love , and die , too , a few month before she . will you see her cross in the convent cemetery ? the Superior be an old penitent of mine , and remember Soeur Marie Madeleine fondly still . " 
 Esmond come to this spot in one sunny evening of spring , and see , amidst a thousand black cross , cast their shadow across the grassy mound , that particular one which mark his mother 's resting - place . many more of those poor creature that lie there have adopt that same name , with which sorrow have rebaptize she , and which fondly seem to hint their individual story of love and grief . he fancy she in tear and darkness , kneel at the foot of her cross , under which her care be bury . surely he kneel down , and say his own prayer there , not in sorrow so much as in awe   for even his memory have no recollection of she , and in pity for the pang which the gentle soul in life have be make to suffer . to this cross she bring they ; for this heavenly bridegroom she exchange the husband who have woo she , the traitor who have leave she . a thousand such hillock lie round about , the gentle daisy spring out of the grass over they , and each bear its cross and requiescat . a nun , veil in black , be kneel hard by , at a sleep sister 's bedside   so fresh make , that the spring have scarce have time to spin a coverlid for it ; beyond the cemetery wall you have glimpse of life and the world , and the spire and gable of the city . a bird come down from a roof opposite , and light first on a cross , and then on the grass below it , whence it fly away presently with a leaf in its mouth : then come a sound as of chant , from the chapel of the sister hard by ; other have long since fill the place which poor Mary Magdeleine once have there , be kneel at the same stall , and hear the same hymn and prayer in which her stricken heart have find consolation . might she sleep in peace might she sleep in peace ; and we , too , when our struggle and pain be over ! but the earth be the Lord 's as the heaven be ; we be alike his creature here and yonder . I take a little flower off the hillock and kiss it , and go my way , like the bird that have just light on the cross by I , back into the world again . silent receptacle of death ; tranquil depth of calm , out of reach of tempest and trouble ! I feel as one who have be walk below the sea , and treading amidst the bone of shipwreck . 




 CHAPTER XIV . 

 the campaign of 1707 , 1708 . 

 during the whole of the year which succeed that in which the glorious battle of Ramillies have be fight , our army make no movement of importance , much to the disgust of very many of our officer remain inactive in Flanders , who say that his grace the Captain - General have have fight enough , and be all for money now , and the enjoyment of his five thousand a year and his splendid palace at Woodstock , which be now be build . and his grace have sufficient occupation fight his enemy at home this year , where it begin to be whisper that his favor be decrease , and his duchess lose her hold on the Queen , who be transfer her royal affection to the famous Mrs. Masham , and Mrs. Masham 's humble servant , Mr. Harley . against their intrigue , our Duke pass a great part of his time intriguing . Mr. Harley be get out of office , and his grace , in so far , have a victory . but her Majesty , convince against her will , be of that opinion still , of which the poet say people be when so convinced , and Mr. Harley before long have his revenge . 
 meanwhile the business of fighting do not go on any way to the satisfaction of Marlborough 's gallant lieutenant . during all 1707 , with the French before we , we have never so much as a battle ; our army in Spain be utterly rout at Almanza by the gallant Duke of Berwick ; and we of Webb 's , which regiment the young Duke have command before his father 's abdication , be a little proud to think that it be our colonel who have achieve this victory . " I think if I have have Galway 's place , and my Fusileers , " say our General , " we would not have lay down our arm , even to our old colonel , as Galway do ; " and Webb 's officer swear if we have have Webb , at least we would not have be take prisoner . our dear old general talk incautiously of himself and of other ; a braver or a more brilliant soldier never live than he ; but he blow his honest trumpet rather more loudly than become a commander of his station , and , mighty man of valor as he be , shake his great spear and bluster before the army too fiercely . 
 Mysterious Mr. Holtz go off on a secret expedition in the early part of 1708 , with great elation of spirit and a prophecy to Esmond that a wonderful something be about to take place . this secret come out on my friend 's return to the army , whither he bring a most rueful and dejected countenance , and own that the great something he have be engage upon have fail utterly . he have be indeed with that luckless expedition of the Chevalier de St. George , who be send by the french king with ship and an army from Dunkirk , and be to have invade and conquer Scotland . but that ill wind which ever oppose all the project upon which the Prince ever embark , prevent the Chevalier 's invasion of Scotland , as ' tis know , and blow poor Monsieur von Holtz back into our camp again , to scheme and foretell , and to pry about as usual . the Chevalier   the king of England , as some of we hold he   go from Dunkirk to the french army to make the campaign against we . the Duke of Burgundy have the command this year , have the Duke of Berry with he , and the famous Mareschal Vendosme and the Duke of Matignon to aid he in the campaign . Holtz , who know everything that be pass in Flanders and France   and the Indies for what I know , insist that there would be no more fighting in 1708 than there have be in the previous year , and that our commander have reason for keep he quiet . indeed , Esmond 's general , who be know as a grumbler , and to have a hearty mistrust of the great Duke , and hundred more officer besides , do not scruple to say that these private reason come to the Duke in the shape of crown - piece from the french king , by whom the Generalissimo be bribe to avoid a battle . there be plenty of man in our line , quidnunc , to whom Mr. Webb listen only too willingly , who could specify the exact sum the Duke get , how much fall to Cadogan 's share , and what be the precise fee give to Doctor Hare . 
 and the success with which the French begin the campaign of 1708 serve to give strength to these report of treason , which be in everybody 's mouth . our general allow the enemy to get between we and Ghent , and decline to attack he , though for eight and forty hour the army be in presence of each other . Ghent be take , and on the same day Monsieur de la Mothe summon bruge ; and these two great city fall into the hand of the French without fire a shot . a few day afterwards La Mothe seize upon the fort of Plashendall : and it begin to be suppose that all Spanish Flanders , as well as Brabant , would fall into the hand of the french troop ; when the Prince Eugene arrive from the Mozelle , and then there be no more shilly - shallying . 
 the Prince of Savoy always signalize his arrival at the army by a great feast   my Lord Duke 's entertainment be both seldom and shabby : and I remember our general return from this dinner with the two commander - in - chief ; his honest head a little excited by wine , which be deal out much more liberally by the Austrian than by the english commander : " now , " say my general , slap the table , with an oath , " he must fight ; and when he be force to it , d - it , no man in Europe can stand up against Jack Churchill . " within a week the battle of Oudenarde be fight , when , hate each other as they might , Esmond 's general and the Commander - in - Chief be force to admire each other , so splendid be the gallantry of each upon this day . 
 the brigade command by Major - General Webb give and receive about as hard knock as any that be deliver in that action , in which Mr. Esmond have the fortune to serve at the head of his own company in his regiment , under the command of their own Colonel as Major - General ; and it be his good luck to bring the regiment out of action as commander of it , the four senior officer above he be kill in the prodigious slaughter which happen on that day . I like to think that Jack Haythorn , who sneer at I for be a bastard and a parasite of Webb 's , as he choose to call I , and with whom I have have word , shake hand with I the day before the battle begin . three day before , poor Brace , our Lieutenant - Colonel , have hear of his eld brother 's death , and be heir to a baronetcy in Norfolk , and four thousand a year . Fate , that have leave he harmless through a dozen campaign , seize on he just as the world be worth live for , and he go into action know , as he say , that the luck be go to turn against he . the Major have just join we a creature of Lord Marlborough , put in much to the dislike of the other officer , and to be a spy upon we , as it be say . I know not whether the truth be so , nor who take the tattle of our mess to headquarters , but Webb 's regiment , as its Colonel , be know to be in the Commander - in - Chief 's black book : " and if he do not dare to break it up at home , " our gallant old chief use to say , " he be determined to destroy it before the enemy ; " so that poor Major Proudfoot be put into a post of danger . 
 Esmond 's dear young Viscount , serve as aide - de - camp to my Lord Duke , receive a wound , and win an honorable name for himself in the Gazette ; and Captain Esmond 's name be send in for promotion by his General , too , whose favorite he be . it make his heart beat to think that certain eye at home , the bright in the world , might read the page on which his humble service be record ; but his mind be make up steadily to keep out of their dangerous influence , and to let time and absence conquer that passion he have still lurk about he . away from Beatrix , it do not trouble he ; but he know as certain that if he return home , his fever would break out again , and avoid Walcote as a Lincolnshire man avoid return to his fen , where he be sure that the ague be lie in wait for he . 
 we of the English party in the army , who be inclined to sneer at everything that come out of Hanover , and to treat as little well than boor and savage the elector 's court and family , be yet force to confess that , on the day of Oudenarde , the young Electoral Prince , then make his first campaign , conduct himself with the spirit and courage of an approve soldier . on this occasion his Electoral Highness have well luck than the King of England , who be with his cousin in the enemy 's camp , and have to run with they at the ignominious end of the day . with the most consummate general in the world before they , and an admirable commander on their own side , they choose to neglect the council , and to rush into a combat with the former , which would have end in the utter annihilation of their army but for the great skill and bravery of the Duke of Vendosme , who remedie , as far as courage and genius might , the disaster occasion by the squabble and folly of his kinsman , the legitimate prince of the blood royal . 
 " if the Duke of Berwick have but be in the army , the fate of the day would have be very different , " be all that poor Mr. von Holtz could say ; " and you would have see that the hero of Almanza be fit to measure sword with the conqueror of Blenheim . " 
 the business relative to the exchange of prisoner be always go on , and be at least that ostensible one which keep Mr. Holtz perpetually on the move between the force of the French and the Allies . I can answer for it , that he be once very near hang as a spy by Major - General Wayne , when he be release and send on to head - quarter by a special order of the Commander - in - chief . he come and go , always favor , wherever he be , by some high though occult protection . he carry message between the Duke of Berwick and his uncle , our Duke . he seem to know as well what be take place in the Prince 's quarter as our own : he bring the compliment of the King of England to some of our officer , the gentleman of Webb 's among the rest , for their behavior on that great day ; and after Wynendael , when our General be chafe at the neglect of our Commander - in - Chief , he say he know how that action be regard by the chief of the french army , and that the stand make before Wynendael wood be the passage by which the Allies enter Lille . 
 " ah ! " say Holtz   and some folk be very willing to listen to he , " if the king come by his own , how change the conduct of affair would be ! his Majesty 's very exile have this advantage , that he be enable to read England impartially , and to judge honestly of all the eminent man . his sister be always in the hand of one greedy favorite or another , through whose eye she see , and to whose flattery or dependant she give away everything . do you suppose that his Majesty , know England so well as he do , would neglect such a man as General Webb ? he ought to be in the House of Peers as Lord Lydiard . the enemy and all Europe know his merit ; it be that very reputation which certain great people , who hate all equality and independence , can never pardon . " it be intend that these conversation should be carry to Mr. Webb . they be welcome to he , for great as his service be , no man could value they more than John Richmond Webb do himself , and the difference between he and Marlborough be notorious , his grace 's enemy in the army and at home begin to court Webb , and set he up against the all - grasp , domineering chief . and soon after the victory of Oudenarde , a glorious opportunity fall into General Webb 's way , which that gallant warrior do not neglect , and which give he the mean of immensely increase his reputation at home . 
 after Oudenarde , and against the counsel of Marlborough , it be say , the Prince of Savoy sit down before Lille , the capital of french Flanders , and commence that siege , the most celebrated of our time , and almost as famous as the siege of Troy itself , for the feat of valor perform in the assault and the defence . the enmity of the Prince of Savoy against the french king be a furious personal hate , quite unlike the calm hostility of our great english general , who be no more move by the game of war than that of billiard , and push forward his squadron , and drive his red battalion hither and thither as calmly as he would combine a stroke or make a cannon with the ball . the game over   and he play it so as to be pretty sure to win it , not the least animosity against the other party remain in the breast of this consummate tactician . whereas between the Prince of Savoy and the French it be guerre a mort . beat off in one quarter , as he have be at Toulon in the last year , he be back again on another frontier of France , assail it with his indefatigable fury . when the Prince come to the army , the smoulder fire of war be light up and burst out into a flame . our phlegmatic dutch ally be make to advance at a quick march our calm Duke force into action . the Prince be an army in himself against the French ; the energy of his hatred , prodigious , indefatigable infectious over hundred of thousand of man . the Emperor 's general be repay , and with a vengeance , the slight the french King have put upon the fiery little abbe of Savoy . brilliant and famous as a leader himself , and beyond all measure daring and intrepid , and enable to cope with almost the good of those famous man of war who command the army of the french king , Eugene have a weapon , the equal of which could not be find in France , since the cannon - shot of Sasbach lay low the noble Turenne , and could hurl Marlborough at the head of the french host , and crush they as with a rock , under which all the gather strength of their strong captain must go down . 
 the English Duke take little part in that vast siege of Lille , which the Imperial Generalissimo pursue with all his force and vigor , far than to cover the besiege line from the Duke of Burgundy 's army , between which and the Imperialists our Duke lay . once , when Prince Eugene be wound , our Duke take his Highness 's place in the trench ; but the siege be with the Imperialists , not with we . a division under Webb and Rantzau be detach into Artois and Picardy upon the most painful and odious service that Mr. Esmond ever see in the course of his military life . the wretched town of the defenceless province , whose young man have be draft away into the french army , which year after year the insatiable war devour , be leave at our mercy ; and our order be to show they none . we find place garrison by invalid , and child and woman ; poor as they be , and as the cost of this miserable war have make they , our commission be to rob these almost starve wretch to tear the food out of their granary , and strip they of their rag . ' twas an expedition of rapine and murder we be send on : our soldier do deed such as an honest man must blush to remember . we bring back money and provision in quantity to the Duke 's camp ; there have be no one to resist we , and yet who dare to tell with what murder and violence , with what brutal cruelty , outrage , insult , that ignoble booty have be ravish from the innocent and miserable victim of the war ? 
 meanwhile , gallantly as the operation before Lille have be conduct , the Allies have make but little progress , and ' twa say when we return to the Duke of Marlborough 's camp , that the siege would never be bring to a satisfactory end , and that the Prince of Savoy would be force to raise it . my Lord Marlborough give this as his opinion openly ; those who mistrust he , and Mr. Esmond own himself to be of the number , hint that the Duke have his reason why Lille should not be take , and that he be pay to that end by the french King . if this be so , and I believe it , General Webb have now a remarkable opportunity of gratify his hatred of the Commander - in - Chief , of balk that shameful avarice , which be one of the basest and most notorious quality of the famous Duke , and of show his own consummate skill as a commander . and when I consider all the circumstance precede the event which will now be relate , that my Lord Duke be actually offer certain million of crown provide that the siege of Lille should be raise : that the imperial army before it be without provision and ammunition , and must have decamp but for the supply that they receive ; that the march of the convoy destine to relieve the siege be accurately know to the French ; and that the force cover it be shamefully inadequate to that end , and by six time inferior to Count de la Mothe 's army , which be send to intercept the convoy ; when ' tis certain that the Duke of Berwick , De la Mothe 's chief , be in constant correspondence with his uncle , the english Generalissimo : I believe on my conscience that ' twa my Lord Marlborough 's intention to prevent those supply , of which the Prince of Savoy stand in absolute need , from ever reach his highness ; that he mean to sacrifice the little army which cover this convoy , and to betray it as he have betray Tollemache at Brest ; as he have betray every friend he have , to further his own scheme of avarice or ambition . but for the miraculous victory which Esmond 's general win over an army six or seven time great than his own , the siege of Lille must have be raise ; and it must be remember that our gallant little force be under the command of a general whom Marlborough hat , that he be furious with the conqueror , and try by the most open and shameless injustice afterwards to rob he of the credit of his victory . 




 CHAPTER XV . 

 GENERAL WEBB win the battle of WYNENDAEL . 

 by the besieger and besiege of Lille , some of the most brilliant feat of valor be perform that ever illustrate any war . on the french side   whose gallantry be prodigious , the skill and bravery of Marshal Boufflers actually eclipse those of his conqueror , the Prince of Savoy   may be mention that dare action of Messieurs de Luxembourg and Tournefort , who , with a body of horse and dragoon , carry powder into the town , of which the besiege be in extreme want , each soldier bring a bag with forty pound of powder behind he ; with which perilous provision they engage our own horse , face the fire of the foot bring out to meet they : and though half of the man be blow up in the dreadful errand they ride on , a part of they get into the town with the succor of which the garrison be so much in want . a french officer , Monsieur du Bois , perform an act equally daring , and perfectly successful . the Duke 's great army lie at Helchin , and cover the siege , and it be necessary for M. de Vendosme to get news of the condition of the place , Captain Dubois perform his famous exploit : not only pass through the line of the siege , but swim afterwards no less than seven moat and ditch : and come back the same way , swim with his letter in his mouth . 
 by these letter Monsieur de Boufflers say that he could undertake to hold the place till October ; and that if one of the convoy of the Allies could be intercept , they must raise the siege altogether . 
 such a convoy as hath be say be now prepare at Ostend , and about to march for the siege ; and on the 27th September we   and the French too   have news that it be on its way . it be compose of 700 wagon , contain ammunition of all sort , and be escort out of Ostend by 2,000 infantry and 300 horse . at the same time M. de la Mothe quit bruge , have with he five - and - thirty battalion , and upwards of sixty squadron and forty gun , in pursuit of the convoy . 
 Major - General Webb have meanwhile make up a force of twenty battalion and three squadron of dragoon at Turout , whence he move to cover the convoy and pursue La Mothe : with whose advanced guard our come up upon the great plain of Turout , and before the little wood and castle of Wynendael ; behind which the convoy be march . 
 as soon as they come in sight of the enemy , our advanced troop be halt , with the wood behind they , and the rest of our force bring up as quickly as possible , our little body of horse be bring forward to the opening of the plain , as our General say , to amuse the enemy . when M. de la Mothe come up , he find we post in two line in front of the wood ; and form his own army in battle face ours , in eight line , four of infantry in front , and dragoon and cavalry behind . 
 the French begin the action , as usual , with a cannonade which last three hour , when they make their attack , advance in eight line , four of foot and four of horse , upon the ally troop in the wood where we be post . their infantry behave ill ; they be order to charge with the bayonet , but , instead , begin to fire , and almost at the very first discharge from our man , break and flee . the cavalry behave well ; with these alone , who be three or four time as numerous as our whole force , Monsieur de la Mothe might have win victory : but only two of our battalion be shake in the least ; and these speedily rally : nor could the repeat attack of the french horse cause our troop to budge an inch from the position in the wood in which our General have place they . 
 after attack for two hour , the French retire at nightfall entirely foil . with all the loss we have inflict upon he , the enemy be still three time strong than we : and it could not be suppose that our General could pursue M. de la Mothe , or do much more than hold our ground about the wood , from which the Frenchman have in vain attempt to dislodge we . La Mothe retire behind his forty gun , his cavalry protect they well than it have be enable to annoy we ; and meanwhile the convoy , which be of more importance than all our little force , and the safe passage of which we would have drop to the last man to accomplish , march away in perfect safety during the action , and joyfully reach the besiege camp before Lille . 
 Major - General Cadogan , my Lord Duke 's Quarter - Master - General ,   and between whom and Mr. Webb there be no love lose , accompany the convoy , and join Mr. Webb with a couple of hundred horse just as the battle be over , and the enemy in full retreat . he offer , readily enough , to charge with his horse upon the French as they fall back ; but his force be too weak to inflict any damage upon they ; and Mr. Webb , command as Cadogan 's senior , think enough be do in hold our ground before an enemy that might still have overwhelm we have we engage he in the open territory , and in secure the safe passage of the convoy . accordingly , the horse bring up by Cadogan do not draw a sword ; and only prevent , by the good countenance they show , any disposition the French might have have to renew the attack on we . and no attack come , at nightfall General Cadogan draw off with his squadron , be bind for head - quarter , the two general at part grimly salute each other . 
 " he will be at Roncq time enough to lick my Lord Duke 's trencher at supper , " say Mr. Webb . 
 our own man lie out in the wood of Wynendael that night , and our General have his supper in the little castle there . 
 " if I be Cadogan , I would have a peerage for this day 's work , " General Webb say ; " and , Harry , thou shouldst have a regiment . thou hast be report in the last two action : thou wert near kill in the first . I shall mention thee in my despatch to his grace the Commander - in - chief , and recommend thee to poor Dick Harwood 's vacant majority . have you ever a hundred guineas to give Cardonnel ? slip they into his hand to - morrow , when you go to head - quarter with my report . " 
 in this report the Major - General be good enough to mention Captain Esmond 's name with particular favor ; and that gentleman carry the despatch to head - quarter the next day , and be not a little pleased to bring back a letter by his grace 's secretary , address to Lieutenant - General Webb . the dutch officer despatch by Count Nassau Woudenbourg , Vaelt - Mareschal Auverquerque 's son , bring back also a complimentary letter to his commander , who have second Mr. Webb in the action with great valor and skill . 
 Esmond , with a low bow and a smile face , present his despatch , and salute Mr. Webb as Lieutenant - General , as he give it in . the gentleman round about he he be rid with his suite on the road to Menin as Esmond come up with he give a cheer , and he thank they , and open the despatch with rather a flushed , eager face . 
 he slap it down on his boot in a rage after he have read it . " ' tis not even writ with his own hand . read it out , Esmond . " and Esmond read it out : 
 " SIR , Mr. Cadogan be just now come in , and have acquaint I with the success of the action you have yesterday in the afternoon against the body of troop command by M. de la Mothe , at Wynendael , which must be attribute chiefly to your good conduct and resolution . you may be sure I shall do you justice at home , and be glad on all occasion to own the service you have do in secure this convoy . your ,   c. , M. " 
 " two line by that d d Cardonnel , and no more , for the taking of Lille for beat five time our number for an action as brilliant as the good he ever fight , " say poor Mr. Webb . " Lieutenant - General ! that be not his doing . I be the old major - general . by , I believe he have be well pleased if I have be beat . " 
 the letter to the dutch officer be in French , and long and more complimentary than that to Mr. Webb . 
 " and this be the man , " he break out , " that be gorge with gold that be cover with title and honor that we win for he and that grudge even a line of praise to a comrade in arm ! have not   he enough ? do not   we fight that he may roll in rich ? well , well , wait for the Gazette , gentleman . the Queen and the country will do we justice if his grace deny it we . " there be tear of rage in the brave warrior 's eye as he speak ; and he dash they off his face on to his glove . he shake his fist in the air . " oh , by the Lord ! " say he , " I know what I have rather have than a peerage ! " 
 " and what be that , sir ? " some of they ask . 
 " I have rather have a quarter of an hour with John Churchill , on a fair green field , and only a pair of rapier between my shirt and his " 
 " Sir ! " interpose one . 
 " tell he so ! I know that be what you mean . I know every word go to he that be drop from every general officer 's mouth . I do not   say he be not brave . curse he ! he be brave enough ; but we will wait for the Gazette , gentleman . God save her Majesty ! she will do us justice . " 
 the Gazette do not come to we till a month afterwards ; when my General and his officer have the honor to dine with Prince Eugene in Lille ; his Highness be good enough to say that we have bring the provision , and ought to share in the banquet . ' twas a great banquet . his Grace of Marlborough be on his Highness 's right , and on his leave the Mareschal de Boufflers , who have so bravely defend the place . the chief officer of either army be present ; and you may be sure Esmond 's General be splendid this day : his tall noble person , and manly beauty of face , make he remarkable anywhere ; he wear , for the first time , the star of the Order of Generosity , that his prussian Majesty have send to he for his victory . his Highness the Prince of Savoy call a toast to the conqueror of Wynendael . my Lord Duke drink it with rather a sickly smile . the aide - de - camp be present : and Harry Esmond and his dear young lord be together , as they always strove to be when duty would permit : they be over against the table where the general be , and could see all that pass pretty well . Frank laugh at my Lord Duke 's glum face : the affair of Wynendael , and the Captain - General 's conduct to Webb , have be the talk of the whole army . when his Highness speak , and give " Le vainqueur de Wynendael ; son armee et sa victoire , " add , " qui nous font diner a Lille aujourd'huy " there be a great cheer through the hall ; for Mr. Webb 's bravery , generosity , and very weakness of character cause he to be belove in the army . 
 " like Hector , handsome , and like Paris , brave ! " whisper Frank Castlewood . " a Venus , an elderly Venus , could not   refuse he a pippin . stand up , Harry . see , we be drink the army of Wynendael . ramillie be nothing to it . huzzay ! huzzay ! " 
 at this very time , and just after our General have make his acknowledgment , some one bring in an English Gazette and be pass it from hand to hand down the table . officer be eager enough to read it ; mother and sister at home must have sicken over it . there scarce come out a Gazette for six year that do not tell of some heroic death or some brilliant achievement . 
 " here it be Action of Wynendael here you be , general , " say Frank , seize hold of the little dingy paper that soldier love to read so ; and , scramble over from our bench , he go to where the General sit , who know he , and have see many a time at his table his laugh , handsome face , which everybody love who see . the general in their great peruke make way for he . he hand the paper over General Dohna 's buff - coat to our General on the opposite side . 
 he come hobble back , and blush at his feat : " I think he would like it , Harry , " the young fellow whisper . " do not   I like to read my name after Ramillies , in the London Gazette ? Viscount Castlewood serve a volunteer I say , what be yonder ? " 
 Mr. Webb , read the Gazette , look very strange slap it down on the table then spring up in his place , and begin to " will your Highness please to " 
 his grace the Duke of Marlborough here jump up too " there be some mistake , my dear General Webb . " 
 " your grace have well rectify it , " say Mr. Webb , hold out the letter ; but he be five off his grace the Prince Duke , who , besides , be high than the General   be seat with the Prince of Savoy , the Electoral Prince of Hanover , and the envoy of Prussia and Denmark , under a baldaquin , and Webb could not reach he , tall as he be . 
 " stay , " say he , with a smile , as if catch at some idea , and then , with a perfect courtesy , draw his sword , he run the Gazette through with the point , and say , " permit I to hand it to your grace . " 
 the Duke look very black . " take it , " say he , to his Master of the Horse , who be wait behind he . 
 the Lieutenant - General make a very low bow , and retire and finish his glass . the Gazette in which Mr. Cardonnel , the Duke 's secretary , give an account of the victory of Wynendael , mention Mr. Webb 's name , but give the sole praise and conduct of the action to the Duke 's favorite , Mr. Cadogan . 
 there be no little talk and excitement occasion by this strange behavior of General Webb , who have almost draw a sword upon the Commander - in - Chief ; but the General , after the first outbreak of his anger , master it outwardly altogether ; and , by his subsequent behavior , have the satisfaction of even more anger the Commander - in - Chief , than he could have do by any public exhibition of resentment . 
 on return to his quarter , and consult with his chief adviser , Mr. Esmond , who be now entirely in the General 's confidence , and treat by he as a friend , and almost a son , Mr. Webb writ a letter to his grace the Commander - in - Chief , in which he say : 
 " your grace must be aware that the sudden perusal of the London Gazette , in which your grace 's secretary , Mr. Cardonnel , hath mention Major - General Cadogan 's name as the officer command in the late action of Wynendael , must have cause a feeling of anything but pleasure to the General who fight that action . 
 " your grace must be aware that Mr. Cadogan be not even present at the battle , though he arrive with squadron of horse at its close , and put himself under the command of his superior officer . and as the result of the battle of Wynendael , in which Lieutenant - General Webb have the good fortune to command , be the capture of Lille , the relief of Brussels , then invest by the enemy under the Elector of Bavaria , the restoration of the great city of Ghent and bruge , of which the enemy   by treason within the wall   have get possession in the previous year , Mr. Webb can not consent to forego the honor of such a success and service , for the benefit of Mr. Cadogan , or any other person . 
 " as soon as the military operation of the year be over , Lieutenant - General Webb will request permission to leave the army , and return to his place in Parliament , where he give notice to his grace the Commander - in Chief , that he shall lay his case before the House of Commons , the country , and her Majesty the Queen . 
 " by his eagerness to rectify that false statement of the Gazette , which have be write by his grace 's secretary , Mr. Cardonnel , Mr. Webb , not be able to reach his grace the Commander - in - chief on account of the gentleman seat between they , place the paper contain the false statement on his sword , so that it might more readily arrive in the hand of his grace the Duke of Marlborough , who surely would wish to do justice to every officer of his army . 
 " Mr. Webb know his duty too well to think of insubordination to his superior officer , or of use his sword in a campaign against any but the enemy of her Majesty . he solicit permission to return to England immediately the military duty will permit , and take with he to England Captain Esmond , of his regiment , who act as his aide - de - camp , and be present during the entire action , and note by his watch the time when Mr. Cadogan arrive at its close . " 
 the Commander - in - Chief could not but grant this permission , nor could he take notice of Webb 's letter , though it be couch in term the most insulting . half the army believe that the city of Ghent and bruge be give up by a treason , which some in our army very well understand ; that the Commander - in - Chief would not have relieve Lille if he could have help himself ; that he would not have fight that year have not the Prince of Savoy force he . when the battle once begin , then , for his own renown , my Lord Marlborough would fight as no man in the world ever fight well ; and no bribe on earth could keep he from beat the enemy . 
    our Grandfather 's hatred of the Duke of Marlborough 
  appear all through his account of these campaign . he 
  always persist that the Duke be the great traitor and 
  soldier history ever tell of : and declare that he take 
  bribe on all hand during the war . my Lord Marquis   for so 
  we may call he here , though he never go by any other name 
  than Colonel Esmond   be in the habit of tell many 
  story which he do not set down in his memoir , and which 
  he have from his friend the Jesuit , who be not always 
  correctly inform , and who persist that Marlborough be 
  look for a bribe of two million of crown before the 
  campaign of ramillie . 

  and our Grandmother use to tell we child , that on his 
  first presentation to my Lord duke , the Duke turn his back 
  upon my Grandfather ; and say to the Duchess , who tell my 
  lady dowager at Chelsey , who afterwards tell Colonel Esmond 
  " Tom Esmond 's bastard have be to my levee : he have the 
  hang - dog look of his rogue of a father " an expression which 
  my Grandfather never forgave . he be as constant in his 
  dislike as in his attachment ; and exceedingly partial to 
  Webb , whose side he take against the more celebrated 
  general . we have General Webb 's portrait now at Castlewood , 
  Va. 
 but the matter be take up by the subordinate ; and half the army might have be by the ear , if the quarrel have not be stop .
