General Cadogan send an intimation to General Webb to say that he be ready if Webb like , and would meet he . this be a kind of invitation our stout old general be always too ready to accept , and ' twa with great difficulty we get the General to reply that he have no quarrel with Mr. Cadogan , who have behave with perfect gallantry , but only with those at head - quarter , who have belie he . Mr. Cardonnel offer General Webb reparation ; Mr. Webb say he have a cane at the service of Mr. Cardonnel , and the only satisfaction he want from he be one he be not likely to get , namely , the truth . the officer in our staff of Webb 's , and those in the immediate suite of the General , be ready to come to blow ; and hence arise the only affair in which Mr. Esmond ever engage as principal , and that be from a revengeful wish to wipe off an old injury . 
 my Lord Mohun , who have a troop in Lord Macclesfield 's regiment of the Horse Guards , ride this campaign with the Duke . he have sink by this time to the very bad reputation ; he have have another fatal duel in Spain ; he have marry , and forsake his wife ; he be a gambler , a profligate , and debauchee . he join just before Oudenarde ; and , as Esmond fear , as soon as Frank Castlewood hear of his arrival , Frank be for seek he out , and kill he . the wound my lord get at Oudenarde prevent their meeting , but that be nearly heal , and Mr. Esmond tremble daily lest any chance should bring his boy and this know assassin together . they meet at the mess - table of Handyside 's regiment at Lille ; the officer command not know of the feud between the two nobleman . 
 Esmond have not see the hateful handsome face of Mohun for nine year , since they have meet on that fatal night in Leicester Field . it be degrade with crime and passion now ; it wear the anxious look of a man who have three death , and who know how many hidden shame , and lust , and crime on his conscience . he bow with a sickly low bow , and slunk away when our host present we round to one another . Frank Castlewood have not know he till then , so changed be he . he know the boy well enough . 
 ' twas curious to look at the two especially the young man , whose face flush up when he hear the hat name of the other ; and who say in his bad French and his brave boyish voice " he have long be anxious to meet my Lord Mohun . " the other only bow , and move away from he . I do he justice , he wish to have no quarrel with the lad . 
 Esmond put himself between they at table . " D - it , " say Frank , " why do you put yourself in the place of a man who be above you in degree ? my Lord Mohun should walk after I . I want to sit by my Lord Mohun . " 
 Esmond whisper to Lord Mohun , that Frank be hurt in the leg at Oudenarde ; and besought the other to be quiet . quiet enough he be for some time ; disregard the many taunt which young Castlewood fling at he , until after several health , when my Lord Mohun get to be rather in liquor . 
 " will you go away , my lord ? " Mr. Esmond say to he , implore he to quit the table . 
 " no , by G , " say my Lord Mohun . " I will not go away for any man ; " he be quite flushed with wine by this time . 
 the talk get round to the affair of yesterday . Webb have offer to challenge the Commander - in - chief : Webb have be ill - use : Webb be the brave , handsome , vainest man in the army . Lord Mohun do not know that Esmond be Webb 's aide - de - camp . he begin to tell some story against the General ; which , from t'other side of Esmond , young Castlewood contradict . 
 " I can not   bear any more of this , " say my Lord Mohun . 
 " nor can I , my lord , " say Mr. Esmond , start up . " the story my Lord Mohun have tell respect General Webb be false , gentleman false , I repeat , " and make a low bow to Lord Mohun , and without a single word more , Esmond get up and leave the dining - room . these affair be common enough among the military of those day . there be a garden behind the house , and all the party turn instantly into it ; and the two gentleman 's coat be off and their point engage within two minute after Esmond 's word have be speak . if Captain Esmond have put Mohun out of the world , as he might , a villain would have be punish and spar further villanie but who be one man to punish another ? I declare upon my honor that my only thought be to prevent Lord Mohun from mischief with Frank , and the end of this meeting be , that after half a dozen pass my lord go home with a hurt which prevent he from lift his right arm for three month . 
 " oh , Harry ! why do not   you kill the villain ? " young Castlewood ask . " I can not   walk without a crutch : but I could have meet he on horseback with sword and pistol . " but Harry Esmond say , " ' twa good to have no man 's life on one 's conscience , not even that villain 's . " and this affair , which do not occupy three minute , be over , the gentleman go back to their wine , and my Lord Mohun to his quarter , where he be lay up with a fever which have spar mischief have it prove fatal . and very soon after this affair Harry Esmond and his General leave the camp for London ; whither a certain reputation have precede the Captain , for my Lady Castlewood of Chelsey receive he as if he have be a conquering hero . she give a great dinner to Mr. Webb , where the General 's chair be crown with laurel ; and her ladyship call Esmond 's health in a toast , to which my kind General be graciously pleased to bear the strong testimony : and take down a mob of at least forty coach to cheer our General as he come out of the House of Commons , the day when he receive the thank of Parliament for his action . the mob huzza'd and applaud he , as well as the fine company : it be splendid to see he wave his hat , and bow , and lay his hand upon his Order of Generosity . he introduce Mr. Esmond to Mr. St. John and the right honorable Robert Harley , Esquire , as he come out of the House walk between they ; and be pleased to make many flattering observation regard Mr. Esmond 's behavior during the three last campaign . 
 Mr. St. John   who have the most winning presence of any man I ever see , except always my peerless young Frank Castlewood   say he have hear of Mr. Esmond before from Captain Steele , and how he have help Mr. Addison to write his famous poem of the " Campaign . " 
 " ' Twas as great an achievement as the victory of Blenheim itself , " Mr. Harley say , who be famous as a judge and patron of letter , and so , perhaps , it may be though for my part I think there be twenty beautiful line , but all the rest be commonplace , and Mr. Addison 's hymn worth a thousand such poem . 
 all the town be indignant at my Lord Duke 's unjust treatment of General Webb , and applaud the vote of thank which the House of Commons give to the General for his victory at Wynendael . ' tis certain that the capture of Lille be the consequence of that lucky achievement , and the humiliation of the old french King , who be say to suffer more at the loss of this great city , than from any of the former victory our troop have win over he . and , I think , no small part of Mr. Webb 's exultation at his victory arise from the idea that Marlborough have be disappoint of a great bribe the french King have promise he , should the siege be raise . the very sum of money offer to he be mention by the Duke 's enemy ; and honest Mr. Webb chuckle at the notion , not only of beat the French , but of beat Marlborough too , and intercept a convoy of three million of french crown , that be on their way to the Generalissimo 's insatiable pocket . when the General 's lady go to the Queen 's drawing - room , all the Tory woman crowd round she with congratulation , and make she a train great than the Duchess of Marlborough 's own . feast be give to the General by all the chief of the Tory party , who vaunt he as the Duke 's equal in military skill ; and perhaps use the worthy soldier as their instrument , whilst he think they be but acknowledge his merit as a commander . as the General 's aide - de - camp and favorite officer , Mr. Esmond come in for a share of his chief 's popularity , and be present to her Majesty , and advance to the rank of Lieutenant - Colonel , at the request of his grateful chief . 
 we may be sure there be one family in which any good fortune that happen to Esmond cause such a sincere pride and pleasure , that he , for his part , be thankful he could make they so happy . with these fond friend , Blenheim and Oudenarde seem to be mere trifling incident of the war ; and Wynendael be its crowning victory . Esmond 's mistress never tired to hear account of the battle ; and I think General Webb 's lady grow jealous of she , for the General be for ever at Kensington , and talk on that delightful theme . as for his aide - de - camp , though , no doubt , Esmond 's own natural vanity be pleased at the little share of reputation which his good fortune have win he , yet it be chiefly precious to he   he may say so , now that he hath long since outlive it ,   because it please his mistress , and , above all , because Beatrix value it . 
 as for the old Dowager of Chelsey , never be an old woman in all England more delighted nor more gracious than she . Esmond have his quarter in her ladyship 's house , where the domestic be instruct to consider he as their master . she bid he give entertainment , of which she defray the charge , and be charm when his guest be carry away tipsy in their coach . she must have his picture take ; and accordingly he be paint by Mr. Jervas , in his red coat , and smile upon a bomb - shell , which be burst at the corner of the piece . she vow that unless he make a great match , she should never die easy , and be for ever bring young lady to Chelsey , with pretty face and pretty fortune , at the disposal of the Colonel . he smile to think how time be alter with he , and of the early day in his father 's lifetime , when a trembling page he stand before she , with her ladyship 's basin and ewer , or crouch in her coach - step . the only fault she find with he be , that he be more sober than an Esmond ought to be ; and would neither be carry to bed by his valet , nor lose his heart to any beauty , whether of St. James 's or Covent Garden . 
 what be the meaning of fidelity in love , and whence the birth of it ? ' tis a state of mind that man fall into , and depend on the man rather than the woman . we love be in love , that be the truth on't . if we have not meet Joan , we should have meet Kate , and adore she . we know our mistress be no well than many other woman , nor no pretty , nor no wiser , nor no wittier . ' tis not for these reason we love a woman , or for any special quality or charm I know of ; we might as well demand that a lady should be the tall woman in the world , like the Shropshire giantess ,   as that she should be a paragon in any other character , before we begin to love she . Esmond 's mistress have a thousand fault beside her charm ; he know both perfectly well ! she be imperious , she be light - minded , she be flighty , she be false , she have no reverence in her character ; she be in everything , even in beauty , the contrast of her mother , who be the most devoted and the least selfish of woman . well , from the very first moment he see she on the stair at Walcote , Esmond know he love Beatrix . there might be well woman he want that one . he care for none other . be it because she be gloriously beautiful ? beautiful as she be , he have hear people say a score of time in their company that Beatrix 's mother look as young , and be the handsomer of the two . why do her voice thrill in his ear so ? she could not sing near so well as Nicolini or Mrs. Tofts ; nay , she sing out of tune , and yet he like to hear she well than St. Cecilia . she have not a fine complexion than Mrs. Steele ,   Dick 's wife , whom he have now get , and who rule poor Dick with a rod of pickle ,   and yet to see her dazzled esmond ; he would shut his eye , and the thought of she dazzle he all the same . she be brilliant and lively in talk , but not so incomparably witty as her mother , who , when she be cheerful , say the fine thing ; but yet to hear she , and to be with she , be Esmond 's great pleasure . day pass away between he and these lady , he scarce know how . he pour his heart out to they , so as he never could in any other company , where he hath generally pass for be moody , or supercilious and silent . this society    be more delightful than that of the great wit to he . may heaven pardon he the lie he tell the Dowager at Chelsey , in order to get a pretext for go away to Kensington : the business at the ordnance which he invent ; the interview with his General , the court and statesman 's levee which he DIDN'T frequent and describe ; who wear a new suit on Sunday at St. James 's or at the Queen 's birthday ; how many coach fill the street at Mr. Harley 's levee ; how many bottle he have have the honor to drink over - night with Mr. St. John at the " Cocoa - Tree , " or at the " Garter " with Mr. Walpole and Mr. Steele .

  folly for a score of WOMEN besides . R. 

     and , indeed , so be his to they , a thousand thousand 
  time more charming , for where be his equal ? R. 
 Mistress Beatrix Esmond have be a dozen time on the point of make great match , so the Court scandal say ; but for his part Esmond never would believe the story against she ; and come back , after three year ' absence from she , not so frantic as he have be perhaps , but still hunger after she and no other ; still hopeful , still kneel , with his heart in his hand for the young lady to take . we be now get to 1709 . she be near twenty - two year old , and three year at Court , and without a husband . 
 " ' tis not for want of be ask , " Lady Castlewood say , look into Esmond 's heart , as she could , with that perceptiveness affection give . " but she will make no mean match , Harry : she will not marry as I would have she ; the person whom I should like to call my son , and Henry Esmond know who that be , be well serve by my not press his claim . beatrix be so wilful , that what I would urge on she , she would be sure to resist . the man who would marry she , will not be happy with she , unless he be a great person , and can put she in a great position . beatrix love admiration more than love ; and long , beyond all thing , for command . why should a mother speak so of her child ? you be my son , too , Harry . you should know the truth about your sister . I think you might cure yourself of your passion , " my lady add , fondly . " other people can cure themselves of that folly , you know . but I see you be still as infatuate as ever . when we read your name in the Gazette , I plead for you , my poor boy . poor boy , indeed ! you be grow a grave old gentleman , now , and I be an old woman . she like your fame well enough , and she like your person . she say you have wit , and fire , and good - breeding , and be more natural than the fine gentleman of the Court . but this be not enough . she want a commander - in - chief , and not a colonel . be a duke to ask she , she would leave an earl whom she have promise . I tell you so before . I know not how my poor girl be so worldly . " 
 " well , " say Esmond , " a man can but give his good and his all . she have that from I . what little reputation I have win , I swear I care for it because I think beatrix would be pleased with it . what care I to be a colonel or a general ? think you ' twill matter a few score year hence , what our foolish honor to - day be ? I would have have a little fame , that she might wear it in her hat . if I have anything well , I would endow she with it . if she want my life , I would give it she . if she marry another , I will say God bless he . I make no boast , nor no complaint . I think my fidelity be folly , perhaps . but so it be . I can not help myself . I love she . you be a thousand time well : the fond , the fair , the dearest of woman . sure , my dear lady , I see all beatrix 's fault as well as you do . but she be my fate . ' tis endurable . I shall not die for not have she . I think I should be no happy if I win she . Que voulez - vous ? as my Lady of Chelsey would say . Je l'aime . " 
 " I wish she would have you , " say Harry 's fond mistress , give a hand to he . he kiss the fair hand   ' twa the prettiest dimple little hand in the world , and my Lady Castlewood , though now almost forty year old , do not look to be within ten year of her age . he kiss and keep she fair hand , as they talk together . 
 " why , " say he , ' should she hear I ? she know what I would say . far or near , she know I be her slave . I have sell myself for nothing , it may be . well , ' tis the price I choose to take . I be worth nothing , or I be worth all . " 
 " you be such a treasure , " Esmond 's mistress be pleased to say , " that the woman who have your love , should not   change it away against a kingdom , I think . I be a country - breed woman , and can not say but the ambition of the town seem mean to I . I never be awe - stricken by my Lady Duchess 's rank and finery , or afraid , " she add , with a sly laugh , " of anything but her temper . I hear of Court lady who pine because her Majesty look cold on they ; and great nobleman who would give a limb that they might wear a garter on the other . this worldliness , which I can not   comprehend , be bear with Beatrix , who , on the first day of she waiting , be a perfect courtier . we be like sister , and she the eld sister , somehow . she tell I I have a mean spirit . I laugh , and say she adore a coach - and - six . I can not reason she out of her ambition . ' tis natural to she , as to I to love quiet , and be indifferent about rank and rich . what be they , Harry ? and for how long do they last ? our home be not here . " she smile as she speak , and look like an angel that be only on earth on a visit . " our home be where the just be , and where our sin and sorrow enter not . my father use to rebuke I , and say that I be too hopeful about heaven . but I can not help my nature , and grow obstinate as I grow to be an old woman ; and as I love my child so , sure our Father love we with a thousand and a thousand time great love . it must be that we shall meet yonder , and be happy . yes , you and my child , and my dear lord . do you know , Harry , since his death , it have always seem to I as if his love come back to I , and that we be part no more . perhaps he be here now , Harry I think he be . forgive I be sure he be : even Mr. Atterbury absolve he , and he die forgiving . oh , what a noble heart he have ! how generous he be ! I be but fifteen and a child when he marry I . how good he be to stoop to I ! he be always good to the poor and humble . " she stop , then presently , with a peculiar expression , as if her eye be look into heaven , and see my lord there , she smile , and give a little laugh . " I laugh to see you , sir , " she say ; " when you come , it seem as if you never be away . " one may put her word down , and remember they , but how describe her sweet tone , sweet than music ! 
 my young lord do not come home at the end of the campaign , and write that he be keep at Bruxelles on military duty . indeed , I believe he be engage in lay siege to a certain lady , who be of the suite of Madame de Soissons , the Prince of Savoy 's mother , who be just dead , and who , like the flemish fortress , be take and retake a great number of time during the war , and occupy by French , English , and imperialist . of course , Mr. Esmond do not think fit to enlighten Lady Castlewood regard the young scapegrace 's doing : nor have he say a word about the affair with Lord Mohun , know how abhorrent that man 's name be to his mistress . Frank do not waste much time or money on pen and ink ; and , when Harry come home with his General , only writ two line to his mother , to say his wound in the leg be almost heal , that he would keep his coming of age next year that the duty aforesaid would keep he at Bruxelles , and that Cousin Harry would tell all the news . 
 but from Bruxelles , know how the Lady Castlewood always like to have a letter about the famous 29th of December , my lord writ she a long and full one , and in this he must have describe the affair with Mohun ; for when Mr. Esmond come to visit his mistress one day , early in the new year , to his great wonderment , she and her daughter both come up and salute he , and after they the Dowager of Chelsey , too , whose chairman have just bring her ladyship from her village to Kensington across the field . after this honor , I say , from the two lady of Castlewood , the Dowager come forward in great state , with her grand tall head - dress of King James 's reign , that , she never forsook , and say , " Cousin Henry , all our family have meet ; and we thank you , cousin , for your noble conduct towards the head of our house . " and point to her blushing cheek , she make Mr. Esmond aware that he be to enjoy the rapture of an embrace there . have salute one cheek , she turn to he the other . " Cousin Harry , " say both the other lady , in a little chorus , " we thank you for your noble conduct ; " and then Harry become aware that the story of the Lille affair have come to his kinswoman 's ear . it please he to hear they all salute he as one of their family . 
 the table of the dining - room be lay for a great entertainment ; and the lady be in gala dress my Lady of Chelsey in her high tour , my Lady Viscountess out of black , and look fair and happy a ravir ; and the Maid of Honor attire with that splendor which naturally distinguish she , and wear on her beautiful breast the french officer 's star which Frank have send home after ramillie . 
 " you see , ' tis a gala day with we , " say she , glance down to the star complacently , " and we have our order on . do not mamma look charming ? ' twa I dress she ! " indeed , Esmond 's dear mistress , blush as he look at she , with her beautiful fair hair , and an elegant dress accord to the mode , appear to have the shape and complexion of a girl of twenty . 
 on the table be a fine sword , with a red velvet scabbard , and a beautiful chase silver handle , with a blue ribbon for a sword - knot . " what be this ? " say the Captain , go up to look at this pretty piece . 
 Mrs. Beatrix advance towards it . " kneel down , " say she : " we dub you our knight with this " and she wave the sword over his head . " my Lady Dowager hath give the sword ; and I give the ribbon , and mamma hath sew on the fringe . " 
 " put the sword on he , beatrix , " say her mother . " you be our knight , Harry our true knight . take a mother 's thank and prayer for defend her son , my dear , dear friend . " she could say no more , and even the Dowager be affect , for a couple of rebellious tear make sad mark down those wrinkle old rose which Esmond have just be allow to salute . 
 " we have a letter from dear Frank , " his mother say , " three day since , whilst you be on your visit to your friend Captain Steele , at Hampton . he tell we all that you have do , and how nobly you have put yourself between he and that that wretch . " 
 " and I adopt you from this day , " say the Dowager , " and I wish I be rich , for your sake , son Esmond , " she add with a wave of her hand ; and as Mr. Esmond dutifully go down on his knee before her ladyship , she cast her eye up to the ceiling ,   the gilt chandelier , and the twelve wax - candle in it , for the party be numerous ,   and invoke a blessing from that quarter upon the newly adopt son . 
 " Dear Frank , " say the other viscountess , " how fond he be of his military profession ! he be study fortification very hard . I wish he be here . we shall keep his coming of age at Castlewood next year . " 
 " if the campaign permit we , " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " I be never afraid when he be with you , " cry the boy 's mother . " I be sure my Henry will always defend he . " 
 " but there will be a peace before next year ; we know it for certain , " cry the Maid of Honor . " Lord Marlborough will be dismiss , and that horrible duchess turn out of all her place . her Majesty will not   speak to she now . do you see she at Bushy , Harry ? she be furious , and she range about the park like a lioness , and tear people 's eye out . " 
 " and the Princess Anne will send for somebody , " say my Lady of Chelsey , take out her medal and kiss it . 
 " do you see the King at Oudenarde , Harry ? " his mistress ask . she be a staunch Jacobite , and would no more have think of deny her king than her God . 
 " I see the young Hanoverian only , " Harry say . " the Chevalier de St. George " 
 " the King , sir , the king ! " say the lady and Miss Beatrix ; and she clap her pretty hand , and cry , " Vive le Roy . " 
 by this time there come a thunder knock , that drive in the door of the house almost . it be three o'clock , and the company be arrive ; and presently the servant announce Captain Steele and his lady . 
 Captain and Mrs. Steele , who be the first to arrive , have drive to Kensington from their country - house , the Hovel at Hampton Wick . " not from our mansion in Bloomsbury Square , " as Mrs. Steele take care to inform the lady . indeed Harry have ride away from Hampton that very morning , leave the couple by the ear ; for from the chamber where he lay , in a bed that be none of the clean , and keep awake by the company which he have in his own bed , and the quarrel which be go on in the next room , he could hear both night and morning the curtain lecture which Mrs. Steele be in the habit of administer to poor Dick . 
 at night it do not matter so much for the culprit ; Dick be fuddle , and when in that way no scolding could interrupt his benevolence . Mr. Esmond could hear he coax and speak in that maudlin manner , which punch and claret produce , to his beloved prue , and beseech she to remember that there be a distiwisht officer ithe rex roob , who would overhear she . she go on , nevertheless , call he a drunken wretch , and be only interrupt in her harangue by the Captain 's snoring . 
 in the morning , the unhappy victim awake to a headache , and consciousness , and the dialogue of the night be resume . " why do you bring captain home to dinner when there be not a guinea in the house ? how be I to give dinner when you leave I without a shilling ? how be I to go traipse to Kensington in my yellow satin sack before all the fine company ? I have   nothing fit to put on ; I never have : " and so the dispute go on Mr. Esmond interrupt the talk when it seem to be grow too intimate by blow his nose as loudly as ever he could , at the sound of which trumpet there come a lull . but Dick be charming , though his wife be odious , and ' twa to give Mr. Steele pleasure , that the lady of Castlewood , who be lady of no small fashion , invite Mrs. Steele . 
 besides the Captain and his lady , there be a great and notable assemblage of company : my Lady of Chelsey have send her lackey and livery to aid the modest attendance at Kensington . there be Lieutenant - General Webb , Harry 's kind patron , of whom the Dowager take possession , and who resplend in velvet and gold lace ; there be Harry 's new acquaintance , the Right Honorable Henry St. John , Esquire , the General 's kinsman , who be charm with the Lady Castlewood , even more than with her daughter ; there be one of the great nobleman in the kingdom , the Scots Duke of Hamilton , just create Duke of Brandon in England ; and two other noble lord of the Tory party , my Lord Ashburnham , and another I have forget ; and for lady , her grace the Duchess of Ormonde and her daughter , the Lady Mary and the Lady Betty , the former one of Mistress beatrix 's colleague in wait on the Queen . 
 " what a party of Tories ! " whisper Captain Steele to Esmond , as we be assemble in the parlor before dinner . indeed , all the company present , save Steele , be of that faction . 
 Mr. St. John make his special compliment to Mrs. Steele , and so charm she that she declare she would have Steele a Tory too . 
 " or will you have I a Whig ? " say Mr. St. John . " I think , madam , you could convert a man to anything . " 
 " if Mr. St. John ever come to Bloomsbury Square I will teach he what I know , " say Mrs. Steele , drop her handsome eye . " do you know Bloomsbury Square ? " 
 " do I know the mall ? do I know the Opera ? do I know the reign toast ? why , Bloomsbury be the very height of the mode , " say Mr. St. John . " ' tis rus in urbe . you have garden all the way to Hampstead , and palace round about you Southampton House and Montague House . " 
 " where you wretche go and fight duel , " cry Mrs. Steele . 
 " of which the lady be the cause ! " say her entertainer . " Madam , be Dick a good swordsman ? how charming the ' Tatler ' be ! we all recognize your portrait in the 49th number , and I have be die to know you ever since I read it . ' Aspasia must be allow to be the first of the beauteous order of love . ' Doth not the passage run so ? ' in this accomplished lady love be the constant effect , though it be never the design ; yet though her mien carry much more invitation than command , to behold she be an immediate check to loose behavior , and to love she be a liberal education . ' " 
 " oh , indeed ! " say Mrs. Steele , who do not seem to understand a word of what the gentleman be say . 
 " who could fail to be accomplish under such a mistress ? " say Mr. St. John , still gallant and bow . 
 " Mistress ! upon my word , sir ! " cry the lady . " if you mean I , sir , I would have you know that I be the Captain 's wife . " 
 " sure we all know it , " answer Mr. St. John , keep his countenance very gravely ; and Steele break in say , " ' twas not about Mrs. Steele I writ that paper though I be sure she be worthy of any compliment I can pay she but of the Lady Elizabeth Hastings . " 
 " I hear Mr. Addison be equally famous as a wit and a poet , " say Mr. St. John . " be it true that his hand be to be find in your ' Tatler , ' Mr. Steele ? " 
 " whether ' tis the sublime or the humorous , no man can come near he , " cry Steele . 
 " a fig , Dick , for your Mr. Addison ! " cry out his lady : " a gentleman who give himself such air and hold his head so high now . I hope your ladyship think as I do : I can not   bear those very fair man with white eyelash a black man for I . "   all the black man at table applaud , and make Mrs. Steele a bow for this compliment .   " as for this Mr. Addison , " she go on , " he come to dine with the Captain sometimes , never say a word to I , and then they walk up stair both tipsy , to a dish of tea . I remember your Mr. Addison when he have but one coat to his back , and that with a patch at the elbow . " 
 " indeed a patch at the elbow ! you interest I , " say Mr. St. John . " ' tis charming to hear of one man of letter from the charming wife of another . " 
 " La , I could tell you ever so much about them , " continue the voluble lady . " what do you think the Captain have get now ? a little hunchback fellow a little hop - o'-my - thumb creature that he call a poet a little popish brat ! " 
 " hush , there be two in the room , " whisper her companion . 
 " well , I call he Popish because his name be Pope , " say the lady . " ' tis only my joke way . and this little dwarf of a fellow have write a pastoral poem all about shepherd and shepherdess , you know . " 
 " a shepherd should have a little crook , " say my mistress , laugh from her end of the table : on which Mrs. Steele say , " she do not know , but the Captain bring home this queer little creature when she be in bed with her first boy , and it be a mercy he have come no soon ; and Dick rave about his genus , and be always rave about some nonsense or other . " 
 " which of the ' Tatlers ' do you prefer , Mrs. Steele ? " ask Mr. St. John . 
 " I never read but one , and think it all a pack of rubbish , sir , " say the lady . " such stuff about Bickerstaffe , and Distaff , and Quarterstaff , as it all be ! there be the Captain go on still with the Burgundy I know he will be tipsy before he stop Captain Steele ! " 
 " I drink to your eye , my dear , " say the Captain , who seem to think his wife charming , and to receive as genuine all the satiric compliment which Mr. St. John pay she . 
 all this while the Maid of Honor have be try to get Mr. Esmond to talk , and no doubt vote he a dull fellow . for , by some mistake , just as he be go to pop into the vacant place , he be place far away from beatrix 's chair , who sit between his grace and my Lord Ashburnham , and shrug her lovely white shoulder , and cast a look as if to say , " pity I , " to her cousin . my Lord Duke and his young neighbor be presently in a very animated and close conversation . Mrs. Beatrix could no more help use her eye than the sun can help shin , and set those it shin on a - burning . by the time the first course be do the dinner seem long to Esmond ; by the time the soup come he fancy they must have be hour at table : and as for the sweet and jelly he think they never would be do . 
 at length the lady rise , beatrix throw a parthian glance at her duke as she retreat ; a fresh bottle and glass be fetch , and toast be call . Mr. St. John ask his grace the Duke of Hamilton and the company to drink to the health of his grace the Duke of Brandon . another lord give General Webb 's health , " and may he get the command the brave officer in the world deserve . " Mr. Webb thank the company , compliment his aide - de - camp , and fight his famous battle over again . 
 " il est fatiguant , " whisper Mr. St. John , " avec sa trompette de Wynendael . " 
 Captain Steele , who be not of our side , loyally give the health of the Duke of Marlborough , the great general of the age . 
 " I drink to the great general with all my heart , " say Mr. Webb ; " there can be no gainsaying that character of he . my glass go to the General , and not to the Duke , Mr. Steele . " and the stout old gentleman empty his bumper ; to which Dick reply by fill and empty a pair of brimmer , one for the General and one for the Duke . 
 and now his Grace of Hamilton , rise up with flash eye   we have all be drink pretty freely , propose a toast to the lovely , to the incomparable Mrs. Beatrix Esmond ; we all drink it with cheer , and my Lord Ashburnham especially , with a shout of enthusiasm . 
 " what a pity there be a Duchess of Hamilton , " whisper St. John , who drink more wine and yet be more steady than most of the other , and we enter the drawing - room where the lady be at their tea . as for poor Dick , we be oblige to leave he alone at the dining - table , where he be hiccup out the line from the " campaign , " in which the great poet have celebrate the great general in the world ; and Harry Esmond find he , half an hour afterwards , in a more advanced stage of liquor , and weep about the treachery of Tom Boxer . 
 the drawing - room be all dark to poor Harry , in spite of the grand illumination . beatrix scarce speak to he . when my Lord Duke go away , she practise upon the next in rank , and ply my young Lord Ashburnham with all the fire of her eye and the fascination of her wit . Most of the party be set to card , and Mr. St. John , after yawn in the face of Mrs. Steele , whom he do not care to pursue any more ; and talk in his most brilliant animate way to Lady Castlewood , whom he pronounce to be beautiful , of a far high order of beauty than her daughter , presently take his leave , and go his way . the rest of the company speedily follow , my Lord Ashburnham the last , throw fiery glance at the smile young temptress , who have bewitch more heart than his in her thrall . 
 no doubt , as a kinsman of the house , Mr. Esmond think fit to be the last of all in it ; he remain after the coach have roll away after his dowager aunt 's chair and flambeaux have march off in the darkness towards Chelsey , and the town 's people have go to bed , who have be draw into the square to gape at the unusual assemblage of chair and chariot , lackey , and torchman . the poor mean wretch linger yet for a few minute , to see whether the girl would vouchsafe he a smile , or a parting word of consolation . but her enthusiasm of the morning be quite die out , or she choose to be in a different mood . she fall to joke about the dowdy appearance of Lady Betty , and mimic the vulgarity of Mrs. Steele ; and then she put up her little hand to her mouth and yawn , light a taper , and shrug her shoulder , and drop Mr. Esmond a saucy curtsy , sail off to bed . 
 " the day begin so well , Henry , that I hop it might have end well , " be all the consolation that poor Esmond 's fond mistress could give he ; and as he trudge home through the dark alone , he think with bitter rage in his heart , and a feeling of almost revolt against the sacrifice he have make : " she would have I , " think he , " have I but a name to give she . but for my promise to her father , I might have my rank and my mistress too . " 
 I suppose a man 's vanity be strong than any other passion in he ; for I blush , even now , as I recall the humiliation of those distant day , the memory of which still smart , though the fever of balk desire have pass away more than a score of year ago . when the writer 's descendant come to read this memoir , I wonder will they have live to experience a similar defeat and shame ? will they ever have kneel to a woman who have listen to they , and play with they , and laugh with they who beckon they with lur and caress , and with yes smile from her eye , have trick they on to their knee , and turn she back and leave they . all this shame Mr. Esmond have to undergo ; and he submit , and revolt , and presently come crouch back for more . 
 after this feste , my young Lord Ashburnham 's coach be for ever roll in and out of Kensington Square ; his lady - mother come to visit Esmond 's mistress , and at every assembly in the town , wherever the Maid of Honor make her appearance , you might be pretty sure to see the young gentleman in a new suit every week , and deck out in all the finery that his tailor or embroiderer could furnish for he . my lord be for ever pay Mr. Esmond compliment : bid he to dinner , offer he horse to ride , and give he a thousand uncouth mark of respect and good - will . at last , one night at the coffee - house , whither my lord come considerably flushed and excited with drink , he rush up to Mr. Esmond , and cry out " give I joy , my dear Colonel ; I be the happy of man . " 
 " the happy of man need no dear colonel to give he joy , " say Mr. Esmond . " what be the cause of this supreme felicity ? " 
 " have not   you hear ? " say he . " do not   you know ? I think the family tell you everything : the adorable Beatrix hath promise to be mine . " 
 " what ! " cry out Mr. Esmond , who have spend happy hour with beatrix that very morning have writ verse for she , that she have sing at the harpsichord . 
 " yes , " say he ; " I wait on her to - day . I see you walk towards Knightsbridge as I pass in my coach ; and she look so lovely , and speak so kind , that I could not   help go down on my knee , and and sure I be the happy of man in all the world ; and I be very young ; but she say I shall get old : and you know I shall be of age in four month ; and there be very little difference between we ; and I be so happy . I should like to treat the company to something . let we have a bottle a dozen bottle and drink the health of the fine woman in England . " 
 Esmond leave the young lord toss off bumper after bumper , and stroll away to Kensington to ask whether the news be true . ' twas only too sure : his mistress 's sad , compassionate face tell he the story ; and then she relate what particular of it she know , and how my young lord have make his offer , half an hour after Esmond go away that morning , and in the very room where the song lie yet on the harpsichord , which Esmond have writ , and they have sing together . 




 BOOK III . 

 contain the END of MR . ESMOND 's ADVENTURES in ENGLAND . 





 CHAPTER I. 

 I come to an end of MY battle and BRUISES . 

 that feverish desire to gain a little reputation which Esmond have have , leave he now perhaps that he have attain some portion of his wish , and the great motive of his ambition be over . his desire for military honor be that it might raise he in beatrix 's eye . ' twas next to nobility and wealth , the only kind of rank she value . it be the stake quickest win or lose too ; for law be a very long game that require a life to practise ; and to be distinguish in letter or the Church would not have forward the poor gentleman 's plan in the least . so he have no suit to play but the red one , and he play it ; and this , in truth , be the reason of his speedy promotion ; for he expose himself more than most gentleman do , and risk more to win more . be he the only man that hath set his life against a stake which may be not worth the winning ? another risk his life   and his honor , too , sometimes ,   against a bundle of bank - note , or a yard of blue ribbon , or a seat in Parliament ; and some for the mere pleasure and excitement of the sport ; as a field of a hundred huntsman will do , each out - bawl and out - gallop the other at the tail of a dirty fox , that be to be the prize of the foremost happy conqueror . 
 when he hear this news of Beatrix 's engagement in marriage , Colonel Esmond knock under to his fate , and resolve to surrender his sword , that could win he nothing now he care for ; and in this dismal frame of mind he determine to retire from the regiment , to the great delight of the captain next in rank to he , who happen to be a young gentleman of good fortune , who eagerly pay Mr. Esmond a thousand guineas for his majority in Webb 's regiment , and be knock on the head the next campaign . perhaps Esmond would not have be sorry to share his fate . he be more the Knight of the Woful Countenance than ever he have be . his moodiness must have make he perfectly odious to his friend under the tent , who like a jolly fellow , and laugh at a melancholy warrior always sigh after Dulcinea at home . 
 both the lady of Castlewood approve of Mr. Esmond quit the army , and his kind General coincide in his wish of retirement and help in the transfer of his commission , which bring a pretty sum into his pocket . but when the Commander - in - Chief come home , and be force , in spite of himself , to appoint Lieutenant - General Webb to the command of a division of the army in Flanders , the Lieutenant - General pray Colonel Esmond so urgently to be his aide - de - camp and military secretary , that Esmond could not resist his kind patron 's entreaty , and again take the field , not attach to any regiment , but under Webb 's order . what must have be the continued agony of fear   and apprehension which rack the gentle breast of wife and matron in those dreadful day , when every Gazette bring account of death and battle , and when the present anxiety over , and the beloved person escape , the doubt still remain that a battle might be fight , possibly , of which the next Flanders letter would bring the account ; so they , the poor tender creature , have to go on sicken and tremble through the whole campaign . whatever these terror be on the part of Esmond 's mistress ,   and that tender of woman must have feel they most keenly for both her son , as she call they , she never allow they outwardly to appear , but hide her apprehension , as she do her charity and devotion . ' twas only by chance that Esmond , wander in Kensington , find his mistress come out of a mean cottage there , and hear that she have a score of poor retainer , whom she visit and comfort in their sickness and poverty , and who bless her daily . she attend the early church daily   though of a Sunday , especially , she encourage and advance all sort of cheerfulness and innocent gayety in her little household : and by note enter into a table - book of hers at this time , and devotional composition writ with a sweet artless fervor , such as the good divine could not surpass , show how fond her heart be , how humble and pious her spirit , what pang of apprehension she endure silently , and with what a faithful reliance she commit the care of those she love to the awful Dispenser of death and life . 
    what indeed ? Psm . xci . 2 , 3 , 7 . R. E. 
 as for her ladyship at Chelsey , Esmond 's newly adopt mother , she be now of an age when the danger of any second party doth not disturb the rest much . she care for trump more than for most thing in life . she be firm enough in her own faith , but no long very bitter against ours . she have a very good - nature , easy french director , Monsieur Gauthier by name , who be a gentleman of the world , and would take a hand of card with Dean Atterbury , my lady 's neighbor at Chelsey , and be well with all the High Church party . no doubt Monsieur Gauthier know what Esmond 's peculiar position be , for he correspond with Holt , and always treat Colonel Esmond with particular respect and kindness ; but for good reason the Colonel and the Abbe never speak on this matter together , and so they remain perfect good friend . 
 all the frequenter of my Lady of Chelsey 's house be of the Tory and High Church party . Madame Beatrix be as frantic about the King as her elderly kinswoman : she wear his picture on her heart ; she have a piece of his hair ; she vow he be the most injure , and gallant , and accomplish , and unfortunate , and beautiful of prince . Steele , who quarrel with very many of his Tory friend , but never with Esmond , use to tell the Colonel that his kinswoman 's house be a rendezvous of Tory intrigue ; that Gauthier be a spy ; that Atterbury be a spy ; that letter be constantly go from that house to the Queen at St. Germains ; on which esmond , laughing , would reply , that they use to say in the army the Duke of Marlborough be a spy too , and as much in correspondence with that family as any Jesuit . and without enter very eagerly into the controversy , Esmond have frankly take the side of his family . it seem to he that King James the Third be undoubtedly King of England by right : and at his sister 's death it would be well to have he than a foreigner over we . no man admire King William more ; a hero and a conqueror , the brave , justest , wisest of man but ' twa by the sword he conquer the country , and hold and govern it by the very same right that the great Cromwell hold it , who be truly and greatly a sovereign . but that a foreign despotic Prince , out of Germany , who happen to be descend from King James the First , should take possession of this empire , seem to Mr. Esmond a monstrous injustice at least , every Englishman have a right to protest , and the English Prince , the heir - at - law , the first of all . what man of spirit with such a cause would not back it ? what man of honor with such a crown to win would not fight for it ? but that race be destine . that Prince have himself against he , an enemy he could not overcome . he never dare to draw his sword , though he have it . he let his chance slip by as he lie in the lap of opera - girl , or snivel at the knee of priest ask pardon ; and the blood of hero , and the devotedness of honest heart , and endurance , courage , fidelity , be all spend for he in vain . 
 but let we return to my Lady of Chelsey , who , when her son Esmond announce to her ladyship that he propose to make the ensue campaign , take leave of he with perfect alacrity , and be down to piquet with her gentlewoman before he have well quit the room on his last visit . " tierce to a king , " be the last word he ever hear she say : the game of life be pretty nearly over for the good lady , and three month afterwards she take to her bed , where she flicker out without any pain , so the Abbe Gauthier write over to Mr. Esmond , then with his General on the frontier of France . the Lady Castlewood be with she at her ending , and have write too , but these letter must have be take by a privateer in the packet that bring they ; for Esmond know nothing of their content until his return to England . 
 my Lady Castlewood have leave everything to Colonel Esmond , " as a reparation for the wrong do to he ; " ' twa writ in her will . but her fortune be not much , for it never have be large , and the honest viscountess have wisely sink most of the money she have upon an annuity which terminate with her life . however , there be the house and furniture , plate and picture at Chelsey , and a sum of money lie at her merchant 's , Sir Josiah Child , which altogether would realize a sum of near three hundred pound per annum , so that Mr. Esmond find himself , if not rich , at least easy for life . likewise there be the famous diamond which have be say to be worth fabulous sum , though the goldsmith pronounce they would fetch no more than four thousand pound . these diamond , however , Colonel Esmond reserve , have a special use for they : but the Chelsey house , plate , good ,   c. , with the exception of a few article which he keep back , be sell by his order ; and the sum result from the sale invest in the public security so as to realize the aforesaid annual income of three hundred pound . 
 have now something to leave , he make a will and despatch it home . the army be now in presence of the enemy ; and a great battle expect every day . ' twa know that the General - in - Chief be in disgrace , and the party at home strong against he , and there be no stroke this great and resolute player would not venture to recall his fortune when it seem desperate . Frank Castlewood be with Colonel Esmond ; his General have gladly take the young nobleman on to his staff . his study of fortification at Bruxelles be over by this time . the fort he be besiege have yield , I believe , and my lord have not only march in with fly color , but march out again . he use to tell his boyish wickedness with admirable humor , and be the most charming young scapegrace in the army . 
 ' tis needless to say that Colonel Esmond have leave every penny of his little fortune to this boy . it be the Colonel 's firm conviction that the next battle would put an end to he : for he feel aweary of the sun , and quite ready to bid that and the earth farewell . Frank would not listen to his comrade 's gloomy foreboding , but swear they would keep his birthday at Castlewood that autumn , after the campaign . he have hear of the engagement at home . " if Prince Eugene go to London , " say Frank , " and Trix can get hold of he , she will jilt Ashburnham for his Highness . I tell you , she use to make eye at the Duke of Marlborough , when she be only fourteen , and ogle poor little Blandford . I would not   marry she , Harry no , not if her eye be twice as big . I will take my fun . I will enjoy for the next three year every possible pleasure . I will sow my wild oat then , and marry some quiet , steady , modest , sensible viscountess ; hunt my harrier ; and settle down at Castlewood . perhaps I will represent the county no , damme , you shall represent the county . you have the brain of the family . by the Lord , my dear old Harry , you have the good head and the kind heart in all the army ; and every man say so and when the Queen die , and the King come back , why should not   you go to the House of Commons , and be a Minister , and be make a peer , and that sort of thing ? you be shoot in the next action ! I wager a dozen of Burgundy you be not touch . Mohun be well of his wound . he be always with Corporal John now . as soon as ever I see his ugly face I will spit in it . I take lesson of Father of Captain Holt at Bruxelles . what a man that be ! he know everything . " esmond bade Frank have a care ; that Father Holt 's knowledge be rather dangerous ; not , indeed , know as yet how far the Father have push his instruction with his young pupil . 
 the gazetteer and writer , both of the french and english side , have give account sufficient of that bloody battle of Blarignies or Malplaquet , which be the last and the hard earn of the victory of the great Duke of Marlborough . in that tremendous combat near upon two hundred and fifty thousand man be engage , more than thirty thousand of whom be slay or wound   the Allies lose twice as many man as they kill of the French , whom they conquer : and this dreadful slaughter very likely take place because a great general 's credit be shake at home , and he think to restore it by a victory . if such be the motif which induce the Duke of Marlborough to venture that prodigious stake , and desperately sacrifice thirty thousand brave life , so that he might figure once more in a Gazette , and hold his place and pension a little long , the event defeat the dreadful and selfish design , for the victory be purchase at a cost which no nation , greedy of glory as it may be , would willingly pay for any triumph . the gallantry of the French be as remarkable as the furious bravery of their assailant . we take a few score of their flag , and a few piece of their artillery ; but we leave twenty thousand of the brave soldier of the world round about the intrenched line , from which the enemy be drive . he retreat in perfect good order ; the panic - spell seem to be break , under which the French have labor ever since the disaster of Hochstedt ; and , fight now on the threshold of their country , they show an heroic ardor of resistance , such as have never meet we in the course of their aggressive war . have the battle be more successful , the conqueror might have get the price for which he wag it . as it be ,   and justly , I think ,   the party adverse to the Duke in England be indignant at the lavish extravagance of slaughter , and demand more eagerly than ever the recall of a chief whose cupidity and desperation might urge he far still . after this bloody fight of Malplaquet , I can answer for it , that in the dutch quarter and our own , and amongst the very regiment and commander whose gallantry be most conspicuous upon this frightful day of carnage , the general cry be , that there be enough of the war . the French be drive back into their own boundary , and all their conquest and booty of Flanders disgorge . as for the Prince of Savoy , with whom our Commander - in - Chief , for reason of his own , consort more closely than ever , ' twa know that he be animate not merely by a political hatred , but by personal rage against the old french king : the Imperial Generalissimo never forget the slight put by Lewis upon the Abbe de Savoie ; and in the humiliation or ruin of his most Christian Majesty , the Holy Roman Emperor find his account . but what be these quarrel to we , the free citizen of England and Holland ! despot as he be , the french monarch be yet the chief of european civilization , more venerable in his age and misfortune than at the period of his most splendid success ; whilst his opponent be but a semi - barbarous tyrant , with a pillaging , murderous horde of Croats and pandour , compose a half of his army , fill our camp with their strange figure , bearded like the miscreant Turks their neighbor , and carry into christian warfare their native heathen habit of rapine , lust , and murder . why should the good blood in England and France be shed in order that the Holy Roman and apostolic master of these ruffian should have his revenge over the christian king ? and it be to this end we be fight ; for this that every village and family in England be deplore the death of beloved son and father . we dare not speak to each other , even at table , of Malplaquet , so frightful be the gap leave in our army by the cannon of that bloody action . ' twa heartrende for an officer who have a heart to look down his line on a parade - day afterwards , and miss hundred of face of comrade humble or of high rank that have gather but yesterday full of courage and cheerfulness round the torn and blackened flag . where be our friend ? as the great Duke review we , rid along our line with his fine suite of prance aide - de - camp and general , stop here and there to thank an officer with those eager smile and bow of which his grace be always lavish , scarce a huzzah could be get for he , though Cadogan , with an oath , ride up and cry " d n you , why do not   you cheer ? " but the man have no heart for that : not one of they but be think , " where be my comrade ? where be my brother that fight by I , or my dear captain that lead I yesterday ? " ' twas the most gloomy pageant I ever look on ; and the " Te Deum " sing by our chaplain , the most woful and dreary satire . 
 Esmond 's General add one more to the many mark of honor which he have receive in the front of a score of battle , and get a wound in the groin , which lay he on his back ; and you may be sure he console himself by abuse the Commander - in - Chief , as he lie groan , " Corporal John 's as fond of I , " he use to say , " as King David be of General Uriah ; and so he always give I the post of danger . " he persist , to his die day , in believe that the Duke intend he should be beat at Wynendael , and send he purposely with a small force , hop that he might be knock on the head there . Esmond and Frank Castlewood both escape without hurt , though the division which our General command suffer even more than any other , have to sustain not only the fury of the enemy 's cannonade , which be very hot and well serve , but the furious and repeat charge of the famous Maison du Roy , which we have to receive and beat off again and again , with volley of shot and hedge of iron , and our four line of musqueteer and pikemen . they say the King of England charge we no less than twelve time that day , along with the french Household . Esmond 's late regiment , General Webb 's own Fusileers , serve in the division which their colonel command . the General be thrice in the centre of the square of the Fusileers , call the fire at the french charge , and , after the action , his grace the Duke of Berwick send his compliment to his old regiment and their Colonel for their behavior on the field . 
 we drink my Lord Castlewood 's health and majority , the 25th of September , the army be then before Mons : and here Colonel Esmond be not so fortunate as he have be in action much more dangerous , and be hit by a spend ball just above the place where his former wound be , which cause the old wound to open again , fever , spit of blood , and other ugly symptom , to ensue ; and , in a word , bring he near to death 's door . the kind lad , his kinsman , attend his eld comrade with a very praiseworthy affectionateness and care until he be pronounce out of danger by the doctor , when Frank go off , pass the winter at Bruxelles , and besiege , no doubt , some other fortress there . very few lad would have give up their pleasure so long and so gayly as Frank do ; his cheerful prattle soothe many long day of Esmond 's pain and languor . Frank be suppose to be still at his kinsman 's bedside for a month after he have leave it , for letter come from his mother at home full of thank to the young gentleman for his care of his eld brother   so it please Esmond 's mistress now affectionately to style he ; nor be Mr. Esmond in a hurry to undeceive she , when the good young fellow be go for his Christmas holiday . it be as pleasant to Esmond on his couch to watch the young man 's pleasure at the idea of be free , as to note his simple effort to disguise his satisfaction on go away . there be day when a flask of champagne at a cabaret , and a red - cheeked partner to share it , be too strong temptation for any young fellow of spirit . I be not go to play the moralist , and cry " Fie . " for age past , I know how old man preach , and what young man practise ; and that patriarch have have their weak moment too , long since Father Noah topple over after discover the vine . Frank go off , then , to his pleasure at Bruxelles , in which capital many young fellow of our army declare they find infinitely great diversion even than in London : and Mr. Henry Esmond remain in his sick - room , where he writ a fine comedy , that his mistress pronounce to be sublime , and that be act no less than three successive night in London in the next year . 
 here , as he lie nursing himself , ubiquitous Mr. Holt reappear , and stop a whole month at Mons , where he not only win over Colonel Esmond to the King 's side in politic   that side be always hold by the Esmond family ; but where he endeavor to reopen the controversial question between the church once more , and to recall Esmond to that religion in which , in his infancy , he have be baptize . Holt be a casuist , both dexterous and learn , and present the case between the english church and his own in such a way that those who grant his premise ought certainly to allow his conclusion . he touch on Esmond 's delicate state of health , chance of dissolution , and so forth ; and enlarge upon the immense benefit that the sick man be likely to forego benefit which the church of England do not deny to those of the roman communion , as how should she , be derive from that church , and only an offshoot from it ? but Mr. Esmond say that his church be the church of his country , and to that he choose to remain faithful : other people be welcome to worship and to subscribe any other set of article , whether at Rome or at Augsburg . but if the good Father mean that Esmond should join the roman communion for fear of consequence , and that all England run the risk of be damn for heresy , Esmond , for one , be perfectly willing to take his chance of the penalty along with the countless million of his fellow - countryman , who be breed in the same faith , and along with some of the noble , the true , the pure , the wise , the most pious and learn man and woman in the world . 
 as for the political question , in that Mr. Esmond could agree with the Father much more readily , and have come to the same conclusion , though , perhaps , by a different way . the right divine , about which Dr. Sacheverel and the High Church party in England be just now make a bother , they be welcome to hold as they choose . if Richard Cromwell , and his father before he have be crown and anoint   and bishop enough would have be find to do it , it seem to Mr. Esmond that they would have have the right divine just as much as any Plantagenet , or Tudor , or Stuart . but the desire of the country be unquestionably for an hereditary monarchy , Esmond think an english king out of St. Germains be well and fitter than a german prince from Herrenhausen , and that if he fail to satisfy the nation , some other Englishman might be find to take his place ; and so , though with no frantic enthusiasm , or worship of that monstrous pedigree which the Tories choose to consider divine , he be ready to say , " God save King James ! " when Queen Anne go the way of king and commoner . 
 " I fear , Colonel , you be no well than a republican at heart , " say the priest with a sigh . 
 " I be an Englishman , " say Harry , " and take my country as I find she . the will of the nation be for church and king , I be for church and king too ; but english church and english king ; and that be why your church be not   mine , though your king be . " 
 though they lose the day at Malplaquet , it be the French who be elate by that action , whilst the conqueror be dispirit , by it ; and the enemy gather together a large army than ever , and make prodigious effort for the next campaign . Marshal Berwick be with the French this year ; and we hear that Mareschal Villars be still suffer of his wound , be eager to bring our Duke to action , and vow he would fight we in his coach . Young Castlewood come fly back from Bruxelles , as soon as he hear that fighting be to begin ; and the arrival of the Chevalier de St. George be announce about May . " it be the King 's third campaign , and it be mine , " Frank like say . he be come back a great Jacobite than ever , and Esmond suspect that some fair conspirator at Bruxelles have be inflame the young man 's ardor . indeed , he own that he have a message from the Queen , Beatrix 's godmother , who have give her name to Frank 's sister the year before he and his sovereign be bear . 
 however desirous Marshal Villars might be to fight , my Lord Duke do not seem disposed to indulge he this campaign . last year his grace have be all for the Whigs and Hanoverians ; but find , on go to England , his country cold towards himself , and the people in a ferment of High Church loyalty , the Duke come back to his army cool towards the Hanoverians , cautious with the Imperialists , and particularly civil and polite towards the Chevalier de St. George . ' tis certain that messenger and letter be continually pass between his grace and his brave nephew , the Duke of Berwick , in the opposite camp . no man 's caress be more opportune than his grace 's , and no man ever utter expression of regard and affection more generously . he profess to Monsieur de Torcy , so Mr. St. John tell the writer , quite an eagerness to be cut in piece for the exiled queen and her family ; nay more , I believe , this year he part with a portion of the most precious part of himself his money which he send over to the royal exile . Mr. Tunstal , who be in the Prince 's service , be twice or thrice in and out of our camp ; the French , in theirs of Arlieu and about Arras . a little river , the Canihe I think ' twa call ,   but this be writ away from book and Europe ; and the only map the writer hath of these scene of his youth , bear no mark of this little stream ,   divide our picket from the enemy 's . our sentry talk across the stream , when they could make themselves understand to each other , and when they could not , grin , and hand each other their brandy - flask or their pouch of tobacco . and one fine day of June , rid thither with the officer who visit the outpost ,   Colonel Esmond be take an airing on horseback , be too weak for military duty ,   they come to this river , where a number of English and Scots be assemble , talk to the good - natured enemy on the other side . 
 Esmond be especially amuse with the talk of one long fellow , with a great curl red moustache , and blue eye , that be half a dozen inch tall than his swarthy little comrade on the french side of the stream , and be ask by the Colonel , salute he , and say that he belong to the Royal Cravats . 
 from his way of say " Royal Cravat , " esmond at once know that the fellow 's tongue have first wag on the bank of the Liffey , and not the Loire ; and the poor soldier a deserter probably do not like to venture very deep into french conversation , lest his unlucky brogue should peep out . he choose to restrict himself to such few expression in the french language as he think he have master easily ; and his attempt at disguise be infinitely amusing . Mr. Esmond whistle Lillibullero , at which Teague 's eye begin to twinkle , and then fling he a dollar , when the poor boy break out with a " God bless that be , Dieu benisse votre honor , " that would infallibly have send he to the provost - marshal have he be on our side of the river . 
 Whilst this parley be go on , three officer on horseback , on the french side , appear at some little distance , and stop as if eye we , when one of they leave the other two , and ride close up to we who be by the stream . " look , look ! " say the Royal Cravat , with great agitation , " pas lui , that be he ; not he , l'autre , " and point to the distant officer on a chestnut horse , with a cuirass shin in the sun , and over it a broad blue ribbon . 
 " please to take Mr. Hamilton 's service to my Lord Marlborough my Lord Duke , " say the gentleman in English : and , look to see that the party be not hostilely dispose , he add , with a smile , " there be a friend of your , gentleman , yonder ; he bid I to say that he see some of your face on the 11th of September last year . " 
 as the gentleman speak , the other two officer ride up , and come quite close . we know at once who it be . it be the king , then two - and - twenty year old , tall and slim , with deep brown eye , that look melancholy , though his lip wear a smile . we take off our hat and salute he . no man , sure , could see for the first time , without emotion , the youthful inheritor of so much fame and misfortune . it seem to Mr. Esmond that the Prince be not unlike young Castlewood , whose age and figure he resemble . the Chevalier de St. George acknowledge the salute , and look at we hard . even the idler on our side of the river set up a hurrah . as for the Royal Cravat , he run to the Prince 's stirrup , kneel down and kiss his boot , and bawl and look a hundred ejaculation and blessing . the prince bid the aide - de - camp give he a piece of money ; and when the party salute we have ride away , Cravat spit upon the piece of gold by way of benediction , and swagger away , pouch his coin and twirl his honest carroty moustache . 
 the officer in whose company Esmond be , the same little captain of Handyside 's regiment , Mr. Sterne , who have propose the garden at Lille , when my Lord Mohun and Esmond have their affair , be an Irishman too , and as brave a little soul as ever wear a sword . " Bedad , " say Roger Sterne , " that long fellow speak French so beautiful that I should not   have know he be not   a foreigner , till he break out with his hulla - ballooing , and only an irish calf can bellow like that . " and Roger make another remark in his wild way , in which there be sense as well as absurdity " if that young gentleman , " say he , " would but ride over to our camp , instead of Villars 's , toss up his hat and say , ' here be I , the king , who will follow I ? ' by the Lord , Esmond , the whole army would rise and carry he home again , and beat villar , and take Paris by the way . " 
 the news of the Prince 's visit be all through the camp quickly , and score of our go down in hope to see he . Major Hamilton , whom we have talk with , send back by a trumpet several silver piece for officer with we . Mr. Esmond receive one of these ; and that medal , and a recompense not uncommon amongst Princes , be the only reward he ever have from a royal person , whom he endeavor not very long after to serve . 
 Esmond quit the army almost immediately after this , follow his general home ; and , indeed , be advise to travel in the fine weather and attempt to take no further part in the campaign . but he hear from the army , that of the many who crowd to see the Chevalier de St. George , Frank Castlewood have make himself most conspicuous : my Lord Viscount rid across the little stream bareheade to where the Prince be , and dismounting and kneeling before he to do he homage . some say that the Prince have actually knight he , but my lord deny that statement , though he acknowledge the rest of the story , and say : " from have be out of favor with Corporal John , " as he call the Duke , " before his grace warn he not to commit those folly , and smile on he cordially ever after . " 
 " and he be so kind to I , " Frank writ , " that I think I would put in a good word for Master Harry , but when I mention your name he look as black as thunder , and say he have never hear of you . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 I GO home , and HARP on the old string . 

 after quit Mons and the army , and as he be wait for a packet at Ostend , Esmond have a letter from his young kinsman Castlewood at Bruxelles , convey intelligence whereof Frank beseech he to be the bearer to London , and which cause Colonel Esmond no small anxiety . 
 the young scapegrace , be one - and - twenty year old , and be anxious to sow his " wild ote , " as he write , have marry Mademoiselle de Wertheim , daughter of Count de Wertheim , Chamberlain to the Emperor , and have a post in the Household of the Governor of the Netherlands . " P.S. , " the young gentleman write : " Clotilda be old than ME , which perhaps may be object to she : but I be so old a raik that the age make no difference , and I be DETERMINED to reform . we be marry at St. Gudule , by Father Holt . she be heart and soul for the GOOD CAUSE . and here the cry be vif - le - Roy , which my mother will JOIN IN , and Trix TOO . break this news to them gently : and tell Mr. Finch , my agent , to press the people for their rent , and send I the ryno anyhow . Clotilda sing , and play on the Spinet BEAUTIFULLY . she be a fair beauty . and if it be a son , you shall stand GODFATHER . I be go to leave the army , have have ENUF of SOLDERING ; and my Lord Duke recommend I . I shall pass the winter here : and stop at least until Clo 's lie in . I call her old CLO , but nobody else shall . she be the clever woman in all Bruxelles : understand painting , music , poetry , and perfect at COOKERY and PUDDENS . I borde with the Count , that be how I come to know she . there be four count her brother . one an Abbey three with the Prince 's army . they have a lawsuit for an immence FORTUNE : but be now in a pore WAY . break this to mother , who will take anything from you . and write , and bid Finch write AMEDIATELY . Hostel de l'Aigle Noire , Bruxelles , Flanders . " 
 so Frank have marry a roman catholic lady , and an heir be expect , and Mr. Esmond be to carry this intelligence to his mistress at London . ' twas a difficult embassy ; and the Colonel feel not a little tremor as he near the capital . 
 he reach his inn late , and send a messenger to Kensington to announce his arrival and visit the next morning . the messenger bring back news that the Court be at Windsor , and the fair beatrix absent and engage in her duty there . only Esmond 's mistress remain in her house at Kensington . she appear in court but once in the year ; beatrix be quite the mistress and ruler of the little mansion , invite the company thither , and engage in every conceivable frolic of town pleasure . whilst her mother , act as the young lady 's protectress and eld sister , pursue her own path , which be quite modest and seclude . 
 as soon as ever Esmond be dress   and he have be awake long before the town , he take a coach for Kensington , and reach it so early that he meet his dear mistress come home from morning prayer . she carry her prayer - book , never allow a footman to bear it , as everybody else do : and it be by this simple sign Esmond know what her occupation have be . he call to the coachman to stop , and jump out as she look towards he . she wear her hood as usual , and she turn quite pale when she see he . to feel that kind little hand near to his heart seem to give he strength . they be soon at the door of her ladyship 's house and within it . 
 with a sweet sad smile she take his hand and kiss it . 
 " how ill you have be : how weak you look , my dear Henry , " she say . 
 ' tis certain the Colonel do look like a ghost , except that ghost do not look very happy , ' tis say . esmond always feel so on return to she after absence , indeed whenever he look in her sweet kind face . 
 " I be come back to be nurse by my family , " say he . " if Frank have not take care of I after my wound , very likely I should have go altogether . " 
 " Poor Frank , good Frank ! " say his mother . " you will always be kind to he , my lord , " she go on . " the poor child never know he be do you a wrong . " 
 " my lord ! " cry out Colonel Esmond . " what do you mean , dear lady ? " 
 " I be no lady , " say she ; " I be Rachel Esmond , Francis Esmond 's widow , my lord . I can not bear that title . would we never have take it from he who have it now . but we do all in our power , Henry : we do all in our power ; and my lord and I that be " 
 " who tell you this tale , dear lady ? " ask the Colonel . 
 " have you not have the letter I writ you ? I writ to you at mon directly I hear it , " say Lady Esmond . 
 " and from whom ? " again ask Colonel Esmond and his mistress then tell he that on her death - bed the Dowager Countess , send for she , have present she with this dismal secret as a legacy . " ' twas very malicious of the Dowager , " Lady Esmond say , " to have have it so long , and to have keep the truth from I . " " Cousin Rachel , " she say , and Esmond 's mistress could not forbear smile as she tell the story " Cousin Rachel , " cry the Dowager , " I have send for you , as the doctor say I may go off any day in this dysentery ; and to ease my conscience of a great load that have be on it . you always have be a poor creature and unfit for great honor , and what I have to say will not , therefore , affect you so much . you must know , Cousin Rachel , that I have leave my house , plate , and furniture , three thousand pound in money , and my diamond that my late revere Saint and Sovereign , King James , present I with , to my Lord Viscount Castlewood . " 
 " to my Frank ? " say Lady Castlewood ; " I be in hope " 
 to Viscount Castlewood , my dear ; Viscount Castlewood and Baron Esmond of Shandon in the Kingdom of Ireland , Earl and Marquis of Esmond under patent of his Majesty King James the Second , confer upon my husband the late Marquis for I be Marchioness of Esmond before God and man . " 
 " and have you leave poor Harry nothing , dear marchioness ? " ask Lady Castlewood   she hath tell I the story completely since with her quiet arch way ; the most charming any woman ever have : and I set down the narrative here at length , so as to have do with it . " and have you leave poor Harry nothing ? " ask my dear lady : " for you know , Henry , " she say with her sweet smile , " I use always to pity Esau and I think I be on his side though papa try very hard to convince I the other way . " 
 " Poor Harry ! " say the old lady . " so you want something leave to poor Harry : he , he !   reach I the drop , cousin . well , then , my dear , since you want poor Harry to have a fortune , you must understand that ever since the year 1691 , a week after the battle of the Boyne , where the Prince of Orange defeat his royal sovereign and father , for which crime he be now suffer in flame   ugh ! ugh !   Henry Esmond hath be Marquis of Esmond and Earl of Castlewood in the United Kingdom , and Baron and Viscount Castlewood of Shandon in Ireland , and a Baronet and his eld son will be , by courtesy , style Earl of Castlewood he ! he ! what do you think of that , my dear ? " 
 " gracious mercy ! how long have you know this ? " cry the other lady   thinking perhaps that the old Marchioness be wander in her wit . 
 " my husband , before he be convert , be a wicked wretch , " the sick sinner continue . " when he be in the Low Countries he seduce a weaver 's daughter ; and add to his wickedness by marry she . and then he come to this country and marry I a poor girl a poor innocent young thing I say , " " though she be past forty , you know , Harry , when she marry : and as for be innocent " " well , " she go on , " I know nothing of my lord 's wickedness for three year after our marriage , and after the burial of our poor little boy I have it do over again , my dear : I have myself marry by Father Holt in Castlewood chapel , as soon as ever I hear the creature be dead and have a great illness then , arise from another sad disappointment I have , the priest come and tell I that my lord have a son before our marriage , and that the child be at nurse in England ; and I consent to let the brat be bring home , and a queer little melancholy child it be when it come .
