 " our intention be to make a priest of he : and he be breed for this , until you pervert he from it , you wicked woman . and I have again hope of give an heir to my lord , when he be call away upon the King 's business , and die fight gloriously at the Boyne water . 
 " should I be disappoint I owe your husband no love , my dear , for he have jilt I in the most scandalous way and I think there would be time to declare the little weaver 's son for the true heir . but I be carry off to prison , where your husband be so kind to I urge all his friend to obtain my release , and use all his credit in my favor that I relent towards he , especially as my director counsel I to be silent ; and that it be for the good of the King 's service that the title of our family should continue with your husband the late viscount , whereby his fidelity would be always secure to the king . and a proof of this be , that a year before your husband 's death , when he think of take a place under the Prince of Orange , Mr. Holt go to he , and tell he what the state of the matter be , and oblige he to raise a large sum for his Majesty ; and engage he in the true cause so heartily , that we be sure of his support on any day when it should be consider advisable to attack the usurper . then his sudden death come ; and there be a thought of declare the truth . but ' twa determined to be good for the King 's service to let the title still go with the young branch ; and there be no sacrifice a Castlewood would not   make for that cause , my dear . 
 " as for Colonel Esmond , he know the truth already . "   " and then , Harry , " my mistress say , ' she tell I of what have happen at my dear husband 's death - bed " . " he doth not intend to take the title , though it belong to he . but it ease my conscience that you should know the truth , my dear . and your son be lawfully Viscount Castlewood so long as his cousin doth not claim the rank . " 
 this be the substance of the Dowager 's revelation . Dean Atterbury have knowledge of it , Lady Castlewood say , and Esmond very well know how : that divine be the clergyman for whom the late lord have send on his death - bed : and when Lady Castlewood would instantly have write to her son , and convey the truth to he , the Dean 's advice be that a letter should be writ to Colonel Esmond rather ; that the matter should be submit to his decision , by which alone the rest of the family be bind to abide . 
 " and can my dear lady doubt what that will be ? " say the Colonel . 
 " it rest with you , Harry , as the head of our house . " 
 " it be settle twelve year since , by my dear lord 's bedside , " say Colonel Esmond . " the child must know nothing of this . Frank and his heir after he must bear our name . ' tis his rightfully ; I have not even a proof of that marriage of my father and mother , though my poor lord , on his death - bed , tell I that Father Holt have bring such a proof to Castlewood . I would not seek it when I be abroad . I go and look at my poor mother 's grave in her convent . what matter to she now ? no court of law on earth , upon my mere word , would deprive my Lord Viscount and set I up . I be the head of the house , dear lady ; but Frank be Viscount of Castlewood still . and rather than disturb he , I would turn monk , or disappear in America . " 
 as he speak so to his dear mistress , for whom he would have be willing to give up his life , or to make any sacrifice any day , the fond creature fling herself down on her knee before he , and kiss both his hand in an outbreak of passionate love and gratitude , such as could not but melt his heart , and make he feel very proud and thankful that God have give he the power to show his love for she , and to prove it by some little sacrifice on his own part . to be able to bestow benefit or happiness on those one love be sure the great blessing confer upon a man and what wealth or name , or gratification of ambition or vanity , could compare with the pleasure Esmond now have of be able to confer some kindness upon his good and dearest friend ? 
 " Dearest saint , " say he " purest soul , that have have so much to suffer , that have bl the poor lonely orphan with such a treasure of love . ' tis for I to kneel , not for you : ' tis for I to be thankful that I can make you happy . hath my life any other aim ? blessed be God that I can serve you ! what pleasure , think you , could all the world give I compare to that ? " 
 " do not   raise I , " she say , in a wild way , to Esmond , who would have lift she . " let I kneel let I kneel , and and worship you . " 
 before such a partial judge as Esmond 's dear mistress own herself to be , any cause which he might plead be sure to be give in his favor ; and accordingly he find little difficulty in reconcile she to the news whereof he be bearer , of her son 's marriage to a foreign lady , Papist though she be . Lady Castlewood never could be bring to think so ill of that religion as other people in England think of it : she hold that our be undoubtedly a branch of the catholic church , but that the Roman be one of the main stem on which , no doubt , many error have be graft   she be , for a woman , extraordinarily well verse in this controversy , have act , as a girl , as secretary to her father , the late dean , and write many of his sermon , under his dictation ; and if Frank have choose to marry a lady of the church of south Europe , as she would call the roman communion , there be no need why she should not welcome she as a daughter - in - law : and accordingly she write to her new daughter a very pretty , touching letter   as Esmond thought , who have cognizance of it before it go , in which the only hint of reproof be a gentle remonstrance that her son have not write to herself , to ask a fond mother 's blessing for that step which he be about take . " Castlewood know very well , " so she write to her son , " that she never deny he anything in her power to give , much less would she think of oppose a marriage that be to make his happiness , as she trust , and keep he out of wild course , which have alarm she a good deal : " and she besought he to come quickly to England , to settle down in his family house of Castlewood   " it be his family house , " say she , to Colonel Esmond , " though only his own house by your forbearance "   and to receive the accompt of her stewardship during his ten year ' minority . by care and frugality , she have get the estate into a well condition than ever it have be since the parliamentary war ; and my lord be now master of a pretty , small income , not encumber of debt , as it have be , during his father 's ruinous time . " but in save my son 's fortune , " say she , " I fear I have lose a great part of my hold on he . " and , indeed , this be the case : her ladyship 's daughter complain that their mother do all for Frank , and nothing for she ; and Frank himself be dissatisfied at the narrow , simple way of his mother 's living at Walcote , where he have be bring up more like a poor parson 's son than a young nobleman that be to make a figure in the world . ' twas this mistake in his early training , very likely , that set he so eager upon pleasure when he have it in his power ; nor be he the first lad that have be spoil by the over - careful fondness of woman . no training be so useful for child , great or small , as the company of their better in rank or natural part ; in whose society they lose the overweening sense of their own importance , which stay - at - home people very commonly learn . 
 but , as a prodigal that be send in a schedule of his debt to his friend , never put all down , and , you may be sure , the rogue keep back some immense swingeing bill , that he do not   dare to own ; so the poor Frank have a very heavy piece of news to break to his mother , and which he have not   the courage to introduce into his first confession . some misgiving Esmond might have , upon receive Frank 's letter , and know into what hand the boy have fall ; but whatever these misgiving be , he keep they to himself , not care to trouble his mistress with any fear that might be groundless . 
 however , the next mail which come from Bruxelles , after Frank have receive his mother 's letter there , bring back a joint composition from himself and his wife , who could spell no well than her young scapegrace of a husband , full of expression of thank , love , and duty to the Dowager Viscountess , as my poor lady now be style ; and along with this letter   which be read in a family council , namely , the Viscountess , Mistress Beatrix , and the writer of this memoir , and which be pronounce to be vulgar by the maid of honor , and feel to be so by the other two , there come a private letter for Colonel Esmond from poor Frank , with another dismal commission for the Colonel to execute , at his good opportunity ; and this be to announce that Frank have see fit , " by the exhortation of Mr. Holt , the influence of his Clotilda , and the blessing of heaven and the saint , " say my lord , demurely , " to change his religion , and be receive into the bosom of that church of which his sovereign , many of his family , and the great part of the civilized world , be member . " and his lordship add a postscript , of which Esmond know the inspire genius very well , for it have the genuine twang of the Seminary , and be quite unlike poor Frank 's ordinary style of writing and thinking ; in which he remind Colonel Esmond that he too be , by birth , of that church ; and that his mother and sister should have his lordship 's prayer to the saint   an inestimable benefit , truly !   for their conversion . 
 if Esmond have want to keep this secret , he could not ; for a day or two after receive this letter , a notice from Bruxelles appear in the Post - boy and other print , announce that " a young irish lord , the Viscount C - stlew d , just come to his majority , and who have serve the last campaign with great credit , as aide - de - camp to his grace the Duke of Marlborough , have declare for the popish religion at Bruxelles , and have walk in a procession barefoot , with a wax - taper in his hand . " the notorious Mr. Holt , who have be employ as a Jacobite agent during the last reign , and many time pardon by King William , have be , the Post - Boy say , the agent of this conversion . 
 the Lady Castlewood be as much cast down by this news as Miss Beatrix be indignant at it . " so , " say she , " Castlewood be no long a home for we , mother . Frank 's foreign wife will bring her confessor , and there will be frog for dinner ; and all Tusher 's and my grandfather 's sermon be fling away upon my brother . I use to tell you that you kill he with the catechism , and that he would turn wicked as soon as he break from his mammy 's lead - string . oh , mother , you would not believe that the young scapegrace be play you trick , and that sneak of a Tusher be not a fit guide for he . oh , those parson , I hate them all ! " say Mistress Beatrix , clap her hand together ; " yes , whether they wear cassock and buckle , or beard and bare foot . there be a horrid irish wretch who never miss a Sunday at Court , and who pay I compliment there , the horrible man ; and if you want to know what parson be , you should see his behavior , and hear he talk of his own cloth . they be all the same , whether they be bishop , or bonze , or indian fakir . they try to domineer , and they frighten we with kingdom come ; and they wear a sanctify air in public , and expect we to go down on our knee and ask their blessing ; and they intrigue , and they grasp , and they backbite , and they slander bad than the bad courtier or the wicked old woman . I hear this Mr. Swift sneer at my Lord Duke of Marlborough 's courage the other day . he ! that Teague from Dublin ! because his grace be not in favor , dare to say this of he ; and he say this that it may get to her Majesty 's ear , and to coax and wheedle Mrs. Masham . they say the Elector of Hanover have a dozen of mistress in his court at Herrenhausen , and if he come to be king over we , I wager that the bishop and Mr. Swift , that want to be one , will coax and wheedle they . oh , those priest and their grave air ! I be sick of their square toe and their rustle cassock . I should like to go to a country where there be not one , or turn Quaker , and get rid of them ; and I would , only the dress be not become , and I have   much too pretty a figure to hide it . have not   I , cousin ? " and here she glance at her person and the look - glass , which tell she rightly that a more beautiful shape and face never be see . 
 " I make that onslaught on the priest , " say Miss Beatrix , afterwards , " in order to divert my poor dear mother 's anguish about Frank . Frank be as vain as a girl , cousin . talk of we girl be vain , what be we to you ? it be easy to see that the first woman who choose would make a fool of he , or the first robe I count a priest and a woman all the same . we be always cabal ; we be not answerable for the fib we tell ; we be always cajole and coax , or threatening ; and we be always make mischief , Colonel Esmond mark my word for that , who know the world , sir , and have to make my way in it . I see as well as possible how Frank 's marriage hath be manage . the Count , our papa - in - law , be always away at the coffee - house . the Countess , our mother , be always in the kitchen look after the dinner . the Countess , our sister , be at the spinet . when my lord come to say he be go on the campaign , the lovely Clotilda burst into tear , and faint so ; he catch she in his arm no , sir , keep your distance , cousin , if you please she cry on his shoulder , and he say , ' oh , my divine , my adore , my beloved Clotilda , be you sorry to part with I ? ' ' oh , my Francisco , ' say she , ' oh my lord ! ' and at this very instant mamma and a couple of young brother , with moustache and long rapier , come in from the kitchen , where they have be eat bread and onion . mark my word , you will have all this woman 's relation at Castlewood three month after she have arrive there . the old count and countess , and the young count and all the little countesse her sister . count ! every one of these wretch say he be a count . Guiscard , that stab Mr. Harvey , say he be a count ; and I believe he be a barber . all frenchman be barber Fiddledee ! do not   contradict I or else dancing - masters , or else priest . " and so she rattle on . 
 " who be it teach you to dance , Cousin Beatrix ? " say the Colonel . 
 she laugh out the air of a minuet , and sweep a low curtsy , come up to the recover with the prettiest little foot in the world point out . her mother come in as she be in this attitude ; my lady have be in her closet , have take poor Frank 's conversion in a very serious way ; the madcap girl run up to her mother , put her arm round her waist , kiss she , try to make her dance , and say : " do not   be silly , you kind little mamma , and cry about Frank turn Papist . what a figure he must be , with a white sheet and a candle , walk in a procession barefoot ! " and she kick off her little slipper   the wonderfull little shoe with wonderful tall red heel : Esmond pounce upon one as it fall close beside he , and she put on the droll little moue , and march up and down the room hold Esmond 's cane by way of taper . serious as her mood be , Lady Castlewood could not refrain from laugh ; and as for Esmond he look on with that delight with which the sight of this fair creature always inspire he : never have he see any woman so arch , so brilliant , and so beautiful . 
 have finish her march , she put out her foot for her slipper . the Colonel kneel down : " if you will be Pope I will turn Papist , " say he ; and her Holiness give he gracious leave to kiss the little stockinged foot before he put the slipper on . 
 Mamma 's foot begin to pat on the floor during this operation , and beatrix , whose bright eye nothing escape , see that little mark of impatience . she run up and embrace her mother , with her usual cry of , " oh , you silly little mamma : your foot be quite as pretty as mine , " say she : " they be , cousin , though she hide them ; but the shoemaker will tell you that he make for both off the same last . " 
 " you be tall than I be , dearest , " say her mother , blush over her whole sweet face " and and it be your hand , my dear , and not your foot he want you to give he ; " and she say it with a hysteric laugh , that have more of tear than laughter in it ; lay her head on her daughter 's fair shoulder , and hide it there . they make a very pretty picture together , and look like a pair of sister the sweet simple matron seem young than her year , and her daughter , if not old , yet somehow , from a commanding manner and grace which she possess above most woman , her mother 's superior and protectress . 
 " but oh ! " cry my mistress , recover herself after this scene , and return to her usual sad tone , " ' tis a shame that we should laugh and be make merry on a day when we ought to be down on our knee and ask pardon . " 
 " ask pardon for what ? " say saucy Mrs. Beatrix " because Frank take it into his head to fast on Fridays and worship image ? you know if you have be bear a Papist , mother , a Papist you would have remain to the end of your day . ' tis the religion of the King and of some of the good quality . for my part , I be no enemy to it , and think Queen Bess be not a penny well than Queen Mary . " 
 " Hush , beatrix ! do not jest with sacred thing , and remember of what parentage you come , " cry my lady . beatrix be order her ribbon , and adjust her tucker , and perform a dozen provokingly pretty ceremony , before the glass . the girl be no hypocrite at least . she never at that time could be bring to think but of the world and her beauty ; and seem to have no more sense of devotion than some people have of music , that can not distinguish one air from another . esmond see this fault in she , as he see many other a bad wife would beatrix Esmond make , he think , for any man under the degree of a Prince . she be bear to shine in great assembly , and to adorn palace , and to command everywhere to conduct an intrigue of politic , or to glitter in a queen 's train . but to sit at a homely table , and mend the stocking of a poor man 's child ! that be no fitting duty for she , or at least one that she would not   have break her heart in try to do . she be a princess , though she have scarce a shilling to her fortune ; and one of her subject the most abject and devoted wretch , sure , that ever drivel at a woman 's knee be this unlucky gentleman ; who bind his good sense , and reason , and independence , hand and foot , and submit they to she . 
 and who do not know how ruthlessly woman will tyrannize when they be let to domineer ? and who do not know how useless advice be ? I could give good counsel to my descendant , but I know they will follow their own way , for all their grandfather 's sermon . a man get his own experience about woman , and will take nobody 's hearsay ; nor , indeed , be the young fellow worth a fig that would . ' tis I that be in love with my mistress , not my old grandmother that counsel I : ' tis I that have fix the value of the thing I would have , and know the price I would pay for it . it may be worthless to you , but ' tis all my life to I . have Esmond possess the Great Mogul 's crown and all his diamond , or all the Duke of Marlborough 's money , or all the ingot sink at Vigo , he would have give they all for this woman . a fool he be , if you will ; but so be a sovereign a fool , that will give half a principality for a little crystal as big as a pigeon 's egg , and call a diamond : so be a wealthy nobleman a fool , that will face danger or death , and spend half his life , and all his tranquillity , cabal for a blue ribbon ; so be a dutch merchant a fool , that hath be know to pay ten thousand crown for a tulip . there be some particular prize we all of we value , and that every man of spirit will venture his life for . with this , it may be to achieve a great reputation for learn ; with that , to be a man of fashion , and the admiration of the town ; with another , to consummate a great work of art or poetry , and go to immortality that way ; and with another , for a certain time of his life , the sole object and aim be a woman . 
 Whilst Esmond be under the domination of this passion , he remember many a talk he have with his intimate , who use to rally our Knight of the Rueful Countenance at his devotion , whereof he make no disguise , to beatrix ; and it be with reply such as the above he meet his friend ' satire . " grant , I be a fool , " say he , " and no well than you ; but you be no well than I. you have your folly you labor for ; give I the charity of mine . what flattery do you , Mr. St. John , stoop to whisper in the ear of a queen 's favorite ? what night of labor doth not the lazy man in the world endure , forego his bottle , and his boon companion , forego Lais , in whose lap he would like to be yawn , that he may prepare a speech full of lie , to cajole three hundred stupid country - gentleman in the House of Commons , and get the hiccupping cheer of the October Club ! what day will you spend in your jolting chariot . "   Mr. Esmond often ride to Windsor , and especially , of later day , with the secretary .   " what hour will you pass on your gouty foot and how humbly will you kneel down to present a despatch you , the proud man in the world , that have not kneel to God since you be a boy , and in that posture whisper , flatter , adore almost , a stupid woman , that be often boozy with too much meat and drink , when Mr. Secretary go for his audience ! if my pursuit be vanity , sure yours be too . " and then the Secretary , would fly out in such a rich flow of eloquence , as this pen can not pretend to recall ; advocate his scheme of ambition , show the great good he would do for his country when he be the undisputed chief of it ; back his opinion with a score of pat sentence from greek and roman authority   of which kind of learn he make rather an ostentatious display , and scornfully vaunt the very art and meanness by which fool be to be make to follow he , opponent to be bribe or silence , doubter convert , and enemy overawe . 
 " I be diogene , " say Esmond , laugh , " that be take up for a ride in Alexander 's chariot . I have no desire to vanquish Darius or to tame Bucephalus . I do not want what you want , a great name or a high place : to have they would bring I no pleasure . but my moderation be taste , not virtue ; and I know that what I do want be as vain as that which you long after . do not grudge I my vanity , if I allow your ; or rather , let we laugh at both indifferently , and at ourselves , and at each other . " 
 " if your charmer hold out , " say St. John , " at this rate she may keep you twenty year besiege she , and surrender by the time you be seventy , and she be old enough to be a grandmother . I do not say the pursuit of a particular woman be not as pleasant a pastime as any other kind of hunting , " he add ; " only , for my part , I find the game will not   run long enough . they knock under too soon that be the fault I find with them . " 
 " the game which you pursue be in the habit of be catch , and use to be pull down , " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " but Dulcinea del Toboso be peerless , eh ? " say the other . " well , honest Harry , go and attack windmill perhaps thou art not more mad than other people , " St. John add , with a sigh . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 a paper out of the " SPECTATOR . " 

 Doth any young gentleman of my progeny , who may read his old grandfather 's paper , chance to be presently suffer under the passion of Love ? there be a humiliating cure , but one that be easy and almost specific for the malady which be , to try an alibi . esmond go away from his mistress and be cure a half - dozen time ; he come back to her side , and instantly fall ill again of the fever . he vow that he could leave she and think no more of she , and so he could pretty well , at least , succeed in quell that rage and longing he have whenever he be with she ; but as soon as he return he be as bad as ever again . truly a ludicrous and pitiable object , at least exhaust everybody 's pity but his dear mistress 's , Lady Castlewood 's , in whose tender breast he repose all his dreary confession , and who never tired of hear he and plead for he . 
 sometimes Esmond would think there be hope . then again he would be plague with despair , at some impertinence or coquetry of his mistress . for day they would be like brother and sister , or the dear friend she , simple , fond , and charming he , happy beyond measure at her good behavior . but this would all vanish on a sudden . either he would be too pressing , and hint his love , when she would rebuff he instantly , and give his vanity a box on the ear ; or he would be jealous , and with perfect good reason , of some new admirer that have spring up , or some rich young gentleman newly arrive in the town , that this incorrigible flirt would set her net and bait to draw in . if Esmond remonstrate , the little rebel would say " who be you ? I shall go my own way , sirrah , and that way be towards a husband , and I do not   want you on the way . I be for your better , Colonel , for your better : do you hear that ? you might do if you have an estate and be young ; only eight year old than I , you say ! pish , you be a hundred year old . you be an old , old Graveairs , and I should make you miserable , that would be the only comfort I should have in marry you . but you have not money enough to keep a cat decently after you have pay your man his wage , and your landlady her bill . do you think I be go to live in a lodging , and turn the mutton at a string whilst your honor nurse the baby ? Fiddlestick , and why do you not get this nonsense knock out of your head when you be in the war ? you be come back more dismal and dreary than ever . you and mamma be fit for each other . you might be Darby and Joan , and play cribbage to the end of your life . " 
 " at least you own to your worldliness , my poor Trix , " say her mother . 
 " worldliness . oh , my pretty lady ! do you think that I be a child in the nursery , and to be frighten by Bogey ! worldliness , to be sure ; and pray , madam , where be the harm of wish to be comfortable ? when you be go , you dearest old woman , or when I be tired of you and have run away from you , where shall I go ? shall I go and be head nurse to my popish sister - in - law , take the child their physic , and whip them , and put them to bed when they be naughty ? shall I be Castlewood 's upper servant , and perhaps marry Tom Tusher ? merci ! I have be long enough Frank 's humble servant . why be I not a man ? I have ten time his brain , and have I wear the well , do not   let your ladyship be frightened have I wear a sword and periwig instead of this mantle and commode to which nature have condemn I   though ' tis a pretty stuff , too Cousin Esmond ! you will go to the Exchange to - morrow , and get the exact counterpart of this ribbon , sir ; do you hear ?   I would have make our name talk about . so would Graveairs here have make something out of our name if he have represent it . my Lord Graveairs would have do very well . yes , you have a very pretty way , and would have make a very decent , grave speaker . " and here she begin to imitate Esmond 's way of carry himself and speak to his face , and so ludicrously that his mistress burst out a - laugh , and even he himself could see there be some likeness in the fantastical malicious caricature . 
 " yes , " say she , " I solemnly vow , own , and confess , that I want a good husband . where be the harm of one ? my face be my fortune . who will come ? buy , buy , buy ! I can not toil , neither can I spin , but I can play twenty - three game on the card . I can dance the last dance , I can hunt the stag , and I think I could shoot fly . I can talk as wicked as any woman of my year , and know enough story to amuse a sulky husband for at least one thousand and one night . I have a pretty taste for dress , diamond , gambling , and old China . I love sugar - plum , Malines lace   that you bring I , cousin , be very pretty , the opera , and everything that be useless and costly . I have get a monkey and a little black boy Pompey , sir , go and give a dish of chocolate to Colonel Graveairs , and a parrot and a spaniel , and I must have a husband . Cupid , you hear ? " 
 " Iss , Missis ! " say Pompey , a little grinning negro Lord Peterborrow give she , with a bird of Paradise in his turbant , and a collar with his mistress 's name on it . 
 " Iss , Missis ! " say beatrix , imitate the child . " and if husband not come , Pompey must go fetch one . " 
 and Pompey go away grin with his chocolate tray as Miss Beatrix run up to her mother and end her sally of mischief in her common way , with a kiss no wonder that upon pay such a penalty her fond judge pardon she . 
 when Mr. Esmond come home , his health be still shatter ; and he take a lodging near to his mistress , at Kensington , glad enough to be serve by they , and to see they day after day . he be enable to see a little company and of the sort he like well . Mr. Steele and Mr. Addison both do he the honor to visit he ; and drink many a glass of good claret at his lodging , whilst their entertainer , through his wound , be keep to diet drink and gruel . these gentleman be Whigs , and great admirer of my Lord Duke of Marlborough ; and Esmond be entirely of the other party . but their different view of politic do not prevent the gentleman from agree in private , nor from allow , on one evening when Esmond 's kind old patron , Lieutenant - General Webb , with a stick and a crutch , hobble up to the Colonel 's lodging   which be prettily situate at Knightsbridge , between London and Kensington , and look over the Gardens , that the Lieutenant - General be a noble and gallant soldier and even that he have be hardly use in the Wynendael affair . he take his revenge in talk , that must be confess ; and if Mr. Addison have have a mind to write a poem about Wynendael , he might have hear from the commander 's own lip the story a hundred time over . 
 Mr. Esmond , force to be quiet , betook himself to literature for a relaxation , and compose his comedy , whereof the prompter 's copy lieth in my walnut escritoire , seal up and docket , " the Faithful Fool , a Comedy , as it be perform by her Majesty 's servant . " ' twas a very sentimental piece ; and Mr. Steele , who have more of that kind of sentiment than Mr. Addison , admire it , whilst the other rather sneered at the performance ; though he own that , here and there , it contain some pretty stroke . he be bring out his own play of " Cato " at the time , the blaze of which quite extinguish Esmond 's farthe candle ; and his name be never put to the piece , which be print as by a Person of Quality . only nine copy be sell , though Mr. Dennis , the great critic , praise it , and say ' twas a work of great merit ; and Colonel Esmond have the whole impression burn one day in a rage , by Jack Lockwood , his man . 
 all this comedy be full of bitter satiric stroke against a certain young lady . the plot of the piece be quite a new one . a young woman be represent with a great number of suitor , select a pert fribble of a peer , in place of the hero   but ill - act , I think , by Mr. Wilks , the Faithful Fool ,   who persist in admire she . in the fifth act , Teraminta be make to discover the merit of Eugenio   the F. F. , and to feel a partiality for he too late ; for he announce that he have bestow his hand and estate upon Rosaria , a country lass , endow with every virtue . but it must be own that the audience yawn through the play ; and that it perish on the third night , with only half a dozen person to behold its agony . esmond and his two mistress come to the first night , and Miss Beatrix fall asleep ; whilst her mother , who have not be to a play since King James the Second 's time , think the piece , though not brilliant , have a very pretty moral . 
 Mr. Esmond dabble in letter , and write a deal of prose and verse at this time of leisure . when displease with the conduct of Miss Beatrix , he would compose a satire , in which he relieve his mind . when smart under the faithlessness of woman , he dash off a copy of verse , in which he hold the whole sex up to scorn . one day , in one of these mood , he make a little joke , in which   swear he to secrecy   he get his friend Dick Steele to help he ; and , compose a paper , he have it print exactly like Steele 's paper , and by his printer , and lay on his mistress 's breakfast - table the follow 
  " SPECTATOR . 

  " no . 341 . " Tuesday , April 1 , 1712 . 

  Mutato nomine de te Fabula narratur . HORACE . 
  thyself the morain of the fable see . CREECH . 
 " Jocasta be know as a woman of learning and fashion , and as one of the most amiable person of this court and country . she be at home two morning of the week , and all the wit and a few of the beauty of London flock to her assembly . when she go abroad to Tunbridge or the Bath , a retinue of adorer rid the journey with she ; and besides the London beaux , she have a crowd of admirer at the Wells , the polite amongst the native of Sussex and Somerset press round her tea - table , and be anxious for a nod from her chair . Jocasta 's acquaintance be thus very numerous . indeed , ' tis one smart writer 's work to keep her visit - book a strong footman be engage to carry it ; and it would require a much strong head even than Jocasta 's own to remember the name of all her dear friend . 
 " either at Epsom Wells or at Tunbridge   for of this important matter Jocasta can not be certain   it be her ladyship 's fortune to become acquainted with a young gentleman , whose conversation be so sprightly , and manner amiable , that she invite the agreeable young spark to visit she if ever he come to London , where her house in Spring Garden should be open to he . Charming as he be , and without any manner of doubt a pretty fellow , Jocasta hath such a regiment of the like continually march round her standard , that ' tis no wonder her attention be distract amongst they . and so , though this gentleman make a considerable impression upon she , and touch her heart for at least three - and - twenty minute , it must be own that she have forget his name . he be a dark man , and may be eight - and - twenty year old . his dress be sober , though of rich material . he have a mole on his forehead over his left eye ; have a blue ribbon to his cane and sword , and wear his own hair . 
 " Jocasta be much flatter by behold her admirer   for that everybody admire who see she be a point which she never can for a moment doubt   in the next pew to she at St. James 's Church last Sunday ; and the manner in which he appear to go to sleep during the sermon though from under his fringed eyelid it be evident he be cast glance of respectful rapture towards Jocasta deeply move and interested she . on come out of church , he find his way to her chair , and make she an elegant bow as she step into it . she see he at Court afterwards , where he carry himself with a most distinguished air , though none of her acquaintance know his name ; and the next night he be at the play , where her ladyship be pleased to acknowledge he from the side - box . 
 " during the whole of the comedy she rack her brain so to remember his name that she do not hear a word of the piece : and have the happiness to meet he once more in the lobby of the playhouse , she go up to he in a flutter , and bid he remember that she keep two night in the week , and that she long to see he at Spring Garden . 
 " he appear on Tuesday , in a rich suit , show a very fine taste both in the tailor and wearer ; and though a knot of we be gather round the charming Jocasta , fellow who pretend to know every face upon the town , not one could tell the gentleman 's name in reply to Jocasta 's eager inquiry , fling to the right and leave of she as he advance up the room with a bow that would become a duke . 
 " Jocasta acknowledge this salute with one of those smile and curtsy of which that lady hath the secret . she curtsy with a languish air , as if to say , ' you be come at last . I have be pin for you : ' and then she finish her victim with a kill look , which declare : ' o philander ! I have no eye but for you . ' Camilla hath as good a curtsy perhaps , and Thalestris much such another look ; but the glance and the curtsy together belong to Jocasta of all the english beauty alone . 
 " ' welcome to London , sir , ' say she . ' one can see you be from the country by your look . ' she would have say ' Epsom , ' or ' Tunbridge , ' have she remember rightly at which place she have meet the strange ; but , alas ! she have forget . 
 " the gentleman say , ' he have be in town but three day ; and one of his reason for come hither be to have the honor of pay his court to Jocasta . ' 
 " she say , ' the water have agree with she but indifferently . ' 
 " ' the water be for the sick , ' the gentleman say : ' the young and beautiful come but to make they sparkle . and as the clergyman read the service on Sunday , ' he add , ' your ladyship remind I of the angel that visit the pool . ' a murmur of approbation salute this sally . Manilio , who be a wit when he be not at card , be in such a rage that he revoke when he hear it . 
 " Jocasta be an angel visit the water ; but at which of the Bethesdas ? she be puzzle more and more ; and , as her way always be , look the more innocent and simple , the more artful her intention be . 
 " ' we be discourse , ' say she , ' about spelling of name and word when you come . why should we say goold and write gold , and call china chayney , and Cavendish Candish , and Cholmondeley Chumley ? if we call Pulteney Poltney , why should not   we call poultry pultry and ' 
 " ' such an enchantress as your ladyship , ' say he , ' be mistress of all sort of spell . ' but this be Dr. Swift 's pun , and we all know it . 
 " ' and and how do you spell your name ? ' say she , come to the point at length ; for this sprightly conversation have last much long than be here set down , and be carry on through at least three dish of tea . 
 " ' oh , madam , ' say he , ' I spell my name with the Y. ' and lay down his dish , my gentleman make another elegant bow , and be go in a moment . 
 " Jocasta hath have no sleep since this mortification , and the stranger 's disappearance . if balk in anything , she be sure to lose her health and temper ; and we , her servant , suffer , as usual , during the angry fit of our Queen . can you help we , Mr. Spectator , who know everything , to read this riddle for she , and set at rest all our mind ? we find in her list , Mr. Berty , Mr. Smith , Mr. Pike , Mr. Tyler who may be Mr. Bertie , Mr. Smyth , Mr. Pyke , Mr. Tiler , for what we know . she hath turn away the clerk of her visit - book , a poor fellow with a great family of child . read I this riddle , good Mr. Shortface , and oblige your admirer oedipus . " 
 " the TRUMPET COFFEE - HOUSE , WHITEHALL . 
 " MR . SPECTATOR , I be a gentleman but little acquainted with the town , though I have have a university education , and pass some year serve my country abroad , where my name be well know than in the coffee - house and St. James 's . 
 " two year since my uncle die , leave I a pretty estate in the county of Kent ; and be at Tunbridge Wells last summer , after my mourning be over , and on the look - out , if truth must be tell , for some young lady who would share with I the solitude of my great kentish house , and be kind to my tenantry   for whom a woman can do a great deal more good than the well - intentione man can , I be greatly fascinate by a young lady of London , who be the toast of all the company at the Wells . every one know Saccharissa 's beauty ; and I think , Mr. Spectator , no one well than herself . 
 " my table - book inform I that I dance no less than seven - and - twenty set with she at the Assembly . I treat she to the fiddle twice . I be admit on several day to her lodging , and receive by she with a great deal of distinction , and , for a time , be entirely her slave . it be only when I find , from common talk of the company at the Wells , and from narrowly watch one , who I once think of ask the most sacred question a man can put to a woman , that I become aware how unfit she be to be a country gentleman 's wife ; and that this fair creature be but a heartless worldly jilt , play with affection that she never mean to return , and , indeed , incapable of return they . ' tis admiration such woman want , not love that touch they ; and I can conceive , in her old age , no more wretched creature than this lady will be , when her beauty hath desert she , when her admirer have leave she , and she hath neither friendship nor religion to console she . 
 " business call I to London , I go to St. James 's church last Sunday , and there opposite I sit my beauty of the Wells . her behavior during the whole service be so pert , languish , and absurd ; she flirt her fan , and ogle and eye I in a manner so indecent , that I be oblige to shut my eye , so as actually not to see she , and whenever I open they beheld hers   and very bright they be   still star at I . I fall in with she afterwards at Court , and at the playhouse ; and here nothing would satisfy she but she must elbow through the crowd and speak to I , and invite I to the assembly , which she hold at her house , not very far from Ch - r - ng Cr - ss . 
 " have make she a promise to attend , of course I keep my promise ; and find the young widow in the midst of a half - dozen of card table , and a crowd of wit and admirer . I make the good bow I could , and advance towards she ; and see by a peculiar puzzled look in her face , though she try to hide her perplexity , that she have forget even my name . 
 " her talk , artful as it be , convince I that I have guess aright . she turn the conversation most ridiculously upon the spelling of name and word ; and I reply with as ridiculous fulsome compliment as I could pay she : indeed , one in which I compare she to an angel visit the sick well , go a little too far ; nor should I have employ it , but that the allusion come from the Second Lesson last Sunday , which we both have hear , and I be press to answer she . 
 " then she come to the question , which I know be await I , and ask how I spelt my name ? ' Madam , ' say I , turn on my heel , ' I spell it with a Y. ' and so I leave she , wonder at the light - heartedness of the town - people , who forget and make friend so easily , and resolve to look elsewhere for a partner for your constant reader , 
 " CYMON WYLDOATS . " 
 " you know my real name , Mr. Spectator , in which there be no such a letter as HUPSILON . but if the lady , whom I have call Saccharissa , wonder that I appear no more at the tea - table , she be hereby respectfully inform the reason Y. " 
 the above be a parable , whereof the writer will now expound the meaning . Jocasta be no other than Miss Esmond , Maid of Honor to her Majesty . she have tell Mr. Esmond this little story of have meet a gentleman somewhere , and forget his name , when the gentleman , with no such malicious intention as those of " Cymon " in the above fable , make the answer simply as above ; and we all laugh to think how little mistress Jocasta - Beatrix have profit by her artifice and precaution . 
 as for Cymon , he be intend to represent yours and her very humble servant , the writer of the apologue and of this story , which we have print on a " Spectator " paper at Mr. Steele 's office , exactly as those famous journal be print , and which be lay on the table at breakfast in place of the real newspaper . Mistress Jocasta , who have plenty of wit , could not live without her Spectator to her tea ; and this sham Spectator be intend to convey to the young woman that she herself be a flirt , and that Cymon be a gentleman of honor and resolution , see all her fault , and determine to break the chain once and for ever . 
 for though enough hath be say about this love - business already enough , at least , to prove to the writer 's heir what a silly fond fool their old grandfather be , who would like they to consider he as a very wise old gentleman ; yet not near all have be tell concern this matter , which , if it be allow to take in Esmond 's journal the space it occupy in his time , would weary his kinsman and woman of a hundred year ' time beyond all endurance ; and form such a diary of folly and drivel , rapture and rage , as no man of ordinary vanity would like to leave behind he . 
 the truth be , that , whether she laugh at he or encourage he ; whether she smile or be cold , and turn her smile on another ; worldly and ambitious , as he know she to be ; hard and careless , as she seem to grow with her court life , and a hundred admirer that come to she and leave she ; Esmond , do what he would , never could get beatrix out of his mind ; think of she constantly at home or away . if he read his name in a Gazette , or escape the shot of a cannon - ball or a great danger in the campaign , as have happen to he more than once , the instant thought after the honor achieve or the danger avoid , be , " what will she say of it ? " " will this distinction or the idea of this peril elate she or touch she , so as to be well inclined towards I ? " he could no more help this passionate fidelity of temper than he could help the eye he see with one or the other seem a part of his nature ; and know every one of her fault as well as the keenest of her detractor , and the folly of an attachment to such a woman , of which the fruition could never bring he happiness for above a week , there be yet a charm about this Circe from which the poor deluded gentleman could not free himself ; and for a much long period than Ulysses   another middle - aged officer , who have travel much , and be in the foreign war ,   Esmond feel himself enthral and besotte by the wile of this enchantress . quit she ! he could no more quit she , as the Cymon of this story be make to quit his false one , than he could lose his consciousness of yesterday . she have but to raise her finger , and he would come back from ever so far ; she have but to say I have discard such and such an adorer , and the poor infatuate wretch would be sure to come and roder about her mother 's house , willing to be put on the rank of suitor , though he know he might be cast off the next week . if he be like Ulysses in his folly , at least she be in so far like Penelope that she have a crowd of suitor , and undo day after day and night after night the handiwork of fascination and the web of coquetry with which she be will not to allure and entertain they . 
 part of her coquetry may have come from her position about the Court , where the beautiful maid of honor be the light about which a thousand beaux come and flutter ; where she be sure to have a ring of admirer round she , crowd to listen to her repartee as much as to admire her beauty ; and where she speak and listen to much free talk , such as one never would have think the lip or ear of Rachel Castlewood 's daughter would have utter or hear . when in wait at Windsor or Hampton , the Court lady and gentleman would be make rid party together ; Mrs. Beatrix in a horseman 's coat and hat , the foremost after the stag - hound and over the park fence , a crowd of young fellow at her heel . if the english country lady at this time be the most pure and modest of any lady in the world the english town and court lady permit themselves word and behavior that be neither modest nor pure ; and claim , some of they , a freedom which those who love that sex most would never wish to grant they . the gentleman of my family that follow after I   for I do not   encourage the lady to pursue any such study , may read in the work of Mr. Congreve , and Dr. Swift and other , what be the conversation and what the habit of our time . 
 the most beautiful woman in England in 1712 , when Esmond return to this country , a lady of high birth , and though of no fortune to be sure , with a thousand fascination of wit and manner , Beatrix Esmond be now six - and - twenty year old , and Beatrix Esmond still . of her hundred adorer she have not choose one for a husband ; and those who have ask have be jilt by she ; and more still have leave she . a succession of near ten year ' crop of beauty have come up since her time , and have be reap by proper HUSBANDmen , if we may make an agricultural simile , and have be house comfortably long ago . her own contemporary be sober mother by this time ; girl with not a tithe of her charm , or her wit , have make good match , and now claim precedence over the spinster who but lately have deride and outshone they . the young beauty be begin to look down on Beatrix as an old maid , and sneer , and call she one of Charles II . 's lady , and ask whether her portrait be not in the Hampton Court Gallery ? but still she reign , at least in one man 's opinion , superior over all the little miss that be the toast of the young lad ; and in Esmond 's eye be ever perfectly lovely and young . 
 who know how many be nearly make happy by possess she , or , rather , how many be fortunate in escape this siren ? ' tis a marvel to think that her mother be the pure and simple woman in the whole world , and that this girl should have be bear from she . I be inclined to fancy , my mistress , who never say a harsh word to her child   and but twice or thrice only to one person , must have be too fond and press with the maternal authority ; for her son and her daughter both revolt early ; nor after their first flight from the nest could they ever be bring back quite to the fond mother 's bosom . Lady Castlewood , and perhaps it be as well , know little of her daughter 's life and real thought . how be she to apprehend what pass in Queen 's ante - chamber and at Court table ? Mrs. Beatrix assert her own authority so resolutely that her mother quickly give in . the maid of honor have her own equipage ; go from home and come back at her own will : her mother be alike powerless to resist she or to lead she , or to command or to persuade she . 
 she have be engage once , twice , thrice , to be marry , Esmond believe . when he quit home , it hath be say , she be promise to my Lord Ashburnham , and now , on his return , behold his lordship be just married to Lady Mary Butler , the Duke of Ormonde 's daughter , and his fine house , and twelve thousand a year of fortune , for which Miss Beatrix have rather covet he , be out of her power . to her Esmond could say nothing in regard to the breaking of this match ; and , ask his mistress about it , all Lady Castlewood answer be : " do not speak to I about it , Harry . I can not tell you how or why they part , and I fear to inquire . I have tell you before , that with all her kindness , and wit , and generosity , and that sort of splendor of nature she have , I can say but little good of poor beatrix , and look with dread at the marriage she will form . her mind be fix on ambition only , and make a great figure ; and , this achieve , she will tire of it as she do of everything . Heaven help her husband , whoever he shall be ! my Lord Ashburnham be a most excellent young man , gentle and yet manly , of very good part , so they tell I , and as my little conversation would enable I to judge : and a kind temper kind and endure I be sure he must have be , from all that he have to endure . but he quit she at last , from some crown piece of caprice or tyranny of her ; and now he have marry a young woman that will make he a thousand time happy than my poor girl ever could . " 
 the rupture , whatever its cause be ,   I hear the scandal , but indeed shall not take pain to repeat at length in this diary the trumpery coffee - house story ,   cause a good deal of low talk ; and Mr. Esmond be present at my lord 's appearance at the birthday with his bride , over whom the revenge that beatrix take be to look so imperial and lovely that the modest downcast young lady could not appear beside she , and Lord Ashburnham , who have his reason for wish to avoid she , slunk away quite shamefaced , and very early . this time his grace the Duke of Hamilton , whom Esmond have see about she before , be constant at Miss Beatrix 's side : he be one of the most splendid gentleman of Europe , accomplish by book , by travel , by long command of the good company , distinguish as a statesman , have be ambassador in King Williamn 's time , and a noble speaker in the Scots ' Parliament , where he have lead the party that be against the Union , and though now five or six and forty year of age , a gentleman so high in stature , accomplish in wit , and favor in person , that he might pretend to the hand of any Princess in Europe . 
 " should you like the Duke for a cousin ? " say Mr. Secretary St. John , whisper to Colonel Esmond in French ; " it appear that the widower console himself . " 
 but to return to our little Spectator paper and the conversation which grow out of it . Miss Beatrix at first be quite bit   as the phrase of that day be   and do not ' smoke " the authorship of the story ; indeed Esmond have try to imitate as well as he could Mr. Steele 's manner   as for the other author of the Spectator , his prose style I think be altogether inimitable ; and Dick , who be the idle and good - natured of man , would have let the piece pass into his journal and go to posterity as one of his own lucubration , but that Esmond do not care to have a lady 's name whom he love send forth to the world in a light so unfavorable . beatrix pishe and psha'd over the paper ; Colonel Esmond watch with no little interest her countenance as she read it . 
 " how stupid your friend Mr. Steele become ! " cry Miss Beatrix . " Epsom and Tunbridge ! will he never have do with Epsom and Tunbridge , and with beaux at church , and Jocastas and lindamira ? why do he not call woman Nelly and Betty , as their godfather and godmother do for they in their baptism ? " 
 " beatrix . beatrix ! " say her mother , ' speak gravely of grave thing . " 
 " Mamma think the Church Catechism come from heaven , I believe , " say beatrix , with a laugh , " and be bring down by a bishop from a mountain . oh , how I use to break my heart over it ! besides , I have a popish godmother , mamma ; why do you give I one ? " 
 " I give you the Queen 's name , " say her mother blushing . " and a very pretty name it be , " say somebody else . 
 beatrix go on read " spell my name with a Y why , you wretch , " say she , turn round to Colonel Esmond , " you have be tell my story to Mr. Steele or stop you have write the paper yourself to turn I into ridicule . for shame , sir ! " 
 poor Mr. Esmond feel rather frightened , and tell a truth , which be nevertheless an entire falsehood . " upon my honor , " say he , " I have not even read the Spectator of this morning . " nor have he , for that be not the Spectator , but a sham newspaper put in its place . 
 she go on reading : her face rather flush as she read . " no , " she say , " I think you could not   have write it . I think it must have be Mr. Steele when he be drunk and afraid of his horrid vulgar wife . whenever I see an enormous compliment to a woman , and some outrageous panegyric about female virtue , I always feel sure that the Captain and his well half have fall out over - night , and that he have be bring home tipsy , or have be find out in " 
 " beatrix ! " cry the Lady Castlewood . 
 " well , mamma ! do not cry out before you be hurt . I be not go to say anything wrong . I will not   give you more annoyance than you can help , you pretty kind mamma . yes , and your little Trix be a naughty little Trix , and she leave undone those thing which she ought to have do , and do those thing which she ought not to have do , and there be well now I will not   go on . yes , I will , unless you kiss I . " and with this the young lady lay aside her paper , and run up to her mother and perform a variety of embrace with her ladyship , say as plain as eye could speak to Mr. Esmond " there , sir : would not you like to play the very same pleasant game ? " 
 " indeed , madam , I would , " say he . 
 " would what ? " ask Miss Beatrix . 
 " what you mean when you look at I in that provoking way , " answer Esmond . 
 " what a confessor ! " cry beatrix , with a laugh . 
 " what be it Henry would like , my dear ? " ask her mother , the kind soul , who be always think what we would like , and how she could please we . 
 the girl run up to her " oh , you silly kind mamma , " she say , kiss she again , " that be what Harry would like ; " and she break out into a great joyful laugh ; and Lady Castlewood blush as bashful as a maid of sixteen . 
 " look at she , Harry , " whisper Beatrix , run up , and speak in her sweet low tone . " do not   the blush become she ? be not   she pretty ? she look young than I be , and I be sure she be a hundred million thousand time well . " 
 Esmond 's kind mistress leave the room , carry she blush away with she . 
 " if we girl at Court could grow such rose as that , " continue beatrix , with her laugh , " what would not   we do to preserve them ? we would clip their stalk and put them in salt and water . but those flower do not   bloom at Hampton Court and Windsor , Henry . " she pause for a minute , and the smile fade away from her April face , give place to a menacing shower of tear ; " oh , how good she be , Harry , " beatrix go on to say . " oh , what a saint she be ! her goodness frighten I . I be not fit to live with she . I should be well I think if she be not so perfect . she have have a great sorrow in her life , and a great secret ; and repent of it . it could not have be my father 's death . she talk freely about that ; nor could she have love he very much though who know what we woman do love , and why ? " 
 " what , and why , indeed , " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " no one know , " beatrix go on , without notice this interruption except by a look , " what my mother 's life be . she hath be at early prayer this morning ; she pass hour in her closet ; if you be to follow her thither , you would find she at prayer now . she tend the poor of the place the horrid dirty poor ! she sit through the curate 's sermon oh , those dreary sermon ! and you see on a beau dire ; but good as they be , people like she be not fit to commune with we of the world . there be always , as it be , a third person present , even when I and my mother be alone . she can not   be frank with I quite ; who be always think of the next world , and of her guardian angel , perhaps that be in company . oh , Harry , I be jealous of that guardian angel ! " here break out Mistress beatrix . " it be horrid , I know ; but my mother 's life be all for heaven , and mine all for earth . we can never be friend quite ; and then , she care more for Frank 's little finger than she do for I I know she do : and she love you , sir , a great deal too much ; and I hate you for it . I would have have she all to myself ; but she would not . in my childhood , it be my father she love   oh , how could she ? I remember he kind and handsome , but so stupid , and not be able to speak after drink wine . and then it be Frank ; and now , it be heaven and the clergyman . how I would have love she ! from a child I use to be in a rage that she love anybody but I ; but she love you all well all , I know she do . and now , she talk of the bless consolation of religion . dear soul ! she think she be happy for believe , as she must , that we be all of we wicked and miserable sinner ; and this world be only a pie - a - terre for the good , where they stay for a night , as we do , come from Walcote , at that great , dreary , uncomfortable Hounslow Inn , in those horrid bed oh , do you remember those horrid bed ? and the chariot come and fetch they to heaven the next morning . " 
 " hush , beatrix , " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " Hush , indeed . you be a hypocrite , too , Henry , with your grave air and your glum face . we be all hypocrite . o dear I ! we be all alone , alone , alone , " say poor beatrix , her fair breast heave with a sigh . 
 " it be I that writ every line of that paper , my dear , " say Mr. Esmond . " you be not so worldly as you think yourself , beatrix , and well than we believe you . the good we have in we we doubt of ; and the happiness that be to our hand we throw away . you bend your ambition on a great marriage and establishment and why ? you will tire of they when you win they ; and be no happy with a coronet on your coach " 
 " than rid pillion with Lubin to market , " say beatrix . " thank you , Lubin ! " 
 " I be a dismal shepherd , to be sure , " answer Esmond , with a blush ; " and require a nymph that can tuck my bed - clothe up , and make I water - gruel . well , Tom Lockwood can do that . he take I out of the fire upon his shoulder , and nurse I through my illness as love will scarce ever do . only good wage , and a hope of my clothe , and the content of my portmanteau . how long be it that Jacob serve an apprenticeship for Rachel ? " 
 " for mamma ? " say beatrix . " it be mamma your honor want , and that I should have the happiness of call you papa ? " 
 Esmond blush again . " I speak of a Rachel that a shepherd court five thousand year ago ; when shepherd be long live than now . and my meaning be , that since I see you first after our separation a child you be then . . . " 
 " and I put on my good stocking to captivate you , I remember , sir . . . " 
 " you have have my heart ever since then , such as it be ; and such as you be , I care for no other woman . what little reputation I have win , it be that you might be pleased with it : and indeed , it be not much ; and I think a hundred fool in the army have get and deserve quite as much . be there something in the air of that dismal old Castlewood that make we all gloomy , and dissatisfied , and lonely under its ruin old roof ? we be all so , even when together and unite , as it seem , follow our separate scheme , each as we sit round the table . " 
 " dear , dreary old place ! " cry beatrix . " Mamma hath never have the heart to go back thither since we leave it , when never mind how many year ago . " and she fling back her curl , and look over her fair shoulder at the mirror superbly , as if she say , " Time , I defy you . " 
 " yes , " say Esmond , who have the art , as she own , of divine many of her thought . " you can afford to look in the glass still ; and only be pleased by the truth it tell you . as for I , do you know what my scheme be ? I think of ask Frank to give I the virginian estate King Charles give our grandfather .   she give a superb curtsy , as much as to say , ' our grandfather , indeed ! thank you , Mr. Bastard . '   yes , I know you be think of my bar - sinister , and so be I. a man can not get over it in this country ; unless , indeed , he wear it across a king 's arm , when ' tis a highly honorable coat ; and I be think of retire into the plantation , and build myself a wigwam in the wood , and perhaps , if I want company , suit myself with a squaw . we will send your ladyship fur over for the winter ; and , when you be old , we will provide you with tobacco . I be not quite clever enough , or not rogue enough I know not which for the Old World . I may make a place for myself in the New , which be not so full ; and find a family there . when you be a mother yourself , and a great lady , perhaps I shall send you over from the plantation some day a little barbarian that be half esmond half Mohock , and you will be kind to he for his father 's sake , who be , after all , your kinsman ; and whom you love a little . " 
 " what folly you be talk , Harry , " say Miss Beatrix , look with her great eye . 
 " ' tis sober earnest , " say Esmond . and , indeed , the scheme have be dwell a good deal in his mind for some time past , and especially since his return home , when he find how hopeless , and even degrade to himself , his passion be . " no , " say he , then : " I have try half a dozen time now . I can bear be away from you well enough ; but be with you be intolerable "   another low curtsy on Mistress Beatrix 's part , " and I will go . I have enough to buy axis and gun for my man , and bead and blanket for the savage ; and I will go and live amongst they . " 
 " Mon ami , " she say quite kindly , and take Esmond 's hand , with an air of great compassion , " you can not   think that in our position anything more than our present friendship be possible . you be our eld brother as such we view you , pity your misfortune , not rebuke you with it . why , you be old enough and grave enough to be our father . I always think you a hundred year old , Harry , with your solemn face and grave air . I feel as a sister to you , and can no more . be not   that enough , sir ? " and she put her face quite close to his who know with what intention ? 
 " it be too much , " say Esmond , turn away . " I can not   bear this life , and shall leave it . I shall stay , I think , to see you married , and then freight a ship , and call it the ' beatrix , ' and bid you all . . . " 
 here the servant , fling the door open , announce his grace the Duke of Hamilton , and Esmond start back with something like an imprecation on his lip , as the nobleman enter , look splendid in his star and green ribbon . he give Mr. Esmond just that gracious bow which he would have give to a lackey who fetch he a chair or take his hat , and seat himself by Miss Beatrix , as the poor Colonel go out of the room with a hang - dog look . 
 Esmond 's mistress be in the low room as he pass down stair . she often meet he as he be come away from Beatrix ; and she beckon he into the apartment . 
 " have she tell you , Harry ? " Lady Castlewood say . 
 " she have be very frank very , " say Esmond . 
 " but but about what be go to happen ? " 
 " what be go to happen ? " say he , his heart beat . 
 " his grace the Duke of Hamilton have propose to she , " say my lady . " he make his offer yesterday . they will marry as soon as his mourning be over ; and you have hear his grace be appoint Ambassador to Paris ; and the Ambassadress go with he . " 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 BEATRIX be new suitor . 

 the gentleman whom Beatrix have select be , to be sure , twenty year old than the Colonel , with whom she quarrel for be too old ; but this one be but a nameless adventurer , and the other the great duke in Scotland , with pretension even to a still high title . my Lord Duke of Hamilton have , indeed , every merit belong to a gentleman , and he have have the time to mature his accomplishment fully , be upwards of fifty year old when Madam Beatrix select he for a bridegroom . Duke Hamilton , then Earl of Arran , have be educate at the famous Scottish university of Glasgow , and , come to London , become a great favorite of Charles the Second , who make he a lord of his bedchamber , and afterwards appoint he ambassador to the french king , under whom the Earl serve two campaign as his Majesty 's aide - de - camp ; and he be absent on this service when King Charles die . 
 King James continue my lord 's promotion make he Master of the Wardrobe and Colonel of the Royal Regiment of Horse ; and his lordship adhere firmly to King James , be of the small company that never quit that unfortunate monarch till his departure out of England ; and then it be , in 1688 namely , that he make the friendship with Colonel Francis Esmond , that have always be , more or less , maintain in the two family . 
 the Earl profess a great admiration for King William always , but never could give he his allegiance ; and be engage in more than one of the plot in the late great King 's reign which always end in the plotter ' discomfiture , and generally in their pardon , by the magnanimity of the King . Lord Arran be twice prisoner in the Tower during this reign , undauntedly say , when offer his release , upon parole not to engage against King William , that he would not give his word , because " he be sure he could not keep it ; " but , nevertheless , he be both time discharge without any trial ; and the King bear this noble enemy so little malice , that when his mother , the Duchess of Hamilton , of her own right , resign her claim on her husband 's death , the Earl be , by patent sign at Loo , 1690 , create Duke of Hamilton , Marquis of Clydesdale , and Earl of Arran , with precedency from the original creation . his grace take the oath and his seat in the scottish parliament in 1700 : be famous there for his patriotism and eloquence , especially in the debate about the Union Bill , which Duke Hamilton oppose with all his strength , though he would not go the length of the scottish gentry , who be for resist it by force of arm . ' Twas say he withdraw his opposition all of a sudden , and in consequence of letter from the King at St. Germains , who entreat he on his allegiance not to thwart the Queen his sister in this measure ; and the Duke , be always bent upon effect the King 's return to his kingdom through a reconciliation between his Majesty and Queen Anne , and quite averse to his landing with arm and french troop , hold aloof , and keep out of Scotland during the time when the Chevalier de St. George 's descent from Dunkirk be project , pass his time in England in his great estate in Staffordshire . 
 when the Whigs go out of office in 1710 , the Queen begin to show his grace the very great mark of her favor . he be create Duke of Brandon and Baron of Dutton in England ; have the Thistle already originally bestow on he by King James the Second , his grace be now promote to the honor of the Garter a distinction so great and illustrious , that no subject hath ever bear they hitherto together . when this objection be make to her Majesty , she be pleased to say , " such a subject as the Duke of Hamilton have a pre - eminent claim to every mark of distinction which a crown head can confer . I will henceforth wear both order myself . " 
 at the chapter hold at Windsor in October , 1712 , the Duke and other knight , include Lord - Treasurer , the new - create Earl of Oxford and Mortimer , be instal ; and a few day afterwards his grace be appoint Ambassador - Extraordinary to France , and his equipage , plate , and livery command , of the most sumptuous kind , not only for his Excellency the Ambassador , but for her Excellency the Ambassadress , who be to accompany he . her arm be already quarter on the coach panel , and her brother be to hasten over on the appoint day to give she away . 
 his lordship be a widower , have marry , in 1698 , Elizabeth , daughter of Digby Lord Gerard , by which marriage great estate come into the Hamilton family ; and out of these estate come , in part , that tragic quarrel which end the Duke 's career . 
 from the loss of a tooth to that of a mistress there be no pang that be not bearable . the apprehension be much more cruel than the certainty ; and we make up our mind to the misfortune when ' tis irremediable , part with the tormentor , and mumble our crust on t'other side of the jaw . I think Colonel Esmond be relieve when a ducal coach and six come and whisk his charmer away out of his reach , and place she in a high sphere . as you have see the nymph in the opera - machine go up to the cloud at the end of the piece where Mars , Bacchus , Apollo , and all the divine company of Olympians be seat , and quaver out her last song as a goddess : so when this portentous elevation be accomplish in the Esmond family , I be not sure that every one of we do not treat the divine Beatrix with special honor ; at least the saucy little beauty carry her head with a toss of supreme authority , and assume a touch - I - not air , which all her friend very good - humoredly bow to . 
 an old army acquaintance of Colonel Esmond 's , honest Tom Trett , who have sell his company , marry a wife , and turn merchant in the city , be dreadfully gloomy for a long time , though live in a fine house on the river , and carry on a great trade to all appearance . at length Esmond see his friend 's name in the Gazette as a bankrupt ; and a week after this circumstance my bankrupt walk into Mr. Esmond 's lodge with a face perfectly radiant with good - humor , and as jolly and careless as when they have sail from Southampton ten year before for Vigo . " this bankruptcy , " say Tom , " have be hang over my head these three year ; the thought hath prevent my sleeping , and I have look at poor Polly 's head on t'other pillow , and then towards my razor on the table , and think to put an end to myself , and so give my woe the slip . but now we be bankrupt : Tom Trett pay as many shilling in the pound as he can ; his wife have a little cottage at Fulham , and her fortune secure to herself . I be afraid neither of bailiff nor of creditor : and for the last six night have sleep easy . " so it be that when Fortune shake her wing and leave he , honest Tom cuddle himself up in his ragged virtue , and fall asleep . 
 Esmond do not tell his friend how much his story apply to Esmond too ; but he laugh at it , and use it ; and have fairly strike his docket in this love transaction , determine to put a cheerful face on his bankruptcy . perhaps Beatrix be a little offended at his gayety . " be this the way , sir , that you receive the announcement of your misfortune , " say she , " and do you come smile before I as if you be glad to be rid of I ? " 
 Esmond would not be put off from his good - humor , but tell she the story of Tom Trett and his bankruptcy . " I have be hanker after the grape on the wall , " say he , " and lose my temper because they be beyond my reach ; be there any wonder ? they be go now , and another have they a tall man than your humble servant have win they . " and the Colonel make his cousin a low bow . 
 " a tall man , Cousin Esmond ! " say she . " a man of spirit would have seal the wall , sir , and seize they ! a man of courage would have fight for them , not gap for them . " 
 " a Duke have but to gape and they drop into his mouth , " say Esmond , with another low bow . 
 " yes , sir , " say she , " a Duke be a tall man than you . and why should I not be grateful to one such as his Grace , who give I his heart and his great name ? it be a great gift he honor I with ; I know ' tis a bargain between we ; and I accept it , and will do my utmost to perform my part of it . ' tis no question of sigh and philandering between a noble man of his grace 's age and a girl who hath little of that softness in her nature . why should I not own that I be ambitious , Harry Esmond ; and if it be no sin in a man to covet honor , why should a woman too not desire it ? shall I be frank with you , Harry , and say that if you have not be down on your knee , and so humble , you might have fare well with I ? a woman of my spirit , cousin , be to be win by gallantry , and not by sigh and rueful face . all the time you be worship and sing hymn to I , I know very well I be no goddess , and grow weary of the incense . so would you have be weary of the goddess too when she be call Mrs. Esmond , and get out of humor because she have not pin - money enough , and be force to go about in an old gown . Eh ! cousin , a goddess in a mob - cap , that have to make her husband 's gruel , cease to be divine I be sure of it . I should have be sulky and scold ; and of all the proud wretch in the world Mr. Esmond be the proud , let I tell he that . you never fall into a passion ; but you never forgive , I think . have you be a great man , you might have be good - humor ; but be nobody , sir , you be too great a man for I ; and I be afraid of you , cousin there ! and I will not   worship you , and you will never be happy except with a woman who will . why , after I belong to you , and after one of my tantrum , you would have put the pillow over my head some night , and smother I , as the black man do the woman in the play that you be so fond of . what be the creature 's name ? Desdemona . you would , you little black - dye Othello ! " 
 " I think I should , beatrix , " say the Colonel . 
 " and I want no such ending . I intend to live to be a hundred , and to go to ten thousand rout and ball , and to play card every night of my life till the year eighteen hundred . and I like to be the first of my company , sir ; and I like flattery and compliment , and you give I none ; and I like to be make to laugh , sir , and who be to laugh at your dismal face , I should like to know ? and I like a coach - and six or a coach - and - eight ; and I like diamond , and a new gown every week ; and people to say ' that be the Duchess how well her grace look make way for Madame l'Ambassadrice d'Angleterre call her Excellency 's people ' that be what I like . and as for you , you want a woman to bring your slipper and cap , and to sit at your foot , and cry , ' o caro ! o bravo ! ' whilst you read your Shakespeares and Miltons and stuff . Mamma would have be the wife for you , have you be a little old , though you look ten year old than she do you do , you glum - faced , blue - bearded little old man ! you might have sit , like Darby and Joan , and flatter each other ; and bill and coo like a pair of old pigeon on a perch . I want my wing and to use they , sir . " and she spread out her beautiful arm , as if indeed she could fly off like the pretty " Gawrie , " whom the man in the story be enamor of . 
 " and what will your Peter Wilkins say to your flight ? " say Esmond , who never admire this fair creature more than when she rebel and laugh at he . 
 " a duchess know her place , " say she , with a laugh . " why , I have a son already make for I , and thirty year old   my Lord Arran , and four daughter . how they will scold , and what a rage they will be in , when I come to take the head of the table ! but I give they only a month to be angry ; at the end of that time they shall love I every one , and so shall Lord Arran , and so shall all his grace 's Scots vassal and follower in the Highlands . I be bent on it ; and when I take a thing in my head , ' tis do . his grace be the great gentleman in Europe , and I will try and make he happy ; and , when the King come back , you may count on my protection , Cousin Esmond for come back the King will and shall ; and I will bring he back from Versailles , if he come under my hoop . " 
 " I hope the world will make you happy , beatrix , " say Esmond , with a sigh . " you will be beatrix till you be my Lady Duchess will you not ? I shall then make your grace my very low bow . " 
 " none of these sigh and this satire , cousin , " she say . " I take his grace 's great bounty thankfully yes , thankfully ; and will wear his honor becomingly . I do not say he hath touch my heart ; but he have my gratitude , obedience , admiration I have tell he that , and no more ; and with that his noble heart be content . I have tell he all even the story of that poor creature that I be engage to and that I could not love ; and I gladly give his word back to he , and jump for joy to get back my own . I be twenty - five year old . " 
 " twenty - six , my dear , " say Esmond .
