 " twenty - five , sir I choose to be twenty - five ; and in eight year no man hath ever touch my heart . yes you do once , for a little , Harry , when you come back after Lille , and engage with that murderer Mohun , and save Frank 's life . I think I could like you ; and mamma beg I hard , on her knee , and I do for a day . but the old chill come over I , Henry , and the old fear of you and your melancholy ; and I be glad when you go away , and engage with my Lord Ashburnham , that I might hear no more of you , that be the truth . you be too good for I , somehow . I could not make you happy , and should break my heart in try , and not be able to love you . but if you have ask I when we give you the sword , you might have have I , sir , and we both should have be miserable by this time . I talk with that silly lord all night just to vex you and mamma , and I succeed , do not   I ? how frankly we can talk of these thing ! it seem a thousand year ago : and , though we be here sit in the same room , there be a great wall between we . my dear , kind , faithful , gloomy old cousin ! I can like now , and admire you too , sir , and say that you be brave , and very kind , and very true , and a fine gentleman for all for all your little mishap at your birth , " say she , wag her arch head . 
 " and now , sir , " say she , with a curtsy , " we must have no more talk except when mamma be by , as his grace be with we ; for he do not half like you , cousin , and be jealous as the black man in your favorite play . " 
 though the very kindness of the word stab Mr. Esmond with the keen pang , he do not show his sense of the wound by any look of his   as Beatrix , indeed , afterwards own to he , but say , with a perfect command of himself and an easy smile , " the interview must not end yet , my dear , until I have have my last word . stay , here come your mother "   indeed she come in here with her sweet anxious face , and Esmond go up kiss her hand respectfully . " my dear lady may hear , too , the last word , which be no secret , and be only a parting benediction accompany a present for your marriage from an old gentleman your guardian ; for I feel as if I be the guardian of all the family , and an old old fellow that be fit to be the grandfather of you all ; and in this character let I make my Lady Duchess her wedding present . they be the diamond my father 's widow leave I . I have think beatrix might have have they a year ago ; but they be good enough for a duchess , though not bright enough for the handsome woman in the world . " and he take the case out of his pocket in which the jewel be , and present they to his cousin . 
 she give a cry of delight , for the stone be indeed very handsome , and of great value ; and the next minute the necklace be where Belinda 's cross be in Mr. Pope 's admirable poem , and glitter on the whitest and most perfectly - shape neck in all England . 
 the girl 's delight at receive these trinket be so great , that after rush to the look - glass and examine the effect they produce upon that fair neck which they surround , Beatrix be run back with her arm extend , and be perhaps for pay her cousin with a price , that he would have like no doubt to receive from those beautiful rosy lip of her , but at this moment the door open , and his Grace the bridegroom elect be announce . 
 he look very black upon Mr. Esmond , to whom he make a very low bow indeed , and kiss the hand of each lady in his most ceremonious manner . he have come in his chair from the palace hard by , and wear his two star of the Garter and the Thistle . 
 " look , my Lord Duke , " say Mistress Beatrix , advance to he , and show the diamond on her breast . 
 " Diamonds , " say his Grace . " Hm ! they seem pretty . " 
 " they be a present on my marriage , " say beatrix . 
 " from her Majesty ? " ask the Duke . " the Queen be very good . " 
 " from my cousin Henry from our cousin Henry " cry both the lady in a breath . 
 " I have not the honor of know the gentleman . I think that my Lord Castlewood have no brother : and that on your ladyship 's side there be no nephew . " 
 " from our cousin , Colonel Henry Esmond , my lord , " say Beatrix , take the Colonel 's hand very bravely , " who be leave guardian to we by our father , and who have a hundred time show his love and friendship for our family . " 
 " the Duchess of Hamilton receive no diamond but from her husband , madam , " say the Duke " may I pray you to restore these to Mr. Esmond ? " 
 " beatrix Esmond may receive a present from our kinsman and benefactor , my Lord Duke , " say Lady Castlewood , with an air of great dignity . " she be my daughter yet : and if her mother sanction the gift no one else hath the right to question it . " 
 " Kinsman and benefactor ! " say the Duke . " I know of no kinsman : and I do not choose that my wife should have for benefactor a " 
 " my lord ! " say Colonel Esmond . 
 " I be not here to bandy word , " say his grace : " frankly I tell you that your visit to this house be too frequent , and that I choose no present for the Duchess of Hamilton from gentleman that bear a name they have no right to . " 
 " my lord ! " break out Lady Castlewood , " Mr. Esmond hath the good right to that name of any man in the world : and ' tis as old and as honorable as your grace 's . " 
 my Lord Duke smile , and look as if Lady Castlewood be mad , that be so talk to he . 
 " if I call he benefactor , " say my mistress , " it be because he have be so to we yes , the noble , the true , the brave , the dearest of benefactor . he would have save my husband 's life from Mohun 's sword . he do save my boy 's , and defend he from that villain . be those no benefit ? " 
 " I ask Colonel Esmond 's pardon , " say his Grace , if possible more haughty than before . " I would say not a word that should give he offence , and thank he for his kindness to your ladyship 's family . my Lord Mohun and I be connect , you know , by marriage though neither by blood nor friendship ; but I must repeat what I say , that my wife can receive no present from Colonel Esmond . " 
 " my daughter may receive present from the Head of our House : my daughter may thankfully take kindness from her father 's , her mother 's , her brother 's dear friend ; and be grateful for one more benefit besides the thousand we owe he , " cry Lady Esmond . " what be a string of diamond stone compare to that affection he hath give we our dear preserver and benefactor ? we owe he not only Frank 's life , but our all yes , our all , " say my mistress , with a heighten color and a trembling voice . " the title we bear be his , if he would claim it . ' tis we who have no right to our name : not he that be too great for it . he sacrifice his name at my die lord 's bedside sacrifice it to my orphan child ; give up rank and honor because he love we so nobly . his father be Viscount of Castlewood and Marquis of Esmond before he ; and he be his father 's lawful son and true heir , and we be the recipient of his bounty , and he the chief of a house that be as old as your own . and if he be content to forego his name that my child may bear it , we love he and honor he and bless he under whatever name he bear " and here the fond and affectionate creature would have kneel to Esmond again , but that he prevent she ; and Beatrix , run up to she with a pale face and a cry of alarm , embrace she and say , " Mother , what be this ? " 
 " ' tis a family secret , my Lord Duke , " say Colonel Esmond : " poor beatrix know nothing of it ; nor do my lady till a year ago . and I have as good a right to resign my title as your grace 's mother to abdicate her to you . " 
 " I should have tell everything to the Duke of Hamilton , " say my mistress , " have his grace apply to I for my daughter 's hand , and not to beatrix . I should have speak with you this very day in private , my lord , have not your word bring about this sudden explanation and now ' tis fit beatrix should hear it ; and know , as I would have all the world know , what we owe to our kinsman and patron . " 
 and then in her touching way , and have hold of her daughter 's hand , and speak to she rather than my Lord Duke , Lady Castlewood tell the story which you know already laud up to the sky her kinsman 's behavior . on his side Mr. Esmond explain the reason that seem quite sufficiently cogent with he , why the succession in the family , as at present it stand , should not be disturb ; and he should remain as he be , Colonel Esmond . 
 " and Marquis of Esmond , my lord , " say his Grace , with a low bow . " permit I to ask your lordship 's pardon for word that be utter in ignorance ; and to beg for the favor of your friendship . to be ally to you , sir , must be an honor under whatever name you be know "   so his Grace be pleased to say ; " and in return for the splendid present you make my wife , your kinswoman , I hope you will please to command any service that James Douglas can perform . I shall never be easy until I repay you a part of my obligation at least ; and ere very long , and with the mission her Majesty hath give I , " say the Duke , " that may perhaps be in my power . I shall esteem it as a favor , my lord , if Colonel Esmond will give away the bride . " 
 " and if he will take the usual payment in advance , he be welcome , " say Beatrix , step up to he ; and , as Esmond kiss she , she whisper , " oh , why do not   I know you before ? " 
 my Lord Duke be as hot as a flame at this salute , but say never a word : beatrix make he a proud curtsy , and the two lady quit the room together . 
 " when do your Excellency go for Paris ? " ask Colonel Esmond . 
 " as soon after the ceremony as may be , " his Grace answer . " ' tis fix for the first of December : it can not be soon . the equipage will not be ready till then . the Queen intend the embassy should be very grand and I have law business to settle . that ill - omen Mohun have come , or be come , to London again : we be in a lawsuit about my late Lord Gerard 's property ; and he hath send to I to meet he . " 




 CHAPTER V. 

 MOHUN appear for the last TIME in this history . 

 besides my Lord Duke of Hamilton and Brandon , who for family reason have kindly promise his protection and patronage to Colonel Esmond , he have other great friend in power now , both able and willing to assist he , and he might , with such ally , look forward to as fortunate advancement in civil life at home as he have get rapid promotion abroad . his grace be magnanimous enough to offer to take Mr. Esmond as secretary on his Paris embassy , but no doubt he intend that proposal should be reject ; at any rate , Esmond could not bear the thought of attend his mistress far than the church - door after her marriage , and so decline that offer which his generous rival make he . 
 other gentleman in power be liberal at least of compliment and promise to Colonel Esmond . Mr. Harley , now become my Lord Oxford and Mortimer , and instal Knight of the Garter on the same day as his Grace of Hamilton have receive the same honor , send to the Colonel to say that a seat in Parliament should be at his disposal presently , and Mr. St. John hold out many flattering hope of advancement to the Colonel when he should enter the House . Esmond 's friend be all successful , and the most successful and triumphant of all be his dear old commander , General Webb , who be now appoint Lieutenant - General of the Land Forces , and receive with particular honor by the Ministry , by the Queen , and the people out of door , who huzza'd the brave chief when they use to see he in his chariot go to the House or to the Drawing - room , or hobble on foot to his coach from St. Stephen 's upon his glorious old crutch and stick , and cheer he as loud as they have ever do Marlborough . 
 that great Duke be utterly disgrace ; and honest old Webb date all his grace 's misfortune from Wynendael , and vow that Fate serve the traitor right . Duchess Sarah have also go to ruin ; she have be force to give up her key , and her place , and her pension : " ah , ah ! " say Webb , ' she would have lock up three million of french crown with her key have I but be knock on the head , but I stop that convoy at Wynendael . " our enemy Cardonnel be turn out of the House of Commons   along with Mr. Walpole   for malversation of public money . Cadogan lose his place of Lieutenant of the Tower . Marlborough 's daughter resign their post of lady of the bedchamber ; and so complete be the Duke 's disgrace , that his son - in - law , Lord Bridgewater , be absolutely oblige to give up his lodging at St. James 's , and have his half - pension , as Master of the Horse , take away . but I think the low depth of Marlborough 's fall be when he humbly send to ask General Webb when he might wait upon he ; he who have command the stout old General , who have injure he and sneer at he , who have keep he dangle in his ante - chamber , who could not even after his great service condescend to write he a letter in his own hand . the nation be as eager for peace as ever it have be hot for war . the Prince of Savoy come amongst we , have his audience of the Queen , and get his famous Sword of Honor , and strove with all his force to form a Whig party together , to bring over the young Prince of Hanover to do anything which might prolong the war , and consummate the ruin of the old sovereign whom he hat so implacably . but the nation be tired of the struggle : so completely weary of it that not even our defeat at Denain could rouse we into any anger , though such an action so lose two year before would have set all England in a fury . ' twa easy to see that the great Marlborough be not with the army . Eugene be oblige to fall back in a rage , and forego the dazzle revenge of his life . ' twa in vain the Duke 's side ask , " would we suffer our arm to be insult ? would we not send back the only champion who could repair our honor ? " the nation have have its bellyful of fighting ; nor could taunt or outcry goad up our Britons any more . 
 for a statesman that be always prate of liberty , and have the grand philosophic maxim in his mouth , it must be own that Mr. St. John sometimes rather act like a turkish than a greek philosopher , and especially fall foul of one unfortunate set of man , the man of letter , with a tyranny a little extraordinary in a man who profess to respect their calling so much . the literary controversy at this time be very bitter , the Government side be the win one , the popular one , and I think might have be the merciful one . ' twa natural that the opposition should be peevish and cry out : some man do so from their heart , admire the Duke of Marlborough 's prodigious talent , and deplore the disgrace of the great general the world ever know : ' twa the stomach that cause other patriot to grumble , and such man cry out because they be poor , and pay to do so . against these my Lord Bolingbroke never show the slight mercy , whip a dozen into prison or into the pillory without the least commiseration . 
 from have be a man of arm Mr. Esmond have now come to be a man of letter , but on a safe side than that in which the above - cite poor fellow venture their liberty and ear . there be no danger on our , which be the win side ; besides , Mr. Esmond please himself by think that he writ like a gentleman if he do not always succeed as a wit . 
 of the famous wit of that age , who have render Queen Anne 's reign illustrious , and whose work will be in all Englishmen 's hand in age yet to come , Mr. Esmond see many , but at public place chiefly ; never have a great intimacy with any of they , except with honest Dick Steele and Mr. Addison , who part company with Esmond , however , when that gentleman become a declared Tory , and live on close term with the lead person of that party . Addison keep himself to a few friend , and very rarely open himself except in their company . a man more upright and conscientious than he it be not possible to find in public life , and one whose conversation be so various , easy , and delightful . write now in my mature year , I own that I think Addison 's politic be the right , and be my time to come over again , I would be a Whig in England and not a Tory ; but with people that take a side in politic , ' tis man rather than principle that commonly bind they . a kindness or a slight put a man under one flag or the other , and he march with it to the end of the campaign . Esmond 's master in war be injure by Marlborough , and hat he : and the lieutenant fight the quarrel of his leader . Webb come to London be use as a weapon by Marlborough 's enemy   and true steel he be , that honest chief ; nor be his aide - de - camp , Mr. Esmond , an unfaithful or unworthy partisan . ' tis strange here , and on a foreign soil , and in a land that be independent in all but the name ,   for that the north american colony shall remain dependant on yonder little island for twenty year more , I never can think ,   to remember how the nation at home seem to give itself up to the domination of one or other aristocratic party , and take a hanoverian king , or a french one , accord as either prevail . and while the Tories , the October club gentleman , the High Church parson that hold by the Church of England , be for have a Papist king , for whom many of their scottish and english leader , firm churchman all , lay down their life with admirable loyalty and devotion ; they be govern by man who have notoriously no religion at all , but use it as they would use any opinion for the purpose of forward their own ambition . the Whigs , on the other hand , who profess attachment to religion and liberty too , be compel to send to Holland or Hanover for a monarch around whom they could rally . a strange series of compromise be that English history ; compromise of principle , compromise of party , compromise of worship ! the lover of English freedom and independence submit their religious conscience to an Act of Parliament ; could not consolidate their liberty without send to Zell or the Hague for a king to live under ; and could not find amongst the proud people in the world a man speak their own language , and understand their law , to govern they . the Tory and High Church patriot be ready to die in defence of a Papist family that have sell we to France ; the great Whig noble , the sturdy republican recusant who have cut off Charles Stuart 's head for treason , be fain to accept a king whose title come to he through a royal grandmother , whose own royal grandmother 's head have fall under Queen Bess 's hatchet . and our proud English noble send to a petty german town for a monarch to come and reign in London and our prelate kiss the ugly hand of his dutch mistress , and think it no dishonor . in England you can but belong to one party or t'other , and you take the house you live in with all its encumbrance , its retainer , its antique discomfort , and ruin even ; you patch up , but you never build up anew . will we of the new world submit much long , even nominally , to this ancient british superstition ? there be sign of the time which make I think that ere long we shall care as little about King George here , and peer temporal and peer spiritual , as we do for King Canute or the Druids . 
 this chapter begin about the wit , my grandson may say , and hath wander very far from their company . the pleasantest of the wit I know be the Doctors Garth and Arbuthnot , and Mr. Gay , the author of " Trivia , " the most charming kind soul that ever laugh at a joke or crack a bottle . Mr. Prior I see , and he be the earthen pot swim with the pot of brass down the stream , and always and justly frightened lest he should break in the voyage . I meet he both at London and Paris , where he be perform piteous congee to the Duke of Shrewsbury , not have courage to support the dignity which his undeniable genius and talent have win he , and write coax letter to Secretary St. John , and think about his plate and his place , and what on earth should become of he should his party go out . the famous Mr. Congreve I see a dozen of time at Button 's , a splendid wreck of a man , magnificently attire , and though gouty , and almost blind , bear a brave face against fortune . 
 the great Mr. Pope   of whose prodigious genius I have no word to express my admiration   be quite a puny lad at this time , appear seldom in public place . there be hundred of man , wit , and pretty fellow frequent the theatre and coffee - house of that day whom " nunc perscribere longum est . " indeed I think the most brilliant of that sort I ever see be not till fifteen year afterwards , when I pay my last visit in England , and meet young Harry Fielding , son of the Fielding that serve in Spain and afterwards in Flanders with we , and who for fun and humor seem to top they all . as for the famous Dr. Swift , I can say of he , " vidi tantum . " he be in London all these year up to the death of the Queen ; and in a hundred public place where I see he , but no more ; he never miss Court of a Sunday , where once or twice he be point out to your grandfather . he would have seek I out eagerly enough have I be a great man with a title to my name , or a star on my coat . at Court the Doctor have no eye but for the very great . Lord Treasurer and St. John use to call he Jonathan , and they pay he with this cheap coin for the service they take of he . he writ their lampoon , fight their enemy , flog and bully in their service , and it must be own with a consummate skill and fierceness . ' Tis say he hath lose his intellect now , and forget his wrong and his rage against mankind . I have always think of he and of Marlborough as the two great man of that age . I have read his book   who doth not know they ?   here in our calm wood , and imagine a giant to myself as I think of he , a lonely fallen Prometheus , groan as the vulture tear he . Prometheus I see , but when first I ever have any word with he , the giant step out of a sedan chair in the Poultry , whither he have come with a tipsy irish servant parade before he , who announce he , bawl out his reverence 's name , whilst his master below be as yet haggle with the chairman . I dislike this Mr. Swift , and hear many a story about he , of his conduct to man , and his word to woman . he could flatter the great as much as he could bully the weak ; and Mr. Esmond , be young and hotter in that day than now , be determine , should he ever meet this dragon , not to run away from his tooth and his fire . 
 man have all sort of motif which carry they onwards in life , and be drive into act of desperation , or it may be of distinction , from a hundred different cause . there be one comrade of Esmond 's , an honest little irish lieutenant of Handyside 's , who owe so much money to a camp sutler , that he begin to make love to the man 's daughter , intend to pay his debt that way ; and at the battle of Malplaquet , fly away from the debt and lady too , he rush so desperately on the french line , that he get his company ; and come a captain out of the action , and have to marry the sutler 's daughter after all , who bring he his cancel debt to her father as poor Roger 's fortune . to run out of the reach of bill and marriage , he run on the enemy 's pike ; and as these do not kill he he be throw back upon t'other horn of his dilemma . our great Duke at the same battle be fight , not the French , but the Tories in England ; and risk his life and the army 's , not for his country but for his pay and place ; and for fear of his wife at home , that only be in life whom he dread . I have ask about man in my own company ,   new draft of poor country boy be perpetually come over to we during the war , and bring from the ploughshare to the sword ,   and find that a half of they under the flag be drive thither on account of a woman : one fellow be jilt by his mistress and take the shilling in despair ; another jilt the girl , and flee from she and the parish to the tent where the law could not disturb he . why go on particularize ? what can the son of Adam and Eve expect , but to continue in that course of love and trouble their father and mother set out on ? oh , my grandson ! I be draw nigh to the end of that period of my history , when I be acquaint with the great world of England and Europe ; my year be past the Hebrew poet 's limit , and I say unto thee , all my trouble and joy too , for that matter , have come from a woman ; as thine will when thy destine course begin . ' twas a woman that make a soldier of I , that set I intriguing afterwards ; I believe I would have spin smock for she have she so bid I ; what strength I have in my head I would have give she ; hath not every man in his degree have his Omphale and Delilah ? Mine befool I on the bank of the Thames , and in dear old England ; thou may find thine own by Rappahannock . 
 to please that woman then I try to distinguish myself as a soldier , and afterwards as a wit and a politician ; as to please another I would have put on a black cassock and a pair of band , and have do so but that a superior fate intervene to defeat that project . and I say , I think the world be like Captain Esmond 's company I speak of anon ; and could you see every man 's career in life , you would find a woman clog he ; or cling round his march and stop he ; or cheer he and goad he : or beckon he out of her chariot , so that he go up to she , and leave the race to be run without he or bring he the apple , and say " eat ; " or fetch he the dagger and whisper " Kill ! yonder lie duncan , and a crown , and an opportunity . " 
 your grandfather fight with more effect as a politician than as a wit : and have private animosity and grievance of his own and his General 's against the great Duke in command of the army , and more information on military matter than most writer , who have never see beyond the fire of a tobacco - pipe at " will 's , " he be enable to do good service for that cause which he embark in , and for Mr. St. John and his party . but he disdain the abuse in which some of the Tory writer indulge ; for instance , Dr. Swift , who actually choose to doubt the Duke of Marlborough 's courage , and be pleased to hint that his grace 's military capacity be doubtful : nor be Esmond 's performance bad for the effect they be intend to produce ,   though no doubt they could not injure the Duke of Marlborough nearly so much in the public eye as the malignant attack of Swift do , which be carefully direct so as to blacken and degrade he ,   because they be writ openly and fairly by Mr. Esmond , who make no disguise of they , who be now out of the army , and who never attack the prodigious courage and talent , only the selfishness and rapacity , of the chief . 
 the Colonel then , have writ a paper for one of the Tory journal , call the Post - Boy ,   a letter upon Bouchain , that the town talk about for two whole day , when the appearance of an italian singer supply a fresh subject for conversation ,   and have business at the Exchange , where Mistress Beatrix want a pair of glove or a fan very likely , Esmond go to correct his paper , and be sit at the printer 's , when the famous Doctor Swift come in , his irish fellow with he that use to walk before his chair , and bawl out his master 's name with great dignity . 
 Mr. Esmond be wait for the printer too , whose wife have go to the tavern to fetch he , and be meantime engage in draw a picture of a soldier on horseback for a dirty little pretty boy of the printer 's wife , whom she have leave behind she . 
 " I presume you be the editor of the Post - Boy , sir ? " say the Doctor , in a grating voice that have an irish twang ; and he look at the Colonel from under his two bushy eyebrow with a pair of very clear blue eye . his complexion be muddy , his figure rather fat , his chin double . he wear a shabby cassock , and a shabby hat over his black wig , and he pull out a great gold watch , at which he look very fierce . 
 " I be but a contributor , Doctor Swift , " say Esmond , with the little boy still on his knee . he be sit with his back in the window , so that the doctor could not see he . 
 " who tell you I be Dr. Swift ? " say the Doctor , eye the other very haughtily . 
 " your Reverence 's valet bawl out your name , " say the Colonel . " I should judge you bring he from Ireland ? " 
 " and pray , sir , what right have you to judge whether my servant come from Ireland or no ? I want to speak with your employer , Mr. Leach . I will thank ye go fetch he . " 
 " where be your papa , Tommy ? " ask the Colonel of the child , a smutty little wretch in a frock . 
 instead of answer , the child begin to cry ; the Doctor 's appearance have no doubt frighten the poor little imp . 
 " send that squall little brat about his business , and do what I bid ye , sir , " say the Doctor . 
 " I must finish , the picture first for Tommy , " say the Colonel , laugh . " here , Tommy , will you have your Pandour with whisker or without ? " 
 " whister , " say Tommy , quite intent on the picture . 
 " who the devil be ye , sir ? " cry the doctor ; " be ye a printer 's man or be ye not ? " he pronounce it like NAUGHT . 
 " your reverence need not   raise the devil to ask who I be , " say Colonel Esmond . " do you ever hear of Doctor Faustus , little Tommy ? or Friar Bacon , who invent gunpowder , and set the Thames on fire ? " 
 Mr. Swift turn quite red , almost purple . " I do not intend any offence , sir , " say he . 
 " I dare say , sir , you offend without meaning , " say the other , dryly . 
 " who be ye , sir ? do you know who I be , sir ? you be one of the pack of Grub Street scribbler that my friend Mr. Secretary hath lay by the heel . how dare ye , sir , speak to I in this tone ? " cry the Doctor , in a great fume . 
 " I beg your honor 's humble pardon if I have offend your honor , " say Esmond in a tone of great humility . " rather than be send to the Compter , or be put in the pillory , there be nothing I would not   do . but Mrs. Leach , the printer 's lady , tell I to mind Tommy whilst she go for her husband to the tavern , and I dare not   leave the child lest he should fall into the fire ; but if your Reverence will hold he " 
 " I take the little beast ! " say the Doctor , start back . " I be engaged to your better , fellow . tell Mr. Leach that when he make an appointment with Dr. Swift he have well keep it , do ye hear ? and keep a respectful tongue in your head , sir , when you address a person like I . " 
 " I be but a poor break - down soldier , " say the Colonel , " and I have   see well day , though I be force now to turn my hand to write . we can not   help our fate , sir . " 
 " you be the person that Mr. Leach hath speak to I of , I presume . have the goodness to speak civilly when you be speak to and tell Leach to call at my lodging in Bury Street , and bring the paper with he to - night at ten o'clock . and the next time you see I , you will know I , and be civil , Mr. Kemp . " 
 Poor Kemp , who have be a lieutenant at the beginning of the war , and fall into misfortune , be the writer of the Post - Boy , and now take honest Mr. Leach 's pay in place of her Majesty 's . Esmond have see this gentleman , and a very ingenious , hardworke honest fellow he be , toil to give bread to a great family , and watch up many a long winter night to keep the wolf from his door . and Mr. St. John , who have liberty always on his tongue , have just send a dozen of the opposition writer into prison , and one actually into the pillory , for what he call libel , but libel not half so violent as those writ on our side . with regard to this very piece of tyranny , Esmond have remonstrate strongly with the Secretary , who laugh and say the rascal be serve quite right ; and tell Esmond a joke of Swift 's regard the matter . Nay , more , this Irishman , when St. John be about to pardon a poor wretch condemn to death for rape , absolutely prevent the Secretary from exercise this act of good - nature , and boast that he have have the man hang ; and great as the Doctor 's genius might be , and splendid his ability , Esmond for one would affect no love for he , and never desire to make his acquaintance . the doctor be at Court every Sunday assiduously enough , a place the Colonel frequent but rarely , though he have a great inducement to go there in the person of a fair maid of honor of her Majesty 's ; and the air and patronage Mr. Swift give himself , forget gentleman of his country whom he know perfectly , his loud talk at once insolent and servile , nay , perhaps his very intimacy with Lord Treasurer and the Secretary , who indulge all his freak and call he Jonathan , you may be sure , be remark by many a person of whom the proud priest himself take no note , during that time of his vanity and triumph . 
 ' Twas but three day after the 15th of November , 1712   esmond mind he well of the date , that he go by invitation to dine with his General , the foot of whose table he use to take on these festive occasion , as he have do at many a board , hard and plentiful , during the campaign . this be a great feast , and of the latter sort ; the honest old gentleman love to treat his friend splendidly : his Grace of Ormonde , before he join his army as generalissimo , my Lord Viscount Bolingbroke , one of her Majesty 's Secretaries of State , my Lord Orkney , that have serve with we abroad , be of the party . his Grace of Hamilton , Master of the Ordnance , and in whose honor the feast have be give , upon his approach departure as Ambassador to Paris , have send an excuse to General Webb at two o'clock , but an hour before the dinner : nothing but the most immediate business , his grace say , should have prevent he have the pleasure of drink a parting glass to the health of General Webb . his absence disappoint Esmond 's old chief , who suffer much from his wound besides ; and though the company be grand , it be rather gloomy . St. John come last , and bring a friend with he : " I be sure , " say my General , bow very politely , " my table hath always a place for Dr. Swift . " 
 Mr. Esmond go up to the Doctor with a bow and a smile : " I give Dr. Swift 's message , " say he , " to the printer : I hope he bring your pamphlet to your lodging in time . " indeed poor Leach have come to his house very soon after the doctor leave it , be bring away rather tipsy from the tavern by his thrifty wife ; and he talk of Cousin Swift in a maudlin way , though of course Mr. Esmond do not allude to this relationship . the Doctor scowl , blush , and be much confused , and say scarce a word during the whole of dinner . a very little stone will sometimes knock down these Goliaths of wit ; and this one be often discomfit when meet by a man of any spirit ; he take his place sulkily , put water in his wine that the other drink plentifully , and scarce say a word . 
 the talk be about the affair of the day , or rather about person than affair : my Lady Marlborough 's fury , her daughter in old clothe and mob - cap look out from their window and see the company pass to the Drawing - room ; the gentleman - usher 's horror when the Prince of Savoy be introduce to her Majesty in a tie - wig , no man out of a full - bottom periwig ever have kiss the Royal hand before ; about the Mohawks and the damage they be do , rush through the town , kill and murder . some one say the ill - omen face of Mohun have be see at the theatre the night before , and Macartney and Meredith with he . mean to be a feast , the meeting , in spite of drink and talk , be as dismal as a funeral . every topic start subside into gloom . his grace of Ormonde go away because the conversation get upon Denain , where we have be defeat in the last campaign . Esmond 's General be affect at the allusion to this action too , for his comrade of Wynendael , the Count of Nassau Woudenbourg , have be slay there . Mr. Swift , when Esmond pledge he , say he drink no wine , and take his hat from the peg and go away , beckon my Lord Bolingbroke to follow he ; but the other bade he take his chariot and save his coach - hire he have to speak with Colonel Esmond ; and when the rest of the company withdraw to card , these two remain behind in the dark . 
 Bolingbroke always speak freely when he have drink freely . his enemy could get any secret out of he in that condition ; woman be even employ to ply he , and take his word down . I have hear that my Lord Stair , three year after , when the Secretary flee to France and become the Pretender 's Minister , get all the information he want by put female spy over St. John in his cup . he speak freely now : " Jonathan know nothing of this for certain , though he suspect it , and by George , Webb will take an Archbishopric , and Jonathan a no , damme Jonathan will take an Arch - bishopric from James , I warrant I , gladly enough . your Duke hath the string of the whole matter in his hand , " the Secretary go on . " we have that which will force Marlborough to keep his distance , and he go out of London in a fortnight . prior hath his business ; he leave I this morning , and mark I , Harry , should fate carry off our august , our beloved , our most gouty and plethoric Queen , and Defender of the Faith , la bonne cause triomphera . a la sante de la bonne cause ! everything good come from France . Wine come from France ; give we another bumper to the bonne cause . " we drink it together . 
 " will the bonne cause turn protestant ? " ask Mr. Esmond . 
 " no , hang it , " say the other , " he will defend our Faith as in duty bind , but he will stick by his own . the Hind and the Panther shall run in the same car , by Jove . righteousness and peace shall kiss each other : and we will have Father Massillon to walk down the aisle of St. Paul 's , cheek by jowl with Dr. Sacheverel . give we more wine ; here be a health to the bonne cause , kneeling damme , let us drink it kneel . " he be quite flushed and wild with wine as he be talk . 
 " and suppose , " say Esmond , who always have this gloomy apprehension , " the bonne cause should give we up to the French , as his father and uncle do before he ? " 
 " give we up to the French ! " start up Bolingbroke ; " be there any english gentleman that fear that ? you who have see Blenheim and Ramillies , afraid of the French ! your ancestor and mine , and brave old Webb 's yonder , have meet they in a hundred field , and our child will be ready to do the like . who be he that wish for more man from England ? my Cousin Westmoreland ? give we up to the French , pshaw ! " 
 " his uncle do , " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " and what happen to his grandfather ? " break out St. John , fill out another bumper . " here be to the great monarch England ever see ; here be to the Englishman that make a kingdom of she . our great King come from Huntingdon , not Hanover ; our father do not   look for a Dutchman to rule we . let he come and we will keep he , and we will show he Whitehall . if he be a traitor let we have he here to deal with he ; and then there be spirit here as great as any that have go before . there be man here that can look at danger in the face and not be frightened at it . traitor ! treason ! what name be these to scare you and I ? be all Oliver 's man dead , or his glorious name forget in fifty year ? be there no man equal to he , think you , as good ay , as good ? God save the king ! and , if the monarchy fail we , God save the British Republic ! " 
 he fill another great bumper , and toss it up and drain it wildly , just as the noise of rapid carriage - wheel approaching be stop at our door , and after a hurried knock and a moment 's interval , Mr. Swift come into the hall , run up stair to the room we be din in , and enter it with a perturbed face . St. John , excited with drink , be make some wild quotation out of Macbeth , but Swift stop he . 
 " drink no more , my lord , for God 's sake ! " say he . " I come with the most dreadful news . " 
 " be the queen dead ? " cry out Bolingbroke , seize on a water - glass . 
 " no , Duke Hamilton be dead : he be murder an hour ago by Mohun and Macartney ; they have a quarrel this morning ; they give he not so much time as to write a letter . he go for a couple of his friend , and he be dead , and Mohun , too , the bloody villain , who be set on he . they fight in Hyde Park just before sunset ; the Duke kill Mohun , and Macartney come up and stab he , and the dog be flee . I have your chariot below ; send to every part of the country and apprehend that villain ; come to the Duke 's house and see if any life be leave in he . " 
 " oh , beatrix , beatrix , " think Esmond , " and here end my poor girl 's ambition ! " 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 POOR BEATRIX . 

 there have be no need to urge upon Esmond the necessity of a separation between he and beatrix : Fate have do that completely ; and I think from the very moment poor beatrix have accept the Duke 's offer , she begin to assume the majestic air of a Duchess , nay , Queen Elect , and to carry herself as one sacred and remove from we common people . her mother and kinsman both fall into her way , the latter scornfully perhaps , and utter his usual gibe at her vanity and his own . there be a certain charm about this girl of which neither Colonel Esmond nor his fond mistress could forego the fascination ; in spite of her fault and her pride and wilfulness , they be force to love she ; and , indeed , might be set down as the two chief flatterer of the brilliant creature 's court . 
 who , in the course of his life , hath not be so bewitched , and worship some idol or another ? year after this passion hath be dead and bury , along with a thousand other worldly care and ambition , he who feel it can recall it out of its grave , and admire , almost as fondly as he do in his youth , that lovely queenly creature . I invoke that beautiful spirit from the shade and love she still ; or rather I should say such a past be always present to a man ; such a passion once feel form a part of his whole being , and can not be separate from it ; it become a portion of the man of to - day , just as any great faith or conviction , the discovery of poetry , the awakening of religion , ever afterwards influence he ; just as the wound I have at Blenheim , and of which I wear the scar , hath become part of my frame and influence my whole body , nay , spirit subsequently , though ' twa get and heal forty year ago . parting and forgetting ! what faithful heart can do these ? our great thought , our great affection , the Truths of our life , never leave we . surely , they can not separate from our consciousness ; shall follow it whithersoever that shall go ; and be of their nature divine and immortal . 
 with the horrible news of this catsstrophe , which be confirm by the weep domestic at the Duke 's own door , Esmond ride homeward as quick as his lazy coach would carry he , devise all the time how he should break the intelligence to the person most concerned in it ; and if a satire upon human vanity could be need , that poor soul afford it in the altered company and occupation in which Esmond find she . for day before , her chariot have be roll the street from mercer to toyshop from goldsmith to laceman : her taste be perfect , or at least the fond bridegroom have think so , and have give her entire authority over all tradesman , and for all the plate , furniture and equipage , with which his grace the Ambassador wish to adorn his splendid mission . she must have her picture by Kneller , a duchess not be complete without a portrait , and a noble one he make , and actually sketch in , on a cushion , a coronet which she be about to wear . she vow she would wear it at King James the Third 's coronation , and never a princess in the land would have become ermine well . esmond find the ante - chamber crowd with milliner and toyshop woman , obsequious goldsmith with jewel , salver , and tankard ; and mercer ' man with hanging , and velvet , and brocade . my Lady Duchess elect be give audience to one famous silversmith from Exeter Change , who bring with he a great chased salver , of which he be point out the beauty as Colonel Esmond enter . " come , " say she , " cousin , and admire the taste of this pretty thing . " I think Mars and Venus be lie in the golden bower , that one gilt Cupid carry off the war - god 's casque another his sword another his great buckler , upon which my Lord Duke Hamilton 's arm with our be to be engrave and a fourth be kneel down to the reclining goddess with the ducal coronet in her hand , God help we ! the next time Mr. Esmond see that piece of plate , the arm be change , the ducal coronet have be replace by a viscount 's ; it form part of the fortune of the thrifty goldsmith 's own daughter , when she marry my Lord Viscount Squanderfield two year after . 
 " be not   this a beautiful piece ? " say Beatrix , examine it , and she point out the arch grace of the cupid , and the fine carving of the languid prostrate Mars . esmond sicken as he think of the warrior dead in his chamber , his servant and child weep around he ; and of this smile creature attire herself , as it be , for that nuptial death - bed . " ' tis a pretty piece of vanity , " say he , look gloomily at the beautiful creature : there be flambeaux in the room light up the brilliant mistress of it . she lift up the great gold salver with her fair arm . 
 " Vanity ! " say she , haughtily . " what be vanity in you , sir , be propriety in I . you ask a jewish price for it , Mr. Graves ; but have it I will , if only to spite Mr. Esmond . " 
 " oh , Beatrix , lie it down ! " say Mr. Esmond . " Herodias ! you know not what you carry in the charger . " 
 she drop it with a clang ; the eager goldsmith run to seize his fallen ware . the lady 's face catch the fright from Esmond 's pale countenance , and her eye shine out like beacon of alarm : " what be it , Henry ! " say she , run to he , and seize both his hand . " what do you mean by your pale face and gloomy tone ? " 
 " come away , come away ! " say Esmond , lead she : she clung frighten to he , and he support she upon his heart , bid the scared goldsmith leave they . the man go into the next apartment , star with surprise , and hug his precious charger . 
 " oh , my beatrix , my sister ! " say Esmond , still hold in his arm the pallid and affrighted creature , " you have the great courage of any woman in the world ; prepare to show it now , for you have a dreadful trial to bear . " 
 she spring away from the friend who would have protect she : " Hath he leave I ? " say she . " we have word this morning : he be very gloomy , and I anger he : but he dare not , he dare not ! " as she speak a burn blush flush over her whole face and bosom . esmond see it reflect in the glass by which she stand , with clench hand , press her swell heart . 
 " he have leave you , " say Esmond , wonder that rage rather than sorrow be in her look . 
 " and he be alive , " cry beatrix , " and you bring I this commission ! he have leave I , and you have not   dare to avenge I ! you , that pretend to be the champion of our house , have let I suffer this insult ! where be Castlewood ? I will go to my brother . " 
 " the Duke be not alive , beatrix , " say Esmond . 
 she look at her cousin wildly , and fall back to the wall as though shoot in the breast : " and you come here , and and you kill he ? " 
 " no ; thank heaven ! " her kinsman say . " the blood of that noble heart doth not stain my sword ! in its last hour it be faithful to thee , Beatrix Esmond . vain and cruel woman ! kneel and thank the awful heaven which award life and death , and chastise pride , that the noble Hamilton die true to you ; at least that ' twa not your quarrel , or your pride , or your wicked vanity , that drive he to his fate . he die by the bloody sword which already have drink your own father 's blood . o woman , o sister ! to that sad field where two corps be lie for the murderer die too by the hand of the man he slay can you bring no mourner but your revenge and your vanity ? God help and pardon thee , Beatrix , as he bring this awful punishment to your hard and rebellious heart . " 
 Esmond have scarce do speak , when his mistress come in . the colloquy between he and Beatrix have last but a few minute , during which time Esmond 's servant have carry the disastrous news through the household . the army of Vanity Fair , wait without , gather up all their frippery and flee aghast . Tender Lady Castlewood have be in talk above with Dean Atterbury , the pious creature 's almoner and director ; and the Dean have enter with she as a physician whose place be at a sick - bed . beatrix 's mother look at Esmond and run towards her daughter , with a pale face and open heart and hand , all kindness and pity . but Beatrix pass she by , nor would she have any of the medicament of the spiritual physician . " I be good in my own room and by myself , " she say . her eye be quite dry ; nor do Esmond ever see they otherwise , save once , in respect to that grief . she give he a cold hand as she go out : " thank you , brother , " she say , in a low voice , and with a simplicity more touching than tear ; " all you have say be true and kind , and I will go away and ask pardon . " the three other remain behind , and talk over the dreadful story . it affect Dr. Atterbury more even than we , as it seem . the death of Mohun , her husband 's murderer , be more awful to my mistress than even the Duke 's unhappy end . esmond give at length what particular he know of their quarrel , and the cause of it . the two nobleman have long be at war with respect to the Lord Gerard 's property , whose two daughter my Lord Duke and Mohun have marry . they have meet by appointment that day at the lawyer 's in Lincoln 's Inn Fields ; have word which , though they appear very trifle to those who hear they , be not so to man exasperate by long and previous enmity . Mohun ask my Lord Duke where he could see his grace 's friend , and within an hour have send two of his own to arrange this deadly duel . it be pursue with such fierceness , and spring from so trifle a cause , that all man agree at the time that there be a party , of which these three notorious brawler be but agent , who desire to take Duke Hamilton 's life away . they fight three on a side , as in that tragic meeting twelve year back , which hath be recount already , and in which Mohun perform his second murder . they rush in , and close upon each other at once without any feint or crossing of sword even , and stab one at the other desperately , each receive many wound ; and Mohun have his death - wound , and my Lord Duke lie by he , Macartney come up and stab his grace as he lie on the ground , and give he the blow of which he die . Colonel Macartney deny this , of which the horror and indignation of the whole kingdom would nevertheless have he guilty , and flee the country , whither he never return . 
 what be the real cause of the Duke Hamilton 's death ? a paltry quarrel that might easily have be make up , and with a ruffian so low , base , profligate , and degrade with former crime and repeat murder , that a man of such renown and princely rank as my Lord Duke might have disdain to sully his sword with the blood of such a villain . but his spirit be so high that those who wish his death know that his courage be like his charity , and never turn any man away ; and he die by the hand of Mohun , and the other two cut - throat that be set on he . the Queen 's ambassador to Paris die , the loyal and devoted servant of the House of Stuart , and a Royal Prince of Scotland himself , and carry the confidence , the repentance of Queen Anne along with his own open devotion , and the good - will of million in the country more , to the Queen 's exiled brother and sovereign . 
 that party to which Lord Mohun belong have the benefit of his service , and now be well rid of such a ruffian . he , and Meredith , and Macartney , be the Duke of Marlborough 's man ; and the two colonel have be break but the year before for drinking perdition to the Tories . his grace be a Whig now and a Hanoverian , and as eager for war as Prince Eugene himself . I say not that he be privy to Duke Hamilton 's death , I say that his party profit by it ; and that three desperate and bloody instrument be find to effect that murder . 
 as Esmond and the Dean walk away from Kensington discourse of this tragedy , and how fatal it be to the cause which they both have at heart , the street - crier be already out with their broadside , shout through the town the full , true , and horrible account of the death of Lord Mohun and Duke Hamilton in a duel . a fellow have get to Kensington , and be cry it in the square there at very early morning , when Mr. Esmond happen to pass by . he drive the man from under Beatrix 's very window , whereof the casement have be set open . the sun be shin though ' twas November : he have see the market - cart roll into London , the guard relieve at the palace , the laborer trudge to their work in the garden between Kensington and the City the wander merchant and hawker fill the air with their cry . the world be go to its business again , although duke lie dead and lady mourn for they ; and king , very likely , lose their chance . so night and day pass away , and to - morrow come , and our place know we not . esmond think of the courier , now gallop on the North road to inform he , who be Earl of Arran yesterday , that he be Duke of Hamilton to - day , and of a thousand great scheme , hope , ambition , that be alive in the gallant heart , beat a few hour since , and now in a little dust quiescent . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 I visit castlewood once MORE . 

 thus , for a third time , Beatrix 's ambitious hope be circumvent , and she might well believe that a special malignant fate watch and pursue she , tear her prize out of her hand just as she seem to grasp it , and leave she with only rage and grief for her portion . whatever her feeling might have be of anger or of sorrow ,   and I fear I that the former emotion be that which most tear her heart ,   she would take no confidant , as people of soft nature would have do under such a calamity ; her mother and her kinsman know that she would disdain their pity , and that to offer it would be but to infuriate the cruel wound which fortune have inflict . we know that her pride be awfully humble and punish by this sudden and terrible blow ; she want no teaching of our to point out the sad moral of her story . her fond mother could give but her prayer , and her kinsman his faithful friendship and patience to the unhappy , stricken creature ; and it be only by hint , and a word or two utter month afterwards , that beatrix show she understand their silent commiseration , and on her part be secretly thankful for their forbearance . the people about the Court say there be that in her manner which frighten away scoff and condolence : she be above their triumph and their pity , and act her part in that dreadful tragedy greatly and courageously ; so that those who like she least be yet force to admire she . we , who watch she after her disaster , could not but respect the indomitable courage and majestic calm with which she bear it . " I would rather see her tear than her pride , " her mother say , who be accustom to bear her sorrow in a very different way , and to receive they as the stroke of God , with an awful submission and meekness . but beatrix 's nature be different to that tender parent 's ; she seem to accept her grief and to defy it ; nor would she allow it   I believe not even in private and in her own chamber   to extort from she the confession of even a tear of humiliation or a cry of pain . friend and child of our race , who come after I , in which way will you bear your trial ? I know one that pray God will give you love rather than pride , and that the Eye all - seeing shall find you in the humble place . not that we should judge proud spirit otherwise than charitably . ' tis nature hath fashion some for ambition and dominion , as it hath form other for obedience and gentle submission . the leopard follow his nature as the lamb do , and act after leopard law ; she can neither help her beauty , nor her courage , nor her cruelty ; nor a single spot on her shin coat ; nor the conquering spirit which impel she ; nor the shot which bring she down . 
 during that well - found panic the Whigs have , lest the Queen should forsake their Hanoverian Prince , bind by oath and treaty as she be to he , and recall her brother , who be ally to she by yet strong tie of nature and duty ; the Prince of Savoy , and the bold of that party of the Whigs , be for bring the young Duke of Cambridge over , in spite of the Queen , and the outcry of her Tory servant , argue that the Electoral Prince , a Peer and Prince of the Blood - Royal of this Realm too , and in the line of succession to the crown , have , a right to sit in the Parliament whereof he be a member , and to dwell in the country which he one day be to govern . nothing but the strong ill will express by the Queen , and the people about she , and menace of the Royal resentment , should this scheme be persist in , prevent it from be carry into effect . 
 the bold on our side be , in like manner , for have our Prince into the country . the undoubted inheritor of the right divine ; the feeling of more than half the nation , of almost all the clergy , of the gentry of England and Scotland with he ; entirely innocent of the crime for which his father suffer brave , young , handsome , unfortunate who in England would dare to molest the Prince should he come among we , and fle himself upon british generosity , hospitality , and honor ? an invader with an army of Frenchmen behind he , Englishmen of spirit would resist to the death , and drive back to the shore whence he come ; but a Prince , alone , arm with his right only , and rely on the loyalty of his people , be sure , many of his friend argue , of welcome , at least of safety , among we . the hand of his sister the Queen , of the people his subject , never could be raise to do he a wrong . but the Queen be timid by nature , and the successive Ministers she have , have private cause for their irresolution . the bold and honester man , who have at heart the illustrious young exile 's cause , have no scheme of interest of their own to prevent they from see the right do , and , provide only he come as an Englishman , be ready to venture their all to welcome and defend he . 
 St. John and Harley both have kind word in plenty for the Prince 's adherent , and give he endless promise of future support ; but hint and promise be all they could be get to give ; and some of his friend be for measure much bold , more efficacious , and more open . with a party of these , some of whom be yet alive , and some whose name Mr. Esmond have no right to mention , he find himself engage the year after that miserable death of Duke Hamilton , which deprive the Prince of his most courageous ally in this country . Dean Atterbury be one of the friend whom Esmond may mention , as the brave bishop be now beyond exile and persecution , and to he , and one or two more , the Colonel open himself of a scheme of his own , that , back by a little resolution on the Prince 's part , could not fail of bring about the accomplishment of their dearest wish . 
 my young Lord Viscount Castlewood have not come to England to keep his majority , and have now be absent from the country for several year . the year when his sister be to be marry and Duke Hamilton die , my lord be keep at Bruxelles by his wife 's lying - in . the gentle Clotilda could not bear her husband out of her sight ; perhaps she mistrust the young scapegrace should he ever get loose from her lead - string ; and she keep he by her side to nurse the baby and administer posset to the gossip . many a laugh poor beatrix have have about Frank 's uxoriousness : his mother would have go to Clotilda when her time be come , but that the mother - in - law be already in possession , and the negotiation for poor beatrix 's marriage be begin . a few month after the horrid catastrophe in Hyde Park , my mistress and her daughter retire to Castlewood , where my lord , it be expect , would soon join they . but , to say truth , their quiet household be little to his taste ; he could be get to come to Walcote but once after his first campaign ; and then the young rogue spend more than half his time in London , not appear at Court or in public under his own name and title , but frequent play , bagnio , and the very bad company , under the name of Captain Esmond   whereby his innocent kinsman get more than once into trouble ; and so under various pretext , and in pursuit of all sort of pleasure , until he plunge into the lawful one of marriage , Frank Castlewood have remain away from this country , and be unknown , save amongst the gentleman of the army , with whom he have serve abroad . the fond heart of his mother be pain by this long absence . ' twas all that Henry Esmond could do to soothe her natural mortification , and find excuse for his kinsman 's levity . 
 in the autumn of the year 1713 , Lord Castlewood think of return home . his first child have be a daughter ; Clotilda be in the way of gratify his lordship with a second , and the pious youth think that , by bring his wife to his ancestral home , by prayer to St. Philip of Castlewood , and what not , heaven might be induce to bless he with a son this time , for whose come the expectant mamma be very anxious . 
 the long - debate peace have be proclaim this year at the end of March ; and France be open to we . just as Frank 's poor mother have make all thing ready for Lord Castlewood 's reception , and be eagerly expect her son , it be by Colonel Esmond 's mean that the kind lady be disappoint of her longing , and oblige to defer once more the darling hope of her heart . 
 esmond take horse to Castlewood . he have not see its ancient gray tower and well - remember wood for nearly fourteen year , and since he ride thence with my lord , to whom his mistress with her young child by her side wave an adieu . what age seem to have pass since then , what year of action and passion , of care , love , hope , disaster ! the child be grow up now , and have story of their own . as for Esmond , he feel to be a hundred year old ; his dear mistress only seem unchanged ; she look and welcome he quite as of old . there be the fountain in the court babble its familiar music , the old hall and its furniture , the carve chair my late lord use , the very flagon he drink from . Esmond 's mistress know he would like to sleep in the little room he use to occupy ; ' twa make ready for he , and wall - flower and sweet herb set in the adjoining chamber , the chaplain 's room . 
 in tear of not unmanly emotion , with prayer of submission to the awful Dispenser of death and life , of good and evil fortune , Mr. Esmond pass a part of that first night at Castlewood , lie awake for many hour as the clock keep tolling   in tone so well remember , look back , as all man will , that revisit their home of childhood , over the great gulf of time , and survey himself on the distant bank yonder , a sad little melancholy boy with his lord still alive his dear mistress , a girl yet , her child sport around she . year ago , a boy on that very bed , when she have bless he and call he her knight , he have make a vow to be faithful and never desert her dear service . have he keep that fond boyish promise ? yes , before heaven ; yes , praise be to God ! his life have be her ; his blood , his fortune , his name , his whole heart ever since have be hers and her child 's . all night long he be dream his boyhood over again , and wake fitfully ; he half fancy he hear Father Holt call to he from the next chamber , and that he be come in and out of from the mysterious window . 
 Esmond rise up before the dawn , pass into the next room , where the air be heavy with the odor of the wall - flower ; look into the brazier where the paper have be burn , into the old press where Holt 's book and paper have be keep , and try the spring and whether the window work still . the spring have not be touch for year , but yield at length , and the whole fabric of the window sink down . he lift it and it relapse into its frame ; no one have ever pass thence since Holt use it sixteen year ago . 
 Esmond remember his poor lord say , on the last day of his life , that Holt use to come in and out of the house like a ghost , and know that the Father like these mystery , and practise such secret disguise , entrance and exit : this be the way the ghost come and go , his pupil have always conjecture . esmond close the casement up again as the dawn be rise over Castlewood village ; he could hear the clinking at the blacksmith 's forge yonder among the tree , across the green , and past the river , on which a mist still lie sleep . 
 Next Esmond open that long cupboard over the woodwork of the mantel - piece , big enough to hold a man , and in which Mr. Holt use to keep sundry secret property of his . the two sword he remember so well as a boy , lie actually there still , and Esmond take they out and wipe they , with a strange curiosity of emotion . there be a bundle of paper here , too , which no doubt have be leave at Holt 's last visit to the place , in my Lord Viscount 's life , that very day when the priest have be arrest and take to Hexham Castle . esmond make free with these paper , and find treasonable matter of King William 's reign , the name of Charnock and Perkins , Sir John Fenwick and Sir John Friend , Rookwood and Lodwick , Lords Montgomery and Allesbury , Clarendon and Yarmouth , that have all be engage in plot against the usurper ; a letter from the Duke of Berwick too , and one from the King at St. Germains , offer to confer upon his trusty and well - beloved Francis Viscount Castlewood the title of Earl and Marquis of Esmond , bestow by patent royal , and in the fourth year of his reign , upon Thomas Viscount Castlewood and the heir - male of his body , in default of which issue the rank and dignity be to pass to Francis aforesaid . 
 this be the paper , whereof my lord have speak , which Holt show he the very day he be arrest , and for an answer to which he would come back in a week 's time . I put these paper hastily into the crypt whence I have take they , be interrupt by a tapping of a light finger at the ring of the chamber - door : ' twa my kind mistress , with her face full of love and welcome . she , too , have pass the night wakefuly , no doubt ; but neither ask the other how the hour have be spend . there be thing we divine without speak , and know though they happen out of our sight . this fond lady hath tell I that she know both day when I be wound abroad . who shall say how far sympathy reach , and how truly love can prophesy ? " I look into your room , " be all she say ; " the bed be vacant , the little old bed ! I know I should find you here . " and tender and blush faintly with a benediction in her eye , the gentle creature kiss he . 
 they walk out , hand - in - hand , through the old court , and to the terrace - walk , where the grass be glisten with dew , and the bird in the green wood above be sing their delicious chorus under the blushing morning sky . how well all thing be remember ! the ancient tower and gable of the hall darkle against the east , the purple shadow on the green slope , the quaint device and carving of the dial , the forest - crown height , the fair yellow plain cheerful with crop and corn , the shin river roll through it towards the pearly hill beyond ; all these be before we , along with a thousand beautiful memory of our youth , beautiful and sad , but as real and vivid in our mind as that fair and always - remember scene our eye beheld once more . we forget nothing . the memory sleep , but waken again ; I often think how it shall be when , after the last sleep of death , the reveillee shall arouse we for ever , and the past in one flash of self - consciousness rush back , like the soul revivify . 
 the house would not be up for some hour yet ,   it be July , and the dawn be only just awake ,   and here Esmond open himself to his mistress , of the business he have in hand , and what part Frank be to play in it . he know he could confide anything to she , and that the fond soul would die rather than reveal it ; and bid she keep the secret from all , he lay it entirely before his mistress   always as staunch a little loyalist as any in the kingdom , and indeed be quite sure that any plan , of his be secure of her applause and sympathy . never be such a glorious scheme to her partial mind , never such a devoted knight to execute it . an hour or two may have pass whilst they be have their colloquy . beatrix come out to they just as their talk be over ; her tall beautiful form rob in sable   which she wear without ostentation ever since last year 's catastrophe , sweep over the green terrace , and cast its shadow before she across the grass . 
 she make we one of her grand curtsy smile , and call we " the young people . " she be old , pale , and more majestic than in the year before ; her mother seem the young of the two . she never once speak of her grief , Lady Castlewood tell Esmond , or allude , save by a quiet word or two , to the death of her hope . 
 when beatrix come back to Castlewood she take to visit all the cottage and all the sick . she set up a school of child , and teach singing to some of they . we have a pair of beautiful old organ in Castlewood Church , on which she play admirably , so that the music there become to be know in the country for many mile round , and no doubt people come to see the fair organist as well as to hear she . Parson Tusher and his wife be establish at the vicarage , but his wife have bring he no child wherewith Tom might meet his enemy at the gate . Honest Tom take care not to have many such , his great shovel - hat be in his hand for everybody . he be profuse of bow and compliment . he behave to Esmond as if the Colonel have be a Commander - in - Chief ; he din at the hall that day , be Sunday , and would not partake of pudding except under extreme pressure . he deplore my lord 's perversion , but drink his lordship 's health very devoutly ; and an hour before at church send the Colonel to sleep , with a long , learn , and refresh sermon . 
 Esmond 's visit home be but for two day ; the business he have in hand call he away and out of the country . Ere he go , he see Beatrix but once alone , and then she summon he out of the long tapestry room , where he and his mistress be sit , quite as in old time , into the adjoining chamber , that have be Viscountess Isabel 's sleep apartment , and where Esmond perfectly well remember see the old lady sit up in the bed , in her night - rail , that morning when the troop of guard come to fetch she . the most beautiful woman in England lie in that bed now , whereof the great damask hanging be scarce fade since Esmond see they last . 
 here stand Beatrix in her black robe , hold a box in her hand ; ' twa that which Esmond have give she before her marriage , stamp with a coronet which the disappointed girl be never to wear ; and contain his aunt 's legacy of diamond . 
 " you have well take these with you , Harry , " say she ; " I have no need of diamond any more . " there be not the least token of emotion in her quiet low voice . she hold out the black shagreen case with her fair arm , that do not shake in the least . esmond see she wear a black velvet bracelet on it , with my Lord Duke 's picture in enamel ; he have give it she but three day before he fall . 
 Esmond say the stone be his no long , and strove to turn off that proffer restoration with a laugh : " of what good , " say he , " be they to I ? the diamond loop to his hat do not set off Prince Eugene , and will not make my yellow face look any handsomer . " 
 " you will give they to your wife , cousin , " say she . " my cousin , your wife have a lovely complexion and shape . " 
 " beatrix , " esmond burst out , the old fire flame out as it would at time , " will you wear those trinket at your marriage ? you whisper once you do not know I : you know I well now : how I seek , what I have sigh for , for ten year , what foregone ! " 
 " a price for your constancy , my lord ! " say she ; ' such a preux chevalier want to be pay . oh fie , cousin ! " 
 " again , " Esmond speak out , " if I do something you have at heart ; something worthy of I and you ; something that shall make I a name with which to endow you ; will you take it ? there be a chance for I once , you say ; be it impossible to recall it ? never shake your head , but hear I ; say you will hear I a year hence . if I come back to you and bring you fame , will that please you ? if I do what you desire most what he who be dead desire most will that soften you ? " 
 " what be it , Henry ? " say she , her face light up ; " what mean you ? " 
 " ask no question , " he say ; " wait , and give I but time ; if I bring back that you long for , that I have a thousand time hear you pray for , will you have no reward for he who have do you that service ? put away those trinket , keep they : it shall not be at my marriage , it shall not be at your ; but if man can do it , I swear a day shall come when there shall be a feast in your house , and you shall be proud to wear they . I say no more now ; put aside these word , and lock away yonder box until the day when I shall remind you of both . all I pray of you now be , to wait and to remember . " 
 " you be go out of the country ? " say beatrix , in some agitation . 
 " yes , to - morrow , " say Esmond . 
 " To Lorraine , cousin ? " say Beatrix , lay her hand on his arm ; ' twa the hand on which she wear the Duke 's bracelet . " stay , Harry ! " continue she , with a tone that have more despondency in it than she be accustom to show . " hear a last word . I do love you . I do admire you who would not , that have know such love as yours have be for we all ? but I think I have no heart ; at least I have never see the man that could touch it ; and , have I find he , I would have follow he in rag have he be a private soldier , or to sea , like one of those buccaneer you use to read to we about when we be child . I would do anything for such a man , bear anything for he : but I never find one . you be ever too much of a slave to win my heart ; even my Lord Duke could not command it . I have not be happy have I marry he . I know that three month after our engagement and be too vain to break it . oh , Harry ! I cry once or twice , not for he , but with tear of rage because I could not be sorry for he . I be frightened to find I be glad of his death ; and be I join to you , I should have the same sense of servitude , the same longing to escape . we should both be unhappy , and you the most , who be as jealous as the Duke be himself . I try to love he ; I try , indeed I do : affect gladness when he come : submit to hear when he be by I , and try the wife 's part I think I be to play for the rest of my day . but half an hour of that complaisance weary I , and what would a lifetime be ? my thought be away when he be speak ; and I be think , oh that this man would drop my hand , and rise up from before my foot ! I know his great and noble quality , great and nobler than mine a thousand time , as yours be , cousin , I tell you , a million and a million time well . but ' twa not for these I take he . I take he to have a great place in the world , and I lose it . I lose it , and do not deplore he and I often think , as I listen to his fond vow and ardent word , oh , if I yield to this man , and meet the other , I shall hate he and leave he ! I be not good , Harry : my mother be gentle and good like an angel . I wonder how she should have have such a child . she be weak , but she would die rather than do a wrong ; I be strong than she , but I would do it out of defiance . I do not care for what the parson tell I with their droning sermon : I use to see they at court as mean and as worthless as the mean woman there . oh , I be sick and weary of the world ! I wait but for one thing , and when ' tis do , I will take Frank 's religion and your poor mother 's , and go into a nunnery , and end like she . shall I wear the diamond then ? they say the nun wear their good trinket the day they take the veil . I will put they away as you bid I ; farewell , cousin : mamma be pace the next room rack her little head to know what we have be say . she be jealous , all woman be . I sometimes think that be the only womanly quality I have . " 
 " Farewell . Farewell , brother . " she give he her cheek as a brotherly privilege . the cheek be as cold as marble . 
 Esmond 's mistress show no sign of jealousy when he return to the room where she be . she have school herself so as to look quite inscrutably , when she have a mind . amongst her other feminine quality she have that of be a perfect dissembler . 
 he ride away from Castlewood to attempt the task he be bind on , and stand or fall by it ; in truth his state of mind be such , that he be eager for some outward excitement to counteract that gnaw malady which he be inwardly endure . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 I travel to FRANCE and bring home a portrait of RIGAUD . 

 Mr. Esmond do not think fit to take leave at Court , or to inform all the world of Pall Mall and the coffee - house , that he be about to quit England ; and choose to depart in the most private manner possible . he procure a pass as for a Frenchman , through Dr. Atterbury , who do that business for he , get the signature even from Lord Bolingbroke 's office , without any personal application to the Secretary . Lockwood , his faithful servant , he take with he to Castlewood , and leave behind there : give out ere he leave London that he himself be sick , and go to Hampshire for country air , and so depart as silently as might be upon his business . 
 as Frank Castlewood 's aid be indispensable for Mr. Esmond 's scheme , his first visit be to Bruxelles   pass by way of Antwerp , where the Duke of Marlborough be in exile , and in the first - name place Harry find his dear young Benedict , the married man , who appear to be rather out of humor with his matrimonial chain , and clog with the obstinate embrace which Clotilda keep round his neck . Colonel Esmond be not present to she ; but Monsieur Simon be , a gentleman of the Royal Cravat   Esmond bethink he of the regiment of his honest Irishman , whom he have see that day after Malplaquet , when he first set eye on the young king ; and Monsieur Simon be introduce to the Viscountess Castlewood , nee Comptesse Wertheim ; to the numerous count , the Lady Clotilda 's tall brother ; to her father the chamberlain ; and to the lady his wife , Frank 's mother - in - law , a tall and majestic person of large proportion , such as become the mother of such a company of grenadier as her warlike son form . the whole race be at free quarter in the little castle nigh to Bruxelles which Frank have take ; ride his horse ; drink his wine ; and live easily at the poor lad 's charge .
