Mr. Esmond have always maintain a perfect fluency in the French , which be his mother tongue ; and if this family   that speak French with the twang which the Flemings use   discover any inaccuracy in Mr. Simon 's pronunciation , ' twa to be attribute to the latter 's long residence in England , where he have marry and remain ever since he be take prisoner at Blenheim . his story be perfectly pat ; there be none there to doubt it save honest Frank , and he be charm with his kinsman 's scheme , when he become acquainted with it ; and , in truth , always admire Colonel Esmond with an affectionate fidelity , and think his cousin the wise and good of all cousin and man . Frank enter heart and soul into the plan , and like it the well as it be to take he to Paris , out of reach of his brother , his father , and his mother - in - law , whose attention rather fatigue he . 
 Castlewood , I have say , be bear in the same year as the Prince of Wales ; have not a little of the Prince 's air , height , and figure ; and , especially since he have see the Chevalier de St. George on the occasion before - name , take no small pride in his resemblance to a person so illustrious ; which likeness he increase by all mean in his power , wear fair brown periwig , such as the Prince wear , and ribbon , and so forth , of the Chevalier 's color . 
 this resemblance be , in truth , the circumstance on which Mr. Esmond 's scheme be found ; and have secure Frank 's secrecy and enthusiasm , he leave he to continue his journey , and see the other personage on whom its success depend . the place whither Mr. Simon next travel be Bar , in Lorraine , where that merchant arrive with a consignment of broadcloth , valuable lace from Malines , and letter for his correspondent there . 
 would you know how a prince , heroic from misfortune , and descend from a line of king , whose race seem to be doom like the Atridae of old would you know how he be employ , when the envoy who come to he through danger and difficulty behold he for the first time ? the young king , in a flannel jacket , be at tennis with the gentleman of his suite , cry out after the ball , and swear like the mean of his subject . the next time Mr. Esmond see he , ' twa when Monsieur Simon take a packet of lace to Miss Oglethorpe : the Prince 's ante - chamber in those day , at which ignoble door man be force to knock for admission to his Majesty . the admission be give , the envoy find the King and the mistress together ; the pair be at card and his Majesty be in liquor . he care more for three honor than three kingdom ; and a half - dozen glass of ratafia make he forget all his woe and his loss , his father 's crown , and his grandfather 's head . 
 Mr. Esmond do not open himself to the Prince then . his Majesty be scarce in a condition to hear he ; and he doubt whether a King who drink so much could keep a secret in his fuddle head ; or whether a hand that shake so , be strong enough to grasp at a crown . however , at last , and after take counsel with the Prince 's adviser , amongst whom be many gentleman , honest and faithful , Esmond 's plan be lay before the king , and her actual Majesty Queen Oglethorpe , in council . the Prince like the scheme well enough ; ' twa easy and daring , and suit to his reckless gayety and lively youthful spirit . in the morning after he have sleep his wine off , he be very gay , lively , and agreeable . his manner have an extreme charm of archness , and a kind simplicity ; and , to do her justice , her Oglethorpean Majesty be kind , acute , resolute , and of good counsel ; she give the Prince much good advice that he be too weak to follow , and love he with a fidelity which he return with an ingratitude quite royal . 
 have his own foreboding regard his scheme should it ever be fulfil , and his usual sceptic doubt as to the benefit which might accrue to the country by bring a tipsy young monarch back to it , Colonel Esmond have his audience of leave and quiet . Monsieur Simon take his departure . at any rate the youth at Bar be as good as the old Pretender at Hanover ; if the bad come to the bad , the Englishman could be deal with as easy as the German . Monsieur Simon trot on that long journey from Nancy to Paris , and see that famous town , stealthily and like a spy , as in truth he be ; and where , sure , more magnificence and more misery be heap together , more rag and lace , more filth and gilding , than in any city in this world . here he be put in communication with the King 's good friend , his half brother , the famous Duke of Berwick ; Esmond recognize he as the stranger who have visit Castlewood now near twenty year ago . his grace open to he when he find that Mr. Esmond be one of Webb 's brave regiment , that have once be his grace 's own . he be the sword and buckler indeed of the Stuart cause : there be no stain on his shield except the bar across it , which Marlborough 's sister leave he . have Berwick be his father 's heir , James the Third have assuredly sit on the english throne . he could dare , endure , strike , speak , be silent . the fire and genius , perhaps , he have not   that be give to base man , but except these he have some of the good quality of a leader . his grace know Esmond 's father and history ; and hint at the latter in such a way as make the Colonel to think he be aware of the particular of that story . but Esmond do not choose to enter on it , nor do the Duke press he . Mr. Esmond say , " no doubt he should come by his name if ever great people come by theirs . " 
 what confirm Esmond in his notion that the Duke of Berwick know of his case be , that when the Colonel go to pay his duty at St. Germains , her Majesty once address he by the title of Marquis . he take the Queen the dutiful remembrance of her goddaughter , and the lady whom , in the day of her prosperity , her Majesty have befriend . the Queen remember Rachel Esmond perfectly well , have hear of my Lord Castlewood 's conversion , and be much edify by that act of heaven in his favor . she know that other of that family have be of the only true church too : " your father and your mother , M. le Marquis , " her Majesty say   that be the only time she use the phrase . Monsieur Simon bow very low , and say he have find other parent than his own , who have teach he differently ; but these have only one king : on which her Majesty be pleased to give he a medal bless by the Pope , which have be find very efficacious in case similar to his own , and to promise she would offer up prayer for his conversion and that of the family : which no doubt this pious lady do , though up to the present moment , and after twenty - seven year , Colonel Esmond be bind to say that neither the medal nor the prayer have have the slight know effect upon his religious conviction . 
 as for the splendor of Versailles , Monsieur Simon , the merchant , only behold they as a humble and distant spectator , see the old king but once , when he go to feed his carps ; and ask for no presentation at his Majesty 's Court . 
 by this time my Lord Viscount Castlewood be get to Paris , where , as the London print presently announce , her ladyship be bring to bed of a son and heir . for a long while afterwards she be in a delicate state of health , and order by the physician not to travel ; otherwise ' twa well know that the Viscount Castlewood propose return to England , and take up his residence at his own seat . 
 whilst he remain at Paris , my Lord Castlewood have his picture do by the famous french painter , Monsieur Rigaud , a present for his mother in London ; and this piece Monsieur Simon take back with he when he return to that city , which he reach about May , in the year 1714 , very soon after which time my Lady Castlewood and her daughter , and their kinsman , Colonel Esmond , who have be at Castlewood all this time , likewise return to London ; her ladyship occupy her house at Kensington , Mr. Esmond return to his lodging at Knightsbridge , nearer the town , and once more make his appearance at all public place , his health greatly improve by his long stay in the country . 
 the portrait of my lord , in a handsome gilt frame , be hang up in the place of honor in her ladyship 's drawing - room . his lordship be represent in his scarlet uniform of Captain of the Guard , with a light brown periwig , a cuirass under his coat , a blue ribbon , and a fall of Bruxelles lace . many of her ladyship 's friend admire the piece beyond measure , and flock to see it ; Bishop Atterbury , Mr. Lesly , good old Mr. Collier , and other amongst the clergy , be delighted with the performance , and many among the first quality examine and praise it ; only I must own that Doctor Tusher happen to come up to London , and see the picture ,   it be ordinarily cover by a curtain , but on this day Miss Beatrix happen to be look at it when the Doctor arrive ,   the Vicar of Castlewood vow he could not see any resemblance in the piece to his old pupil , except , perhaps , a little about the chin and the periwig ; but we all of we convince he that he have not see Frank for five year or more ; that he know no more about the Fine Arts than a ploughboy , and that he must be mistake ; and we send he home assure that the piece be an excellent likeness . as for my Lord Bolingbroke , who honor her ladyship with a visit occasionally , when Colonel Esmond show he the picture he burst out laugh , and ask what devilry he be engage on ? esmond own simply that the portrait be not that of Viscount Castlewood ; besought the Secretary on his honor to keep the secret ; say that the lady of the house be enthusiastic Jacobites , as be well know ; and confess that the picture be that of the Chevalier St. George . 
 the truth be , that Mr. Simon , wait upon Lord Castlewood one day at Monsieur Rigaud 's whilst his lordship be sit for his picture , affect to be much strike with a piece represent the Chevalier , whereof the head only be finish , and purchase it of the painter for a hundred crown . it have be intend , the artist say , for Miss Oglethorpe , the Prince 's mistress , but that young lady quit Paris , have leave the work on the artist 's hand ; and take this piece home , when my lord 's portrait arrive , Colonel Esmond , alias Monsieur Simon , have copy the uniform and other accessory from my lord 's picture to fill up Rigaud 's incomplete canvas : the Colonel all his life have be a practitioner of painting , and especially follow it during his long residence in the city of Flanders , among the masterpiece of Van Dyck and Rubens . my grandson hath the piece , such as it be , in Virginia now . 
 at the commencement of the month of June , Miss Beatrix Esmond , and my Lady Viscountess , her mother , arrive from Castlewood ; the former to resume her service at Court , which have be interrupt by the fatal catastrophe of Duke Hamilton 's death . she once more take her place , then , in her Majesty 's suite and at the Maids ' table , be always a favorite with Mrs. Masham , the Queen 's chief woman , partly perhaps on account of their bitterness against the Duchess of Marlborough , whom Miss Beatrix love no well than her rival do . the gentleman about the Court , my Lord Bolingbroke amongst other , own that the young lady have come back handsomer than ever , and that the serious and tragic air which her face now involuntarily wear become she well than her former smile and archness . 
 all the old domestic at the little house of Kensington Square be change ; the old steward that have serve the family any time these five - and - twenty year , since the birth of the child of the house , be despatch into the kingdom of Ireland to see my lord 's estate there : the housekeeper , who have be my lady 's woman time out of mind , and the attendant of the young child , be send away grumble to Walcote , to see to the new painting and prepare of that house , which my Lady Dowager intend to occupy for the future , give up Castlewood to her daughter - in - law that might be expect daily from France . another servant the Viscountess have be dismiss too with a gratuity on the pretext that her ladyship 's train of domestic must be diminish ; so , finally , there be not leave in the household a single person who have belong to it during the time my young Lord Castlewood be yet at home . 
 for the plan which Colonel Esmond have in view , and the stroke he intend , ' twa necessary that the very small number of person should be put in possession of his secret . it scarce be know , except to three or four out of his family , and it be keep to a wonder . 
 on the 10th of June , 1714 , there come by Mr. Prior 's messenger from Paris a letter from my Lord Viscount Castlewood to his mother , say that he have be foolish in regard of money matter , that he be ashamed to own he have lose at play , and by other extravagance ; and that instead of have great entertainment as he have hop at Castlewood this year , he must live as quiet as he could , and make every effort to be save . so far every word of poor Frank 's letter be true , nor be there a doubt that he and his tall brother - in - law have spend a great deal more than they ought , and engage the revenue of the Castlewood property , which the fond mother have husband and improve so carefully during the time of her guardianship . 
 his " Clotilda , " Castlewood go on to say , " be still delicate , and her physician think her lie - in have good take place at Paris . he should come without her ladyship , and be at his mother 's house about the 17th or 18th day of June , propose to take horse from Paris immediately , and bring but a single servant with he ; and he request that the lawyer of Gray 's inn might be invite to meet he with their account , and the land - steward come from Castlewood with his , so that he might settle with they speedily , raise a sum of money whereof he stand in need , and be back to his viscountess by the time of her lie - in . " then his lordship give some of the news of the town , send his remembrance to kinsfolk , and so the letter end . ' twa put in the common post , and no doubt the french police and the English there have a copy of it , to which they be exceed welcome . 
 two day after another letter be despatch by the public post of France , in the same open way , and this , after give news of the fashion at Court there , end by the following sentence , in which , but for those that have the key , ' twould be difficult for any man to find any secret lurk at all : 
 " the King will take   medicine on Thursday . his Majesty be well than he hath be of late , though incommode by indigestion from his too great appetite . Madame Maintenon continue well . they have perform a play of mon . Racine at St. Cyr . the Duke of Shrewsbury and Mr. Prior , our envoy , and all the english nobility here be present at it .   the Viscount Castlewood 's passport   be refuse to he , ' twa say ; his lordship be sue by a goldsmith for Vaisselle plate , and a pearl necklace supply to Mademoiselle Meruel of the French Comedy . ' tis a pity such news should get abroad   and travel to England   about our young nobility here . Mademoiselle Meruel have be send to the Fort l'Evesque ; they say she have order not only plate , but furniture , and a chariot and horse   under that lord 's name , of which extravagance his unfortunate Viscountess know nothing . 
 " his Majesty will be   eighty - two year of age on his next birthday . the Court prepare to celebrate it with a great feast . Mr. Prior be in a sad way about their refuse at home to send he his plate . all here admire my Lord Viscount 's portrait , and say it be a masterpiece of Rigaud . have you see it ? it be   at the Lady Castlewood 's house in Kensington Square . I think no english painter could produce such a piece . 
 " our poor friend the Abbe hath be at the Bastile , but be now transport to the Conciergerie   where his friend may visit he . they be to ask for   a remission of his sentence soon . let we hope the poor rogue will have repent in prison . 
 " the Lord Castlewood   have have the affair of the plate make up , and depart for England . 
 " be not this a dull letter ? I have a cursed headache with drink with Mat and some more over - night , and tipsy or sober am 
 " thine ever . " 
 all this letter , save some dozen of word which I have put above between bracket , be mere idle talk , though the substance of the letter be as important as any letter well could be . it tell those that have the key , that the King will take the Viscount Castlewood 's passport and travel to England under that lord 's name . his Majesty will be at the Lady Castlewood 's house in Kensington Square , where his friend may visit he ; they be to ask for the Lord Castlewood . this note may have pass under Mr. Prior 's eye , and those of our new ally the French , and teach they nothing ; though it explain sufficiently to person in London what the event be which be about to happen , as ' twill show those who read my memoir a hundred year hence , what be that errand on which Colonel Esmond of late have be busy . silently and swiftly to do that about which other be conspire , and thousand of Jacobites all over the country clumsily caballing ; alone to effect that which the leader here be only talk about ; to bring the Prince of Wales into the country openly in the face of all , under Bolingbroke 's very eye , the wall placard with the proclamation sign with the Secretary 's name , and offer five hundred pound reward for his apprehension : this be a stroke , the playing and winning of which might well give any adventurous spirit pleasure : the loss of the stake might involve a heavy penalty , but all our family be eager to risk that for the glorious chance of win the game . 
 nor shall it be call a game , save perhaps with the chief player , who be not more or less sceptical than most public man with whom he have acquaintance in that age .   be there ever a public man in England that altogether believe in his party ? be there one , however doubtful , that will not fight for it ?   Young Frank be ready to fight without much thinking , he be a Jacobite as his father before he be ; all the Esmonds be royalist . give he but the word , he would cry , " God save King James ! " before the palace guard , or at the Maypole in the Strand ; and with respect to the woman , as be usual with they , ' twa not a question of party but of faith ; their belief be a passion ; either Esmond 's mistress or her daughter would have die for it cheerfully . I have laugh often , talk of King William 's reign , and say I think Lady Castlewood be disappoint the King do not persecute the family more ; and those who know the nature of woman may fancy for themselves , what need not here be write down , the rapture with which these neophyte receive the mystery when make known to they ; the eagerness with which they look forward to its completion ; the reverence which they pay the minister who initiate they into that secret Truth , now know only to a few , but presently to reign over the world . sure there be no bound to the trustingness of woman . look at Arria worship the drunken clodpate of a husband who beat she ; look at Cornelia treasure as a jewel in her maternal heart the oaf her son ; I have know a woman preach Jesuit 's bark , and afterwards Dr. Berkeley 's tar - water , as though to swallow they be a divine decree , and to refuse they no well than blasphemy . 
 on his return from France Colonel Esmond put himself at the head of this little knot of fond conspirator . no death or torture he know would frighten they out of their constancy . when he detail his plan for bring the King back , his eld mistress think that that restoration be to be attribute under heaven to the Castlewood family and to its chief , and she worship and love Esmond , if that could be , more than ever she have do . she doubt not for one moment of the success of his scheme , to mistrust which would have seem impious in her eye . and as for beatrix , when she become acquainted with the plan , and join it , as she do with all her heart , she give Esmond one of she search bright look . " ah , Harry , " say she , " why be you not the head of our house ? you be the only one fit to raise it ; why do you give that silly boy the name and the honor ? but ' tis so in the world ; those get the prize that do not   deserve or care for it . I wish I could give you your silly prize , cousin , but I can not ; I have try , and I can not . " and she go away , shake her head mournfully , but always , it seem to Esmond , that her liking and respect for he be greatly increase , since she know what capability he have both to act and bear ; to do and to forego . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 the ORIGINAL of the portrait come to ENGLAND . 

 ' Twas announce in the family that my Lord Castlewood would arrive , have a confidential french gentleman in his suite , who act as secretary to his lordship , and who , be a Papist , and a foreigner of a good family , though now in rather a menial place , would have his meal serve in his chamber , and not with the domestic of the house . the Viscountess give up her bedchamber contiguous to her daughter 's , and have a large convenient closet attach to it , in which a bed be put up , ostensibly for Monsieur Baptiste , the Frenchman ; though , ' tis needless to say , when the door of the apartment be lock , and the two guest retire within it , the young viscount become the servant of the illustrious Prince whom he entertain , and give up gladly the more convenient and airy chamber and bed to his master . Madam Beatrix also retire to the upper region , her chamber be convert into a sitting - room for my lord . the well to carry the deceit , beatrix affect to grumble before the servant , and to be jealous that she be turn out of her chamber to make way for my lord . 
 no small preparation be make , you may be sure , and no slight tremor of expectation cause the heart of the gentle lady of Castlewood to flutter , before the arrival of the personage who be about to honor their house . the chamber be ornament with flower ; the bed cover with the very fine of linen ; the two lady insist on make it themselves , and kneel down at the bedside and kiss the sheet out of respect for the web that be to hold the sacred person of a king . the toilet be of silver and crystal ; there be a copy of " Eikon Basilike " lay on the writing - table ; a portrait of the martyr King hang always over the mantel , have a sword of my poor Lord Castlewood underneath it , and a little picture or emblem which the widow love always to have before her eye on wake , and in which the hair of her lord and her two child be work together . her book of private devotion , as they be all of the English Church , she carry away with she to the upper apartment , which she destine for herself . the lady show Mr. Esmond , when they be complete , the fond preparation they have make . ' twas then Beatrix knelt down and kiss the linen sheet . as for her mother , Lady Castlewood make a curtsy at the door , as she would have do to the altar on enter a church , and own that she consider the chamber in a manner sacred . 
 the company in the servant ' hall never for a moment suppose that these preparation be make for any other person than the young viscount , the lord of the house , whom his fond mother have be for so many year without see . both lady be perfect housewife , have the great skill in the making of confection , scented water ,   c. , and keep a notable superintendence over the kitchen . calf enough be kill to feed an army of prodigal son , Esmond think , and laugh when he come to wait on the lady , on the day when the guest be to arrive , to find two pair of the fine and roundest arm to be see in England   my Lady Castlewood be remarkable for this beauty of her person , cover with flour up above the elbow , and prepare paste , and turn rolling - pin in the housekeeper 's closet . the guest would not arrive till supper - time , and my lord would prefer have that meal in his own chamber . you may be sure the bright plate of the house be lay out there , and can understand why it be that the lady insist that they alone would wait upon the young chief of the family . 
 take horse , Colonel Esmond ride rapidly to Rochester , and there await the King in that very town where his father have last set his foot on the English shore . a room have be provide at an inn there for my Lord Castlewood and his servant ; and Colonel Esmond time his ride so well that he have scarce be half an hour in the place , and be look over the balcony into the yard of the inn , when two traveller ride in at the inn gate , and the Colonel run down , the next moment embrace his dear young lord . 
 my lord 's companion , act the part of a domestic , dismount , and be for hold the viscount 's stirrup ; but Colonel Esmond , call to his own man , who be in the court , bid he take the horse and settle with the lad who have ride the post along with the two traveller , cry out in a cavalier tone in the french language to my lord 's companion , and affect to grumble that my lord 's fellow be a Frenchman , and do not know the money or habit of the country : " my man will see to the horse , Baptiste , " say Colonel Esmond : " do you understand English ? " " very leetle ! " " so , follow my lord and wait upon he at dinner in his own room . " the landlord and his people come up presently bear the dish ; ' twa well they make a noise and stir in the gallery , or they might have find Colonel Esmond on his knee before Lord Castlewood 's servant , welcome his Majesty to his kingdom , and kiss the hand of the king . we tell the landlord that the Frenchman would wait on his master ; and Esmond 's man be order to keep sentry in the gallery without the door . the Prince din with a good appetite , laugh and talk very gayly , and condescendingly bid his two companion to sit with he at table . he be in well spirit than poor Frank Castlewood , who esmond think might be woe - begone on account of part with his divine Clotilda ; but the Prince wish to take a short siesta after dinner , and retire to an inner chamber where there be a bed , the cause of poor Frank 's discomfiture come out ; and burst into tear , with many expression of fondness , friendship , and humiliation , the faithful lad give his kinsman to understand that he now know all the truth , and the sacrifice which Colonel Esmond have make for he . 
 see no good in acquaint poor Frank with that secret , Mr. Esmond have entreat his mistress also not to reveal it to her son . the Prince have tell the poor lad all as they be rid from Dover : " I have as lief he have shoot I , cousin , " Frank say : " I know you be the good , and the brave , and the kind of all man "   so the enthusiastic young fellow go on ; " but I never think I owe you what I do , and can scarce bear the weight of the obligation . " 
 " I stand in the place of your father , " say Mr. Esmond , kindly , " and sure a father may dispossess himself in favor of his son . I abdicate the twopenny crown , and invest you with the kingdom of Brentford ; do not   be a fool and cry ; you make a much tall and handsomer viscount than ever I could . " but the fond boy , with oath and protestation , laughter and incoherent outbreak of passionate emotion , could not be get , for some little time , to put up with Esmond 's raillery ; want to kneel down to he , and kiss his hand ; ask he and implore he to order something , to bid Castlewood give his own life or take somebody else 's ; anything , so that he might show his gratitude for the generosity Esmond show he . 
 " the K - , he laugh , " Frank say , point to the door where the sleeper be , and speak in a low tone . " I do not   think he should have laugh as he tell I the story . as we ride along from Dover , talk in French , he speak about you , and your come to he at Bar ; he call you ' le grand serieux , ' Don Bellianis of Greece , and I do not   know what name ; mimic your manner "   here Castlewood laugh himself   " and he do it very well . he seem to sneer at everything . he be not like a king : somehow Harry , I fancy you be like a king . he do not seem to think what a stake we be all play . he would have stop at Canterbury to run after a barmaid there , have I not implore he to come on . he hath a house at Chaillot , where he use to go and bury himself for week away from the Queen , and with all sort of bad company , " say Frank , with a demure look ; " you may smile , but I be not the wild fellow I be ; no , no , I have be teach well , " say Castlewood devoutly , make a sign on his breast . 
 " thou art my dear brave boy , " say Colonel Esmond , touch at the young fellow 's simplicity , " and there will be a noble gentleman at Castlewood so long as my Frank be there . " 
 the impetuous young lad be for go down on his knee again , with another explosion of gratitude , but that we hear the voice from the next chamber of the august sleeper , just wake , call out : " eh , La - Fleur , un verre d'eau ! " his Majesty come out yawn : " a pest , " say he , " upon your English ale , ' tis so strong that , ma foi , it hath turn my head . " 
 the effect of the ale be like a spur upon our horse , and we ride very quickly to London , reach Kensington at nightfall . Mr. Esmond 's servant be leave behind at Rochester , to take care of the tired horse , whilst we have fresh beast provide along the road . and gallop by the Prince 's side the Colonel explain to the Prince of Wales what his movement have be ; who the friend be that know of the expedition ; whom , as Esmond conceive , the Prince should trust ; entreat he , above all , to maintain the very close secrecy until the time should come when his Royal Highness should appear . the town swarm with friend of the Prince 's cause ; there be score of correspondent with St. Germains ; jacobite know and secret ; great in station and humble ; about the Court and the Queen ; in the Parliament , Church , and among the merchant in the City . the Prince have friend numberless in the army , in the Privy Council , and the officer of State . the great object , as it seem , to the small band of person who have concert that bold stroke , who have bring the Queen 's brother into his native country , be , that his visit should remain unknown till the proper time come , when his presence should surprise friend and enemy alike ; and the latter should be find so unprepared and disunite , that they should not find time to attack he . we fear more from his friend than from his enemy . the lie and tittle - tattle send over to St. Germains by the Jacobite agent about London , have do an incalculable mischief to his cause , and wofully misguide he , and it be from these especially , that the person engage in the present venture be anxious to defend the chief actor in it . 
    the manager be the Bishop , who can not be hurt by have 
  his name mention , a very active and loyal Nonconformist 
  Divine , a lady in the high favor at Court , with whom 
  Beatrix Esmond have communication , and two nobleman of the 
  great rank , and a member of the House of Commons , who be 
  implicate in more transaction than one in behalf of the 
  Stuart family . 
 the party reach London by nightfall , leave their horse at the Posting - House over against Westminster , and be ferry over the water , where Lady Esmond 's coach be already in wait . in another hour we be all land at Kensington , and the mistress of the house have that satisfaction which her heart have yearn after for many year , once more to embrace her son , who , on his side , with all his waywardness , ever retain a most tender affection for his parent . 
 she do not refrain from this expression of her feeling , though the domestic be by , and my Lord Castlewood 's attendant stand in the hall . Esmond have to whisper to he in French to take his hat off . Monsieur Baptiste be constantly neglect his part with an inconceivable levity : more than once on the ride to London , little observation of the strange , light remark , and word betoken the great ignorance of the country the Prince come to govern , have hurt the susceptibility of the two gentleman form his escort ; nor could either help own in his secret mind that they would have have his behavior otherwise , and that the laughter and the lightness , not to say license , which characterize his talk , scarce befit such a great Prince , and such a solemn occasion . not but that he could act at proper time with spirit and dignity . he have behave , as we all know , in a very courageous manner on the field . Esmond have see a copy of the letter the Prince have writ with his own hand when urge by his friend in England to abjure his religion , and admire that manly and magnanimous reply by which he refuse to yield to the temptation . Monsieur Baptiste take off his hat , blush at the hint Colonel Esmond venture to give he , and say : " Tenez , elle est jolie , la petite mere . Foi de Chevalier ! elle est charmante ; mais l'autre , qui est cette nymphe , cet astre qui brille , cette Diane qui descend sur nous ? " and he start back , and push forward , as beatrix be descend the stair . she be in color for the first time at her own house ; she wear the diamond Esmond give she ; it have be agree between they , that she should wear these brilliant on the day when the King should enter the house , and a Queen she look , radiant in charm , and magnificent and imperial in beauty . 
 Castlewood himself be startle by that beauty and splendor ; he step back and gaze at his sister as though he have not be aware before   nor be he very likely   how perfectly lovely she be , and I think blush as he embrace she . the Prince could not keep his eye off she ; he quite forget his menial part , though he have be school to it , and a little light portmanteau prepare expressly that he should carry it . he press forward before my Lord Viscount . ' twa lucky the servant ' eye be busy in other direction , or they must have see that this be no servant , or at least a very insolent and rude one . 
 again Colonel Esmond be oblige to cry out , " baptiste , " in a loud imperious voice , " have a care to the valise ; " at which hint the wilful young man grind his tooth together with something very like a curse between they , and then give a brief look of anything but pleasure to his mentor . be remind , however , he shoulder the little portmanteau , and carry it up the stair , esmond precede he , and a servant with lighted taper . he fling down his burden sulkily in the bedchamber : " a Prince that will wear a crown must wear a mask , " say Mr. Esmond in French . 
 " ah peste ! I see how it be , " say Monsieur Baptiste , continue the talk in French . " the great Serious be seriously " " alarm for Monsieur Baptiste , " break in the Colonel . esmond neither like the tone with which the Prince speak of the lady , nor the eye with which he regard they . 
 the bedchamber and the two room adjoin it , the closet and the apartment which be to be call my lord 's parlor , be already light and await their occupier ; and the collation lay for my lord 's supper . Lord Castlewood and his mother and sister come up the stair a minute afterwards , and , so soon as the domestic have quit the apartment , Castlewood and Esmond uncover , and the two lady go down on their knee before the Prince , who graciously give a hand to each . he look his part of Prince much more naturally than that of servant , which he have just be try , and raise they both with a great deal of nobility , as well as kindness in his air . " Madam , " say he , " my mother will thank your ladyship for your hospitality to her son ; for you , madam , " turn to beatrix , " I can not bear to see so much beauty in such a posture . you will betray Monsieur Baptiste if you kneel to he ; sure ' tis his place rather to kneel to you . " 
 a light shine out of her eye ; a gleam bright enough to kindle passion in any breast . there be time when this creature be so handsome , that she seem , as it be , like Venus reveal herself a goddess in a flash of brightness . she appear so now ; radiant , and with eye bright with a wonderful lustre . a pang , as of rage and jealousy , shoot through Esmond 's heart , as he catch the look she give the Prince ; and he clench his hand involuntarily and look across to Castlewood , whose eye answer his alarm - signal , and be also on the alert . the Prince give his subject an audience of a few minute , and then the two lady and Colonel Esmond quit the chamber . Lady Castlewood press his hand as they descend the stair , and the three go down to the low room , where they wait awhile till the traveller above should be refresh and ready for their meal . 
 Esmond look at Beatrix , blaze with her jewel on her beautiful neck . " I have keep my word , " say he : " and I mine , " say beatrix , look down on the diamond . 
 " be I the Mogul Emperor , " say the Colonel , " you should have all that be dig out of Golconda . " 
 " these be a great deal too good for I , " say Beatrix , drop her head on her beautiful breast , ' so be you all , all ! " and when she look up again , as she do in a moment , and after a sigh , her eye , as they gaze at her cousin , wear that melancholy and inscrutable look which ' twa always impossible to sound . 
 when the time come for the supper , of which we be advertise by a knock overhead , Colonel Esmond and the two lady go to the upper apartment , where the Prince already be , and by his side the young Viscount , of exactly the same age , shape , and with feature not dissimilar , though Frank 's be the handsomer of the two . the Prince sit down and bade the lady sit . the gentleman remain stand : there be , indeed , but one more cover lay at the table : " which of you will take it ? " say he . 
 " the head of our house , " say Lady Castlewood , take her son 's hand , and look towards Colonel Esmond with a bow and a great tremor of the voice ; " the Marquis of Esmond will have the honor of serve the king . " 
 " I shall have the honor of wait on his Royal Highness , " say Colonel Esmond , fill a cup of wine , and , as the fashion of that day be , he present it to the King on his knee . 
 " I drink to my hostess and her family , " say the Prince , with no very well - please air ; but the cloud pass immediately off his face , and he talk to the lady in a lively , rattle strain , quite undisturbed by poor Mr. Esmond 's yellow countenance , that , I dare say , look very glum . 
 when the time come to take leave , Esmond march homeward to his lodging , and meet Mr. Addison on the road that night , walk to a cottage he have at Fulham , the moon shin on his handsome serene face : " what cheer , brother ? " say Addison , laugh : " I think it be a footpad advance in the dark , and behold ' tis an old friend . we may shake hand , Colonel , in the dark , ' tis well than fight by daylight . why should we quarrel , because I be a Whig and thou art a tory ? turn thy step and walk with I to Fulham , where there be a nightingale still sing in the garden , and a cool bottle in a cave I know of ; you shall drink to the pretender if you like , and I will drink my liquor my own way : I have have enough of good liquor ? no , never ! there be no such word as enough as a stopper for good wine . thou wilt not come ? come any day , come soon . you know I remember Simois and the Sigeia tellus , and the praelia mixta mero , mixta mero , " he repeat , with ever so slight a touch of merum in his voice , and walk back a little way on the road with Esmond , bid the other remember he be always his friend , and indebte to he for his aid in the " campaign " poem . and very likely Mr. Under - Secretary would have step in and take t'other bottle at the Colonel 's lodging , have the latter invite he , but Esmond 's mood be none of the gayest , and he bid his friend an inhospitable good - night at the door . 
 " I have do the deed , " think he , sleepless , and look out into the night ; " he be here , and I have bring he ; he and Beatrix be sleep under the same roof now . whom do I mean to serve in bring he ? be it the Prince ? be it Henry Esmond ? have I not good have join the manly creed of Addison yonder , that scout the old doctrine of right divine , that boldly declare that Parliament and people consecrate the Sovereign , not bishop , nor genealogy , nor oil , nor coronation . " the eager gaze of the young Prince , watch every movement of Beatrix , haunt Esmond and pursue he . the Prince 's figure appear before he in his feverish dream many time that night . he wish the deed undo for which he have labor so . he be not the first that have regret his own act , or bring about his own undoing . undo ? should he write that word in his late year ? no , on his knee before heaven , rather be thankful for what then he deem his misfortune , and which hath cause the whole subsequent happiness of his life . 
 Esmond 's man , honest John Lockwood , have serve his master and the family all his life , and the Colonel know that he could answer for John 's fidelity as for his own . John return with the horse from Rochester betime the next morning , and the Colonel give he to understand that on go to Kensington , where he be free of the servant ' hall , and indeed court Miss Beatrix 's maid , he be to ask no question , and betray no surprise , but to vouch stoutly that the young gentleman he should see in a red coat there be my Lord Viscount Castlewood , and that his attendant in gray be Monsieur Baptiste the Frenchman . he be to tell his friend in the kitchen such story as he remember of my Lord Viscount 's youth at Castlewood ; what a wild boy he be ; how he use to drill Jack and cane he , before ever he be a soldier ; everything , in fine , he know respect my Lord Viscount 's early day . Jack 's idea of painting have not be much cultivate during his residence in Flanders with his master ; and , before my young lord 's return , he have be easily get to believe that the picture bring over from Paris , and now hang in Lady Castlewood 's drawing - room , be a perfect likeness of her son , the young lord . and the domestic have all see the picture many time , and catch but a momentary imperfect glimpse of the two stranger on the night of their arrival , never have a reason to doubt the fidelity of the portrait ; and next day , when they see the original of the piece habit exactly as he be represent in the painting , with the same periwig , ribbon , and uniform of the guard , quite naturally address the gentleman as my Lord Castlewood , my Lady Viscountess 's son . 
 the secretary of the night previous be now the viscount ; the viscount wear the secretary 's gray frock ; and John Lockwood be instruct to hint to the world below stair that my lord be a Papist , and very devout in that religion , his attendant might be no other than his chaplain from Bruxelles ; hence , if he take his meal in my lord 's company there be little reason for surprise . Frank be far caution to speak English with a foreign accent , which task he perform indifferently well , and this caution be the more necessary because the Prince himself scarce speak our language like a native of the island : and John Lockwood laugh with the folk below stair at the manner in which my lord , after five year abroad , sometimes forget his own tongue , and speak it like a Frenchman . " I warrant , " say he , " that , with the english beef and beer , his lordship will soon get back the proper use of his mouth ; " and , to do his new lordship justice , he take to beer and beef very kindly . 
 the Prince drink so much , and be so loud and imprudent in his talk after his drink , that Esmond often tremble for he . his meal be serve as much as possible in his own chamber , though frequently he make his appearance in Lady Castlewood 's parlor and drawing - room , call beatrix ' sister , " and her ladyship " mother , " or " madam " before the servant . and , choose to act entirely up to the part of brother and son , the Prince sometimes salute Mrs. Beatrix and Lady Castlewood with a freedom which his secretary do not like , and which , for his part , set Colonel Esmond tear with rage . 
 the guest have not be three day in the house when poor Jack Lockwood come with a rueful countenance to his master , and say : " my Lord that be the gentleman have be tamper with Mrs. Lucy   Jack 's sweetheart , and give her guineas and a kiss . " I fear that Colonel Esmond 's mind be rather relieved than otherwise when he find that the ancillary beauty be the one whom the Prince have select . his royal taste be know to lie that way , and continue so in after life . the heir of one of the great name , of the great kingdom , and of the great misfortune in Europe , be often content to lay the dignity of his birth and grief at the wooden shoe of a french chambermaid , and to repent afterwards   for he be very devout   in ash take from the dust - pan . ' tis for mortal such as these that nation suffer , that party struggle , that warrior fight and bleed . a year afterwards gallant head be fall , and Nithsdale in escape , and derwentwater on the scaffold ; whilst the heedless ingrate , for whom they risk and lose all , be tipple with his seraglio of mistress in his petite maison of Chaillot . 
 blushing to be force to bear such an errand , Esmond have to go to the Prince and warn he that the girl whom his Highness be bribe be John Lockwood 's sweetheart , an honest resolute man , who have serve in six campaign , and fear nothing , and who know that the person call himself Lord Castlewood be not his young master : and the Colonel beseech the Prince to consider what the effect of a single man 's jealousy might be , and to think of other design he have in hand , more important than the seduction of a waiting - maid , and the humiliation of a brave man . 
 ten time , perhaps , in the course of as many day , Mr. Esmond have to warn the royal young adventurer of some imprudence or some freedom . he receive these remonstrance very testily , save perhaps in this affair of poor Lockwood 's , when he deign to burst out a - laugh , and say , " what ! the soubrette have peach to the amoureux , and Crispin be angry , and Crispin have serve , and Crispin have be a corporal , have he ? tell he we will reward his valor with a pair of color , and recompense his fidelity . " 
 Colonel Esmond venture to utter some other word of entreaty , but the Prince , stamp imperiously , cry out , " Assez , milord : je m'ennuye a la preche ; I be not come to London to go to the sermon . " and he complain afterwards to Castlewood , that " le petit jaune , le noir Colonel , le Marquis Misanthrope "   by which facetious name his Royal Highness be pleased to designate Colonel Esmond , " fatigue he with his grand air and virtuous homily . " 
 the Bishop of Rochester , and other gentleman engage in the transaction which have bring the Prince over , wait upon his Royal Highness , constantly ask for my Lord Castlewood on their arrival at Kensington , and be openly conduct to his Royal Highness in that character , who receive they either in my lady 's drawing - room below , or above in his own apartment ; and all implore he to quit the house as little as possible , and to wait there till the signal should be give for he to appear . the lady entertain he at card , over which amusement he spend many hour in each day and night . he pass many hour more in drinking , during which time he would rattle and talk very agreeably , and especially if the Colonel be absent , whose presence always seem to frighten he ; and the poor " Colonel Noir " take that hint as a command accordingly , and seldom intrude his black face upon the convivial hour of this august young prisoner . except for those few person of whom the porter have the list , Lord Castlewood be deny to all friend of the house who wait on his lordship . the wound he have receive have break out again from his journey on horseback , so the world and the domestic be inform . and Doctor A ,   his physician   I shall not mention his name , but he be physician to the Queen , of the Scots nation , and a man remarkable for his benevolence as well as his wit , give order that he should be keep perfectly quiet until the wound should heal . with this gentleman , who be one of the most active and influential of our party , and the other before speak of , the whole secret lay ; and it be keep with so much faithfulness , and the story we tell so simple and natural , that there be no likelihood of a discovery except from the imprudence of the Prince himself , and an adventurous levity that we have the great difficulty to control . as for Lady Castlewood , although she scarce speak a word , ' twa easy to gather from her demeanor , and one or two hint she drop , how deep her mortification be at find the hero whom she have choose to worship all her life   and whose restoration have form almost the most sacred part of her prayer , no more than a man , and not a good one . she think misfortune might have chasten he ; but that instructress have rather render he callous than humble . his devotion , which be quite real , keep he from no sin he have a mind to . his talk show good - humor , gayety , even wit enough ; but there be a levity in his act and word that he have bring from among those libertine devotee with whom he have be breed , and that shock the simplicity and purity of the English lady , whose guest he be . esmond speak his mind to beatrix pretty freely about the Prince , get her brother to put in a word of warning . beatrix be entirely of their opinion ; she think he be very light , very light and reckless ; she could not even see the good look Colonel Esmond have speak of . the Prince have bad tooth , and a decide squint . how could we say he do not squint ? his eye be fine , but there be certainly a cast in they . she rally he at table with wonderful wit ; she speak of he invariably as of a mere boy ; she be more fond of Esmond than ever , praise he to her brother , praise he to the Prince , when his Royal Highness be pleased to sneer at the Colonel , and warmly espouse his cause : " and if your Majesty do not give he the Garter his father have , when the Marquis of Esmond come to your Majesty 's court , I will hang myself in my own garter , or will cry my eye out . " " rather than lose those , " say the Prince , " he shall be make Archbishop and Colonel of the Guard "   it be Frank Castlewood who tell I of this conversation over their supper .

 " yes , " cry she , with one of her laugh I fancy I hear it now . thirty year afterwards I hear that delightful music . " yes , he shall be Archbishop of Esmond and Marquis of Canterbury . " 
 " and what will your ladyship be ? " say the Prince ; " you have but to choose your place . " 
 " I , " say beatrix , " will be mother of the maid to the Queen of his Majesty King James the Third Vive le Roy ! " and she make he a great curtsy , and drink a part of a glass of wine in his honor . 
 " the Prince seize hold of the glass and drink the last drop of it , " Castlewood say , " and my mother , look very anxious , rise up and ask leave to retire . but that Trix be my mother 's daughter , Harry , " Frank continue , " I do not   know what a horrid fear I should have of she . I wish I wish this business be over . you be old than I be , and wiser , and well , and I owe you everything , and would die for you before George I would ; but I wish the end of this be come . " 
 neither of we very likely pass a tranquil night ; horrible doubt and torment rack Esmond 's soul : ' twa a scheme of personal ambition , a daring stroke for a selfish end he know it . what care he , in his heart , who be king ? be not his very sympathy and secret conviction on the other side on the side of People , Parliament , Freedom ? and here be he , engage for a Prince that have scarce hear the word liberty ; that priest and woman , tyrant by nature , both make a tool of . the misanthrope be in no well humor after hear that story , and his grim face more black and yellow than ever . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 we ENTERTAIN a VERY distinguished guest AT KENSINGTON . 

 should any clue be find to the dark intrigue at the latter end of Queen Anne 's time , or any historian be inclined to follow it , ' twill be discover , I have little doubt , that not one of the great personage about the Queen have a define scheme of policy , independent of that private and selfish interest which each be bent on pursue : St. John be for St. John , and Harley for Oxford , and Marlborough for John Churchill , always ; and accord as they could get help from St. Germains or Hanover , they send over proffer of allegiance to the Princes there , or betray one to the other : one cause , or one sovereign , be as good as another to they , so that they could hold the good place under he ; and like Lockit and Peachem , the newgate chief in the " Rogues ' Opera , " Mr. Gay write afterwards , have each in his hand document and proof of treason which would hang the other , only he do not dare to use the weapon , for fear of that one which his neighbor also carry in his pocket . think of the great Marlborough , the great subject in all the world , a conqueror of prince , that have march victorious over Germany , Flanders , and France , that have give the law to sovereign abroad , and be worship as a divinity at home , force to sneak out of England his credit , honor , place , all take from he ; his friend in the army break and ruin ; and fly before Harley , as abject and powerless as a poor debtor before a bailiff with a writ . a paper , of which Harley get possession , and show beyond doubt that the Duke be engage with the Stuart family , be the weapon with which the Treasurer drive Marlborough out of the kingdom . he flee to Antwerp , and begin intrigue instantly on the other side , and come back to England , as all know , a Whig and a Hanoverian . 
 though the Treasurer turn out of the army and office every man , military or civil , know to be the Duke 's friend , and give the vacant post among the Tory party ; he , too , be play the double game between Hanover and St. Germains , await the expected catastrophe of the Queen 's death to be Master of the State , and offer it to either family that should bribe he well , or that the nation should declare for . whichever the King be , Harley 's object be to reign over he ; and to this end he supplant the former famous favorite , decry the action of the war which have make Marlborough 's name illustrious , and disdain no more than the great fall competitor of his , the mean art , flattery , intimidation , that would secure his power . if the great satirist the world ever hath see have writ against Harley , and not for he , what a history have he leave behind of the last year of Queen Anne 's reign ! but Swift , that scorn all mankind , and himself not the least of all , have this merit of a faithful partisan , that he love those chief who treat he well , and stick by Harley bravely in his fall , as he gallantly have support he in his well fortune . 
 incomparably more brilliant , more splendid , eloquent , accomplish than his rival , the great St. John could be as selfish as Oxford be , and could act the double part as skilfully as ambidextrous Churchill . he whose talk be always of liberty , no more shrunk from use persecution and the pillory against his opponent than if he have be at Lisbon and Grand Inquisitor . this lofty patriot be on his knee at Hanover and St. Germains too ; notoriously of no religion , he toast Church and Queen as boldly as the stupid Sacheverel , whom he use and laugh at ; and to serve his turn , and to overthrow his enemy , he could intrigue , coax , bully , wheedle , fawn on the Court favorite and creep up the back - stair as silently as Oxford , who supplant Marlborough , and whom he himself supplant . the crash of my Lord Oxford happen at this very time whereat my history be now arrive . he be come to the very last day of his power , and the agent whom he employ to overthrow the conqueror of Blenheim , be now engage to upset the conqueror 's conqueror , and hand over the staff of government to Bolingbroke , who have be pant to hold it . 
 in expectation of the stroke that be now prepare , the irish regiment in the french service be all bring round about Boulogne in Picardy , to pass over if need be with the Duke of Berwick ; the soldier of France no long , but subject of James the Third of England and Ireland King . the fidelity of the great mass of the Scots   though a most active , resolute , and gallant Whig party , admirably and energetically order and discipline , be know to be in Scotland too   be notoriously unshaken in their king . a very great body of Tory clergy , nobility , and gentry , be public partisan of the exile Prince ; and the indifferent might be count on to cry King George or King James , accord as either should prevail . the Queen , especially in her latter day , incline towards her own family . the Prince be lie actually in London , within a stone 's cast of his sister 's palace ; the first Minister topple to his fall , and so totter that the weak push of a woman 's finger would send he down ; and as for Bolingbroke , his successor , we know on whose side his power and his splendid eloquence would be on the day when the Queen should appear openly before her Council and say : " this , my lord , be my brother ; here be my father 's heir , and mine after I . " 
 during the whole of the previous year the Queen have have many and repeat fit of sickness , fever , and lethargy , and her death have be constantly look for by all her attendant . the Elector of Hanover have wish to send his son , the Duke of Cambridge to pay his court to his cousin the Queen , the Elector say ; in truth , to be on the spot when death should close her career . frightened perhaps to have such a memento mori under her royal eye , her Majesty have angrily forbid the young Prince 's come into England . either she desire to keep the chance for her brother open yet ; or the people about she do not wish to close with the Whig candidate till they could make term with he . the quarrel of her Ministers before her face at the Council board , the prick of conscience very likely , the importunity of her Ministers , and constant turmoil and agitation round about she , have weaken and irritate the Princess extremely ; her strength be give way under these continual trial of her temper , and from day to day it be expect she must come to a speedy end of they . just before Viscount Castlewood and his companion come from France , her Majesty be take ill . the St. Anthony 's fire break out on the royal leg ; there be no hurry for the presentation of the young lord at Court , or that person who should appear under his name ; and my Lord Viscount 's wound break out opportunely , he be keep conveniently in his chamber until such time as his physician would allow he to bend his knee before the Queen . at the commencement of July , that influential lady , with whom it have be mention that our party have relation , come frequently to visit her young friend , the Maid of Honor , at Kensington , and my Lord Viscount   the real or supposititious , who be an invalid at Lady Castlewood 's house . 
 on the 27th day of July , the lady in question , who hold the most intimate post about the Queen , come in her chair from the Palace hard by , bring to the little party in Kensington Square intelligence of the very high importance . the final blow have be strike , and my Lord of Oxford and Mortimer be no long Treasurer . the staff be as yet give to no successor , though my Lord Bolingbroke would undoubtedly be the man . and now the time be come , the Queen 's Abigail say : and now my Lord Castlewood ought to be present to the Sovereign . 
 after that scene which Lord Castlewood witness and describe to his cousin , who pass such a miserable night of mortification and jealousy as he think over the transaction , no doubt the three person who be set by nature as protector over Beatrix come to the same conclusion , that she must be remove from the presence of a man whose desire towards she be express only too clearly ; and who be no more scrupulous in seek to gratify they than his father have be before he . I suppose Esmond 's mistress , her son , and the Colonel himself , have be all secretly debate this matter in their mind , for when Frank break out , in his blunt way , with : " I think Beatrix have well be anywhere but here , " Lady Castlewood say : " I thank you , Frank , I have think so , too ; " and Mr. Esmond , though he only remark that it be not for he to speak , show plainly , by the delight on his countenance , how very agreeable that proposal be to he . 
 " one see that you think with we , Henry , " say the viscountess , with ever so little of sarcasm in her tone : " beatrix be good out of this house whilst we have our guest in it , and as soon as this morning 's business be do , she ought to quit London . " 
 " what morning 's business ? " ask Colonel Esmond , not know what have be arrange , though in fact the stroke next in importance to that of bring the Prince , and of have he acknowledge by the Queen , be now be perform at the very moment we three be converse together . 
 the Court - lady with whom our plan be concert , and who be a chief agent in it , the Court physician , and the Bishop of Rochester , who be the other two most active participator in our plan , have hold many council in our house at Kensington and elsewhere , as to the mean good to be adopt for present our young adventurer to his sister the Queen . the simple and easy plan propose by Colonel Esmond have be agree to by all party , which be that on some rather private day , when there be not many person about the Court , the Prince should appear there as my Lord Castlewood , should be greet by his sister in waiting , and lead by that other lady into the closet of the Queen . and accord to her Majesty 's health or humor , and the circumstance that might arise during the interview , it be to be leave to the discretion of those present at it , and to the Prince himself , whether he should declare that it be the Queen 's own brother , or the brother of Beatrix Esmond , who kiss her royal hand . and this plan be determine on , we be all wait in very much anxiety for the day and signal of execution . 
 two morning after that supper , it be the 27th day of July , the Bishop of Rochester breakfasting with Lady Castlewood and her family , and the meal scarce over , Doctor A. 's coach drive up to our house at Kensington , and the doctor appear amongst the party there , enliven a rather gloomy company ; for the mother and daughter have have word in the morning in respect to the transaction of that supper , and other adventure perhaps , and on the day succeed . Beatrix 's haughty spirit brook remonstrance from no superior , much less from her mother , the gentle of creature , whom the girl command rather than obey . and feel she be wrong , and that by a thousand coquetry   which she could no more help exercise on every man that come near she , than the sun can help shin on great and small   she have provoke the Prince 's dangerous admiration , and allure he to the expression of it , she be only the more wilful and imperious the more she feel her error . 
 to this party , the Prince be serve with chocolate in his bedchamber , where he lie late , sleep away the fume of his wine , the doctor come , and by the urgent and startling nature of his news , dissipate instantly that private and minor unpleasantry under which the family of Castlewood be labor . 
 he ask for the guest ; the guest be above in his own apartment : he bid Monsieur Baptiste go up to his master instantly , and request that MY LORD viscount CASTLEWOOD would straightway put his uniform on , and come away in the Doctor 's coach now at the door . 
 he then inform Madam Beatrix what her part of the comedy be to be : " in half an hour , " say he , " her Majesty and her favorite lady will take the air in the Cedar - walk behind the new Banqueting - house . her Majesty will be draw in a garden - chair , Madam Beatrix Esmond and she BROTHER , MY LORD VISCOUNT CASTLEWOOD , will be walk in the private garden ,   here be Lady Masham 's key ,   and will come unawares upon the Royal party . the man that draw the chair will retire , and leave the Queen , the favorite , and the maid of honor and her brother together ; Mistress Beatrix will present her brother , and then ! and then , my Lord Bishop will pray for the result of the interview , and his Scots clerk will say amen ! quick , put on your hood , Madam Beatrix ; why doth not his Majesty come down ? such another chance may not present itself for month again . " 
 the Prince be late and lazy , and indeed have all but lose that chance through his indolence . the Queen be actually about to leave the garden just when the party reach it ; the Doctor , the Bishop , the maid of honor and her brother go off together in the physician 's coach , and have be go half an hour when Colonel Esmond come to Kensington Square . 
 the news of this errand , on which beatrix be go , of course for a moment put all thought of private jealousy out of Colonel Esmond 's head . in half an hour more the coach return ; the Bishop descend from it first , and give his arm to Beatrix , who now come out . his lordship go back into the carriage again , and the maid of honor enter the house alone . we be all gaze at she from the upper window , try to read from her countenance the result of the interview from which she have just come . 
 she come into the drawing - room in a great tremor and very pale ; she ask for a glass of water as her mother go to meet she , and after drink that and put off her hood , she begin to speak " we may all hope for the good , " say she ; " it have cost the Queen a fit . her Majesty be in her chair in the Cedar - walk , accompany only by Lady , when we enter by the private wicket from the west side of the garden , and turn towards she , the Doctor follow we . they wait in a side walk hide by the shrub , as we advance towards the chair . my heart throb so I scarce could speak ; but my Prince whisper , ' Courage , beatrix , ' and march on with a steady step . his face be a little flushed , but he be not afraid of the danger . he who fight so bravely at Malplaquet fear nothing . " Esmond and Castlewood look at each other at this compliment , neither like the sound of it . 
 " the Prince uncover , " beatrix continue , " and I see the queen turn round to Lady Masham , as if ask who these two be . her Majesty look very pale and ill , and then flush up ; the favorite make we a signal to advance , and I go up , lead my Prince by the hand , quite close to the chair : ' your Majesty will give my Lord Viscount your hand to kiss , ' say her lady , and the Queen put out her hand , which the Prince kiss , kneel on his knee , he who should kneel to no mortal man or woman . 
 " ' you have be long from England , my lord , ' say the Queen : ' why be you not here to give a home to your mother and sister ? ' 
 " ' I be come , Madam , to stay now , if the Queen desire I , ' say the Prince , with another low bow . 
 " ' you have take a foreign wife , my lord , and a foreign religion ; be not that of England good enough for you ? ' 
 " ' in return to my father 's church , ' say the Prince , ' I do not love my mother the less , nor be I the less faithful servant of your majesty . ' 
 " here , " say beatrix , " the favorite give I a little signal with her hand to fall back , which I do , though I die to hear what should pass ; and whisper something to the Queen , which make her Majesty start and utter one or two word in a hurried manner , look towards the Prince , and catch hold with her hand of the arm of her chair . he advance still near towards it ; he begin to speak very rapidly ; I catch the word , ' Father , blessing , forgiveness , ' and then presently the Prince fall on his knee ; take from his breast a paper he have there , hand it to the Queen , who , as soon as she see it , fling up both her arm with a scream , and take away that hand near the Prince , and which he endeavor to kiss . he go on speak with great animation of gesture , now clasp his hand together on his heart , now open they as though to say : ' I be here , your brother , in your power . ' Lady Masham run round on the other side of the chair , kneel too , and speak with great energy . she clasp the Queen 's hand on her side , and pick up the paper her Majesty have let fall . the Prince rise and make a further speech as though he would go ; the favorite on the other hand urge her mistress , and then , run back to the Prince , bring he back once more close to the chair . again he kneel down and take the Queen 's hand , which she do not withdraw , kiss it a hundred time ; my lady all the time , with sob and supplication , speak over the chair . this while the queen sit with a stupefied look , crumple the paper with one hand , as my Prince embrace the other ; then of a sudden she utter several pierce shriek , and burst into a great fit of hysteric tear and laughter . ' enough , enough , sir , for this time , ' I hear Lady Masham say : and the chairman , who have withdraw round the banqueting - room , come back , alarm by the cry . ' quick , ' say Lady Masham , ' get some help , ' and I run towards the Doctor , who , with the Bishop of Rochester , come up instantly . Lady Masham whisper the Prince he might hope for the very good ; and to be ready to - morrow ; and he hath go away to the Bishop of Rochester 's house , to meet several of his friend there . and so the great stroke be strike , " say Beatrix , go down on her knee , and clasp her hand . " God save the king : God save the king ! " 
 beatrix 's tale tell , and the young lady herself calm somewhat of her agitation , we ask with regard to the Prince , who be absent with Bishop Atterbury , and be inform that ' twa likely he might remain abroad the whole day . beatrix 's three kinsfolk look at one another at this intelligence : ' twa clear the same thought be pass through the mind of all . 
 but who should begin to break the news ? Monsieur Baptiste , that be Frank Castlewood , turn very red , and look towards Esmond ; the Colonel bite his lip , and fairly beat a retreat into the window : it be Lady Castlewood that open upon beatrix with the news which we know would do anything but please she . 
 " we be glad , " say she , take her daughter 's hand , and speak in a gentle voice , " that the guest be away . " 
 beatrix draw back in an instant , look round she at we three , and as if divine a danger . " why glad ? " say she , her breast begin to heave ; " be you so soon tired of he ? " 
 " we think one of we be devilishly too fond of he , " cry out Frank Castlewood . 
 " and which be it you , my lord , or be it mamma , who be jealous because he drink my health ? or be it the head of the family "   here she turn with an imperious look towards Colonel Esmond , " who have take of late to preach the King sermon ? " 
 " we do not say you be too free with his Majesty . " 
 " I thank you , madam , " say beatrix , with a toss of the head and a curtsey . 
 but her mother continue , with very great calmness and dignity " at least we have not say so , though we might , be it possible for a mother to say such word to her own daughter , your father 's daughter . " 
 " eh ? mon pere , " break out beatrix , " be no well than other person ' father . " and again she look towards the Colonel . 
 we all feel a shock as she utter those two or three french word ; her manner be exactly imitate from that of our foreign guest . 
 " you have not learn to speak French a month ago , beatrix , " say her mother , sadly , " nor to speak ill of your father . " 
 beatrix , no doubt , see that slip she have make in her flurry , for she blush crimson : " I have learn to honor the king , " say she , draw up , " and ' twere as well that other suspect neither his Majesty nor I . " 
 " if you respect your mother a little more , " Frank say , " trix , you would do yourself no hurt . " 
 " I be no child , " say she , turn round on he ; " we have live very well these five year without the benefit of your advice or example , and I intend to take neither now . why do not the head of the house speak ? " she go on ; " he rule everything here . when his chaplain have do sing the psalm , will his lordship deliver the sermon ? I be tired of the psalm . " the Prince have use almost the very same word in regard to Colonel Esmond that the imprudent girl repeat in her wrath . 
 " you show yourself a very apt scholar , madam , " say the Colonel ; and , turn to his mistress , " do your guest use these word in your ladyship 's hearing , or be it to beatrix in private that he be pleased to impart his opinion regard my tiresome sermon ? " 
 " have you see he alone ? " cry my lord , start up with an oath : " by God , have you see he alone ? " 
 " be he here , you would not   dare so to insult I ; no , you would not dare ! " cry Frank 's sister . " keep your oath , my lord , for your wife ; we be not use here to such language . till you come , there use to be kindness between I and mamma , and I care for she when you never do , when you be away for year with your horse and your mistress , and your popish wife . " 
 " by - , " say my lord , rap out another oath , " Clotilda be an angel ; how dare you say a word against Clotilda ? " 
 Colonel Esmond could not refrain from a smile , to see how easy Frank 's attack be draw off by that feint : " I fancy Clotilda be not the subject in hand , " say Mr. Esmond , rather scornfully ; " her ladyship be at Paris , a hundred league off , prepare baby - linen . it be about my Lord Castlewood 's sister , and not his wife , the question be . " 
 " he be not my Lord Castlewood , " say beatrix , " and he know he be not ; he be Colonel Francis Esmond 's son , and no more , and he wear a false title ; and he live on another man 's land , and he know it . " here be another desperate sally of the poor beleaguered garrison , and an alerte in another quarter . " again , I beg your pardon , " say Esmond . " if there be no proof of my claim , I have no claim . if my father acknowledge no heir , yours be his lawful successor , and my Lord Castlewood hath as good a right to his rank and small estate as any man in England . but that again be not the question , as you know very well ; let we bring our talk back to it , as you will have I meddle in it . and I will give you frankly my opinion , that a house where a Prince lie all day , who respect no woman , be no house for a young unmarried lady ; that you be well in the country than here ; that he be here on a great end , from which no folly should divert he ; and that have nobly do your part of this morning , beatrix , you should retire off the scene awhile , and leave it to the other actor of the play . " 
 as the Colonel speak with a perfect calmness and politeness , such as ' tis to be hop he hath always show to woman ,   his mistress stand by he on one side of the table , and Frank Castlewood on the other , hemming in poor beatrix , that be behind it , and , as it be , surround she with our approach . 
    my dear father saith quite truly , that his manner towards 
  our sex be uniformly courteous . from my infancy upwards , 
  he treat I with an extreme gentleness , as though I be a 
  little lady . I can scarce remember   though I try he 
  often   ever hear a rough word from he , nor be he less 
  grave and kind in his manner to the humblest negress on 
  his estate . he be familiar with no one except my mother , 
  and it be delightful to witness up to the very last day 
  the confidence between they . he be obey eagerly by all 
  under he ; and my mother and all her household live in a 
  constant emulation to please he , and quite a terror lest in 
  any way they should offend he . he be the humblest man 
  with all this ; the least exact , the more easily 
  content ; and Mr. Benson , our minister at Castlewood , who 
  attend he at the last , ever say " I know not what 
  Colonel Esmond 's doctrine be , but his life and death be 
  those of a devout Christian . " R. E. W. 
 have twice sally out and be beat back , she now , as I expect , try the ultima ratio of woman , and have recourse to tear . her beautiful eye fill with they ; I never could bear in she , nor in any woman , that expression of pain : " I be alone , " sob she ; " you be three against I my brother , my mother , and you . what have I do , that you should speak and look so unkindly at I ? be it my fault that the Prince should , as you say , admire I ? do I bring he here ? do I do aught but what you bade I , in make he welcome ? do you not tell I that our duty be to die for he ? do you not teach I , mother , night and morning to pray for the king , before even ourselves ? what would you have of I , cousin , for you be the chief of the conspiracy against I ; I know you be , sir , and that my mother and brother be act but as you bid they ; whither would you have I go ? " 
 " I would but remove from the Prince , " say Esmond , gravely , " a dangerous temptation ; heaven forbid I should say you would yield ; I would only have he free of it . your honor need no guardian , please God , but his imprudence doth . he be so far remove from all woman by his rank , that his pursuit of they can not but be unlawful . we would remove the dearest and fairest of our family from the chance of that insult , and that be why we would have you go , dear beatrix . " 
 " Harry speak like a book , " say Frank , with one of his oath , " and , by - , every word he saith be true . you can not   help be handsome , Trix ; no more can the Prince help follow you . my counsel be that you go out of harm 's way ; for , by the Lord , be the Prince to play any trick with you , King as he be , or be to be , Harry Esmond and I would have justice of he . " 
 " be not two such champion enough to guard I ? " say beatrix , something sorrowfully ; ' sure , with you two watching , no evil could happen to I . " 
 " in faith , I think not , beatrix , " say Colonel Esmond ; " nor if the Prince know we would he try . " 
 " but do he know you ? " interpose Lady Castlewood , very quiet : " he come of a country where the pursuit of king be think no dishonor to a woman . let we go , dear beatrix . shall we go to Walcote or to Castlewood ? we be well away from the city ; and when the Prince be acknowledge , and our champion have restore he , and he hath his own house at St. James 's or Windsor , we can come back to ours here . do you not think so , Harry and Frank ? " 
 Frank and Harry think with she , you may be sure . 
 " we will go , then , " say beatrix , turn a little pale ; " Lady Masham be to give I warn to - night how her Majesty be , and to - morrow " 
 " I think we have well go to - day , my dear , " say my Lady Castlewood ; " we might have the coach and sleep at Hounslow , and reach home to - morrow . ' tis twelve o'clock ; bid the coach , cousin , be ready at one . " 
 " for shame ! " burst out beatrix , in a passion of tear and mortification . " you disgrace I by your cruel precaution ; my own mother be the first to suspect I , and would take I away as my gaoler .
