I will not go with you , mother ; I will go as no one 's prisoner . if I want to deceive , do you think I could find no mean of evade you ? my family suspect I . as those mistrust I that ought to love I most , let I leave they ; I will go , but I will go alone : to Castlewood , be it . I have be unhappy there and lonely enough ; let I go back , but spare I at least the humiliation of set a watch over my misery , which be a trial I can not   bear . let I go when you will , but alone , or not at all . you three can stay and triumph over my unhappiness , and I will bear it as I have bear it before . let my gaoler - in - chief go order the coach that be to take I away . I thank you , Henry Esmond , for your share in the conspiracy . all my life long I will thank you , and remember you , and you , brother , and you , mother , how shall I show my gratitude to you for your careful defence of my honor ? " 
 she sweep out of the room with the air of an empress , fling glance of defiance at we all , and leave we conqueror of the field , but scar , and almost ashamed of our victory . it do indeed seem hard and cruel that we three should have conspire the banishment and humiliation of that fair creature . we look at each other in silence : ' twa not the first stroke by many of our action in that unlucky time , which , be do , we wish undo . we agree it be good she should go alone , speak stealthily to one another , and under our breath , like person engage in an act they feel ashamed in do . 
 in a half - hour , it might be , after our talk she come back , her countenance wear the same defiant air which it have bear when she leave we . she hold a shagreen - case in her hand ; Esmond know it as contain his diamond which he have give to she for her marriage with Duke Hamilton , and which she have wear so splendidly on the inauspicious night of the Prince 's arrival . " I have bring back , " say she , " to the Marquis of Esmond the present he deign to make I in day when he trust I well than now . I will never accept a benefit or a kindness from Henry Esmond more , and I give back these family diamond , which belong to one king 's mistress , to the gentleman that suspect I would be another . have you be upon your message of coach - caller , my Lord Marquis ? will you send your valet to see that I do not run away ? " we be right , yet , by her manner , she have put we all in the wrong ; we be conqueror , yet the honor of the day seem to be with the poor oppress girl . 
 that luckless box contain the stone have first be ornament with a baron 's coronet , when beatrix be engage to the young gentleman from whom she part , and afterwards the gilt crown of a duchess figure on the cover , which also poor Beatrix be destine never to wear . Lady Castlewood open the case mechanically and scarce think what she do ; and behold , besides the diamond , Esmond 's present , there lie in the box the enamel miniature of the late Duke , which Beatrix have lay aside with her mourning when the King come into the house ; and which the poor heedless thing very likely have forget . 
 " do you leave this , too , beatrix ? " say her mother , take the miniature out , and with a cruelty she do not very often show ; but there be some moment when the tender woman be cruel , and some triumph which angel can not   forego . 
    this remark show how unjustly and contemptuously even the 
  good of man will sometimes judge of our sex . Lady 
  Castlewood have no intention of triumph over her daughter ; 
  but from a sense of duty alone point out her deplorable 
  wrong . H. E. 
 have deliver this stab , Lady Castlewood be frighten at the effect of her blow . it go to poor beatrix 's heart : she flush up and pass a handkerchief across her eye , and kiss the miniature , and put it into her bosom : " I have forget it , " say she ; " my injury make I forget my grief : my mother have recall both to I . Farewell , mother ; I think I never can forgive you ; something hath break between we that no tear nor year can repair . I always say I be alone ; you never love I , never and be jealous of I from the time I sit on my father 's knee . let I go away , the soon the well : I can bear to be with you no more . " 
 " go , child , " say her mother , still very stern ; " go and bend your proud knee and ask forgiveness ; go , pray in solitude for humility and repentance . ' tis not your reproach that make I unhappy , ' tis your hard heart , my poor beatrix ; may God soften it , and teach you one day to feel for your mother . " 
 if my mistress be cruel , at least she never could be get to own as much . her haughtiness quite overtop Beatrix 's ; and , if the girl have a proud spirit , I very much fear it come to she by inheritance . 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 our guest quit US as not be hospitable ENOUGH . 

 beatrix 's departure take place within an hour , she maid go with she in the post - chaise , and a man arm on the coach - box to prevent any danger of the road . Esmond and Frank think of escort the carriage , but she indignantly refuse their company , and another man be send to follow the coach , and not to leave it till it have pass over Hounslow Heath on the next day . and these two form the whole of Lady Castlewood 's male domestic , Mr. Esmond 's faithful John Lockwood come to wait on his mistress during their absence , though he would have prefer to escort Mrs. Lucy , his sweetheart , on her journey into the country . 
 we have a gloomy and silent meal ; it seem as if a darkness be over the house , since the bright face of beatrix have be withdraw from it . in the afternoon come a message from the favorite to relieve we somewhat from this despondency . " the Queen hath be much shake , " the note say ; ' she be well now , and all thing will go well . let MY LORD CASTLEWOOD be ready against we send for he . " 
 at night there come a second billet : " there hath be a great battle in Council ; Lord Treasurer hath break his staff , and hath fall never to rise again ; no successor be appoint . Lord B receive a great Whig company to - night at Golden Square . if he be trim , other be true ; the Queen hath no more fit , but be a - bed now , and more quiet . be ready against morning , when I still hope all will be well . " 
 the Prince come home shortly after the messenger who bear this billet have leave the house . his Royal Highness be so much the well for the Bishop 's liquor , that to talk affair to he now be of little service . he be help to the Royal bed ; he call Castlewood familiarly by his own name ; he quite forget the part upon the acting of which his crown , his safety , depend . ' twa lucky that my Lady Castlewood 's servant be out of the way , and only those hear he who would not betray he . he inquire after the adorable beatrix , with a royal hiccup in his voice ; he be easily get to bed , and in a minute or two plunge in that deep slumber and forgetfulness with which Bacchus reward the votary of that god . we wish Beatrix have be there to see he in his cup . we regret , perhaps , that she be go . 
 one of the party at Kensington Square be fool enough to ride to Hounslow that night , coram latronibus , and to the inn which the family use ordinarily in their journey out of London . Esmond desire my landlord not to acquaint Madam Beatrix with his coming , and have the grim satisfaction of pass by the door of the chamber where she lie with she maid , and of watch her chariot set forth in the early morning . he see her smile and slip money into the man 's hand who be order to ride behind the coach as far as Bagshot . the road be open , and the other servant arm , it appear she dispense with the escort of a second domestic ; and this fellow , bid his young mistress adieu with many bow , go and take a pot of ale in the kitchen , and return in company with his brother servant , John Coachman , and his horse , back to London . 
 they be not a mile out of Hounslow when the two worthy stop for more drink , and here they be scar by see Colonel Esmond gallop by they . the man say in reply to Colonel Esmond 's stern question , that his young mistress have send her duty ; only that , no other message : she have have a very good night , and would reach Castlewood by nightfall . the Colonel have no time for further colloquy , and gallop on swiftly to London , have business of great importance there , as my reader very well knoweth . the thought of beatrix rid away from the danger soothe his mind not a little . his horse be at Kensington Square   honest Dapple know the way thither well enough   before the tipsy guest of last night be awake and sober . 
 the account of the previous evening be know all over the town early next day . a violent altercation have take place before the Queen in the Council Chamber ; and all the coffee - house have their version of the quarrel . the news bring my Lord Bishop early to Kensington Square , where he await the waking of his Royal master above stair , and speak confidently of have he proclaim as Prince of Wales and heir to the throne before that day be over . the Bishop have entertain on the previous afternoon certain of the most influential gentleman of the true british party . his Royal highness have charm all , both scot and English , Papists and Churchmen : " even Quakers , " say he , " be at our meeting ; and , if the stranger take a little too much british punch and ale , he will soon grow more accustomed to those liquor ; and my Lord Castlewood , " say the Bishop with a laugh , " must bear the cruel charge of have be for once in his life a little tipsy . he toast your lovely sister a dozen time , at which we all laugh , " say the Bishop , " admire so much fraternal affection . where be that charming nymph , and why doth she not adorn your ladyship 's tea - table with her bright eye ? " 
 her ladyship say , dryly , that beatrix be not at home that morning ; my Lord Bishop be too busy with great affair to trouble himself much about the presence or absence of any lady , however beautiful . 
 we be yet at table when Dr. A come from the Palace with a look of great alarm ; the shock the Queen have have the day before have act on she severely ; he have be send for , and have order she to be blood . the surgeon of Long Acre have come to cup the Queen , and her Majesty be now more easy and breathe more freely . what make we start at the name of Mr. Ayme ? " il faut etre aimable pour etre aime , " say the merry Doctor ; Esmond pull his sleeve , and bid he hush . it be to Ayme 's house , after his fatal duel , that my dear Lord Castlewood , Frank 's father , have be carry to die . 
 no second visit could be pay to the Queen on that day at any rate ; and when our guest above give his signal that he be awake , the Doctor , the Bishop , and Colonel Esmond wait upon the Prince 's levee , and bring he their news , cheerful or dubious . the doctor have to go away presently , but promise to keep the Prince constantly acquaint with what be take place at the Palace hard by . his counsel be , and the Bishop 's , that as soon as ever the Queen 's malady take a favorable turn , the Prince should be introduce to she bedside ; the Council summon ; the guard at Kensington and St. James 's , of which two regiment be to be entirely rely on , and one know not to be hostile , would declare for the Prince , as the Queen would before the Lords of her Council , designate he as the heir to her throne . 
 with locked door , and Colonel Esmond act as secretary , the Prince and his Lordship of Rochester pass many hour of this day , compose Proclamations and Addresses to the Country , to the Scots , to the Clergy , to the People of London and England ; announce the arrival of the exile descendant of three sovereign , and his acknowledgment by his sister as heir to the throne . every safeguard for their liberty , the Church and People could ask , be promise to they . the Bishop could answer for the adhesion of very many prelate , who besought of their flock and brother ecclesiastic to recognize the sacred right of the future sovereign , and to purge the country of the sin of rebellion . 
 during the composition of these paper , more messenger than one come from the Palace regard the state of the august patient there lie . at mid - day she be somewhat well ; at evening the torpor again seize she , and she wander in her mind . at night Dr. A be with we again , with a report rather more favorable : no instant danger at any rate be apprehend . in the course of the last two year her Majesty have have many attack similar , but more severe . 
 by this time we have finish a half - dozen of Proclamations ,   the wording of they so as to offend no party , and not to give umbrage to whig or Dissenters , require very great caution ,   and the young Prince , who have indeed show , during a long day 's labor , both alacrity at seize the information give he , and ingenuity and skill in turn the phrase which be to go out sign by his name , here exhibit a good - humor and thoughtfulness that ought to be set down to his credit . 
 " be these paper to be mislay , " say he , " or our scheme to come to mishap , my Lord Esmond 's writing would bring he to a place where I heartily hope never to see he ; and so , by your leave , I will copy the paper myself , though I be not very strong in spelling ; and if they be find they will implicate none but the person they most concern ; " and so , have carefully copy the Proclamations out , the Prince burn those in Colonel Esmond 's handwriting : " and now , and now , gentleman , " say he , " let we go to supper , and drink a glass with the lady . my Lord Esmond , you will sup with we to - night ; you have give we of late too little of your company . " 
 the Prince 's meal be commonly serve in the chamber which have be Beatrix 's bedroom , adjoin that in which he sleep . and the dutiful practice of his entertainer be to wait until their Royal guest bade they take their place at table before they sit down to partake of the meal . on this night , as you may suppose , only Frank Castlewood and his mother be in wait when the supper be announce to receive the Prince ; who have pass the whole of the day in his own apartment , with the Bishop as his Minister of State , and Colonel Esmond officiate as Secretary of his Council . 
 the Prince 's countenance wear an expression by no means pleasant ; when look towards the little company assemble , and wait for he , he do not see Beatrix 's bright face there as usual to greet he . he ask Lady Esmond for his fair introducer of yesterday : her ladyship only cast her eye down , and say quietly , beatrix could not be of the supper that night ; nor do she show the least sign of confusion , whereas Castlewood turn red , and Esmond be no less embarrassed . I think woman have an instinct of dissimulation ; they know by nature how to disguise their emotion far well than the most consummate male courtier can do . be not the well part of the life of many of they spend in hide their feeling , in cajole their tyrant , in mask over with fond smile and artful gayety , their doubt , or their grief , or their terror ? 
 our guest swallow his supper very sulkily ; it be not till the second bottle his Highness begin to rally . when Lady Castlewood ask leave to depart , he send a message to beatrix , hop she would be present at the next day 's dinner , and apply himself to drink , and to talk afterwards , for which there be subject in plenty . 
 the next day , we hear from our informer at Kensington that the Queen be somewhat well , and have be up for an hour , though she be not well enough yet to receive any visitor . 
 at dinner a single cover be lay for his Royal Highness ; and the two gentleman alone wait on he . we have have a consultation in the morning with Lady Castlewood , in which it have be determine that , should his Highness ask further question about beatrix , he should be answer by the gentleman of the house . 
 he be evidently disturb and uneasy , look towards the door constantly , as if expect some one . there come , however , nobody , except honest John Lockwood , when he knock with a dish , which those within take from he ; so the meal be always arrange , and I believe the council in the kitchen be of opinion that my young lord have bring over a priest , who have convert we all into Papists , and that Papists be like Jews , eat together , and not choose to take their meal in the sight of Christians . 
 the Prince try to cover his displeasure ; he be but a clumsy dissembler at that time , and when out of humor could with difficulty keep a serene countenance ; and have make some foolish attempt at trivial talk , he come to his point presently , and in as easy a manner as he could , say to Lord Castlewood , he hop , he request , his lordship 's mother and sister would be of the supper that night . as the time hang heavy on he , and he must not go abroad , would not Miss Beatrix hold he company at a game of card ? 
 at this , look up at Esmond , and take the signal from he , Lord Castlewood inform his Royal Highness   that his sister Beatrix be not at Kensington ; and that her family have think it well she should quit the town . 
    in London we address the Prince as Royal Highness 
  invariably , though the woman persist in give he the 
  title of King . 
 " not at Kensington ! " say he ; " be she ill ? she be well yesterday ; wherefore should she quit the town ? be it at your order , my lord , or Colonel Esmond 's , who seem the master of this house ? " 
 " not of this , sir , " say Frank very nobly , " only of our house in the country , which he hath give to we . this be my mother 's house , and Walcote be my father 's , and the Marquis of Esmond know he hath but to give his word , and I return his to he . " 
 " the Marquis of Esmond ! the Marquis of Esmond , " say the Prince , toss off a glass , " meddle too much with my affair , and presume on the service he hath do I . if you want to carry your suit with Beatrix , my lord , by block she up in gaol , let I tell you that be not the way to win a woman . " 
 " I be not aware , sir , that I have speak of my suit to Madam Beatrix to your Royal Highness . " 
 " bah , bah , Monsieur ! we need not be a conjurer to see that . it make itself see at all moment . you be jealous , my lord , and the maid of honor can not look at another face without yours begin to scowl . that which you do be unworthy , Monsieur ; be inhospitable be , be lache , yes , lache : "   he speak rapidly in French , his rage carry he away with each phrase :   " I come to your house ; I risk my life ; I pass it in ennui ; I repose myself on your fidelity ; I have no company but your lordship 's sermon or the conversation of that adorable young lady , and you take she from I , and you , you rest ! merci , Monsieur ! I shall thank you when I have the mean ; I shall know to recompense a devotion a little importunate , my lord a little importunate . for a month past your air of protector have annoy I beyond measure . you deign to offer I the crown , and bid I take it on my knee like King John eh ! I know my history , Monsieur , and mock myself of frown baron . I admire your mistress , and you send she to a Bastile of the Province ; I enter your house , and you mistrust I . I will leave it , Monsieur ; from to - night I will leave it . I have other friend whose loyalty will not be so ready to question mine . if I have garter to give away , ' tis to nobleman who be not so ready to think evil . bring I a coach and let I quit this place , or let the fair beatrix return to it . I will not have your hospitality at the expense of the freedom of that fair creature . " 
 this harangue be utter with rapid gesticulation such as the french use , and in the language of that nation . the Prince stride up and down the room ; his face flush , and his hand tremble with anger . he be very thin and frail from repeat illness and a life of pleasure . either Castlewood or Esmond could have break he across their knee , and in half a minute 's struggle put an end to he ; and here he be insult we both , and scarce deign to hide from the two , whose honor it most concerned , the passion he feel for the young lady of our family . my Lord Castlewood reply to the Prince 's tirade very nobly and simply . 
 " Sir , " say he , " your Royal Highness be pleased to forget that other risk their life , and for your cause . very few englishman , please God , would dare to lay hand on your sacred person , though none would ever think of respect ours . our family 's life be at your service , and everything we have except our honor . " 
 " Honor ! bah , sir , who ever think of hurt your honor ? " say the Prince with a peevish air . 
 " we implore your Royal Highness never to think of hurt it , " say Lord Castlewood with a low bow . the night be warm , the window be open both towards the Gardens and the Square . Colonel Esmond hear through the closed door the voice of the watchman call the hour , in the square on the other side . he open the door communicate with the Prince 's room ; Martin , the servant that have ride with Beatrix to Hounslow , be just go out of the chamber as Esmond enter it , and when the fellow be go , and the watchman again sing his cry of " Past ten o'clock , and a starlight night , " Esmond speak to the Prince in a low voice , and say " your Royal Highness hear that man . " 
 " Apres , Monsieur ? " say the Prince . 
 " I have but to beckon he from the window , and send he fifty yard , and he return with a guard of man , and I deliver up to he the body of the person call himself James the Third , for whose capture Parliament hath offer a reward of 500L. , as your Royal Highness see on our ride from Rochester . I have but to say the word , and , by the heaven that make I , I would say it if I think the Prince , for his honor 's sake , would not desist from insult our . but the first gentleman of England know his duty too well to forget himself with the humblest , or peril his crown for a deed that be shameful if it be do . " 
 " have your lordship anything to say , " say the Prince , turn to Frank Castlewood , and quite pale with anger ; " any threat or any insult , with which you would like to end this agreeable night 's entertainment ? " 
 " I follow the head of our house , " say Castlewood , bow gravely . " at what time shall it please the Prince that we should wait upon he in the morning ? " 
 " you will wait on the Bishop of Rochester early , you will bid he bring his coach hither ; and prepare an apartment for I in his own house , or in a place of safety . the King will reward you handsomely , never fear , for all you have do in his behalf . I wish you a good night , and shall go to bed , unless it please the Marquis of Esmond to call his colleague , the watchman , and that I should pass the night with the Kensington guard . fare you well , be sure I will remember you . my Lord Castlewood , I can go to bed to - night without need of a chamberlain . " and the Prince dismiss we with a grim bow , lock one door as he speak , that into the supping - room , and the other through which we pass , after we . it lead into the small chamber which Frank Castlewood or MONSIEUR BAPTISTE occupy , and by which Martin enter when Colonel Esmond but now see he in the chamber . 
 at an early hour next morning the Bishop arrive , and be closet for some time with his master in his own apartment , where the Prince lay open to his counsellor the wrong which , accord to his version , he have receive from the gentleman of the Esmond family . the worthy prelate come out from the conference with an air of great satisfaction ; he be a man full of resource , and of a most assured fidelity , and possess of genius , and a hundred good quality ; but captious and of a most jealous temper , that could not help exult at the downfall of any favorite ; and he be pleased in spite of himself to hear that the Esmond Ministry be at an end . 
 " I have soothe your guest , " say he , come out to the two gentleman and the widow ; who have be make acquainted with somewhat of the dispute of the night before .   by the version we give she , the Prince be only make to exhibit anger because we doubt of his intention in respect to beatrix ; and to leave we , because we question his honor .   " but I think , all thing consider , ' tis as well he should leave this house ; and then , my Lady Castlewood , " say the Bishop , " my pretty beatrix may come back to it . " 
 " she be quite as well at home at Castlewood , " Esmond 's mistress say , " till everything be over . " 
 " you shall have your title , Esmond , that I promise you , " say the good Bishop , assume the air of a Prime Minister . " the Prince hath express himself most nobly in regard of the little difference of last night , and I promise you he hath listen to my sermon , as well as to that of other folk , " say the Doctor , archly ; " he hath every great and generous quality , with perhaps a weakness for the sex which belong to his family , and hath be know in score of popular sovereign from King David downward . " 
 " my lord , my lord ! " break out Lady Esmond , " the levity with which you speak of such conduct towards our sex shock I , and what you call weakness I call deplorable sin . " 
 " sin it be , my dear creature , " say the Bishop , with a shrug , take snuff ; " but consider what a sinner King Solomon be , and in spite of a thousand of wife too . " 
 " enough of this , my lord , " say Lady Castlewood , with a fine blush , and walk out of the room very stately . 
 the Prince enter it presently with a smile on his face , and if he feel any offence against we on the previous night , at present exhibit none . he offer a hand to each gentleman with great courtesy . " if all your bishop preach so well as Doctor Atterbury . " say he , " I do not   know , gentleman , what may happen to I . I speak very hastily , my lord , last night , and ask pardon of both of you . but I must not stay any long , " say he , " give umbrage to good friend , or keep pretty girl away from their home . my Lord Bishop hath find a safe place for I , hard by at a curate 's house , whom the Bishop can trust , and whose wife be so ugly as to be beyond all danger ; we will decamp into those new quarter , and I leave you , thank you for a hundred kindness here . where be my hostess , that I may bid her farewell ; to welcome she in a house of my own , soon , I trust , where my friend shall have no cause to quarrel with I . " 
 Lady Castlewood arrive presently , blush with great grace , and tear fill her eye as the Prince graciously salute she . she look so charming and young , that the doctor , in his banter way , could not help speak of her beauty to the Prince ; whose compliment make her blush , and look more charming still . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 a great scheme , and who balk it . 

 as character write with a secret ink come out with the application of fire , and disappear again and leave the paper white , as soon as it be cool ; a hundred name of man , high in repute and favor the Prince 's cause , that be writ in our private list , would have be visible enough on the great roll of the conspiracy , have it ever be lay open under the sun . what crowd would have press forward , and subscribe their name and protest their loyalty , when the danger be over ! what a number of whig , now high in place and creature of the all - powerful Minister , scorn Mr. Walpole then ! if ever a match be gain by the manliness and decision of a few at a moment of danger ; if ever one be lose by the treachery and imbecility of those that have the card in their hand , and might have play they , it be in that momentous game which be enact in the next three day , and of which the noble crown in the world be the stake . 
 from the conduct of my Lord Bolingbroke , those who be interested in the scheme we have in hand , see pretty well that he be not to be trust . should the Prince prevail , it be his lordship 's gracious intention to declare for he : should the hanoverian party bring in their sovereign , who more ready to go on his knee , and cry , " God Save King George ? " and he betray the one Prince and the other ; but exactly at the wrong time . when he should have strike for King James , he falter and coquet with the Whigs ; and have commit himself by the most monstrous profession of devotion , which the Elector rightly scorn , he prove the justness of their contempt for he by fly and take renegade service with St. Germains , just when he should have keep aloof : and that Court despise he , as the manly and resolute man who establish the Elector in England have before do . he sign his own name to every accusation of insincerity his enemy make against he ; and the King and the Pretender alike could show proof of St. John 's treachery under his own hand and seal . 
 our friend keep a pretty close watch upon his motion , as on those of the brave and hearty Whig party , that make little concealment of theirs . they would have in the elector , and use every mean in their power to effect their end . my Lord Marlborough be now with they . his expulsion from power by the Tories have throw that great captain at once on the Whig side . we hear he be come from Antwerp ; and , in fact , on the day of the Queen 's death , he once more land on English shore . a great part of the army be always with their illustrious leader ; even the Tories in it be indignant at the injustice of the persecution which the Whig officer be make to undergo . the chief of these be in London , and at the head of they one of the most intrepid man in the world , the Scots Duke of Argyle , whose conduct on the second day after that to which I have now bring down my history , end , as such honesty and bravery deserve to end , by establish the present Royal race on the english throne . 
 meanwhile there be no slight difference of opinion amongst the councillor surround the Prince , as to the plan his Highness should pursue . his female Minister at Court , fancy she see some amelioration in the Queen , be for wait a few day , or hour it might be , until he could be bring to she bedside , and acknowledge as her heir . Mr. Esmond be for have he march thither , escort by a couple of troop of Horse Guards , and openly present himself to the Council . during the whole of the night of the 29th-30th July , the Colonel be engage with gentleman of the military profession , whom ' tis needless here to name ; suffice it to say that several of they have exceed high rank in the army , and one of they in especial be a General , who , when he hear the Duke of Marlborough be come on the other side , wave his crutch over his head with a huzzah , at the idea that he should march out and engage he . of the three Secretaries of State , we know that one be devoted to we . the Governor of the Tower be ours ; the two company on duty at Kensington barrack be safe ; and we have intelligence , very speedy and accurate , of all that take place at the Palace within . 
 at noon , on the 30th of July , a message come to the Prince 's friend that the Committee of Council be sit at Kensington Palace , their Graces of Ormonde and Shrewsbury , the Archbishop of Canterbury and the three Secretaries of State , be there assemble . in an hour afterwards , hurried news be bring that the two great Whig Dukes , Argyle and Somerset , have break into the Council - chamber without a summon , and take their seat at table . after hold a debate there , the whole party proceed to the chamber of the Queen , who be lie in great weakness , but still sensible , and the Lords recommend his Grace of Shrewsbury as the fit person to take the vacant place of Lord Treasurer ; her Majesty give he the staff , as all know . " and now , " writ my messenger from Court , " now or never be the TIME . " 
 now or never be the time indeed . in spite of the Whig Dukes , our side have still the majority in the Council , and Esmond , to whom the message have be bring ,   the personage at Court not be aware that the Prince have quit his lodging in Kensington Square ,   and Esmond 's gallant young aide - de - camp , Frank Castlewood , put on sword and uniform , take a brief leave of their dear lady , who embrace and bless they both , and go to her chamber to pray for the issue of the great event which be then pende . 
 Castlewood speed to the barrack to give warning to the captain of the Guard there ; and then go to the " King 's arm " tavern at Kensington , where our friend be assemble , have come by party of two and three , rid or in coach , and be get together in the upper chamber , fifty - three of they ; their servant , who have be instruct to bring arm likewise , be below in the garden of the tavern , where they be serve with drink . out of this garden be a little door that lead into the road of the Palace , and through this it be arrange that master and servant be to march ; when that signal be give , and that personage appear , for whom all be wait . there be in our company the famous officer next in command to the Captain - General of the Forces , his grace the Duke of Ormonde , who be within at the Council . there be with he two more lieutenant - general , nine major - general and brigadier , seven colonel , eleven Peers of Parliament , and twenty - one member of the House of Commons . the Guard be with we within and without the Palace : the Queen be with we ; the Council   save the two Whig Dukes , that must have succumb ; the day be our own , and with a beat heart Esmond walk rapidly to the Mall of Kensington , where he have part with the Prince on the night before . for three night the Colonel have not be to bed : the last have be pass summon the Prince 's friend together , of whom the great majority have no sort of inkling of the transaction pende until they be tell that he be actually on the spot , and be summon to strike the blow . the night before and after the altercation with the Prince , my gentleman , have suspicion of his Royal Highness , and fear lest he should be minded to give we the slip , and fly off after his fugitive beauty , have spend , if the truth must be tell , at the " greyhound " tavern , over against my Lady Castlewood 's house in Kensington Square , with an eye on the door , lest the Prince should escape from it . the night before that he have pass in his boot at the " Crown " at Hounslow , where he must watch forsooth all night , in order to get one moment 's glimpse of beatrix in the morning . and fate have decree that he be to have a fourth night 's ride and wakefulness before his business be end . 
 he run to the curate 's house in Kensington Mall , and ask for Mr. Bates , the name the Prince go by . the curate 's wife say Mr. Bates have go abroad very early in the morning in his boot , say he be go to the Bishop of Rochester 's house at Chelsey . but the Bishop have be at Kensington himself two hour ago to seek for Mr. Bates , and have return in his coach to his own house , when he hear that the gentleman be go thither to seek he . 
 this absence be most unpropitious , for an hour 's delay might cost a kingdom ; Esmond have nothing for it but to hasten to the " King 's arm , " and tell the gentleman there assemble that Mr. George   as we call the Prince there   be not at home , but that Esmond would go fetch he ; and take a General 's coach that happen to be there , Esmond drive across the country to Chelsey , to the Bishop 's house there . 
 the porter say two gentleman be with his lordship , and Esmond run past this sentry up to the locked door of the Bishop 's study , at which he rattle , and be admit presently . of the Bishop 's guest one be a brother prelate , and the other the Abbe g . 
 " where be Mr. George ? " say Mr. Esmond ; " now be the time . " the Bishop look scared : " I go to his lodging , " he say , " and they tell I he be come hither . I return as quick as coach would carry I ; and he hath not be here . " 
 the Colonel burst out with an oath ; that be all he could say to their reverence ; run down the stair again , and bid the coachman , an old friend and fellow - campaigner , drive as if he be charge the French with his master at Wynendael they be back at Kensington in half an hour . 
 again Esmond go to the curate 's house . Mr. Bates have not return . the Colonel have to go with this blank errand to the gentleman at the " King 's arm , " that be grow very impatient by this time . 
 out of the window of the tavern , and look over the garden wall , you can see the green before Kensington Palace , the Palace gate   round which the Ministers ' coach be stand , and the barrack building . as we be look out from this window in gloomy discourse , we hear presently trumpet blow , and some of we run to the window of the front - room , look into the High Street of Kensington , and see a regiment of horse come . 
 " it be Ormonde 's guard , " say one . 
 " no , by God , it be Argyle 's old regiment ! " say my General , clap down his crutch . 
 it be , indeed , Argyle 's regiment that be bring from Westminster , and that take the place of the regiment at Kensington on which we could rely . 
 " oh , Harry ! " say one of the general there present , " you be bear under an unlucky star ; I begin to think that there be no Mr. George , nor Mr. Dragon either . ' tis not the peerage I care for , for our name be so ancient and famous , that merely to be call Lord Lydiard would do I no good ; but ' tis the chance you promise I of fight Marlborough . " 
 as we be talk , Castlewood enter the room with a disturb air . 
 " what news , Frank ? " say the Colonel . " be Mr. George come at last ? " 
 " damn he , look here ! " say Castlewood , hold out a paper . " I find it in the book the what you call it , ' Eikum Basilikum , ' that villain Martin put it there he say his young mistress bid he . it be direct to I , but it be mean for he I know , and I break the seal and read it . " 
 the whole assembly of officer seem to swim away before Esmond 's eye as he read the paper ; all that be write on it be : " beatrix Esmond be send away to prison , to Castlewood , where she will pray for happy day . " 
 " can you guess where he be ? " say Castlewood . 
 " yes , " say Colonel Esmond . he know full well , Frank know full well : our instinct tell whither that traitor have flee . 
 he have courage to turn to the company and say , " gentleman , I fear very much that Mr. George will not be here to - day ; something hath happen and and I very much fear some accident may befall he , which must keep he out of the way . have have your noon 's draught , you have well pay the reckoning and go home ; there can be no game where there be no one to play it . " 
 some of the gentleman go away without a word , other call to pay their duty to her Majesty and ask for her health . the little army disappear into the darkness out of which it have be call ; there have be no writing , no paper to implicate any man . some few officer and member of Parliament have be invite over night to breakfast at the " King 's arm , " at Kensington ; and they have call for their bill and go home . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

 AUGUST 1st , 1714 . 

 " do my mistress know of this ? " Esmond ask of Frank , as they walk along . 
 " my mother find the letter in the book , on the toilet - table . she have writ it ere she have leave home , " Frank say . " Mother meet she on the stair , with her hand upon the door , try to enter , and never leave she after that till she go away . he do not think of look at it there , nor have Martin the chance of tell he . I believe the poor devil mean no harm , though I half kill he ; he think ' twa to Beatrix 's brother he be bring the letter . " 
 Frank never say a word of reproach to I for have bring the villain amongst we . as we knock at the door I say , " when will the horse be ready ? " Frank point with his cane , they be turn the street that moment . 
 we go up and bade adieu to our mistress ; she be in a dreadful state of agitation by this time , and that Bishop be with she whose company she be so fond of . 
 " do you tell he , my lord , " say Esmond , " that beatrix be at Castlewood ? " the Bishop blush and stammer : " well , " say he , " I . . . " 
 " you serve the villain right , " break out Mr. Esmond , " and he have lose a crown by what you tell he . " 
 my mistress turn quite white , " Henry , Henry , " say she , " do not kill he . " 
 " it may not be too late , " say Esmond ; " he may not have go to Castlewood ; pray God , it be not too late . " the Bishop be break out with some banale phrase about loyalty , and the sacredness of the Sovereign 's person ; but Esmond sternly bid he hold his tongue , burn all paper , and take care of Lady Castlewood ; and in five minute he and Frank be in the saddle , John Lockwood behind they , rid towards Castlewood at a rapid pace . 
 we be just get to Alton , when who should meet we but old Lockwood , the porter from Castlewood , John 's father , walk by the side of the Hexton flying - coach , who sleep the night at Alton . Lockwood say his young mistress have arrive at home on Wednesday night , and this morning , Friday , have despatch he with a packet for my lady at Kensington , say the letter be of great importance . 
 we take the freedom to break it , while Lockwood star with wonder , and cry out his " Lord bless I 's , " and " who have a thought it be , " at the sight of his young lord , whom he have not see these seven year . 
 the packet from Beatrix contain no news of importance at all . it be write in a jocular strain , affect to make light of her captivity . she ask whether she might have leave to visit Mrs. Tusher , or to walk beyond the court and the garden wall . she give news of the peacock , and a fawn she have there . she bid her mother send her certain gown and smock by old Lockwood ; she send her duty to a certain Person , if certain other person permit she to take such a freedom ; how that , as she be not able to play card with he , she hop he would read good book , such as Doctor Atterbury 's sermon and " Eikon Basilike : " she be go to read good book ; she think she pretty mamma would like to know she be not cry her eye out . 
 " who be in the house besides you , Lockwood ? " say the Colonel . 
 " there be the laundry - maid , and the kitchen - maid , Madam Beatrix 's maid , the man from London , and that be all ; and he sleepeth in my lodge away from the maid , " say old Lockwood . 
 Esmond scribble a line with a pencil on the note , give it to the old man , and bid he go on to his lady . we know why Beatrix have be so dutiful on a sudden , and why she speak of " Eikon Basilike . " she writ this letter to put the Prince on the scent , and the porter out of the way . 
 " we have a fine moonlight night for rid on , " say Esmond ; " Frank , we may reach Castlewood in time yet . " all the way along they make inquiry at the post - house , when a tall young gentleman in a gray suit , with a light brown periwig , just the color of my lord 's , have be see to pass . he have set off at six that morning , and we at three in the afternoon . he ride almost as quickly as we have do ; he be seven hour a - head of we still when we reach the last stage . 
 we ride over Castlewood Downs before the breaking of dawn . we pass the very spot where the car be upset fourteen year since , and Mohun lie . the village be not up yet , nor the forge light , as we ride through it , pass by the elm , where the rook be still roost , and by the church , and over the bridge . we get off our horse at the bridge and walk up to the gate . 
 " if she be safe , " say Frank , trembling , and his honest eye fill with tear , " a silver statue to our Lady ! " he be go to rattle at the great iron knocker on the oak gate ; but Esmond stop his kinsman 's hand . he have his own fear , his own hope , his own despair and grief , too ; but he speak not a word of these to his companion , or show any sign of emotion . 
 he go and tap at the little window at the porter 's lodge , gently , but repeatedly , until the man come to the bar . 
 " who be there ? " say he , look out ; it be the servant from Kensington . 
 " my Lord Castlewood and Colonel Esmond , " we say , from below . " open the gate and let we in without any noise . " 
 " my Lord Castlewood ? " say the other ; " my lord 's here , and in bed . " 
 " open , d n you , " say Castlewood , with a curse . 
 " I shall open to no one , " say the man , shut the glass window as Frank draw a pistol . he would have fire at the porter , but Esmond again hold his hand . 
 " there be more way than one , " say he , " of enter such a great house as this . " Frank grumble that the west gate be half a mile round . " but I know of a way that be not a hundred yard off , " say Mr. Esmond ; and lead his kinsman close along the wall , and by the shrub which have now grow thick on what have be an old moat about the house , they come to the buttress , at the side of which the little window be , which be Father Holt 's private door . esmond climb up to this easily , break a pane that have be mend , and touch the spring inside , and the two gentleman pass in that way , treading as lightly as they could ; and so go through the passage into the court , over which the dawn be now redden , and where the fountain plash in the silence . 
 they speed instantly to the porter 's lodge , where the fellow have not fasten his door that lead into the court ; and pistol in hand come upon the terrify wretch , and bid he be silent . then they ask he   Esmond 's head reel , and he almost fall as he speak   when Lord Castlewood have arrive ? he say on the previous evening , about eight of the clock . " and what then ? " his lordship sup with his sister . " do the man wait ? " yes , he and my lady 's maid both wait : the other servant make the supper ; and there be no wine , and they could give his lordship but milk , at which he grumble ; and and Madam Beatrix keep Miss Lucy always in the room with she . and there be a bed across the court in the Chaplain 's room , she have arrange my lord be to sleep there . Madam Beatrix have come down stair laugh with the maid , and have lock herself in , and my lord have stand for a while talk to she through the door , and she laugh at he . and then he pace the court awhile , and she come again to the upper window ; and my lord implore she to come down and walk in the room ; but she would not , and laugh at he again , and shut the window ; and so my lord , utter what seem curse , but in a foreign language , go to the Chaplain 's room to bed . 
 " be this all ! " " All , " the man swear upon his honor ; all as he hop to be save . " stop , there be one thing more . my lord , on arrive , and once or twice during supper , do kiss his sister , as be natural , and she kiss he . " at this esmond grind his tooth with rage , and wellnigh throttle the amazed miscreant who be speak , whereas Castlewood , seize hold of his cousin 's hand , burst into a great fit of laughter . 
 " if it amuse thee , " say Esmond in French , " that your sister should be exchange of kiss with a stranger , I fear poor beatrix will give thee plenty of sport . " esmond darkly think , how Hamilton , Ashburnham , have before be master of those rose that the young Prince 's lip be now feed on . he sicken at that notion . her cheek be desecrate , her beauty tarnish ; shame and honor stand between it and he . the love be dead within he ; have she a crown to bring he with her love , he feel that both would degrade he . 
 but this wrath against Beatrix do not lessen the angry feeling of the Colonel against the man who have be the occasion if not the cause of the evil . Frank sit down on a stone bench in the court - yard , and fairly fall asleep , while Esmond pace up and down the court , debate what should ensue . what matter how much or how little have pass between the Prince and the poor faithless girl ? they be arrive in time perhaps to rescue her person , but not her mind ; have she not instigate the young Prince to come to she ; suborn servant , dismiss other , so that she might communicate with he ? the treacherous heart within she have surrender , though the place be safe ; and it be to win this that he have give a life 's struggle and devotion ; this , that she be ready to give away for the bribe of a coronet or a wink of the Prince 's eye . 
 when he have think his thought out he shake up poor Frank from his sleep , who rise yawning , and say he have be dream of Clotilda . " you must back I , " say Esmond , " in what I be go to do . I have be think that yonder scoundrel may have be instruct to tell that story , and that the whole of it may be a lie ; if it be , we shall find it out from the gentleman who be asleep yonder . see if the door lead to my lady 's room , "   so we call the room at the north - west angle of the house ,   ' see if the door be bar as he saith . " we try ; it be indeed as the lackey have say , close within . 
 " it may have be open and shut afterwards , " say poor Esmond ; " the foundress of our family let our ancestor in in that way . " 
 " what will you do , Harry , if if what that fellow saith should turn out untrue ? " the young man look scared and frighten into his kinsman 's face ; I dare say it wear no very pleasant expression . 
 " let we first go see whether the two story agree , " say Esmond ; and go in at the passage and open the door into what have be his own chamber now for wellnigh five - and - twenty year . a candle be still burn , and the Prince asleep dress on the bed Esmond do not care for make a noise . the Prince start up in his bed , see two man in his chamber . " Qui est la " say he , and take a pistol from under his pillow . 
 " it be the Marquis of Esmond , " say the Colonel , " come to welcome his Majesty to his house of Castlewood , and to report of what hath happen in London . pursuant to the King 's order , I pass the night before last , after leave his Majesty , in wait upon the friend of the king . it be a pity that his Majesty 's desire to see the country and to visit our poor house should have cause the King to quit London without notice yesterday , when the opportunity happen which in all human probability may not occur again ; and have the King not choose to ride to Castlewood , the Prince of Wales might have sleep at St. James 's . " 
 " ' Sdeath ! gentleman , " say the Prince , start off his bed , whereon he be lie in his clothe , " the Doctor be with I yesterday morning , and after watch by my sister all night , tell I I might not hope to see the Queen . " 
 " it would have be otherwise , " say Esmond with another bow ; " as , by this time , the Queen may be dead in spite of the Doctor . the Council be meet , a new Treasurer be appoint , the troop be devoted to the King 's cause ; and fifty loyal gentleman of the great name of this kingdom be assemble to accompany the Prince of Wales , who might have be the acknowledge heir of the throne , or the possessor of it by this time , have your Majesty not choose to take the air . we be ready ; there be only one person that fail we , your Majesty 's gracious " 
 " Morbleu , Monsieur , you give I too much Majesty , " say the Prince , who have now rise up and seem to be look to one of we to help he to his coat . but neither stir . 
 " we shall take care , " say Esmond , " not much oftener to offend in that particular . " 
 " what mean you , my lord ? " say the Prince , and mutter something about a guet - a - pen , which Esmond catch up . 
 " the snare , Sir , " say he , " be not of our laying ; it be not we that invite you . we come to avenge , and not to compass , the dishonor of our family . " 
 " Dishonor ! Morbleu , there have be no dishonor , " say the Prince , turn scarlet , " only a little harmless playing . " 
 " that be mean to end seriously . " 
 " I swear , " the Prince break out impetuously , " upon the honor of a gentleman , my lord " 
 " that we arrive in time . no wrong hath be do , Frank , " say Colonel Esmond , turn round to young Castlewood , who stand at the door as the talk be go on . " see ! here be a paper whereon his Majesty have deign to commence some verse in honor , or dishonor , of Beatrix . here be ' Madame ' and ' flamme , ' ' Cruelle ' and ' rebelle , ' and ' Amour ' and ' Jour ' in the Royal writing and spelling . have the gracious lover be happy , he have not pass his time in sigh . " in fact , and actually as he be speak , Esmond cast his eye down towards the table , and see a paper on which my young Prince have be scrawl a madrigal , that be to finish his charmer on the morrow . 
 " Sir , " say the Prince , burn with rage   he have assume his Royal coat unassiste by this time , " do I come here to receive insult ? " 
 " to confer they , may it please your Majesty , " say the Colonel , with a very low bow , " and the gentleman of our family be come to thank you . " 
 " Malediction ! " say the young man , tear start into his eye with helpless rage and mortification . " what will you with I , gentleman ? " 
 " if your Majesty will please to enter the next apartment , " say Esmond , preserve his grave tone , " I have some paper there which I would gladly submit to you , and by your permission I will lead the way ; " and , take the taper up , and backing before the Prince with very great ceremony , Mr. Esmond pass into the little Chaplain 's room , through which we have just enter into the house : " please to set a chair for his Majesty , Frank , " say the Colonel to his companion , who wonder almost as much at this scene , and be as much puzzle by it , as the other actor in it . then go to the crypt over the mantel - piece , the Colonel open it , and draw thence the paper which so long have lain there . 
 " here , may it please your Majesty , " say he , " be the Patent of Marquis send over by your Royal Father at St. Germains to Viscount Castlewood , my father : here be the witness certificate of my father 's marriage to my mother , and of my birth and christening ; I be christen of that religion of which your sainted sire give all through life so shin example . these be my title , dear Frank , and this what I do with they : here go Baptism and marriage , and here the Marquisate and the August Sign - Manual , with which your predecessor be pleased to honor our race . " and as Esmond speak he set the paper burn in the brazier . " you will please , sir , to remember , " he continue , " that our family hath ruin itself by fidelity to your : that my grandfather spend his estate , and give his blood and his son to die for your service ; that my dear lord 's grandfather   for lord you be now , Frank , by right and title too   die for the same cause ; that my poor kinswoman , my father 's second wife , after give away her honor to your wicked perjure race , send all her wealth to the king ; and get in return , that precious title that lie in ash , and this inestimable yard of blue ribbon . I lie this at your foot and stamp upon it : I draw this sword , and break it and deny you ; and , have you complete the wrong you design we , by heaven I would have drive it through your heart , and no more pardon you than your father pardon Monmouth . Frank will do the same , will not   you , cousin ? " 
 Frank , who have be look on with a stupid air at the paper , as they flame in the old brazier , take out his sword and break it , hold his head down : " I go with my cousin , " say he , give Esmond a grasp of the hand . " Marquis or not , by - , I stand by he any day . I beg your Majesty 's pardon for swearing ; that be that be I be for the Elector of Hanover . it be all your Majesty 's own fault . the Queen 's dead most likely by this time . and you might have be King if you have not   come dangle after Trix . " 
 " thus to lose a crown , " say the young Prince , start up , and speak French in his eager way ; " to lose the lovely woman in the world ; to lose the loyalty of such heart as your , be not this , my lord , enough of humiliation ? Marquis , if I go on my knee will you pardon I ? no , I can not   do that , but I can offer you reparation , that of honor , that of gentleman . favor I by cross the sword with mine : yours be broke see , yonder in the armoire be two ; " and the Prince take they out as eager as a boy , and hold they towards Esmond : " ah ! you will ? merci , monsieur , merci ! " 
 extremely touch by this immense mark of condescension and repentance for wrong do , Colonel Esmond bow down so low as almost to kiss the gracious young hand that confer on he such an honor , and take his guard in silence . the sword be no soon meet , than Castlewood knock up Esmond 's with the blade of his own , which he have break off short at the shell ; and the Colonel fall back a step drop his point with another very low bow , and declare himself perfectly satisfied . 
 " eh bien , Vicomte ! " say the young Prince , who be a boy , and a french boy , " il ne nous reste qu'une choose a faire : " he place his sword upon the table , and the finger of his two hand upon his breast : " we have one more thing to do , " say he ; " you do not divine it ? " he stretch out his arm : " embrasson nous ! " 
 the talk be scarce over when Beatrix enter the room : what come she to seek there ? she start and turn pale at the sight of her brother and kinsman , draw sword , break sword - blade , and paper yet smoulder in the brazier . 
 " Charming Beatrix , " say the Prince , with a blush which become he very well , " these lord have come a - horseback from London , where my sister lie in a despaired state , and where her successor make himself desire . pardon I for my escapade of last evening . I have be so long a prisoner , that I seize the occasion of a promenade on horseback , and my horse naturally bear I towards you . I find you a queen in your little court , where you deign to entertain I . present my homage to your maid of honor . I sigh as you sleep , under the window of your chamber , and then retire to seek rest in my own . it be there that these gentleman agreeably rouse I . yes , milords , for that be a happy day that make a Prince acquaint , at whatever cost to his vanity , with such a noble heart as that of the Marquis of Esmond . Mademoiselle , may we take your coach to town ? I see it in the hangar , and this poor Marquis must be drop with sleep . " 
 " will it please the King to breakfast before he go ? " be all beatrix could say . the rose have shudder out of her cheek ; her eye be glare ; she look quite old . she come up to Esmond and hiss out a word or two : " if I do not love you before , cousin , " say she , " think how I love you now . " if word could stab , no doubt she would have kill Esmond ; she look at he as if she could . 
 but her keen word give no wound to Mr. Esmond ; his heart be too hard . as he look at she , he wonder that he could ever have love she . his love of ten year be over ; it fall down dead on the spot , at the Kensington Tavern , where Frank bring he the note out of " Eikon Basilike . " the Prince blush and bow low , as she gaze at he , and quit the chamber . I have never see she from that day . 
 horse be fetched and put to the chariot presently . my lord ride outside , and as for Esmond he be so tired that he be no soon in the carriage than he fall asleep , and never wake till night , as the coach come into Alton . 
 as we drive to the " Bell " Inn come a mitred coach with our old friend Lockwood beside the coachman . my Lady Castlewood and the Bishop be inside ; she give a little scream when she see we . the two coach enter the inn almost together ; the landlord and people come out with light to welcome the visitor . 
 we in our coach spring out of it , as soon as ever we see the dear lady , and above all , the Doctor in his cassock . what be the news ? be there yet time ? be the queen alive ? these question be put hurriedly , as Boniface stand wait before his noble guest to bow they up the stair . 
 " be she safe ? " be what Lady Castlewood whisper in a flutter to Esmond . 
 " All be well , thank God , " say he , as the fond lady take his hand and kiss it , and call he her preserver and her dear . she be not   think of Queens and crown . 
 the Bishop 's news be reassure : at least all be not lose ; the Queen yet breathe , or be alive when they leave London , six hour since .   " it be Lady Castlewood who insist on come , " the Doctor say .   Argyle have march up regiment from Portsmouth , and send abroad for more ; the whig be on the alert , a pest on they ,   I be not sure but the Bishop swear as he speak ,   and so too be our people . and all might be save , if only the Prince could be at London in time . we call for horse , instantly to return to London . we never go up poor crestfallen Boniface 's stair , but into our coach again . the Prince and his Prime Minister in one , Esmond in the other , with only his dear mistress as a companion . 
 Castlewood gallop forwards on horseback to gather the Prince 's friend and warn they of his coming . we travel through the night . esmond discourse to his mistress of the event of the last twenty - four hour ; of Castlewood 's ride and his ; of the Prince 's generous behavior and their reconciliation . the night seem short enough ; and the starlit hour pass away serenely in that fond company . 
 so we come along the road ; the Bishop 's coach head our ; and , with some delay in procure horse , we get to Hammersmith about four o'clock on Sunday morning , the first of August , and half an hour after , it be then bright day , we ride by my Lady Warwick 's house , and so down the street of Kensington . 
 early as the hour be , there be a bustle in the street and many people move to and fro . round the gate lead to the Palace , where the guard be , there be especially a great crowd . and the coach ahead of we stop , and the Bishop 's man get down to know what the concourse mean ? 
 there presently come from out of the gate Horse Guards with their trumpet , and a company of herald with their tabard . the trumpet blow , and the herald - at - arm come forward and proclaim GEORGE , by the Grace of God , of Great Britain , France , and Ireland , King , Defender of the Faith . and the people shout God save the king ! 
 among the crowd shout and wave their hat , I catch sight of one sad face , which I have know all my life , and see under many disguise . it be no other than poor Mr. Holt 's , who have slip over to England to witness the triumph of the good cause ; and now behold its enemy victorious , amidst the acclamation of the english people . the poor fellow have forget to huzzah or to take his hat off , until his neighbor in the crowd remark his want of loyalty , and curse he for a Jesuit in disguise , when he ruefully uncover and begin to cheer . sure he be the most unlucky of man : he never play a game but he lose it ; or engage in a conspiracy but ' twa certain to end in defeat . I see he in Flanders after this , whence he go to Rome to the head - quarter of his order ; and actually reappear among we in America , very old , and busy , and hopeful . I be not sure that he do not assume the hatchet and moccasin there ; and , attire in a blanket and war - paint , skulk about a missionary amongst the Indians . he lie bury in our neighboring province of Maryland now , with a cross over he , and a mound of earth above he ; under which that unquiet spirit be for ever at peace . 
 with the sound of King George 's trumpet , all the vain hope of the weak and foolish young Pretender be blow away ; and with that music , too , I may say , the drama of my own life be end . that happiness , which hath subsequently crown it , can not be write in word ; ' tis of its nature sacred and secret , and not to be speak of , though the heart be ever so full of thankfulness , save to Heaven and the one ear alone to one fond being , the true and tender and pure wife ever man be bless with . as I think of the immense happiness which be in store for I , and of the depth and intensity of that love which , for so many year , hath bless I , I own to a transport of wonder and gratitude for such a boon nay , be thankful to have be endow with a heart capable of feeling and know the immense beauty and value of the gift which God hath bestow upon I . sure , love vincit omnia ; be immeasurably above all ambition , more precious than wealth , more noble than name . he know not life who know not that : he hath not feel the high faculty of the soul who hath not enjoy it . in the name of my wife I write the completion of hope , and the summit of happiness . to have such a love be the one blessing , in comparison of which all earthly joy be of no value ; and to think of she , be to praise God . 
 it be at Bruxelles , whither we retreat after the failure of our plot our Whig friend advise we to keep out of the way that the great joy of my life be bestow upon I , and that my dear mistress become my wife . we have be so accustomed to an extreme intimacy and confidence , and have live so long and tenderly together , that we might have go on to the end without thinking of a close tie ; but circumstance bring about that event which so prodigiously multiply my happiness and her   for which I humbly thank Heaven , although a calamity befell we , which , I blush to think , hath occur more than once in our house . I know not what infatuation of ambition urge the beautiful and wayward woman , whose name hath occupy so many of these page , and who be serve by I with ten year of such constant fidelity and passion ; but ever after that day at Castlewood , when we rescue she , she persist in hold all her family as her enemy , and leave we , and escape to France , to what a fate I disdain to tell . nor be her son 's house a home for my dear mistress ; my poor Frank be weak , as perhaps all our race hath be , and lead by woman . those around he be imperious , and in a terror of his mother 's influence over he , lest he should recant , and deny the creed which he have adopt by their persuasion . the difference of their religion separate the son and the mother : my dear mistress feel that she be sever from her child and alone in the world alone but for one constant servant on whose fidelity , praise be Heaven , she could count . ' twas after a scene of ignoble quarrel on the part of Frank 's wife and mother   for the poor lad have be make to marry the whole of that german family with whom he have connect himself , that I find my mistress one day in tear , and then besought she to confide herself to the care and devotion of one who , by God 's help , would never forsake she . and then the tender matron , as beautiful in her Autumn , and as pure as virgin in their spring , with blush of love and " eye of meek surrender , " yield to my respectful importunity , and consent to share my home . let the last word I write thank she , and bless she who hath bless it . 
 by the kindness of Mr. Addison , all danger of prosecution , and every obstacle against our return to England , be remove ; and my son Frank 's gallantry in Scotland make his peace with the King 's government . but we two care no long to live in England : and Frank formally and joyfully yield over to we the possession of that estate which we now occupy , far away from Europe and its trouble , on the beautiful bank of the Potomac , where we have build a new Castlewood , and think with grateful heart of our old home . in our transatlantic country we have a season , the calm and most delightful of the year , which we call the indian summer : I often say the autumn of our life resemble that happy and serene weather , and be thankful for its rest and its sweet sunshine . Heaven hath bless we with a child , which each parent love for her resemblance to the other . our diamond be turn into plough and axis for our plantation ; and into negro , the happy and merriest , I think , in all this country : and the only jewel by which my wife set any store , and from which she hath never part , be that gold button she take from my arm on the day when she visit I in prison , and which she wear ever after , as she tell I , on the tender heart in the world .
